Also compute the daily methanol requirement if external carbon is used.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — NO₃⁻-N to be removed:** $$\Delta N = (40 - 1) - 6 = 33\,mg/L$$ $$\text{Mass/d} = 33 \times 25{,}000 \times 10^3 / 10^6 = 825\,kg/d = 8.25 \times 10^5\,g/d$$ **Step 2 — Volumetric denitrification rate:** $$q_{D,vol} = 0.06 \times 3000 = 180\,g\,N/m^3 \cdot d$$ **Step 3 — Anoxic tank volume:** $$V = \frac{8.25 \times 10^5}{180} = 4583.3\,m^3 \approx 4585\,m^3$$ **Step 4 — Hydraulic retention time:** $$HRT = \frac{4583.3}{25{,}000} \times 24 = 4.4\,h$$ **Step 5 — Methanol dose (theoretical):** $$C_{CH_3OH} = 2.47 \times 33 = 81.51\,mg/L$$ $$\text{Daily methanol} = 81.51 \times 25{,}000 \times 10^3 / 10^6 = 2037.75\,kg/d \approx 2038\,kg/d$$ **Step 6 — Actual dose with safety factor (1.2):** $$C_{actual} = 1.2 \times 81.51 = 97.81\,mg/L$$ $$\text{Daily} = 2445\,kg/d$$ **Results Table:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Anoxic volume | **4585 m³** | | HRT | **4.4 h** | | Theoretical methanol | **2038 kg/d** | | Practical methanol (1.2 SF) | **2445 kg/d** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently: > 1. **Forget the 1.2 safety factor** on methanol dose (theoretical value ignores residual BOD and process variability). > 2. **Confuse Q in m³/d vs L/d** during mass-balance calculations — always normalize units first. > 3. **Use aerobic yield (0.6) instead of denitrifier yield (0.45–0.50)** when computing biomass production. > 4. **Forget to add the 1 mg/L NH₃-N residual** in the TKN mass balance. > 5. **Omit the alkalinity recovery credit** (3.57 mg CaCO₃/mg N) when designing downstream re-aeration or pH control. > 6. **Mix up anoxic and anaerobic DO conditions** — anoxic means NO₃⁻ is present and DO is very low, NOT zero. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Denitrification (Module 1)** ### Core Concepts - [ ] Denitrification = anoxic reduction of NO₃⁻ → N₂ by facultative heterotrophs. - [ ] Four-step enzymatic cascade: NO₃⁻ → NO₂⁻ → NO → N₂O → N₂. - [ ] Distinguish **anoxic** (DO < 0.5 mg/L, NO₃⁻ present) from **anaerobic** (DO ≈ 0, NO₃⁻ absent). ### Numericals to Memorize - [ ] **2.86 mg O₂ / mg NO₃⁻-N** — oxygen credit factor. - [ ] **2.47 mg CH₃OH / mg NO₃⁻-N** — theoretical methanol dose. - [ ] **3.57 mg CaCO₃ / mg NO₃⁻-N** — alkalinity recovery. - [ ] **0.45–0.50 mg VSS / mg COD** — biomass yield of denitrifiers. - [ ] **BOD₅ : NO₃⁻-N ≥ 4:1** — minimum C/N ratio for complete denitrification. ### Operating Conditions - [ ] DO < 0.5 mg/L (inhibits *nosZ* above this). - [ ] pH 7.0 – 8.5 (acidic pH → N₂O leakage). - [ ] Temperature 20 – 30 °C (θ ≈ 1.08). - [ ] HRT in anoxic tank: 0.5 – 4 hours. - [ ] SRT (sludge age): 10 – 25 days at 20 °C. ### Reactor Types - [ ] **Pre-anoxic (MLE):** influent BOD as C source; lower energy. - [ ] **Post-anoxic (Bardenpho):** endogenous or external C; better effluent. - [ ] **Tertiary anoxic filter (BAF):** polishing for very low TN. ### Common Pitfalls - [ ] Don't use 0.5 mg/L as anaerobic DO threshold — anoxic, not anaerobic. - [ ] Don't forget the alkalinity recovery reduces acid demand in post-treatment. - [ ] Don't apply 2.86 factor to NO₂⁻-N and NO₃⁻-N separately without proper mass balance. - [ ] Don't ignore the **1.2 safety factor** on methanol dose. ### KTU 2024 Favourite Topics - [ ] Methanol stoichiometry (3.43 vs 2.47 — board expects the 2.47 number). - [ ] N₂O greenhouse gas concerns. - [ ] Pre-anoxic vs post-anoxic selection logic. - [ ] The 2.86 oxygen credit applied to aeration tank design. - [ ] Complete MLE design numerical with HRT, volume, recycle ratios. > [!NOTE] > **One-line definition to write in exams:** > *"Denitrification is the microbiological anoxic reduction of nitrate to nitrogen gas by facultative heterotrophs using nitrate as a terminal electron acceptor, recovering 3.57 mg CaCO₃ of alkalinity and saving 2.86 mg O₂ per mg NO₃⁻-N removed."* ---

topics.title: Simultaneous Nitrification and Denitrification. Phosphorus Removal: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simultaneous Nitrification and Denitrification. Phosphorus Removal: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Simultaneous Nitrification and Denitrification (SND)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Simultaneous Nitrification and Denitrification (SND)** is a biological wastewater treatment process in which both the oxidation of ammonia-nitrogen to nitrite/nitrate (nitrification) and the reduction of nitrite/nitrate to nitrogen gas (denitrification) occur concurrently within a single reactor under identical operational conditions, typically in a single aerobic sludge floc or biofilm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SND is a deviation from the classical two-stage (anoxic + aerobic) nitrification-denitrification system. It exploits **dissolved oxygen (DO) gradients** within microbial flocs, where the outer aerobic zone supports autotrophic nitrifiers, while the inner anoxic core supports heterotrophic denitrifiers.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a single sponge floating in a fish tank:
- The **outer surface** of the sponge is exposed to oxygen-rich water → this is where nitrifying bacteria (which need oxygen) convert ammonia to nitrate.
- The **inner core** of the sponge has very little oxygen (anoxic) → this is where denitrifying bacteria (which need absence of oxygen) convert nitrate to nitrogen gas.
- Both reactions happen in the **same sponge (reactor), at the same time**, just in different microscopic zones!

This eliminates the need for two separate tanks, saving **capital cost, footprint, and energy** required for internal recycling.

### Key Operational Metric
> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Indicator:** A successful SND system is characterized by a DO concentration between **0.5 – 1.5 mg/L** in the bulk liquid, which is critical for maintaining the co-existence of both microbial populations.

---

## 1.2 Phosphorus Removal – Introduction

### Formal Academic Definition
**Phosphorus removal** in wastewater treatment refers to the engineered elimination of phosphorus compounds (orthophosphate, polyphosphate, and organic phosphorus) from municipal and industrial effluents to prevent **eutrophication** of receiving water bodies. The discharge standard is typically **< 1.0 mg/L** as P (KTU/CPHEEO standard).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of phosphorus as "plant food" that causes excessive algae growth in lakes and rivers (eutrophication). Removing phosphorus is like putting the lake on a diet. There are two ways:
1. **Chemical dieting (Chemical Precipitation):** Add alum/lime to "bind" phosphorus into a solid and settle it out.
2. **Biological dieting (Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal – EBPR):** Train certain bacteria ("Phosphate Accumulating Organisms" or PAOs) to "eat" extra phosphorus and store it inside their cells, then remove them as waste sludge.

### Standard Discharge Metric
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPHEEO / KTU Benchmark:** Effluent Total Phosphorus limit is **1.0 mg/L** for discharge into surface waters and **2.0 mg/L** for discharge into sewers (as per IS 3025 and KTU 2024 Scheme references).

### Two Principal Pathways
- **Chemical Precipitation:** Using **Alum [Al₂(SO₄)₃·14H₂O]**, **Ferric Chloride [FeCl₃]**, or **Lime [Ca(OH)₂]**.
- **Biological Removal (EBPR / Bio-P):** Using **Polyphosphate Accumulating Organisms (PAOs)** such as *Accumulibacter phosphatis* and *Tetrasphaera*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive: Simultaneous Nitrification and Denitrification (SND)

### 2.1.1 Underlying Mechanisms
Three primary theories explain the occurrence of SND in a single aerated tank:

1. **Floc Theory (Most accepted for activated sludge):**
   - Activated sludge forms **flocs** of diameter typically **100 – 500 μm**.
   - **Outer aerobic layer (DO > 2 mg/L):** Nitrifiers (Nitrosomonas, Nitrobacter) oxidize NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻.
   - **Inner anoxic core (DO ≈ 0):** Denitrifiers use the NO₃⁻ produced externally and internally available organic carbon to reduce NO₃⁻ → N₂.

2. **Microenvironment/Gradient Theory:**
   - Steep **DO diffusion gradients** at the floc-water interface create localized anoxic zones.
   - The **Penetration Depth (δ)** of oxygen is given by Fick's Law.

3. **Reactor Macro-gradient Theory:**
   - Imperfect mixing in the aeration tank creates alternating aerobic/anoxic micro-regions.

### 2.1.2 Factors Affecting SND Performance
- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO):** Optimal range **0.5 – 1.5 mg/L**; too high suppresses anoxic zones, too low inhibits nitrification.
- **Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio (C/N):** Optimal **6:1 to 10:1**; provides organic carbon for denitrifiers.
- **Floc Size & Density:** Larger flocs favor anoxic core formation.
- **pH:** Best between **7.0 – 8.5**.
- **Temperature:** Mesophilic range **20 – 30 °C** is ideal.
- **F/M Ratio:** Typical value **0.05 – 0.15 kg BOD/kg MLSS·d**.

### 2.1.3 Advantages of SND
- **~30% reduction in aeration energy** (no need for complete nitrification).
- Eliminates internal mixed liquor recycle (MLR) pumps.
- Lower alkalinity consumption (~50% less lime needed).
- Reduced sludge production.
- Compact reactor footprint.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive: Phosphorus Removal Methods

### 2.2.1 Chemical Precipitation
Phosphorus reacts with metal salts to form insoluble precipitates.

**General Reaction:**
$$\text{PO}_4^{3-} + \text{Metal Salt} \rightarrow \text{Metal Phosphate (Insoluble)} \downarrow$$

**Specific Reactions:**

With Alum:
$$\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{PO}_4^{3-} \rightarrow 2\text{AlPO}_4 \downarrow + 3\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 14\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

With Ferric Chloride:
$$\text{FeCl}_3 + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \rightarrow \text{FePO}_4 \downarrow + 3\text{Cl}^-$$

With Lime:
$$5\text{Ca}(\text{OH})_2 + 3\text{PO}_4^{3-} \rightarrow \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{OH} \downarrow + 9\text{OH}^-$$

**Stoichiometric Molar Ratios:**
- Al:P = **1:1**
- Fe:P = **1:1**
- Ca:P = **1.5:1** (to **2.5:1** in practice)

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Dose:** Actual doses are 1.5 to 2.5 times stoichiometric due to side reactions with alkalinity.

### 2.2.2 Biological Phosphorus Removal (Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal – EBPR)
EBPR exploits a group of bacteria called **Phosphate Accumulating Organisms (PAOs)**. The process relies on alternating **anaerobic** and **aerobic** conditions.

**Step 1 – Anaerobic Phase:**
- PAOs take up **Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs)** like acetate.
- Energy is obtained by hydrolyzing stored polyphosphate (Poly-P) → releases **orthophosphate (PO₄³⁻)** into the bulk liquid.
- VFAs are stored as **Polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs)**.

**Step 2 – Aerobic Phase:**
- PAOs oxidize stored **PHAs** to generate energy.
- This energy is used to **luxury uptake** of PO₄³⁻ from the bulk liquid and re-store as Poly-P (up to 6–8% of cell dry weight, vs. 1–2% in normal cells).
- Excess phosphorus is removed with **waste activated sludge (WAS)**.

### 2.2.3 Process Configurations for EBPR
- **A²/O (Anaerobic/Anoxic/Oxic)**
- **Modified Bardenpho (5-stage)**
- **University of Cape Town (UCT) Process**
- **Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR)** with EBPR cycle.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units | Remarks / Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ demand for nitrification | $\text{O}_2 = 4.57 \times \text{NH}_3\text{-N oxidized}$ | kg O₂/kg N | 4.57 kg O₂ per kg NH₄⁺-N oxidized |
| 2 | Oxygen credit from denitrification | $\text{O}_2 = 2.86 \times \text{NO}_3\text{-N denitrified}$ | kg O₂/kg N | Recovers 2.86 kg O₂/kg NO₃⁻-N |
| 3 | SND Oxygen Savings | $\Delta\text{O}_2 = 2.86 \times Q \times (N_{\text{in}} - N_{\text{out}})$ | kg O₂/d | Compared to conventional Nitrification alone |
| 4 | Effluent Alkalinity required | $\text{Alk} \geq 7.14 \times \text{NH}_3\text{-N removed}$ | mg/L as CaCO₃ | For complete nitrification |
| 5 | Chemical P Dose (Alum) | $\text{Alum} = 9.6 \times P_{\text{removed}}$ | mg/L | Empirical factor at pH 6.5–7.5 |
| 6 | Chemical P Dose (FeCl₃) | $\text{FeCl}_3 = 5.2 \times P_{\text{removed}}$ | mg/L | Empirical factor |
| 7 | Molar Al:P Ratio | $\vert n_{Al} / n_P \vert = 1:1$ | mol/mol | Stoichiometric |
| 8 | EBPR P Removal per kg VSS wasted | $P_{\text{removed}} = 0.03$ to $0.06$ kg P/kg VSS | kg/kg | For sludge design |
| 9 | F/M Ratio | $F/M = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V \times X}$ | d⁻¹ | kg BOD/kg MLSS·d |
| 10 | SRT (Solids Retention Time) | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w}$ | days | Critical for SND ≥ 15–20 d |
| 11 | Oxygen penetration depth (Fick) | $\delta = \sqrt{2 D_s C_s / k_0}$ | m | Used in floc diffusion model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These formulas are directly used in **design of aeration tanks**, **recirculation pumps**, and **chemical dosing systems** in municipal STPs such as the ones operated by Kerala Water Authority (KWA).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Oxygen Balance in an SND Reactor

The fundamental advantage of SND lies in the **oxygen credit** obtained when nitrate generated in the aerobic zone is denitrified in the anoxic core, eliminating the need for further oxidation.

### Step 1: Define Influent and Effluent Nitrogen States
- Influent NH₄⁺-N concentration: $N_0$ (mg/L)
- Effluent NH₄⁺-N concentration: $N_{e,\text{NH}}$ (mg/L)
- Effluent NO₃⁻-N concentration: $N_{e,\text{NO}_3}$ (mg/L)

### Step 2: Mass of Ammonia Nitrified
Ammonia nitrified = Ammonia removed from influent minus ammonia leaving the system.

$$
N_{\text{nitrified}} = Q(N_0 - N_{e,\text{NH}})
$$

where $Q$ is the wastewater flow rate in m³/d.

### Step 3: Stoichiometric Oxygen Required for Nitrification
The standard stoichiometric oxygen requirement for full nitrification of ammonia to nitrate is **4.57 kg O₂ per kg NH₄⁺-N oxidized**.

$$
O_2^{\text{nitrification}} = 4.57 \times Q(N_0 - N_{e,\text{NH}})
$$

### Step 4: Mass of Nitrate Denitrified
Nitrate denitrified = Nitrate produced by nitrification minus nitrate leaving the effluent (assuming negligible NO₂⁻ accumulation).

$$
N_{\text{denitrified}} = Q(N_0 - N_{e,\text{NH}} - N_{e,\text{NO}_3})
$$

### Step 5: Oxygen Recovered by Denitrification
Each kg of NO₃⁻-N reduced to N₂ recovers **2.86 kg O₂** equivalent.

$$
O_2^{\text{recovered}} = 2.86 \times Q(N_0 - N_{e,\text{NH}} - N_{e,\text{NO}_3})
$$

### Step 6: Net Oxygen Requirement in SND
Subtract the oxygen credit from the gross requirement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2^{\text{net, SND}} &= O_2^{\text{nitrification}} - O_2^{\text{recovered}} \\
&= 4.57 Q(N_0 - N_{e,\text{NH}}) - 2.86 Q(N_0 - N_{e,\text{NH}} - N_{e,\text{NO}_3}) \\
&= Q[4.57(N_0 - N_{e,\text{NH}}) - 2.86(N_0 - N_{e,\text{NH}}) + 2.86 N_{e,\text{NO}_3}] \\
&= Q[(1.71)(N_0 - N_{e,\text{NH}}) + 2.86 N_{e,\text{NO}_3}]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Comparison with Conventional Nitrification Only
For a system with only nitrification (no denitrification), the oxygen demand is:

$$
O_2^{\text{conventional}} = 4.57 Q(N_0 - N_{e,\text{NH}})
$$

### Step 8: Net Aeration Energy Saved

$$
\text{\% Savings} = \dfrac{O_2^{\text{conventional}} - O_2^{\text{net, SND}}}{O_2^{\text{conventional}}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the algebraic difference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{\% Savings} &= \dfrac{2.86 Q(N_0 - N_{e,\text{NH}} - N_{e,\text{NO}_3})}{4.57 Q(N_0 - N_{e,\text{NH}})} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{2.86}{4.57} \times \dfrac{N_{\text{denitrified}}}{N_{\text{nitrified}}} \times 100 \\
&\approx 62.6 \times \dfrac{N_{\text{denitrified}}}{N_{\text{nitrified}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If 80% of the nitrified ammonia is denitrified within the SND reactor, the aeration energy savings approach **50%** — a major KTU design problem insight.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Chemical Phosphorus Dose Calculation

The chemical dose required to precipitate phosphorus is determined by **stoichiometry adjusted by an empirical efficiency factor**.

### Step 1: Required Phosphorus Removal
$$
P_{\text{remove}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{out}}
$$

where $P_{\text{in}}$ and $P_{\text{out}}$ are influent and effluent total phosphorus (mg/L).

### Step 2: Stoichiometric Metal Moles Needed
For alum, the reaction is 1:1 molar Al:P. Moles of Al needed per mole of P:

$$
n_{\text{Al}} = n_P = \dfrac{P_{\text{remove}} \times Q}{M_P \times 10^6}
$$

where $M_P = 31$ g/mol (atomic mass of P) and $Q$ is flow in L/d (or convert units appropriately).

### Step 3: Convert Moles of Al to Mass of Alum
Molar mass of commercial alum (Al₂(SO₄)₃·14H₂O) is **594 g/mol**, containing **2 atoms of Al (54 g Al per mole of alum)**.

$$
m_{\text{alum, stoich}} = n_{\text{Al}} \times \dfrac{M_{\text{alum}}}{2}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Efficiency Factor (α)
Real-world practice requires **α = 1.5 to 2.5** times stoichiometric dose to account for:
- Side reactions with bicarbonate alkalinity
- Incomplete mixing
- Hydroxide complex formation

$$
m_{\text{alum, actual}} = \alpha \times m_{\text{alum, stoich}}
$$

### Step 5: Concentration of Alum Dose

$$
D_{\text{alum}} = \alpha \times 9.6 \times P_{\text{remove}}
$$

where the factor **9.6** combines molar mass ratios and unit conversions (mg/L of alum per mg/L of P removed at α = 1.0).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A wastewater treatment plant receives $Q = 10{,}000$ m³/d with $N_0 = 40$ mg/L NH₄⁺-N. Effluent contains 2 mg/L NH₄⁺-N and 5 mg/L NO₃⁻-N. Calculate:
(i) Nitrified load (kg/d)
(ii) Denitrified load (kg/d)
(iii) Net oxygen required (kg O₂/d) under SND
(iv) % aeration energy saved vs. nitrification only

### Solution:

**(i) Nitrified Load:**

$$
N_{\text{nitrified}} = 10{,}000 \times (40 - 2) = 3.8 \times 10^5 \text{ g/d} = 380 \text{ kg/d}
$$

**(ii) Denitrified Load:**

$$
N_{\text{denitrified}} = 10{,}000 \times (40 - 2 - 5) = 3.3 \times 10^5 \text{ g/d} = 330 \text{ kg/d}
$$

**(iii) Net O₂ Required (SND):**

$$
O_2^{\text{net, SND}} = 4.57 \times 380 - 2.86 \times 330 = 1736.6 - 943.8 = 792.8 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}
$$

**(iv) Aeration Savings:**

$$
O_2^{\text{conventional}} = 4.57 \times 380 = 1736.6 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}
$$

$$
\text{\% Savings} = \dfrac{1736.6 - 792.8}{1736.6} \times 100 = 54.4\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exam:** Always quote the 2.86 factor for denitrification oxygen credit and 4.57 for nitrification demand. These are **frequently asked in Part A (3 marks)** questions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: SND vs. Conventional Nitrification-Denitrification

| Parameter | Conventional Two-Stage | SND (Single Stage) | Phosphorus Removal (EBPR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reactor Count | 2 (Anoxic + Aerobic) | 1 | 2-3 (Anaerobic + Aerobic + Settler) |
| Internal Recycle | Required (MLR ~200–400%) | Not Required | Required (RAS ~50–100%) |
| Aeration Energy | High | ~30–50% lower | Moderate |
| Sludge Yield | Higher | Lower | Higher (PAO sludge) |
| Footprint | Large | Compact | Moderate |
| Capital Cost | High | Low | Moderate |
| Key Control | DO, MLR ratio | DO (0.5–1.5 mg/L) | Anaerobic contact time, VFAs |
| KTU Exam Frequency | Very High | High | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SND Mechanism within an Activated Sludge Floc

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Influent["Influent NH4+ N and BOD"] --> Reactor["Aerobic Tank DO 0.5 to 1.5 mg per L"]
    Reactor --> FlocA["Sludge Floc 100 to 500 micrometer"]
    FlocA --> OuterZone["Outer Aerobic Zone DO greater than 2 mg per L"]
    FlocA --> InnerZone["Inner Anoxic Core DO approx 0 mg per L"]
    OuterZone --> Nitrosomonas["Nitrosomonas Oxidizes NH4 to NO2"]
    OuterZone --> Nitrobacter["Nitrobacter Oxidizes NO2 to NO3"]
    Nitrosomonas --> NitritePool["NO2 Pool"]
    Nitrobacter --> NitratePool["NO3 Pool"]
    NitratePool --> Diffuse["NO3 Diffuses into Anoxic Core"]
    Diffuse --> InnerZone
    InnerZone --> Denitrifiers["Denitrifiers Use NO3 as Electron Acceptor"]
    Denitrifiers --> N2Gas["N2 Gas Escapes to Atmosphere"]
    Denitrifiers --> Energy["Energy Yielded to Denitrifiers"]
    N2Gas --> Effluent["Treated Effluent Low NH4 and Low NO3"]
    Energy --> Effluent
    Influent --> Reactor
    style Reactor fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style FlocA fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14
    style OuterZone fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style InnerZone fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style N2Gas fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d
```

## 4.2 EBPR (Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal) Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Inf["Raw Wastewater with P and VFAs"] --> Anaerobic["Anaerobic Tank\nNo DO, No NO3\nHRT 1 to 2 h"]
    Anaerobic --> VFAUptake["PAOs Take Up VFAs\nStore as PHA\nRelease PO4 to Liquid"]
    VFAUptake --> Aerobic["Aerobic Tank\nDO 2 to 3 mg per L\nHRT 2 to 4 h"]
    Aerobic --> LuxuryUptake["PAOs Oxidize PHA\nLuxury Uptake of PO4\nStore as Poly P inside cells"]
    LuxuryUptake --> Settler["Secondary Clarifier\nSolid Liquid Separation"]
    Settler --> Eff["Treated Effluent P less than 1 mg per L"]
    Settler --> RAS["Return Activated Sludge RAS"]
    Settler --> WAS["Waste Activated Sludge WAS\nRich in Phosphorus"]
    RAS --> Anaerobic
    WAS --> SludgeTreatment["Sludge Treatment and Disposal"]
    style Anaerobic fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14
    style Aerobic fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style Settler fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style WAS fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style Eff fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
```

```mermaid

        A3 --> MLR
        A3 --> EffC[Effluent]
    end
    subgraph SND["Simultaneous Nitrification Denitrification"]

    Inf --> B1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define Simultaneous Nitrification and Denitrification (SND). List any two factors that affect its performance.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** SND is a biological wastewater treatment process in which nitrification (oxidation of ammonia to nitrate by autotrophs) and denitrification (reduction of nitrate to nitrogen gas by heterotrophs) occur concurrently within the **same reactor** under identical operating conditions, exploiting dissolved oxygen gradients within microbial flocs or biofilms.
>
> **Two factors affecting SND:**
> 1. **Dissolved Oxygen (DO) Concentration:** Optimal range is 0.5–1.5 mg/L in bulk liquid; too high suppresses anoxic zones inside flocs, too low inhibits nitrifiers.
> 2. **Carbon-to-Nitrogen (C/N) Ratio:** A C/N of 6:1 to 10:1 ensures sufficient organic carbon is available as electron donor for denitrifiers in the anoxic core.

**Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for definition, ½ mark each for two factors with brief justification = 3 marks.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**Mention the typical effluent phosphorus discharge limit and name two chemical precipitants used for phosphorus removal.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Effluent P Limit:** **1.0 mg/L** as Total Phosphorus for discharge into inland surface waters, as per CPHEEO manual adopted by KTU/KSPCB.
>
> **Two chemical precipitants:**
> 1. **Alum [Al₂(SO₄)₃·14H₂O]** – forms aluminum phosphate (AlPO₄) precipitate.
> 2. **Ferric Chloride [FeCl₃]** – forms ferric phosphate (FePO₄) precipitate.
>
> *(Lime [Ca(OH)₂] is also acceptable.)*

**Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for limit, 1 mark each for two precipitants (with formula) = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) – SND Focused

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **mechanism of Simultaneous Nitrification and Denitrification** in a single aerobic activated sludge tank. Discuss the role of **DO gradients** within the sludge floc. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A municipal STP handles $Q = 25{,}000$ m³/d with influent NH₄⁺-N = **45 mg/L**. The SND system achieves effluent NH₄⁺-N = **3 mg/L** and effluent NO₃⁻-N = **6 mg/L**.
   Calculate:
   (i) Daily nitrified nitrogen load (kg/d)
   (ii) Daily denitrified nitrogen load (kg/d)
   (iii) Net oxygen requirement under SND (kg O₂/d)
   (iv) Percentage aeration energy saved compared to nitrification alone. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

1. **Definition of SND:** A single aerobic reactor with controlled low DO (**0.5 – 1.5 mg/L**) where nitrification and denitrification occur simultaneously. *(1 mark)*

2. **Floc Structure (Sketch):** Activated sludge floc of diameter **100 – 500 μm** is depicted with an outer aerobic shell and an inner anoxic core. *(2 marks)*

3. **Aerobic Outer Zone:**
   - Nitrifiers (*Nitrosomonas* → NH₄⁺ to NO₂⁻; *Nitrobacter* → NO₂⁻ to NO₃⁻) thrive here.
   - DO > 2 mg/L at floc surface, sufficient for autotrophic activity. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Anoxic Inner Core:**
   - DO ≈ 0 mg/L; heterotrophic denitrifiers reduce NO₃⁻ to N₂ using organic carbon (BOD) as electron donor. *(1.5 marks)*

5. **Role of DO Gradient:**
   - Fickian diffusion: oxygen concentration decreases from floc surface to center.
   - Critical floc size (~150 μm) maintains anoxic core; smaller flocs cannot sustain denitrification.
   - DO gradient is the **driving force** for the two co-existing microenvironments. *(1 mark)*

#### Model Solution (b):

**(i) Nitrified Load:**

$$
N_{\text{nitrified}} = Q \times (N_0 - N_{e,\text{NH}}) = 25{,}000 \times (45 - 3) \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
N_{\text{nitrified}} = 25{,}000 \times 42 \times 10^{-3} = 1050 \text{ kg N/d}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Denitrified Load:**

$$
N_{\text{denitrified}} = 25{,}000 \times (45 - 3 - 6) \times 10^{-3} = 25{,}000 \times 36 \times 10^{-3} = 900 \text{ kg N/d}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(iii) Net Oxygen Required (SND):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2^{\text{net, SND}} &= 4.57 \times N_{\text{nitrified}} - 2.86 \times N_{\text{denitrified}} \\
&= 4.57 \times 1050 - 2.86 \times 900 \\
&= 4798.5 - 2574 \\
&= 2224.5 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(iv) % Aeration Energy Saved:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2^{\text{conv}} &= 4.57 \times 1050 = 4798.5 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d} \\
\text{\% Savings} &= \dfrac{4798.5 - 2224.5}{4798.5} \times 100 = 53.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Phosphorus Removal Focused

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**a)** Explain **Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal (EBPR)** with a flow diagram. Discuss the roles of **PAOs**, **PHAs**, and **Polyphosphate** in the process. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A STP uses **alum dosing** for chemical phosphorus removal. The influent total phosphorus is **8 mg/L** and the desired effluent P is **1 mg/L**. Flow = **15,000 m³/d**.
   Calculate:
   (i) Phosphorus removed per day (kg/d)
   (ii) Stoichiometric alum dose (kg/d) [molar mass of alum = 594 g/mol, P = 31 g/mol, Al = 27 g/mol]
   (iii) Actual alum dose required (kg/d) at efficiency factor α = 1.8. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

1. **EBPR Definition:** A biological process that uses **Phosphate Accumulating Organisms (PAOs)** to remove phosphorus beyond normal metabolic needs by exposing them to alternating anaerobic and aerobic conditions. *(1 mark)*

2. **Flow Diagram** (refer Section 4.2): Influent → Anaerobic → Aerobic → Settler → Effluent. *(2 marks)*

3. **Anaerobic Phase:**
   - PAOs hydrolyze stored **Poly-P** to release PO₄³⁻ (energy source).
   - VFAs (acetate) are taken up and stored as **PHAs** inside cells. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Aerobic Phase:**
   - PAOs oxidize PHAs to generate energy.
   - **Luxury uptake** of PO₄³⁻ from bulk liquid — stored as Poly-P (up to 6–8% of cell dry weight).
   - Phosphorus removed from system via **waste activated sludge (WAS)**. *(1.5 marks)*

5. **Roles Summary:**
   - **PAOs:** Functional microbes (*Accumulibacter*, *Tetrasphaera*).
   - **PHAs:** Carbon/energy reserve.
   - **Poly-P:** Phosphorus storage polymer. *(1 mark)*

#### Model Solution (b):

**(i) Phosphorus Removed per Day:**

$$
P_{\text{remove}} = (8 - 1) \times 15{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 7 \times 15 = 105 \text{ kg P/d}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**(ii) Stoichiometric Alum Dose:**

**Step 1:** Moles of P removed per day:

$$
n_P = \dfrac{105 \times 10^3}{31} = 3387.1 \text{ mol/d}
$$

**Step 2:** Moles of Al required (Al:P = 1:1):

$$
n_{\text{Al}} = 3387.1 \text{ mol/d}
$$

**Step 3:** Moles of alum (each mole has 2 Al atoms):

$$
n_{\text{alum}} = \dfrac{3387.1}{2} = 1693.55 \text{ mol/d}
$$

**Step 4:** Mass of alum:

$$
m_{\text{alum, stoich}} = 1693.55 \times 594 \times 10^{-3} = 1006.0 \text{ kg/d}
$$

*(3.5 marks)*

**(iii) Actual Alum Dose at α = 1.8:**

$$
m_{\text{alum, actual}} = 1.8 \times 1006.0 = 1810.8 \text{ kg/d}
$$

*(2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always report loads in **kg/d**, not g/d (a frequent 1-mark deduction).
> 2. **Using only stoichiometric dose without α factor:** Examiners will deduct 2 marks if the **efficiency multiplier (α = 1.5–2.5)** is ignored.
> 3. **Confusing PAOs with nitrifiers:** PAOs are **heterotrophs**, not autotrophs. Nitrifiers (Nitrosomonas) are different organisms.
> 4. **Oxygen credit factor:** Always use **2.86** for denitrification credit and **4.57** for nitrification demand. Interchanging them leads to wrong answers.
> 5. **SND DO range:** Writing DO > 2 mg/L is **incorrect** for SND; the SND sweet spot is **0.5–1.5 mg/L**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **SND** = Nitrification + Denitrification in **one reactor** at **DO 0.5–1.5 mg/L**.
- ✅ **SND mechanism** = Floc theory → aerobic shell + anoxic core.
- ✅ **Nitrification O₂ demand** = **4.57 kg O₂/kg NH₄⁺-N**.
- ✅ **Denitrification O₂ credit** = **2.86 kg O₂/kg NO₃⁻-N**.
- ✅ **SND advantages** = ~30–50% aeration energy saving, no MLR pump, compact footprint.
- ✅ **Phosphorus effluent standard** = **1.0 mg/L** (CPHEEO / KTU 2024).
- ✅ **Chemical P removal precipitants** = Alum, FeCl₃, Lime (Ca(OH)₂).
- ✅ **Alum stoichiometric dose** ≈ **9.6 × ΔP (mg/L)** at α = 1.0.
- ✅ **Practical dose multiplier** α = **1.5 to 2.5** times stoichiometric.
- ✅ **EBPR** = Alternating **anaerobic + aerobic** conditions exploiting **PAOs**.
- ✅ **PAOs storage cycle:** VFAs → PHAs (anaerobic); PO₄³⁻ → Poly-P (aerobic).
- ✅ **Luxury P uptake** = 6–8% of cell dry weight (vs. 1–2% normal cells).
- ✅ **Effluent Alkalinity** requirement for nitrification = **7.14 × NH₄⁺-N removed** (mg/L as CaCO₃).
- ✅ **F/M ratio for SND** = 0.05–0.15 kg BOD/kg MLSS·d.
- ✅ **SRT for SND** = ≥ **15–20 days** to retain slow-growing nitrifiers.
- ✅ **EBPR key design parameter** = Anaerobic contact time ≥ 1–2 h with adequate VFA availability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phosphorus Removal by Chemical Precipitation: Principles of Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Phosphorus Removal by Chemical Precipitation: Principles of Process ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Chemical Precipitation for Phosphorus Removal** is a physico-chemical unit process in which soluble orthophosphate ions ($PO_4^{3-}$, $HPO_4^{2-}$, $H_2PO_4^{-}$) present in municipal and industrial wastewater are converted into insoluble metallic phosphate compounds through controlled addition of multivalent metal cations (typically $Al^{3+}$, $Fe^{3+}$, $Fe^{2+}$, or $Ca^{2+}$). These metal salts destabilize the dissolved phosphate equilibrium and drive the solution beyond the solubility product ($K_{sp}$) of the target precipitate, forcing the phosphorus out of the aqueous phase as a settleable/filterable sludge. According to KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 1: Characteristics of Sewage & Primary Treatment), chemical precipitation is classified as an **advanced (tertiary) treatment step** used to bring residual total phosphorus ($P_{total}$) concentrations down to **< 1 mg/L** — a regulatory threshold required to prevent eutrophication in receiving water bodies. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Chemical precipitation is examined under the broader Module 1 theme of *Removal of Specific Pollutants*. Students must master the stoichiometric reactions, the *molar ratio* concept (Me:P), and the effect of **pH** on precipitation efficiency. These three are the most heavily tested components in University ESE papers. ### 1.2 Forms of Phosphorus in Domestic Sewage Before precipitation chemistry is discussed, the species of phosphorus in raw sewage must be identified because precipitation only directly removes **orthophosphate**. The other forms must first be hydrolysed/oxidised. | Phosphorus Form | Chemical Formula | % in Raw Sewage (typical) | Treatability by Direct Precipitation | |---|---|---|---| | Orthophosphate | $PO_4^{3-}$, $HPO_4^{2-}$, $H_2PO_4^{-}$ | 50 – 70 | **Directly precipitable** | | Polyphosphate (condensed) | $P_2O_7^{4-}$, $P_3O_{10}^{5-}$ | 20 – 40 | Requires acid hydrolysis first | | Organically bound P | P bound to proteins, lipids, nucleic acids | 5 – 15 | Requires biological mineralisation | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **The "Magnet & Dust" Analogy:** Imagine phosphate ions in water as invisible "dust particles" floating around. Now throw in a chemical "magnet" (like alum or ferric chloride). The magnet grabs hold of the dust and forms a heavy, visible clump that sinks to the bottom — this heavy clump is the **chemical sludge** you scrape out. The cleaner water on top is your treated effluent. In real engineering terms, the "magnet" is a trivalent metal cation, the "dust" is orthophosphate, the "clump" is $AlPO_4 \downarrow$ or $FePO_4 \downarrow$, and the "sinking" is sedimentation/clarification in a secondary clarifier. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Design Metrics The following **bold** constants and benchmarks are universally applied in KTU numerical problems: - **Molar mass of P** = **31 g/mol**; of $PO_4^{3-}$ = **95 g/mol** - **Stoichiometric molar ratio Me:P** = **1:1** for Al and Fe precipitation (theoretical) - **Field-applied molar ratio** = **1.5 : 1 to 3 : 1** (excess metal is always dosed) - **Standard pH window for Al precipitation** = **5.5 – 7.0** - **Standard pH window for Fe precipitation** = **4.5 – 7.0** - **Standard pH window for Ca precipitation (HAP)** = **> 9.5** - **Target effluent TP** = **< 1 mg/L** (KTU / CPCB discharge norm) - **Ksp of $AlPO_4$** = **9.84 × 10⁻²¹** at 25 °C - **Ksp of $FePO_4$** = **1.3 × 10⁻²²** at 25 °C - **Ksp of $Ca_5(OH)(PO_4)_3$ (Hydroxyapatite)** = **~ 10⁻⁵⁸** (extremely low) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Governing Chemistry — Three Precipitation Systems The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to differentiate between **alum-based, iron-based, and lime-based** precipitation systems. Each has a unique chemistry, optimal pH, sludge chemistry, and operating cost profile. #### 2.1.1 Aluminium-Based Precipitation (Alum / Sodium Aluminate) Aluminium salts commonly used are **aluminium sulphate ($Al_2(SO_4)_3 \cdot 18H_2O$)** and **aluminium chloride ($AlCl_3$)**. The principal reaction producing aluminium phosphate is: $$Al^{3+} + PO_4^{3-} \rightarrow AlPO_4 \downarrow$$ However, in real wastewater (which contains natural alkalinity as $HCO_3^{-}$), a side-reaction consumes alkalinity: $$Al_2(SO_4)_3 \cdot 18H_2O + 3Ca(HCO_3)_2 \rightarrow 2Al(OH)_3 \downarrow + 3CaSO_4 + 6CO_2 + 18H_2O$$ This **alkalinity destruction** is critical because it directly lowers the wastewater pH. Designers must therefore dose sufficient metal salt (or pre-raise pH using lime/NaOH) to both (a) satisfy alkalinity demand and (b) achieve optimum precipitation pH of **5.5 – 6.5**. #### 2.1.2 Iron-Based Precipitation Two iron salts dominate: **ferric chloride ($FeCl_3$)** and **ferrous sulphate ($FeSO_4 \cdot 7H_2O$, copperas)**. Ferric chloride is the most efficient on a mass-dose basis. $$Fe^{3+} + PO_4^{3-} \rightarrow FePO_4 \downarrow$$ When ferrous iron is used, it must first be oxidised to ferric — typically by dissolved oxygen or by addition of chlorine ($Cl_2$): $$4Fe^{2+} + O_2 + 8OH^{-} + 2H_2O \rightarrow 4Fe(OH)_3 \downarrow$$ The $Fe(OH)_3$ floc then acts as a "sweep coagulant" that physically adsorbs and co-precipitates fine phosphate colloids. This **sweep coagulation** mechanism is why iron dosing often achieves lower residual P than aluminium at equivalent molar ratios. #### 2.1.3 Lime-Based Precipitation (Calcium Hydroxide) At **pH > 9.5**, calcium reacts with phosphate to form **Hydroxyapatite (HAP)**, an extremely insoluble mineral: $$5Ca^{2+} + 4OH^{-} + 3HPO_4^{2-} \rightarrow Ca_5(OH)(PO_4)_3 \downarrow + 3H_2O$$ Lime precipitation generates a much larger sludge mass (predominantly $CaCO_3$ from alkalinity neutralisation) and raises pH dramatically. It is rarely used for municipal plants but is common in industrial wastewater treatment where high-strength phosphate streams (e.g., fertiliser industry) are encountered. ### 2.2 Why Precipitation Occurs — The Solubility Product Argument A precipitate forms only when the **Ion Activity Product (IAP)** of the reacting species exceeds the **solubility product constant ($K_{sp}$)**. For $AlPO_4$: $$IAP = \{Al^{3+}\} \cdot \{PO_4^{3-}\} > K_{sp} = 9.84 \times 10^{-21}$$ Because $PO_4^{3-}$ is the strongest precipitating species but exists predominantly as $HPO_4^{2-}$ and $H_2PO_4^{-}$ at neutral pH, the actual equilibrium expression uses the **conditional solubility product** that accounts for pH-dependent speciation. This is why **pH control is the single most important operational lever** in chemical phosphorus removal. ### 2.3 Factors Governing Precipitation Efficiency (KF – Key Factors) For the KTU 14-mark question, examiners almost always ask students to "discuss the factors affecting chemical precipitation of phosphorus." Memorise the following eight: | # | Factor | Effect on Precipitation | Optimal Range | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **pH** | Controls $PO_4$ speciation and metal hydroxide formation | Al: 5.5–6.5; Fe: 4.5–6.0; Ca: > 9.5 | | 2 | **Me : P Molar Ratio** | Higher ratio → lower residual P but more sludge | 1.5 : 1 to 2 : 1 (Al/Fe) | | 3 | **Mixing Energy (G-value)** | Rapid mix disperses coagulant; slow mix builds floc | Rapid: G = 700–1000 s⁻¹; Slow: G = 20–75 s⁻¹ | | 4 | **Temperature** | Lower T → slower kinetics, higher viscosity | 15–25 °C optimum | | 5 | **Competing Anions** | $OH^{-}$, $CO_3^{2-}$, $S^{2-}$ precipitate metal away | Pre-soften if hard water | | 6 | **Alkalinity** | Consumes metal; reduces effective dose | Bicarbonate alkalinity < 200 mg/L preferred | | 7 | **Initial P Concentration** | Higher influent P requires higher absolute dose | — | | 8 | **Co-existing Suspended Solids** | Provide nuclei for floc growth | Higher SS can aid sweep coagulation | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All high-yield formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — memorise these.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $D_{metal} = \dfrac{Q \times (P_{in} - P_{out}) \times R \times MW_{metal}}{31 \times 10^6}$ | Metal salt dose required (mol-Me basis × MW) | kg/d | | 2 | $Sludge_{mass} = Q \times [0.5 \, P_{removed}] + [Me_{dose} \times 1.5]$ | Empirical sludge yield (Al/Fe system) | kg/d | | 3 | $\dfrac{Me}{P} = \dfrac{n_{Me}}{n_P}$ (molar) or $\dfrac{[\text{Me dose (mg/L)} / MW_{Me}]}{[\text{P dose (mg/L)} / 31]}$ | Molar ratio expression | dimensionless | | 4 | $K_{sp} = [M^{n+}][A^{m-}]$ | Solubility product | $(mol/L)^{n+m}$ | | 5 | $Alkalinity_{consumed} = \dfrac{50}{27} \times Al_{dose} \text{ (as CaCO}_3)$ | Alkalinity destruction by alum | mg/L as $CaCO_3$ | | 6 | $Alkalinity_{consumed} = \dfrac{50}{28} \times Fe_{dose}$ | Alkalinity destruction by Fe | mg/L as $CaCO_3$ | | 7 | $Ca(OH)_2 \text{ dose} = 1.5 \times \dfrac{74}{31} \times P_{removed}$ | Stoichiometric lime dose | mg/L | | 8 | $\eta_P = \dfrac{P_{in} - P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Removal efficiency | % | **Note on engineering utility:** Formula 1 is routinely used to size chemical storage tanks and dosing pumps; Formula 2 is used to design sludge handling facilities (thickener, digester, dewatering); Formula 8 appears in almost every KTU numerical problem. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Chemical precipitation is used in **over 70 % of European wastewater treatment plants** that require enhanced biological phosphorus removal (EBPR) backup, and it is the **only practical method** for removing phosphorus from industrial effluents (fertiliser, dairy, semiconductor industries). It is also deployed as a **polishing step** downstream of biological P-removal to meet stringent **< 0.5 mg/L TP** discharge limits imposed by EU Urban Wastewater Treatment Directive (91/271/EEC) and India's CPCB norms for Sensitive Zones. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Stoichiometric Metal Dose for Target Effluent P **Problem Setup (typical KTU numerical):** A wastewater treatment plant receives a flow of **$Q = 10,000 \, m^3/d$** with influent total phosphorus **$P_{in} = 8 \, mg/L$**. Design a ferric chloride dosing system to achieve **$P_{out} = 1 \, mg/L$**, using a design **Me : P molar ratio of 1.5 : 1** (typical field value to account for side reactions and alkalinity demand). Compute (i) ferric chloride dose in mg/L and kg/d, (ii) alkalinity destruction, and (iii) sludge produced. #### Step 1: Mass of phosphorus to be removed per litre $$P_{removed} = P_{in} - P_{out} = 8 - 1 = 7 \, mg/L \text{ as P}$$ This is the orthophosphate-equivalent concentration that must be precipitated. #### Step 2: Convert P-removed to molar units $$\text{Molar concentration of P removed} = \dfrac{7 \, mg/L}{31 \, g/mol} = 0.2258 \times 10^{-3} \, mol/L = 2.258 \times 10^{-4} \, mol/L$$ #### Step 3: Apply the Me : P molar ratio (1.5 : 1) The mass of $Fe^{3+}$ required per litre: $$n_{Fe^{3+}} = 1.5 \times n_P = 1.5 \times 2.258 \times 10^{-4} = 3.387 \times 10^{-4} \, mol/L$$ #### Step 4: Convert molar Fe to mass Fe (atomic mass = 55.85 g/mol) $$[Fe^{3+}]_{mass} = 3.387 \times 10^{-4} \times 55.85 = 0.01892 \, g/L = 18.92 \, mg/L \text{ as Fe}$$ #### Step 5: Convert Fe mass to $FeCl_3$ mass (MW of $FeCl_3$ = 162.2 g/mol) Each mole of $FeCl_3$ yields 1 mole of $Fe^{3+}$. Therefore: $$[FeCl_3] = 18.92 \times \dfrac{162.2}{55.85} = 18.92 \times 2.905 = 54.96 \, mg/L$$ **Dose of commercial $FeCl_3$ = 54.96 mg/L** #### Step 6: Convert mg/L to kg/d $$\text{Chemical consumption} = 54.96 \, mg/L \times 10{,}000 \, m^3/d = 54.96 \times 10{,}000 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-6} \, kg/d$$ $$= 549.6 \, kg/d$$ #### Step 7: Compute Alkalinity Destruction Each mole of $FeCl_3$ consumes 3 moles of bicarbonate alkalinity (as $CaCO_3$): $$Alkalinity_{destroyed} = 54.96 \times \dfrac{50}{55.85} = 49.2 \, mg/L \text{ as CaCO}_3$$ If raw sewage alkalinity < 49.2 mg/L, **lime must be supplemented** to maintain buffer capacity. #### Step 8: Compute Sludge Production Using empirical relation (Formula 2 from cheat sheet): $$Sludge = Q \times [0.5 \times P_{removed} + 1.5 \times Me_{dose}]$$ $$Sludge = 10{,}000 \times [0.5 \times 7 + 1.5 \times 18.92] = 10{,}000 \times [3.5 + 28.38]$$ $$Sludge = 10{,}000 \times 31.88 = 318{,}800 \, g/d = 318.8 \, kg/d \text{ dry solids}$$ #### Step 9: Final Consolidated Result | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | $FeCl_3$ dose | **54.96 mg/L ≈ 55 mg/L** | | Daily consumption | **549.6 kg/d** | | Alkalinity consumed | **49.2 mg/L as $CaCO_3$** | | Sludge generated | **318.8 kg/d dry solids** | ### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Precipitant The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to **compare** the three precipitation systems. This matrix is a 14-mark ready answer. | Criterion | Aluminium ($Al^{3+}$) | Iron ($Fe^{3+}$) | Lime ($Ca^{2+}$) | |---|---|---|---| | Optimum pH | 5.5 – 6.5 | 4.5 – 6.0 | > 9.5 | | Stoichiometric molar ratio | 1 : 1 | 1 : 1 | 5 : 3 | | Field dose (Me : P) | 1.5 – 2.5 : 1 | 1.5 – 3 : 1 | 10 – 20 : 1 (excess Ca) | | Sludge mass (kg/kg P removed) | 4 – 6 | 3 – 5 | 8 – 15 (very high) | | Sludge dewaterability | Good | Moderate | Poor (calcium-rich) | | Capital cost | Moderate | Moderate | High (corrosion-resistant reactors) | | Operating cost (chemical) | High | Moderate | Low (lime cheap) | | Effluent achievable (TP) | 0.5 – 1.0 mg/L | 0.3 – 0.8 mg/L | 0.5 – 1.0 mg/L | | Impact on downstream UV disinfection | Negligible | Negligible | Severe (high pH) | | Common application | Municipal STP upgrade | Industrial + Municipal | Industrial (fertiliser, mining) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Chemical Precipitation in an Activated Sludge Plant The diagram below shows where chemical precipitation is dosed into a conventional activated-sludge system. **Two dosing points** are possible: **Pre-precipitation** (before primary clarifier) and **Co-precipitation** (into aeration tank or before secondary clarifier). The most common in KTU-referenced designs is **co-precipitation**. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Sewage
Influent TP 6 to 10 mg per L] --> B[Primary Clarifier] B --> B1[Primary Sludge
to Sludge Handling] B --> C[Aeration Tank
Activated Sludge Process] C --> D[Co Precipitation Dosing Point
FeCl3 or Alum Dose] D --> E[Secondary Clarifier] E --> E1[Waste Activated Sludge
with Chemical Sludge] E --> F[Effluent TP less than 1 mg per L] E --> E2[Recycle RAS to Aeration Tank] F --> G[Disinfection
and Discharge] classDef tank fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef point fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class B,C,E tank class D point class F,G,E1 out ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — pH Control Loop The diagram below illustrates how pH is monitored and controlled in an alum/iron precipitation reactor. This is a high-yield concept because the KTU 2024 scheme emphasises **automatic control and instrumentation**. ```mermaid flowchart TB IN[Influent Wastewater
Q in m3 per d] --> MIX1[Rapid Mix Tank
G 700 to 1000 per s
Retention 30 to 60 s] CHEM[Chemical Storage Tank
Alum or FeCl3 Solution] --> PUMP[Variable Speed Dosing Pump] --> MIX1 MIX1 --> MIX2[Slow Mix Flocculator
G 20 to 75 per s
Retention 20 to 30 min] MIX2 --> SED[Clarifier
Surface Overflow 20 to 30 m3 per m2 per d] SED --> SLUDGE[Chemical Sludge
to Thickener and Digester] SED --> PH[pH Sensor Probe
Online Monitoring] PH --> CTRL[PID Controller
Setpoint 6.0 to 6.5] CTRL --> PUMP SED --> EFF[Treated Effluent
TP less than 1 mg per L] classDef sensor fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef reactor fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class PH,CTRL sensor class MIX1,MIX2,SED reactor class EFF,SLUDGE out ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Solubility Diagram — pH vs Residual P ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph pH_Acidic["Acidic Zone pH less than 5"] R1[Al and Fe PO4 Soluble
Residual P High] end subgraph pH_Optimum["Optimum Zone pH 5.5 to 7.0"] R2[AlPO4 and FePO4 Precipitate
Residual P Minimum] end subgraph pH_Alkaline["Alkaline Zone pH greater than 8"] R3[Metal Hydroxides Dominate
Ca5 OH PO4 3 forms for Lime] end R1 --> R2 R2 --> R3 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** **Q1.** Define chemical precipitation as applied to phosphorus removal from wastewater. List **any four** factors affecting the efficiency of the process. (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** Chemical precipitation is a tertiary treatment process in which soluble orthophosphates in wastewater are converted into insoluble metallic phosphates by adding multivalent cations such as $Al^{3+}$, $Fe^{3+}$, or $Ca^{2+}$. The insoluble compounds are then removed via sedimentation or filtration, achieving effluent TP < 1 mg/L. **Four factors affecting efficiency:** 1. **pH of wastewater** — controls phosphate speciation and metal solubility (Al: 5.5–6.5; Fe: 4.5–6.0; Ca: > 9.5). 2. **Molar ratio of metal to phosphorus** — excess metal ensures complete precipitation but increases sludge. 3. **Mixing intensity** — rapid mix disperses coagulant; slow mix promotes floc growth. 4. **Alkalinity** — competes with phosphate for metal ions; high alkalinity demands higher dose. --- **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Q2.** Write the balanced chemical reaction for phosphorus removal using (a) alum and (b) lime. State the optimum pH for each. (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** **(a) Alum precipitation (optimum pH 5.5 – 6.5):** $$Al_2(SO_4)_3 \cdot 18H_2O + 2PO_4^{3-} \rightarrow 2AlPO_4 \downarrow + 3SO_4^{2-} + 18H_2O$$ **(b) Lime precipitation (optimum pH > 9.5):** $$10Ca(OH)_2 + 6HPO_4^{2-} \rightarrow 2Ca_5(OH)(PO_4)_3 \downarrow + 6OH^{-} + 6H_2O$$ --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]** ### **Question A: (14 Marks)** **(a)** With the help of balanced chemical equations, explain the **principles of phosphorus removal** from wastewater using **aluminium and iron salts**. Discuss the role of **pH** and **molar ratio** in determining the efficiency of precipitation. (7 Marks) **(b)** A municipal STP treats **$Q = 25,000 \, m^3/d$** of sewage with **$P_{in} = 10 \, mg/L$**. Design a **ferric chloride dosing system** to achieve **$P_{out} = 1 \, mg/L$** at a Me : P molar ratio of **2 : 1**. Compute (i) the daily mass of $FeCl_3$ required, (ii) the alkalinity destroyed (as $CaCO_3$), and (iii) the chemical sludge produced per day. (7 Marks) --- ### **Complete Model Solution for Question A:** #### Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks) **Step 1: Aluminium chemistry (2 marks)** The dominant reaction is: $$Al^{3+} + PO_4^{3-} \rightarrow AlPO_4 \downarrow \quad (K_{sp} = 9.84 \times 10^{-21})$$ At pH 5.5 – 6.5, $Al^{3+}$ is the dominant Al species and $H_2PO_4^{-}$ is the dominant P species. The proton-exchange equilibrium: $$Al^{3+} + H_2PO_4^{-} \rightarrow AlPO_4 \downarrow + 2H^{+}$$ releases $H^{+}$, lowering pH. **Alkalinity must therefore be sufficient** to buffer the reaction. **Step 2: Iron chemistry (2 marks)** For ferric chloride: $$Fe^{3+} + PO_4^{3-} \rightarrow FePO_4 \downarrow \quad (K_{sp} = 1.3 \times 10^{-22})$$ Plus a parallel **sweep coagulation** mechanism: $$Fe^{3+} + 3OH^{-} \rightarrow Fe(OH)_3 \downarrow$$ The $Fe(OH)_3$ floc enmeshes fine phosphate colloids, improving removal beyond pure stoichiometric precipitation. **Step 3: Role of pH (1.5 marks)** - At low pH, metals remain soluble and don't precipitate phosphate. - At high pH, metals form hydroxides rather than phosphates. - The "minimum solubility window" is narrow (Al: 5.5–6.5; Fe: 4.5–6.0). **Step 4: Role of molar ratio (1.5 marks)** - Theoretical stoichiometric ratio = 1:1. - Practical ratio = 1.5:1 to 3:1 because side reactions consume metal (alkalinity neutralisation, hydroxide formation). - Beyond optimum ratio, sludge volume rises sharply without proportional P removal. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks) **Step 1: Phosphorus removed** $$P_{removed} = 10 - 1 = 9 \, mg/L \text{ as P}$$ **Step 2: Moles of P removed per litre** $$n_P = \dfrac{9 \, mg/L}{31 \, g/mol} = 0.2903 \times 10^{-3} \, mol/L$$ **Step 3: Moles of $Fe^{3+}$ required (ratio 2 : 1)** $$n_{Fe^{3+}} = 2 \times 0.2903 \times 10^{-3} = 5.806 \times 10^{-4} \, mol/L$$ **Step 4: Mass of $Fe^{3+}$ per litre** $$[Fe^{3+}] = 5.806 \times 10^{-4} \times 55.85 = 0.03243 \, g/L = 32.43 \, mg/L$$ **Step 5: Convert to mass of $FeCl_3$ (MW = 162.2 g/mol)** $$[FeCl_3] = 32.43 \times \dfrac{162.2}{55.85} = 32.43 \times 2.905 = 94.21 \, mg/L$$ **Step 6: Daily consumption** $$Daily \, FeCl_3 = 94.21 \, mg/L \times 25{,}000 \, m^3/d \times 10^3 \, L/m^3 \times 10^{-6} \, kg/mg$$ $$= 94.21 \times 25 = 2355.3 \, kg/d$$ **Step 7: Alkalinity destroyed** $$Alkalinity_{destroyed} = 94.21 \times \dfrac{50}{55.85} = 84.34 \, mg/L \text{ as CaCO}_3$$ **Step 8: Sludge produced** $$Sludge = 25{,}000 \times [0.5 \times 9 + 1.5 \times 32.43]$$ $$= 25{,}000 \times [4.5 + 48.65] = 25{,}000 \times 53.15$$ $$= 1{,}328{,}750 \, g/d = 1328.75 \, kg/d \text{ dry solids}$$ **Final Answer Box:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | $FeCl_3$ concentration | **94.21 mg/L** | | Daily $FeCl_3$ requirement | **2355.3 kg/d** | | Alkalinity destroyed | **84.34 mg/L as $CaCO_3$** | | Sludge generated | **1328.75 kg/d dry solids** | --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice): (14 Marks)** **(a)** Compare **alum, ferric chloride, and lime** as phosphorus precipitants under the following heads: chemistry, optimum pH, sludge volume, and cost. (7 Marks) **(b)** Explain with a **process flow diagram** how chemical precipitation is integrated into an activated sludge plant. Discuss the merits of **pre-precipitation vs. co-precipitation** dosing points. (7 Marks) **Model Answer Outline:** **(a) Comparison Table** (already provided in §3.2 — reproduce the matrix). **(b) Process integration** (draw the Mermaid flow from §4.1 and label it): - Pre-precipitation: dosed before primary clarifier; reduces load on biology; produces more sludge; risk of low pH damaging downstream bio-process. - Co-precipitation: dosed into aeration tank or before secondary clarifier; **most common**; combines with bio-P removal; reduces $FeCl_3$ requirement; controls sludge bulking. - Post-precipitation: dosed after secondary clarifier with dedicated tertiary clarifier/filter; produces **clearest effluent** (< 0.5 mg/L TP); highest chemical cost. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Board Pattern):** > > 1. **Forgetting alkalinity destruction** — when computing alum/Fe doses, students often ignore that part of the chemical is consumed by $HCO_3^{-}$. Examiners deduct **up to 2 marks** for this omission. > 2. **Confusing pH windows** — Alum operates at **5.5–6.5**, NOT at neutral pH. Writing "optimum pH = 7" loses a full mark. > 3. **Using mass ratio instead of molar ratio** — the Me : P ratio is **always molar** in KTU problems. Using mass ratio is a fundamental error. > 4. **Ignoring polyphosphate hydrolysis** — chemical precipitation does NOT remove polyphosphates directly. Examiners expect students to mention that **acid hydrolysis or biological mineralisation upstream** is required. > 5. **Forgetting sludge handling implication** — chemical precipitation **increases sludge mass by 30–60 %**. Any design answer that omits sludge thickening/dewatering is incomplete. > 6. **Wrong MW values** — $FeCl_3$ = **162.2**, $Al_2(SO_4)_3 \cdot 18H_2O$ = **666.4**, $Ca(OH)_2$ = **74**. Wrong constants → wrong answers → **zero marks for that sub-part**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Chemical precipitation removes soluble orthophosphate by converting it to insoluble $AlPO_4$, $FePO_4$, or $Ca_5(OH)(PO_4)_3$ using multivalent metal salts. - **Three precipitants:** Alum, Ferric chloride, Lime — each with distinct optimum pH windows. - **Optimum pH:** Al = 5.5–6.5; Fe = 4.5–6.0; Lime = > 9.5. - **Molar ratio (Me:P):** Theoretical 1:1; field-applied 1.5–3:1. - **Key reactions:** $Al^{3+} + PO_4^{3-} \rightarrow AlPO_4 \downarrow$ and $Fe^{3+} + PO_4^{3-} \rightarrow FePO_4 \downarrow$. - **Lime reaction:** $5Ca^{2+} + 4OH^{-} + 3HPO_4^{2-} \rightarrow Ca_5(OH)(PO_4)_3 \downarrow + 3H_2O$. - **Ksp values:** $AlPO_4 = 9.84 \times 10^{-21}$; $FePO_4 = 1.3 \times 10^{-22}$; HAP = ~$10^{-58}$. - **Eight governing factors:** pH, molar ratio, mixing, temperature, competing anions, alkalinity, influent P, suspended solids. - **Target effluent:** TP < 1 mg/L (CPCB / KTU norm). - **Sludge increase:** 30–60 % over biological-only plant. - **Alkalinity destruction:** Must be replaced using lime if raw alkalinity is insufficient. - **Dosing points:** Pre-, co-, or post-precipitation; co-precipitation is industry default. - **Always remember:** chemical precipitation only removes **orthophosphate**; polyphosphates require pre-hydrolysis. - **Calculation tip:** Convert P from mg/L to mol/L using **MW = 31 g/mol**; metal conversion uses its atomic mass / molecular weight as appropriate.

topics.title: Chemicals Applied
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chemicals Applied

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemicals Applied in Wastewater Treatment** refers to the systematic addition of inorganic and organic chemical reagents at various stages of the treatment train — preliminary, primary, secondary, and tertiary — to enhance physical separation, accelerate biological reactions, neutralize toxic constituents, control pathogens, and condition the generated sludge for safe disposal.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic belongs to the **unit operations and unit processes** framework, where chemicals act as the *functional additives* that bridge the gap between raw wastewater characteristics and the stringent discharge norms prescribed by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** and the **Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB)**.

## 1.2 Classification of Chemicals Used

The chemicals are broadly classified into six functional families:

| S. No. | Functional Class | Primary Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coagulants | Destabilize colloidal suspensions |
| 2 | Flocculants | Aggregate destabilized particles into settleable flocs |
| 3 | Disinfectants | Inactivate pathogenic microorganisms |
| 4 | pH Adjusters | Maintain optimal pH for downstream processes |
| 5 | Nutrient Supplements | Balance C:N:P ratio for biological growth |
| 6 | Sludge Conditioners | Improve dewaterability of biosolids |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "Cleaning a Murky Pond":**
> Imagine a pond filled with floating dirt, suspended clay, and bacteria. To clean it: (1) You sprinkle **alum** (a coagulant) — like a magnet that neutralizes the electrostatic repulsion keeping clay particles apart. (2) You stir gently and add a **polymer** (a flocculant) — like a "sticky net" that pulls neutralized particles into large, heavy clumps that sink. (3) You finally add **chlorine** (a disinfectant) — like an "invisible sanitiser" that destroys the harmful germs. This is exactly the chemical logic applied in municipal sewage treatment plants across Kerala — from Kochi to Thiruvananthapuram.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

The dosage of every chemical is governed by two universally tracked parameters:

- **Jar Test Optimum Dose** — typically **5 – 150 mg/L** for coagulants, expressed in **parts per million (ppm)**.
- **Stoichiometric Requirement** — calculated from molar mass balance, expressed in **kg/day** as:

$$ \text{Chemical Dose (kg/day)} = Q \times C \times 10^{-3} $$

where **$Q$** is the flow rate in **m³/day** and **$C$** is the desired concentration in **mg/L**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Chemistry — The "Why"

The need for chemicals arises because **raw municipal wastewater contains four problematic fractions**:

1. **Suspended solids** (settleable + colloidal) — too small to settle by gravity alone.
2. **Dissolved organics** — require biological oxidation but need balanced nutrients.
3. **Pathogens** — must be inactivated before discharge.
4. **Extreme pH or toxicants** — inhibit biological treatment.

Each fraction demands a chemically targeted solution.

## 2.2 Coagulants — Mechanism of Action

> [!NOTE]
> **The Colloidal Stability Problem:** Colloidal particles (size **$0.001$ – $1\ \mu m$**) carry surface negative charges (typically **$-10$ to $-30$ mV** zeta potential), causing mutual electrostatic repulsion. Coagulants supply **cations** that compress the electrical double layer and neutralize this charge.

### 2.2.1 Common Coagulants

| Chemical | Formula | Molecular Weight (g/mol) | Typical Dose (mg/L) | pH Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Alum | $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | **594** | 5 – 150 | 5.5 – 7.5 |
| Ferric Chloride | $\text{FeCl}_3 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | **270.3** | 5 – 150 | 4.0 – 11.0 |
| Ferric Sulfate | $\text{Fe}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$ | **399.9** | 10 – 200 | 4.0 – 11.0 |
| Lime | $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ | **74.1** | 50 – 500 | 6.5 – 11.0 |
| Sodium Aluminate | $\text{NaAlO}_2$ | **82** | 10 – 100 | 6.0 – 8.5 |

### 2.2.2 The Coagulation Reaction (Alum Example)

When alum is added to alkaline water, it hydrolyzes:

$$ \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{Al}^{3+} + 3\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 14\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

The trivalent $\text{Al}^{3+}$ ion then forms an amorphous hydroxide floc:

$$ \text{Al}^{3+} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Al(OH)}_3 \downarrow + 3\text{H}^+ $$

The released **$H^+$** ions lower the pH, which is why alkalinity must be sufficient or **lime** must be co-added to buffer the system.

## 2.3 Flocculants — Bridging Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-molecular-weight polymers ($10^6$ – $10^7$ g/mol)** adsorb simultaneously onto multiple destabilized particles, forming physical "bridges" that create large, rapid-settling flocs. This is called **interparticle bridging** and is distinct from charge neutralization.

| Polymer Type | Charge | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Cationic | Positive | Sludge dewatering, secondary clarification |
| Anionic | Negative | High-turbidity raw water, primary clarification |
| Non-ionic | Neutral | Settling aid after metal coagulants |

## 2.4 Disinfectants — CT Concept

> [!NOTE]
> The efficacy of any disinfectant is governed by the **CT value** — the product of residual disinfectant concentration (**$C$**, in mg/L) and contact time (**$T$**, in minutes).

$$ \text{CT} = C \times T \quad \left[\text{mg/L} \cdot \text{min}\right] $$

| Disinfectant | CT for 99.9% (3-log) inactivation of *E. coli* | By-products |
|---|---|---|
| Chlorine ($\text{Cl}_2$) | 0.3 – 0.6 | THMs, HAAs |
| Chlorine Dioxide ($\text{ClO}_2$) | 0.4 – 0.8 | Chlorite ($\text{ClO}_2^-$) |
| Ozone ($\text{O}_3$) | 0.02 – 0.06 | Bromate ($\text{BrO}_3^-$) |
| UV (254 nm) | 30 – 60 mJ/cm² | None (photoreactivation possible) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Dose (kg/d)} = Q \times C \times 10^{-3}$ | Chemical feed rate calculation | kg/day |
| $\text{CT} = C \times T$ | Disinfection efficacy index | mg·min/L |
| $\text{Alkalinity need} = \text{Coagulant dose} \times 0.5$ | Rule of thumb for alum (as $\text{CaCO}_3$) | mg/L |
| $\text{Polymer dose} = 0.1\%\ \text{to}\ 2\%$ of dry solids | For sludge conditioning | mg/L |
| $\text{TOC : N : P} = 100 : 5 : 1$ | Optimal biological treatment ratio | dimensionless |
| $\text{Cl}_2\text{ residual} = 0.2$ – $2.0$ mg/L | Post-chlorination standard | mg/L |
| $\text{pH}_{\text{opt}} = 6.5$ – $7.5$ | For nitrification biology | dimensionless |
| $\text{Sludge cake solids} \geq 20\%$ | Target after chemical conditioning | % |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Chemical Dose from Stoichiometry (Alum for Phosphorus Removal)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A **50,000 m³/day** wastewater treatment plant needs to dose **alum** to precipitate phosphate as **$\text{AlPO}_4$**. The raw wastewater contains **10 mg/L** of phosphorus (as P). Calculate the stoichiometric alum dose required.

### Step 1: Write the Balanced Reaction

The reaction between alum and phosphate in water proceeds as:

$$ \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{PO}_4^{3-} \rightarrow 2\text{AlPO}_4 \downarrow + 3\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 14\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

### Step 2: Identify Molar Masses

- Alum ($\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O}$) = **594 g/mol**
- Phosphorus (P) = **31 g/mol**
- Aluminum (Al) = **27 g/mol**

### Step 3: Set Up the Stoichiometric Ratio

From the balanced equation, **1 mole of alum precipitates 2 moles of phosphate (as P)**.

$$ \text{Moles of P per day} = \frac{Q \times [P]}{M_P} $$

$$ = \frac{50{,}000 \times 10}{31 \times 1000} = \frac{500{,}000}{31{,}000} = 16.13\ \text{kmol/day} $$

### Step 4: Calculate Moles of Alum Required

$$ \text{Moles of alum} = \frac{16.13}{2} = 8.065\ \text{kmol/day} $$

### Step 5: Convert to Mass

$$ \text{Mass of alum} = 8.065 \times 594 = 4{,}790.6\ \text{kg/day} \approx 4{,}791\ \text{kg/day} $$

### Step 6: Add Safety Factor (1.2 × – 1.5 ×)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real-world KTU/board problems and field practice, a **stoichiometric excess of 1.2× to 1.5×** is applied to account for competing reactions (with alkalinity, natural organics, etc.).

$$ \text{Practical dose} = 4{,}791 \times 1.3 = 6{,}228.3\ \text{kg/day} $$

### Step 7: Express Final Answer

$$ \boxed{\text{Alum required} \approx 6.23\ \text{tonnes/day (at 1.3× stoichiometric factor)} } $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Chlorine Dose for Disinfection

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A 25,000 m³/day STP needs to achieve a **coliform count of $\leq 1000$ MPN/100 mL** in the effluent. The raw wastewater has **$10^7$ MPN/100 mL** and a chlorine demand of **3 mg/L**. The plant uses a chlorination contact tank with **30 minutes** detention. Find the chlorine dose and verify the CT.

### Step 1: Determine the Log Removal Required

$$ \text{Log Removal} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{10^7}{10^3}\right) = 4\ \text{log units} $$

### Step 2: Apply the Standard CT Relationship

For 4-log inactivation of coliforms at pH 7 and 20 °C, the required CT ≈ **4 mg·min/L**.

### Step 3: Solve for Required Residual $C$

$$ C = \frac{\text{CT}}{T} = \frac{4}{30} = 0.133\ \text{mg/L} $$

### Step 4: Add Chlorine Demand

$$ \text{Total dose} = \text{Demand} + \text{Residual} = 3 + 0.133 = 3.133\ \text{mg/L} $$

### Step 5: Convert to Daily Mass

$$ \text{Cl}_2\ \text{required} = 25{,}000 \times 3.133 \times 10^{-3} = 78.3\ \text{kg/day} $$

$$ \boxed{\text{Cl}_2\ \text{feed} = 3.13\ \text{mg/L} \equiv 78.3\ \text{kg/day} } $$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Chemical Selection Decision Tree

| Wastewater Characteristic | Recommended Chemical | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| High turbidity (> 100 NTU) | Alum + Anionic Polymer | Charge neutralization + bridging |
| High color (textile effluent) | Ferric Chloride + Lime | Strong coagulation at high pH |
| High phosphate (> 5 mg/L) | Alum or FeCl₃ | Forms insoluble metal phosphates |
| Low alkalinity (< 50 mg/L) | Lime co-dosing | Buffers pH drop from coagulant |
| High ammonia (> 20 mg/L) | breakpoint chlorination | $\text{Cl}_2 : \text{NH}_3\text{-N} = 10 : 1$ |
| Anaerobic digester foaming | Antifoam (silicone / polyglycol) | Surface tension disruption |
| Heavy metals (Cd, Pb, Cr) | Lime to pH 9 – 10 | Hydroxide precipitation |
| Sludge bulking | Chlorine (shock dose 5 – 10 mg/L) | Disinfects filamentous bacteria |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Chemical Dosing Points in a Conventional Activated Sludge Plant (ASP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Influent["Raw Sewage Influent"] --> Screen["Bar Screen"]
    Screen --> Grit["Grit Chamber"]
    Grit --> PST["Primary Sedimentation Tank"]
    PST --> PSTchem{"Coagulant Dosing?"}
    PSTchem -- "Yes" --> PSTdose["Alum + Lime\n(10 - 50 mg/L)"]
    PSTchem -- "No" --> Aeration
    PSTdose --> Aeration["Aeration Tank\n(Biological Oxidation)"]
    Aeration --> Aerchem{"Nutrient Dosing?"}
    Aerchem -- "If C:N:P imbalanced" --> Nutchem["Urea / DAP /\nPhosphoric Acid"]
    Aerchem -- "Balanced" --> Clarifier
    Nutchem --> Clarifier["Secondary Clarifier"]
    Clarifier --> Disinfect{"Disinfection Required?"}
    Disinfect -- "Yes" --> Chlor["Chlorine Contact Tank\n(Cl2 / NaOCl / O3)"]
    Disinfect -- "No" --> Effluent
    Chlor --> Effluent["Treated Effluent Out"]
    Clarifier --> WAS["Waste Activated Sludge (WAS)"]
    WAS --> Thickener["Sludge Thickener"]
    Thickener --> Condition{"Chemical\nConditioning?"}
    Condition -- "Yes" --> Polymerdose["Cationic Polymer\n(2 - 8 kg/ton DS)"]
    Condition -- "No" --> Dewater
    Polymerdose --> Dewater["Sludge Dewatering\n(Centrifuge / Belt Press)"]
    Dewater --> Cake["Sludge Cake Disposal"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Chemical Dosing System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Storage["Chemical Storage Tank\n(HDPE / FRP / MSRL)"] --> Pump1["Metering Pump\n(Dia / Peristaltic)"]
    Pump1 --> StaticMixer["Static In-line Mixer"]
    StaticMixer --> Reactor["Reactor / Contact Basin"]
    Reactor --> FlashMix["Flash Mixer\n(100 - 300 rpm, 30 - 60 s)"]
    FlashMix --> Flocculator["Slow Stirrer\n(20 - 50 rpm, 15 - 30 min)"]
    Flocculator --> Settler["Clarifier / Thickener"]
    Settler --> Out1["Treated Stream"]
    Settler --> Out2["Sludge to Processing"]
    Storage -- "Level sensor feedback" --> PLC["PLC / SCADA Controller"]
    Pump1 -- "Flow rate feedback" --> PLC
    PLC -- "PID control signal" --> Pump1
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Coagulation–Flocculation–Sedimentation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Raw Wastewater\n(Colloidal SS, Turbidity)"] --> B["Chemical Dosing\n(Alum / FeCl3)"]
    B --> C["Flash Mixing\n(Charge Neutralization)"]
    C --> D["Slow Mixing\n(Bridging Flocculation)"]
    D --> E["Sedimentation\n(Floc Settling)"]
    E --> F1["Clarified Water\n→ to Biological Stage"]
    E --> F2["Sludge Blanket\n→ to Sludge Handling"]
```
