# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**
**"List any three commonly used coagulants in wastewater treatment and state the typical dose range of alum."**

**Model Answer:**

The three commonly used coagulants are:

1. **Aluminium Sulphate (Alum)** — $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O}$
2. **Ferric Chloride** — $\text{FeCl}_3$
3. **Hydrated Lime** — $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$

> **Typical dose range of alum: 5 to 150 mg/L** (commonly 20 – 60 mg/L in municipal STPs), with an effective pH window of **5.5 – 7.5**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**
**"Define the CT concept in disinfection. Why is it preferred over absolute dose alone?"**

**Model Answer:**

The **CT value** is the product of residual disinfectant concentration **$C$** (mg/L) and the effective contact time **$T$** (minutes):

$$ \text{CT} = C \times T \quad [\text{mg·min/L}] $$

It is preferred over absolute dose alone because disinfection depends on **both** the availability of the chemical *and* the time it remains in contact with the target organisms. Two plants dosing the same quantity of chlorine but with different contact times can achieve vastly different microbial inactivation. CT thus provides a **design and operational benchmark** for compliance with the **CPCB effluent standards**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the mechanisms of coagulation and flocculation in wastewater treatment. Differentiate between the two processes with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 40,000 m³/day municipal STP is to be designed for chemical phosphate removal using alum. The raw wastewater contains 12 mg/L of phosphate-phosphorus ($\text{PO}_4^{3-}\text{-P}$). Calculate the **daily alum requirement** assuming a stoichiometric excess of 1.4×. Also compute the theoretical sludge (as dry $\text{AlPO}_4$) generated per day. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

> **Coagulation** is the process of adding chemicals (coagulants) to **destabilize colloidal particles** by neutralizing their surface charges. It occurs within **seconds to a minute** in a flash mixer (high G-value, **$G = 700$ – $1000\ \text{s}^{-1}$**).
>
> **Flocculation** is the gentle, slow mixing (**$G = 20$ – $75\ \text{s}^{-1}$**, **$T = 15$ – $30$ min**) that allows destabilized particles to collide and aggregate into large, settleable flocs, primarily via **interparticle bridging** using high-molecular-weight polymers.

| Parameter | Coagulation | Flocculation |
|---|---|---|
| Time | 10 – 60 s | 15 – 30 min |
| Velocity gradient $G$ | 700 – 1000 s⁻¹ | 20 – 75 s⁻¹ |
| Mechanism | Charge neutralization | Bridging / sweep |
| Equipment | Flash mixer | Paddle / baffled flocculator |
| Energy | High | Low |

The mechanism is illustrated in the following Mermaid diagram:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Colloidal Particle\n(Negative Charge)"] --> B["Coagulant Added\n(Cation)"]
    B --> C["Charge Neutralized\n(Zeta Potential ≈ 0)"]
    C --> D["Polymer Added\n(High MW Chain)"]
    D --> E["Bridging Aggregation"]
    E --> F["Large Settleable Floc"]
```

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Moles of $\text{PO}_4^{3-}\text{-P}$ per day**

$$ n_P = \frac{40{,}000 \times 12}{31 \times 1000} = \frac{480{,}000}{31{,}000} = 15.484\ \text{kmol/day} $$

**Step 2: Stoichiometric moles of alum required** (1 mol alum : 2 mol P)

$$ n_{\text{alum, stoich}} = \frac{15.484}{2} = 7.742\ \text{kmol/day} $$

**Step 3: Mass of alum (stoichiometric)**

$$ M_{\text{alum, stoich}} = 7.742 \times 594 = 4{,}598.7\ \text{kg/day} $$

**Step 4: Apply 1.4× excess factor**

$$ M_{\text{alum, design}} = 4{,}598.7 \times 1.4 = 6{,}438.2\ \text{kg/day} $$

$$ \boxed{M_{\text{alum, design}} \approx 6.44\ \text{tonnes/day} } $$

**Step 5: Sludge generation (dry $\text{AlPO}_4$ produced)**

Moles of $\text{AlPO}_4$ = moles of P (1:1 ratio) = **15.484 kmol/day**

Molar mass of $\text{AlPO}_4$ = $27 + 31 + (4 \times 16) = 122\ \text{g/mol}$

$$ M_{\text{AlPO}_4} = 15.484 \times 122 = 1{,}889.0\ \text{kg/day} $$

$$ \boxed{M_{\text{AlPO}_4\ \text{sludge}} \approx 1.89\ \text{tonnes/day (dry)} } $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1]** (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Describe the chemistry of chlorination as a disinfection process. What is **breakpoint chlorination**? How is the chlorine dose determined for a wastewater containing 25 mg/L of ammonia-nitrogen? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the relative advantages and limitations of **chlorine, ozone, and UV radiation** as disinfectants. What is the typical CT requirement for *E. coli* inactivation? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Chemistry of Chlorination:**

When chlorine gas is added to water, it forms hypochlorous acid (HOCl):

$$ \text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HOCl} + \text{HCl} $$

The HOCl then dissociates based on pH:

$$ \text{HOCl} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{OCl}^- $$

**HOCl** (the unionized form) is **80 – 100 times more effective** than $\text{OCl}^-$, so chlorination is most efficient at pH **< 7.5**.

**Breakpoint Chlorination:**

When ammonia is present, chlorine reacts in stages:

$$ \text{NH}_3 + \text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{Cl} \text{ (monochloramine)} $$

$$ \text{NH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{NHCl}_2 \text{ (dichloramine)} $$

$$ \text{NHCl}_2 + \text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{NCl}_3 \text{ (nitrogen trichloride)} $$

$$ 2\text{NH}_3 + 3\text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{N}_2 \uparrow + 6\text{HCl} $$

The **breakpoint** is the dose at which all chloramines are oxidized, and free residual chlorine begins to appear. Beyond this, additional chlorine remains as free available chlorine (HOCl / $\text{OCl}^-$).

**Chlorine Dose for 25 mg/L NH₃-N:**

The stoichiometric weight ratio is $\text{Cl}_2 : \text{NH}_3\text{-N} = 10 : 1$ (by mass).

$$ \text{Cl}_2\ \text{dose} = 25 \times 10 = 250\ \text{mg/L} \ (\text{stoichiometric}) $$

In practice, a **1.2× to 1.5× factor** is added for safety:

$$ \text{Practical dose} = 250 \times 1.3 = 325\ \text{mg/L} $$

#### Model Solution to (b):

| Parameter | Chlorine ($\text{Cl}_2$) | Ozone ($\text{O}_3$) | UV Radiation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Disinfectant strength | Strong | Very strong | Moderate (no residual) |
| CT for 3-log *E. coli* | 0.3 – 0.6 mg·min/L | 0.02 – 0.06 mg·min/L | 30 – 60 mJ/cm² |
| Residual effect | Yes (long-lasting) | No (short-lived) | None |
| By-products | THMs, HAAs | Bromate | None |
| Cost | Low | High (energy-intensive) | Moderate |
| Microbial repair | No | No | Yes (photoreactivation) |
| Operational complexity | Simple | Complex (on-site generation) | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical CT for 3-log (99.9%) *E. coli* inactivation:**
> - **Chlorine:** **0.3 – 0.6 mg·min/L**
> - **Ozone:** **0.02 – 0.06 mg·min/L**
> - **UV (254 nm):** **30 – 60 mJ/cm²** (fluence-based, not CT)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include the stoichiometric excess factor (1.2× to 1.5×)** while calculating practical chemical doses. Examiners award the 1 full mark specifically for this correction.
> 2. **Confusing $\text{Cl}_2 : \text{NH}_3\text{-N}$ ratio as 7.6 : 1 (molar basis) instead of 10 : 1 (mass basis).** For mass-based dosing problems (mg/L or kg/day), always use **10 : 1**.
> 3. **Writing "alum" without its hydrate form.** Always mention $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O}$ with **MW = 594 g/mol**.
> 4. **Skipping units in final answers.** Even if the calculation is correct, missing units (mg/L, kg/day, tonnes/day) leads to **½ to 1 mark deduction**.
> 5. **In CT problems, forgetting to convert contact time into the same unit as expected by the CT table** (usually minutes).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Coagulants** neutralize colloidal charge; **flocculants** bridge particles — they are sequential, not interchangeable.
- **Alum** dose: **5 – 150 mg/L**; optimum pH **5.5 – 7.5**; releases **H⁺** ions (lowers alkalinity).
- **Ferric salts** work over a **wider pH range (4 – 11)** and are more effective for **high-color / high-organic** wastewaters.
- **Lime** is preferred when the wastewater also needs **pH elevation** and **heavy-metal precipitation**.
- **Polymeric flocculants** are dosed at **0.1 – 2 % of dry solids** and are classified as **cationic, anionic, or non-ionic**.
- **Disinfection CT** = **$C \times T$** — for *E. coli*, **Chlorine needs 0.3 – 0.6 mg·min/L**, **Ozone needs 0.02 – 0.06 mg·min/L**.
- **Breakpoint chlorination** for ammonia removal uses a **$\text{Cl}_2 : \text{NH}_3\text{-N}$ ratio of 10 : 1 (mass basis)**.
- **Chemical dose (kg/day)** = **$Q \times C \times 10^{-3}$** — universally used for all feed-rate calculations.
- **Optimal biological nutrient ratio** for C:N:P is **100:5:1** — deviations require dosing **urea, DAP, or phosphoric acid**.
- **Sludge conditioners** (cationic polymers) reduce moisture content; target **cake solids $\geq 20\%$**.
- **Safety factor** of **1.2× to 1.5×** must always be applied to stoichiometric doses for field application.
- **Discharge to environment must follow CPCB/KSPCB norms**: BOD $\leq 30$ mg/L, TSS $\leq 100 mg/L (or 50 for inland water)$, fecal coliform $\leq 1000$ MPN/100 mL.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemistry of Phosphorus Precipitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Chemistry of Phosphorus Precipitation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Phosphorus Precipitation** is a physico-chemical unit operation in wastewater engineering wherein dissolved reactive phosphate species (ortho-phosphate, polyphosphate, and organically bound phosphorus) are converted into insoluble, settleable, and filterable solid compounds through the addition of multivalent metal cations (Al³⁺, Fe³⁺, Fe²⁺, or Ca²⁺) or specialized polymers, followed by pH adjustment, coagulation, flocculation, and solid–liquid separation. The overarching governing reaction framework can be written in its most general stoichiometric form as: $$\text{M}^{n+} + n\text{PO}_{4}^{3-} \longrightarrow \text{M}_3(\text{PO}_4)_n \,(s) \downarrow$$ where **M** denotes the precipitating metal cation of valence *n*. The driving force is the solubility product constant ($K_{sp}$) of the sparingly soluble metallic phosphate formed. The lower the $K_{sp}$ at a given temperature, the more thermodynamically favoured the precipitation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to (i) write balanced precipitation reactions for Al, Fe, and Ca systems, (ii) compute stoichiometric metal dosages from influent phosphate concentration, (iii) identify the optimal pH window for each metal, and (iv) justify the use of lime (Ca(OH)₂) over alum for high-alkalinity industrial wastewaters. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Fishing Net" Imagine phosphorus in wastewater as tiny, invisible, dissolved "sugar" particles floating invisibly in a swimming pool. No matter how long you let the pool stand, the particles will not sink because they are genuinely dissolved at the ionic level. Now, if you sprinkle a chemical "hook" (a metal salt such as aluminium sulphate) into the water, each hook grabs one or more phosphate ions and forms a *visible, heavy, snowflake-like solid* (a chemical floc). The "snowflake" is too heavy to remain suspended, so it sinks to the bottom, where it can be scraped out. That is precisely the engineering essence of phosphorus precipitation: - **Dissolved PO₄** = the invisible pollutant - **Metal salt dosed** = the chemical "hook" - **Insoluble metal phosphate** = the heavy, removable "snowflake" - **pH adjustment** = the regulator that decides whether the hooks actually grip > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Phosphorus precipitation is *not* a biological process. It is purely inorganic chemistry — it works independently of microbial activity, which is why it is often used as a *polishing step* after biological phosphorus removal (Bio-P) to meet stringent discharge limits of **< 0.5 mg P/L**. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Examinations - Influent total phosphorus (TP) range in municipal wastewater: **4–15 mg/L as P** - Regulatory discharge limit (KSPCB, India): **< 1 mg/L** (general) or **< 0.5 mg/L** (sensitive zones) - Molar mass of P (atomic): **30.97 g/mol** (≈ **31 g/mol**) - Molar mass of PO₄³⁻ (ion): **94.97 g/mol** (≈ **95 g/mol**) - Conversion factor: **1 mg/L as P = 3.07 mg/L as PO₄³⁻** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Principal Chemical Precipitation Systems #### 2.1.1 Aluminium-Based Precipitation (Alum / Sodium Aluminate) **Mode 1 — Direct precipitation of aluminium phosphate:** $$\text{Al}^{3+} + \text{PO}_{4}^{3-} \longrightarrow \text{AlPO}_{4} \,(s) \quad K_{sp} \approx 9.84 \times 10^{-21}$$ **Mode 2 — Aluminium hydroxide sweep (the dominant mechanism in real WWTPs):** The aluminium dose is usually *in excess* of the stoichiometric phosphate demand. The excess Al³⁺ hydrolyses with water to form an amorphous aluminium hydroxide floc: $$\text{Al}^{3+} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Al(OH)}_3 \,(s) + 3\text{H}^+$$ The Al(OH)₃ floc has a huge surface area and acts as an **adsorbent "sponge"** that physically entrains phosphate ions via surface complexation: $$\text{Al(OH)}_3 \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \longrightarrow \text{Al(OH)}_3 \cdot \text{PO}_4 \cdot (n-1)\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{OH}^-$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why both modes exist:** Below pH ~6, direct AlPO₄ precipitation dominates. Near pH 6.0–7.5, Al(OH)₃ sweep flocculation dominates. Above pH 7.5, the amphoteric Al(OH)₃ re-dissolves as Al(OH)₄⁻, *destroying* the removal efficiency. This is why pH control is non-negotiable. #### 2.1.2 Iron-Based Precipitation (Ferric Chloride / Ferrous Sulphate / Ferric Sulphate) **Ferric (Fe³⁺) system:** $$\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{PO}_{4}^{3-} \longrightarrow \text{FePO}_{4} \,(s) \quad K_{sp} \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-22}$$ $$\text{Fe}^{3+} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_3 \,(s) \downarrow + 3\text{H}^+$$ **Ferrous (Fe²⁺) system (requires oxidation):** $$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 \,(s)$$ Ferrous iron is *poorly effective* on its own because Fe²⁺ does not hydrolyse readily at neutral pH. It must first be oxidised to Fe³⁺, either biologically (in trickling filters / biofilters) or chemically: $$4\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{O}_2 + 10\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_3 \,(s) + 8\text{H}^+$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Operational pH Windows (KTU Board Favourite):** > | Metal System | Effective pH Range | Optimum pH | > |---|---|---| > | Al³⁺ | 5.5 – 7.5 | 6.0 – 7.0 | > | Fe³⁺ | 4.5 – 7.0 | 5.0 – 6.5 | > | Fe²⁺ (after oxidation) | 7.0 – 8.0 | 7.5 | > | Ca²⁺ (lime) | 9.5 – 11.0 | > 9.5 | #### 2.1.3 Calcium-Based Precipitation (Lime Process) **Mode 1 — Tricalcium phosphate (TCP):** $$3\text{Ca}^{2+} + 2\text{PO}_4^{3-} \longrightarrow \text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 \,(s) \quad K_{sp} \approx 2.07 \times 10^{-33}$$ **Mode 2 — Hydroxyapatite (HAP) — the thermodynamically favoured product at high pH:** $$5\text{Ca}^{2+} + 3\text{PO}_4^{3-} + \text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{OH} \,(s) \downarrow$$ HAP is the *most insoluble* calcium phosphate mineral; it forms dense, crystalline sludge that dewaters well — an important advantage over the gelatinous Al(OH)₃ / Fe(OH)₃ sludges. **Lime addition chemistry (acidity neutralisation):** $$\text{Ca(OH)}_2 + 2\text{HCO}_3^- \longrightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 \,(s) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{OH}^-$$ This is why lime dosage is determined by the **alkalinity of the wastewater**, not just by the phosphate load. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Engineering Logic 1. **Why pH control is the heart of the process:** Each metal phosphate has a *solubility minimum* at a specific pH. Operating outside this window re-dissolves the precipitate. For alum, this window is razor-thin (pH 6–7); for lime, it is broad (pH > 9.5) but requires a high chemical dose to maintain. 2. **Why we overdose metal:** Pure stoichiometric metal cannot achieve < 1 mg P/L. The "sweep floc" mechanism (excess metal hydroxide adsorbing residual phosphate) is what carries the process to very low residuals. 3. **Why mixing intensity is graded:** Rapid mix (G = 700–1000 s⁻¹, ~30 s) disperses the metal salt and nucleates micro-flocs. Slow mix (G = 20–75 s⁻¹, 15–30 min) grows them into settleable macro-flocs. A single high-speed mix destroys the floc structure. 4. **Why two-stage addition is used in advanced plants:** First dose precipitates the bulk of phosphate; second dose (in a downstream point) "polishes" residual P by sweep flocculation. This economises metal and produces lower residual P. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Stoichiometric metal: P molar ratio (Al) | $n_{\text{Al}} : n_{\text{P}} = 1 : 1$ | mol/mol | Alum dosing | | 2 | Stoichiometric metal: P molar ratio (Fe³⁺) | $n_{\text{Fe}} : n_{\text{P}} = 1 : 1$ | mol/mol | Ferric dosing | | 3 | Stoichiometric metal: P molar ratio (Ca, HAP) | $n_{\text{Ca}} : n_{\text{P}} = 5 : 3$ | mol/mol | Lime dosing | | 4 | Alum dose formula (P removal) | $D_{\text{alum}} = [\text{P}] \times \dfrac{1.5 \times 666}{31}$ | mg/L | Includes 1.5× excess | | 5 | Lime dose (above alkalinity) | $D_{\text{lime}} = [\text{P}] \times \dfrac{5}{3} \times \dfrac{56}{31} \times 1.5 + \text{Alk}_{\text{extra}}$ | mg/L as CaO | High-pH P removal | | 6 | PO₄³⁻ ↔ P conversion | $1 \,\text{mg/L as P} = 3.07 \,\text{mg/L as PO}_4$ | — | Unit conversion | | 7 | Solubility product relation | $K_{sp} = [\text{M}^{n+}]^a [\text{PO}_4^{3-}]^b$ | varies | Precipitate stability | | 8 | Sludge production (Al) | $Y_{\text{sludge}} = 6.1 \times \text{P}_{\text{removed}}$ | kg/kg P | Dewatering design | | 9 | Sludge production (Fe) | $Y_{\text{sludge}} = 5.2 \times \text{P}_{\text{removed}}$ | kg/kg P | Dewatering design | | 10 | Sludge production (Ca-HAP) | $Y_{\text{sludge}} = 5.6 \times \text{P}_{\text{removed}}$ | kg/kg P | Dewatering design | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"AFC"** — *Alum For Clear water (pH 6–7); Ferric For flexibility (pH 4.5–7); CaO For high-pH industrial streams.* ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Municipal sewage treatment plants (STPs):** Used as a tertiary polishing step to comply with KSPCB / CPCB discharge norms of < 1 mg P/L. - **Industrial wastewater (fertiliser, dairy, food processing):** Lime is preferred because it simultaneously removes phosphate *and* raises pH to precipitate proteins and heavy metals. - **Lake restoration projects:** Whole-lake chemical treatment with alum or iron salts (called "lake seeding") is used to control eutrophication. - **Sludge management:** The phosphate-laden sludge is rich in P and can be valorised as a slow-release fertiliser (struvite, hydroxyapatite) — a circular-economy angle now favoured in KTU M.Tech-level questions. - **Reverse osmosis pre-treatment:** Pre-precipitation of P prevents membrane scaling by calcium phosphate. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Molar Ratio for Hydroxyapatite (HAP) **Given:** HAP formation reaction: $5\text{Ca}^{2+} + 3\text{PO}_4^{3-} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{OH}$ **Step 1.** Identify the charge balance. The reaction must be electrically neutral. LHS: $(+2)(5) + (-3)(3) + (-1)(1) = +10 - 9 - 1 = 0$ ✓ RHS: $\text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{OH}$ is a neutral solid compound. ✓ **Step 2.** Identify the molar ratio of Ca to P: $$\dfrac{n_{\text{Ca}}}{n_{\text{P}}} = \dfrac{5}{3} \approx 1.667$$ **Step 3.** Convert this to a *mass ratio* for engineering dosing. Use molar masses: Ca = **40 g/mol**, P = **31 g/mol**. $$\text{Mass ratio} = \dfrac{5 \times 40}{3 \times 31} = \dfrac{200}{93} = 2.151 \,\dfrac{\text{g Ca}}{\text{g P}}$$ **Step 4.** For 10 mg/L of P to be removed: $$\text{Stoichiometric Ca required} = 10 \times 2.151 = 21.51 \,\text{mg/L as Ca}$$ **Step 5.** Convert Ca to CaO (lime, molecular weight 56): $$21.51 \times \dfrac{56}{40} = 21.51 \times 1.4 = 30.11 \,\text{mg/L as CaO}$$ **Step 6.** Apply a 1.5× engineering safety factor (accounting for alkalinity demand and kinetic inefficiency): $$D_{\text{lime, design}} = 30.11 \times 1.5 = 45.17 \,\text{mg/L as CaO}$$ > [!NOTE] > **The "1.5× factor" is a Board Examiner Favourite:** Always include it. It accounts for (i) side reactions with bicarbonate alkalinity, (ii) incomplete mixing in real reactors, and (iii) the kinetic sluggishness of HAP nucleation. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Alum Dose for a Target Residual P **Given:** - Influent P = 12 mg/L - Target residual P = 0.5 mg/L - P to be removed = 12 − 0.5 = 11.5 mg/L - Reaction: $\text{Al}^{3+} + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \rightarrow \text{AlPO}_4$ - Molar mass of Al = **27 g/mol**; P = **31 g/mol**; $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (commercial alum) = **594 g/mol** **Step 1.** Convert P to moles: $$n_{\text{P}} = \dfrac{11.5 \,\text{mg/L}}{31 \,\text{g/mol}} = 0.371 \,\text{mmol/L}$$ **Step 2.** Stoichiometric Al required (1:1 molar ratio): $$n_{\text{Al}} = 0.371 \,\text{mmol/L}$$ $$m_{\text{Al}} = 0.371 \times 27 = 10.02 \,\text{mg/L as Al}$$ **Step 3.** Convert Al dose to commercial alum dose. Each mole of $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O}$ provides 2 moles of Al³⁺. So: $$n_{\text{alum}} = \dfrac{0.371}{2} = 0.1855 \,\text{mmol/L}$$ $$m_{\text{alum}} = 0.1855 \times 594 = 110.2 \,\text{mg/L as commercial alum}$$ **Step 4.** Apply 1.5× excess factor for sweep flocculation: $$D_{\text{alum, design}} = 110.2 \times 1.5 = 165.3 \,\text{mg/L}$$ **Step 5.** Sludge produced (using coefficient 6.1 kg/kg P from §2.3): $$\text{Sludge} = 11.5 \,\text{mg/L} \times 6.1 = 70.15 \,\text{mg/L}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Checkpoint:** A 165 mg/L alum dose consumes alkalinity. The $\text{H}^+$ released will depress pH. Calculate this: > $$\text{Alkalinity consumed} = 3 \times 0.1855 \times 50 = 27.8 \,\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$ > (factor 50 = equivalent weight of CaCO₃) > If the natural alkalinity is < 28 mg/L as CaCO₃, **lime addition** is required to buffer pH — a common follow-up question in KTU exams. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Comparison Table: Dose Required Per mg P Removed $$\begin{aligned} \text{For 1 mg P removed:}\\[4pt] \text{Alum } (\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O}) &: \quad 1 \times \dfrac{594}{2 \times 31} = 9.58 \,\text{mg}\\[4pt] \text{Ferric chloride } (\text{FeCl}_3) &: \quad 1 \times \dfrac{162.2}{31} = 5.23 \,\text{mg}\\[4pt] \text{Lime as CaO (HAP)} &: \quad 1 \times \dfrac{200}{93} \times \dfrac{56}{40} = 3.01 \,\text{mg (stoich.)}\\[4pt] \text{But lime total dose} &\approx 1.5 \times \text{alkalinity} + 3.0 \approx 200\text{–}400 \,\text{mg (incl. pH control)} \end{aligned}$$ > **Engineering Inference:** Pure stoichiometric lime is the *cheapest per kg*, but the alkalinity burden makes the *total* lime dose massive. This is why alum/ferric are preferred in low-alkalinity municipal sewage, and lime is preferred in high-strength industrial wastewater with built-in alkalinity. ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — pH Optimum Derivation from $K_{sp}$ (Aluminium Phosphate) For AlPO₄, $K_{sp} = [\text{Al}^{3+}][\text{PO}_4^{3-}] = 9.84 \times 10^{-21}$ Phosphate speciation is pH-dependent: $$[\text{PO}_4^{3-}] = \dfrac{C_T \cdot K_{a1} K_{a2} K_{a3}}{[\text{H}^+]^3 + K_{a1}[\text{H}^+]^2 + K_{a1}K_{a2}[\text{H}^+] + K_{a1}K_{a2}K_{a3}}$$ where $C_T$ = total dissolved phosphate, and $K_{a1} = 7.1 \times 10^{-3}$, $K_{a2} = 6.2 \times 10^{-8}$, $K_{a3} = 4.4 \times 10^{-13}$. - At **pH 5**: $[\text{PO}_4^{3-}] / C_T \approx 10^{-12}$ → precipitation poor - At **pH 6.5**: $[\text{PO}_4^{3-}] / C_T \approx 10^{-8}$ → optimum - At **pH 8**: $[\text{PO}_4^{3-}] / C_T \approx 10^{-4}$, but Al(OH)₃ redissolves as $\text{Al(OH)}_4^-$ → poor This is the *thermodynamic explanation* for the pH 6.0–7.0 optimum of alum. ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) > **Problem:** A wastewater contains 15 mg/L of total phosphorus (as P). It is to be treated with commercial ferric chloride (FeCl₃) to reduce P to 1 mg/L. The desired Fe:P molar ratio is 1.5:1 (including sweep floc). Calculate: > (a) The FeCl₃ dose in mg/L. > (b) The sludge produced in kg/d for a 10 MLD plant. > > *(Molar masses: Fe = 56, P = 31, FeCl₃ = 162.2; sludge coefficient for Fe = 5.2 kg/kg P removed)* **Solution:** **(a)** P removed = 15 − 1 = 14 mg/L Moles of P removed: $$n_{\text{P}} = \dfrac{14}{31} = 0.4516 \,\text{mmol/L}$$ Moles of Fe required (1.5:1 ratio): $$n_{\text{Fe}} = 0.4516 \times 1.5 = 0.6774 \,\text{mmol/L}$$ Mass of FeCl₃: $$m_{\text{FeCl}_3} = 0.6774 \times 162.2 = 109.9 \,\text{mg/L}$$ **(b)** Sludge produced per day: $$\text{Sludge} = 14 \,\text{mg/L} \times 5.2 \times 10 \times 10^6 \,\text{L/d} = 728 \,\text{kg/d}$$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Phosphorus Precipitation Train ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Influent Wastewater
P = 8-15 mg/L] --> B[Rapid Mix Tank
G = 700-1000 per s
t = 30 s] B --> C[Coagulation Zone
pH Adjustment
Alum OR FeCl3 OR Lime Dosed] C --> D[Slow Mix / Flocculation
G = 20-75 per s
t = 15-30 min] D --> E[Clarifier
Settling of Metal Phosphate Floc] E --> F[Clarified Effluent
P less than 1 mg/L] E --> G[Phosphate-Rich Sludge
to Thickener and Dewatering] G --> H[Sludge Cake
Land Application or
Phosphorus Recovery] I[Chemical Dosing Unit] -->|Alum OR Ferric| B J[Lime Slurry System] -->|High pH Streams| C K[pH Probe and Controller] --> C style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style G fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style H fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92 style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 ``` ### 4.2 Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Precipitant ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose Precipitant] --> B{Influent P level?} B -->|less than 5 mg/L| C[Bio-P + Polishing] B -->|5 to 20 mg/L| D{pH of Wastewater?} B -->|greater than 20 mg/L| E[Lime Process
Industrial] D -->|pH 6.5 to 7.5
Low Alkalinity| F[Alum Al2SO4 3
Optimum pH 6-7] D -->|pH 4.5 to 7
Flexible Range| G[Ferric Chloride
FeCl3] D -->|pH greater than 8.5
High Alkalinity| H[Lime CaOH 2
HAP Formation] F --> I[Sludge AlPO4 and AlOH3
Voluminous] G --> J[Sludge FePO4 and FeOH3
Easier to Dewater] H --> K[Sludge Ca5PO4 3 OH
Dense, Crystalline] style F fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style H fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style I fill:#fff9c4 style J fill:#fff9c4 style K fill:#fff9c4 ``` ### 4.3 Phosphorus Speciation vs pH Diagram (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Total Dissolved P] --> B{pH Region} B -->|pH less than 2| C[H3PO4 dominant] B -->|pH 2 to 7.2| D[H2PO4- dominant] B -->|pH 7.2 to 12.3| E[HPO4 2- dominant] B -->|pH greater than 12.3| F[PO4 3- dominant] D --> G[Alum Effective
Al precipitates H2PO4-] E --> H[Ferric Effective
Fe precipitates HPO4 2-] F --> I[Lime Effective
Ca precipitates PO4 3-] style C fill:#ffcdd2 style D fill:#c8e6c9 style E fill:#bbdefb style F fill:#ffe0b2 ``` ### 4.4 Reactor Geometry and Mixing Zones (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Influent Pipe] --> B[Rapid Mix Chamber
Volume 1 to 3 m3
HRT 30 s] B --> C[Flocculation Basin
Tapered G
HRT 20 to 30 min] C --> D[Tube Settler or Lamella Clarifier
Surface Overflow less than 30 m3 per m2 d] D --> E[Effluent P less than 1 mg/L] F[Chemical Feed Pump] -->|Variable Speed| B G[Sludge Recycle Line] --> C H[Sludge Draw-off] --> D style B fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style C fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Q1.** Define phosphorus precipitation. List the three main chemical systems used in tertiary wastewater treatment for phosphorus removal. **Model Answer:** Phosphorus precipitation is a physico-chemical treatment process in which dissolved ortho-phosphate ions present in wastewater are converted into insoluble metallic phosphate compounds by the addition of multivalent metal cations, followed by pH adjustment and solid–liquid separation. The three main chemical systems are: 1. **Aluminium-based** — using aluminium sulphate (alum) or sodium aluminate. 2. **Iron-based** — using ferric chloride (FeCl₃), ferric sulphate, or ferrous sulphate (FeSO₄·7H₂O). 3. **Calcium-based** — using lime, Ca(OH)₂, which forms hydroxyapatite at high pH. *(3 marks — KTU board expects all three systems with one example each.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]** **Q2.** Why is pH control critical in alum-based phosphorus precipitation? State the optimum pH range. **Model Answer:** pH control is critical because aluminium phosphate precipitation efficiency and the stability of the Al(OH)₃ sweep floc are strongly pH-dependent. - **Below pH 5.5:** Al³⁺ remains in solution as free ion; Al(OH)₃ floc does not form adequately. - **Optimum pH 6.0–7.0:** Both direct AlPO₄ precipitation and Al(OH)₃ sweep flocculation occur simultaneously, achieving maximum P removal. - **Above pH 7.5:** Amphoteric Al(OH)₃ re-dissolves to form soluble aluminate ion, $\text{Al(OH)}_4^-$, and the previously-precipitated phosphate redissolves, drastically reducing removal efficiency. Hence, pH must be maintained at **6.0–7.0** for alum dosing to be effective. *(3 marks — KTU examiner allocates 1 mark for definition, 1 for optimum range, 1 for explanation of amphoteric dissolution.)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With balanced chemical equations, explain the chemistry of phosphorus precipitation using (i) alum, and (ii) lime. State the optimum pH for each system. **(b) [7 Marks]** A municipal wastewater has an influent total phosphorus of 10 mg/L (as P) and an alkalinity of 150 mg/L as CaCO₃. Calculate the stoichiometric dose of commercial aluminium sulphate, $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (molar mass 594), required to precipitate 90% of the phosphorus. Use a molar ratio of 1:1 for Al:P. --- **Model Solution to Q-A (a):** **(i) Alum System:** The principal reactions are: $$\text{Al}^{3+} + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \longrightarrow \text{AlPO}_4 \,(s) \downarrow \quad \text{(direct precipitation)}$$ $$\text{Al}^{3+} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Al(OH)}_3 \,(s) \downarrow + 3\text{H}^+ \quad \text{(sweep floc formation)}$$ $$\text{Al(OH)}_3 + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \longrightarrow \text{Al(OH)}_3 \cdot \text{PO}_4 \,(\text{surface complexation})$$ Optimum pH = **6.0 to 7.0**. Above pH 7.5, Al(OH)₃ redissolves as $\text{Al(OH)}_4^-$. **(ii) Lime System:** $$3\text{Ca}^{2+} + 2\text{PO}_4^{3-} \longrightarrow \text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 \,(s) \downarrow \quad (K_{sp} = 2.07 \times 10^{-33})$$ $$5\text{Ca}^{2+} + 3\text{PO}_4^{3-} + \text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{OH} \,(s) \downarrow \quad \text{(Hydroxyapatite)}$$ The lime first neutralises wastewater alkalinity: $$\text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CaCO}_3 \,(s) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ Only *after* alkalinity is consumed does the pH rise above 9.5, enabling phosphate precipitation as HAP. Optimum pH = **> 9.5**, typically 9.5–11.0. *(Full 7 marks: 3.5 for alum system with equations + pH, 3.5 for lime system with equations + pH.)* --- **Model Solution to Q-A (b):** **Given:** P_initial = 10 mg/L; 90% removal → P_removed = 9 mg/L; Al:P molar ratio = 1:1. **Step 1.** Moles of P removed: $$n_{\text{P}} = \dfrac{9 \,\text{mg/L}}{31 \,\text{g/mol}} = 0.2903 \,\text{mmol/L}$$ **Step 2.** Moles of Al required (1:1): $$n_{\text{Al}} = 0.2903 \,\text{mmol/L}$$ **Step 3.** Moles of alum required (each mole of alum provides 2 moles of Al): $$n_{\text{alum}} = \dfrac{0.2903}{2} = 0.1452 \,\text{mmol/L}$$ **Step 4.** Mass dose of commercial alum: $$m_{\text{alum}} = 0.1452 \,\text{mmol/L} \times 594 \,\text{g/mol} = 86.2 \,\text{mg/L}$$ **Step 5.** Verify alkalinity adequacy: Each mole of alum releases 6 moles of H⁺ (from 2 Al³⁺ × 3 H⁺/Al). H⁺ produced: $$0.2903 \times 3 = 0.871 \,\text{mmol/L} \text{ of H}^+$$ Alkalinity consumed: $$0.871 \times 50 = 43.5 \,\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$ Available alkalinity = 150 mg/L > 43.5 mg/L ✓ (no external alkali needed) **Final Answer:** $D_{\text{alum, stoichiometric}} = 86.2 \,\text{mg/L}$ of commercial aluminium sulphate. *(If a 1.5× excess factor is applied: 86.2 × 1.5 = 129.3 mg/L — KTU examiners may accept either as long as the assumption is stated.)* *(Full 7 marks: 1 mark for setup, 2 for moles of P, 1 for mole ratio, 1 for alum stoichiometry, 1 for unit conversion, 1 for alkalinity check.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* > **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare alum, ferric chloride, and lime as phosphorus precipitants. Your answer must include (i) two balanced reactions for each, (ii) the optimum pH, and (iii) the nature and amount of sludge produced. **(b) [7 Marks]** An industrial effluent from a dairy contains 25 mg/L total phosphorus, 400 mg/L alkalinity (as CaCO₃), and has pH 7.2. Design a lime-based precipitation system to bring P down to 0.5 mg/L. Compute: (i) the lime dose (as CaO) and (ii) the daily sludge mass for a 5 MLD plant. Assume HAP formation with a 1.5× stoichiometric excess. --- **Model Solution to Q-B (a):** **Alum:** $\text{Al}^{3+} + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \to \text{AlPO}_4$; $\text{Al}^{3+} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{Al(OH)}_3 + 3\text{H}^+$. Optimum pH 6–7. Sludge: voluminous, gelatinous Al(OH)₃ + AlPO₄, ~6.1 kg/kg P removed. **Ferric Chloride:** $\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \to \text{FePO}_4$; $\text{Fe}^{3+} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{Fe(OH)}_3 + 3\text{H}^+$. Optimum pH 4.5–6.5 (works over broader pH). Sludge: dense Fe(OH)₃ + FePO₄, ~5.2 kg/kg P removed, easier to dewater than alum sludge. **Lime:** $5\text{Ca}^{2+} + 3\text{PO}_4^{3-} + \text{OH}^- \to \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{OH}$ (HAP); $\text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 \to 2\text{CaCO}_3 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Optimum pH > 9.5. Sludge: dense, crystalline CaCO₃ + HAP, ~5.6 kg/kg P removed, plus high CaCO₃ mass from alkalinity neutralisation. **Conclusion:** Alum best for low-alkalinity municipal sewage; Ferric for variable-pH streams; Lime for industrial waste with built-in alkalinity and where sludge dewatering is critical. *(7 marks: 2 marks per system for reactions + pH, 1 mark for sludge comparison.)* --- **Model Solution to Q-B (b):** **Given:** P_initial = 25 mg/L, P_target = 0.5 mg/L, alkalinity = 400 mg/L, plant flow = 5 MLD = 5 × 10⁶ L/d. **Step 1.** P removed: $$\Delta P = 25 - 0.5 = 24.5 \,\text{mg/L}$$ **Step 2.** Stoichiometric Ca required for HAP (Ca:P = 5:3): $$\dfrac{m_{\text{Ca}}}{24.5} = \dfrac{5 \times 40}{3 \times 31} = 2.151$$ $$m_{\text{Ca}} = 24.5 \times 2.151 = 52.7 \,\text{mg/L as Ca}$$ **Step 3.** Convert to CaO (MW 56): $$m_{\text{CaO}} = 52.7 \times \dfrac{56}{40} = 73.78 \,\text{mg/L as CaO}$$ **Step 4.** Apply 1.5× excess: $$m_{\text{CaO, design}} = 73.78 \times 1.5 = 110.67 \,\text{mg/L}$$ **Step 5.** Alkalinity demand check: Ca(OH)₂ needed to neutralise 400 mg/L alkalinity. $$\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \text{ for alkalinity} = 400 \times \dfrac{74}{100} = 296 \,\text{mg/L as Ca(OH)}_2$$ This is the *dominant* lime demand. Convert to CaO equivalent: 296 × (56/74) = 224 mg/L as CaO. **Step 6.** **Total CaO dose** = 110.67 + 224 = **334.7 mg/L as CaO**. **Step 7.** Sludge produced (HAP coefficient 5.6 kg/kg P, plus CaCO₃): P-sludge: $$24.5 \,\text{mg/L} \times 5.6 \times 5 \times 10^6 \,\text{L/d} = 686 \,\text{kg/d}$$ CaCO₃ sludge (1 mol CaCO₃ = 100 g per 2 mol HCO₃⁻ = 100 g per 100 g CaCO₃ equivalent): $$400 \,\text{mg/L} \times 1 \times 5 \times 10^6 \,\text{L/d} = 2000 \,\text{kg/d}$$ **Total sludge = 686 + 2000 = 2686 kg/d ≈ 2.69 tonnes/d** *(7 marks: 1 for P removed, 2 for stoichiometric CaO, 1 for 1.5× factor, 1 for alkalinity demand, 1 for sludge from P, 1 for total.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting the 1.5× excess factor** — the examiner explicitly deducts 1 mark if the design dose equals the stoichiometric dose. > 2. **Confusing Al₂(SO₄)₃ with Al₂(SO₄)₃·14H₂O** — these have very different molar masses (342 vs 594). Many students write the wrong formula. *Always verify the hydrate used in industry.* > 3. **Forgetting to convert PO₄ to P** — the problem states "P" in mg/L but the student divides by 95 (PO₄ molar mass) instead of 31 (P atomic mass). This halves the answer. > 4. **Not checking alkalinity demand** — KTU expects you to *comment* on whether the natural alkalinity is sufficient to buffer the H⁺ released. Skipping this loses 1 mark. > 5. **Writing "CaPO₄" instead of Ca₃(PO₄)₂ or Ca₅(PO₄)₃OH** — examiners demand the *correct stoichiometry*. Guessing loses 2 marks immediately. > 6. **Not specifying pH** — every chemical equation must be accompanied by the operating pH window. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - Phosphorus precipitation converts **dissolved PO₄³⁻** to **insoluble metal phosphate sludge** using Al³⁺, Fe³⁺, or Ca²⁺. - **Three core reactions to memorise:** AlPO₄, FePO₄, Ca₃(PO₄)₂ / Ca₅(PO₄)₃OH. - **Optimum pH windows (high-yield):** Al → 6–7, Fe³⁺ → 4.5–6.5, Fe²⁺ → 7–8, Ca → > 9.5. - **Conversion factor:** 1 mg P = 3.07 mg PO₄³⁻. - **Design rule of thumb:** Apply **1.5×** the stoichiometric metal dose for sweep flocculation. - **Alum:** cheapest for low-alkalinity municipal sewage; produces voluminous sludge. - **Ferric:** broadest pH tolerance; produces dense, easily dewatered sludge. - **Lime:** best for high-alkalinity industrial wastewater; produces dense crystalline HAP sludge; high total dose due to alkalinity neutralisation. - **Sludge coefficients (kg/kg P):** Al → 6.1, Fe → 5.2, Ca → 5.6. - **Alkalinity check:** Always verify that the wastewater has enough natural alkalinity (typically > 1.5× stoichiometric H⁺ produced) to buffer pH; otherwise, lime co-dosing is needed. - **Two-stage dosing** is used in advanced plants: bulk precipitation in stage 1, polishing by sweep floc in stage 2. - **Sludge valorisation:** HAP sludge is a candidate for phosphorus recovery as fertiliser (KTU 2024 sustainability emphasis). > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Line Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** > **"Alum 6-7, Ferric 5, Lime 9.5 — and always dose 1.5× more than stoichiometry."** ---

topics.title: Process Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Process Configuration** in biological wastewater treatment refers to the systematic arrangement and interconnection of physical unit operations and biological reaction vessels that govern the contact pattern between the influent substrate, the active microbial biomass, and the electron acceptor (oxygen/nitrate/sulphate). It defines the *hydraulic regime* (flow pattern through the reactor), the *biomass retention strategy* (suspended versus attached), the *staging philosophy* (single-stage versus multi-stage), and the *electron-acceptor sequence* (aerobic → anoxic → anaerobic) used to achieve the desired removal of carbonaceous BOD, nitrogenous compounds, and phosphorus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Process configuration is the *first design decision* a wastewater engineer makes. The choice of configuration dictates every downstream parameter — reactor volume, aeration demand, sludge production, recycle ratios, and ultimately the capex/opex of the plant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Cooking" Analogy

Think of treating wastewater exactly like cooking a complex dish in a kitchen:

- **The raw ingredients** are the pollutants (organic matter, ammonia, suspended solids).
- **The chefs** are the microorganisms.
- **The recipe (process configuration)** dictates the order of cooking, how long each ingredient is heated, whether you stir it continuously (complete-mix) or leave it layered (plug-flow), and whether you add oxygen (boiling on the stove) or starve it of oxygen (slow braising in a sealed pot).

If you change the **recipe** — i.e., the configuration — even with the same ingredients and the same chefs, you get a completely different dish. A **conventional activated sludge plant** is like a *fast stir-fry* (high-rate, short contact, separate sludge return), while an **oxidation ditch** is like a *slow simmer* (low-rate, long contact, no separate settler needed in the loop), and a **Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR)** is a *one-pot meal* where fill, react, settle, and decant happen in the *same vessel* in time sequence.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Units |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Food-to-Microorganism ratio | **F/M** | $0.05 - 0.6$ | $\text{kg BOD} / (\text{kg MLVSS·d})$ |
| Hydraulic Retention Time | **HRT / $\theta$** | $4 - 36$ | $\text{hours}$ |
| Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT) | **$\theta_c$** | $3 - 40$ | $\text{days}$ |
| Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids | **MLSS** | $1500 - 5000$ | $\text{mg/L}$ |
| Mixed Liquor Volatile Suspended Solids | **MLVSS** | $1200 - 4000$ | $\text{mg/L}$ |
| Recycle Ratio (RAS) | **R** | $0.25 - 1.0$ | dimensionless |
| Volumetric Loading Rate | **VLR** | $0.3 - 2.0$ | $\text{kg BOD} / (\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{d})$ |
| Dissolved Oxygen set-point | **DO** | $1.5 - 2.5$ | $\text{mg/L}$ |
| Temperature (design) | **T** | $10 - 30$ | $^\circ\text{C}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All the above parameters are *configuration-dependent*. The same wastewater fed to a **plug-flow** reactor versus a **complete-mix** reactor will produce very different F/M and DO profiles along the reactor length.

## 1.4 Classification Overview

Process configurations can be classified along four independent axes:

1. **Biomass state** → Suspended growth versus Attached (biofilm) growth.
2. **Hydraulic regime** → Continuous flow (steady-state) versus Batch (time-oriented).
3. **Reactor staging** → Single-stage versus Multi-stage (in series or parallel).
4. **Redox environment** → Aerobic, Anoxic, Anaerobic, or a sequence/combination thereof.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Process Configurations

### 2.1.1 Based on Biomass Retention Mode

#### A. Suspended-Growth Processes
The active biomass is maintained in **free suspension** within the mixed liquor, kept in motion by aeration or mechanical mixing. A downstream **clarifier** is mandatory to separate the biological solids (return activated sludge, RAS) from the clarified effluent.

- *Examples:* Conventional Activated Sludge, Complete-Mix, Extended Aeration, Oxidation Ditch, SBR, MBR.

#### B. Attached-Growth (Biofilm) Processes
Microorganisms grow as a **fixed film** on an inert support medium (rock, plastic, sand, or moving carriers). The wastewater flows past the film; the biomass remains in the reactor and the effluent leaves clarified.

- *Examples:* Trickling Filter, Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC), Biological Aerated Filter (BAF), Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor (MBBR), Fluidized Bed Reactor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Attached-growth systems decouple *Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)* from *Solids Retention Time (SRT)*. This allows very high SRT (and thus slow-growing specialists like nitrifiers) to thrive even at very short HRT — a major advantage for cold climates and low-strength wastewaters.

### 2.1.2 Based on Hydraulic Flow Regime

| Regime | Definition | Typical Reactor Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Plug Flow (PFR)** | Fluid moves as discrete "plugs" with no back-mixing. Concentration varies with *position* only. | Long rectangular aeration tanks, trickling filters |
| **Complete Mix (CSTR)** | Instantaneous and uniform mixing. Concentration is *uniform* throughout the tank. | Circular or square aeration tanks with high mixing |
| **Fed-Batch / SBR** | No inflow during reaction. Fill, React, Settle, Decant occur sequentially *in time*. | Sequencing Batch Reactor |
| **Hybrid** | Combination — e.g., plug-flow reactor with re-aeration zones. | Modified Ludzack-Ettinger (MLE), Bardenpho |

### 2.1.3 Based on Electron Acceptor Sequence (Nutrient Removal)

This is the foundation of modern **Biological Nutrient Removal (BNR)** configurations:

- **Aerobic zone** — Oxygen is the terminal electron acceptor. Removes carbonaceous BOD and nitrifies ammonia to nitrate.
- **Anoxic zone** — Nitrate is the terminal electron acceptor. Denitrification converts $\text{NO}_3^- \rightarrow \text{N}_2$.
- **Anaerobic zone** — No oxygen, no nitrate. Phosphorus-accumulating organisms (PAOs) release phosphate and uptake volatile fatty acids.

The canonical configurations are: **A/O** (anaerobic + aerobic for Bio-P), **A²/O** (anaerobic + anoxic + aerobic for N and P), **MLE** (Modified Ludzack-Ettinger), **Bardenpho (5-stage)**, **UCT**, **VIP**, and **SHARON/ANAMMOX** for sidestream treatment.

## 2.2 Why Process Configuration Controls Treatment Efficiency — The "Why" Behind the "How"

The fundamental design equation that ties configuration to performance is the **biomass-mass balance** combined with the **substrate-removal kinetics**.

For a complete-mix reactor with cell recycle, the **mean cell residence time** $\theta_c$ (also called SRT) governs *who lives* in the system. The SRT is the *engineer's master knob*:

- **Low SRT (3–5 d)** → Only fast-growing heterotrophs survive → high F/M, high sludge yield, *no nitrification*.
- **High SRT (10–20 d)** → Nitrifiers (slow-growing) also survive → nitrification occurs.
- **Very high SRT (20–40 d)** → Endogenous respiration dominates, low sludge yield, "extended aeration" mode.
- **Ultra-high SRT (50+ d, e.g., MBR, aerobic digesters)** → Approach endogenous decay; very low yield.

This is why **configuration choice** (which determines the achievable SRT) is more impactful than any single kinetic coefficient.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations.** Every KTU numerical problem on Module 1 reduces to one or more of these.

### 2.3.1 Reactor Sizing Equations

| # | Equation | Description | Variables |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V = Q \cdot \theta$ | Reactor volume from HRT | $V$ = volume (m³), $Q$ = flow (m³/d), $\theta$ = HRT (d) |
| 2 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT) | $X$ = MLSS, $Q_w$ = wasting flow, subscripts |
| 3 | $X = \dfrac{\theta_c \cdot Y \cdot (S_0 - S)}{(\theta_c \cdot k_d + 1) \cdot \theta}$ | Steady-state biomass in CSTR with recycle | $Y$ = yield coeff., $S_0, S$ = substrate in/out, $k_d$ = decay |
| 4 | $\dfrac{S_0 - S}{\theta \cdot X} = \dfrac{k \cdot S}{K_s + S}$ | Monod rate in a complete-mix reactor | $k$ = max specific rate, $K_s$ = half-saturation |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot U - k_d$ | Lawrence-McCarty relationship | $U$ = specific substrate utilization rate |
| 6 | $\dfrac{X}{\theta} = \dfrac{Y \cdot (S_0 - S)}{\theta} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$ | Net observed yield biomass | for activated sludge design |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{X}{X_r - X}$ | Recycle ratio (RAS) | $X_r$ = return sludge concentration |
| 8 | $\text{F/M} = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X}$ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio | $\text{kg BOD}/(\text{kg MLVSS·d})$ |
| 9 | $\text{VLR} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | Volumetric Loading Rate | $\text{kg BOD}/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{d})$ |
| 10 | $\text{OUR} = a \cdot Q \cdot S_0 + b \cdot V \cdot X$ | Oxygen Uptake Rate (aeration design) | $a$ = oxygen per kg BOD, $b$ = endogenous coeff. |

### 2.3.2 Design Parameters and Boundary Conditions

| Parameter | Symbol | Conventional AS | Extended Aeration | High-Rate | SBR | MBR |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| HRT (h) | $\theta$ | $4 - 8$ | $18 - 36$ | $1 - 3$ | $12 - 24$ | $4 - 8$ |
| SRT (d) | $\theta_c$ | $5 - 15$ | $20 - 40$ | $2 - 4$ | $10 - 30$ | $20 - 60$ |
| F/M ($\text{d}^{-1}$) | F/M | $0.2 - 0.5$ | $0.05 - 0.15$ | $0.5 - 1.5$ | $0.1 - 0.4$ | $0.05 - 0.2$ |
| MLSS (mg/L) | $X$ | $1500 - 3000$ | $3000 - 6000$ | $1000 - 2000$ | $2000 - 5000$ | $8000 - 15000$ |
| RAS ratio | $R$ | $0.25 - 0.75$ | $0.5 - 1.0$ | $0.1 - 0.3$ | N/A | $0.5 - 1.0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Civil/environmental engineers use this table as a *first-pass screen* during feasibility studies. If the client demands nitrification on a small footprint, the engineer immediately eliminates "high-rate" and selects "extended aeration" or "MBR" — purely from the configuration selection, *before* any detailed kinetics are run.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Biomass Concentration in a Complete-Mix Reactor with Recycle (Activated Sludge)

This is the most-tested derivation in the KTU Module 1 syllabus. We derive equation (3) from the formula sheet rigorously.

### Step 1 — Define the Control Volume and Streams

Consider a single complete-mix aeration tank of volume $V$ receiving:
- Influent flow $Q$ with substrate concentration $S_0$ and biomass $X_0 \approx 0$.
- Return Activated Sludge (RAS) flow $Q_r$ with biomass $X_r$ and substrate $S \approx 0$.
- Combined inflow $Q + Q_r$ enters the tank. Tank has uniform MLSS $= X$ and effluent $S$.
- Effluent flow $Q$ leaves with biomass $X_e$ and substrate $S$.
- Wasting flow $Q_w$ leaves with biomass $X_w \approx X$.

### Step 2 — Mass Balance on Substrate

The substrate balance is:
$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} - \text{Utilization}$$

Assuming steady state, accumulation $= 0$:

$$Q S_0 + Q_r (0) = Q S + Q_r S + V \cdot r_{su}$$

Here, $r_{su}$ is the substrate utilization rate per unit volume. Using Monod kinetics:

$$r_{su} = \frac{k \cdot S \cdot X}{K_s + S}$$

Substituting:

$$Q (S_0 - S) = Q_r S + V \cdot \frac{k \cdot S \cdot X}{K_s + S}$$

Because the recycle line enters the tank — not bypassing it — the substrate mass entering via recycle also dilutes, so combining terms:

$$Q (S_0 - S) = V \cdot \frac{k \cdot S \cdot X}{K_s + S}$$

### Step 3 — Mass Balance on Biomass

A similar balance on the active biomass $X$ (using observed yield $Y_{obs}$):

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Net Growth}$$

$$0 = Q X_0 + Q_r X_r - (Q + Q_w) X_e - Q_w X_w + V \cdot (Y_{obs} \cdot r_{su} - k_d X)$$

Assuming $X_0 = 0$ and $X_e = 0$ (ideal clarifier with biomass capture in sludge blanket):

$$0 = Q_r X_r - Q_w X + V \cdot (Y_{obs} \cdot r_{su} - k_d X)$$

Dividing by $V$ and rearranging:

$$\frac{Q_r X_r}{V} = \frac{Q_w X}{V} + k_d X - Y_{obs} r_{su}$$

### Step 4 — Define the Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT)

By definition, $\theta_c$ is the *total mass of biomass in the system* divided by the *rate at which biomass is wasted*:

$$\theta_c = \frac{V \cdot X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}$$

For an ideal clarifier where essentially all biomass leaves via the waste line, $X_e = 0$, $X_w = X$:

$$\theta_c = \frac{V \cdot X}{Q_w X} = \frac{V}{Q_w}$$

Hence $Q_w / V = 1 / \theta_c$. Substituting into the biomass balance:

$$\frac{Q_r X_r}{V} = \frac{X}{\theta_c} + k_d X - Y_{obs} r_{su}$$

### Step 5 — Combine the Substrate and Biomass Balances

The net observed yield is related to true yield by:

$$Y_{obs} = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$$

Substituting $r_{su}$ from the substrate balance and the observed yield relation, after algebraic manipulation we obtain the **canonical design equation for MLVSS in a CSTR with recycle**:

$$X = \frac{\theta_c \cdot Y \cdot (S_0 - S)}{(\theta_c \cdot k_d + 1) \cdot \theta}$$

This is equation (3) from the KTU formula sheet. It is the **master equation** that ties the chosen configuration (via $\theta_c$, $\theta$) to the operating MLVSS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Higher $\theta_c$ increases the numerator more than the denominator in early stages, but as $\theta_c$ becomes large, $X$ approaches a saturation value $Y(S_0 - S) / (k_d \theta)$ — meaning the reactor reaches a "biomass ceiling" dictated by endogenous decay. This ceiling is exactly why **extended aeration** plants have higher MLSS yet *lower* observed sludge yield.

### Step 6 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A complete-mix activated sludge reactor treats $Q = 10000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}$ of wastewater with $S_0 = 250 \, \text{mg/L}$ BOD. The desired effluent BOD is $S = 20 \, \text{mg/L}$. The HRT is $\theta = 6 \, \text{h} = 0.25 \, \text{d}$ and the SRT is $\theta_c = 10 \, \text{d}$. Assume $Y = 0.6 \, \text{mg VSS/mg BOD}$ and $k_d = 0.06 \, \text{d}^{-1}$. Calculate the MLVSS.

**Step (a) — Reactor volume:**

$$V = Q \cdot \theta = 10000 \times 0.25 = 2500 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step (b) — Numerator of the MLVSS equation:**

$$\theta_c \cdot Y \cdot (S_0 - S) = 10 \times 0.6 \times (250 - 20) = 10 \times 0.6 \times 230 = 1380 \, \text{mg·d/L}$$

**Step (c) — Denominator of the MLVSS equation:**

$$(\theta_c \cdot k_d + 1) \cdot \theta = (10 \times 0.06 + 1) \times 0.25 = (0.6 + 1) \times 0.25 = 1.6 \times 0.25 = 0.4 \, \text{d}$$

**Step (d) — Compute MLVSS:**

$$X = \frac{1380}{0.4} = 3450 \, \text{mg/L} \approx 3.45 \, \text{g/L}$$

**Step (e) — Sanity check via F/M:**

$$\text{F/M} = \frac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X} = \frac{250}{0.25 \times 3450} = \frac{250}{862.5} = 0.29 \, \text{d}^{-1}$$

This F/M of 0.29 d⁻¹ falls neatly within the **conventional activated sludge** band of $0.2 - 0.5 \, \text{d}^{-1}$ — confirming that the configuration is consistent with our chosen SRT and HRT.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selecting Configuration Based on Treatment Goal

| Treatment Goal | Best-Fit Configuration | Rationale |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Carbonaceous BOD removal only (small plant) | **Extended Aeration** / Oxidation Ditch | High SRT gives low sludge yield, simple operation, no separate sludge handling |
| Carbonaceous BOD + Nitrification | **Conventional AS with longer SRT ($\theta_c \ge 10$ d)** or **SBR** | Allows slow-growing nitrifiers to establish |
| Carbonaceous BOD + Nitrification + Denitrification | **MLE / Bardenpho (4 or 5 stage)** | Provides anoxic zone for $\text{NO}_3^- \to \text{N}_2$ |
| Carbonaceous BOD + Biological Phosphorus Removal | **A/O** (anaerobic + aerobic) | PAOs need anaerobic contact to uptake VFAs |
| Simultaneous N and P removal | **A²/O** or **UCT** | Three-zone sequence exploits PAO/DGAO competition |
| High-strength industrial wastewater | **Anaerobic (UASB / AnMBR)** first, then **aerobic polishing** | Energy-positive first stage; aerobic polishing meets discharge |
| Very small flow / intermittent flow | **SBR** | One tank, no separate clarifier, fully automated |
| Very strict effluent, water reuse | **MBR** (Membrane Bioreactor) | Ultra-high MLSS, complete solids capture |
| Cold climate nitrification | **MBBR** or **MBR** | Decoupled HRT and SRT — nitrifiers retained on carriers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The configuration choice is *not* an aesthetic decision — it is dictated by the influent (BOD, TKN, TP, temperature, flow variability) and the effluent standard (discharge to river vs reuse for irrigation vs industrial reuse).** Every choice in the analytical matrix above can be traced to a specific constraint in the Lawrence-McCarty or McKinney model.

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Conventional Activated Sludge Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    influent["Influent Q S0"] --> primaryCl["Primary Clarifier"]
    primaryCl --> primaryEff["Primary Effluent"]
    primaryCl --> primarySludge["Primary Sludge to Digester"]
    primaryEff --> aeration["Aeration Tank Volume V with MLSS X"]
    returnLine["Return Activated Sludge RAS Qr Xr"] --> aeration
    aeration --> mixedLiq["Mixed Liquor"]
    mixedLiq --> secondaryCl["Secondary Clarifier"]
    secondaryCl --> finalEff["Treated Effluent S"]
    secondaryCl --> ras["RAS Line Qr Xr back to Aeration"]

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Sequencing Batch Reactor Time-Cycle Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cycle["SBR Time Cycle Total 4 to 8 hours"] --> fill1["Phase 1 FILL Static or Mixed"]
    cycle --> react1["Phase 2 REACT Aerobic Anoxic or Anaerobic"]
    cycle --> settle1["Phase 3 SETTLE Quiescent"]
    cycle --> decant1["Phase 4 DECANT Supernatant Removed"]
    cycle --> idle1["Phase 5 IDLE Sludge Withdrawal"]
    fill1 --> react1
    react1 --> settle1
    settle1 --> decant1
    decant1 --> idle1
    idle1 -.loops back.-> fill1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — A²/O Multi-Stage BNR Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inBNR["Influent with C N and P"] --> anaerZ["Anaerobic Zone PAO Release P"]
    anaerZ --> anoxZ["Anoxic Zone Denitrification NO3 to N2"]
    anoxZ --> aeroZ["Aerobic Zone Nitrification and P Uptake"]
    aeroZ --> clrBNR["Secondary Clarifier"]
    clrBNR --> effBNR["Effluent low C N P"]
    clrBNR --> rasBNR["RAS Qr Xr to Anoxic Zone"]
    internalR["Internal Recycle Qir from Aerobic to Anoxic"] -.recycles nitrate.-> anoxZ
    aeroZ --> nitrLoop["Nitrate produced by nitrifiers"]
    nitrLoop -.flows via internal recycle.-> internalR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering pedagogy:** The A²/O block diagram illustrates a critical insight — the *internal recycle* of nitrate-rich mixed liquor from the aerobic zone back to the anoxic zone is the *lifeblood* of biological nitrogen removal. Without it, denitrification cannot proceed. The KTU examiner frequently tests whether students can identify that this internal recycle is what differentiates an A²/O from a simple A/O.
