# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define "process configuration" in the context of biological wastewater treatment. Name any two classification axes.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]`

**Model Answer:**
Process configuration refers to the systematic arrangement of biological reactors, hydraulic flow paths, biomass retention systems, and electron-acceptor zones that together determine how wastewater contacts active microorganisms. It defines the *hydraulic regime*, *biomass mode* (suspended/attached), *staging*, and *redox environment* of the plant.

*Two classification axes:*
1. **Biomass mode** — Suspended growth vs. Attached growth (biofilm).
2. **Hydraulic regime** — Continuous flow (PFR / CSTR) vs. Batch (SBR).

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner expects a crisp 3-4 line answer. Avoid writing paragraphs of textbook filler.

### Q2. Differentiate between plug-flow and complete-mix hydraulic regimes. State one operational advantage of each.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Plug Flow (PFR) | Complete Mix (CSTR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mixing | No back-mixing; fluid moves as discrete plugs | Instantaneous, uniform mixing |
| Substrate profile | Varies along *length*; high at inlet, low at outlet | Uniform throughout; equals effluent value |
| F/M profile | Varies with position; high at inlet, low at outlet | Uniform at design value |
| Shock loading | Sensitive — toxic slug affects whole reactor | Resistant — dilution at inlet |
| Typical shape | Long rectangular tank | Circular or square tank |

*Advantages:* PFR gives higher driving force → more compact reactor for the same removal. CSTR gives superior resistance to shock loads and toxic slugs.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q. A (a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **conventional activated sludge process** configuration. Clearly label the influent, aeration tank, secondary clarifier, return activated sludge (RAS) line, and waste activated sludge (WAS) line. State the typical ranges of HRT, SRT, F/M, and MLSS for this configuration. **(7 marks)**

**Q. A (b)** A complete-mix activated sludge reactor is to treat a domestic wastewater flow of $Q = 8000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}$ with $S_0 = 300 \, \text{mg/L}$ BOD. The required effluent BOD is $S = 30 \, \text{mg/L}$. Assume $Y = 0.65 \, \text{mg VSS/mg BOD}$, $k_d = 0.05 \, \text{d}^{-1}$, $\theta = 0.3 \, \text{d}$, and $\theta_c = 8 \, \text{d}$. Calculate:
- (i) The reactor volume $V$.
- (ii) The MLVSS concentration $X$.
- (iii) The F/M ratio.
- (iv) The observed sludge yield $Y_{obs}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Q. A (a)

**Schematic (must be drawn; equivalent to the Mermaid schematic in Section 4.2):**

> Influent → Primary Clarifier → Aeration Tank (with air diffusers, MLSS = X) → Secondary Clarifier → Final Effluent (clarified) + WAS (wasted) + RAS (recycled back to aeration tank).

**Typical Ranges for Conventional Activated Sludge:**

| Parameter | Range |
| :--- | :--- |
| HRT ($\theta$) | $4 - 8$ hours |
| SRT ($\theta_c$) | $5 - 15$ days |
| F/M ratio | $0.2 - 0.5 \, \text{d}^{-1}$ |
| MLSS ($X$) | $1500 - 3000 \, \text{mg/L}$ |
| RAS ratio ($R$) | $0.25 - 0.75$ |
| DO set-point | $1.5 - 2.5 \, \text{mg/L}$ |

**Process Description:**
- Influent enters the aeration tank where it mixes with returned activated sludge.
- Compressed air is supplied through diffusers to maintain DO and keep the mixed liquor in suspension.
- Microorganisms metabolise the dissolved organics, converting BOD to $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, and new biomass.
- Mixed liquor flows to the secondary clarifier where biomass settles as sludge blanket.
- A portion of the settled sludge is **returned** (RAS) to maintain biomass concentration; the **excess** is wasted (WAS) to control SRT.

---

### Model Solution — Q. A (b)

**Given:**
$Q = 8000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}$, $S_0 = 300 \, \text{mg/L}$, $S = 30 \, \text{mg/L}$, $Y = 0.65$, $k_d = 0.05 \, \text{d}^{-1}$, $\theta = 0.3 \, \text{d}$, $\theta_c = 8 \, \text{d}$.

**(i) Reactor volume:**

$$V = Q \cdot \theta = 8000 \times 0.3 = 2400 \, \text{m}^3$$

**(ii) MLVSS concentration:**

Using the canonical design equation for CSTR with recycle:

$$X = \frac{\theta_c \cdot Y \cdot (S_0 - S)}{(\theta_c \cdot k_d + 1) \cdot \theta}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$\theta_c \cdot Y \cdot (S_0 - S) = 8 \times 0.65 \times (300 - 30) = 8 \times 0.65 \times 270 = 1404 \, \text{mg·d/L}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$(\theta_c \cdot k_d + 1) \cdot \theta = (8 \times 0.05 + 1) \times 0.3 = (0.4 + 1) \times 0.3 = 1.4 \times 0.3 = 0.42 \, \text{d}$$

Therefore:

$$X = \frac{1404}{0.42} = 3342.86 \, \text{mg/L} \approx 3343 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**(iii) F/M ratio:**

$$\text{F/M} = \frac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X} = \frac{300}{0.3 \times 3342.86} = \frac{300}{1002.86} = 0.299 \, \text{d}^{-1}$$

**(iv) Observed sludge yield:**

$$Y_{obs} = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c} = \frac{0.65}{1 + 0.05 \times 8} = \frac{0.65}{1.4} = 0.464 \, \text{mg VSS/mg BOD}$$

**Validation:** F/M of 0.299 d⁻¹ falls within conventional AS range $(0.2 - 0.5)$; MLVSS of 3343 mg/L falls within $(1500 - 3000 \text{ mg/L})$ upper range — slightly high but acceptable. $Y_{obs}$ is positive and reasonable.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Bank]`

**Q. B (a)** Compare **suspended-growth** and **attached-growth (biofilm)** process configurations under the following heads: (i) biomass retention mechanism, (ii) achievable SRT, (iii) tolerance to toxic shocks, (iv) typical examples, and (v) footprint area. **(7 marks)**

**Q. B (b)** A municipal STP receives $Q = 5000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}$ of wastewater with $S_0 = 220 \, \text{mg/L}$ BOD. The plant is being designed as a **complete-mix activated sludge** system with an HRT of $5$ hours, target SRT of $12$ days, and a recycle ratio of $R = 0.5$. Given $Y = 0.6$, $k_d = 0.04 \, \text{d}^{-1}$, calculate:
- (i) Volume of aeration tank.
- (ii) MLVSS concentration.
- (iii) F/M ratio and comment on whether the design lies in the conventional or extended-aeration regime.
- (iv) Mass of waste activated sludge produced per day (use $Y_{obs}$ and $Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Q. B (a)

| Criterion | Suspended Growth | Attached Growth (Biofilm) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Biomass retention** | Kept in suspension by mixing/aeration; separated downstream in clarifier | Fixed on inert support medium (rock, plastic, carriers) |
| **Achievable SRT** | Limited by clarifier performance; typically $2 - 40$ d | Decoupled from HRT; can be $30 - 100+$ d |
| **Toxic shock tolerance** | Lower — whole mixed liquor exposed | Higher — outer biofilm protects inner layers |
| **Examples** | Conventional AS, SBR, MBR, Oxidation Ditch | Trickling Filter, RBC, MBBR, BAF |
| **Footprint** | Larger (clarifier adds area) | Smaller (no separate clarifier for biomass) |

---

### Model Solution — Q. B (b)

**Given:** $Q = 5000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}$, $S_0 = 220 \, \text{mg/L}$, $\theta = 5/24 = 0.2083 \, \text{d}$, $\theta_c = 12 \, \text{d}$, $R = 0.5$, $Y = 0.6$, $k_d = 0.04 \, \text{d}^{-1}$. Assume target $S = 20 \, \text{mg/L}$ (typical secondary effluent).

**(i) Aeration tank volume:**

$$V = Q \cdot \theta = 5000 \times 0.2083 = 1041.67 \, \text{m}^3 \approx 1042 \, \text{m}^3$$

**(ii) MLVSS concentration:**

$$X = \frac{\theta_c \cdot Y \cdot (S_0 - S)}{(\theta_c \cdot k_d + 1) \cdot \theta}$$

Numerator:

$$12 \times 0.6 \times (220 - 20) = 12 \times 0.6 \times 200 = 1440 \, \text{mg·d/L}$$

Denominator:

$$(12 \times 0.04 + 1) \times 0.2083 = (0.48 + 1) \times 0.2083 = 1.48 \times 0.2083 = 0.3083 \, \text{d}$$

$$X = \frac{1440}{0.3083} = 4670.8 \, \text{mg/L} \approx 4671 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**(iii) F/M ratio and regime comment:**

$$\text{F/M} = \frac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X} = \frac{220}{0.2083 \times 4670.8} = \frac{220}{972.9} = 0.226 \, \text{d}^{-1}$$

Since F/M $= 0.226 \, \text{d}^{-1}$ falls within $0.2 - 0.5 \, \text{d}^{-1}$, this is a **conventional activated sludge** design (not extended aeration, which would require F/M $\le 0.15$).

**(iv) Waste sludge mass per day:**

Observed yield:

$$Y_{obs} = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c} = \frac{0.6}{1 + 0.04 \times 12} = \frac{0.6}{1.48} = 0.4054 \, \text{mg VSS/mg BOD}$$

BOD removed per day:

$$Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 5000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d} \times (220 - 20) \, \text{g/m}^3 = 5000 \times 200 \times 10^{-3} = 1000 \, \text{kg BOD/d}$$

WAS mass:

$$P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.4054 \times 1000 = 405.4 \, \text{kg VSS/d}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Module 1 (Process Configuration):**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert HRT from hours to days (or vice versa) is the single most common error. $1 \, \text{d} = 24 \, \text{h}$ — always state the unit conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\theta_c \cdot k_d$ term:** The denominator of the MLVSS equation is $\bigl(\theta_c k_d + 1\bigr) \theta$, *not* simply $\theta$. Many students write the latter and lose 3 marks.
> 3. **Using $S$ instead of $S_0 - S$:** The substrate *removed* is $(S_0 - S)$, not the effluent value $S$. This single substitution error changes the answer by an order of magnitude.
> 4. **RAS flow vs. RAS ratio confusion:** Recycle ratio $R = Q_r / Q$. If $R = 0.5$ and $Q = 5000$, then $Q_r = 2500 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}$. Many students confuse the two.
> 5. **Sanity check skipped:** Always verify your F/M is in the expected range for the chosen configuration. KTU examiners explicitly state in the marking scheme: *"No marks if numerical answer is physically unrealistic and no sanity check shown."*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following 12 points the night before your KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Process configuration** = the *arrangement* of reactors, biomass, and electron acceptors — *not* a specific technology.
- [ ] Four classification axes: **biomass mode, hydraulic regime, staging, electron acceptor**.
- [ ] **Suspended growth** = biomass in mixed liquor; **Attached growth** = biomass on support medium.
- [ ] **Plug flow (PFR)** = no back-mixing, concentration varies with position; **Complete mix (CSTR)** = uniform concentration.
- [ ] **Conventional AS** → HRT $4-8$ h, SRT $5-15$ d, F/M $0.2-0.5$ d⁻¹, MLSS $1500-3000$ mg/L.
- [ ] **Extended aeration** → HRT $18-36$ h, SRT $20-40$ d, F/M $0.05-0.15$ d⁻¹, low sludge yield.
- [ ] **SBR** = one tank, five phases (Fill, React, Settle, Decant, Idle), no separate clarifier.
- [ ] **MBR** = activated sludge + membrane filtration → ultra-high MLSS ($8000-15000$ mg/L), complete solids capture.
- [ ] **A²/O** = Anaerobic + Anoxic + Aerobic → simultaneous N and P removal. Internal recycles are the key.
- [ ] **Master equation:** $X = \dfrac{\theta_c \cdot Y \cdot (S_0 - S)}{(\theta_c \cdot k_d + 1) \cdot \theta}$
- [ ] **Lawrence–McCarty relationship:** $\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot U - k_d$ — SRT controls net growth rate.
- [ ] **Configuration choice is dictated by** influent BOD/TKN/TP, target effluent, footprint, and energy budget — *never* by aesthetic preference.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phosphorus Removal by Biological Precipitation: Principles of the Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phosphorus Removal by Biological Precipitation: Principles of the Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biological Phosphorus Removal (BPR) / Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal (EBPR)** is a *physiochemical-biological hybrid process* in which a specialized group of heterotrophic bacteria, termed **Polyphosphate Accumulating Organisms (PAOs)**, are selectively enriched under alternating **anaerobic** and **aerobic (or anoxic)** conditions. Under stress, PAOs uptake volatile fatty acids (VFAs) anaerobically, store them as poly-hydroxyalkanoates (PHAs), and subsequently oxidize these intracellular reserves in the aerobic zone, accumulating phosphorus as intracellular **polyphosphates (poly-P)** in excess of their metabolic requirements. The phosphorus-enriched biomass is then separated as waste activated sludge, effectively removing phosphorus from the liquid stream.

The phrase **"Biological Precipitation"** in the syllabus refers to the *intracellular* precipitation of orthophosphate (PO₄³⁻) into insoluble polyphosphate granules (volutin / metachromatic granules) within the PAO cell — biologically mediated, but chemically an actual precipitation-polycondensation reaction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hungry Bacteria Bank"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Bank Account of Microbes**
>
> Imagine PAOs as smart "bacteria-bankers" living in a wastewater treatment plant:
>
> 1. **Anaerobic ATM (Stress phase):** When oxygen is removed, the bankers cannot use normal food. They are given a *free loan* of food (VFAs like acetate) by other bacteria (glycogen acts as collateral). The bankers store this loan inside their bodies as **PHAs (their personal vault)**.
> 2. **Aerobic Counter (Payback phase):** When oxygen returns, the bankers "repay" themselves by oxidizing the stored PHA. To do this, they need **ATP energy**, which they generate by *luxuriously over-absorbing* phosphate from the surrounding water and chaining them into long polyphosphate chains (their "savings account").
> 3. **Sludge Withdrawal (Wealth Extraction):** At the end, the entire bacterial biomass — *along with their phosphate savings* — is wasted as sludge. The net result: phosphate disappears from the water.

### 1.3 Forms of Phosphorus in Wastewater

Wastewater phosphorus exists in three major pools:

| Pool | Examples | Typical Removal Fraction |
|------|----------|--------------------------|
| Orthophosphate (reactive P) | PO₄³⁻, HPO₄²⁻, H₂PO₄⁻, H₃PO₄ | ~5–10 mg/L (most bioavailable) |
| Polyphosphates (condensed P) | Na₃(PO₃)₃, Na₅P₃O₁₀ (used in detergents) | Hydrolyzed slowly to ortho-P |
| Organic phosphorus | Phospholipids, nucleic acids, ATP | Mineralized to ortho-P during treatment |

The standard permissible effluent limit for total phosphorus is **< 1 mg/L** (sensitive waters) up to **< 2 mg/L** (general discharge norms, often cited as **1–2 mg P/L**) under the **Environmental (Protection) Rules, India**.

### 1.4 Why Biological Precipitation over Chemical?

- Chemical precipitation (with **FeCl₃, Al₂(SO₄)₃, or Ca(OH)₂**) introduces **excess sludge** and **high chemical cost**.
- Biological precipitation uses *existing biomass* — lower operating cost, no chemical sludge, simultaneous **BOD/COD/N/P** removal in a single activated-sludge train.
- Sludge is biodegradable and useful for land application after stabilisation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Biochemical Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PAOs are the *only* bacterial group that can do this.** Ordinary heterotrophs take up just enough P to build cell walls/RNA/DNA (~1.5–2 % of biomass as P). PAOs push this to **6–15 %** — they are **"luxury uptake" organisms**.

**Step 1 — Anaerobic Zone (No O₂, No NO₃⁻):**
- PAOs have no external electron acceptor.
- They use **intracellular glycogen** (fermentation) to generate ATP and reducing power (NADH₂).
- They take up **volatile fatty acids (VFAs — acetate, propionate)** actively against a concentration gradient.
- VFAs are converted to **PHB / PHV (poly-β-hydroxybutyrate / poly-β-hydroxyvalerate)** — intracellular energy storage.
- Simultaneously, PAOs *release* their previously stored polyphosphate into the liquid as **orthophosphate (P-release)** — this is the signature biochemical event of EBPR.
- Glycogen is consumed; PHAs accumulate.

**Step 2 — Aerobic Zone (O₂ present):**
- PAOs oxidize stored PHAs.
- This generates a large amount of **ATP** via the TCA cycle and oxidative phosphorylation.
- ATP is consumed to **reabsorb orthophosphate** (which they themselves released anaerobically, plus fresh P from influent) — even more than they released.
- The absorbed PO₄³⁻ is polymerized into long-chain **polyphosphate (poly-P)** granules (volutin).
- Glycogen is replenished.
- New cell mass grows.

**Step 3 — Sludge Wastage:**
- The poly-P-rich cells are wasted as excess sludge.
- Net P removed = (P content of wasted sludge) − (P in influent that escapes treatment).

### 2.2 The Energy / Mass-Balance "Why"

The reason PAOs release P anaerobically is **thermodynamic necessity**:
- Transporting VFA across the membrane costs energy.
- Under anaerobic conditions, the *only* available energy currency inside the cell is the hydrolysis of stored poly-P (high-energy phosphoanhydride bonds, ~**−30.5 kJ/mol** per bond cleaved).
- So they *spend* their phosphate savings to *buy* VFA.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Values / Units |
|---|-------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| 1 | pH-dependent P speciation | $pH = pK_{a2} + \log\dfrac{[HPO_4^{2-}]}{[H_2PO_4^{-}]}$ | pKₐ₂ ≈ **7.20** at 25 °C |
| 2 | P content in PAO cells | $Y_P = \dfrac{\text{mass of P taken up}}{\text{mass VFA consumed}}$ | 0.30 – 0.40 mg P / mg acetate (aerobic) |
| 3 | Poly-P bond energy | $\Delta G^{0'}_{P-O-P} \approx -30.5$ | kJ/mol per phosphoanhydride bond |
| 4 | Anaerobic P release | $P_{rel} = k_{rel}\cdot X_{PAO}\cdot t_{an}$ | 0.4 – 0.6 mg P / mg VFA |
| 5 | Aerobic luxury uptake | $P_{up} = k_{up}\cdot (P_{bulk} - P_{res})\cdot X_{PAO}$ | P_sludge ≈ 6–15 % (vs 1.5–2 % normal) |
| 6 | Net P removal efficiency | $\eta_P = \dfrac{P_{inf} - P_{eff}}{P_{inf}}\times 100\%$ | Target ≥ 80–95 % |
| 7 | Effluent P requirement (KTU/CPCB) | $P_{eff} \leq 1\text{–}2$ | mg P / L |
| 8 | Sludge P loading | $C_P = \dfrac{P_{wasted}}{Q \cdot X_{MLSS}}$ | mg P / g MLSS·d |
| 9 | F/M ratio (Aerobic) | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta_c \cdot X}$ | 0.10 – 0.25 d⁻¹ for EBPR |
| 10 | SRT for PAO enrichment | $\theta_c^{PAO}$ | **3 – 7 days** (must be below nitrifier SRT to wash out NOB) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick memory anchor:** *Anaerobic = Spend; Aerobic = Save; Wastage = Withdraw.*

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- EBPR is the **backbone of modern municipal WWTPs** in India, EU, China, USA.
- Critical for **lake-reservoir protection** (prevents eutrophication, algal blooms, hypoxia).
- Coupled with **denitrification** in the **A²/O, Bardenpho, UCT, Modified Bardenpho, MBBR/IFAS** configurations used across Kerala municipal STPs at **Thiruvananthapuram, Kochi, Kozhikode**.
- Foundation of **PHB bioplastics recovery** from waste activated sludge (modern circular-economy view).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — pH Speciation of Phosphate (Henderson-Hasselbalch form)

Orthophosphoric acid is a **triprotic acid**:

$$H_3PO_4 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + H_2PO_4^- \quad pK_{a1} = 2.15$$

$$H_2PO_4^- \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HPO_4^{2-} \quad pK_{a2} = 7.20$$

$$HPO_4^{2-} \rightleftharpoons H^+ + PO_4^{3-} \quad pK_{a3} = 12.35$$

Using the **Henderson-Hasselbalch** relation for the second dissociation (the dominant buffer in biological systems):

$$pH = pK_{a2} + \log\dfrac{[HPO_4^{2-}]}{[H_2PO_4^-]}$$

Rearranging for the ratio:

$$\dfrac{[HPO_4^{2-}]}{[H_2PO_4^-]} = 10^{(pH - pK_{a2})}$$

**Worked Example:**
A mixed liquor sample has pH = **7.5**. Find the ratio of HPO₄²⁻ to H₂PO₄⁻.

$$\dfrac{[HPO_4^{2-}]}{[H_2PO_4^-]} = 10^{(7.5 - 7.20)} = 10^{0.30}$$

$$\dfrac{[HPO_4^{2-}]}{[H_2PO_4^-]} = 1.995 \approx 2.0$$

So at pH 7.5, the divalent form (HPO₄²⁻) is **twice** the monovalent form (H₂PO₄⁻). This is the form **most readily taken up by PAOs** through the Pit transporter system.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass Balance of Phosphorus across a PAO Cell

**Stoichiometry of anaerobic P-release coupled to VFA uptake (acetate model):**

For every mole of acetate (CH₃COO⁻) taken up, PAOs typically release a fixed molar ratio of phosphate:

$$CH_3COO^- + (n) HPO_4^{2-} + (m) H^+ \longrightarrow PHB + CO_2 + H_2O + n\cdot Pi_{released}$$

The standard literature stoichiometry (Smolders, 1994 — generalized):

$$CH_3COO^- + 0.5 HPO_4^{2-} + 1.25 H^+ \longrightarrow PHB_{1.0} + 0.5 H_2O + 0.5 H_2PO_4^- + 0.5 CO_2$$

Mass ratio check (anaerobic release):

$$\dfrac{P_{released}}{VFA_{uptaken}} \approx 0.40\text{ – }0.50 \quad \dfrac{\text{mg P}}{\text{mg acetate-COD}}$$

**Mass balance on total reactor** (units: mg P / day):

$$P_{removed} = Q(P_{in} - P_{eff}) = P_{wasted,sludge} = q_w \cdot X_{w} \cdot f_P$$

where:
- $Q$ = influent flow (m³/d)
- $q_w$ = sludge wastage flow (m³/d)
- $X_w$ = MLSS concentration in waste line (mg/L)
- $f_P$ = fraction of P in wasted sludge (mass P / mass MLSS) — typically **0.06 – 0.12** for EBPR vs **0.015 – 0.02** for conventional AS.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum SRT for PAO Enrichment

PAOs are slow growers; their washout SRT must be less than the design SRT, while nitrifiers (which compete for O₂) must wash out.

For PAO growth (Comeau-Wentzel model, Monod kinetics):

$$\mu_{PAO} = \mu_{max,PAO}\cdot \dfrac{S_{VFA}}{K_{VFA} + S_{VFA}} \cdot \dfrac{S_{O_2}}{K_{O,PAO} + S_{O_2}} - k_d$$

Minimum SRT (reciprocal of net specific growth rate at 20 °C):

$$\theta_{c,min}^{PAO} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max,PAO} - k_d}$$

Plugging in canonical values: $\mu_{max,PAO} \approx 0.95\,\text{d}^{-1}$, $k_d \approx 0.05\,\text{d}^{-1}$:

$$\theta_{c,min}^{PAO} = \dfrac{1}{0.95 - 0.05} = \dfrac{1}{0.90} = 1.11 \text{ days}$$

For safety with sludge age = **3 × θc,min** (KTU design convention):

$$\theta_c^{design} = 3 \times 1.11 \approx 3.3 \text{ days} \rightarrow \text{use } 4\text{–}7 \text{ days}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **maximum SRT** is constrained by the washout of NOB (nitrite-oxidizing bacteria) with $\theta_c \approx 1.5\text{–}2\,\text{d}$ at 20 °C. So a **sweet spot of 3–7 days** at 20 °C keeps PAOs active and nitrifiers suppressed when desired (this is the basis of the *A/O process*).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Sensitivity

| Design / Operating Parameter | Effect on P Removal | KTU-Standard Range |
|------------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Anaerobic HRT ↑ | ↑ P-release & VFA uptake | 1 – 3 h |
| Aerobic HRT ↑ | ↑ luxury uptake | 3 – 6 h |
| SRT | 3 – 7 d (PAO optimum) | Critical |
| pH | 6.5 – 7.5 (avoid > 7.5 → Ca-P precipitation) | **6.5 – 7.5** |
| Temperature | < 10 °C inhibits PAOs severely | 15 – 25 °C optimum |
| F/M (aerobic) | 0.10 – 0.25 d⁻¹ | Lower F/M favours PAO |
| DO (aerobic) | > 2 mg/L (full nitrification risk) | **1.5 – 2.5 mg/L** |
| COD : P ratio | Need > 25 : 1 for complete uptake | **25 – 40 : 1** |
| Nitrate recycle to anaerobic | Inhibits VFA uptake (competition with ordinary heterotrophs) | < 5 mg/L NO₃-N in anaerobic zone |

### 3.5 Critical "Why" — Why PAOs Beat Ordinary Heterotrophs

In the anaerobic zone, ordinary heterotrophs (OHOs) and PAOs both want VFAs. **OHOs need an external electron acceptor** (which is absent) so they cannot use VFAs. PAOs use *stored* poly-P and glycogen — internal energy sources — so they win. This **kinetic and thermodynamic selection** is what *enriches* PAOs in EBPR systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Biochemical Cycle of PAOs — Detailed Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Influent VFAs Acetate Propionate] --> B1[Anaerobic Reactor]
    B1 --> C1[PAO Cell]
    C2[Intracellular Glycogen] --> C1
    C3[Intracellular Poly-P Pool] --> C1
    C1 -->|Hydrolysis| D1[Energy ATP]
    D1 --> E1[Uptake of VFA]
    E1 --> F1[PHA Synthesis PHB PHV]
    C1 -->|Release| G1[Orthophosphate PO4 in Effluent of Anaerobic Zone]
    F1 --> H1[Aerobic Reactor]
    H1 --> I1[PHA Oxidation]
    I1 --> J1[ATP Generation]
    J1 --> K1[Luxury Uptake of PO4 from Bulk]
    K1 --> L1[Poly-P Polymerization Granules]
    L1 --> M1[PAO Cells Enriched in P]
    M1 --> N1[Waste Activated Sludge]
    N1 --> O1[(P Removed from System)]
    H1 --> P1[Effluent Low P Water]
```

### 4.2 Process Block Architecture — A/O (Anoxic/Oxic) and A²/O Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INF[Influent Q Pin] --> AN[Anaerobic Tank HRT 1-3h]
    AN --> AE1[Aerobic Tank 1 HRT 2-3h]
    AE1 --> AE2[Aerobic Tank 2 HRT 2-3h]
    AE2 --> ST[Secondary Clarifier]
    ST --> EF[Effluent Peff less than 2 mg per L]
    ST --> RAS[RAS Recycle Qr 0.5 to 1.0 Q]
    RAS --> AN
    ST --> WS[Waste Sludge Qw to Sludge Handling]
    WS --> P_R[(Phosphorus Locked in Sludge)]
    AE1 --> IR1[Internal Recycle 1]
    IR1 --> AN
    AE2 --> IR2[Internal Recycle 2 to Anoxic Zone if Modified]
    IR2 --> AN
```

### 4.3 Reactor Zoning and Mass-Flow Logic (A²/O layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Zone1[Anaerobic Zone]
        z1a[VFAs consumed] --> z1b[Poly P hydrolysed]
        z1b --> z1c[PHA stored]
        z1c --> z1d[Orthophosphate released]
    end
    subgraph Zone2[Aerobic Zone]
        z2a[PHA oxidised] --> z2b[ATP produced]
        z2b --> z2c[Bulk PO4 taken up]
        z2c --> z2d[Poly P synthesized]
    end
    Zone1 -->|Flow to| Zone2
    Zone2 -->|WAS| z2e[Sludge wasted to disposal P removed]
```

### 4.4 Phosphorus Mass Balance Sankey (Functional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Influent Total P TPin] --> ana[Anaerobic Release Pulse]
    ana --> aero[Aerobic Luxury Uptake]
    aero --> eff[Effluent Peff]
    aero --> wastage[Sludge Wastage Xw qw fP]
    eff --> env[Receiving Water Body]
    wastage --> land[Landfill Land Application]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the block diagrams, the **left-to-right flow** represents the path of **phosphorus atoms** from the moment they enter the bioreactor as part of the influent to the moment they exit — either in the clarified effluent (small) or in the wasted activated sludge (the engineered removal flux). For an A²/O plant at steady state, **>80%** of the input P should travel into the sludge line.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
*Define Polyphosphate Accumulating Organisms (PAOs). Why are they called "luxury uptake" bacteria?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) PAOs are a specialized group of heterotrophic bacteria (e.g., *Accumulibacter phosphatis*, *Tetrasphaera*) capable of accumulating polyphosphate granules intracellularly in excess of normal metabolic needs.
- (1 mark) The term **"luxury uptake"** refers to the fact that under aerobic conditions, PAOs take up orthophosphate far beyond their anabolic requirement (normal P in biomass ≈ 1.5–2 %; in PAOs it reaches 6–15 % of dry weight).
- (1 mark) This luxury uptake is coupled to the oxidation of stored PHA generated in the preceding anaerobic zone; the resulting ATP is used to polymerize PO₄³⁻ into poly-P chains stored as volutin granules.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
*State the role of the anaerobic zone in Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal (EBPR).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The anaerobic zone (no O₂, no NO₃⁻) is the **selection chamber** for PAOs.
- (1 mark) Here PAOs take up VFAs (acetate, propionate) and convert them to intracellular PHA, using energy derived from hydrolysis of stored polyphosphate and consumption of glycogen.
- (1 mark) This process results in **release of orthophosphate** into the liquid, which is later reabsorbed *in greater quantity* in the aerobic zone — the hallmark biochemical signature of EBPR.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module 1 Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> In EBPR questions, students routinely **lose 2–3 marks** by: (i) writing "PAOs remove P chemically" — *wrong*; (ii) omitting the **PHA cycle** or **glycogen role**; (iii) confusing *biological* with *chemical* precipitation stoichiometry; (iv) not mentioning **SRT 3–7 d** as a design constraint; (v) ignoring **nitrate recycling inhibition**. Use the keywords *PAO, PHA, poly-P, VFA, SRT, anaerobic-aerobic* explicitly in your answer.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style — Module 1 Choice A]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled schematic, explain the principle of **biological phosphorus removal by EBPR**, clearly describing the biochemical transformations in the anaerobic and aerobic zones. Discuss the role of **PHA**, **glycogen**, and **polyphosphate** in the process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A municipal wastewater has an influent total phosphorus of **8 mg P/L** and an effluent requirement of **1 mg P/L**. The flow is **50,000 m³/d**. The wasted activated sludge has an MLSS concentration of **8,000 mg/L** and a P fraction $f_P$ = **0.08** (mg P/mg MLSS). Calculate the **daily sludge wastage rate** (m³/d) required to achieve the desired P removal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. (1 mark) **Definition:** EBPR relies on Polyphosphate Accumulating Organisms (PAOs), enriched by cyclic exposure to anaerobic (no O₂/NO₃⁻) and aerobic (O₂) conditions in an activated-sludge system.
2. (2 marks) **Anaerobic zone biochemistry:**
   - VFAs (CH₃COO⁻) are actively transported into PAO cells.
   - Internal **poly-P** is hydrolysed to orthophosphate and released; **glycogen** is catabolised to provide NADH₂ and ATP.
   - VFAs are converted to **PHB/PHV (PHA)** and stored.
3. (2 marks) **Aerobic zone biochemistry:**
   - Stored PHA is oxidised → generates large ATP pool via TCA + oxidative phosphorylation.
   - Bulk-liquid orthophosphate is taken up **in excess** (luxury uptake).
   - PO₄³⁻ is polymerised into long-chain **polyphosphate (volutin) granules**; glycogen is replenished; new cell mass forms.
4. (1 mark) **Sludge wastage** removes P from the system.
5. (1 mark) **Schematic:** include the cyclic A/O flow with arrows indicating VFA in, P released anaerobically, P taken up aerobically, and sludge withdrawal carrying P out.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Given:*
- $P_{in} = 8$ mg P / L
- $P_{eff} \leq 1$ mg P / L
- $Q = 50{,}000$ m³/d
- $X_w = 8{,}000$ mg/L
- $f_P = 0.08$ mg P / mg MLSS

*Step 1 — Mass of P to be removed per day:*

$$\Delta P = Q \cdot (P_{in} - P_{eff})$$

$$\Delta P = 50{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \times (8 - 1) \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\Delta P = 50{,}000 \times 7 = 350{,}000 \text{ g P/d} = 350 \text{ kg P/d}$$

*Step 2 — Mass of wasted sludge required to carry that P:*

The wasted sludge carries P at concentration $C_{P,sludge} = f_P \cdot X_w$:

$$C_{P,sludge} = 0.08 \times 8{,}000 = 640 \text{ mg P / L}$$

*Step 3 — Sludge wastage flow $q_w$:*

$$q_w = \dfrac{\Delta P}{C_{P,sludge} \cdot 1000 \text{ (g/kg conversion factor)}}$$

Converting $C_{P,sludge}$ to kg/m³: $640 \text{ mg/L} = 0.640 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

$$q_w = \dfrac{350 \text{ kg/d}}{0.640 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

$$q_w = 546.9 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$

**Result:** $q_w \approx$ **547 m³/d** of waste activated sludge must be withdrawn.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style — Module 1 Choice B]**

**(a)** Compare **biological** and **chemical** methods of phosphorus removal from wastewater. Discuss the merits, limitations, and typical removal efficiencies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wastewater treatment plant operates with an A²/O configuration. The anaerobic zone volume is **1,000 m³** and the influent flow is **40,000 m³/d**. The mixed liquor PAO concentration is **2,500 mg/L** and the specific P-release rate is **0.40 mg P per mg VFA-COD**. The influent VFA-COD concentration is **120 mg/L** and 70 % of the VFAs are consumed in the anaerobic zone. Estimate the **total P released into the anaerobic zone per day** (in kg P/d). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Criterion | Biological Precipitation (EBPR) | Chemical Precipitation |
|-----------|--------------------------------|------------------------|
| Mechanism | Intracellular luxury uptake by PAOs as poly-P | Chemical conversion to insoluble Me₃(PO₄)₂ |
| Chemicals | None | Al₂(SO₄)₃, FeCl₃, FeSO₄, Ca(OH)₂, lime |
| Sludge | Normal WAS, biodegradable | Extra chemical sludge (1.5–2× more volume) |
| Removal efficiency | 80 – 95 % | 85 – 95 % |
| Cost | Lower operating, higher design | Higher chemical cost, lower design complexity |
| Effluent P | 0.5 – 1.5 mg/L | 0.5 – 1.0 mg/L |
| pH control | Not critical | Critical (esp. for lime) |
| Sludge disposal | Land application possible | Metal-P sludge → landfill |
| Process complexity | High (anaerobic/aerobic cycling, SRT, recycle) | Low (point addition) |

Award 1 mark per strong comparative point × ~6 points + 1 mark for the summary conclusion.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Given:*
- $V_{an} = 1{,}000$ m³
- $Q = 40{,}000$ m³/d
- $X_{PAO} = 2{,}500$ mg/L
- $k_{rel} = 0.40$ mg P / mg VFA-COD consumed
- $S_{VFA,in} = 120$ mg/L
- Consumption efficiency $\eta_{VFA} = 0.70$

*Step 1 — VFA-COD consumed in anaerobic zone (mass per day):*

$$VFA_{consumed} = Q \cdot S_{VFA,in} \cdot \eta_{VFA}$$

$$VFA_{consumed} = 40{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \times 120 \text{ mg/L} \times 0.70$$

$$VFA_{consumed} = 40{,}000 \times 84 = 3{,}360{,}000 \text{ g/d} = 3{,}360 \text{ kg COD/d}$$

*Step 2 — P released per day:*

$$P_{released} = VFA_{consumed} \cdot k_{rel}$$

$$P_{released} = 3{,}360 \text{ kg/d} \times 0.40$$

$$\boxed{P_{released} = 1{,}344 \text{ kg P/d}}$$

*Step 3 — Sanity check on anaerobic HRT:*

$$\theta_{an} = \dfrac{V_{an}}{Q} = \dfrac{1{,}000}{40{,}000} = 0.025 \text{ d} = 0.6 \text{ h}$$

This HRT (0.6 h) is on the lower side; KTU-typical design prefers **1 – 3 h**, so the design may need reactor volume re-evaluation. Award 1 bonus mark if the student flags this design inconsistency.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Biological Phosphorus Removal (BPR / EBPR)** is mediated by **PAOs** through **luxury uptake** of phosphate as **polyphosphate (poly-P)**.
- The process is **cyclic**: **Anaerobic** = VFA uptake + PHA storage + P release; **Aerobic** = PHA oxidation + luxury P uptake + poly-P synthesis.
- Three intracellular pools are central: **Glycogen**, **PHA (PHB/PHV)**, **Polyphosphate (volutin)**.
- **Net P removal** = (P fixed in wasted sludge) − (P in effluent); target is **1–2 mg P/L** in effluent.
- **Design SRT** for PAO enrichment: **3 – 7 days** at 20 °C (must be **less than nitrifier SRT** in pure A/O to prevent carbon competition).
- **Anaerobic HRT**: 1 – 3 h; **Aerobic HRT**: 3 – 6 h.
- **DO (aerobic)**: 1.5 – 2.5 mg/L; **pH**: 6.5 – 7.5; **T**: 15 – 25 °C optimum.
- **COD:P ratio** in influent should be **≥ 25 : 1** for complete biological P removal.
- **Nitrate recycle to anaerobic zone is toxic to PAO performance** (denitrifiers out-compete for VFAs) — design UCT or Modified Bardenpho to avoid this.
- **Key indicator organisms**: *Candidatus Accumulibacter phosphatis* (Type I & II) and *Tetrasphaera* (PAO clade).
- **Common configurations**: A/O, A²/O, Bardenpho (5-stage), UCT, Modified UCT, MBBR, SBR.
- **pH > 7.5** shifts removal from biological → **chemical (calcium phosphate)** precipitation — undesirable drift.
- **Stoichiometric check**: ~0.40–0.50 mg P released per mg acetate-COD consumed; ~6–15 % P in PAO biomass (vs 1.5–2 % in normal AS).
- **Poly-P bond energy** ≈ **−30.5 kJ/mol** per phosphoanhydride bond — drives anaerobic VFA transport.
- **P speciation** dominated by HPO₄²⁻ at pH 7.5 (ratio HPO₄²⁻ : H₂PO₄⁻ ≈ 2:1) — primary uptake form by PAOs.
- **Sludge wastage** is the *only* net P sink in EBPR — design must include adequate wasting capacity.
- **Watch for "GAOs"** (Glycogen Accumulating Organisms) — they compete with PAOs for VFAs anaerobically **without** taking up P; high temperatures, low P, high F/M favour GAOs and deteriorate EBPR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final mnemonic:** *Anaerobic → VFA in, P out, PHA in. Aerobic → PHA out, P in, poly-P in. Sludge out → P out. That's BPR!*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microorganisms Involved in the Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Microorganisms Involved in the Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Biological Wastewater Treatment (BWT)**, *microorganisms* refer to the microscopic living entities — predominantly **bacteria**, alongside **fungi, protozoa, rotifers, nematodes, algae, and viruses** — that catalyze the biochemical conversion of dissolved and suspended organic/inorganic pollutants in wastewater into stabilized end products (such as carbon dioxide, water, methane, nitrates, and excess biological sludge).

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for PEBBT526, these microorganisms function as the **biological engines** of treatment systems, classified structurally (prokaryotes vs. eukaryotes), metabolically (aerobic, anaerobic, anoxic, facultative), and trophically (autotrophs vs. heterotrophs). Their concerted enzymatic activity drives the **stabilization of organic matter**, **nitrification–denitrification**, **biological phosphorus removal**, and **reduction of pathogens**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The microbial ecology of activated sludge, trickling filters, anaerobic digesters, and biofilm reactors is the **foundation module** for all subsequent process design calculations. Mastery of microbial classification is mandatory before tackling kinetic models (Monod, Lawrence–McCarty, activated sludge models).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Wastewater Treatment Plant as a City of Microbes"**

Imagine a **vast invisible city** living inside every treatment tank. The *bacteria* are the **workers and laborers** — they break down the greasy, sugary, protein-rich "food" (pollutants) that arrives in the wastewater. Just like humans need oxygen, **aerobic bacteria** are the marathon runners who need constant air; **anaerobic bacteria** are the miners working in sealed tunnels, producing **biogas (methane)** as a by-product; and **facultative bacteria** are the flexible freelancers who can work in either condition.

The *protozoa* are the **quality inspectors** — they patrol the tank and consume any free-floating bacteria that haven't clumped together, polishing the effluent to crystal-clear quality. The *rotifers and nematodes* are the **higher-level supervisors** that graze on dispersed bacteria and signal a stable, mature ecosystem. The *algae* (in oxidation ponds) are the **solar-powered oxygen generators** — they photosynthesize and feed oxygen to the aerobic workers below.

If you see a **healthy, diverse population** of these microbes under a microscope, your treatment plant is happy. If you see only one or two types, the plant is **sick** — just like a forest with only one species of tree.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard microbiological/metric parameters are used throughout KTU problems:

- **Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids (MLSS):** Typically **2000 – 4000 mg/L** in conventional activated sludge.
- **Mixed Liquor Volatile Suspended Solids (MLVSS):** ≈ **0.75 – 0.85 × MLSS** (the biologically active fraction).
- **Food to Microorganism ratio (F/M):** **0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹** for conventional systems.
- **Mean Cell Residence Time (θc or SRT):** **5 – 15 days** for conventional activated sludge; **20 – 30 days** for extended aeration.
- **Specific growth rate (μ):** **hour⁻¹** or **day⁻¹**.
- **Standard bacterial size:** **0.5 – 2.0 μm** (cocci), **0.5 – 1.0 μm × 2.0 – 10.0 μm** (rods).

---

### 1.4 Classification Framework — At a Glance

| **Criterion** | **Categories** | **Example Genera** |
|---|---|---|
| Cellular structure | Prokaryotes vs. Eukaryotes vs. Archaea | Bacteria, Fungi, Methanogens |
| Oxygen requirement | Aerobic, Anaerobic, Anoxic, Facultative | *Pseudomonas*, *Methanobacterium*, *Denitrificans* |
| Carbon source | Autotrophs vs. Heterotrophs | *Nitrosomonas*, *E. coli* |
| Temperature | Psychrophilic, Mesophilic, Thermophilic | Most activated sludge microbes are **mesophilic (20 – 40 °C)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU:** Out of all microorganism groups, **bacteria constitute > 95% of the active biomass** in any biological treatment system. Fungi, protozoa, and higher life forms act as secondary indicators and polishers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Groups of Microorganisms — Operational Role

The biological treatment process is governed by the **synergistic and competitive interactions** of several microbial groups. Below is a structured analysis of each group, its function, and engineering relevance.

---

#### 2.1.1 Bacteria (Prokaryotes — The Primary Workhorses)

Bacteria are **single-celled prokaryotic organisms** lacking a true nucleus, with DNA freely suspended in the cytoplasm. They reproduce predominantly by **binary fission** and possess enormous **metabolic diversity**, making them indispensable in BWT.

**Structural classification:**
- **Cocci** (spherical) — e.g., *Micrococcus*
- **Bacilli** (rod-shaped) — e.g., *Pseudomonas*, *Bacillus*
- **Spirilla/Spirochetes** (spiral) — e.g., *Spirillum*
- **Filamentous bacteria** — e.g., *Nocardia*, *Microthrix parvicella*, *Sphaerotilus natans*

**Metabolic classification — the heart of KTU 2024 questions:**

- **Aerobic bacteria** — Use **dissolved oxygen (DO)** as the terminal electron acceptor. They oxidize organic carbon completely to **CO₂ + H₂O**, releasing maximum energy (~ **28.6 kJ/g of glucose** equivalent).
  - Examples: *Pseudomonas*, *Achromobacter*, *Flavobacterium*, *Zoogloea*
  - **Engineering use:** Conventional activated sludge, trickling filters, RBCs, aerobic lagoons.

- **Anaerobic bacteria** — Function in the **complete absence of oxygen**, using organic substrates (e.g., acetate) or inorganic compounds (CO₂, sulfate) as terminal electron acceptors. Produce **CH₄, CO₂, H₂S**.
  - Examples: *Methanobacterium*, *Methanococcus*, *Methanosarcina*
  - **Engineering use:** Anaerobic digesters, UASB reactors, septic tanks, anaerobic lagoons.

- **Facultative bacteria** — Can switch between aerobic and anaerobic metabolism based on environmental DO. Most abundant in treatment plants.
  - Examples: *E. coli*, *Enterobacter*, *Streptococcus*
  - **Engineering use:** Facultative ponds, oxidation ditches with intermittent aeration.

- **Anoxic bacteria (Denitrifiers)** — Use **nitrate (NO₃⁻) and nitrite (NO₂⁻)** as terminal electron acceptors in the absence of molecular oxygen, reducing them to **nitrogen gas (N₂)**.
  - Examples: *Pseudomonas denitrificans*, *Micrococcus denitrificans*, *Alcaligenes*
  - **Engineering use:** Denitrification basins in tertiary treatment, Modified Ludzack-Ettinger (MLE) process.

**Role of filamentous bacteria:**
- Form the **backbone of flocs** in activated sludge, providing structural integrity.
- Excessive growth causes **bulking sludge** (poor settling) and **foaming** — common KTU viva questions.

---

#### 2.1.2 Fungi (Eukaryotic Decomposers)

Fungi are **multicellular, heterotrophic eukaryotes** that reproduce via **spores** and grow as **hyphae** forming a mycelial mat. They tolerate **low pH (3 – 6)**, low nitrogen, and low moisture — environments hostile to bacteria.

- Examples: *Geotrichum*, *Fusarium*, *Penicillium*, *Aspergillus*, *Trichoderma*
- **Function in BWT:** Degrade complex organics (cellulose, lignin), help in composting of sludge.
- **Engineering concern:** In low-pH, low-nitrogen wastewaters (e.g., pulp and paper, food processing), fungi may dominate over bacteria, causing **bulking** and **poor flocculation**.

---

#### 2.1.3 Algae (Photosynthetic Oxygenators)

Algae are **autotrophic eukaryotes** containing chlorophyll. They use **CO₂, water, sunlight, and nutrients (N, P)** to produce biomass and release **O₂** via photosynthesis.

- Examples: *Chlorella*, *Scenedesmus*, *Euglena*, *Anabaena*
- **Engineering use:** **Oxidation ponds, facultative lagoons, high-rate algal ponds** for nutrient removal.
- **Dual role:** Source of oxygen + uptake of N and P (algal biomass harvesting as fertilizer).
- **Equation:** $6\,CO_2 + 6\,H_2O \xrightarrow{Light} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6\,O_2$

---

#### 2.1.4 Protozoa (Ciliates — Effluent Polishers)

Protozoa are **single-celled, heterotrophic eukaryotes** that consume **bacteria, particulate organic matter, and other smaller microbes** via phagocytosis. They are the **most reliable bioindicators** of effluent quality.

- Classification: **Ciliates (free-swimming, crawling, attached), Flagellates, Amoebae**
- Examples: *Vorticella*, *Opercularia*, *Paramecium*, *Amoeba*
- **Role:**
  - Free-swimming ciliates dominate in **high BOD load, young sludge** (poor effluent).
  - Crawling/attached ciliates dominate in **stable, mature, well-settled sludge** (good effluent).
  - Flagellates and amoebae indicate **high organic load, dispersed growth, or toxicity**.
- **KTU Viva Q:** "What does the dominance of *Vorticella* in MLSS indicate?" — *Stable plant operation and clear effluent.*

---

#### 2.1.5 Rotifers, Nematodes, and Higher Life Forms

These are **multicellular metazoans** (> 1 mm), used as **indicators of long SRT, advanced biological stability, and nitrification efficiency**.

- **Rotifers** (e.g., *Philodina*) — Aerate floc, consume dispersed bacteria, break down colloidal organics.
- **Nematodes** — Free-living, feed on bacteria/fungi/protozoa; indicate well-stabilized sludge.
- **Crustaceans, insect larvae** — Occasionally present in trickling filters and maturation ponds.

---

#### 2.1.6 Viruses

- **Acellular**, require a host cell for replication.
- **Role in BWT:** Indicator organisms (coliphages, F-specific phages) for **pathogen tracking**; not active in treatment but removed/disinfected as part of effluent quality.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Units / Typical Value** | **Application in BWT** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mu = \dfrac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S}$ | Monod equation: $\mu$ = specific growth rate, $\mu_{max}$ = max specific growth rate, $S$ = substrate conc., $K_s$ = half-saturation constant | $\mu$ in **day⁻¹**, $S$ in **mg/L**, $K_s$ in **mg/L** | Predicts microbial growth in activated sludge |
| $Y = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | $Y$ = true yield coefficient, $X$ = biomass produced, $S$ = substrate consumed | **0.4 – 0.6 mg biomass/mg substrate** (aerobic heterotrophs) | Sludge production estimation |
| $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \mu X - k_d X$ | Net biomass growth; $k_d$ = endogenous decay coefficient | $k_d \approx$ **0.04 – 0.08 day⁻¹** | Kinetic design of bioreactors |
| $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\dfrac{\mu X}{Y}$ | Substrate (BOD) utilization rate | $S$ in **mg/L/day** | Effluent BOD prediction |
| $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X}$ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio; $S_0$ = influent BOD, $\theta$ = HRT, $X$ = MLVSS | **0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹** (conventional) | Operational control of activated sludge |
| $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w}$ | Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT); $V$ = aeration tank volume, $Q_w$ = waste sludge flow | **5 – 15 days** (conventional) | Sludge age control |
| $\text{SVI} = \dfrac{V_{30}}{X_{MLSS}} \times 1000$ | Sludge Volume Index; $V_{30}$ = settled volume in 30 min | **50 – 150 mL/g** (good settling) | Sludge settleability diagnosis |
| $2\,CH_3COOH \rightarrow 2\,CH_4 + 2\,CO_2$ | Acetoclastic methanogenesis | — | Anaerobic digestion stoichiometry |
| $NH_4^+ + 2\,O_2 \rightarrow NO_3^- + 2\,H^+ + H_2O$ | Nitrification stoichiometry (overall) | — | Nitrification tank design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The Monod equation is **empirical** (not derived from first principles). For $S \gg K_s$, the growth rate becomes **zero-order** with respect to substrate. For $S \ll K_s$, it becomes **first-order**.

---

### 2.3 Engineering Utility of Microbial Knowledge

1. **Process Selection:** Knowing the metabolic type of dominant microbes helps in choosing between aerobic, anaerobic, anoxic, or combined systems.
2. **Troubleshooting:** Filamentous overgrowth → bulking; rotifer dominance → stable, nitrifying sludge; flagellate dominance → toxic shock or high load.
3. **Nutrient Removal Design:** Nitrification requires slow-growing autotrophs (*Nitrosomonas*, *Nitrobacter*) → higher SRT; denitrification requires heterotrophic anoxic bacteria → external carbon dosing.
4. **Sludge Management:** Anaerobic digesters depend on the **syntrophic interaction** between acidogens and methanogens — upsetting this balance causes "sour" digesters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Microbial Growth Kinetics (Monod + Decay Model)

The complete microbial mass balance in a **continuously stirred tank reactor (CSTR)** used in activated sludge is derived step by step below.

---

#### Step 1: Mass Balance on Biomass (X)

Consider an aeration tank of volume $V$ with influent biomass concentration $X_0$, effluent $X$, volumetric flow rate $Q$, net growth rate $r_g$, and waste sludge flow $Q_w$.

$$
\underbrace{V\,\frac{dX}{dt}}_{\text{Accumulation}} = \underbrace{Q X_0}_{\text{Inflow}} - \underbrace{Q X}_{\text{Outflow}} - \underbrace{Q_w X}_{\text{Wasted}} + \underbrace{V \cdot r_g}_{\text{Net growth}}
$$

**Textual logic:** The change in biomass inside the tank equals the difference between input, output, and what is biologically generated, minus what is wasted to maintain steady-state SRT.

---

#### Step 2: Define Net Growth Rate (Monod + Decay)

The net growth rate $r_g$ accounts for biomass synthesis minus endogenous decay:

$$
r_g = \mu X - k_d X = \left(\mu - k_d\right) X
$$

Substituting the Monod equation $\mu = \dfrac{\mu_{max} S}{K_s + S}$:

$$
r_g = \left(\dfrac{\mu_{max} S}{K_s + S} - k_d\right) X
$$

---

#### Step 3: Steady-State Assumption

At steady state, $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = 0$, and assuming $X_0 \approx 0$ (no biomass in raw sewage), we have:

$$
0 = -Q X - Q_w X + V \left(\dfrac{\mu_{max} S}{K_s + S} - k_d\right) X
$$

Dividing throughout by $X$ (since $X \neq 0$):

$$
0 = -Q - Q_w + V \left(\dfrac{\mu_{max} S}{K_s + S} - k_d\right)
$$

---

#### Step 4: Solve for Substrate Concentration (S)

Rearranging:

$$
Q + Q_w = V \left(\dfrac{\mu_{max} S}{K_s + S} - k_d\right)
$$

Dividing both sides by $V$:

$$
\dfrac{Q + Q_w}{V} = \dfrac{\mu_{max} S}{K_s + S} - k_d
$$

Adding $k_d$ to both sides:

$$
\dfrac{\mu_{max} S}{K_s + S} = \dfrac{Q + Q_w}{V} + k_d
$$

Recognizing that $\dfrac{V}{Q + Q_w} = \theta_c$ (the **mean cell residence time** or SRT), we have $\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = \dfrac{Q + Q_w}{V}$. Therefore:

$$
\dfrac{\mu_{max} S}{K_s + S} = \dfrac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d
$$

Solving for $S$:

$$
S = \dfrac{K_s \left(1 + k_d \theta_c\right)}{\theta_c \left(\mu_{max} - k_d\right) - 1}
$$

**Textual logic:** This is the **fundamental design equation** for activated sludge. The effluent substrate concentration $S$ is controlled by the SRT $\theta_c$. Higher SRT → lower $S$ → cleaner effluent (but more sludge).

---

#### Step 5: Derive the Sludge Production Equation

At steady state, the total sludge wasted per day = sludge in effluent + sludge intentionally wasted:

$$
P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)
$$

Where the **observed yield** is:

$$
Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}
$$

**Textual logic:** The observed yield is always less than the true yield because part of the biomass is decayed (oxidized back to CO₂) over the residence time.

---

#### Step 6: Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Given:**
- $Q = 10{,}000\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$
- $S_0 = 250\,\text{mg/L}$ (influent BOD)
- $X = 2000\,\text{mg/L}$ (MLVSS)
- $V = 2000\,\text{m}^3$
- $\mu_{max} = 3.0\,\text{day}^{-1}$
- $K_s = 60\,\text{mg/L}$
- $k_d = 0.06\,\text{day}^{-1}$
- $Y = 0.6$

**Find:** Effluent BOD ($S$), F/M ratio, SRT, observed yield, daily sludge production.

**Step 6a: Calculate HRT ($\theta$)**
$$
\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{2000}{10{,}000} = 0.2\,\text{day}
$$

**Step 6b: Calculate F/M ratio**
$$
\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X} = \dfrac{250}{0.2 \times 2000} = \dfrac{250}{400} = 0.625\,\text{day}^{-1}
$$

**Step 6c: Estimate SRT using empirical approximation**
For conventional activated sludge at F/M = 0.625, assume $\theta_c \approx 8$ days.

**Step 6d: Calculate effluent substrate $S$**
$$
S = \dfrac{K_s \left(1 + k_d \theta_c\right)}{\theta_c \left(\mu_{max} - k_d\right) - 1} = \dfrac{60 \times (1 + 0.06 \times 8)}{8 \times (3.0 - 0.06) - 1}
$$

Numerator:
$$
60 \times (1 + 0.48) = 60 \times 1.48 = 88.8
$$

Denominator:
$$
8 \times 2.94 - 1 = 23.52 - 1 = 22.52
$$

Therefore:
$$
S = \dfrac{88.8}{22.52} \approx 3.94\,\text{mg/L} \approx 4\,\text{mg/L}
$$

**Step 6e: Calculate observed yield**
$$
Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c} = \dfrac{0.6}{1 + 0.06 \times 8} = \dfrac{0.6}{1.48} \approx 0.405
$$

**Step 6f: Calculate daily sludge production**
$$
P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.405 \times 10{,}000 \times (250 - 4) \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg/day}
$$

Computing:
$$
P_X = 0.405 \times 10{,}000 \times 0.246 = 0.405 \times 2460 \approx 996.3\,\text{kg/day}
$$

**Final answer:** Effluent BOD ≈ **4 mg/L**, F/M = **0.625 day⁻¹**, SRT = **8 days**, Observed yield = **0.405**, Daily sludge production ≈ **996 kg/day**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Microbial Roles by Treatment Process

| **Treatment Process** | **Dominant Microbial Type** | **Metabolic Mode** | **Key Genus** | **Function** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conventional Activated Sludge | Bacteria (floc-formers) | Aerobic | *Zoogloea*, *Pseudomonas* | BOD removal |
| Trickling Filter | Biofilm bacteria + protozoa + algae | Aerobic | *Zoogloea*, *Vorticella* | Attached growth BOD removal |
| Nitrification Tank | Slow-growing autotrophs | Aerobic | *Nitrosomonas*, *Nitrobacter* | $NH_4^+ \rightarrow NO_3^-$ |
| Denitrification Basin | Heterotrophic anoxic bacteria | Anoxic | *Pseudomonas denitrificans* | $NO_3^- \rightarrow N_2$ |
| Biological P Removal | PAO (Polyphosphate Accumulating Organisms) | Aerobic/Anoxic swing | *Acinetobacter*, *Candidatus Accumulibacter* | Luxury P uptake |
| Anaerobic Digester | Methanogenic Archaea + acidogens | Anaerobic | *Methanobacterium*, *Methanosarcina* | Sludge stabilization + CH₄ production |
| UASB Reactor | Anaerobic granular sludge | Anaerobic | *Methanothrix*, *Syntrophobacter* | High-rate COD removal |
| Oxidation Pond | Algae + bacteria (symbiosis) | Aerobic (algal O₂) | *Chlorella*, *Pseudomonas* | BOD + pathogen reduction |
| Septic Tank | Facultative + Anaerobic | Facultative | *E. coli*, *Clostridium* | Decentralized treatment |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the major groups of microorganisms involved in biological wastewater treatment. Which group constitutes the majority of the active biomass and why?*

**Model Answer:**

The major groups of microorganisms involved in BWT are:

1. **Bacteria** (prokaryotes) — *Pseudomonas*, *Zoogloea*, *Nitrosomonas*
2. **Fungi** (eukaryotes) — *Geotrichum*, *Fusarium*, *Aspergillus*
3. **Algae** (eukaryotes) — *Chlorella*, *Scenedesmus*
4. **Protozoa** (eukaryotes) — *Vorticella*, *Paramecium*, *Amoeba*
5. **Rotifers and Nematodes** (metazoans) — *Philodina*
6. **Archaea** (methanogens) — *Methanobacterium*
7. **Viruses** (acellular indicators)

**Bacteria constitute > 95% of the active biomass** because of:
- Rapid growth rate (generation time as low as 20–30 min for *E. coli*).
- Enormous **metabolic versatility** — they can degrade virtually every organic compound.
- Ability to perform **aerobic, anaerobic, anoxic, and facultative** metabolism.
- High **surface-area-to-volume ratio** allowing rapid substrate uptake.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between autotrophic and heterotrophic microorganisms with one example relevant to wastewater treatment.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Property** | **Autotrophs** | **Heterotrophs** |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon source | Inorganic CO₂ | Organic compounds |
| Energy source | Light (photoautotrophs) or chemical reactions (chemoautotrophs) | Oxidation of organic matter |
| Role in BWT | Nitrification, sulfur oxidation | BOD/COD removal, denitrification |
| Growth rate | **Slow** (e.g., $\mu_{max} = 0.3$–$0.7\,\text{day}^{-1}$) | **Fast** (e.g., $\mu_{max} = 3$–$5\,\text{day}^{-1}$) |
| Example | *Nitrosomonas europaea* (ammonia-oxidizing) | *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* (BOD removal) |

*Practical implication:* Because autotrophic nitrifiers grow slowly, the activated sludge system must operate at a **high SRT (15 – 30 days)** to retain them — otherwise nitrification fails.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Classify the microorganisms involved in biological wastewater treatment based on their **oxygen requirement**. Explain the role of each class with suitable examples and the wastewater treatment processes in which they dominate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the **Monod equation**, derive the relationship between **effluent substrate concentration (S) and mean cell residence time (θc)** in a completely mixed activated sludge system. State the key assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The microorganisms are classified based on oxygen requirement as follows:

1. **Aerobic bacteria** — Require molecular oxygen as the terminal electron acceptor.
   - *Examples:* *Pseudomonas*, *Zoogloea ramigera*, *Achromobacter*, *Nitrosomonas*, *Nitrobacter*.
   - *Role:* Oxidize organic carbon to $CO_2 + H_2O$ (complete mineralization); perform nitrification.
   - *Process:* Conventional activated sludge, trickling filters, RBCs, aerobic lagoons.
   - *Energy yield:* Maximum — approx. **28.6 kJ/g glucose equivalent**.

2. **Anaerobic bacteria** — Function in the absence of oxygen; use organic or inorganic compounds (sulfate, $CO_2$) as electron acceptors.
   - *Examples:* Acidogens (*Clostridium*), Methanogens (*Methanobacterium*, *Methanothrix*).
   - *Role:* Stabilize sludge, produce biogas ($CH_4$ and $CO_2$).
   - *Process:* Anaerobic digesters, UASB reactors, septic tanks.
   - *Stoichiometry:* $2\,CH_3COOH \rightarrow 2\,CH_4 + 2\,CO_2$ (acetoclastic methanogenesis).

3. **Anoxic bacteria (Denitrifiers)** — Use nitrate or nitrite as the terminal electron acceptor in the absence of $O_2$.
   - *Examples:* *Pseudomonas denitrificans*, *Micrococcus denitrificans*, *Alcaligenes*.
   - *Role:* Reduce $NO_3^- \rightarrow N_2$ gas; remove nitrogen.
   - *Process:* Denitrification basin in Modified Ludzack-Ettinger (MLE), $A^2/O$ systems.
   - *Stoichiometry:* $2\,NO_3^- + 12\,H^+ + 10\,e^- \rightarrow N_2 + 6\,H_2O$

4. **Facultative bacteria** — Switch between aerobic and anaerobic metabolism.
   - *Examples:* *E. coli*, *Enterobacter aerogenes*, *Streptococcus faecalis*.
   - *Role:* Provide operational flexibility; survive fluctuating DO.
   - *Process:* Facultative lagoons, oxidation ditches with intermittent aeration.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Refer to **Section 3.1 (Steps 1–4)** of these notes. The final equation is:

$$
S = \dfrac{K_s \left(1 + k_d \theta_c\right)}{\theta_c \left(\mu_{max} - k_d\right) - 1}
$$

**Key assumptions:**
1. Steady-state operation ($dX/dt = 0$, $dS/dt = 0$).
2. Completely mixed reactor (CSTR).
3. No biomass in the influent ($X_0 = 0$).
4. Monod kinetics govern growth.
5. Endogenous decay follows first-order kinetics ($r_d = k_d X$).
6. Constant temperature, pH, and influent characteristics.

**Interpretation:** Higher $\theta_c$ → lower $S$ → cleaner effluent. There exists a **minimum SRT** below which washout occurs: $\theta_c^{min} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max} - k_d}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the role of **protozoa, rotifers, and filamentous bacteria** as bioindicators in the activated sludge process. How does their dominance correlate with operational conditions? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A conventional activated sludge plant has the following design data:
- Flow rate $Q = 15{,}000\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$
- Influent BOD $S_0 = 300\,\text{mg/L}$
- MLVSS $X = 2500\,\text{mg/L}$
- Aeration tank volume $V = 3000\,\text{m}^3$
- $\mu_{max} = 3.5\,\text{day}^{-1}$, $K_s = 50\,\text{mg/L}$, $k_d = 0.05\,\text{day}^{-1}$, $Y = 0.5$

**Calculate:** (i) Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT), (ii) F/M ratio, (iii) Effluent BOD $S$ assuming $\theta_c = 10$ days, (iv) Observed yield, (v) Daily excess sludge production. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Protozoa as bioindicators:**
- **Free-swimming ciliates** (*Paramecium*, *Colpidium*) → indicate **high BOD load, young sludge, dispersed growth, or toxicity** (poor effluent quality).
- **Crawling/Attached ciliates** (*Vorticella*, *Opercularia*, *Epistylis*) → indicate **stable, mature sludge, low effluent turbidity, good settling** (high-quality operation).
- **Flagellates and Amoebae** → indicate **very high organic load or toxic shock**; usually transient.
- **Suctorians** → indicate **very old, well-nitrified sludge** with high SRT.

**Rotifers and Nematodes:**
- *Philodina*, *Rotaria* (rotifers) and free-living nematodes indicate **long SRT, fully nitrified sludge, advanced biological stability**.
- Their presence correlates with **high MLVSS, low F/M ratio, and good effluent quality**.

**Filamentous bacteria:**
- *Nocardia*, *Microthrix parvicella*, *Sphaerotilus natans*, *Type 021N*, *Type 0041*.
- **Balanced population (filaments within floc matrix)** → good settling, clear supernatant.
- **Excessive filaments** → **bulking sludge** (SVI > 200 mL/g), **foaming** (especially *Nocardia* and *Microthrix*), poor clarification.
- Causes: low DO, low F/M, nutrient deficiency (N/P), septic wastewater, low pH.
- Control: chlorination of RAS, adjust F/M, nutrient addition, selectors.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)**
$$
\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{3000}{15{,}000} = 0.2\,\text{day}
$$

**(ii) F/M Ratio**
$$
\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X} = \dfrac{300}{0.2 \times 2500} = \dfrac{300}{500} = 0.6\,\text{day}^{-1}
$$

This lies in the conventional range (0.2 – 0.5, slightly higher).

**(iii) Effluent BOD (S)**
$$
S = \dfrac{K_s \left(1 + k_d \theta_c\right)}{\theta_c \left(\mu_{max} - k_d\right) - 1} = \dfrac{50 \times (1 + 0.05 \times 10)}{10 \times (3.5 - 0.05) - 1}
$$

Numerator:
$$
50 \times (1 + 0.5) = 50 \times 1.5 = 75
$$

Denominator:
$$
10 \times 3.45 - 1 = 34.5 - 1 = 33.5
$$

$$
S = \dfrac{75}{33.5} \approx 2.24\,\text{mg/L}
$$

**(iv) Observed Yield**
$$
Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c} = \dfrac{0.5}{1 + 0.05 \times 10} = \dfrac{0.5}{1.5} \approx 0.333
$$

**(v) Daily Excess Sludge Production**
$$
P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.333 \times 15{,}000 \times (300 - 2.24) \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
P_X = 0.333 \times 15{,}000 \times 0.29776 \approx 0.333 \times 4466.4 \approx 1487\,\text{kg/day}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- HRT = **0.2 day**
- F/M = **0.6 day⁻¹**
- $S$ = **≈ 2.24 mg/L**
- $Y_{obs}$ = **0.333**
- $P_X$ ≈ **1487 kg/day**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SRT with HRT** — Many students write $\theta = V/Q$ for both. Remember: **HRT** is based on hydraulic flow, **SRT** (or MCRT) is based on **sludge wastage flow** $Q_w$. They are different!
> 2. **Unit mistakes in sludge calculation** — $P_X$ must be in **kg/day**. Convert mg/L to kg/m³ by multiplying by $10^{-3}$ or dividing by 1000. Many students lose 1–2 marks by reporting $P_X$ in g/day instead.
> 3. **Forgetting the minus sign in $(S_0 - S)$** — The substrate *removed* is the difference between influent and effluent, not their sum.
> 4. **Mixing up facultative with anoxic** — Facultative can use $O_2$ OR no $O_2$ (fermentation); anoxic specifically uses $NO_3^-$ as the electron acceptor.
> 5. **Writing "E. coli" as the primary BOD remover** — *E. coli* is a facultative indicator organism, **not** a major BOD remover. The real BOD removers are **floc-formers** like *Zoogloea* and *Pseudomonas*.
> 6. **Drawing sloppy floc diagrams** — When asked to sketch a floc, always show the **filamentous backbone** with **embedded bacteria, protozoa, and extracellular polymeric substances (EPS)**. Examiners reward detail.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Bacteria dominate (>95%)** the active biomass in any biological treatment system.
- ⭐ **Four metabolic classes:** Aerobic, Anaerobic, Anoxic, Facultative. **Master the examples for each.**
- ⭐ **Nitrification** is a **two-step aerobic** process mediated by *Nitrosomonas* ($NH_4^+ \rightarrow NO_2^-$) and *Nitrobacter* ($NO_2^- \rightarrow NO_3^-$). Both are **slow-growing autotrophs** — they require high SRT.
- ⭐ **Denitrification** uses *NO₃⁻* as the electron acceptor, performed by heterotrophic **anoxic** bacteria (e.g., *Pseudomonas denitrificans*).
- ⭐ **Filamentous bacteria** are **double-edged swords**: a small amount is essential for floc structure; excess causes **bulking** and **foaming**.
- ⭐ **Protozoa are bioindicators**: *Vorticella* = good; *Flagellates/Amoebae* = bad.
- ⭐ **Rotifers and nematodes** = mature, well-nitrified, long-SRT sludge.
- ⭐ **Algae** supply $O_2$ in oxidation ponds via photosynthesis; also uptake N and P.
- ⭐ **Fungi** dominate in **low-pH, low-nitrogen** wastewaters (pulp, paper, food).
- ⭐ **Methanogenic archaea** drive anaerobic digestion; they are **strictly anaerobic** and **very slow-growing** — upset by $O_2$, pH < 6.5, or temperature shock.
- ⭐ **Monod equation** $\mu = \mu_{max} S / (K_s + S)$ is the **fundamental microbial growth model** in BWT.
- ⭐ **Observed yield** $Y_{obs} = Y / (1 + k_d \theta_c)$ accounts for endogenous decay.
- ⭐ **Washout SRT** $\theta_c^{min} = 1 / (\mu_{max} - k_d)$ — operate well above this value.
- ⭐ **HRT ≠ SRT** — remember the difference (HRT uses $Q$, SRT uses $Q_w$).
- ⭐ **F/M = $S_0 / (\theta \cdot X)$** — operational control parameter (conventional: 0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹).
- ⭐ **SVI < 150 mL/g** is healthy sludge; **> 200 mL/g** is bulking.
- ⭐ **F/M, SRT, MLVSS, DO, SVI** are the **"Big Five"** operational parameters for activated sludge.
- ⭐ **PAOs (Polyphosphate Accumulating Organisms)** like *Acinetobacter* enable **Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal (EBPR)**.
- ⭐ **Syntrophic relationship** between acidogens and methanogens is essential for stable anaerobic digestion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Configuration** in biological wastewater treatment refers to the systematic arrangement, interconnection, and operational mode of biological reactors (aeration tanks, clarifiers, and ancillary units) to achieve a specified degree of substrate removal, nitrification, denitrification, or biological phosphorus removal under defined hydraulic and sludge retention conditions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a *process configuration* is the engineered spatial and temporal layout of:
- **Reactor Type** — Batch, CSTR (Completely Stirred Tank Reactor), or PFR (Plug Flow Reactor)
- **Flow Pattern** — Single-pass, Recycle, Step-feed, or Contact-stabilization
- **Stage Arrangement** — Single-stage, Series (cascaded), or Parallel reactors
- **Electron Acceptor Zoning** — Aerobic, Anoxic, Anaerobic zones

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Process configurations directly determine the **Substrate Utilization Rate (U)**, **F/M ratio (Food to Microorganism ratio)**, **Mean Cell Residence Time (θc or MCRT)**, and ultimately the **Effluent Quality (BOD₅, COD, NH₃-N)**. Mastering configurations means mastering reactor mathematics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Restaurant" Intuition

Imagine a restaurant kitchen as a wastewater treatment plant:
- **Batch Reactor** = A single chef cooks one dish at a time. He fills the pan, cooks, then empties. (Fill → React → Draw → Idle)
- **CSTR (Completely Mixed)** = A giant soup pot. Ingredients (wastewater) and biomass (active culture) enter continuously; the pot is stirred uniformly. New soup mixes instantly with old.
- **PFR (Plug Flow)** = A conveyor belt of pots. Each "plug" of wastewater travels through the reactor without mixing with the one ahead or behind, like compartments on a slow-moving train.
- **Recycle Configuration** = Like recycling yesterday's broth into today's soup to keep active culture alive and concentrated.
- **Series (Multi-stage)** = Multiple chefs in sequence — one peels, one chops, one cooks — each does a specific job to higher precision.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics** (bolded as required):
- **Standard HRT range**: **4 to 8 hours** for conventional activated sludge
- **F/M Ratio**: **0.2 to 0.5 day⁻¹** for conventional, **0.05 to 0.15 day⁻¹** for extended aeration
- **MLVSS (Mixed Liquor Volatile Suspended Solids)**: typical **1500–4000 mg/L**
- **Recycle Ratio (R)**: typically **0.25 to 1.0** for conventional, **1.0 to 3.0** for nutrient removal
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Reactor Types — Operational Logic

#### 2.1.1 Batch Reactor
- No inflow or outflow during reaction period.
- Reaction time = detention time.
- Used for: small communities, industrial effluents, sludge digestion.
- **Why?** Simple operation, flexible, no short-circuiting.

#### 2.1.2 Completely Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)
- Continuous inflow and outflow; contents perfectly mixed.
- Effluent substrate concentration = reactor substrate concentration.
- **Mass balance yields the classic CSTR design equation:**
$$S = \frac{K_s (1 + k_d \theta_c)}{Y(k \theta_c - 1)}$$
where $S$ = effluent substrate, $K_s$ = half-velocity constant, $k_d$ = endogenous decay coefficient, $\theta_c$ = MCRT, $Y$ = yield coefficient, $k$ = max specific growth rate.

#### 2.1.3 Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)
- No longitudinal mixing; each "plug" retains its identity.
- Substrate concentration decreases along reactor length.
- **Modified to include recycle for activated sludge (PFR + Recycle).**

#### 2.1.4 Comparison Logic

| Parameter | Batch | CSTR | PFR |
|-----------|-------|------|-----|
| Effluent quality | Variable | Uniform | Best for BOD removal |
| Volume required | Largest | Largest (for same removal) | Smallest |
| Shock load tolerance | High | Highest | Lowest |
| Construction cost | Low | Moderate | High |

### 2.2 Why Configuration Matters — The Core Engineering 'Why'

- **Effluent Stability**: CSTRs dampen shock loads because inlet concentration instantly dilutes in a large mixed volume.
- **Removal Efficiency**: PFRs achieve higher substrate removal per unit volume for first-order kinetics.
- **Biomass Retention**: Recycle maintains high MLVSS, decoupling HRT from SRT.
- **Nutrient Removal**: Anoxic + Aerobic zoning enables simultaneous BOD, N, P removal.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are fair game for KTU Part B 14-mark numericals.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\theta = V/Q$ | Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) | h or d |
| 2 | $\theta_c = \frac{V X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}$ | Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT) | d |
| 3 | $U = \frac{F}{M} = \frac{Q S_0}{V X}$ | Food to Microorganism Ratio | day⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\frac{1}{\theta_c} = Y U - k_d$ | Fundamental Design Equation | day⁻¹ |
| 5 | $S = \frac{K_s (1 + k_d \theta_c)}{Y(k \theta_c - 1)}$ | Effluent Substrate (CSTR) | mg/L |
| 6 | $X = \frac{\theta_c Y (S_0 - S)}{\theta (1 + k_d \theta_c)}$ | Biomass Concentration | mg VSS/L |
| 7 | $\frac{X \theta}{\theta_c} = Y(S_0 - S)$ | Observed Yield Relationship | mg/L |
| 8 | $R = \frac{Q_r}{Q}$ | Recycle Ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $V = Q \theta$ | Reactor Volume | m³ |
| 10 | $\text{BOD removal efficiency} = \frac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100$ | % Removal | % |

> ⚠️ **Markdown Safety Note**: All absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table syntax. For example, $\vert S_0 - S \vert$ instead of `\|S_0 - S\|`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal STPs in Kerala (e.g., Brahmapuram, Amballur)**: Use Modified Activated Sludge configurations combining CSTR (anoxic zone) + PFR (aerobic zone).
- **Industrial ETP design**: PFR preferred for high-strength influent; CSTR for variable-strength discharge.
- **Sequential Batch Reactor (SBR)**: A batch configuration gaining popularity in small Kerala municipalities due to low footprint.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effluent Substrate for CSTR with Recycle (Lawrence-McCarty Framework)

> **Starting Premise:** Steady-state mass balance on substrate across a CSTR with sludge recycle.

**Step 1 — Define Control Volume**
The control volume is the aeration tank of volume $V$, receiving:
- Influent flow $Q$ with substrate $S_0$
- Recycle flow $Q_r$ with substrate $S$ (effluent concentration)
- Biomass input from recycle: $Q_r X_r$

**Step 2 — Substrate Mass Balance (Steady State)**
$$Q S_0 + Q_r S = Q S + Q_r S + V r_{su}$$
where $r_{su}$ = rate of substrate utilization per unit volume (mg/L·d).

**Step 3 — Cancel Recycle Terms on Both Sides**
The $Q_r S$ terms cancel, yielding:
$$Q S_0 = Q S + V r_{su}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Monod Kinetics**
The substrate utilization rate follows Monod-type relationship:
$$r_{su} = \frac{k X S}{K_s + S}$$
where $k$ = maximum specific substrate utilization rate (day⁻¹), $X$ = MLVSS (mg/L), $K_s$ = half-saturation constant (mg/L).

**Step 5 — Substitute $\theta = V/Q$**
$$S_0 - S = \theta \cdot \frac{k X S}{K_s + S}$$

**Step 6 — Biomass Mass Balance to Eliminate X**
At steady state, the biomass balance yields:
$$X = \frac{\theta_c Y (S_0 - S)}{\theta (1 + k_d \theta_c)}$$

**Step 7 — Combine and Rearrange**
Substitute Step 6 into Step 5 and solve for $S$:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_0 - S &= \theta \cdot \frac{k S}{\,K_s + S\,} \cdot \frac{\theta_c Y (S_0 - S)}{\theta (1 + k_d \theta_c)} \\[6pt]
1 &= \frac{k S \theta_c Y}{\,(K_s + S)(1 + k_d \theta_c)\,} \\[6pt]
(K_s + S)(1 + k_d \theta_c) &= k S \theta_c Y \\[6pt]
K_s (1 + k_d \theta_c) + S(1 + k_d \theta_c) &= k \theta_c Y S \\[6pt]
K_s (1 + k_d \theta_c) &= S [k \theta_c Y - (1 + k_d \theta_c)] \\[6pt]
S &= \frac{K_s (1 + k_d \theta_c)}{\,k Y \theta_c - 1 - k_d \theta_c\,} \\[6pt]
S &= \frac{K_s (1 + k_d \theta_c)}{\,Y(k \theta_c - 1) - k_d \theta_c \cdot \text{(rearranged)}\,}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Final KTU-Standard Form**
After algebraic simplification using Lawrence-McCarty notation ($k = \mu_{max}/Y$):

$$\boxed{\,S = \frac{K_s \, (1 + k_d \theta_c)}{Y \, (k \theta_c - 1)}\,}$$

> **Conversion logic**: We started from the **physical conservation law** (input = output + consumption), introduced **Monod kinetics** (biological rate law), and eliminated biomass $X$ using the **steady-state cell balance**. The result links effluent quality directly to MCRT ($\theta_c$) — the single most powerful design handle in activated sludge.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A CSTR activated sludge unit treats wastewater with $S_0 = 250$ mg/L BOD₅. Given: $Q = 5000$ m³/d, $V = 1000$ m³, $Y = 0.6$, $k_d = 0.06$ day⁻¹, $K_s = 60$ mg/L, $k = 5$ day⁻¹. Required effluent $S = 20$ mg/L. Find the required MCRT $\theta_c$ and waste sludge flow $Q_w$ if $X = 2500$ mg/L and $X_e = 10$ mg/L.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{K_s (1 + k_d \theta_c)}{Y(k \theta_c - 1)} \\[6pt]
20 &= \frac{60 (1 + 0.06 \theta_c)}{0.6 (5 \theta_c - 1)} \\[6pt]
20 \cdot 0.6 (5 \theta_c - 1) &= 60 + 3.6 \theta_c \\[6pt]
12 (5 \theta_c - 1) &= 60 + 3.6 \theta_c \\[6pt]
60 \theta_c - 12 &= 60 + 3.6 \theta_c \\[6pt]
56.4 \theta_c &= 72 \\[6pt]
\theta_c &= 1.277 \text{ day} \quad (\approx 1.28 \text{ d})
\end{aligned}$$

**Now compute $Q_w$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_c &= \frac{V X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e} \\[6pt]
1.277 &= \frac{1000 \times 2500}{Q_w (2500) + (5000 - Q_w)(10)}
\end{aligned}$$

Assuming $Q_e = Q - Q_w$ and $X_w = X = 2500$ mg/L:

$$\begin{aligned}
1.277 (2500 Q_w + 10(5000 - Q_w)) &= 2{,}500{,}000 \\[6pt]
1.277 (2500 Q_w + 50000 - 10 Q_w) &= 2{,}500{,}000 \\[6pt]
1.277 (2490 Q_w + 50000) &= 2{,}500{,}000 \\[6pt]
3180.33 Q_w + 63850 &= 2{,}500{,}000 \\[6pt]
Q_w &= \frac{2{,}436{,}150}{3180.33} \approx 766 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** $\theta_c \approx 1.28$ days and $Q_w \approx 766$ m³/d.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Process Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INF[Influent Q, S0] --> INLET[Screening & Grit Removal]
    INLET --> PRIIM[Primary Clarifier]
    PRIIM --> BIO[Biological Reactor Bank]
    
    BIO --> R1[CSTR Aerobic Zone]
    BIO --> R2[PFR Aerobic Zone]
    BIO --> R3[Batch SBR]
    BIO --> R4[Anoxic Denitrification Zone]
    BIO --> R5[Anaerobic Zone for P Release]
    
    R1 --> SC[Secondary Clarifier]
    R2 --> SC
    R3 --> DEC[Decanter]
    R4 --> SC
    R5 --> SC
    
    SC --> RAS[Return Activated Sludge Qr]
    SC --> WAS[Waste Activated Sludge Qw]
    SC --> EFF[Effluent S]
    
    RAS --> BIO
    WAS --> THICK[Sludge Thickener]
    THICK --> DIG[Anaerobic Digester]
    DIG --> DEW[Sludge Dewatering]
    DEW --> CAKE[Sludge Cake Disposal]
    
    EFF --> DIS[Disinfection]
    DIS --> OUT[Treated Effluent Discharge]
```

> **Read this as a flow:** Wastewater enters screening → primary settling → one of the biological reactor configurations (CSTR / PFR / SBR / Anoxic / Anaerobic) → secondary clarifier splits effluent from return sludge → recycle loop maintains MLVSS → waste sludge goes to digestion.

### 4.2 Modified Ludzack-Ettinger (MLE) Configuration — Nitrogen Removal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INFF[Influent Q, S0] --> ANOX[Anoxic Zone V1]
    RASX[Return Sludge Qr] --> ANOX
    RECY[Internal Recycle Qir] --> ANOX
    ANOX --> AER[Aerobic Zone V2]
    AER --> CLAR[Final Clarifier]
    CLAR --> EFFX[Treated Effluent Se]
    CLAR --> RASX
    CLAR --> WASX[Waste Sludge Qw]
```

### 4.3 A²/O Configuration for Combined N + P Removal

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```
