# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reagent-Dose Equation

> [!NOTE]
> The mass of reagent needed to neutralize a given wastewater flow is derived from the **equivalent principle**. Walk through the derivation cleanly because KTU asks for "deduce" / "show that" style questions worth $3 - 5$ marks.

### Step 1 — Write the Neutralization Reaction in Equivalent Form

Consider sulfuric acid in wastewater being neutralized by sodium hydroxide:

$$\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Moles of acid : moles of base = $1 : 2$.

### Step 2 — Introduce the Concept of Equivalents

By definition:

$$\text{Number of equivalents} = \frac{\text{mass (g)}}{E} = \frac{\text{mass (g)} \cdot n}{M}$$

where $M$ is the molecular weight (g/mol) and $n$ is the number of replaceable $\text{H}^+$ or $\text{OH}^-$ per molecule (the **valency factor**).

For $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$: $M = 98$, $n = 2$, $E = 49$ g/eq.
For $\text{NaOH}$: $M = 40$, $n = 1$, $E = 40$ g/eq.

### Step 3 — State the Equivalence Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **At the point of complete neutralization, the equivalents of acid consumed equal the equivalents of base added.**

$$\text{eq}_{\text{acid}} = \text{eq}_{\text{base}}$$

### Step 4 — Express Equivalents in Terms of Normality and Volume

By definition, Normality $N$ is the number of equivalents per litre of solution:

$$N = \frac{\text{eq}}{V (\text{L})}$$

Therefore:

$$N_a V_a = N_b V_b$$

### Step 5 — Convert Normality of Reagent to Mass

If the alkali is dosed as a solid of equivalent weight $E_b$:

$$\text{Mass of alkali (g)} = N_b V_b E_b$$

Replacing $N_b V_b$ with $N_a V_a$:

$$\text{Mass of alkali (g)} = N_a V_a E_b$$

### Step 6 — Generalize to a Wastewater Stream of Volume $V_w$

In a continuous process, $V_a$ is replaced by the volume of wastewater $V_w$ that contains the acid:

$$m_{\text{alkali}} = N_a \cdot V_w \cdot E_b \quad \text{[in grams]}$$

or, in kg:

$$m_{\text{alkali}} = \frac{N_a \cdot V_w \cdot E_b}{1000} \quad \text{[in kg]}$$

This is the **master design equation** used in all stoichiometric neutralization design.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Lime Dose for Acidic Industrial Effluent (KTU-style 14-mark type)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem.** An industry discharges $Q = 500\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ of acidic wastewater at normality $N_a = 0.04\,\text{N}$ (chiefly $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$). Design a lime $[\text{Ca(OH)}_2]$ neutralization system. Compute: (a) the daily mass of lime required at $1.10 \times$ stoichiometric; (b) the daily mass of $\text{CaSO}_4$ sludge produced; (c) the volume of neutralization tank for an HRT of $20$ min. Use $M_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2} = 74$, $M_{\text{CaSO}_4} = 136$.

### Step (a) — Stoichiometric Lime Required

Molecular weight $M = 74$ g/mol. Valency factor $n = 2$ (each $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ provides two $\text{OH}^-$). Therefore:

$$E_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2} = \frac{M}{n} = \frac{74}{2} = 37\,\text{g/eq}$$

The mass of lime at exact stoichiometry:

$$m_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{N_a \cdot V_w \cdot E_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2}}{1000}$$

Plugging in numerical values:

$$m_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{0.04 \times 500 \times 37}{1000}$$

Computing the numerator step by step:

$$0.04 \times 500 = 20 \quad \text{(eq/day of acid)}$$

$$20 \times 37 = 740 \quad \text{(g of lime per day at stoichiometry)}$$

Therefore:

$$m_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{740}{1000} = 0.74\,\text{kg/day}$$

With a $1.10 \times$ factor for safety against residual acidity:

$$m_{\text{design}} = 1.10 \times 0.74 = 0.814\,\text{kg/day}$$

### Step (b) — Sludge Mass of $\text{CaSO}_4$

Reaction: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

Moles of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ per day = $N_a \times V_w / 2 = 0.04 \times 500 / 2 = 10\,\text{mol/day}$ (since $n=2$ for $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$).

Equivalently, eq/day = $N_a V_w = 20\,\text{eq/day}$. Moles of $\text{CaSO}_4$ produced = $20 / 2 = 10\,\text{mol/day}$.

Mass of $\text{CaSO}_4$ per day:

$$m_{\text{CaSO}_4} = 10 \times 136 = 1360\,\text{g/day} = 1.36\,\text{kg/day}$$

### Step (c) — Tank Volume

Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) of $20$ minutes = $\dfrac{20}{60 \times 24} = 0.01389$ days.

Flow $Q = 500\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$:

$$V_{\text{tank}} = Q \times \text{HRT} = 500 \times 0.01389 = 6.944\,\text{m}^3 \;\approx\; 7.0\,\text{m}^3$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Strong-Acid / Strong-Base $\text{pH}$ Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem.** $200\,\text{mL}$ of $0.10\,\text{M}$ $\text{HCl}$ is mixed with $300\,\text{mL}$ of $0.05\,\text{M}$ $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$. Find the final $\text{pH}$.

### Step 1 — Moles of $\text{H}^+$ from $\text{HCl}$

$$\text{HCl} \longrightarrow \text{H}^+ + \text{Cl}^-$$

$$n_{\text{H}^+} = 0.10 \times 0.200 = 0.020\,\text{mol} = 20\,\text{mmol}$$

### Step 2 — Moles of $\text{OH}^-$ from $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$

$$\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Ca}^{2+} + 2\,\text{OH}^-$$

$$n_{\text{OH}^-} = 2 \times 0.05 \times 0.300 = 0.030\,\text{mol} = 30\,\text{mmol}$$

### Step 3 — Net Excess of $\text{OH}^-$

$$\Delta = 30 - 20 = 10\,\text{mmol of OH}^- \text{ (excess)}$$

### Step 4 — Concentration in Total Volume

Total volume $V_{\text{total}} = 200 + 300 = 500\,\text{mL} = 0.500\,\text{L}$.

$$[\text{OH}^-] = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{0.500} = 0.020\,\text{M}$$

### Step 5 — Compute $\text{pOH}$ then $\text{pH}$

$$\text{pOH} = -\log_{10}(0.020) = -\log_{10}(2 \times 10^{-2}) = 2 - 0.3010 = 1.699$$

$$\text{pH} = 14 - 1.699 = 12.301$$

The mixture is **strongly alkaline** — neutralization has overshot, so an acidic-correction stage is mandatory.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Equivalents Calculation with Mixed Acids

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem.** A wastewater contains $196\,\text{mg/L}$ of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ and $73\,\text{mg/L}$ of $\text{HCl}$. Compute (i) the total normality of the acid mixture and (ii) the kg of $\text{NaOH}$ needed per $\text{m}^3$ to neutralize.

### Step (i) — Total Normality

For $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$: $M = 98$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $E = 49$ g/eq.

$$N_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = \frac{196\,\text{mg/L}}{49\,\text{g/eq}} = \frac{0.196}{49} = 0.0040\,\text{N}$$

For $\text{HCl}$: $M = 36.5$ g/mol, $n = 1$, $E = 36.5$ g/eq.

$$N_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{73\,\text{mg/L}}{36.5\,\text{g/eq}} = \frac{0.073}{36.5} = 0.0020\,\text{N}$$

Total normality (acids are additive in equivalents):

$$N_{\text{total}} = 0.0040 + 0.0020 = 0.0060\,\text{N}$$

### Step (ii) — NaOH Required

For $\text{NaOH}$: $E = 40$ g/eq.

$$\text{Dose}_{\text{NaOH}} = N_{\text{total}} \times E_{\text{NaOH}} = 0.0060 \times 40 = 0.24\,\text{g/L} = 240\,\text{g/m}^3 = 0.24\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Neutralization Unit

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid block below maps the **physical sequence** of unit operations in an industrial neutralization train, the signal flow between sensors, controllers, and pumps, and the reagent-handling subsystem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InletA[Inlet Sump - Acidic Wastewater] --> ScreenA[Bar Screen and Grit Removal]
    ScreenA --> EQ1[Equalization Tank - HRT 6 to 12 hr]
    EQ1 --> PumpA[Transfer Pump P1]
    PumpA --> pHProbe1[Online pH Probe Node P1]
    pHProbe1 --> CSTR1[Stage 1 CSTR - Coarse Neutralization]
    CSTR1 --> pHProbe2[Online pH Probe Node P2]
    pHProbe2 --> CSTR2[Stage 2 CSTR - Fine Tuning]
    CSTR2 --> pHProbe3[Online pH Probe Node P3]
    pHProbe3 --> Discharge[Neutralized Effluent to Biological Stage]
    ReagentSilo[Lime or NaOH Reagent Silo] --> ReagentPump[Reagent Dosing Pump P2]
    ReagentPump --> CSTR1
    ReagentPump --> CSTR2
    CSTR1 -- feedback signal --> ControllerNode[PID Controller]
    CSTR2 -- feedback signal --> ControllerNode
    pHProbe1 -- measurement --> ControllerNode
    pHProbe2 -- measurement --> ControllerNode
    pHProbe3 -- measurement --> ControllerNode
    ControllerNode -- command --> ReagentPump
    SludgeLine[CSTR1 Bottom - CaSO4 Sludge] --> SludgeThick[Sludge Thickener]
    SludgeThick --> SludgeCake[Sludge Cake to Landfill]
```

## 4.2 Process Logic — Decision Tree for Reagent Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartNode[Influent pH Measurement] --> DecisionA{pH less than 6.5}
    DecisionA -- Yes --> DecisionB{Required Throughput}
    DecisionA -- No --> DecisionC{pH greater than 8.5}
    DecisionC -- Yes --> AcidPath[Use Sulfuric Acid or Flue Gas CO2]
    DecisionC -- No --> AcceptNode[Effluent within 6.5 to 8.5 - No Neutralization Required]
    DecisionB -- High Flow and Cost Sensitive --> LimePath[Use Lime CaOH2 - Cheap and Bulk]
    DecisionB -- Low Flow and Cleanliness Required --> NaOHPath[Use NaOH - Soluble and Clean]
    DecisionB -- Buffered System with Variable pH --> Na2CO3Path[Use Na2CO3 - Self Buffering with CO2 Release]
    LimePath --> StoichCheck[Stoichiometric Calculation - 1.10x Safety Factor]
    NaOHPath --> StoichCheck
    Na2CO3Path --> StoichCheck
    AcidPath --> StoichCheck
    StoichCheck --> MixDesign[Two Stage CSTR with 20 min HRT]
    MixDesign --> DischargeCheck{pH Probe Reads 6.5 to 7.5}
    DischargeCheck -- Yes --> FinalOut[Send to Biological Treatment]
    DischargeCheck -- No --> FeedbackLoop[Return to Stage 2 CSTR]
```

## 4.3 The Titration Curve Schematic (Block-level Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AcidReg[Strong Acid Region - pH below 2] --> BufferA[Buffer Plateau Region - pH 3 to 5]
    BufferA --> EquivPointA[First Equivalence Point]
    EquivPointA --> PlateauA[Plateau Region]
    PlateauA --> EquivPointB[Second Equivalence Point]
    EquivPointB --> BufferB[Buffer Plateau Region - pH 9 to 11]
    BufferB --> BaseReg[Strong Base Region - pH above 12]
    EquivPointA -.-> KeyConcept1[Steep pH Jump at Equivalence]
    EquivPointB -.-> KeyConcept2[Choose Indicator in Steep Region]
    KeyConcept1 --> KTU_Hint[KTU Tip - Phenolphthalein for NaOH H2SO4]
    KeyConcept2 --> KTU_Hint
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual takeaway for KTU viva:** A titration curve has a **flat plateau in the buffer region** and a **steep jump at the equivalence point**. Choosing the right indicator (phenolphthalein, methyl orange) is equivalent to "hitting" the steep part of the curve where $\text{pH}$ changes rapidly with the addition of one drop of titrant.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the valency factor $n$** is the #1 mark-deduction error. $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ has $n=2$, not $1$. Always write $E = M/n$ explicitly on the answer script.
> 2. **Mixing up Normality (N) and Molarity (M)** — for $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, $N = 2 \times M$. Students writing $N = M$ lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **In continuous-flow designs**, always report the **daily** mass of reagent (kg/day), not the instantaneous mass. KTU expects $24$-hour averaged answers.
> 4. **Forgetting the safety factor** ($1.05 \times$ to $1.20 \times$ stoichiometric) when the question says "design" — this costs the "design completeness" mark.
> 5. **Units** — never write "g" where "kg" is expected. KTU answer scripts get strict unit-marking.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define neutralization in wastewater engineering. Why is it classified as a pre-treatment process?**

**Model Answer:**

Neutralization is the chemical unit operation in which the hydrogen-ion concentration of an acidic effluent or the hydroxyl-ion concentration of an alkaline effluent is adjusted by adding a counter-reagent (alkali or acid respectively) so that the resulting $\text{pH}$ falls in the range $6.5$ to $7.5$ — the optimum for downstream biological processes. It is classified as **pre-treatment** because (i) biological reactors (activated sludge, biofilm reactors) are highly sensitive to $\text{pH}$ extremes, with microbial enzymatic activity collapsing outside $6.5 - 8.5$; (ii) acidic effluents corrode concrete and steel infrastructure; and (iii) regulatory discharge norms (CPCB / KSPCB) require a $\text{pH}$ of $6.5$ to $8.5$ for inland-surface-water discharge.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State any three chemical reagents used to neutralize acidic wastewater and write balanced equations for each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Lime** $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$: $\quad \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 \downarrow + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$
2. **Caustic Soda** $\text{NaOH}$: $\quad \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$
3. **Soda Ash** $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$: $\quad \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part-B question carries **14 marks** and is divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Below, the model question paper offers both Option A and Option B, of which the student answers **only one**.

### ⭐ Option A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **two-stage CSTR neutralization system** for a strong acid–strong base industrial wastewater. List the reagents commonly used and the criteria for reagent selection. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A two-stage CSTR (continuous stirred-tank reactor) neutralization system uses **two reactors in series** with online $\text{pH}$ measurement and PID-controlled reagent dosing. The first CSTR performs **coarse** $\text{pH}$ correction by bringing the effluent from a strongly acidic or alkaline $\text{pH}$ into the buffer-plateau region. The second CSTR performs **fine** adjustment to the target $6.5$ to $7.5$ band.

**Working principle:**
1. Acidic wastewater enters Stage 1 from the equalization tank.
2. A lime slurry or $\text{NaOH}$ solution is dosed under PID control using the $\text{pH}$ probe in Stage 1.
3. Stage 1 effluent flows by gravity to Stage 2 where a fine-tuning dose of reagent (often $\text{NaOH}$ for acidic streams) is added.
4. The final $\text{pH}$ probe in Stage 2 transmits to the controller, which adjusts the Stage-2 reagent pump.
5. Neutralized effluent ($\text{pH}\, 6.5 - 7.5$) is discharged to the biological treatment stage.
6. $\text{CaSO}_4$ sludge (when lime is used) settles at the CSTR bottom and is bled to a sludge thickener.

**Common reagents:** $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$, $\text{NaOH}$, $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ (for acid); $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, $\text{HCl}$, $\text{CO}_2$ (for alkali).

**Reagent selection criteria:** cost per kg, sludge production, solubility, buffer behaviour, availability, and safety. Lime is the cheapest but produces sludge; $\text{NaOH}$ is cleaner but costlier; $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ is self-buffering.

---

**Q. (b)** A pickling-industry wastewater flow is $250\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ with an acid strength of $0.05\,\text{N}$ (chiefly $\text{HCl}$). Design a **caustic-soda neutralization system**. Compute: (i) the daily mass of $\text{NaOH}$ at $1.05\times$ stoichiometry; (ii) the HRT in minutes if the neutralization tank volume is $4.0\,\text{m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $Q = 250\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$, $N_a = 0.05\,\text{N}$, $E_{\text{NaOH}} = 40\,\text{g/eq}$, safety factor $= 1.05$, $V_{\text{tank}} = 4.0\,\text{m}^3$.

**(i) Daily mass of $\text{NaOH}$:**

Equivalents of acid per day:

$$\text{eq}_{\text{acid}} = N_a \times V_w = 0.05 \times 250 = 12.5\,\text{eq/day}$$

Mass of $\text{NaOH}$ at stoichiometry:

$$m_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{12.5 \times 40}{1000} = 0.500\,\text{kg/day}$$

With $1.05\times$ safety:

$$m_{\text{design}} = 1.05 \times 0.500 = 0.525\,\text{kg/day}$$

**(ii) Hydraulic Retention Time:**

Convert flow to $\text{m}^3/\text{min}$:

$$Q = \frac{250}{24 \times 60} = 0.17361\,\text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\text{HRT} = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{4.0}{0.17361} = 23.04\,\text{min}$$

The designed HRT $\approx 23$ min lies in the typical $5 - 30$ min range for neutralization tanks — the design is acceptable.

---

### ⭐ Option B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** Describe the **two-point titration method** for determining the normality of an unknown acidic wastewater. Why is it preferred over a single-point titration in industrial practice? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **two-point titration method** uses two indicators — **methyl orange** (color change at $\text{pH} \approx 3.1 - 4.4$, detects the strong-acid equivalence point) and **phenolphthalein** (color change at $\text{pH} \approx 8.2 - 10$, detects the total acidity including weak acids).

**Procedure:**
1. Pipette $V_a = 25.0\,\text{mL}$ of wastewater into a $250\,\text{mL}$ conical flask.
2. Add 2–3 drops of **methyl orange** indicator. Titrate against standard $\text{NaOH}$ ($N_b = 0.10\,\text{N}$, say) until the color changes from red to yellow. Record $V_1$.
3. Repeat with a fresh $25.0\,\text{mL}$ sample, but this time add 2–3 drops of **phenolphthalein**. Titrate until the color changes from colorless to faint pink. Record $V_2$.
4. The difference $(V_2 - V_1)$ corresponds to the **weak-acid fraction**, and $V_1$ corresponds to the **strong-acid fraction** of the total acidity.

**Why preferred in industry:**
- Industrial wastewater contains a **mixture of strong and weak acids** (e.g., $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ + acetic acid from pickling + rinsing).
- A single indicator at a fixed $\text{pH}$ end-point cannot separately quantify these fractions.
- Two-point titration allows **complete characterization** of the acid mixture so the correct stoichiometric dose of neutralizing reagent can be computed for each fraction.

---

**Q. (b)** A pharmaceutical-industry alkaline effluent at $\text{pH} = 11.50$ and flow rate $Q = 800\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ is to be neutralized using $98\%$ commercial $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (density $1840\,\text{kg/m}^3$). Determine: (i) the daily volume of commercial acid required; (ii) the daily mass of $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ salt produced (assuming the alkali is pure $\text{NaOH}$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\text{pH} = 11.50 \Rightarrow [\text{H}^+] = 10^{-11.50} = 3.162 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{M}$ (negligible).
$Q = 800\,\text{m}^3/\text{day} = 800{,}000\,\text{L/day}$.

**Step 1 — Normality of the alkaline wastewater (from $\text{pOH}$):**

$$\text{pOH} = 14 - 11.50 = 2.50$$

$$[\text{OH}^-] = 10^{-2.50} = 3.162 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{M}$$

Since the alkali is $\text{NaOH}$ ($n=1$):

$$N_b = [\text{OH}^-] = 3.162 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalents of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ required per day:**

$$\text{eq}_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = N_b \times V_w = 3.162 \times 10^{-3} \times 800{,}000 = 2529.6\,\text{eq/day}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of pure $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (using $E = 49$ g/eq):**

$$m_{\text{pure}} = \frac{2529.6 \times 49}{1000} = 123.95\,\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of $98\%$ commercial $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$:**

$$m_{\text{commercial}} = \frac{123.95}{0.98} = 126.48\,\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 5 — Daily volume of commercial acid:**

$$V_{\text{acid}} = \frac{126.48\,\text{kg/day}}{1840\,\text{kg/m}^3} = 0.0687\,\text{m}^3/\text{day} = 68.74\,\text{L/day}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ produced:**

Reaction: $2\,\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

Moles of $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ per day = eq/day of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ / 2 = $2529.6 / 2 = 1264.8\,\text{mol/day}$.

$$m_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4} = 1264.8 \times 142 = 179{,}602\,\text{g/day} \approx 179.6\,\text{kg/day}$$

where $M_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4} = 142\,\text{g/mol}$.

**Summary answers:** (i) $V_{\text{acid}} = 68.7\,\text{L/day}$; (ii) $m_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4} = 179.6\,\text{kg/day}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist (last-minute KTU prep):**

- ✅ Neutralization = adjustment of $\text{pH}$ to $6.5$–$7.5$ using a counter-reagent.
- ✅ Core reaction template: $\text{Acid} + \text{Base} \rightarrow \text{Salt} + \text{Water}$.
- ✅ $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$; $\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = 14$ (at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$).
- ✅ $K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 10^{-14}$ at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$.
- ✅ Equivalent weight $E = M / n$ where $n$ = number of replaceable $\text{H}^+$ (acid) or $\text{OH}^-$ (base).
- ✅ For $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$: $E = 49$ g/eq, $n=2$. For $\text{HCl}$: $E = 36.5$ g/eq, $n=1$. For $\text{NaOH}$: $E = 40$ g/eq. For $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$: $E = 37$ g/eq. For $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$: $E = 53$ g/eq.
- ✅ Stoichiometric equality: $N_a V_a = N_b V_b$.
- ✅ Reagent dose (g) = $N_a V_w E_b$ ; in kg, divide by $1000$.
- ✅ Common reagents — acidic WW: lime ($\text{Ca(OH)}_2$), $\text{NaOH}$, $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$. Alkaline WW: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, $\text{HCl}$, flue-gas $\text{CO}_2$.
- ✅ Lime is **cheapest but sludge-producing**; $\text{NaOH}$ is **cleaner but costlier**; $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ is **self-buffering via $\text{CO}_2$ evolution**.
- ✅ Enthalpy of strong-acid–strong-base neutralization $\approx -57.1\,\text{kJ/mol}$ — exothermic, so temperature rise must be designed for.
- ✅ Two-stage CSTR is the industry standard: coarse $\text{pH}$ correction in Stage 1, fine-tuning in Stage 2.
- ✅ Tank HRT: $5$ to $30$ min typical; mixing by mechanical impeller or hydraulic jump.
- ✅ Two-point titration (methyl orange + phenolphthalein) characterizes **strong + weak acid fractions** of industrial wastewater.
- ✅ **Design safety factor** $1.05\times$ to $1.20\times$ stoichiometric is mandatory for residual-acid / residual-base safety.
- ✅ **Regulatory discharge $\text{pH}$** for inland surface water = $6.5$–$8.5$ (CPCB).
- ✅ **Commonly missed points in KTU scripts:** forgetting $n$, mixing $N$ with $M$, ignoring temperature correction for $K_w$, omitting safety factor, wrong units.
- ✅ **Engineering intuition:** neutralization is a **buffer-plateau-defeating** operation — the steeper the titration curve around $\text{pH} 7$, the easier the control; strong acid + strong base gives the steepest curve and easiest control.
- ✅ **Real-world analogy:** baking soda into a lemon-juice pool — excess base over-corrects, so two-stage CSTR is the "fine-tuning" step analogous to "taste-testing" the water.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equalization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equalization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Equalization** is a preliminary/primary treatment unit operation in wastewater engineering that employs a holding basin (equalization basin) to dampen temporal variations in influent characteristics—specifically **hydraulic flow rate (Q)** and **pollutant concentration (C)**—such that the downstream biological treatment processes receive a quasi-steady, homogenized feed. It functions as a **hydraulic and mass-load buffer** between the variable-strength raw wastewater source and the sensitive downstream biokinetic reactors.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, equalization is classified as a **flow and load-smoothing unit** placed downstream of screening and grit removal but upstream of primary sedimentation and biological reactors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition for Exams:**
> *"Equalization is the provision of surge storage or holding capacity in a wastewater treatment system to allow flow and constituent concentrations to be equalized over a working period, thereby minimizing the effects of hydraulic and organic shock loads on downstream treatment units."*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pouring a **bucket of water with inconsistent flow** (sometimes a trickle, sometimes a gush) into a **funnel feeding a sensitive machine** (the downstream reactor). Without the funnel, the machine would either run dry (during trickles) or overflow (during gushes) and produce inconsistent output. The funnel (equalization basin) **collects the erratic supply, holds it, and lets it drain out at a steady, controlled rate**. The machine downstream receives a uniform supply regardless of the upstream chaos.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Variants of Equalization:**
> 1. **Flow Equalization (Hydraulic Smoothing):** Dampens fluctuations in *flow rate* alone.
> 2. **Concentration/Quality Equalization:** Dampens fluctuations in *pollutant strength* (BOD, COD, pH, toxic substances, temperature). Always includes flow smoothing as a subset benefit.
> 
> In practice, **concentration equalization with flow smoothing is the design goal** for industrial and combined wastewaters.

### Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Typical Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** **2 to 8 hours** for municipal; **8 to 24 hours** for industrial wastewater with high diurnal variation.
- **Basin Depth:** **3 to 5 m** (standard).
- **Freeboard:** **0.5 to 1.0 m**.
- **Mixing Power (Velocity Gradient G):** **0.3 to 1.0 s⁻¹** for suspended-growth mixing.
- **pH Adjustment Range:** Often coupled with equalization to **6.5 – 8.0** before biological treatment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Function:** Equalization does **NOT** remove pollutants by itself (only marginal BOD reduction of **10–30%** occurs incidentally due to biological activity during long HRTs). Its role is purely **conditioning/buffering**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives of Equalization (Operational Logic)

The bullet-list below represents the **core engineering rationale**—the "WHY" behind every equalization basin in a treatment plant:

- **Dampen Peak Flows:** Prevents hydraulic shock/overloading of downstream biological reactors and clarifiers during high-flow hours.
- **Smooth Pollutant Concentrations:** Prevents organic/inhibitor shock loads that can disrupt microbial populations in activated sludge, trickling filters, or anaerobic digesters.
- **Improve Treatment Efficiency:** Steady feed enables biological reactors to operate at their **optimal design point (F/M ratio)**, raising overall removal efficiency.
- **Reduce Chemical Dosing Variability:** pH adjustment, coagulant dosing, and chlorination become more uniform → lower chemical costs.
- **Provide Operational Flexibility:** Acts as a holding tank during equipment maintenance or upset conditions upstream.
- **Mitigate Toxic Shocks:** Especially critical for industrial effluents containing inhibitory compounds (heavy metals, cyanides, phenols).

### 2.2 Types / Configurations of Equalization Basins

| Type | Mechanism | Best Suited For | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **In-line (Online) Equalization** | All flow passes through basin; outflow is pumped out at controlled rate | Industrial wastewater; high peak flows | Large volume; high pumping cost |
| **Off-line (Side-line) Equalization** | Only excess flow (above average) is diverted to basin during peaks | Municipal plants; moderate variation | Does NOT smooth concentration; only flow |
| **Variable Volume Basin** | Basin level fluctuates; gravity discharge (overflow weir) | Gravity-fed systems; high upstream variation | Requires large depth; risk of solids settling |
| **Constant Volume Basin (with Pumps)** | Basin level held nearly constant; inflow = outflow via pump control | Most efficient concentration equalization | Higher O&M cost (pump maintenance) |
| **Aerated Equalization** | Basin fitted with diffused aeration to keep contents aerobic | High-strength industrial wastewater (prevents septicity) | Higher energy cost |

### 2.3 Mixing and Aeration Requirements

Equalization basins require **continuous mixing** to:
1. Prevent settling of suspended solids (scum/sludge buildup at bottom).
2. Maintain uniform concentration throughout the basin.
3. Provide oxygen if aerobic conditions are desired (to prevent odor/septicity).

**Power Requirement for Mixing (Camp-Stein Equation):**

$$P = G^2 \cdot V \cdot \mu$$

Where:
- $P$ = Power input (W or J/s)
- $G$ = Mean velocity gradient (s⁻¹); typically **0.3 to 1.0 s⁻¹**
- $V$ = Volume of basin (m³)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of wastewater (N·s/m² ≈ 0.001 at 20°C for water)

> **Real-world utility:** For a 500 m³ basin with G = 0.5 s⁻¹, μ = 0.001 N·s/m²:
> $P = (0.5)^2 \times 500 \times 0.001 = 0.125$ kW. This is a tractable, low-power mixing requirement.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula Number | Equation | Application / Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F-1** | $V = \int (Q_{in} - Q_{out}) \, dt$ | Volume of variable-volume basin (mass balance) | $V$ = volume (m³); $Q$ = flow (m³/s or m³/h) |
| **F-2** | $V_{max} = \sum (Q_{in,i} - \bar{Q}) \cdot \Delta t$ | Discrete summation form of F-1 | $\bar{Q}$ = average inflow (m³/h) |
| **F-3** | $\theta = V / Q$ | Hydraulic residence time (HRT) | $\theta$ = HRT (h); $V$ in m³; $Q$ in m³/h |
| **F-4** | $C_{out} = \dfrac{C_{in}}{1 + k\theta}$ | CSTR effluent concentration (first-order reaction, e.g., BOD decay) | $C$ = concentration (mg/L); $k$ = rate constant (h⁻¹) |
| **F-5** | $C_{out} = C_{in} \cdot e^{-k\theta}$ | Plug-flow (PFR) effluent concentration | Same as F-4 |
| **F-6** | $\bar{C} = \dfrac{\sum Q_i C_i}{\sum Q_i}$ | Flow-weighted mean concentration (mass balance) | Flow-weighted average |
| **F-7** | $P = G^2 V \mu$ | Mixing power (Camp-Stein) | $G$ (s⁻¹); $V$ (m³); $\mu$ (N·s/m²) |
| **F-8** | $\dfrac{\sigma_{out}}{\sigma_{in}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi f \theta)^2}}$ | Smoothing ratio for periodic variation (CSTR transfer function) | $f$ = frequency of variation (h⁻¹); $\sigma$ = standard deviation |
| **F-9** | $V = Q \cdot \theta_{design}$ | Basic design volume using chosen HRT | Direct product |
| **F-10** | $A = V / d$ | Surface area from volume and design depth | $A$ = area (m²); $d$ = depth (m) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** CSTR formula (F-4) gives **higher effluent concentration** than PFR (F-5) for the same HRT. This is why **CSTR equalization basins discharge at a more uniform (less varying) concentration**—they are inherently dampers. This paradox is a common KTU question!

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Wastewater Treatment Plants (IWWTPs):** Pharmaceutical, food-processing, textile, and petrochemical industries produce highly variable waste streams. Equalization is **mandatory** to prevent shock loading of biological reactors.
- **Combined Sewer Systems:** During storm events, equalization basins (often underground or covered) hold excess combined sewage to prevent CSO (Combined Sewer Overflow) discharges to receiving waters.
- **Municipal STPs:** For plants receiving significant industrial discharge or serving communities with widely varying morning/evening flows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Volume of a Variable-Volume Equalization Basin (Cumulative Flow Method)

This is the most commonly tested derivation in KTU exams. It comes from the principle of **conservation of mass** for a control volume with variable outflow.

**Starting Point — Mass Balance (Water):**

The volume stored in the basin at any time $t$ equals the cumulative difference between inflow and outflow:

$$V(t) = \int_0^t \left[ Q_{in}(t) - Q_{out}(t) \right] dt$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

**Step 1:** Consider a time interval $\Delta t$ during which $Q_{in}$ exceeds the desired constant outflow $\bar{Q}$ (the design average flow to downstream).

**Step 2:** The *excess* volume accumulated during this interval is:

$$\Delta V_i = \left( Q_{in,i} - \bar{Q} \right) \cdot \Delta t_i$$

**Step 3:** The basin must store this excess volume to prevent overloading downstream. So the total required volume is the **sum (or integral) of all positive deviations** from $\bar{Q}$ over the design period (typically 24 hours for diurnal variation):

$$V_{max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \max \left[ 0, \; (Q_{in,i} - \bar{Q}) \cdot \Delta t_i \right]$$

Equivalently, in continuous form:

$$V_{max} = \int_0^{T} \max \left[ 0, \; Q_{in}(t) - \bar{Q} \right] dt$$

**Step 4:** Practically, this is solved **graphically** using a **cumulative inflow curve** plotted against time. The maximum ordinate between the cumulative inflow curve and a straight line of slope $\bar{Q}$ (drawn tangent to the curve) gives $V_{max}$.

**Worked Example for Clarity:**

Suppose diurnal inflow data over 24 hours is given in 2-hour intervals:

| Time (h) | $Q_{in}$ (m³/h) | $\bar{Q}$ (m³/h) | $(Q_{in} - \bar{Q}) \cdot \Delta t$ (m³) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0–2 | 50 | 100 | −100 |
| 2–4 | 80 | 100 | −40 |
| 4–6 | 150 | 100 | +100 |
| 6–8 | 250 | 100 | +300 |
| 8–10 | 300 | 100 | +400 |
| 10–12 | 220 | 100 | +240 |
| 12–14 | 180 | 100 | +160 |
| 14–16 | 130 | 100 | +60 |
| 16–18 | 90 | 100 | −20 |
| 18–20 | 70 | 100 | −60 |
| 20–22 | 60 | 100 | −80 |
| 22–24 | 50 | 100 | −100 |

**Cumulative sum of $(Q_{in} - \bar{Q}) \cdot \Delta t$** (running total = storage required):

| Time (h) | Storage (m³) |
|---|---|
| 2 | −100 |
| 4 | −140 |
| 6 | −40 |
| 8 | +260 |
| 10 | +660 |
| 12 | +900 ← **PEAK** |
| 14 | +1060 ← **ACTUAL PEAK** |
| 16 | +1120 ← **PEAK** |
| 18 | +1100 |
| 20 | +1040 |
| 22 | +960 |
| 24 | +860 (final storage = 0 if designed correctly; offset is start-up residual) |

The **maximum storage required = 1120 m³**. This is the design volume of the equalization basin (after adding a safety factor of 1.1 to 1.25 for solids accumulation and freeboard).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: CSTR Equalization — Effluent Concentration Formula

**Starting Point — Steady-State Mass Balance on a Completely Mixed Equalization Basin:**

$$Q \cdot C_{in} = Q \cdot C_{out} + r \cdot V$$

Where $r$ is the rate of pollutant removal per unit volume. Assuming **first-order kinetics** (typical for BOD/COD decay):

$$r = k \cdot C_{out}$$

(The rate is expressed in terms of effluent concentration since the basin is CSTR — perfectly mixed.)

**Substitution:**

$$Q \cdot C_{in} = Q \cdot C_{out} + k \cdot C_{out} \cdot V$$

**Algebraic Manipulation:**

$$Q \cdot C_{in} = C_{out} \left( Q + kV \right)$$

$$C_{out} = \frac{Q \cdot C_{in}}{Q + kV}$$

**Divide numerator and denominator by Q:**

$$C_{out} = \frac{C_{in}}{1 + kV/Q}$$

**Substitute $\theta = V/Q$ (HRT):**

$$\boxed{C_{out} = \frac{C_{in}}{1 + k\theta}}$$

This is the standard KTU formula. The CSTR acts as a **low-pass filter** — high-frequency oscillations in $C_{in}$ are damped to yield a smoother $C_{out}$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Variance Reduction (Smoothing Efficiency)

The CSTR acts as a **first-order low-pass filter** for concentration variations. For a sinusoidal (or any periodic) variation of frequency $f$ in the input, the amplitude of the output variation is reduced by a factor given by the transfer function magnitude:

$$\left\vert H(j\omega) \right\vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega \theta)^2}}$$

Where $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency. The smoothing ratio (output standard deviation / input standard deviation) is:

$$\frac{\sigma_{out}}{\sigma_{in}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi f \theta)^2}}$$

**Key Insight for Exams:**

- If $\theta$ is large or $f$ is high, the ratio → 0 (heavy smoothing).
- If $\theta$ is small or $f$ is low, the ratio → 1 (no smoothing).
- **A 24-hour diurnal variation** ($f = 1/24$ h⁻¹) with $\theta = 8$ h gives:

$$\frac{\sigma_{out}}{\sigma_{in}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi/3)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 4.39}} = 0.43$$

So **57% smoothing** of the diurnal variation is achieved.

### 3.4 Design Volume Selection — Engineering Heuristics (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Design Criterion | Formula / Rule | Typical KTU Value |
|---|---|---|
| Volume from HRT | $V = Q \cdot \theta$ | $\theta = 4$ to 8 h (flow-only); $\theta = 8$ to 24 h (concentration) |
| Volume from cumulative flow (graphical) | $V_{max}$ from cumulative curve | Maximum ordinate between curve and tangent |
| Volume from peaking factor | $V = Q_{avg} \cdot (PF - 1) \cdot t_{peak}$ | PF = peaking factor; $t_{peak}$ = duration of peak flow |
| Volume from mass balance (concentration) | $V$ sized to meet target $\sigma_{out}/\sigma_{in}$ | Iterative calculation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Equalization Basin in a Typical WWTP Flow Train

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Inlet Sewer / Collection System] --> B[Bar Screen]
    B --> C[Grit Chamber]
    C --> D[Influent Flow Distribution Box]
    D --> E[Equalization Basin with Aerators and Mixers]
    E --> F[Pump Well Constant Outflow]
    F --> G[Primary Clarifier]
    G --> H[Biological Reactor ASP / TF / SBR]
    H --> I[Secondary Clarifier]
    I --> J[Disinfection]
    J --> K[Treated Effluent to Receiving Water]

    E1[Inlet Flow Probe Q in] -.-> E
    E2[Inlet Concentration Probe C in] -.-> E
    E3[Outlet Flow Probe Q out] -.-> E
    E4[Basin Level Sensor] -.-> E

    E --> E5[Blower for Aeration Mixing]
    E --> E6[Submersible Mixers for Solids Suspension]
    E --> E7[Scum Removal Skimmer at Top]
    E --> E8[Sludge Draw off at Bottom]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Conceptual Operating Modes of Equalization Basin

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VAR[Variable Volume Mode]
        direction TB
        I1[Inflow Variable] --> B1[Equalization Basin with Variable Level]
        B1 --> O1[Outflow Constant via Overflow Weir]
    end

    subgraph CON[Constant Volume Mode]
        direction TB
        I2[Inflow Variable] --> B2[Equalization Basin Constant Level]
        B2 --> P1[Variable Speed Pump]
        P1 --> O2[Outflow Variable Matches Inflow]
    end

    subgraph OFF[Off line Mode]
        direction TB
        I3[Main Stream Bypass] --> B3[Only Excess to Basin]
        B3 --> O3[Returns to Main Stream]
    end
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description (Text-Based for Reference)

For KTU descriptive answers, the standard schematic includes:

- **Inlet zone** with diffusion baffle to dissipate inlet momentum.
- **Main basin** with mechanical mixers (submersible) or diffused aeration grid at bottom.
- **Outlet zone** with a low-velocity withdrawal well or floating decanter to prevent short-circuiting and scum carry-over.
- **Scum removal** at top via skimmer.
- **Sludge drawoff** at bottom via hopper and sludge pump.
- **Instrumentation:** pH probe, DO probe, level sensor, flow meters, and ideally a TOC/COD probe for feedback control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Short-Circuiting Note:** Inlet and outlet should be located on **opposite ends** of the basin, with **baffle walls or diffuser curtains** to maximize the actual HRT approaching the theoretical HRT. Short-circuiting is the #1 cause of poor equalization performance in poorly designed basins.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define equalization. List the objectives of providing equalization in a wastewater treatment plant.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Equalization is a unit operation that employs a holding basin to dampen temporal variations in hydraulic flow rate and pollutant concentration of wastewater, so that downstream treatment processes receive a uniform feed.

**Objectives (1.5 marks — any 3 of the following):**
1. To dampen peak flows and prevent hydraulic shock on downstream units.
2. To equalize pollutant concentrations (BOD, COD, pH, toxicants) to prevent organic/inhibitor shock.
3. To improve the efficiency of biological treatment by allowing steady-state operation.
4. To reduce variability in chemical dosing (e.g., pH adjustment, chlorination).
5. To provide buffer storage for operational flexibility during upstream upsets.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*Differentiate between in-line and off-line equalization basins.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | In-line (Online) Equalization | Off-line (Side-line) Equalization |
|---|---|---|
| Flow path | Entire wastewater stream passes through basin | Only excess (peak) flow is diverted to basin |
| Concentration smoothing | Yes (both flow and concentration equalized) | No (concentration not smoothed) |
| Volume required | Large (must hold full diurnal flow) | Smaller (only peak excess) |
| Typical use | Industrial wastewater; high variation | Municipal plants with moderate peaks |
| Operational cost | Higher (pumping large volumes) | Lower |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with neat sketches the different types of equalization basins. Discuss the design criteria for sizing an equalization basin.

**(b) [7 marks)** An industry discharges wastewater with the following diurnal flow pattern. Design the volume of a variable-volume equalization basin assuming the average outflow rate equals the 24-hour mean inflow rate.

| Time (h) | 0–4 | 4–8 | 8–12 | 12–16 | 16–20 | 20–24 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Q_{in}$ (m³/h) | 100 | 300 | 500 | 400 | 200 | 100 |

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

**Types of Equalization Basins (3.5 marks):**

1. **Variable Volume Basin:** Basin level fluctuates with inflow. Outflow is controlled by a fixed weir or gravity discharge. The basin volume is the cumulative excess above the average outflow.
2. **Constant Volume Basin (with Pumps):** Basin level maintained nearly constant; variable-speed pumps match the outflow to the inflow in real time. Provides maximum concentration equalization.
3. **In-line vs Off-line:** In-line processes the entire stream through the basin; off-line diverts only the excess above the desired downstream flow rate.
4. **Aerated vs Mechanically Mixed:** Aerated basins use diffused air (also maintains aerobic conditions for high-strength waste); mechanically mixed basins use submersible mixers (lower energy for low-strength waste).

**Design Criteria (3.5 marks):**

1. **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** 2–8 h for flow equalization; 8–24 h for concentration equalization in industrial applications.
2. **Volume Determination:** From cumulative inflow curve (graphical method) or via numerical integration $V = \sum (Q_{in} - \bar{Q}) \cdot \Delta t$.
3. **Basin Geometry:** Depth 3–5 m, freeboard 0.5–1.0 m, length:width ≥ 2:1 to minimize short-circuiting.
4. **Mixing:** Velocity gradient G = 0.3–1.0 s⁻¹; power calculated from Camp-Stein equation.
5. **Aeration (if aerobic):** Air supply ≥ 0.005–0.01 m³ air/m³ wastewater·min for odor control.
6. **Inlet/Outlet:** Diffuser at inlet, submerged withdrawal at outlet; baffle walls to prevent short-circuiting.
7. **Solids Management:** Bottom sloped (1:50 to 1:100) to hopper for sludge removal.
8. **Instrumentation:** Level sensor, pH probe, DO probe, flow meters for monitoring and control.

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Calculate average flow $\bar{Q}$ (1 mark):**

$$\bar{Q} = \frac{1}{T} \sum Q_{in,i} \cdot \Delta t = \frac{100+300+500+400+200+100}{6} \times \frac{1}{4 \text{ h unit}}$$

$$\text{Total volume in 24 h} = (100 + 300 + 500 + 400 + 200 + 100) \times 4 = 6400 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\bar{Q} = 6400 / 24 = 266.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 2: Compute $(Q_{in} - \bar{Q}) \cdot \Delta t$ for each interval (2 marks):**

| Interval (h) | $Q_{in}$ (m³/h) | $Q_{in} - \bar{Q}$ (m³/h) | $\Delta V$ (m³) | Cumulative $V$ (m³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0–4 | 100 | −166.67 | −666.67 | −666.67 |
| 4–8 | 300 | +33.33 | +133.33 | −533.33 |
| 8–12 | 500 | +233.33 | +933.33 | **+400.00** |
| 12–16 | 400 | +133.33 | +533.33 | +933.33 ← **PEAK** |
| 16–20 | 200 | −66.67 | −266.67 | +666.67 |
| 20–24 | 100 | −166.67 | −666.67 | 0.00 ✓ |

**Step 3: Identify maximum cumulative volume (1 mark):**

$$V_{max} = 933.33 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Apply safety factor (1 mark):**

$$V_{design} = 1.20 \times 933.33 = 1120 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Determine dimensions (1 mark):**

With assumed depth $d = 4$ m and freeboard 0.5 m:
$$A = V_{design} / d = 1120 / 4 = 280 \text{ m}^2$$
Adopt rectangular basin: 20 m × 14 m, with length:width ratio 1.43:1 (close to 2:1 — may extend to 20 m × 14 m × 4 m depth).

**Step 6: Mixing power (1 mark):**

$$P = G^2 V \mu = (0.5)^2 \times 1120 \times 0.001 = 0.28 \text{ kW}$$

Adequate; specify two 0.15 kW submersible mixers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the steady-state effluent concentration equation for a Completely Mixed Flow Equalization Basin assuming first-order reaction kinetics. Explain how a CSTR equalization basin acts as a "low-pass filter" for concentration variations.

**(b) [7 marks]** A wastewater treatment plant receives industrial effluent with a BOD varying sinusoidally between 200 mg/L and 600 mg/L over a 24-hour cycle (i.e., amplitude 200 mg/L, mean 400 mg/L). The equalization basin has a volume of 2000 m³ and receives a constant inflow rate of 250 m³/h. The BOD decay constant is k = 0.05 h⁻¹ (first-order). Calculate: (i) The HRT of the basin. (ii) The smoothing ratio $\sigma_{out}/\sigma_{in}$ at the diurnal frequency. (iii) The amplitude of BOD variation in the effluent.

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

The starting mass balance for a CSTR with first-order reaction is:

$$Q C_{in} = Q C_{out} + k C_{out} V$$

(The reaction rate is $kC_{out}$ per unit volume, multiplied by V gives total removal rate; this is the CSTR assumption because the entire basin is at the effluent concentration.)

Rearranging:

$$Q C_{in} = C_{out} (Q + kV)$$

$$C_{out} = \frac{Q C_{in}}{Q + kV} = \frac{C_{in}}{1 + kV/Q}$$

Substituting $\theta = V/Q$:

$$\boxed{C_{out} = \frac{C_{in}}{1 + k\theta}}$$

**Low-Pass Filter Behavior (3 marks):**

A CSTR's transfer function for concentration (treating it as a first-order system) is:

$$H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + \theta s}$$

For sinusoidal input of frequency $f$ (with $s = j\omega = j2\pi f$):

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi f \theta)^2}}$$

This means:
- **Low-frequency (slow) variations** pass through with little attenuation.
- **High-frequency (rapid) variations** are strongly damped (smoothing ratio → 0).
- Therefore, the CSTR acts as a **low-pass filter**: rapid, sharp spikes in influent concentration are smoothed out, while slow trends pass through nearly unchanged.

For a periodic influent $C_{in}(t) = \bar{C} + A \cos(2\pi f t)$, the effluent becomes:

$$C_{out}(t) = \bar{C}_{out} + A \cdot \vert H(j\omega) \vert \cos(2\pi f t - \phi)$$

where $\bar{C}_{out} = \bar{C}_{in}/(1+k\theta)$ and $\phi = \tan^{-1}(2\pi f \theta)$.

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $V = 2000$ m³
- $Q = 250$ m³/h
- $k = 0.05$ h⁻¹
- Diurnal cycle → $f = 1/24$ h⁻¹
- $A_{in} = 200$ mg/L (amplitude)

**(i) HRT (1 mark):**

$$\theta = V/Q = 2000 / 250 = 8 \text{ h}$$

**(ii) Smoothing Ratio (3 marks):**

$$\frac{\sigma_{out}}{\sigma_{in}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi f \theta)^2}}$$

Compute $2\pi f \theta$:

$$2\pi f \theta = 2\pi \times (1/24) \times 8 = \frac{16\pi}{24} = \frac{2\pi}{3} \approx 2.094$$

Square it:

$$(2\pi f \theta)^2 \approx 4.386$$

Smoothing ratio:

$$\frac{\sigma_{out}}{\sigma_{in}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 4.386}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5.386}} = \frac{1}{2.321} \approx 0.431$$

**(iii) Effluent Amplitude (3 marks):**

$$A_{out} = A_{in} \times \frac{\sigma_{out}}{\sigma_{in}} = 200 \times 0.431 = 86.2 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Effluent mean BOD (extra, for completeness):**

$$\bar{C}_{out} = \frac{\bar{C}_{in}}{1 + k\theta} = \frac{400}{1 + 0.05 \times 8} = \frac{400}{1.4} = 285.7 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Effluent BOD variation:** from $285.7 - 86.2 = 199.5$ mg/L to $285.7 + 86.2 = 371.9$ mg/L (a range of only ~170 mg/L instead of the influent 400 mg/L swing).

**Conclusion:** The equalization basin reduces the BOD variation amplitude from ±200 mg/L to ±86.2 mg/L — a **~57% reduction** in fluctuation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in F-1:** Students often mix seconds and hours. Always use **uniform time units** for $Q$ and $\Delta t$. KTU markers deduct **0.5–1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Confusing CSTR vs PFR Kinetics:** CSTR uses $C_{out}$ in the rate expression; PFR uses $C_{in}$. Markers deduct **2 marks** if a student uses the PFR formula for a CSTR equalization problem.
> 3. **Forgetting the Safety Factor in Volume Design:** Always apply a factor of **1.1 to 1.25** for sludge accumulation and uncertainty. Bare $V_{max}$ without factor → **−0.5 mark**.
> 4. **Inlet/Outlet Short-Circuiting Ignored:** Marks are lost when students don't mention **baffle walls or diffuser design** to prevent short-circuiting. Always add a sentence on hydraulic flow pattern.
> 5. **Mixing Power Calculation:** Some students use **$P = G \cdot V \cdot \mu$** (incorrect) instead of **$P = G^2 \cdot V \cdot \mu$** (correct — Camp-Stein equation). This is a **1–2 mark** penalty.
> 6. **Forgetting Freeboard:** Volume calculation should not include the **0.5–1.0 m freeboard** in working volume; it is a separate addition. Marks lost for over-designing.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Equalization (Module 2):**
> 
> - **Definition:** Holding basin to dampen flow and concentration variations.
> - **Purpose:** Prevent hydraulic, organic, and toxic shock loads on downstream biological units.
> - **HRT ranges:** 2–8 h (municipal/flow); 8–24 h (industrial/concentration).
> - **Volume design:** Cumulative inflow curve (graphical) or $\sum (Q_{in} - \bar{Q}) \Delta t$ (numerical). Apply 1.1–1.25 safety factor.
> - **CSTR Effluent formula:** $C_{out} = C_{in} / (1 + k\theta)$ — this is the **most-tested** formula.
> - **PFR comparison:** $C_{out} = C_{in} \cdot e^{-k\theta}$ — gives lower $C_{out}$ than CSTR for the same $\theta$.
> - **Smoothing ratio:** $\sigma_{out}/\sigma_{in} = 1 / \sqrt{1 + (2\pi f \theta)^2}$ — high $\theta$ or high $f$ → strong smoothing.
> - **Mixing power:** $P = G^2 V \mu$; $G = 0.3–1.0$ s⁻¹.
> - **Configurations:** In-line vs Off-line; Variable volume vs Constant volume (pumped).
> - **Geometry:** Depth 3–5 m, L:B ≥ 2:1, baffles for anti-short-circuiting, bottom slope 1:50–1:100.
> - **Coupled units:** Often combined with pH adjustment and nutrient (N, P) supplementation.
> - **Marginal benefits:** 10–30% incidental BOD removal, prevention of septicity/odor (if aerated).
> - **Instruments needed:** Level sensor, flow meter, pH probe, DO probe, ideally online TOC/COD probe.
> - **Real-world value:** Essential for industrial WWTP design; saves downstream biological units from failure; reduces chemical costs.
> - **Common KTU exam trap:** Forgetting the difference between *flow* equalization (smoothing Q only) and *concentration* equalization (smoothing both Q and C).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sedimentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sedimentation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Sedimentation** is a fundamental **physical (gravity-based) unit operation** in biological wastewater treatment wherein **suspended particulate matter** is separated from the liquid phase (wastewater) under the influence of the **gravitational force field**. It exploits the density difference between the solid particles (ρₛ > ρw) and the surrounding fluid medium. In the strict context of the KTU 2024 Biological Wastewater Treatment syllabus (PEBBT526), sedimentation is classified by its **position in the treatment train** and by the **settling behavior of the particles**. ### Classification by Position in Treatment Train | Stage | Name | Function | Typical Removal | |---|---|---|---| | Pre-primary | Grit Chamber | Removes inorganic grit (sand) | Inorganics only | | Primary | Primary Clarifier | Removes settleable organic & inorganic SS | **50–70% SS, 25–40% BOD** | | Secondary | Secondary Clarifier | Separates biological flocs from treated effluent | **90–99% of biological solids** | | Tertiary | Tertiary Clarifier | Final polishing | High clarit | ### Classification by Particle Settling Behavior (Type I – IV) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Four Types of Settling:** > 1. **Type I – Discrete Particle Settling:** Individual, non-flocculating particles (e.g., sand, grit). > 2. **Type II – Flocculent Settling:** Particles agglomerate during settling, increasing mass & velocity (e.g., primary clarifier sludge). > 3. **Type III – Zone / Hindered Settling:** Mass settling of a thick slurry, where particles settle as a blanket (e.g., activated sludge thickening). > 4. **Type IV – Compression Settling:** Mechanical compression of sludge at the bottom under its own weight (e.g., gravity thickener bottom zone). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"Imagine a glass of muddy river water left on a windowsill."** > > After a few hours, you observe a clear layer of water at the top and a dense, dark layer of silt at the bottom. **Sedimentation is precisely this natural phenomenon — engineered and accelerated inside massive concrete tanks called clarifiers.** > > In a wastewater plant, we don't have hours — we have **2 to 3 hours**. So engineers design tanks with **low horizontal velocity**, **laminar flow**, and **scraper mechanisms** that continuously remove the settled sludge from the bottom without disturbing the settling zone above. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **Density of water (ρw):** **1000 kg/m³** (at 4 °C) / **998.2 kg/m³** (at 20 °C) - **Dynamic viscosity of water (μ):** **1.002 × 10⁻³ N·s/m²** (at 20 °C) - **Kinematic viscosity (ν):** **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** (at 20 °C) - **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²** - **Standard SOR range:** **24 – 40 m³/m²/day** (primary), **15 – 30 m³/m²/day** (secondary) - **Standard detention time (θ):** **2 – 3 hours** (primary), **1.5 – 2.5 hours** (secondary) --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle A particle suspended in quiescent water is subjected to three primary forces: 1. **Gravitational Force (Fg):** Pulls the particle downward. 2. **Buoyant Force (Fb):** Equal to the weight of displaced fluid (Archimedes' principle) – acts upward. 3. **Hydrodynamic Drag Force (Fd):** Opposes motion, governed by **Stokes' Law** (laminar regime) or **Newton's Law** (turbulent regime). The **net settling velocity (Vs)** is reached when these forces balance, yielding a terminal velocity. This is the basis of all sedimentation design. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework (Why & How) ### Step 1 – Particle Identification Identify whether the particle is **discrete, flocculent, or zone-settling**. This dictates whether **Type I** analysis (Stokes' Law) or **Type II / III** settling column tests are required. ### Step 2 – Velocity Determination For **Type I settling**, compute **Stokes' settling velocity (Vs)**. This is the **minimum surface overflow rate** the clarifier must NOT exceed if the particle is to be 100% removed. ### Step 3 – Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) Calculation SOR is the **defining design parameter** for clarifiers. It represents the hydraulic loading per unit surface area. ### Step 4 – Weir Loading Rate (WLR) Ensures the effluent withdrawal does not resuspend settled sludge. Excess WLR causes hydraulic short-circuiting. ### Step 5 – Detention Time Check Retention time must be sufficient for the slowest-settling target particle to reach the sludge hopper. ### Step 6 – Sludge Handling Sludge is scraped to a central/floor hopper by slowly moving scraper arms (0.5–1.0 m/min) and pumped out. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following formulas constitute the high-yield mathematical core for KTU 2024 board examinations on Sedimentation.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \mu}$ | **Stokes' Law** (laminar, Re < 1) | m/s | Type I settling | | 2 | $V_s = \dfrac{4 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_w}$ | **Newton's Law** (turbulent) | m/s | Type I, coarse particles | | 3 | $SOR = \dfrac{Q}{A_s} = \dfrac{Q}{L \cdot B}$ | Surface Overflow Rate | m³/m²/day | All clarifier design | | 4 | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{A_s \cdot H}{Q}$ | Detention Time | hours | Process check | | 5 | $WLR = \dfrac{Q}{L_w}$ | Weir Loading Rate | m³/m/day | Effluent weir design | | 6 | $V_{scour} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \cdot k \cdot (S_s - 1) \cdot g \cdot d}{f}}$ | **Scour Velocity** (Camp's Eq.) | m/s | Prevents resuspension | | 7 | $\% \text{ Removal} = \left(1 - \dfrac{V_s}{SOR}\right) \times 100$ | Fractional Removal | % | For discrete particles | | 8 | $Re = \dfrac{V_s \cdot d \cdot \rho_w}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number check | dimensionless | Settling regime | | 9 | $Cd = \dfrac{24}{Re} + \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{Re}} + 0.34$ | Drag Coefficient (Schiller-Naumann) | dimensionless | Transitional settling | | 10 | $A_s = \dfrac{Q}{SOR}$ | Required Surface Area | m² | Final design | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These equations govern the design of every municipal STP (Sewage Treatment Plant) and ETP (Effluent Treatment Plant) across India — from Kerala Water Authority's 5 MLD plants to large 500 MLD installations. Every civil/environmental engineering project with a clarifier uses these exact equations. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stokes' Law (Terminal Settling Velocity) ### Statement A spherical particle falling through a viscous fluid attains a **constant terminal velocity (Vs)** when the net gravitational force equals the hydrodynamic drag. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Net Gravitational Force** The effective downward force on the particle is the difference between gravity and buoyancy. $$F_{net} = V_p \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g$$ where $V_p = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}$ is the volume of a spherical particle. Thus: $$F_{net} = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g$$ **Step 2 — Hydrodynamic Drag Force (Laminar Regime)** For low Reynolds numbers (Re < 1), Stokes showed that: $$F_d = 3 \pi \mu d V_s$$ **Step 3 — Force Balance at Terminal Velocity** At terminal velocity, $F_{net} = F_d$: $$\dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g = 3 \pi \mu d V_s$$ **Step 4 — Solve for Vs** Divide both sides by $3 \pi d$: $$V_s = \dfrac{d^2 \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g}{18 \mu}$$ This is **Stokes' Law** — the foundational equation for discrete particle settling. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) and Particle Removal Efficiency ### Conceptual Basis Consider an ideal rectangular clarifier of length L, width B, and depth H. The horizontal velocity through the tank is: $$V_h = \dfrac{Q}{H \cdot B}$$ A particle entering at the top surface (height H) must settle a distance H to reach the bottom. The time available for settling is the **detention time θ**: $$\theta = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{Q} = \dfrac{L \cdot B \cdot H}{Q}$$ The particle settles at velocity Vs. The distance it falls during detention: $$h_{fall} = V_s \cdot \theta = V_s \cdot \dfrac{L \cdot B \cdot H}{Q} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{V_{tank}}{Q}$$ **Condition for 100% Removal:** The particle must reach the bottom before exiting the tank, so: $$h_{fall} \geq H$$ $$V_s \cdot \dfrac{L \cdot B \cdot H}{Q} \geq H$$ $$V_s \geq \dfrac{Q}{L \cdot B} = SOR$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** Therefore, **the surface overflow rate (SOR) IS the minimum settling velocity (Vs) for 100% removal.** All particles with $V_s \geq SOR$ are completely removed. **General Fractional Removal:** For a clarifier operating at a given SOR, the fraction of particles of settling velocity Vs that are removed is: $$\% \text{ Removal} = \dfrac{V_s}{SOR} \times 100 \quad \text{(for } V_s < SOR \text{)}$$ $$\% \text{ Removal} = 100\% \quad \text{(for } V_s \geq SOR \text{)}$$ **Total Removal for a Particle Size Distribution:** $$R_{total} = \int_0^{SOR} \left(1 - \dfrac{V_s}{SOR}\right) \cdot f(V_s) \, dV_s + \int_{SOR}^{\infty} f(V_s) \, dV_s$$ where $f(V_s)$ is the probability density function of settling velocities. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Scour Velocity (Camp's Equation) ### Why This Matters If horizontal velocity in the clarifier exceeds a critical value, it can **resuspend already-settled particles**, undoing the separation. **Step 1 — Critical Shear Stress** The bottom shear stress needed to dislodge a particle of diameter d is: $$\tau_c = k \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot d$$ where $k$ is a dimensionless constant (typically **0.04 to 0.06**). **Step 2 — Darcy-Weisbach Relation** The shear stress exerted by horizontal flow velocity Vh on the bottom is: $$\tau_b = \dfrac{f}{8} \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_h^2$$ where $f$ is the Darcy-Weisbach friction factor. **Step 3 — Equate and Solve** Setting $\tau_b = \tau_c$ to find the threshold scour velocity: $$\dfrac{f}{8} \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_{scour}^2 = k \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot d$$ $$V_{scour} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \cdot k \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot d}{f \cdot \rho_w}}$$ Since $\rho_s - \rho_w = \rho_w (S_s - 1)$ where $S_s = \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho_w}$ is the specific gravity: $$\boxed{V_{scour} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \cdot k \cdot (S_s - 1) \cdot g \cdot d}{f}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Design Rule:** Horizontal velocity $V_h$ in the clarifier must be **less than** $V_{scour}$ to prevent scouring of settled sludge. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sedimentation Clarifier ```mermaid flowchart TD Influent["Raw Wastewater Influent
Q in m3 per day"] InfDist["Influent Distribution Well
Equalizes hydraulic load"] InletZone["Inlet Diffusion Zone
Energy dissipator baffle"] SettleZone["Settling Zone
Volume equals Q times theta
SOR governs surface area"] SludgeBlanket["Sludge Blanket Zone
Type III hindered settling region"] SludgeHopper["Sludge Hopper
Scraper arms at 0.5 to 1.0 m per min"] EffluentWeir["Effluent Launder and Weir
WLR controlled at 185 to 370 m3 per m per day"] Effluent["Clarified Effluent to next stage"] SludgePump["Sludge Pump to digester"] Scraper["Travelling Bridge Scraper or Rotary Scraper"] Influent --> InfDist InfDist --> InletZone InletZone --> SettleZone SettleZone --> SludgeBlanket SludgeBlanket --> SludgeHopper SludgeHopper --> Scraper Scraper --> SludgePump SettleZone --> EffluentWeir EffluentWeir --> Effluent ``` ## 4.2 Four Types of Settling — Comparative Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph T1["Type 1 Discrete"] T1A["Single spherical particle"] T1B["Stokes Law applies"] T1C["No inter-particle interaction"] T1A --> T1B --> T1C end subgraph T2["Type 2 Flocculent"] T2A["Particles coalesce during fall"] T2B["Settling column test required"] T2C["Removal via mass fraction curve"] T2A --> T2B --> T2C end subgraph T3["Type 3 Zone Hindered"] T3A["High concentration suspension"] T3B["Solid liquid interface descends"] T3C["Applied to activated sludge"] T3A --> T3B --> T3C end subgraph T4["Type 4 Compression"] T4A["Sludge at the base compresses"] T4B["Mechanism is mechanical squeeze"] T4C["Occurs in thickeners"] T4A --> T4B --> T4C end ``` ## 4.3 Circular vs Rectangular Clarifier Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Choose Clarifier Geometry"] Q1{"Flow Q greater than 25 MLD"} Rect["Use Rectangular Clarifier"] Circ["Use Circular Clarifier"] Sludge1{"Sludge has high fibre content"} CircSludge["Use Circular with centre feed"] Mech1["Use Chain and Flight Scraper"] Start --> Q1 Q1 -- No --> Sludge1 Q1 -- Yes --> Rect Sludge1 -- Yes --> CircSludge Sludge1 -- No --> Circ Circ --> Mech1 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) and state its typical design value for a primary clarifier.** **Model Answer:** The **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR)** is defined as the volume of wastewater applied per unit surface area of the clarifier per unit time. It is mathematically expressed as: $$SOR = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$$ where $Q$ is the flow rate and $A_s$ is the surface area. It has units of **m³/m²/day** (or equivalently m/day). **Typical design SOR for a primary clarifier:** **24 to 40 m³/m²/day** (≈ 30 to 45 m/day for municipal wastewater). **Significance:** SOR governs the **minimum settling velocity (Vs)** that a particle must possess to be 100% removed. Particles with $V_s \geq SOR$ are completely captured. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for value + significance)* --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between Type I and Type II settling with suitable examples.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Type I Settling | Type II Settling | |---|---|---| | Particle nature | Discrete, non-flocculating | Flocculent, agglomerating | | Velocity | Constant (terminal) | Increases with depth/time | | Governing law | Stokes' Law | Settling column test | | Removal efficiency | $R = V_s / SOR$ | Iso-removal curves | | Typical example | Sand, grit | Primary clarifier sludge | **Type I** assumes particles do not interact, while **Type II** particles grow in mass and size as they descend, altering their settling velocity — making analysis more complex. *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question B-A (Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **A primary sedimentation tank is to be designed for a sewage flow of 5 MLD. Assume:** - Detention time (θ) = **2 hours** - Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) = **30 m³/m²/day** - Depth (H) = **3.0 m** - Length-to-width ratio (L:B) = **4:1** **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Determine the **surface area**, **length**, and **width** of the tank. **Part (b) [7 marks]:** Determine (i) the **horizontal velocity**, (ii) the **weir loading rate** if a single effluent weir of length 8 m is provided, and (iii) comment on whether the WLR is acceptable. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Surface Area from SOR:** $$A_s = \dfrac{Q}{SOR} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^6 \, L/day}{30 \, m^3/m^2/day \times 1000 \, L/m^3}$$ Wait — let us keep units consistent. $Q = 5 \, MLD = 5000 \, m^3/day$. $$A_s = \dfrac{5000}{30} = 166.67 \, m^2$$ **Step 2 — Length and Width:** Given $L:B = 4:1$, so $A_s = L \cdot B = 4B^2$: $$4B^2 = 166.67$$ $$B^2 = 41.67 \Rightarrow B = 6.45 \, m$$ $$L = 4 \times 6.45 = 25.81 \, m$$ **Step 3 — Cross-check using Detention Time:** $$\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{L \cdot B \cdot H}{Q}$$ $$V = \theta \cdot Q = \dfrac{2 \, h}{24} \times 5000 = 416.67 \, m^3$$ $$A_s = \dfrac{V}{H} = \dfrac{416.67}{3.0} = 138.89 \, m^2$$ This is **less than** the 166.67 m² required by SOR. Hence, **SOR governs the design** (the more stringent parameter), and we adopt $A_s = 166.67 \, m^2$. > For detention time to also be satisfied, **increase depth slightly**: > $$H_{new} = \dfrac{416.67}{166.67} = 2.5 \, m$$ > > So a depth of **2.5 m** satisfies both criteria. **Final Dimensions:** L ≈ **25.81 m**, B ≈ **6.45 m**, H = **2.5 m**. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) Horizontal Velocity:** $$V_h = \dfrac{Q}{24 \times 3600 \times B \cdot H}$$ $$V_h = \dfrac{5000 \, m^3/day}{86400 \, s/day \times 6.45 \times 2.5} = \dfrac{0.0579 \, m^3/s}{16.125 \, m^2}$$ $$V_h = 3.59 \times 10^{-3} \, m/s = 3.59 \, mm/s$$ **Recommended range:** 0.5 to 2.5 mm/s. Our value is at the upper limit — acceptable. **(ii) Weir Loading Rate:** $$WLR = \dfrac{Q}{L_w} = \dfrac{5000 \, m^3/day}{8 \, m} = 625 \, m^3/m/day$$ **(iii) Acceptability Comment:** > [!WARNING] > The recommended WLR for primary clarifiers is **185 to 370 m³/m/day**. Our computed **625 m³/m/day** is **excessive** — solids will be carried over with the effluent. **Solution:** Increase weir length to **at least 14 m** (preferably using a V-notch weir spanning both sides of the tank) to bring WLR below 370 m³/m/day. --- ### **Question B-B (Choice B)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **A grit chamber receives a flow of 10 MLD of sewage with grit particles having:** - Specific gravity $S_s$ = **2.65** - Diameter $d$ = **0.2 mm** - Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ = **1.002 × 10⁻³ N·s/m²** - Density of water $\rho_w$ = **1000 kg/m³** - $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the **Stokes' settling velocity (Vs)** and verify if it lies in the laminar regime. **Part (b) [7 marks]:** Calculate the **scour velocity (V_scour)** assuming $k = 0.05$ and $f = 0.03$. Comment on the design. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Apply Stokes' Law:** $$V_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \mu}$$ $$V_s = \dfrac{9.81 \times (2.65 - 1.0) \times 1000 \times (0.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$ **Numerator:** $$9.81 \times 1.65 \times 1000 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} = 9.81 \times 1.65 \times 4 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$= 64.746 \times 10^{-5} = 6.4746 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Denominator:** $$18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} = 18.036 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Therefore:** $$V_s = \dfrac{6.4746 \times 10^{-4}}{18.036 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0359 \, m/s$$ Converting to m/day: $V_s = 0.0359 \times 86400 = 3101.76 \, m/day$. **Step 2 — Verify Laminar Regime via Reynolds Number:** $$Re = \dfrac{V_s \cdot d \cdot \rho_w}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.0359 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$Re = \dfrac{7.18 \times 10^{-3}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.165$$ Since **Re < 1 assumption is slightly violated (Re ≈ 7)**, we are in the **transitional regime**. For more accuracy, apply the Schiller-Naumann correction: $$C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re} + \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{Re}} + 0.34 = \dfrac{24}{7.165} + \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{7.165}} + 0.34$$ $$C_D = 3.350 + 1.121 + 0.34 = 4.811$$ Corrected velocity (Newton's equation): $$V_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_w}}$$ $$V_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 9.81 \times 1.65 \times 1000 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3}}{3 \times 4.811 \times 1000}}$$ $$V_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{12.949}{14.433}} = \sqrt{0.8973} = 0.947 \, m/s$$ > [!NOTE] > Stokes' Law **overestimates** in transitional regime. The corrected Vs = **0.947 m/s** (more conservative for design). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Apply Camp's Scour Equation:** $$V_{scour} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \cdot k \cdot (S_s - 1) \cdot g \cdot d}{f}}$$ **Numerator (inside root):** $$8 \times 0.05 \times (2.65 - 1) \times 9.81 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$= 8 \times 0.05 \times 1.65 \times 9.81 \times 0.0002$$ $$= 0.4 \times 1.65 \times 9.81 \times 0.0002 = 0.001295 \, m^2/s^2$$ **Divide by f:** $$\dfrac{0.001295}{0.03} = 0.04318$$ **Therefore:** $$V_{scour} = \sqrt{0.04318} = 0.2078 \, m/s$$ **Step 2 — Design Comment:** > [!WARNING] > **Design Rule:** The horizontal flow velocity $V_h$ in a grit chamber should be: > - **Greater than** $V_{scour}$ to keep organic particles in suspension (preventing their settling). > - **Less than** $V_s$ of grit (so grit can settle out). > > For our case: $V_s = 0.947 \, m/s$, $V_{scour} = 0.208 \, m/s$. > Recommended $V_h = 0.25$ to $0.30 \, m/s$ — **between** these two values. > Hence **design is acceptable** — grit will settle, while organic matter remains in suspension. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Points in KTU 2024 Sedimentation Questions:** > 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing m³/day with m³/s in the same equation. Always declare unit conversions in a separate step. > 2. **SOR vs θ conflict:** When the area from SOR differs from the area computed via θ × Q / H, students often fail to identify the **governing (larger) area** that actually controls design. > 3. **Stokes' Law applicability:** Applying Stokes' without verifying **Re < 1** is a frequent error. Always compute Re. > 4. **Weir length assumption:** Assuming a single side weir without justification is incorrect — V-notch weirs spanning both longitudinal sides are standard practice. > 5. **Forgetting $k$ and $f$ in scour equation:** Defaulting to $k = 0.06$, $f = 0.025$ without stating assumed values loses method marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Sedimentation = gravity-based separation** of suspended solids; relies on $\rho_s > \rho_w$. - **Four settling types:** Discrete (I), Flocculent (II), Zone/Hindered (III), Compression (IV). - **Stokes' Law:** $V_s = \dfrac{g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18\mu}$ — valid only for **Re < 1**. - **SOR** is the **single most important design parameter** — it equals the minimum Vs for 100% removal. - **Detention time (θ)** typical values: **2–3 h (primary)**, **1.5–2.5 h (secondary)**. - **Weir Loading Rate (WLR)** must stay below **370 m³/m/day (primary)** and **185 m³/m/day (secondary)**. - **Scour velocity** must be exceeded by horizontal velocity to keep organics suspended in grit chambers. - **Clarifier types:** Rectangular (chain & flight scraper), Circular (centre-feed with rotary scraper). - **Type II analysis requires a settling column test** — not Stokes' Law. - **Activated sludge separation = secondary clarification = Type III (zone settling)**. - **Schiller-Naumann** equation used to correct Stokes' Law in the transitional regime. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these 10 formulas and 4 settling types — they account for ≥ 70% of KTU marks in this module.**

topics.title: Flotation (Oil & Grease Removal)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flotation (Oil & Grease Removal)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Flotation** is a **physical-chemical separation process** used in wastewater treatment to remove suspended matter (oils, greases, fine suspended solids, fibres, and biological sludges) by introducing fine gas bubbles (usually air) into the liquid. The bubbles attach to the particles, collectively reducing their bulk density below that of the surrounding water, causing them to **float to the surface** where they form a scum blanket that is subsequently skimmed off.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** Flotation is classified as a **primary treatment process** when used for oil and grease removal from industrial effluents (petroleum refineries, food processing, metal finishing) and as a **tertiary/sludge thickening process** when used for biological solids separation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bubble Lift" Principle

Imagine dropping a small pebble into a bathtub full of water — it **sinks** because it's denser than water. But now, imagine tying tiny helium balloons to that pebble. The combined object becomes lighter than water, and **floats up to the ceiling**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** In flotation, compressed air is dissolved into wastewater under high pressure. When the pressure is suddenly released, microscopic air bubbles (typically **30–100 µm in diameter**) nucleate and attach to oil droplets, grease globules, or suspended particles. These "ballooned" particles rise to the surface **5–10 times faster** than they would settle by gravity — which is why flotation is preferred over sedimentation for **light, oily, or buoyant** contaminants.

## 1.3 Why Oil & Grease Removal is Critical

| Parameter | Typical Limit | Regulation Source |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Oil & Grease (in effluent discharge) | **< 10 mg/L** | KSPCB / CPCB Norms |
| Free Oil (skimmable) | Removed at source | API Separator Standard |
| Emulsified Oil | Requires chemical/DAF treatment | Refinery Effluent Guidelines |

> [!WARNING]
> **Why We Cannot Discharge Oil to Sewers:** Oil & grease coats sewer walls, causes **corrosion of concrete pipes** (via fatty acid formation), creates **fire hazards** at pump stations, and forms a **toxic film** on biological treatment units, preventing oxygen transfer to microbes.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Specific Gravity of Common Oils:** Crude oil ≈ **0.85–0.95**, Lubricating oil ≈ **0.88–0.94**, Vegetable oil ≈ **0.92–0.93**
- **Standard Air Solubility in Water (20°C, 1 atm):** ≈ **18.7 mg/L** (≈ 9.18 mL/L)
- **Typical Flotation Bubble Diameter:** **30–100 µm** for DAF; **0.5–2 mm** for IAF
- **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** **20–60 minutes** for dissolved air flotation
- **Air-to-Solids (A/S) Ratio:** **0.005 – 0.060** (mass of air released per mass of solids entering)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "How" Behind Flotation

The separation mechanism in flotation is governed by **three sequential physical phenomena**:

1. **Nucleation & Bubble Generation:** Air is dissolved in water under elevated pressure (typically **3–6 bar** in DAF systems). When depressurized through a needle valve or special orifice, the supersaturated water releases air as **micro-bubbles** (bubble size ∝ 1/ΔP).

2. **Particle–Bubble Attachment:** Occurs via three mechanisms:
   - **Collision:** Particle physically contacts the rising bubble.
   - **Adhesion:** Hydrophobic particles (oils/greases) are attracted to the air–water interface due to **surface tension forces**.
   - **Entrapment:** Fine bubbles form within floc structures, lifting them en masse.

3. **Rise to Surface:** The buoyant bubble–particle aggregate rises to the surface where it forms a stable **scum layer**, removed by mechanical skimmers.

## 2.2 Classification of Flotation Systems

| Type | Bubble Size | Mechanism | Application |
|------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** | 30–100 µm | Pressure-induced supersaturation | O&G removal, sludge thickening |
| **Induced Air Flotation (IAF)** | 0.5–2 mm | Mechanical impeller dispersion | High O&G loads (refineries) |
| **Dispersed Air Flotation (Foam Fractionation)** | 1–5 mm | Diffuser-induced bubbles | Surfactant/fibre removal |
| **Vacuum Flotation** | 30–100 µm | Vacuum-depressurized water | Pulp & paper industries |

## 2.3 Theoretical Foundation: Henry's Law

The entire DAF process hinges on **Henry's Law**, which governs gas solubility in liquids under pressure:

$$C = k_H \cdot P$$

Where:
- $C$ = concentration of dissolved gas (mg/L)
- $k_H$ = Henry's constant (mg/L·atm)
- $P$ = partial pressure of gas (atm)

For air in water at **20°C**: $k_H \approx 18.7$ mg/(L·atm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** By pressurizing water from 1 atm to **5 atm**, the dissolved air concentration increases **5-fold**, providing the supersaturated "reservoir" of air that becomes available as micro-bubbles upon depressurization.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|--------------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | $C_s = k_H \cdot P$ | Dissolved gas concentration | Henry's Law |
| 2 | $A/S = \dfrac{1.3 \cdot S_a \cdot (fP - 1)}{X_0}$ | Air-to-Solids ratio (mass basis) | DAF Design |
