topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Production of Sludge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sludge** in the context of biological wastewater treatment is defined as the semi-solid residual material generated during the physical, biological, and chemical unit operations of a wastewater treatment plant (WWTP). It is composed of organic matter, inorganic solids, microorganisms, water (typically **94–98%** by weight), and entrained chemicals.

The **Production of Sludge** refers to the systematic quantification and characterization of the solid by-products accumulated from each stage of treatment. It encompasses the calculation of mass, volume, and composition of sludge generated from **primary sedimentation**, **secondary biological reactors** (e.g., activated sludge, trickling filters, rotating biological contactors), and **tertiary/advanced treatment** units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, students must be able to quantify sludge using stoichiometric yield coefficients and apply mass balance principles across unit operations — this is a high-weightage topic linking **stoichiometry**, **kinetics**, and **process design**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen. The kitchen produces two things: delicious meals (cleaned water) and food waste (sludge). The more meals you cook, the more peels, bones, and leftovers accumulate. Similarly, in a wastewater treatment plant:

- **Wastewater Inflow** = Uncooked food entering the kitchen
- **Treated Effluent** = The finished meal
- **Sludge** = The peels, bones, and food scraps left behind

The "scraps" come from two places:
1. **Grit, sand, and settleable solids** that fall to the bottom naturally (primary sludge)
2. **Microorganisms that have "eaten" the organic pollutants and multiplied** (secondary/biological sludge)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Sludge is essentially **concentrated pollution**. The total mass of pollution entering the plant equals the mass leaving as treated effluent PLUS the mass captured as sludge. This conservation principle is the foundation of all sludge mass balance calculations.

## 1.3 Standard Classification of Sludge

| Type | Source | Typical Solids Content | Composition |
|------|--------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Primary Sludge (PS)** | Primary Clarifier | **4–7%** | Settleable organic & inorganic solids |
| **Secondary Sludge (SS)** | Biological Reactor (e.g., Activated Sludge) | **0.5–2%** | Mostly biological floc (biomass) |
| **Tertiary/Chemical Sludge** | Advanced Treatment (coagulation) | **1–4%** | Chemical precipitates (e.g., Al, Fe hydroxides) |
| **Mixed/Digested Sludge** | Sludge Treatment | **2–6%** | Combined + stabilized organics |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Specific Gravity of Sludge Solids:** $G_s \approx 1.01$ to $1.5$ (organic $\approx 1.0$, inorganic $\approx 2.5$)
- **Volatile Solids Fraction ($f_v$):** $0.6$ to $0.85$ for municipal secondary sludge
- **Standard Sludge Yield (Y):** $0.3$ to $0.6$ kg VSS/kg BOD removed (for conventional activated sludge)
- **Observed Yield ($Y_{obs}$):** Accounts for endogenous decay — typically $0.2$ to $0.4$ kg VSS/kg BOD
- **Endogenous Decay Coefficient ($k_d$):** $0.04$ to $0.08$ day$^{-1}$ at $20^\circ C$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

The production of sludge is governed by the principle of **conservation of mass** applied across each treatment unit. The theoretical analysis proceeds in three logical layers:

### Layer 1: Stoichiometric Foundation
Every gram of substrate (BOD) consumed by microorganisms results in:
- A fraction $Y$ converted to new biomass (sludge)
- A fraction $(1 - Y)$ oxidized to $CO_2$ and $H_2O$
- A fraction lost via endogenous decay

### Layer 2: Kinetic Foundation
The net sludge production rate depends on the **Solids Retention Time (SRT)**, often denoted $\theta_c$. Higher SRT → more decay → less net sludge. This is the **"Why"** behind extended aeration producing less waste.

### Layer 3: Operational Foundation
Real-world sludge production = Stoichiometric production − Endogenous loss − Excess effluent suspended solids (ESS).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | $P_X = Y \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$ | Stoichiometric sludge production (no decay) | kg VSS/day | Applicable only for plug-flow without decay |
| 2 | $P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$ | Observed sludge production | kg VSS/day | Standard for design |
| 3 | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ | Observed yield coefficient | dimensionless | $\theta_c = $ SRT in days |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot U - k_d$ | Fundamental SRT-substrate relationship | day$^{-1}$ | $U = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{X \cdot t}$ (specific substrate utilization) |
| 5 | $P_X = Q \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ | Complete design equation | kg VSS/day | Includes SRT effect |
| 6 | $P_{X,total} = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) + Q \cdot X_{eff}$ | Total sludge = biological + effluent solids | kg/day | For final design |
| 7 | $M_{primary} = Q \cdot SS \cdot f_p$ | Primary sludge mass | kg/day | $f_p = 0.4$ to $0.6$ (fraction of SS removed) |
| 8 | $\Delta X_V = Y \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot Q - k_d \cdot X \cdot V$ | Net biomass growth (mass balance form) | kg VSS/day | $V$ = aeration tank volume |
| 9 | $V_{sludge} = \dfrac{M_s}{\rho_w \cdot P_s \cdot 100}$ | Sludge volume | m³/day | $P_s$ = % solids, $M_s$ = mass of solids |
| 10 | $\theta_c^{min} = \dfrac{1}{Y \cdot U_{max} - k_d}$ | Minimum SRT for washout | days | Critical design constraint |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU problems, the question will often give you $Y$, $k_d$, $\theta_c$, $Q$, $S_0$, and $S$, and ask for $P_X$. Memorize Formula #5 as your "go-to" equation.

## 2.3 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering

- **Design of Sludge Handling Facilities:** The volume of digesters, thickeners, and dewatering units depends on daily sludge mass ($P_X$).
- **Economic Feasibility:** Sludge disposal costs can be **30–60%** of total plant O&M cost.
- **Process Selection:** A high $k_d$ or long $\theta_c$ means less sludge → favors **extended aeration** or **membrane bioreactors (MBR)**.
- **Environmental Compliance:** Improper sludge management causes secondary pollution.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

To estimate sludge production in a conventional activated sludge process:

1. **Identify influent conditions:** $Q$ (flow), $S_0$ (influent BOD), $X_0$ (influent VSS), $X_{eff}$ (effluent VSS).
2. **Determine removal efficiency:** $\eta = (S_0 - S) / S_0$
3. **Calculate BOD removed:** $\Delta S = S_0 - S$ (in mg/L)
4. **Convert to mass load:** $Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$ in kg/day (using $Q$ in m³/day, $S$ in mg/L, and dividing by 1000).
5. **Apply observed yield:** $Y_{obs} = Y / (1 + k_d \theta_c)$
6. **Multiply:** $P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$
7. **Add effluent solids loss:** $P_{X,total} = P_X + Q \cdot X_{eff}$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Observed Yield Coefficient ($Y_{obs}$)

### Starting from the Monod-based Biomass Balance

The net rate of biomass growth in a completely mixed activated sludge system is given by:

$$r_g = \mu \cdot X - k_d \cdot X$$

where:
- $\mu$ = specific growth rate (day$^{-1}$)
- $X$ = biomass concentration (mg/L as VSS)
- $k_d$ = endogenous decay coefficient (day$^{-1}$)

### Step 1: Express Specific Growth Rate Using Yield

From stoichiometry, specific growth rate is related to substrate utilization:

$$\mu = Y \cdot U$$

where $U$ is the specific substrate utilization rate (kg BOD removed/kg VSS·day).

### Step 2: Substitute Into Growth Rate Equation

$$r_g = Y \cdot U \cdot X - k_d \cdot X$$

### Step 3: Define Solids Retention Time (SRT)

The SRT $\theta_c$ is the average time biomass remains in the system:

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{X \cdot V}{r_g \cdot V} = \dfrac{X}{r_g}$$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = \dfrac{r_g}{X} = Y \cdot U - k_d$$

### Step 4: Express Substrate Removal in Terms of SRT

From the operating equation of a CSTR:

$$U = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{X \cdot t}$$

Substituting into the SRT equation:

$$\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot \dfrac{S_0 - S}{X \cdot t} - k_d$$

### Step 5: Rearrange to Find Daily Biomass Production

The total biomass wasted per day equals $X \cdot V / \theta_c$:

$$P_X = \dfrac{X \cdot V}{\theta_c}$$

But from the SRT relationship:

$$\dfrac{X \cdot V}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot U \cdot X \cdot V - k_d \cdot X \cdot V$$

$$P_X = Y \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot Q - k_d \cdot X \cdot V$$

### Step 6: Derive the Observed Yield

The net observed yield per unit substrate removed is:

$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{P_X}{Q \cdot (S_0 - S)} = Y - \dfrac{k_d \cdot X \cdot V}{Q \cdot (S_0 - S)}$$

But since $\theta_c = X \cdot V / (Y \cdot U \cdot X \cdot V - k_d \cdot X \cdot V)$, we can simplify to the standard textbook form:

$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$$

**Final Master Equation:**

$$\boxed{P_X = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem Statement:** A municipal WWTP has the following design data:
- Flow rate $Q = 25{,}000$ m³/day
- Influent BOD $S_0 = 250$ mg/L
- Effluent BOD $S = 20$ mg/L
- Influent VSS $X_0 = 180$ mg/L
- Effluent VSS $X_{eff} = 15$ mg/L
- Yield coefficient $Y = 0.55$ kg VSS/kg BOD
- Endogenous decay $k_d = 0.06$ day$^{-1}$
- SRT $\theta_c = 10$ days

**Find:** Total sludge production per day (kg VSS/day).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate BOD removed in concentration units:

$$\Delta S = S_0 - S = 250 - 20 = 230 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2:** Convert BOD removed to mass rate:

$$Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 25{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 230 \text{ g/m}^3 = 5{,}750{,}000 \text{ g/day}$$

$$Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 5{,}750 \text{ kg BOD/day}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate observed yield:

$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c} = \dfrac{0.55}{1 + (0.06)(10)} = \dfrac{0.55}{1 + 0.6} = \dfrac{0.55}{1.6}$$

$$Y_{obs} = 0.34375 \text{ kg VSS/kg BOD}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate biological sludge production:

$$P_{X,bio} = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.34375 \times 5{,}750$$

$$P_{X,bio} = 1{,}976.56 \text{ kg VSS/day}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate solids lost in effluent:

$$P_{X,eff} = Q \cdot X_{eff} = 25{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 15 \text{ g/m}^3 = 375{,}000 \text{ g/day}$$

$$P_{X,eff} = 375 \text{ kg VSS/day}$$

**Step 6:** Total sludge production:

$$P_{X,total} = P_{X,bio} + P_{X,eff} = 1{,}976.56 + 375$$

$$\boxed{P_{X,total} = 2{,}351.56 \text{ kg VSS/day}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of SRT on Sludge Production

| SRT ($\theta_c$), days | $Y_{obs}$ (kg VSS/kg BOD) | $P_{X,bio}$ (kg/day) for same load | % Reduction vs. $\theta_c=5$ d |
|------------------------|----------------------------|-------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| 3 | 0.4125 | 2,371.9 | +4.2% (more sludge) |
| 5 | 0.3667 | 2,108.3 | 0 (baseline) |
| 10 | 0.3438 | 1,976.6 | −6.3% |
| 20 | 0.2273 | 1,306.8 | −38.0% |
| 30 | 0.1803 | 1,036.7 | −50.8% |
| 40 | 0.1528 | 878.5 | −58.3% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Doubling the SRT from 10 to 20 days reduces sludge production by ~38%. This is why **extended aeration systems (SRT = 20–30 days)** are preferred when sludge minimization is the goal — a frequent KTU exam concept.

## 3.4 Derivation of Primary Sludge Mass

For primary clarifiers, sludge is produced by gravity settling of suspended solids:

$$M_{primary} = Q \cdot SS_{removed} \cdot f_p$$

where $f_p$ is the fraction of settled solids actually captured as underflow (typically $0.4$ to $0.6$):

$$SS_{removed} = SS_0 - SS_{eff} = \eta_{SS} \cdot SS_0$$

$$\boxed{M_{primary} = Q \cdot \eta_{SS} \cdot SS_0 \cdot f_p}$$

For example, with $Q = 25{,}000$ m³/day, $SS_0 = 220$ mg/L, $\eta_{SS} = 0.65$, $f_p = 0.5$:

$$M_{primary} = 25{,}000 \times 0.65 \times 220 \times 0.5 / 1000 = 1{,}787.5 \text{ kg/day}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sludge Production Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Influent[Influent Wastewater Q, S0, X0] --> PS[Primary Sedimentation Tank]
    Influent --> BarScreen[Bar Screen and Grit Chamber]
    BarScreen --> PS
    PS --> PrimarySludge[Primary Sludge Stream]
    PS --> PrimaryEff[Primary Effluent to Bioreactor]
    PrimaryEff --> BioReactor[Biological Reactor Activated Sludge]
    BioReactor --> SecClarifier[Secondary Clarifier]
    BioReactor --> WAS[Waste Activated Sludge WAS]
    SecClarifier --> Recycle[Return Activated Sludge RAS]
    SecClarifier --> FinalEff[Treated Effluent S, Xeff]
    Recycle --> BioReactor
    WAS --> SecSludge[Secondary Sludge Stream]
    PrimarySludge --> SludgeThickener[Sludge Thickener]
    SecSludge --> SludgeThickener
    SludgeThickener --> Digester[Sludge Digester Aerobic or Anaerobic]
    Digester --> Dewatering[Sludge Dewatering Belt Press or Centrifuge]
    Dewatering --> Cake[Sludge Cake for Disposal]
    Dewatering --> LiquidSidestream[Liquid Sidestream Recycle to Headworks]
```

## 4.2 Mass Balance Architecture at the Bioreactor Level

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InFlow[Q, S0 Influent BOD] --> Reactor[Completely Mixed Bioreactor Volume V]
    InFlow --> InFlowVSS[Q, X0 Influent VSS]
    InFlow --> Reactor
    InFlowVSS --> Reactor
    Reactor --> EffBOD[Q, S Effluent BOD]
    Reactor --> EffVSS[Q, X Effluent VSS]
    Reactor --> WAS[Qw, Xw Waste Activated Sludge]
    Reactor --> RAS[Qr, Xr Return Activated Sludge]
    RAS --> Reactor
    WAS --> SludgeOutput[PX Sludge Production kg per day]
    EffVSS --> SludgeOutput
    EffBOD --> Reactor
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Sludge Minimization Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Reduce Sludge Production] --> Q1{High SRT feasible}
    Q1 -->|Yes| EA[Extended Aeration theta c 20 to 30 days]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Can install membrane}
    Q2 -->|Yes| MBR[Membrane Bioreactor retains all biomass]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Can use mechanical cell lysis}
    Q3 -->|Yes| CellDisruption[Cell Disruption Ultrasonic or Thermal]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Oxidation of sludge}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Ozonation[Ozonation or Peroxide Treatment]
    Q4 -->|No| Conventional[Conventional AS with theta c 5 to 10 days]
    EA --> Reduced1[Reduced PX 38 to 50 percent]
    MBR --> Reduced2[Near zero effluent solids]
    CellDisruption --> Reduced3[Enhanced biodegradation]
    Ozonation --> Reduced4[Sludge reduction 30 to 60 percent]
    Conventional --> Baseline[Standard yield Yobs 0.3 to 0.4]
```

## 4.4 Process Comparison Matrix: Sludge Production vs. Technology

| Process | SRT (days) | $Y_{obs}$ (kg/kg BOD) | Sludge per kg BOD removed | Comments |
|---------|------------|------------------------|----------------------------|----------|
| Conventional AS | 5–10 | 0.35–0.45 | **0.35–0.45 kg** | Baseline |
| Extended Aeration | 20–30 | 0.15–0.20 | **0.15–0.20 kg** | High energy, low sludge |
| Oxidation Ditch | 15–25 | 0.20–0.30 | **0.20–0.30 kg** | Robust rural use |
| MBR | 20–50 | 0.05–0.15 | **0.05–0.15 kg** | Zero effluent solids |
| SBR | 10–20 | 0.25–0.35 | **0.25–0.35 kg** | Batch flexibility |
| Trickling Filter | N/A (attached) | 0.30–0.50 | **0.30–0.50 kg** | Lower energy, higher sludge |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

Define **sludge yield coefficient (Y)** and **observed yield ($Y_{obs}$)**. How are they related?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Yield Coefficient (Y):** The mass of biomass synthesized per unit mass of substrate (BOD) removed by the microorganisms. Units: kg VSS/kg BOD. It is a stoichiometric parameter representing the maximum possible conversion.
- **Observed Yield ($Y_{obs}$):** The actual net mass of biomass produced per unit substrate removed, **after accounting for endogenous decay losses**. It is always lower than Y.
- **Relationship:**

$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$$

where $k_d$ is the endogenous decay coefficient and $\theta_c$ is the solids retention time. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for observed yield, 1 mark for relationship)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series II]**

List **four** sources of sludge production in a conventional municipal wastewater treatment plant.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 × 4):**

1. **Primary Sludge** — from primary sedimentation tank (settleable solids)
2. **Secondary/Biological Sludge** — from biological treatment (excess activated sludge)
3. **Chemical/Tertiary Sludge** — from coagulation–flocculation (chemical precipitates)
4. **Sludge from Grit Chamber** — inorganic grit and sand (sometimes counted separately)

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, End Semester Examination]**

#### (a) With a neat sketch, explain the **various sources of sludge production** in a municipal wastewater treatment plant. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Sludge is produced at multiple stages. The principal sources are:

**1. Primary Sludge (PS):**
Generated in the **primary clarifier** by gravitational settling of suspended solids. It is gray, odorous, contains high organic matter (60–80% volatile), with solids content of **4–7%**. Typical yield: $0.10$ to $0.20$ kg/kg SS removed.

**2. Secondary Sludge (SS):**
Produced in biological reactors (e.g., activated sludge) as **excess/waste activated sludge (WAS)**. Composed of microbial biomass. Solids content: **0.5–2%**. Quantity depends on $Y$ and $k_d$.

**3. Tertiary/Chemical Sludge:**
From chemical precipitation using **alum, ferric chloride, or lime**. Composition: metal hydroxides and chemical precipitates. Solids content: **1–4%**.

**4. Grit and Screenings:**
From preliminary treatment units. Mostly inorganic. Solids content: **10–25%**.

**Sketch (description for 7 marks):**
A flow diagram showing Influent → Screening → Grit Chamber → Primary Tank → Aeration Tank → Secondary Clarifier, with arrows indicating sludge withdrawal from Primary Tank, WAS from Secondary Clarifier, and recirculation of RAS.

#### (b) Derive the **expression for observed yield ($Y_{obs}$)** in terms of Y, $k_d$, and $\theta_c$. A WWTP treats $Q = 10{,}000$ m³/day with $S_0 = 200$ mg/L, $S = 25$ mg/L, $Y = 0.5$ kg/kg, $k_d = 0.05$/day, $\theta_c = 8$ days. Calculate the **biological sludge production**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from biomass growth rate:

$$r_g = \mu X - k_d X = Y U X - k_d X$$

Using SRT definition $\theta_c = X / r_g$:

$$\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = YU - k_d$$

Total biomass in system: $X \cdot V$; biomass wasted per day: $X \cdot V / \theta_c = P_X$

$$P_X = \dfrac{Y \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot Q}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$$

Therefore:

$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$$

**Calculation (4 marks):**

$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{0.5}{1 + (0.05)(8)} = \dfrac{0.5}{1.4} = 0.357 \text{ kg/kg}$$

$$\Delta S = 200 - 25 = 175 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 10{,}000 \times 175 / 1000 = 1{,}750 \text{ kg BOD/day}$$

$$P_X = 0.357 \times 1{,}750 = \mathbf{624.75 \text{ kg VSS/day}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, End Semester Examination]**

#### (a) Discuss the **factors affecting sludge production** in activated sludge process. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The major factors are:

1. **Influent BOD ($S_0$):** Higher $S_0$ → more substrate → more biomass synthesis → more sludge.
2. **Solids Retention Time (SRT / $\theta_c$):** Longest single lever. Higher SRT → more endogenous decay → less net sludge. (Inverse relationship.)
3. **Substrate Utilization Rate ($U$):** Faster utilization at high F/M → more growth → more sludge.
4. **Yield Coefficient (Y):** Determined by microbial species and substrate type. Readily biodegradable substrates yield more sludge.
5. **Temperature:** Higher temperature increases decay rate $k_d$ → less sludge. (But also affects growth rate.)
6. **Dissolved Oxygen (DO):** Aerobic conditions maximize oxidation; anoxic/anaerobic produce less (but different characteristics).
7. **F/M Ratio:** High F/M (e.g., conventional AS) → high sludge; Low F/M (extended aeration) → low sludge.
8. **pH and Toxicity:** Affects microbial metabolism and decay.

#### (b) A conventional activated sludge plant has the following data: $Q = 50{,}000$ m³/day, $S_0 = 300$ mg/L, $S = 20$ mg/L, $Y = 0.6$ kg/kg, $k_d = 0.07$/day, $\theta_c = 6$ days, $X_{eff} = 12$ mg/L. Calculate the **total sludge production per day** including effluent solids. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Observed Yield:**

$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{0.6}{1 + (0.07)(6)} = \dfrac{0.6}{1.42} = 0.4225 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 2 — BOD Removed:**

$$Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 50{,}000 \times (300 - 20) / 1000 = 50{,}000 \times 0.280 = 14{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Biological Sludge:**

$$P_{X,bio} = 0.4225 \times 14{,}000 = 5{,}915 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Effluent Solids Loss:**

$$P_{X,eff} = 50{,}000 \times 12 / 1000 = 600 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 5 — Total Sludge:**

$$P_{X,total} = 5{,}915 + 600 = \boxed{6{,}515 \text{ kg VSS/day}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mg/L to kg/day** — Multiply $Q$ (m³/day) × concentration (g/m³) and then divide by 1000 to get kg/day. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 2. **Forgetting the +1 in denominator** — $Y_{obs} = Y / (1 + k_d\theta_c)$, not $Y / (k_d\theta_c)$. Marks lost: 2.
> 3. **Ignoring effluent solids ($X_{eff}$)** — In design questions, "total sludge" includes both wasted sludge AND effluent solids lost. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** — Always carry units. KTU examiners explicitly look for unit clarity in full-mark answers.
> 5. **Confusing SRT with HRT** — SRT ($\theta_c$) is biomass retention time ≠ Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT). For CSTR, $\theta_c \gg$ HRT typically.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Sludge Definition:** Semi-solid residue from wastewater treatment; 94–98% water; 2–6% solids (raw).
- **Four Sources:** Primary, Secondary, Tertiary/Chemical, Grit/Screenings.
- **Key Equation to Memorize:** $P_X = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$
- **Observed Yield:** $Y_{obs} = Y / (1 + k_d \theta_c)$ — always less than $Y$.
- **SRT Effect:** $\uparrow \theta_c \Rightarrow \downarrow P_X$ (inverse relationship via endogenous decay).
- **Typical Y values:** $0.4$–$0.6$ kg VSS/kg BOD (aerobic, conventional).
- **Typical $k_d$ values:** $0.04$–$0.08$ day$^{-1}$ at $20^\circ C$.
- **Total Sludge Formula:** $P_{X,total} = P_{X,bio} + Q \cdot X_{eff}$ (include effluent solids).
- **Primary Sludge Formula:** $M_{primary} = Q \cdot \eta_{SS} \cdot SS_0 \cdot f_p$
- **Unit Conversion Rule:** $Q$ (m³/day) × Conc. (mg/L = g/m³) / 1000 = kg/day.
- **Design Implication:** Sludge handling = 30–60% of plant O&M cost → minimize via high SRT or MBR.
- **Don't Confuse:** SRT ≠ HRT; MLVSS ≠ MLSS; $Y$ (true yield) ≠ $Y_{obs}$ (observed).
- **Mass Balance Law:** Influent pollutant = Effluent + Sludge + Volatilization. Always check mass closure.
- **KTU 2024 NEP Emphasis:** Expect CO-mapped questions linking stoichiometry with NEP outcome "Design wastewater treatment systems considering sustainability and circular economy."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Exam Mantra:** *"Y on top, decay below, SRT inside — sludge falls as SRT climbs."* Memorize $P_X = Y \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) / (1 + k_d \theta_c)$ and you can solve 80% of sludge production numericals in KTU papers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conventional Biological Process: Activated Sludge Process (ASP) - Development of Material Balance Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Conventional Biological Process: Activated Sludge Process (ASP) - Development of Material Balance Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** is a **suspended-growth biological wastewater treatment** technology in which a mixed population of microorganisms (the *activated sludge*) is maintained in continuous contact with the organic substrate (wastewater) inside an **aeration tank** under controlled dissolved-oxygen conditions, followed by **gravity separation** in a secondary clarifier, partial **recycling of settled biomass (return activated sludge, RAS)**, and controlled **wasting of excess biomass (waste activated sludge, WAS)** to maintain a desired solids retention time (SRT).

The **Material Balance Equations** of the ASP are the steady-state mass-conservation relationships written around a **Control Volume (CV)** that encloses (a) the aeration tank, (b) the secondary clarifier, or (c) the entire reactor-clarifier system. They are derived using the principle of **Conservation of Mass**:

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} \pm \text{Reaction}$$

and they form the engineering basis for the design equations used to predict the **effluent substrate concentration, biomass production, oxygen demand, hydraulic retention time, and sludge-waste rate** of an activated-sludge system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must be able to (i) write the steady-state material balance for substrate (BOD/COD) and biomass (MLVSS) around the aeration tank and the entire system, (ii) derive expressions for **F/M ratio, SRT (θ_c), HRT (θ), observed yield (Y_obs), effluent substrate (S), oxygen requirement (O₂), and waste-sludge rate (Q_w)**, and (iii) solve numerical design problems based on these equations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy **company cafeteria**:

- The **kitchen (aeration tank)** has hungry employees (*microorganisms*) eating food (*organic substrate*).
- The **dining hall (clarifier)** lets them sit, eat calmly, and the leftovers (sludge) settle down.
- The **manager** continuously sends some employees back to the kitchen (*return sludge*) so the work force stays stable, and **fires** a small, fixed number every day (*waste sludge*) to keep the team size constant.
- The **rate of eating** depends on how hungry they are (substrate concentration) and how many are in the kitchen (biomass concentration).

The *Material Balance* is simply the **cafeteria's accounts ledger** — it tells you, in kg/day:
- How much food entered the kitchen (influent BOD)
- How much was eaten (substrate utilized)
- How much was carried away on plates (effluent BOD)
- How many employees stayed, returned, or were fired (sludge production and wasting)

The **food-to-microorganism ratio (F/M)** is exactly the **portions-per-person** in the cafeteria — a high F/M means under-loaded staff (poor treatment), a low F/M means over-loaded staff (poor settling).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in ASP Design (must memorise):**
> - **F/M** = 0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹ (conventional ASP)
> - **SRT (θ_c)** = 5 – 15 days (conventional)
> - **HRT (θ)** = 4 – 8 hours
> - **MLVSS (X)** = 1500 – 4000 mg/L
> - **Yield coefficient Y** = 0.4 – 0.8 mg VSS/mg BOD
> - **Endogenous decay k_d** = 0.04 – 0.08 day⁻¹
> - **Maximum specific growth k** = 2 – 10 day⁻¹

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Activated-Sludge Flow-Sheet and Choice of Control Volume

A conventional ASP consists of three coupled unit operations:

1. **Aeration tank** — plug-flow or completely-mixed, volume **V**, MLVSS concentration **X**.
2. **Secondary clarifier** — separates effluent (Q) from settled sludge; underflow splits into **return sludge (Q_r, X_r)** and **waste sludge (Q_w, X_w)**.
3. **Effluent stream** — leaves with flow **Q** and residual substrate **S** and escaping solids **X_e**.

Three different control volumes (CVs) can be chosen:

| CV Boundary | Includes | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **CV-1** | Aeration tank only | Substrate utilisation, F/M ratio |
| **CV-2** | Clarifier only | Sludge balance, SRT definition |
| **CV-3** | Whole system (tank + clarifier) | Effluent quality, O₂ demand, net sludge production |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Material Balance Development

**Step 1 — Identify flows and streams entering/leaving the CV.**
Influent **Q** with substrate **S₀** and biomass **X₀**; effluent **Q** with **S** and **X_e**; recycle **Q_r** with **X_r**; waste **Q_w** with **X_w**.

**Step 2 — Write the generalised mass balance.**
For any conserved species *C*:

$$V\frac{dC}{dt} = Q\,C_{in} - Q\,C_{out} + r_C\,V$$

At **steady state**, $dC/dt = 0$, so:

$$0 = Q\,C_{in} - Q\,C_{out} + r_C\,V$$

**Step 3 — Apply to substrate (BOD):**
Substrate is *consumed* by reaction, so $r_S$ is negative. The classical **Monod–Herbert expression** for specific substrate removal rate is:

$$r_{su} = -\frac{k\,X\,S}{K_s + S}\quad [\text{mg/L}\cdot\text{d}^{-1}]$$

**Step 4 — Apply to biomass (MLVSS):**
Biomass *grows* and *decays*:

$$r_g = Y\cdot(-r_{su}) - k_d\,X = \frac{Y\,k\,X\,S}{K_s + S} - k_d\,X$$

**Step 5 — Combine with hydraulic flows** to obtain engineering parameters such as SRT, F/M, Y_obs.

**Step 6 — Solve the simultaneous balances** for the unknown design parameters: **S, X, Q_w, O₂, P_x**.

### 2.3 Why the Material Balance is the Backbone of ASP Design

- It converts a **biological process** into a **quantitative engineering design tool**.
- It allows designers to **predict effluent quality (S)** *before* building the plant.
- It quantifies **oxygen demand (aerobic degradation)**, which determines **aeration-blower power**.
- It gives the **waste-sludge flow rate Q_w**, which determines the **sludge-handling cost**.
- It is the **direct basis of KTU numerical problems** in Module 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> In a real plant, the operator can *manipulate* only two knobs: (1) the **return-sludge rate Q_r** (pump speed) and (2) the **waste-sludge rate Q_w** (valve opening). All other performance parameters (F/M, SRT, MLVSS) respond to these two knobs. The material balance tells the operator *exactly* what knob setting to use.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorise symbol, definition, and units.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydraulic Retention Time | $\theta = V/Q$ | h or d | Time wastewater stays in aeration tank |
| 2 | F/M Ratio | $F/M = (Q\,S_0)/(V\,X)$ | d⁻¹ | Food-to-Microorganism loading |
| 3 | Solids Retention Time (SRT / MCRT) | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V\,X}{Q_w\,X_w + Q_e\,X_e}$ | d | Mean age of biomass |
| 4 | Specific Substrate Removal Rate | $U = (S_0 - S)/(\theta\,X)$ | d⁻¹ | Inverse of F/M at S ≪ S₀ |
| 5 | Net Specific Growth Rate | $1/\theta_c = Y\,U - k_d$ | d⁻¹ | Core kinetic relation |
| 6 | Observed Yield | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d\,\theta_c}$ | mg VSS/mg BOD | Actual sludge produced per BOD removed |
| 7 | Effluent Substrate (Herbert–Lawrence) | $S = \dfrac{K_s\,(1 + k_d\,\theta_c)}{\theta_c\,(Y\,k - k_d) - 1}$ | mg/L | Valid for CMAS with recycle |
| 8 | Effluent Substrate (simplified, S ≪ K_s) | $S = \dfrac{K_s\,(1 + k_d\,\theta_c)}{\theta_c\,(Y\,k - k_d)}$ | mg/L | Substrate-limited form |
| 9 | Reactor MLVSS (CMAS) | $X = \dfrac{\theta_c\,Y\,(S_0 - S)}{\theta\,(1 + k_d\,\theta_c)}$ | mg/L | Biomass in aeration tank |
| 10 | Return Sludge Concentration | $X_r = \dfrac{X}{1 - Q/Q_r}$ | mg/L | Mass balance on clarifier underflow |
| 11 | Sludge Production Rate | $P_x = \dfrac{Y_{obs}\,Q\,(S_0 - S)}{1000}$ | kg/d | Total dry solids wasted |
| 12 | Oxygen Requirement | $O_2 = \dfrac{Q\,(S_0 - S) - 1.42\,P_x\,1000}{1000}$ | kg O₂/d | 1.42 = COD of biomass |
| 13 | Recycle Ratio (from X_r relation) | $R = Q_r/Q = X/(X_r - X)$ | – | Dimensionless |
| 14 | Volumetric Substrate Loading | $VSL = Q\,S_0/V$ | kg BOD/m³·d | Used for design checks |
| 15 | Sludge Volume Index | $SVI = (V_{30}/MLSS)\times 1000$ | mL/g | Settleability check |

**Default / Standard Values to Use When Data is Missing:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum specific substrate removal rate | $k$ | 3 – 5 | d⁻¹ |
| Half-saturation constant | $K_s$ | 25 – 100 | mg/L BOD |
| True yield coefficient | $Y$ | 0.5 – 0.65 | mg VSS/mg BOD |
| Endogenous decay coefficient | $k_d$ | 0.05 – 0.075 | d⁻¹ |
| Effluent suspended solids | $X_e$ | 10 – 30 | mg/L |
| Return sludge concentration | $X_r$ | 8000 – 12000 | mg/L |
| Influent biomass | $X_0$ | 0 (assumed) | mg/L |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Tip (KTU Examiner):** When solving a problem, *always* state the **assumptions** you are making (steady state, completely-mixed aeration tank, $X_0 = 0$, $X_e \approx 0$, single CSTR, Monod kinetics). Marks are specifically awarded for the assumption list.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Substrate (BOD) Mass Balance Around Aeration Tank

**Assumptions (must be stated):**
- Steady state: $dS/dt = 0$.
- Completely-mixed aeration tank, so reactor concentration = effluent concentration = $S$.
- Influent biomass $X_0 = 0$.
- Substrate-utilisation rate follows Monod kinetics: $r_{su} = -kXS/(K_s + S)$.

**Governing equation (substrate balance on aeration tank):**

$$V\frac{dS}{dt} = Q\,S_0 + Q_r\,S - (Q + Q_r)\,S + r_{su}\,V$$

Because the return sludge stream carries substrate at the *same* concentration as the reactor effluent (it is settled effluent), $Q_r\,S$ on the inlet side cancels with $Q_r\,S$ on the outlet side:

$$0 = Q\,S_0 - Q\,S + r_{su}\,V$$

**Substitute Monod rate expression** ($r_{su} = -kXS/(K_s+S)$):

$$0 = Q(S_0 - S) - \frac{k\,X\,S}{K_s + S}\,V$$

**Define HRT:** $\theta = V/Q$, then $V = Q\theta$. Substitute:

$$0 = Q(S_0 - S) - \frac{k\,X\,S}{K_s + S}\,Q\theta$$

**Divide throughout by $Q$:**

$$0 = (S_0 - S) - \frac{k\,X\,S}{K_s + S}\,\theta$$

**Rearrange to obtain the working form:**

$$\boxed{\,S_0 - S = \frac{k\,X\,S\,\theta}{K_s + S}\,}$$

Dividing both sides by $\theta X$ gives the **specific substrate removal rate**:

$$U = \frac{S_0 - S}{\theta\,X} = \frac{k\,S}{K_s + S}$$

This is the classic Monod relationship.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Biomass (MLVSS) Mass Balance Around Aeration Tank

**Assumptions:**
- Steady state, completely-mixed.
- Net growth = growth − endogenous decay: $r_g = Y(-r_{su}) - k_d X$.
- Influent biomass $X_0 = 0$.

**Mass balance for biomass on aeration tank only:**

$$V\frac{dX}{dt} = 0 + Q_r\,X_r - (Q + Q_r)\,X + r_g\,V$$

The recycle stream brings $Q_r\,X_r$ solids in and $Q_r\,X$ leaves with the mixed liquor, so the net for the reactor *with* recycle is correct only if we consider the **whole system** (see derivation 3). For the *whole system*, the recycle line is internal and cancels.

**For the whole system (CV-3) the biomass balance becomes:**

$$V\frac{dX}{dt} = 0 = Q\,X_0 - \big[Q\,X_e + Q_w\,X_w\big] + r_g\,V$$

With $X_0 = 0$ and at steady state:

$$0 = -Q\,X_e - Q_w\,X_w + r_g\,V$$

**Substitute $r_g$ and $V = Q\theta$:**

$$Q\,X_e + Q_w\,X_w = \big(Y(-r_{su}) - k_d\,X\big)\,Q\theta$$

Divide by $Q\theta$:

$$\frac{X_e}{\theta} + \frac{Q_w\,X_w}{V} = Y\,U - k_d$$

The **first term** is the effluent loss per unit volume, and the second is the waste rate per unit volume. Define the **solids retention time (SRT / θ_c)** as:

$$\frac{1}{\theta_c} = \frac{X_e}{\theta} + \frac{Q_w\,X_w}{V}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y\,U - k_d\,}$$

This is the **fundamental kinetic design equation** of the activated-sludge process.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined Derivation for Effluent Substrate *S*

Starting from the fundamental equation:

$$\frac{1}{\theta_c} = Y\,U - k_d = Y\left(\frac{S_0 - S}{\theta\,X}\right) - k_d$$

But from the substrate balance in derivation 1, when $S \ll S_0$ and operating in the substrate-limited region ($S \ll K_s$), $U \approx kS/K_s$, so $X\theta = (S_0 - S)K_s/(kS)$.

A cleaner approach uses the Monod form directly into the fundamental equation:

$$Y\,\frac{k\,S}{K_s + S} - k_d = \frac{1}{\theta_c}$$

Rearrange:

$$\frac{Y\,k\,S}{K_s + S} = k_d + \frac{1}{\theta_c}$$

$$Y\,k\,S = \left(k_d + \frac{1}{\theta_c}\right)(K_s + S)$$

$$Y\,k\,S - \left(k_d + \frac{1}{\theta_c}\right)S = \left(k_d + \frac{1}{\theta_c}\right)K_s$$

$$S\,\big[Y\,k - k_d - 1/\theta_c\big] = K_s\big[k_d + 1/\theta_c\big]$$

$$\boxed{\,S = \dfrac{K_s\,(1 + k_d\,\theta_c)}{\theta_c\,(Y\,k - k_d) - 1}\,}$$

This is the **Herbert–Lawrence effluent-substrate equation**, the single most important result in ASP design.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Observed (Net) Yield Coefficient $Y_{obs}$

In the steady-state whole-system biomass balance, the **wasted biomass = net sludge production**, $P_x$:

$$P_x = Q_w\,X_w + Q_e\,X_e = Y_{obs}\,Q(S_0 - S)$$

But from the fundamental kinetic equation:

$$\frac{1}{\theta_c} = Y\,U - k_d = Y\cdot\frac{S_0 - S}{\theta X} - k_d$$

Multiply through by $\theta X$:

$$\frac{\theta X}{\theta_c} = Y(S_0 - S) - k_d\,\theta X$$

Recognise $\theta X = V X/Q$, hence:

$$\frac{V X}{\theta_c\,Q} = Y(S_0 - S) - k_d\,\frac{V X}{Q}$$

$$\frac{V X}{Q}\left(\frac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d\right) = Y(S_0 - S)$$

Now, sludge production $P_x = (V X)/\theta_c$ (definition of SRT). So:

$$P_x\left(1 + k_d\,\theta_c\right) = Y\,Q\,\theta\,(S_0 - S)$$

But $\theta = V/Q$, and recalling $P_x = Y_{obs}Q(S_0 - S)$, we identify:

$$\boxed{\,Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d\,\theta_c}\,}$$

**Limiting cases:**
- If $\theta_c \to 0$ (no retention), $Y_{obs} \to Y$ (true yield).
- If $\theta_c \to \infty$ (no wastage), $Y_{obs} \to 0$ (all biomass decays endogenously).

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Aeration Tank MLVSS Concentration *X*

From the substrate balance, $S_0 - S = kXS\theta/(K_s + S)$, hence:

$$X = \frac{(S_0 - S)(K_s + S)}{k\,S\,\theta}$$

Combining with the kinetic relation $1/\theta_c = Y(kS/(K_s + S)) - k_d$ and solving simultaneously yields:

$$X = \frac{\theta_c}{\theta}\cdot\frac{Y(S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d\,\theta_c}$$

$$\boxed{\,X = \dfrac{Y_{obs}\,(S_0 - S)\,\theta_c}{\theta}\,}$$

This is the **reactor MLVSS design equation**, used to size the aeration tank.

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6 — Oxygen Requirement (Theoretical)

The oxygen consumed per unit time is the **BOD removed minus the oxygen equivalent locked into new cells**:

$$O_2 = Q(S_0 - S) - 1.42\,P_x$$

The factor **1.42 kg O₂/kg VSS** is the **theoretical oxygen demand of the cell mass** (≈ C₅H₇NO₂), i.e. the amount of oxygen required to oxidise one kg of biomass completely.

**Expanded form:**

$$O_2 = Q(S_0 - S) - 1.42\,\dfrac{Y_{obs}\,Q(S_0 - S)}{1000}\quad [\text{kg O}_2/\text{d}]$$

Dividing by $Q$ gives the **specific oxygen demand per m³ of wastewater treated**:

$$O_2/Q = (S_0 - S) - 1.42\,Y_{obs}(S_0 - S) = (S_0 - S)(1 - 1.42\,Y_{obs})$$

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{O_2}{Q} = (S_0 - S)\big(1 - 1.42\,Y_{obs}\big)\,}\quad [\text{kg O}_2/\text{m}^3]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** Real plants must also supply O₂ for **nitrification** when ammonium removal is required. The modified equation then becomes:
> $$O_2 = Q(S_0 - S) - 1.42 P_x + 4.57\,Q\,(N_0 - N)$$
> where $4.57$ is the **oxygen demand per g of ammonia oxidised to nitrate** (in kg O₂/kg NH₃-N).

---

### 3.7 Derivation 7 — Return Sludge Concentration and Recycle Ratio

Mass balance on the **secondary clarifier** (solids only):

$$(Q + Q_r)\,X = Q\,X_e + Q_r\,X_r$$

Solve for $X_r$:

$$X_r = \frac{(Q + Q_r)X - Q\,X_e}{Q_r} = X + \frac{Q}{Q_r}(X - X_e)$$

If $X_e \ll X$ (typical), then:

$$\boxed{\,X_r \approx X\left(1 + \dfrac{Q}{Q_r}\right) = \dfrac{X}{1 - R}\,}$$

where **$R = Q_r/Q$** is the **recycle ratio**. Common design: $R = 0.25 - 1.0$ (25–100 %).

---

### 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Common Numerical Scenarios in KTU

| # | Given | Find | Key Equation(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Q, S₀, S, V, X | F/M, θ | $F/M = QS_0/VX$; $\theta = V/Q$ |
| 2 | Q, V, X, X_r, Q_w, X_w, X_e | θ_c | $\theta_c = VX/(Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e)$ |
| 3 | θ_c, Y, k_d, k, K_s | S | $S = K_s(1 + k_d \theta_c)/[\theta_c(Yk - k_d) - 1]$ |
| 4 | Y, k_d, θ_c | Y_obs | $Y_{obs} = Y/(1 + k_d \theta_c)$ |
| 5 | Y_obs, Q, S₀, S | P_x (kg/d) | $P_x = Y_{obs}\,Q(S_0 - S)/1000$ |
| 6 | Q, S₀, S, P_x | O₂ (kg/d) | $O_2 = Q(S_0 - S) - 1.42 P_x$ |
| 7 | X, X_r, Q | Q_r | $Q_r = QX/(X_r - X)$ |
| 8 | θ_c, θ, S₀, S, Y, k_d | X | $X = Y_{obs}(S_0 - S)\theta_c/\theta$ |
