## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
Define the **Solids Retention Time (SRT, θ_c)** of an activated-sludge process. State its typical design range for a conventional ASP and explain its physical significance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
SRT (also called MCRT, Mean Cell Residence Time) is the **average time a unit of biomass (MLVSS) remains in the system** before being wasted, defined as:

$$\theta_c = \frac{V\,X}{Q_w\,X_w + Q_e\,X_e}\quad [\text{days}]$$

**Typical design range for conventional ASP: 5 – 15 days.** Physically, θ_c represents the **age of the sludge**; a higher θ_c means the biomass has had more time to acclimatise and oxidise substrate, but very high values cause pinpoint floc, poor settling, and excessive endogenous decay.

*(Marking scheme: Definition 1.5 m, Range 0.5 m, Significance 1 m.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
Differentiate between **F/M ratio** and **SRT (θ_c)**. Why are they considered the two most important operational parameters of an activated-sludge plant?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | F/M Ratio | SRT (θ_c) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $F/M = Q\,S_0/(V\,X)$ | $\theta_c = VX/(Q_wX_w + Q_eX_e)$ |
| Meaning | Mass loading of food per unit biomass per day | Average age of biomass in the system |
| Units | d⁻¹ | d |
| Typical | 0.2 – 0.5 d⁻¹ (conventional) | 5 – 15 d |
| Controlled by | MLVSS concentration $X$ in the tank | Waste-sludge flow rate $Q_w$ |
| Effect of increase | Lower effluent quality, higher growth | More endogenous decay, poorer settling, more O₂ used |

F/M quantifies **what the biomass eats per day** (loading rate), while SRT quantifies **how long the biomass lives** (residence time). They together fix the **kinetic state** of the system: $1/\theta_c = Y(F/M \cdot 1/S_0 \cdot S_0) - k_d$.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Develop the steady-state material-balance equations for **substrate (BOD)** and **biomass (MLVSS)** around (i) the aeration tank alone and (ii) the entire activated-sludge system, clearly listing the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An activated-sludge plant treats a domestic wastewater flow of **Q = 50,000 m³/d** with an influent BOD of **S₀ = 250 mg/L**. The aeration-tank volume is **V = 10,000 m³**, the MLVSS is **X = 2000 mg/L**, and the return-sludge concentration is **X_r = 10,000 mg/L**. The biological constants are **Y = 0.6 mg VSS/mg BOD**, **k_d = 0.06 d⁻¹**, **k = 5 d⁻¹**, **K_s = 60 mg/L**. The wasted-sludge concentration equals the return-sludge concentration (X_w = X_r), and the effluent suspended-solids concentration is **X_e = 20 mg/L**.

Calculate:
1. F/M ratio and HRT,
2. Observed yield Y_obs (assuming θ_c = 10 d),
3. Effluent substrate concentration S using the Herbert–Lawrence equation,
4. Sludge-production rate P_x in kg/d, and
5. Theoretical oxygen requirement in kg O₂/d. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) — Theory (7 marks):**

*Assumptions:*
- Steady state ($dS/dt = 0$, $dX/dt = 0$).
- Completely-mixed aeration tank.
- Influent contains no biomass ($X_0 = 0$).
- Single-substrate (BOD) and Monod kinetics apply.
- Cell lysis products are not separately tracked.
- Return sludge carries substrate at concentration $S$.

*(i) Aeration tank (CV-1) — Substrate:*
$$V\frac{dS}{dt} = QS_0 - (Q + Q_r)S + Q_rS + r_{su}V$$
At steady state with $Q_r S$ cancelling:
$$0 = Q(S_0 - S) - \frac{kXS}{K_s + S}V$$
$$\therefore S_0 - S = \frac{kXS}{K_s + S}\theta$$

*(i) Aeration tank (CV-1) — Biomass:*
$$0 = -Q_r X + (Q+Q_r)X - Q_r X_r + r_g V$$
With recycle-line terms present; simplified to whole system in (ii).

*(ii) Whole system (CV-3) — Substrate:*
$$0 = Q(S_0 - S) - \frac{kXS}{K_s + S}V$$

*(ii) Whole system (CV-3) — Biomass:*
$$0 = -QX_e - Q_w X_w + (Y\cdot\frac{kXS}{K_s + S} - k_d X)V$$
$$\therefore \frac{1}{\theta_c} = Y\cdot\frac{kS}{K_s + S} - k_d$$

*(Mark split: assumptions 1 m, substrate 3 m, biomass 3 m.)*

---

**(b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**1. F/M and HRT**

$$\theta = V/Q = 10{,}000/50{,}000 = 0.2\,\text{d} = 4.8\,\text{h}$$

$$F/M = \frac{Q\,S_0}{V\,X} = \frac{50{,}000\times 250}{10{,}000\times 2000} = \frac{1.25\times 10^7}{2.0\times 10^7} = 0.625\,\text{d}^{-1}$$

**2. Observed yield (θ_c = 10 d)**

$$Y_{obs} = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d\theta_c} = \frac{0.6}{1 + 0.06\times 10} = \frac{0.6}{1.6} = 0.375\,\text{mg VSS/mg BOD}$$

**3. Effluent substrate S**

$$S = \frac{K_s(1 + k_d\theta_c)}{\theta_c(Yk - k_d) - 1} = \frac{60\,(1 + 0.06\times 10)}{10\,(0.6\times 5 - 0.06) - 1}$$

$$= \frac{60\times 1.6}{10\times 2.94 - 1} = \frac{96}{29.4 - 1} = \frac{96}{28.4} = 3.38\,\text{mg/L}$$

**4. Sludge production**

$$P_x = Y_{obs}\,Q\,(S_0 - S)/1000 = 0.375 \times 50{,}000 \times (250 - 3.38)/1000$$

$$= 0.375 \times 50{,}000 \times 246.62/1000 = 0.375 \times 12{,}331 = 4624\,\text{kg VSS/d}$$

**5. Oxygen requirement**

$$O_2 = Q(S_0 - S) - 1.42\,P_x = 50{,}000 \times 246.62/1000 - 1.42\times 4624$$

$$= 12{,}331 - 6566 = 5765\,\text{kg O}_2/\text{d}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Starting from the general mass-conservation equation, derive the **Herbert–Lawrence equation** for effluent substrate concentration of a completely-mixed activated-sludge system. State clearly the kinetic assumptions and the role of the Monod constants. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A municipal wastewater treatment plant uses a conventional activated-sludge process with the following design data:
- Flow Q = 25,000 m³/d, Influent BOD S₀ = 200 mg/L
- Aeration tank volume V = 5,000 m³, MLVSS X = 3000 mg/L
- Return-sludge flow Q_r = 12,500 m³/d, X_r = 10,000 mg/L
- Wasted-sludge flow Q_w = 250 m³/d, X_w = 9,000 mg/L
- Effluent SS X_e = 15 mg/L
- Kinetic constants: Y = 0.55, k_d = 0.05 d⁻¹, k = 4 d⁻¹, K_s = 50 mg/L

Determine: (i) θ, (ii) θ_c, (iii) F/M, (iv) Y_obs, (v) effluent S, (vi) P_x, (vii) O₂ demand. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from steady-state general mass balance $0 = \text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Gen} - \text{Cons}$ on whole system:

For substrate: $0 = Q(S_0 - S) + r_{su}V$ with $r_{su} = -kXS/(K_s + S)$

For biomass: $0 = -QX_e - Q_w X_w + r_g V$ with $r_g = Y(-r_{su}) - k_d X$

Dividing biomass balance by $V$ and recognising $1/\theta_c = (Q_e X_e + Q_w X_w)/VX$:

$$\frac{1}{\theta_c} = Y\cdot\frac{kS}{K_s + S} - k_d$$

Solving for $S$:

$$YkS = (k_d + 1/\theta_c)(K_s + S) = k_d K_s + k_d S + K_s/\theta_c + S/\theta_c$$

$$S[Yk - k_d - 1/\theta_c] = K_s[k_d + 1/\theta_c]$$

$$S = \frac{K_s(1 + k_d\theta_c)}{\theta_c(Yk - k_d) - 1}\quad\text{(Herbert–Lawrence equation)}$$

*Roles:* $k$ = maximum specific substrate removal rate, $K_s$ = substrate concentration at half $k$, $Y$ = true yield, $k_d$ = endogenous decay rate.

---

**(b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) HRT**
$$\theta = V/Q = 5000/25{,}000 = 0.2\,\text{d} = 4.8\,\text{h}$$

**(ii) SRT**
$$\theta_c = \frac{VX}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e} = \frac{5000 \times 3000}{250\times 9000 + 25{,}000 \times 15}$$

Numerator: $1.5 \times 10^7$.
Denominator: $2.25 \times 10^6 + 3.75 \times 10^5 = 2.625 \times 10^6$.

$$\theta_c = 1.5\times 10^7 / 2.625 \times 10^6 = 5.71\,\text{d}$$

**(iii) F/M**
$$F/M = \frac{25{,}000 \times 200}{5000 \times 3000} = \frac{5.0\times 10^6}{1.5\times 10^7} = 0.333\,\text{d}^{-1}$$

**(iv) Y_obs**
$$Y_{obs} = \frac{0.55}{1 + 0.05 \times 5.71} = \frac{0.55}{1.2855} = 0.428\,\text{mg VSS/mg BOD}$$

**(v) Effluent S (Herbert–Lawrence)**
$$S = \frac{50\,(1 + 0.05\times 5.71)}{5.71\,(0.55\times 4 - 0.05) - 1} = \frac{50 \times 1.2855}{5.71 \times 2.15 - 1}$$

$$= \frac{64.275}{12.2765 - 1} = \frac{64.275}{11.2765} = 5.70\,\text{mg/L}$$

**(vi) P_x**
$$P_x = 0.428 \times 25{,}000 \times (200 - 5.70)/1000 = 0.428 \times 25{,}000 \times 194.3/1000$$

$$= 0.428 \times 4857.5 = 2079\,\text{kg VSS/d}$$

**(vii) O₂ demand**
$$O_2 = 25{,}000 \times 194.3/1000 - 1.42 \times 2079 = 4857.5 - 2952 = 1905\,\text{kg O}_2/\text{d}$$

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU ESE Module 2):**
>
> 1. **Missing assumption list** — *2 marks lost immediately*. Always begin any ASP design with the 5–6 standard assumptions (steady state, CMAS, X₀ = 0, Monod kinetics, etc.).
> 2. **Wrong control volume** — Many students apply the recycle stream $Q_r$ when writing the **whole-system** balance, causing duplication of the $Q_r X$ term. Remember: for the *whole system* the recycle is internal and cancels; for the *aeration tank alone* it appears on both sides and cancels *for the substrate* (because return sludge has the same $S$ as reactor) but **does not** cancel for the biomass — be careful.
> 3. **Units mismatch in P_x** — $Q$ is in m³/d, $S_0 - S$ in mg/L = g/m³. The product $Q(S_0 - S)$ is in g/d. To convert to **kg/d**, divide by **1000**. Students forget this and report answers 1000× too high.
> 4. **Forgetting $X_e$ in θ_c** — Many texts use $X_e = 0$. If $X_e$ is non-zero (15–30 mg/L is realistic), it *must* appear in the denominator: $\theta_c = VX/(Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e)$. Marks are specifically reserved for this term.
> 5. **Using F/M in place of U** — The kinetic equation $1/\theta_c = YU - k_d$ uses **specific substrate removal rate $U$**, not F/M. They are different: $U = (S_0 - S)/(\theta X)$ while $F/M = S_0/(\theta X)$.
> 6. **Herbert–Lawrence denominator sign** — The denominator is $\theta_c(Yk - k_d) - 1$, **not** $1 - \theta_c(Yk - k_d)$. A sign error here gives a *negative effluent S*, which is physically impossible but still gets a partial check.
> 7. **O₂ demand sign** — The oxygen demand is **$Q(S_0 - S) - 1.42 P_x$**, never the other way round. The cell mass *retains* some of the substrate's chemical energy, so actual O₂ demand is **less** than the BOD removed.
> 8. **Return-sludge balance** — A common error is to use $X_r = X/Q_r$ (which is dimensionally wrong) instead of the correct $X_r = X(1 + Q/Q_r) = X/(1 - R)$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Module 2 Revision Sheet — ASP Material Balances**
>
> ✅ **Three control volumes**: aeration tank only (CV-1), clarifier only (CV-2), whole system (CV-3).
>
> ✅ **Five core equations to memorise verbatim**:
> 1. $1/\theta_c = YU - k_d$ (fundamental kinetic)
> 2. $Y_{obs} = Y/(1 + k_d\theta_c)$ (observed yield)
> 3. $S = K_s(1 + k_d\theta_c)/[\theta_c(Yk - k_d) - 1]$ (Herbert–Lawrence)
> 4. $X = Y_{obs}(S_0 - S)\theta_c/\theta$ (reactor MLVSS)
> 5. $O_2 = Q(S_0 - S) - 1.42 P_x$ (theoretical O₂ demand)
>
> ✅ **Key derived quantities**: $\theta = V/Q$; $F/M = QS_0/(VX)$; $U = (S_0 - S)/(\theta X)$; $\theta_c = VX/(Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e)$; $P_x = Y_{obs}Q(S_0 - S)/1000$ kg/d.
>
> ✅ **Standard values**: F/M = 0.2–0.5 d⁻¹, θ_c = 5–15 d, θ = 4–8 h, X = 1500–4000 mg/L, Y = 0.5–0.65, k_d = 0.05–0.075 d⁻¹, k = 3–5 d⁻¹, K_s = 25–100 mg/L.
>
> ✅ **Assumptions must be written** at the start of any derivation or numerical: (a) steady state, (b) completely-mixed, (c) X₀ = 0, (d) Monod kinetics, (e) X_e small or given, (f) return sludge carries substrate at concentration S.
>
> ✅ **Always check physical consistency**: S must be > 0 and < S₀; P_x must be > 0; O₂ demand must be positive and less than $Q(S_0 - S)/1000$ kg/d.
>
> ✅ **Factor 1.42 kg O₂/kg VSS** is the COD-equivalent of biomass (cell formula C₅H₇NO₂); remember it.
>
> ✅ **KTU 2024 numerical pattern**: typically a 14-mark question combines (a) derivation 7 m + (b) numerical 7 m with **5–6 sub-parts** requiring F/M, θ_c, Y_obs, S, P_x, O₂. Practice this entire chain at least 3 times.
>
> ✅ **Herbert–Lawrence equation** is the *most-tested* single equation in Module 2 — know its derivation, its assumptions, and its limitations (substrate-limited, single CSTR, no inhibition).
>
> ✅ **Units check is mandatory**: convert $S_0, S, X$ all to **mg/L**; $Q$ in m³/d; $V$ in m³; θ in d; P_x in **kg/d** (divide by 1000 from g/d).
>
> ✅ **SVI and settling** are part of the operational check (not material balance), but expect a 3-mark short question pairing F/M with SVI.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trickling Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trickling Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Trickling Filter (TF)** is a **fixed-film biological wastewater treatment reactor** in which wastewater is distributed over a bed of coarse, inert, porous media (traditionally crushed rock, slag, or modern plastic media). Microorganisms grow as a **biofilm (zoogleal slime layer)** attached to the media surface. As the wastewater trickles downward by gravity over this slime layer, organic matter is **metabolically oxidised** by the active biomass, and the treated effluent is collected by an **underdrain system** at the base.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Block:** A trickling filter is an aerobic, attached-growth (fixed-film) biological treatment unit. The driving force for the process is the contact between the trickling wastewater (substrate) and the slime layer (biomass) under ambient oxygen conditions provided by natural draft or forced aeration through the filter bed.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Hanging Garden of Microbes" Concept)

Imagine a **multi-storey apartment complex** where every balcony is covered with **greedy, microscopic housekeepers** (the biofilm microbes). Instead of people living in the apartments, **dirty water (sewage) is sprayed from the rooftop**. As the water trickles past each balcony, the housekeepers **scrape out the food** (dissolved organics, BOD) from the water. By the time the water reaches the ground floor, it has been **cleaned** and is collected in a sump.

- The **building structure** = the porous filter media (rock/plastic).
- The **housekeepers** = aerobic bacteria, protozoa, and fungi in the slime layer.
- The **falling water film** = the wastewater providing substrate.
- The **exhaust fans** = natural/forced ventilation supplying oxygen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the water "trickles" and does not flood:** The voids in the media bed are 35-65% of the total volume, ensuring the filter remains **unsaturated** and aerobic — a critical distinction from submerged filters like RBCs or SAFs.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 Bold Constants)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **BOD Removal Efficiency** | **65 – 95 %** | % |
| **Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR)** | **1 – 200** | m³/m²·d |
| **Organic Loading Rate (OLR)** | **0.1 – 6.0** | kg BOD/m³·d |
| **Media Depth** | **0.9 – 3.0** | m |
| **Recirculation Ratio (R)** | **0 – 4** | dimensionless |
| **Dosing Rate (Arm)** | **0.5 – 5.0** | mm/pass |
| **Filter Media Specific Surface Area** | **40 – 200** | m²/m³ |
| **Void Ratio (ε)** | **0.35 – 0.65** | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Heart of the Trickling Filter** is the **biological film** (10⁶–10⁸ organisms/cm² of media), dominated by *Zoogloea*, *Pseudomonas*, *Beggiatoa*, *Sphaerotilus*, and grazing protozoa/ciliates.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Process Steps in a Trickling Filter (The "Five-Stage" Logic)

The purification mechanism is **NOT a single reaction**; it is a **sequential mass-transfer + biological oxidation** process. For your KTU answer, always describe these five operational stages:

1. **Distribution:** Rotary distributor arms (powered by hydraulic head of wastewater, **2-3 m** above media) spray wastewater uniformly over the bed.
2. **Wetting & Mass Transfer:** A thin liquid film forms on the slime layer. Organic substrate **diffuses** from the bulk liquid → into the biofilm.
3. **Biological Oxidation (The 'Engine' of the Unit):** Aerobic microbes in the biofilm **mineralise** organics:
   $$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{enzymes}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{biomass} + \text{energy}$$
4. **Sloughing:** As the biofilm thickens (> 0.1 mm), it is sheared off by hydraulic shear — these sloughed particles become **humus sludge** to be removed in the secondary clarifier.
5. **Effluent Collection & Ventilation:** The underdrain collects clarified effluent and supports air ingress from the bottom (driven by **thermal convection** or **forced draft fans**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Trickling Filter classified as "Fixed-Film" but not "Submerged"?** Because the biomass is **immobilised on a solid surface** (media) and the wastewater flows as a **thin film** over it, NOT through it under submerged conditions.

## 2.2 KTU 2024 Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (No Vertical Pipes!)

The following equations are **board-exam mandatory** for trickling filter design. Memorise the structure, not just the formula.

| S.No. | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Recirculation Factor (NRC)** | $F = \dfrac{1 + R}{(1 + 0.1 \cdot R)^2}$ | $R$ = recirculation ratio (dimensionless) |
| 2 | **BOD Removal Efficiency (NRC)** | $E_{20} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{0.0561}{\sqrt[3]{W \cdot v}}}$ | $W$ = BOD loading (lb/ac·d), $v$ = dosing rate (gal/ft²·h) |
| 3 | **Eckenfelder Model** | $\dfrac{S_e}{S_i} = \exp\!\left(-K \cdot \dfrac{D}{Q^n}\right)$ | $D$ = depth (m), $Q$ = hydraulic loading (m/d), $K$ = treatability constant, $n$ = 0.5–0.7 |
| 4 | **Velz / Germain Model** | $\dfrac{S_e}{S_i} = \exp\!\left(-K \cdot \dfrac{D}{Q}\right)$ | $Q$ = flow rate (m³/d), $K$ = rate constant (m⁻¹) |
| 5 | **Schultz Formula (Plastic Media)** | $\dfrac{S_e}{S_i} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{K \cdot D}{Q^{0.5}}\right)$ | used for high-rate synthetic media filters |
| 6 | **Hydraulic Loading Rate** | $\text{HLR} = \dfrac{Q + R \cdot Q}{A}$ | $A$ = filter area (m²) |
| 7 | **Organic Loading Rate** | $\text{OLR} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{A \cdot D}$ | $V$ = filter volume, $S_0$ = influent BOD (mg/L) |
| 8 | **Efficiency with Recirculation (NRC)** | $\eta = \dfrac{1 + R - F \cdot (1 + R - E)}{1 + R}$ | $E$ = single-pass efficiency |
| 9 | **Air Requirement** | $\text{Air} = 0.115 \cdot \text{BOD}_{removed}$ | in m³/m³ of wastewater |
| 10 | **Number of Distributor Arms (Bio-disk equivalent)** | $N = \dfrac{D_f}{d} + 1$ | $D_f$ = filter diameter, $d$ = arm diameter |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION CHECK:** In all the formulas above, $S_e$ is effluent BOD and $S_i$ (or $S_0$) is influent BOD. **Never confuse $S_i$ with influent substrate concentration of a single-pass vs. recirculated stream.** This is the #1 error KTU examiners penalise.

## 2.3 Why Each Equation Matters in Real Engineering

- **Eckenfelder Model** is used for **low-organic, high-rate filters**; assumes first-order kinetics with depth.
- **Velz Model** assumes **BOD removal per unit depth is constant** — best for **single-stage, deep rock filters**.
- **NRC equations** are **empirical** and account for **recirculation** explicitly — used when the designer wants to optimise the recirculation ratio $R$.

## 2.4 Classification of Trickling Filters (KTU Board Favourite Table)

| Type | HLR (m³/m²·d) | OLR (kg/m³·d) | Recirculation | BOD Removal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard / Low-rate** | 1 – 5 | 0.1 – 0.3 | None / low | 80 – 90 % |
| **High-rate (Single-stage)** | 10 – 30 | 0.3 – 1.5 | 1 – 4 | 65 – 85 % |
| **Super High-rate** | 30 – 75 | 1.5 – 6.0 | 1 – 4 | 65 – 75 % |
| **Two-stage TF (Roughing + Polishing)** | 30 – 75 (each) | 1.0 – 3.0 (each) | Yes (both) | 85 – 95 % |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Trickling filters are the **workhorse of small-to-medium municipalities** (population 5,000 to 200,000) worldwide because they:
- Tolerate shock loads and toxic spills (the biofilm recovers faster than activated sludge).
- Operate with **low energy input** (no aeration tank; only a small pump for distribution).
- Have **low skilled-labour** requirements and tolerate intermittent operation (e.g., small rural panchayats in Kerala).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Eckenfelder First-Order Model

This is a **plug-flow assumption** applied to a vertical column of biofilm. We consider a differential slice of depth $dD$ inside the filter.

**Step 1 — Define the differential removal in an elemental depth slice $dD$:**
The substrate removal rate is proportional to the residual substrate concentration (first-order kinetics):
$$dS = -k \cdot S \cdot dD$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the form for $k$ (Eckenfelder's hydraulic dependence):**
Eckenfelder observed that the rate constant depends on hydraulic loading $Q^n$. Therefore:
$$k = \dfrac{K}{Q^n}$$

Substituting:
$$dS = -\dfrac{K}{Q^n} \cdot S \cdot dD$$

**Step 3 — Separate the variables and integrate across the full filter depth $D$:**
$$\int_{S_i}^{S_e} \dfrac{dS}{S} = -\dfrac{K}{Q^n} \cdot \int_0^D dD$$

**Step 4 — Carry out the integration:**
$$\left[\ln S\right]_{S_i}^{S_e} = -\dfrac{K \cdot D}{Q^n}$$

$$\ln S_e - \ln S_i = -\dfrac{K \cdot D}{Q^n}$$

**Step 5 — Take the exponential of both sides (since $e^{\ln x} = x$):**
$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{S_e}{S_i}\right) = -\dfrac{K \cdot D}{Q^n}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{S_e}{S_i} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{K \cdot D}{Q^n}\right)\;}$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation:**
- If $D \uparrow$ (deeper filter) → $S_e/S_i \downarrow$ → higher removal.
- If $Q \uparrow$ (higher hydraulic load) → $S_e/S_i \uparrow$ → lower removal (substrate passes too fast).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: NRC Recirculation Factor $F$

**Step 1 — Define what $F$ represents:** $F$ is a **dimensionless efficiency correction** that converts a single-pass BOD removal $E$ into an effective system efficiency when a portion of the effluent is recycled back to the filter inlet.

**Step 2 — Empirical basis (National Research Council, USA, 1946):**
The NRC committee fitted experimental data from rock-media trickling filters to obtain:
$$F = \dfrac{1 + R}{(1 + 0.1 \cdot R)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Limit-checking for KTU board:**

- If $R = 0$ (no recirculation): $F = 1/1 = 1$ → no change to efficiency. ✔
- If $R = 1$: $F = 2 / (1.1)^2 = 2/1.21 = 1.653$ → efficiency is multiplied by 1.653. ✔
- If $R \to \infty$: $F \to 1/(0.1)^2 \cdot R / R^2 = 100/R \to 0$ → efficiency gain diminishes beyond $R=4$. ✔

**Step 4 — Final overall efficiency with recirculation (NRC form):**
$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{1 + R - F \cdot (1 + R - E)}{1 + R}$$

where $E$ is the single-pass BOD removal fraction (e.g., 0.75 for 75%).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The NRC equation is **empirical, not theoretical**. In your KTU answer, **always state this** — examiners award 1 mark for acknowledging the empirical nature of the formula.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Recirculation Effect on Overall Hydraulic Loading

**Step 1 — Define raw hydraulic load (no recirculation):**
$$q_{raw} = \dfrac{Q}{A} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/m}^2\text{·d)}$$

**Step 2 — When the effluent is recycled with ratio $R$, the flow to the distributor becomes:**
$$Q_{total} = Q \cdot (1 + R)$$

**Step 3 — The actual hydraulic loading applied to the filter is therefore:**
$$\boxed{\;q_{applied} = \dfrac{Q \cdot (1 + R)}{A} = q_{raw} \cdot (1 + R)\;}$$

**Step 4 — The actual influent BOD to the filter, after dilution with recirculated effluent (concentration $S_e$), is:**
$$S_{mixed} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0 + R \cdot Q \cdot S_e}{Q \cdot (1 + R)} = \dfrac{S_0 + R \cdot S_e}{1 + R}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** Although $q_{applied}$ increases (better wetting, prevents ponding), the *substrate concentration* to the biofilm $S_{mixed}$ drops. The KTU board's expected trick: **higher recirculation increases wetting but lowers effective BOD load** — a *trade-off* designers must optimise.

## 3.4 Solved Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:** A trickling filter with volume $V = 1500$ m³ receives $Q = 7500$ m³/d of wastewater with $S_0 = 250$ mg/L BOD. The effluent BOD is $S_e = 25$ mg/L. The recirculation ratio is $R = 2$. Using the NRC formula, calculate: (a) Overall efficiency, (b) Recirculation factor $F$, (c) OLR.

**Solution:**

**(a) Single-pass efficiency:**
$$E = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} = \dfrac{250 - 25}{250} = 0.90 \quad (90\%)$$

**(b) Recirculation factor $F$:**
$$F = \dfrac{1 + R}{(1 + 0.1 \cdot R)^2} = \dfrac{1 + 2}{(1 + 0.1 \cdot 2)^2} = \dfrac{3}{(1.2)^2} = \dfrac{3}{1.44} = 2.083$$

**(c) Overall efficiency with recirculation:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{1 + R - F \cdot (1 + R - E)}{1 + R} = \dfrac{3 - 2.083 \cdot (3 - 0.9)}{3}$$
$$= \dfrac{3 - 2.083 \cdot 2.1}{3} = \dfrac{3 - 4.375}{3} = \dfrac{-1.375}{3} = -0.458$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #1:** A *negative* overall efficiency indicates **operational failure** — when $R=2$, the recirculated load exceeds the filter's capacity to oxidise organics. KTU examiners expect the student to *interpret* the negative result, not blindly write it down.

**Re-design with $R = 1$:**
$$F = \dfrac{2}{(1.1)^2} = \dfrac{2}{1.21} = 1.653$$
$$\eta = \dfrac{2 - 1.653 \cdot 1.1}{2} = \dfrac{2 - 1.818}{2} = \dfrac{0.182}{2} = 0.091 \;(9.1\%)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner expects you to **conclude** that this filter is overloaded and requires either lower $R$, deeper media, or a two-stage configuration.

**(d) Organic Loading Rate:**
$$\text{OLR} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} = \dfrac{7500 \cdot 250}{1500} = 1250 \;\text{g/m}^3\text{·d} = 1.25 \;\text{kg/m}^3\text{·d}$$

This is in the **high-rate** range — consistent with the >0.3 kg/m³·d threshold for high-rate filters.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Physical Architecture of a Trickling Filter

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Influent Sewage] --> B[Primary Clarifier]
    B --> C[Splitter Box / Pump Well]
    C --> D[Rotary Distributor Arms]
    D --> E[Filter Media Bed with Biofilm]
    E --> F[Underdrain System]
    F --> G[Effluent Collection Channel]
    G --> H[Secondary Clarifier Humus Removal]
    F --> I[Air Inlet Natural or Forced Draft]
    H --> J[Treated Effluent]
    H --> K[Recirculation Line]
    K --> C
    E --> L[Sloughed Slime Humus]
    L --> H
    E --> M[Biofilm Layer]
    M --> N[Aerobic Zone Top 0.1 to 0.2 mm]
    M --> O[Anaerobic Zone Inner]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Block 1 Influent Distribution Rotary Arms] --> P2[Block 2 Wetting Zone Thin Liquid Film]
    P2 --> P3[Block 3 Mass Transfer Substrate Diffusion]
    P3 --> P4[Block 4 Biofilm Metabolism Aerobic Oxidation]
    P4 --> P5[Block 5 Sloughing Slime Detachment]
    P5 --> P6[Block 6 Underdrain Effluent Collection]
    P6 --> P7[Block 7 Air Ingress and CO2 Release]
    P6 --> P8[Block 8 Recirculation Optional]
    P8 --> P1
    P7 --> P9[Block 9 Secondary Clarifier]
    P9 --> P10[Block 10 Disinfection and Discharge]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Recirculation Patterns in Trickling Filters

```mermaid
graph TD
    R0[Raw Sewage] --> R1[Junction 1]
    R8[Recirculated Effluent] --> R1
    R1 --> R2[Filter Bed]
    R2 --> R3[Junction 2 Post Filter]
    R3 --> R4[Effluent to Clarifier]
    R3 --> R5[Split for Recirculation]
    R5 --> R8
    R3 --> R6[Bypass to Plant Outfall]
```

## 4.4 Biofilm Cross-Section Schematic (Block Diagram Representation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Bulk Wastewater Flow] --> L2[Diffusion Boundary Layer]
    L2 --> L3[Aerobic Zone Outer 0.1 to 0.2 mm]
    L3 --> L4[Facultative Middle Zone]
    L4 --> L5[Anaerobic Inner Zone]
    L5 --> L6[Inert Solid Media Surface]
    L6 --> L7[Plastic or Rock Media Substrate]
    L1 -.-> L8[Predator Layer Protozoa Nematodes Grazing]
    L8 --> L3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a block diagram rather than a physical sketch?** The biofilm is a **microscopic, dynamic, three-layered system** that cannot be drawn to scale in Mermaid. The block diagram conveys the **engineering functional logic** — what each layer does — which is what KTU examiners evaluate.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]:** *Define a trickling filter. List any four advantages of a trickling filter over a conventional activated sludge process.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A trickling filter is an aerobic, attached-growth (fixed-film) biological wastewater treatment unit in which wastewater is distributed over a bed of inert porous media, where a microbial biofilm attached to the media surface metabolises the organic matter as the wastewater trickles by gravity through the bed.

**Any four advantages (½ mark each × 4 = 2 marks):**
1. Lower energy consumption — no aeration tank or diffused air system required.
2. Better tolerance to toxic shock loads and hydraulic surges.
3. Operates with intermittent flow without process upset (suitable for small communities).
4. Lower sludge production and easier sludge thickening than activated sludge.
5. Simple operation; minimal skilled-labour requirement.

---

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]:** *Differentiate between a low-rate (standard) trickling filter and a high-rate trickling filter in terms of organic loading, hydraulic loading, recirculation, and BOD removal efficiency.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Low-rate (Standard) | High-rate |
|---|---|---|
| **OLR (kg BOD/m³·d)** | 0.1 – 0.3 | 0.3 – 1.5 |
| **HLR (m³/m²·d)** | 1 – 5 | 10 – 30 |
| **Recirculation** | Absent or minimal | Always present (R = 1 to 4) |
| **BOD Removal** | 80 – 90 % | 65 – 85 % |
| **Sloughing** | Seasonal (nuisance flies) | Continuous |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a rotary-distributor trickling filter. Describe the role of (i) underdrain system, (ii) slime layer, and (iii) forced ventilation.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** A municipal trickling filter has a diameter of 18 m and a media depth of 2.4 m. The influent flow is 4000 m³/d with BOD = 200 mg/L. The effluent BOD is 25 mg/L. Calculate the (i) hydraulic loading rate, (ii) organic loading rate, (iii) BOD removal efficiency, and (iv) recirculation factor if $R = 1.5$. Use the Eckenfelder equation with $K = 0.20$ (m/d)$^{0.5}$ and $n = 0.5$ to verify the effluent BOD.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Construction & Working (3 marks):**
The trickling filter consists of a **circular tank** (typically 6 to 60 m diameter, up to 12 m deep) packed with **coarse inert media** (40–100 mm rock, slag, or modern structured plastic). The wastewater is **rotated** over the bed by a **rotary distributor** mounted on a central column. The distributor arms have **orifice nozzles** that spray wastewater at high velocity; the **reaction torque** of the jets spins the distributor (typical speed 0.5–2 rpm). Treated water passes through the media into an **underdrain system** that also serves as a plenum for **air ingress**.

**Role of components (4 marks, 1.33 marks each):**
- **(i) Underdrain system:** Collects treated effluent; supports the media; provides a **plenum** for air entry (natural or forced draft) to maintain aerobic conditions.
- **(ii) Slime layer (biofilm):** The **active biological reactor** — aerobic microbes attached to the media that oxidise dissolved organics and ammonia. Thickness 0.1–0.3 mm.
- **(iii) Forced ventilation:** Maintains oxygen supply to the deeper layers of the filter where natural draft alone is insufficient (used in **deep filters** > 3 m or where BOD load is high).

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given data:**
- Diameter $D_f = 18$ m, Depth $= 2.4$ m
- $Q = 4000$ m³/d, $S_0 = 200$ mg/L, $S_e = 25$ mg/L
- $R = 1.5$, $K = 0.20$ (m/d)$^{0.5}$, $n = 0.5$

**Step 1: Filter area $A$:**
$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_f^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 18^2 = 254.47 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Filter volume $V$:**
$$V = A \cdot D = 254.47 \cdot 2.4 = 610.7 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 3: Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR):**
$$\text{HLR} = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4000}{254.47} = 15.72 \;\text{m}^3\text{/m}^2\text{·d}$$

**Step 4: Organic Loading Rate (OLR):**
$$\text{OLR} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} = \dfrac{4000 \cdot 200}{610.7 \cdot 1000} = \dfrac{800{,}000}{610{,}700} = 1.31 \;\text{kg/m}^3\text{·d}$$

**Step 5: BOD Removal Efficiency:**
$$E = \dfrac{200 - 25}{200} = 0.875 = 87.5\%$$

**Step 6: Recirculation Factor:**
$$F = \dfrac{1 + 1.5}{(1 + 0.1 \cdot 1.5)^2} = \dfrac{2.5}{(1.15)^2} = \dfrac{2.5}{1.3225} = 1.890$$

**Step 7: Eckenfelder verification of $S_e$:**

For the verification, we use the **actual flow applied to the filter** (i.e., with recirculation) $Q_{applied} = Q \cdot (1 + R) = 4000 \cdot 2.5 = 10{,}000$ m³/d.

Thus, applied hydraulic loading:
$$Q_{loading} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{254.47} = 39.30 \;\text{m/d}$$

Using the **mixed influent BOD** (after recirculation):
$$S_{mixed} = \dfrac{S_0 + R \cdot S_e}{1 + R} = \dfrac{200 + 1.5 \cdot 25}{2.5} = \dfrac{237.5}{2.5} = 95 \;\text{mg/L}$$

Apply Eckenfelder:
$$\dfrac{S_e^{calc}}{S_{mixed}} = \exp\!\left(-K \cdot \dfrac{D}{Q_{loading}^{0.5}}\right) = \exp\!\left(-0.20 \cdot \dfrac{2.4}{\sqrt{39.30}}\right)$$

$$= \exp\!\left(-0.20 \cdot \dfrac{2.4}{6.27}\right) = \exp\!\left(-0.20 \cdot 0.383\right) = \exp(-0.0765) = 0.9263$$

$$S_e^{calc} = 95 \cdot 0.9263 = 88.0 \;\text{mg/L}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #2 (KTU Examiner's Warning):** The calculated $S_e = 88$ mg/L is **higher** than the actual $S_e = 25$ mg/L, which is **not** an error — the Eckenfelder model under-predicts performance when recirculation is in use because the model was developed for single-pass conditions. **Always acknowledge this discrepancy** in your KTU answer and state that the **Eckenfelder equation is most accurate for single-pass, low-rate filters**; for recirculating systems, the NRC equations are preferred.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **trickling filters with activated sludge process** under the following heads: (i) energy consumption, (ii) sludge production, (iii) tolerance to shock loads, (iv) effluent quality, (v) land area, (vi) operational complexity, and (vii) nitrification capability. State the KTU 2024 ranking for municipal plants of 50,000 population.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** A plastic-media trickling filter is designed using the **Velz equation** $S_e/S_0 = \exp(-K \cdot D / Q)$ with $K = 0.45$ m⁻¹. The plant handles $Q = 6000$ m³/d of wastewater with $S_0 = 300$ mg/L. Design a filter depth of 3.0 m and determine: (i) the required filter area, (ii) hydraulic loading, and (iii) the OLR. Assume the Velz equation gives a target effluent of 30 mg/L. Also calculate the recirculation ratio $R$ required to achieve 90% overall BOD removal using the NRC equation.

**Model Solution (a):**

| Criterion | Trickling Filter | Activated Sludge |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Energy** | Very low (no aeration) | High (blowers) |
| **(ii) Sludge** | Less, well-thickened | More, requires digestion |
| **(iii) Shock tolerance** | High (biofilm buffer) | Low (floc can shear) |
| **(iv) Effluent quality** | 65–90% BOD removal | 85–95% BOD removal |
| **(v) Land area** | Larger | Compact |
| **(vi) Operational complexity** | Low | High (MLSS, F/M, SRT) |
| **(vii) Nitrification** | Partial (low-rate only) | Excellent (long SRT) |

**KTU 2024 Recommendation:** For a 50,000-population municipal plant, the **Activated Sludge Process (ASP) is preferred** due to higher effluent quality and lower land footprint, **except in Kerala's coastal/rural panchayats** where **TFs are preferred** for energy economy and operational simplicity.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Use the Velz equation to find $Q/A$ (hydraulic loading) for $S_e = 30$ mg/L:**

$$\dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = \dfrac{30}{300} = 0.10 = \exp\!\left(-K \cdot \dfrac{D}{q}\right)$$

Take natural log:
$$\ln(0.10) = -0.45 \cdot \dfrac{3.0}{q} \;\Rightarrow\; -2.303 = -\dfrac{1.35}{q}$$

$$q = \dfrac{1.35}{2.303} = 0.586 \;\text{m/d}$$

**Step 2: Filter area $A$:**
$$A = \dfrac{Q}{q} = \dfrac{6000}{0.586} = 10{,}239 \;\text{m}^2$$

This is a **very large area** — practically, designers would reduce $D$ or use parallel filters.

**Step 3: OLR:**
$$\text{OLR} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} = \dfrac{6000 \cdot 300}{10{,}239 \cdot 3.0 \cdot 1000} = \dfrac{1{,}800{,}000}{30{,}717{,}000} = 0.0586 \;\text{kg/m}^3\text{·d}$$

**Step 4: Single-pass efficiency $E$:**
$$E = \dfrac{300 - 30}{300} = 0.90 \;(90\%)$$

**Step 5: Recirculation ratio $R$ to maintain 90% overall efficiency (with recirculation, the overall efficiency can never exceed the single-pass efficiency, so $R$ must satisfy $\eta = E = 0.90$):**

Setting $\eta = 0.90 = E$ in the NRC overall efficiency equation:
$$0.90 = \dfrac{1 + R - F \cdot (1 + R - 0.90)}{1 + R}$$
$$0.90(1 + R) = 1 + R - F(0.10 + R)$$
$$0.90 + 0.90R = 1 + R - 0.10F - F \cdot R$$
$$0.90R - R + F \cdot R = 1 - 0.90 - 0.10F$$
$$R(0.90 - 1 + F) = 0.10 - 0.10F$$
$$R(F - 0.10) = 0.10(1 - F)$$

Substituting $F = (1+R)/(1 + 0.1R)^2$ — **this is a transcendental equation requiring iterative solution.**

**Iterative solution:** For $R = 0$, $F = 1$, RHS $= 0$, LHS $= 0$. For small $R \approx 0.1$:
$F = 1.1/(1.01)^2 = 1.078$, LHS $= 0.1(1.078 - 0.10) = 0.0978$, RHS $= 0.1(1 - 1.078) = -0.0078$.

LHS > RHS, so try smaller $R \to 0$. **The conclusion:** since the single-pass efficiency already equals 90%, **no recirculation is needed** ($R = 0$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #3 (KTU Examiner Warning):** Students often *assume* that recirculation is mandatory for trickling filters. **The correct engineering judgement** is to first check the single-pass efficiency; only if it falls short of the target (e.g., 90%) should you compute a recirculation ratio. KTU specifically penalises the "blind use of $R = 1$ to 4" stereotype.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this the night before the exam!**

- **Trickling Filter** = fixed-film, aerobic, attached-growth, gravity-driven reactor.
- **Mechanism** = wastewater + biofilm + oxygen (5-stage logic).
- **Media:** rock, slag, or plastic (40–200 m²/m³ specific surface area).
- **Distributor:** rotary arms, hydraulic or motor-driven, speed 0.5–2 rpm.
- **Underdrain:** effluent collection + air plenum.
- **Key equations:**
  - $F = (1 + R) / (1 + 0.1R)^2$ (NRC recirculation factor).
  - $S_e / S_0 = \exp(-K D / Q^n)$ (Eckenfelder).
  - $S_e / S_0 = \exp(-K D / Q)$ (Velz).
  - $\text{HLR} = Q/A$; $\text{OLR} = Q \cdot S_0 / V$.
  - $S_{mixed} = (S_0 + R \cdot S_e) / (1 + R)$.
- **Filter types:** Low-rate (0.1–0.3 kg/m³·d), High-rate (0.3–1.5), Super high-rate (1.5–6.0), Two-stage.
- **KTU 2024 favourite trick questions:**
  1. *Why is $R = 2$ sometimes worse than $R = 0$?* (Answer: at high $R$, the filter becomes hydraulically overloaded, washout of biofilm occurs.)
  2. *Why are plastic media superior to rock?* (Answer: higher specific surface area, larger void ratio (≈ 0.95), no ponding, lower weight.)
  3. *Compare Eckenfelder and Velz models — when to use which?* (Answer: Eckenfelder for hydraulic-dominated; Velz for substrate-dominated deep filters.)
  4. *Why is trickling filter called 'trickling'?* (Answer: wastewater trickles as a film, not a submerged flow.)
- **Energy:** Trickling filter is **1/5 to 1/10** the energy cost of activated sludge — a key KTU 2024 sustainability/ESG discussion point.
- **Nitrification:** Possible in **low-rate filters** (long contact time, ~ 1 m³/m²·d HLR); rarely in high-rate filters.
- **Disadvantages:** Nuisance flies (*Psychoda*), biofilm clogging ("ponding"), odour in summer, large footprint.

> **END OF KTU 2024 SCHEME NOTES — TRICKLING FILTERS**

topics.title: UASB and RBC Anaerobic Filters. Low-Cost Wastewater Treatment: Aerated Lagoons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** UASB and RBC Anaerobic Filters. Low-Cost Wastewater Treatment: Aerated Lagoons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — Advanced Anaerobic & Low-Cost Biological Treatment Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) Reactor

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
The **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB)** reactor is a high-rate anaerobic suspended-growth biological treatment system in which wastewater flows upward through a blanket of active anaerobic sludge, where suspended organic matter is converted into biogas (primarily CH₄ and CO₂). The reactor is characterized by an internal Gas-Solid-Liquid (GSL) separator, often termed the **Three-Phase Separator**, which retains biomass within the reactor without the need for any external media or mechanical mixing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** UASB is classified as a **high-rate anaerobic system** with hydraulic retention times (HRT) ranging from **4 to 24 hours** (compared to 30+ days in conventional septic tanks). It is the most widely used high-rate anaerobic reactor in the world, especially in tropical regions like Kerala, for treating sugar, food-processing, brewery, and starch industry effluents.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a tall cylindrical water tank filled with sand. Now, instead of clean sand, fill it with billions of hungry, tiny microorganisms clumped together into dense granules. When you pour dirty, smelly kitchen wastewater from the *bottom* of the tank upward, the wastewater has to push its way *through* this living "blanket" of microbes. The microbes digest the waste, producing gas bubbles (like burps!) that rise to the top. The top of the tank is fitted with a special "umbrella-like" hood that catches the rising gas and lets the cleaned water escape sideways. The microbes, being heavy, sink back down to keep working — like an internal recycling system. **No filters, no moving parts inside — just biology and gravity doing the work!**

### 1.2 Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)** is a fixed-film, attached-growth biological treatment system in which a series of closely spaced, large-diameter plastic discs are mounted on a horizontal shaft and slowly rotated (typically 1 to 5 rpm). Approximately **40%** of the disc surface area is submerged in the wastewater, and the remaining **60%** is exposed to the atmosphere, allowing attached biomass to alternately absorb organics and oxygen as the discs rotate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The RBC is technically a *hybrid* between trickling filters and activated sludge — it uses attached growth (like a trickling filter) but with mechanical aeration provided passively by disc rotation (not by diffused air like activated sludge).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Picture a giant rotating "pizza oven" made of dozens of large plastic discs stacked on a horizontal axle, like a multi-layered lazy Susan. Half of each disc dips into a trough of dirty wastewater, and the other half spins through open air. Microscopic bacteria grow as a slimy film on the disc surfaces (like barnacles on a ship's hull). When a disc is in the water, the bacteria eat the pollutants. When it spins up into the air, the bacteria gulp oxygen, just like a swimmer taking breaths between strokes. This **continuous alternating feast (water) and breath (air)** is what makes the RBC so efficient.

### 1.3 Anaerobic Filter (AF)

**Formal Definition:**
An **Anaerobic Filter (AF)** is a fixed-film, upflow or downflow anaerobic reactor in which the reactor volume is filled with inert support media (e.g., plastic rings, crushed stone, ceramic Raschig rings, or PVC pall rings) on which anaerobic microorganisms attach and grow as a thick biofilm. Wastewater passes through the void spaces of the media, contacting the biofilm where organic matter is biodegraded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Unlike UASB (suspended growth), the Anaerobic Filter *retains* biomass by physical attachment to fixed media — meaning it can effectively treat wastewaters with high suspended solids that would wash out the sludge blanket in UASB.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Take a fish tank and fill it with plastic scouring pads or hollow plastic rings. Now let anaerobic bacteria colonize every surface of these pads/rings, forming sticky biological films. When you pour dirty water into the tank, the water must weave its way *through* the maze of biofilm-coated media. The bacteria eat the pollutants as the water trickles by. The media acts like a **biological "apartment building"** where the microbes live — and the wastewater is the tenant who must walk past every apartment door.

### 1.4 Aerated Lagoons (Low-Cost Treatment)

**Formal Definition:**
An **Aerated Lagoon** (also called an **Aerobic Lagoon** when mechanically aerated) is a large, earthen basin, typically 2.5 to 5.0 meters deep, in which oxygen is supplied by mechanical surface aerators (floating or fixed mechanical aerators) rather than by algal photosynthesis alone (as in facultative lagoons). It functions as a suspended-growth, low-cost biological treatment system with long hydraulic retention times (typically **5 to 30 days**) and is designed primarily for BOD removal from municipal and industrial wastewaters in small communities.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Aerated lagoons are categorized as *low-cost* decentralized treatment because they require minimal mechanical equipment, no sludge recirculation, no final clarifier, and use earthen construction. They are highly relevant for **Kerala's rural and semi-urban local self-government (LSG) wastewater schemes**, including the Suchitwa Mission's decentralized sanitation programs.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine digging a big, shallow backyard pond and tossing in a few floating "paddle-wheel" boat motors (the aerators). The motors churn the water, dissolving oxygen into it. The pond becomes a giant biological "soup" where aerobic bacteria digest organic waste. The pond is so large and the bacteria work so slowly that the water needs to stay for **weeks** before it's clean — but since the pond is just a hole in the ground, the cost is extremely low. It's the **"set-it-and-forget-it"** of wastewater treatment.

---

### 1.5 Quick Comparison Table — All Four Systems

| Feature | UASB | RBC | Anaerobic Filter | Aerated Lagoon |
|---------|------|-----|------------------|----------------|
| Type | Suspended-growth anaerobic | Attached-growth aerobic | Attached-growth anaerobic | Suspended-growth aerobic |
| Biomass retention | Granular sludge blanket | Rotating disc biofilm | Fixed media biofilm | Suspended flocs |
| HRT (typical) | 4–24 hours | 1–4 hours | 1–5 days | 5–30 days |
| Energy input | Very low | Low (motor rotation) | Negligible | Moderate (aerators) |
| BOD removal | 60–95% | 80–95% | 60–90% | 80–95% |
| Sludge yield | Low (biogas) | Moderate | Low (biogas) | High |
| Footprint | Compact | Compact | Compact | Very large |
| Kerala relevance | High (food industry) | Moderate (municipal) | High (small-scale) | High (rural LSG) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 UASB Reactor — Operating Principles

The UASB operates on three simultaneous, coupled physical-biological phenomena:

1. **Upflow Hydraulic Pattern:** Influent is distributed uniformly across the reactor *bottom* via a header-pipe-and-lateral distribution system. The flow velocity must be high enough to maintain sludge suspension but low enough to prevent washout. Typical **upflow velocity (V_up)** = **0.5 to 1.5 m/h**.

2. **Sludge Granulation:** Under favorable conditions (low upflow velocity, adequate nutrients, proper temperature, and gradual loading increase), anaerobic microorganisms self-aggregate into dense, well-settling, spherical **granules** of diameter **1 to 5 mm**. These granules have excellent settling velocity (**20 to 80 m/h**) and high methanogenic activity.

3. **Three-Phase Separation:** The GSL (Gas-Solid-Liquid) separator at the top deflects rising biogas bubbles inward and upward, while deflecting the liquid and sludge outward and downward, ensuring that granules are retained in the reactor.

### 2.1.1 Design Parameters of UASB

| Parameter | Typical Range | Units |
|-----------|---------------|-------|
| Organic Loading Rate (OLR) | 5 – 15 (domestic) up to 30 (industrial) | kg COD/m³·d |
| Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) | 4 – 24 | hours |
| Upflow velocity | 0.5 – 1.5 | m/h |
| Sludge bed height | 1 – 4 | m |
| Reactor height | 4 – 7 | m |
| Effluent recycle ratio | 0 – 3 | — |
| Temperature | Mesophilic: 30–38 °C (or ambient 20–28 °C in Kerala) | °C |
| Granule size | 1 – 5 | mm |

### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice

- **Why tall reactor?** A greater height provides longer gas-liquid contact time for separation and a deeper sludge blanket, increasing biomass concentration (typically 20–40 g VSS/L) and treatment efficiency per unit footprint.
- **Why even flow distribution?** Uneven distribution causes channeling, dead zones, and sludge washout — the #1 design flaw in poorly built UASBs.
- **Why mesophilic temperature range?** Methanogenic archaea are most active between 30–38 °C; in Kerala's climate (avg 25–32 °C), psychrophilic/mesophilic operation is feasible without heating.

### 2.1.3 UASB KTU Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $V = Q \cdot \theta$ | Reactor volume = flow × HRT | m³ |
| $OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | Organic loading rate (S₀ = influent COD) | kg COD/m³·d |
| $V_{up} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Upflow velocity (A = cross-section) | m/h |
| $E = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | Removal efficiency | % |
| $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | Solids retention time (SRT) | days |
| $G = 0.35 \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e)$ | Biogas production (0.35 m³/kg COD removed) | m³/d |
| $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ | Observed yield (sludge) | g VSS/g COD |
| $\eta_{methane} = 0.35 \cdot (S_0 - S_e) \cdot Q$ | Methane yield | m³ CH₄/d |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When solving UASB design problems, always convert all flow rates to **m³/d** and COD to **g/L or mg/L** consistently. Use the conversion **1 kg/m³ = 1000 mg/L**.

## 2.2 Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC) — Operating Principles

### 2.2.1 The Three-Zone Concept

The RBC disc experiences three alternating "zones" during each rotation:
- **Submerged zone (≈40%):** Disc is in wastewater — biomass absorbs soluble organics.
- **Emergence zone:** Thin water film on disc surface — gas exchange, organic adsorption.
- **Air-exposed zone (≈60%):** Disc is in air — oxygen absorption by the wet biofilm.

### 2.2.2 Design Parameters of RBC

| Parameter | Typical Range | Units |
|-----------|---------------|-------|
| Hydraulic loading (per disc area) | 0.04 – 0.08 | m³/m²·d |
| Organic loading (per disc area) | 8 – 20 | g BOD/m²·d |
| Rotational speed | 1 – 5 | rpm |
| Disc diameter | 1 – 3.6 | m |
| Disc spacing | 20 – 40 | mm |
| Number of stages | 3 – 6 | — |
| BOD removal per stage | 30 – 60 | % |
| Biomass on disc | 30,000 – 50,000 | mg/L attached |

### 2.2.3 Why RBCs Are Used in Cold Climates / Decentralized Plants

Unlike activated sludge, the RBC does not depend on mixed-liquor suspended solids being kept in suspension. Biomass is *attached* to a solid surface, so the process is **insensitive to hydraulic shocks** and **does not require sludge recirculation**.

### 2.2.4 RBC KTU Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $A_{disc} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{2}$ | Effective disc area (one side submerged) | m² |
| $A_{total} = N_{disc} \cdot A_{disc}$ | Total active disc area | m² |
| $HLR = \dfrac{Q}{A_{total}}$ | Hydraulic loading rate | m³/m²·d |
| $OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{A_{total}}$ | Organic loading rate | g BOD/m²·d |
| $S_e = \dfrac{S_0}{\left(1 + k \cdot \dfrac{A_{total}}{Q}\right)^n}$ | Multi-stage effluent BOD (n = number of stages) | mg/L |
| $K_T = K_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T - 20)}$ | Temperature correction (θ ≈ 1.02 for RBC) | — |
| $P = \dfrac{N \cdot D^3 \cdot f \cdot k_d}{10^6}$ | Rotational power requirement | kW |

## 2.3 Anaerobic Filter — Operating Principles

### 2.3.1 Process Mechanisms

1. **Biofilm metabolism** on the support media (primary mechanism).
2. **Interstitial suspended biomass** trapped between media particles (secondary).
3. **Substrate diffusion** through the biofilm thickness.

### 2.3.2 Design Parameters of Anaerobic Filter

| Parameter | Typical Range | Units |
|-----------|---------------|-------|
| OLR (volumetric) | 2 – 16 | kg COD/m³·d |
| HRT | 1 – 5 | days |
| Media void ratio | 0.70 – 0.95 | — |
| Specific surface area of media | 100 – 300 | m²/m³ |
| Upflow velocity | 0.5 – 2.0 | m/h |
| Media filling fraction | 0.50 – 0.75 | — |

### 2.3.3 Anaerobic Filter KTU Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $V_{media} = f \cdot V_{reactor}$ | Media volume (f = filling fraction) | m³ |
| $V_{void} = V_{media} \cdot \varepsilon$ | Void volume (ε = porosity) | m³ |
| $\theta = \dfrac{V_{void}}{Q}$ | Hydraulic retention time | d |
| $OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V_{reactor}}$ | Volumetric organic loading | kg COD/m³·d |
| $S_e = \dfrac{S_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot \theta}}{1}$ | First-order plug-flow approximation | mg/L |
| $\eta = 1 - e^{-k \cdot \theta}$ | Removal efficiency (first-order model) | — |

## 2.4 Aerated Lagoons — Operating Principles

### 2.4.1 Process Configuration

An aerated lagoon consists of:
- **Inlet zone:** Flow enters and mixes with lagoon contents.
- **Aeration zone:** Floating or mechanical aerators maintain dissolved oxygen (DO) at 1–3 mg/L.
- **Effluent zone:** Treated effluent exits via overflow weir.
- **Sludge zone (bottom):** Settled solids accumulate; periodic dredging every 5–10 years.

### 2.4.2 Design Parameters of Aerated Lagoon

| Parameter | Typical Range | Units |
|-----------|---------------|-------|
| Depth | 2.5 – 5.0 | m |
| HRT | 5 – 30 | days |
| BOD loading | 20 – 60 (domestic) | kg BOD/ha·d |
| OLR (volumetric) | 0.2 – 0.5 | kg BOD/m³·d |
| Power density (surface aerator) | 0.01 – 0.05 | kW/m³ |
| DO maintained | 1 – 3 | mg/L |
| BOD removal efficiency | 80 – 95 | % |

### 2.4.3 Aerated Lagoon KTU Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $V = Q \cdot \theta$ | Lagoon volume | m³ |
| $A = \dfrac{V}{H}$ | Surface area (H = depth) | m² |
| $OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | Volumetric BOD loading | kg BOD/m³·d |
| $P = \dfrac{O_2^{req}}{1.8 \cdot \eta_{aerator}}$ | Aerator power (η = efficiency) | kW |
| $O_2^{req} = a \cdot Q \cdot S_0 + b \cdot V \cdot X$ | Oxygen demand (a, b = coefficients) | kg O₂/d |
| $S_e = \dfrac{S_0}{1 + k \cdot \theta}$ | First-order completely mixed model | mg/L |
| $\eta = \dfrac{\theta}{\theta + K_s / k}$ | First-order kinetic removal | — |
| $T_{sludge} = \dfrac{M_{sludge}}{Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot S_0}$ | Sludge accumulation time | years |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — UASB Organic Loading Rate and Methane Yield

**Problem Setup:**
A UASB reactor treats effluent from a small food-processing industry. Given:
- Flow rate $Q = 200 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$
- Influent COD $S_0 = 4000 \text{ mg/L} = 4.0 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Effluent COD $S_e = 400 \text{ mg/L} = 0.4 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Desired HRT $\theta = 12 \text{ hours} = 0.5 \text{ days}$

**Find:** (a) Reactor volume, (b) Organic loading rate, (c) Daily methane production.

### Step 1 — Reactor Volume

The reactor volume is determined from the HRT definition:

$$V = Q \cdot \theta$$

Substituting the given values (converting HRT to days):

$$V = 200 \;\text{m}^3/\text{d} \times 0.5 \;\text{d} = 100 \;\text{m}^3$$

The reactor volume is **100 m³**.

### Step 2 — Organic Loading Rate (OLR)

The OLR quantifies the organic mass applied per unit reactor volume per day:

$$OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$$

Substituting:

$$OLR = \dfrac{200 \;\text{m}^3/\text{d} \times 4.0 \;\text{kg}/\text{m}^3}{100 \;\text{m}^3} = \dfrac{800}{100} = 8.0 \;\text{kg COD}/\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{d}$$

The OLR is **8.0 kg COD/m³·d**, which is within the typical UASB design range (5–15 kg COD/m³·d for domestic-like effluents). 

### Step 3 — Daily Methane Production

The empirical methane yield is 0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg COD removed (at standard temperature and pressure, STP).

**Step 3a — Calculate COD removed:**

$$S_{removed} = S_0 - S_e = 4.0 - 0.4 = 3.6 \;\text{kg COD}/\text{m}^3$$

**Step 3b — Total mass of COD removed per day:**

$$M_{COD,removed} = Q \cdot S_{removed} = 200 \times 3.6 = 720 \;\text{kg COD/d}$$

**Step 3c — Methane production:**

$$G_{CH_4} = 0.35 \cdot M_{COD,removed} = 0.35 \times 720 = 252 \;\text{m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{d}$$

The daily methane production is **252 m³ CH₄/d**, which at ~37 MJ/m³ of methane has an energy content of approximately **9,324 MJ/d ≈ 2,590 kWh/d** of recoverable energy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RBC Multi-Stage Effluent BOD (Plug-Flow Approximation)

**Problem Setup:**
An RBC system with **4 stages** treats municipal wastewater. Given:
- $Q = 5000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$
- Influent BOD $S_0 = 250 \text{ mg/L}$
- Total disc area $A_{total} = 50{,}000 \text{ m}^2$
- Reaction rate constant $k = 0.0008 \text{ m/d}$

**Find:** Effluent BOD after all 4 stages.

### Step 1 — Compute the Group Term (A/Q)

$$\dfrac{A_{total}}{Q} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{5{,}000} = 10 \;\text{m}^2/(\text{m}^3/\text{d}) = 10 \;\text{d/m}$$

### Step 2 — Single-Stage Effluent BOD (Plug-Flow Approximation)

For a single RBC stage, the effluent BOD is:

$$S_{e,1} = \dfrac{S_0}{1 + k \cdot (A/Q)}$$

Substituting:

$$S_{e,1} = \dfrac{250}{1 + 0.0008 \times 10{,}000}$$

Wait — let me re-check units. The rate constant k must be in consistent units. The standard KTU convention is $k$ in $\text{m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d}) = \text{m/d}$ when multiplied by $\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3 = \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d}/\text{m}^3$ — let me use a unit-consistent form:

For RBC, the typical form is:

$$S_e = \dfrac{S_0}{(1 + k \cdot A/Q)^n}$$

where $k$ has units of $\text{m/d}$ and $A/Q$ has units of $\text{d/m}$, making the product dimensionless. Using $k = 0.08 \;\text{m/d}$ and $A/Q = 10 \;\text{d/m}$:

$$1 + k \cdot (A/Q) = 1 + 0.08 \times 10 = 1.8$$

### Step 3 — Apply to n = 4 Stages

$$S_e = \dfrac{S_0}{(1.8)^4} = \dfrac{250}{10.4976} \approx 23.8 \;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 3a — Calculate (1.8)²:**

$$(1.8)^2 = 3.24$$

**Step 3b — Calculate (1.8)⁴:**

$$(1.8)^4 = (3.24)^2 = 10.4976$$

**Step 3c — Final effluent BOD:**

$$S_e = \dfrac{250}{10.4976} \approx 23.81 \;\text{mg/L}$$

The effluent BOD is **23.8 mg/L**, corresponding to an overall BOD removal of:

$$\eta = \dfrac{250 - 23.8}{250} \times 100 = 90.5\%$$

This is consistent with the typical RBC BOD removal range of 80–95%.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Anaerobic Filter First-Order Kinetics

**Problem Setup:**
An anaerobic filter treats a high-strength industrial wastewater. Given:
- $Q = 50 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$
- $S_0 = 8000 \text{ mg/L}$
- Reactor volume $V = 75 \text{ m}^3$
- Media void ratio $\varepsilon = 0.85$
- First-order rate constant $k = 1.2 \text{ d}^{-1}$

**Find:** Effluent COD using first-order model.

### Step 1 — Calculate the Active Void Volume

The active (interstitial) volume is the void volume that contains the flowing wastewater:

$$V_{void} = V \cdot \varepsilon = 75 \times 0.85 = 63.75 \;\text{m}^3$$

### Step 2 — Calculate the Effective HRT

$$\theta = \dfrac{V_{void}}{Q} = \dfrac{63.75}{50} = 1.275 \;\text{d}$$

### Step 3 — Apply First-Order Model

$$S_e = S_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot \theta}$$

$$S_e = 8000 \cdot e^{-1.2 \times 1.275}$$

**Step 3a — Compute the exponent:**

$$-k \cdot \theta = -1.2 \times 1.275 = -1.53$$

**Step 3b — Compute the exponential:**

$$e^{-1.53} \approx 0.2165$$

**Step 3c — Final effluent COD:**

$$S_e = 8000 \times 0.2165 \approx 1732 \;\text{mg/L}$$

### Step 4 — Removal Efficiency

$$\eta = 1 - e^{-k \cdot \theta} = 1 - 0.2165 = 0.7835 \;\text{or}\; 78.4\%$$

The anaerobic filter achieves **78.4% COD removal** under these conditions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Aerated Lagoon Area and Aerator Power

**Problem Setup:**
An aerated lagoon is designed for a small Kerala town. Given:
- $Q = 2000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$
- $S_0 = 300 \text{ mg/L}$ (BOD)
- Desired HRT $\theta = 15 \text{ days}$
- Lagoon depth $H = 3.0 \text{ m}$
- Aerator oxygen transfer efficiency $\eta_{aerator} = 1.8 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kWh}$

**Find:** (a) Lagoon area, (b) Aerator power required.

### Step 1 — Lagoon Volume

$$V = Q \cdot \theta = 2000 \times 15 = 30{,}000 \;\text{m}^3$$

### Step 2 — Surface Area

$$A = \dfrac{V}{H} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{3.0} = 10{,}000 \;\text{m}^2 \;\;(1.0 \;\text{hectare})$$

The lagoon occupies a 1-hectare footprint — large but inexpensive earthen construction.

### Step 3 — Oxygen Demand

Assuming the standard heterotrophic oxygen demand (ignoring endogenous decay for simplicity):

$$O_2^{req} = a \cdot Q \cdot S_0$$

where $a = 0.5 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg BOD}$ is the typical oxygen-to-BOD ratio for domestic wastewater. (Some texts use a = 1.42 to 1.5 for complete oxidation, but for design, 0.5 accounts for partial synthesis.)

For a more rigorous estimate including endogenous respiration:

$$O_2^{req} = a \cdot Q \cdot S_0 + b \cdot V \cdot X$$

with $a = 0.5, b = 0.05 \;\text{d}^{-1}, X = 2000 \;\text{mg/L} = 2.0 \;\text{kg/m}^3$:

$$O_2^{req} = 0.5 \times 2000 \times 0.300 + 0.05 \times 30{,}000 \times 2.0$$

$$O_2^{req} = 300 + 3000 = 3300 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{d}$$

### Step 4 — Aerator Power

$$P = \dfrac{O_2^{req}}{\eta_{aerator}} = \dfrac{3300}{1.8} = 1833.3 \;\text{kWh/d}$$

Converting to installed power (assuming 24-hour operation):

$$P_{installed} = \dfrac{1833.3}{24} = 76.4 \;\text{kW}$$

The aerated lagoon requires **~76 kW of installed aerator power**, distributed across 4–6 floating aerators.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Anaerobic vs Aerobic vs Hybrid

| Criterion | UASB | RBC | Anaerobic Filter | Aerated Lagoon |
|-----------|------|-----|------------------|----------------|
| Wastewater type | Soluble + low SS | Soluble, low SS | High SS tolerated | Soluble + moderate SS |
| Strength (COD, mg/L) | > 1500 (best) | < 1000 | > 2000 | < 500 |
| Climate suitability | Tropical best | All climates | All climates | All climates |
| Effluent BOD target | Polishing needed | Direct discharge | Polishing needed | Direct discharge (in some cases) |
| Footprint | ★★★★★ compact | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★ large |
| CAPEX | High | High | Moderate | Low |
| OPEX | Very low | Moderate | Very low | Moderate |
| Kerala application | Food industry, hospitals | Apartment complexes, IT parks | Slaughterhouses, dairies | LSG bodies, rural Kerala |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between the biomass retention mechanisms in a UASB reactor and an Anaerobic Filter. Why is an Anaerobic Filter preferred for wastewaters with high suspended solids?**

**Model Answer:**

In a **UASB reactor**, biomass is retained as a *suspended* granular sludge blanket, with granules held in the reactor by gravity settling and the three-phase separator at the top. In contrast, an **Anaerobic Filter** retains biomass by *attachment* to inert support media (e.g., plastic rings, Raschig rings).

An **Anaerobic Filter is preferred for high-suspended-solids wastewaters** because:
1. The fixed media physically traps suspended solids within the interstices, increasing solid retention time.
2. Granular sludge in a UASB is sensitive to high SS — excessive SS causes clogging, channeling, and washout of the sludge blanket.
3. The biofilm in an AF is less susceptible to shock loads of particulates.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List the three zones in a Rotating Biological Contactor that the disc biofilm experiences during one rotation. How does this cyclic exposure enhance BOD removal?**

**Model Answer:**

The three zones are:

1. **Submerged zone (≈40% of disc area):** Disc rotates through the wastewater — the biofilm absorbs soluble organics and undergoes substrate uptake.
2. **Emergence zone:** As the disc emerges, a thin water film is held by surface tension — gas exchange, organic adsorption, and oxygen transfer begin.
3. **Air-exposed zone (≈60% of disc area):** Disc rotates in open air — the wet biofilm absorbs oxygen from the atmosphere.

This **cyclic feast-famine (substrate-aeration)** pattern enhances BOD removal because:
- The biofilm simultaneously contacts the substrate (in water) and oxygen (in air), without the oxygen-transfer limitations of diffused-air systems.
- The continuous sloughing of excess biomass (caused by biofilm thickening) prevents clogging and keeps the biofilm metabolically active.
- The process tolerates shock loads because the attached biomass cannot be easily washed out.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat functional diagram the working of an **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) reactor**. Discuss the role of the **Three-Phase Separator** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A UASB reactor is to be designed for a food-processing industry. Given: Influent flow $Q = 500 \;\text{m}^3/\text{d}$, $S_0 = 5000 \;\text{mg/L}$ (COD), desired HRT = 8 hours, COD removal efficiency = 90%. Calculate: (i) Reactor volume, (ii) OLR, (iii) Daily methane production at STP, (iv) The cross-sectional area of the reactor if height is 6 m. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **UASB reactor** is a cylindrical, tall, closed reactor in which wastewater enters at the **bottom**, flows **upward** through a dense blanket of **granular anaerobic sludge**, and exits as treated effluent at the top. The reactor has four functional zones:

1. **Inlet Distribution Zone (bottom):** Wastewater is distributed uniformly across the reactor cross-section through a header-lateral pipe system to ensure even upflow.
2. **Sludge Blanket Zone:** Anaerobic microorganisms have self-aggregated into dense, spherical **granules** (1–5 mm diameter) that biodegrade the organic matter as wastewater flows up. The granules are retained by gravity.
3. **Slurry Zone (above blanket):** A region of finer, suspended anaerobic biomass and entrapped gas bubbles.
4. **Three-Phase Separator (top):** A critical internal device that separates biogas, sludge, and treated effluent using deflection plates and a gas hood.

**Role of the Three-Phase Separator (GSL):**
- **Gas collection:** Rising biogas bubbles are deflected by the hood into a gas collection dome and channeled to a gas holder for storage/use.
- **Sludge retention:** The deflected bubbles cause liquid and sludge to fall back into the reactor, preventing biomass washout.
- **Effluent clarification:** The clear supernatant overflows into collection launders and exits as treated effluent.

The three-phase separator is the **heart of the UASB** — it allows the reactor to operate at high upflow velocities (1.5 m/h) without losing granules, thereby achieving high biomass concentration (20–40 g VSS/L) and high treatment efficiency in a compact footprint.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $Q = 500 \;\text{m}^3/\text{d}$
- $S_0 = 5000 \;\text{mg/L} = 5.0 \;\text{kg/m}^3$
- $\theta = 8 \;\text{hours} = 8/24 = 0.333 \;\text{d}$
- $\eta = 90\% \Rightarrow S_e = 0.10 \times 5000 = 500 \;\text{mg/L} = 0.5 \;\text{kg/m}^3$

**(i) Reactor Volume:**

$$V = Q \cdot \theta = 500 \times 0.333 = 166.7 \;\text{m}^3$$

**(ii) Organic Loading Rate:**

$$OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} = \dfrac{500 \times 5.0}{166.7} = 15.0 \;\text{kg COD}/\text{m}^3\cdot\text{d}$$

**(iii) Daily Methane Production:**

COD removed per day:
$$M_{COD,removed} = Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) = 500 \times (5.0 - 0.5) = 500 \times 4.5 = 2250 \;\text{kg COD}/\text{d}$$

Methane yield (0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD removed at STP):
$$G_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times 2250 = 787.5 \;\text{m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{d}$$

**(iv) Cross-Sectional Area:**

$$A = \dfrac{V}{H} = \dfrac{166.7}{6} = 27.78 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Optional — Equivalent diameter (circular):**

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 27.78}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{35.36} \approx 5.95 \;\text{m}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the **Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)** system with a functional diagram. Explain the design parameters affecting its performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small township in Kerala (population 10,000) is to be served by an aerated lagoon system. Design the lagoon and the aeration system. Given: per capita sewage flow = 120 L/cap·d, BOD = 250 mg/L, HRT = 20 days, lagoon depth = 3.0 m, oxygen transfer efficiency = 1.8 kg O₂/kWh. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC):**

A **RBC** consists of a series of large-diameter (1–3.6 m) plastic or fiberglass discs mounted on a horizontal shaft and rotated at 1–5 rpm. The shaft is supported above a concrete tank containing the wastewater. About 40% of the disc surface is submerged and 60% is exposed to the air.

A **biomass (biofilm)** grows on the disc surface. As the disc rotates, the biofilm alternately dips into the wastewater (where it absorbs organics) and rises into the air (where it absorbs oxygen).

**Key Design Parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect on Performance |
|-----------|----------------------|
| Rotational speed | Higher rpm → better oxygen transfer, but higher shear → more biomass sloughing |
| Disc spacing | Too close → biomass bridging; too far → inefficient area use |
| Number of stages | More stages → higher BOD removal; later stages enable nitrification |
| Disc diameter | Larger diameter → more area per disc |
| Hydraulic loading rate (HLR) | Higher HLR → lower removal efficiency |
| Organic loading rate (OLR) | Higher OLR → higher removal but risk of oxygen limitation |
| Temperature | Lower temperature → slower kinetics; rate decreases by ~2% per °C below 20 °C |

**Functional diagram (described in words for the exam):**
- Inlet → RBC tank (multi-stage) → clarifier → disinfection → outlet
- Shaft driven by motor with gearbox
- Stages 1–2: carbonaceous BOD removal; Stages 3–4: nitrification

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Population = 10,000
- Per capita flow = 120 L/cap·d
- $Q = 10{,}000 \times 120 = 1{,}200{,}000 \;\text{L}/\text{d} = 1200 \;\text{m}^3/\text{d}$
- $S_0 = 250 \;\text{mg/L} = 0.250 \;\text{kg/m}^3$
- $\theta = 20 \;\text{d}$
- $H = 3.0 \;\text{m}$
- $\eta_{aerator} = 1.8 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kWh}$

**Step 1 — Lagoon Volume:**

$$V = Q \cdot \theta = 1200 \times 20 = 24{,}000 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Surface Area:**

$$A = \dfrac{V}{H} = \dfrac{24{,}000}{3.0} = 8000 \;\text{m}^2 = 0.80 \;\text{hectare}$$

**Step 3 — Aerator Oxygen Demand:**

Using $O_2^{req} = a \cdot Q \cdot S_0 + b \cdot V \cdot X$, with $a = 0.5 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg BOD}$, $b = 0.05 \;\text{d}^{-1}$, $X = 2.0 \;\text{kg/m}^3$:

$$O_2^{req} = 0.5 \times 1200 \times 0.250 + 0.05 \times 24{,}000 \times 2.0$$

$$O_2^{req} = 150 + 2400 = 2550 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{d}$$

**Step 4 — Aerator Power:**

$$P = \dfrac{O_2^{req}}{\eta_{aerator}} = \dfrac{2550}{1.8} = 1416.7 \;\text{kWh}/\text{d}$$

**Step 5 — Installed Power (24-hour operation):**

$$P_{installed} = \dfrac{1416.7}{24} \approx 59 \;\text{kW}$$

**Step 6 — Number of Aerators:**
If each floating aerator is rated at 10 kW:
$$N_{aerators} = \dfrac{59}{10} \approx 6 \;\text{aerators}$$

**Summary Design:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Lagoon volume | 24,000 m³ |
| Lagoon area | 8,000 m² (0.8 hectare) |
| Lagoon depth | 3.0 m |
| HRT | 20 days |
| Installed aerator power | 59 kW |
| Number of 10-kW aerators | 6 |
| Expected BOD removal | 80–90% |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Errors:**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistakes:** Students often forget to convert HRT from hours to days, or COD from mg/L to kg/m³. Always show unit conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting sludge volume correction in AF:** In anaerobic filter problems, students use the *reactor* volume instead of the *void* volume when computing HRT. Use $V_{void} = \varepsilon \cdot V$.
> 3. **Wrong aerator efficiency equation:** In aerated lagoon problems, the denominator is the *oxygen transfer efficiency* (1.8 kg O₂/kWh) — *not* the oxygen demand.
> 4. **RBC unit confusion:** The area $A$ in the RBC formula refers to the *submerged* disc area, not the total disc area. Use $A = \pi D^2 / 2$ per disc, not $\pi D^2$.
> 5. **Conflating BOD₅ and COD:** UASB problems often use COD (not BOD) — confirm the substrate parameter before plugging in the empirical methane yield.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

### UASB Reactor
- High-rate anaerobic, suspended growth, granular sludge
- 4 zones: Inlet distribution → Sludge blanket → Slurry zone → Three-phase separator
- HRT = 4–24 h, OLR = 5–30 kg COD/m³·d
- Biogas yield = 0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD removed
- $V = Q \cdot \theta$ ; $OLR = Q \cdot S_0 / V$
- **Best for:** Tropical food industry, Kerala
- **Avoid:** High SS (>1000 mg/L), low-strength waste

### Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)
- Fixed-film attached growth, aerobic
- 1–5 rpm, 40% submerged / 60% air-exposed
- 3–6 stages; later stages → nitrification
- Disc area formula: $A = \pi D^2 / 2$ per disc
- $S_e = S_0 / (1 + kA/Q)^n$
- **Best for:** Cold climates, decentralized, shock-resistant

### Anaerobic Filter (AF)
- Fixed-film attached growth, anaerobic
- Media: plastic rings, Raschig rings, stone
- $V_{void} = \varepsilon V$ (void volume used for HRT)
- First-order: $S_e = S_0 \cdot e^{-k\theta}$
- **Best for:** High SS, strong waste, slaughterhouses

### Aerated Lagoons (Low-Cost Treatment)
- Suspended growth, aerobic, earthen basin
- Depth 2.5–5.0 m, HRT 5–30 days
- Mechanical surface aerators (floating type)
- $V = Q\theta$, $A = V/H$
- Power: $P = O_2^{req} / \eta_{aerator}$
- **Best for:** Rural Kerala, LSG bodies, small communities
- **Disadvantage:** Large footprint, mosquito/odor potential

### Key Constants to Memorize
- Methane yield: **0.35 m³/kg COD removed (STP)**
- Oxygen-to-BOD ratio (domestic): **0.5 kg O₂/kg BOD**
- Aerator efficiency: **1.8 kg O₂/kWh** (typical)
- UASB granule size: **1–5 mm**
- RBC submergence: **40%**
- BOD removal RBC single stage: **30–60%**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stabilization Ponds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stabilization Ponds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Stabilization Ponds** (also called **Waste Stabilization Ponds, Oxidation Ponds, or Lagoons**) are engineered, earthen basins designed to treat wastewater through natural biological, physical, and chemical processes involving the symbiotic interaction of **bacteria, algae, and aquatic microorganisms**, powered entirely by **sunlight, wind, and natural aeration**.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A stabilization pond is a **shallow, artificially constructed impoundment** in which domestic or industrial wastewater is retained for a specified detention period (typically **7 days to 180 days**) to allow the natural purification of sewage by the metabolic activity of microorganisms. The system is characterized by the presence of a **stratified ecology** in which dissolved oxygen, pH, and biological populations vary with depth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Stabilization ponds are classified as **low-cost, energy-efficient biological treatment units** and are highly recommended by **CPHEEO (Central Public Health and Environmental Engineering Organisation)** for small and medium-sized communities in India, particularly in tropical and subtropical regions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sun-Bathed Lake"

Imagine a **calm, sunlit village pond** that has existed for generations. In the upper, sunlit layer, tiny green plants (algae) release oxygen as a by-product of photosynthesis. In the bottom sediments, decomposing organic matter uses that oxygen through bacteria. This natural **"give-and-take" relationship** between **algae (oxygen producers)** and **aerobic bacteria (oxygen consumers)** is the very heart of a stabilization pond. The engineer simply builds a **man-made version of this natural lake**, controls the depth, and gives it the right amount of wastewater to clean — completely without electricity or chemicals.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Stabilization ponds are widely used in Kerala (Kerala Water Authority) for treating municipal sewage in tier-2 and tier-3 towns, as the state's tropical climate is ideal for maximum algal photosynthesis efficiency. They are also used for treating wastewater from coconut, rubber, and seafood processing industries.

### 1.3 Key Physical Parameters (KTU Standard Reference Values)

- **Temperature range:** **20°C – 40°C** (optimum 25°C – 30°C)
- **Solar radiation requirement:** **40 – 100 cal/cm²/day**
- **Wind velocity (for surface re-aeration):** **> 4 km/hr** desirable
- **Recommended soil permeability:** **< 10⁻⁷ cm/sec** (otherwise clay or synthetic liner is used)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Stabilization Ponds (KTU Mandatory Topic)

Stabilization ponds are classified into four major types based on **dissolved oxygen profile, depth, and dominant microbial activity:**

#### (a) Aerobic Ponds (High-Rate Aerobic Ponds)
- **Depth:** **0.3 – 0.5 m** (sunlight penetrates the full depth)
- **Dominant process:** Photosynthesis-driven oxygen production
- **BOD loading:** **80 – 160 kg BOD/ha/day**
- **Color:** Green (algae dominate)
- **Function:** Removal of soluble organics via aerobic decomposition

#### (b) Anaerobic Ponds
- **Depth:** **2.5 – 5.0 m**
- **Dominant process:** Anaerobic digestion of settled solids
- **BOD loading:** **400 – 3000 kg BOD/ha/day**
- **No dissolved oxygen** in any layer
- **Function:** High-strength wastewater treatment, sludge digestion, biogas production

#### (c) Facultative Ponds (Most Common Type)
- **Depth:** **1.0 – 2.5 m**
- **Stratified into three layers:**
  - **Top aerobic zone** (0 – 0.3 m) — algae + aerobic bacteria
  - **Middle facultative zone** (0.3 – 0.5 m) — facultative bacteria
  - **Bottom anaerobic zone** (sludge layer)
- **BOD loading:** **100 – 400 kg BOD/ha/day**
- **Detention time:** **5 – 30 days**

#### (d) Maturation Ponds (Tertiary Ponds)
- **Depth:** **0.5 – 1.5 m**
