- **Function:** Polishing effluent; pathogen removal; fecal coliform reduction
- **Detention time:** **7 – 20 days**
- **Typical arrangement:** Series of 3 ponds (e.g., **3-cell maturation pond system**)

### 2.2 The Algae-Bacteria Symbiosis (Core Biological Principle)

| Layer | Depth | Process | Key Organism |
|-------|-------|---------|--------------|
| Surface (aerobic) | 0 – 0.3 m | Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ | Algae (Chlorella, Euglena) |
| Middle (facultative) | 0.3 – 0.5 m | Both aerobic and anaerobic degradation | Facultative bacteria |
| Bottom (anaerobic) | 0.5 m – base | Anaerobic digestion, sludge formation | Anaerobic bacteria |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photosynthetic Oxygenation Formula:** The oxygen produced by algae in a facultative pond during daytime is governed by **P = Solar energy × Algal concentration × Quantum yield**. At night, respiration consumes this oxygen, but the pond remains net **oxygen-positive** over a 24-hour cycle.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** All vertical absolute-value and separator symbols in this table are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve the markdown table structure.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical KTU Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $V = Q \times t$ | Pond volume (volumetric balance) | m³ |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{V}{D} = \dfrac{Q \times t}{D}$ | Required pond surface area | m² |
| 3 | $L_{org} = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{A}$ | Areal (surface) BOD loading rate | kg BOD/ha/day |
| 4 | $L_v = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V}$ | Volumetric BOD loading rate | kg BOD/m³/day |
| 5 | $\dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k \cdot t}$ | **Marais-Shaw** complete-mix model (zero-order kinetics with recycle of biomass) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + k \cdot t}$ | Fractional BOD removal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = e^{-k \cdot t}$ | First-order plug-flow model | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = \dfrac{4 \cdot a \cdot e^{1/(2d)}}{(1+a)^2 \cdot e^{a/(2d)} - (1-a)^2 \cdot e^{-a/(2d)}}$ | **Wehner-Wilhelm** equation for non-ideal ponds (with $a = \sqrt{1 + 4 k t d}$ and $d = D_p t / L^2$ dispersion number) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$ | Temperature correction of rate constant (with $\theta = 1.05$ for ponds) | 1/day |
| 10 | $N_e = \dfrac{N_i}{1 + k_b \cdot t}$ | Coliform removal (Chick's law) with $k_b = 0.7$/day typical | MPN/100 mL |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Application

Stabilization ponds form the **backbone of low-cost sanitation** in developing countries. They are especially preferred where:
- **Land is inexpensive** (e.g., rural Kerala)
- **Skilled operators are scarce** (the system is virtually self-operating)
- **Energy supply is unreliable** (no aerators or pumps required)
- The treatment **goal is pathogen reduction** (e.g., 2-log to 4-log fecal coliform removal)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Marais-Shaw Complete-Mix Pond Equation

The Marais-Shaw equation models a facultative pond as a **complete-mix reactor with biomass recycle from the bottom sediment layer**. The derivation proceeds as follows.

**Step 1: Mass balance on substrate (BOD) in the pond**

$$
\text{Rate of accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} - \text{Removal}
$$

Assuming steady-state (accumulation = 0) and complete-mix behavior:

$$
Q \cdot S_0 = Q \cdot S_e + V \cdot r_{sub}
$$

where $r_{sub}$ is the volumetric substrate utilization rate.

**Step 2: Apply first-order kinetics for substrate removal**

$$
r_{sub} = k \cdot S_e
$$

where $k$ is the first-order BOD removal rate constant (1/day).

**Step 3: Substitute and simplify**

$$
Q \cdot S_0 = Q \cdot S_e + V \cdot k \cdot S_e
$$

**Step 4: Divide both sides by $Q$**

$$
S_0 = S_e + \dfrac{V}{Q} \cdot k \cdot S_e
$$

**Step 5: Recall that $V / Q = t$ (hydraulic detention time)**

$$
S_0 = S_e \cdot (1 + k \cdot t)
$$

**Step 6: Rearrange to get the classical Marais-Shaw form**

$$
\dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k \cdot t}
$$

**Step 7: Define the BOD removal efficiency $\eta$**

$$
\eta = 1 - \dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + k \cdot t}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** For $k = 0.10$/day and $t = 20$ days, $\eta = 1 - 1/(1+2) = 1 - 0.333 = 66.7\%$. This is consistent with **field data from Kerala-type facultative ponds**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: BOD Removal vs. Detention Time (k = 0.10/day)

| Detention Time $t$ (days) | $1 + k \cdot t$ | Effluent Ratio $S_e / S_0$ | BOD Removal $\eta$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 5 | 1.5 | 0.667 | 33.3% |
| 10 | 2.0 | 0.500 | 50.0% |
| 15 | 2.5 | 0.400 | 60.0% |
| 20 | 3.0 | 0.333 | 66.7% |
| 25 | 3.5 | 0.286 | 71.4% |
| 30 | 4.0 | 0.250 | 75.0% |
| 50 | 6.0 | 0.167 | 83.3% |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Design Problem)

**Problem:** A small town in Kerala has a **population of 50,000**. The per-capita sewage flow is **150 Lpcd** and the **influent BOD is 250 mg/L**. Design a facultative stabilization pond with **detention time 25 days** and **depth 1.5 m**. Verify the loading rate and expected BOD removal. Assume $k = 0.10$/day.

**Step 1: Calculate the average daily flow**

$$
Q = P \times q = 50{,}000 \times 150 = 7{,}500{,}000 \text{ L/day}
$$

$$
Q = 7{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the required pond volume**

$$
V = Q \times t = 7{,}500 \times 25 = 187{,}500 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the required surface area**

$$
A = \dfrac{V}{D} = \dfrac{187{,}500}{1.5} = 125{,}000 \text{ m}^2 = 12.5 \text{ ha}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the areal BOD loading rate**

$$
L_{org} = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{A} = \dfrac{7{,}500 \times 250 \times 10^{-3}}{12.5} = 150 \text{ kg BOD/ha/day}
$$

**Step 5: Verify against CPHEEO standard**

The value of **150 kg BOD/ha/day** lies within the standard facultative pond range of **100 – 400 kg BOD/ha/day** ✓ (Design is acceptable).

**Step 6: Calculate expected BOD removal efficiency using Marais-Shaw**

$$
\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + k \cdot t} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.10 \times 25} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{3.5} = 0.714
$$

**Step 7: Calculate effluent BOD**

$$
S_e = S_0 \times (1 - \eta) = 250 \times (1 - 0.714) = 250 \times 0.286 = 71.5 \text{ mg/L}
$$

### 3.4 Design Validation Check (Comprehensive Analytical Matrix)

| Parameter | Calculated | KTU/CPHEEO Standard | Status |
|-----------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Detention time | 25 days | 5 – 30 days | ✓ |
| Depth | 1.5 m | 1.0 – 2.5 m | ✓ |
| Areal BOD loading | 150 kg/ha/day | 100 – 400 kg/ha/day | ✓ |
| Effluent BOD | 71.5 mg/L | < 100 mg/L for discharge | ✓ |
| BOD removal | 71.4% | 60 – 90% | ✓ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Stabilization Ponds (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STABILIZATION PONDS"]
    nodeA --> nodeB["AEROBIC POND"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["ANAEROBIC POND"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["FACULTATIVE POND"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["MATURATION POND"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Depth 0.3 to 0.5 m"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["BOD 80 to 160 kg per ha per day"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Algal Photosynthesis Dominant"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Depth 2.5 to 5.0 m"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["BOD 400 to 3000 kg per ha per day"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["No Dissolved Oxygen"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Depth 1.0 to 2.5 m"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["BOD 100 to 400 kg per ha per day"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Three Layered Ecology"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3a["Top Aerobic Zone"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3b["Middle Facultative Zone"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3c["Bottom Anaerobic Sludge"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["Depth 0.5 to 1.5 m"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["Tertiary Polishing Function"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["Pathogen Removal Focus"]
    style nodeA fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeB fill:#22c55e,color:#000000
    style nodeC fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    style nodeD fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style nodeE fill:#facc15,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Algae-Bacteria Symbiotic Cycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inf["INFLUENT WASTEWATER BOD SUBSTRATE"]
    bact["AEROBIC BACTERIA DEGRADATION ZONE"]
    co2["CO2 RELEASED INTO WATER"]
    alga["ALGAL PHOTOSYNTHESIS ZONE SUNLIT"]
    o2["O2 RELEASED INTO WATER"]
    out["TREATED EFFLUENT BOD REDUCED"]
    inf --> bact
    bact --> co2
    co2 --> alga
    alga --> o2
    o2 --> bact
    bact --> out
    style inf fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    style bact fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style co2 fill:#475569,color:#ffffff
    style alga fill:#22c55e,color:#000000
    style o2 fill:#facc15,color:#000000
    style out fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Typical Schematic — Facultative Pond Cross-Section (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inlet["RAW SEWAGE INLET"]
    p1["INLET ZONE"]
    p2["FACULTATIVE POND BODY 1.0 TO 2.5 m DEEP"]
    topA["SURFACE AEROBIC LAYER ALGAE PLUS O2"]
    midA["MIDDLE FACULTATIVE LAYER"]
    botA["BOTTOM ANAEROBIC SLUDGE LAYER"]
    outlet["TREATED EFFLUENT OUTLET"]
    inlet --> p1
    p1 --> p2
    p2 --> topA
    p2 --> midA
    p2 --> botA
    topA --> outlet
    midA --> outlet
    botA --> outlet
    style inlet fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    style p1 fill:#475569,color:#ffffff
    style p2 fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style topA fill:#22c55e,color:#000000
    style midA fill:#facc15,color:#000000
    style botA fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    style outlet fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a stabilization pond. List the various types of stabilization ponds.*

**Model Answer:**
A stabilization pond is a shallow, artificially constructed earthen basin in which wastewater is treated by the natural symbiotic activity of algae and bacteria, using solar energy as the driving force. The main types are:
1. **Aerobic (high-rate) pond** – depth 0.3–0.5 m
2. **Anaerobic pond** – depth 2.5–5.0 m
3. **Facultative pond** – depth 1.0–2.5 m (most common)
4. **Maturation (tertiary) pond** – depth 0.5–1.5 m for pathogen removal

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the role of algae in a facultative stabilization pond.*

**Model Answer:**
Algae are the **primary source of dissolved oxygen** in a facultative pond. Through photosynthesis (6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂), algae convert solar energy into chemical energy and **release molecular oxygen**. This oxygen supports aerobic bacteria that oxidize organic matter (BOD) in the wastewater. In turn, the bacteria release CO₂, which the algae use for photosynthesis. This **mutually beneficial algae–bacteria symbiosis** is the biological engine of facultative ponds.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In 14-mark questions, students commonly lose marks by (1) **failing to draw a labelled sketch** of the facultative pond, (2) **omitting units** in the BOD loading calculation, and (3) **forgetting the temperature correction** of the rate constant $k$. Always show step-by-step derivations with units — even if the question is "explain in brief." Marks are awarded for clarity and completeness, not brevity.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A.(a) Explain the working principle of a facultative stabilization pond with a neat sketch. Discuss its design criteria.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:** A facultative pond operates on the principle of **algae–bacteria symbiosis** and **vertical stratification**.

**Three zones function simultaneously:**

1. **Surface aerobic zone (top 0.3 m):** Sunlight penetrates; algae (Chlorella, Euglena, Scenedesmus) photosynthesize and produce O₂. Aerobic bacteria use this O₂ to oxidize soluble BOD into CO₂ and H₂O.

2. **Middle facultative zone:** Facultative bacteria can function in either aerobic or anaerobic mode depending on local O₂ availability. They further degrade organics that escape the top layer.

3. **Bottom anaerobic zone:** Suspended solids settle to form sludge. Anaerobic bacteria digest this sludge, producing CH₄, CO₂, and H₂S. This is essentially **in-situ sludge stabilization**.

**Design Criteria (CPHEEO/KTU standard):**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Detention time | 5 – 30 days |
| Depth | 1.0 – 2.5 m |
| BOD loading | 100 – 400 kg/ha/day |
| BOD removal | 60 – 90% |
| Freeboard | 0.5 – 1.0 m |
| Shape | Rectangular, length:width ≈ 2:1 to 4:1 |
| Soil permeability | < 10⁻⁷ cm/sec |

**Sketch (to be drawn by student in exam):** Cross-section of facultative pond with three labelled zones, inlet, outlet, freeboard, and sludge layer.

---

**Q.A.(b) Design a facultative stabilization pond for a residential colony with the following data: Population = 50,000; sewage flow = 150 Lpcd; BOD = 250 mg/L. Assume detention time = 25 days, depth = 1.5 m. Verify the loading rate and compute the BOD removal efficiency (use k = 0.10/day).** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $P = 50{,}000$, $q = 150$ Lpcd, $S_0 = 250$ mg/L, $t = 25$ days, $D = 1.5$ m, $k = 0.10$/day.

**Step 1 — Sewage flow:**

$$
Q = 50{,}000 \times 150 = 7{,}500{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 7{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 2 — Pond volume:**

$$
V = Q \times t = 7{,}500 \times 25 = 187{,}500 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Surface area:**

$$
A = \dfrac{V}{D} = \dfrac{187{,}500}{1.5} = 125{,}000 \text{ m}^2 = 12.5 \text{ ha}
$$

**Step 4 — Areal BOD loading:**

$$
L_{org} = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{A} = \dfrac{7{,}500 \times 0.250}{12.5} = 150 \text{ kg BOD/ha/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification:** $L_{org} = 150$ kg/ha/day lies within the standard facultative pond range of **100 – 400 kg BOD/ha/day** ✓.

**Step 6 — BOD removal efficiency (Marais-Shaw):**

$$
\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + k \cdot t} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.10 \times 25} = 1 - 0.2857 = 0.714 \text{ or } 71.4\%
$$

**Step 7 — Effluent BOD:**

$$
S_e = 250 \times (1 - 0.714) = 71.5 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Conclusion:** Pond area = 12.5 ha, BOD removal = 71.4%, effluent BOD ≈ 71.5 mg/L. **Design is acceptable.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B.(a) Compare aerobic, anaerobic, and facultative stabilization ponds in detail.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Aerobic Pond | Anaerobic Pond | Facultative Pond |
|---------|--------------|----------------|------------------|
| Depth | 0.3 – 0.5 m | 2.5 – 5.0 m | 1.0 – 2.5 m |
| Oxygen | Throughout | Absent | Top layer only |
| BOD loading | 80 – 160 kg/ha/day | 400 – 3000 kg/ha/day | 100 – 400 kg/ha/day |
| Detention time | 3 – 5 days | 20 – 50 days | 5 – 30 days |
| Algal role | Very high | Negligible | High (top zone) |
| Temperature sensitivity | High | Low | Moderate |
| Sludge accumulation | Low | Very high | Moderate |
| Effluent quality | Excellent BOD & DO | Poor, malodorous | Good, with pathogens |
| Typical use | Polishing, algal biomass | High-strength industrial | Municipal sewage |

---

**Q.B.(b) Enumerate the advantages, disadvantages, and design considerations of stabilization ponds.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages:**
1. Low construction cost (earthwork + clay liner only)
2. **No electricity** required — fully solar-powered
3. Minimal operation and maintenance
4. Effective pathogen and BOD removal
5. Constructable in unskilled-labour settings
6. Compatible with intermittent flows
7. Long life (sludge removal only every 5–10 years)

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Large land area** required
2. Mosquito and odour nuisance if poorly designed
3. Performance drops in cold climates
4. Possible algal blooms in effluent
5. Groundwater contamination risk if liner fails
6. Limited hydraulic flexibility
7. Seepage losses in porous soils

**Design Considerations:**
1. Detention time based on BOD loading and temperature
2. Depth selection (climate, BOD strength)
3. Freeboard (≥ 0.5 m)
4. Inlet/outlet positioning (avoid short-circuiting)
5. Multi-cell series configuration (≥ 3 cells preferred for pathogen reduction)
6. Liner specification (clay or HDPE for permeable soils)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Sheet for Stabilization Ponds**

- ⭐ **Stabilization ponds = natural, solar-powered biological reactors** that use algae-bacteria symbiosis.
- ⭐ **Four types:** Aerobic (shallow), Anaerobic (deep), Facultative (most common), Maturation (polishing).
- ⭐ **Facultative pond depth = 1.0 – 2.5 m** is the *most-asked KTU value*.
- ⭐ **BOD loading for facultative pond = 100 – 400 kg BOD/ha/day** (CPHEEO standard).
- ⭐ **Marais-Shaw equation:** $S_e / S_0 = 1 / (1 + k \cdot t)$ — used for **complete-mix** design.
- ⭐ **Wehner-Wilhelm equation** is used for **non-ideal / dispersed-flow** ponds.
- ⭐ **First-order plug-flow equation:** $S_e / S_0 = e^{-k \cdot t}$ is the simplest model.
- ⭐ **Temperature correction:** $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$, with $\theta = 1.05$.
- ⭐ **Algae produce O₂ during daytime** via photosynthesis; **bacteria consume O₂** to oxidize BOD.
- ⭐ **Volume formula:** $V = Q \times t$; **Area formula:** $A = V / D$.
- ⭐ **Coliform removal** follows Chick's law: $N_e = N_i / (1 + k_b t)$, $k_b \approx 0.7$/day.
- ⭐ **Kerala is ideal for stabilization ponds** because of high solar radiation, warm temperature, and abundant land.
- ⭐ **Freeboard, L:W ratio, multi-cell design, and clay/HDPE liner** are always discussed in design sections.
- ⭐ **Sludge accumulation rate ≈ 0.05 m³/person/year** in facultative ponds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oxidation Ditches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Oxidation Ditches # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Oxidation Ditch (OD):** A modified activated sludge biological treatment process in which wastewater is circulated in an oval/ring-shaped channel at a velocity of **0.25 – 0.35 m/s** using mechanical aeration devices (brushes, rotors, or disc aerators). It operates as an **extended aeration system** with long hydraulic retention time (**HRT = 24–48 hours**), high solids retention time (**SRT = 20–30 days**), and mixed liquor suspended solids (**MLSS = 3000–6000 mg/L**), achieving simultaneous **organic matter (BOD) removal, nitrification, and partial denitrification**. In the KTU 2024 scheme, the oxidation ditch is categorized under **Suspended-Growth Aerobic Biological Treatment Systems** and is the most energy-efficient variant of the activated sludge process for **small to medium-sized communities (5,000 – 1,00,000 population equivalent)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Lazy River:** > Imagine a circular, shallow **"lazy river"** at a water park. Passengers (wastewater) enter the river at one point and are carried continuously around the loop by paddles/blowers (rotor aerators). While circulating slowly (typically 0.3 m/s ≈ 1.08 km/hr), microbes (the "bacteria passengers") in the water consume the organic load (the "trash"). The water makes so many loops that almost all organic matter is digested before the water exits at the clarifier. The slow, long journey = long **HRT**; the bacteria staying put inside the loop = high **SRT**; and the paddles splashing oxygen = aeration. ## 1.3 Key Physical & Design Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | |---|---| | Channel depth | **1.0 – 3.5 m** | | Channel width | **2.0 – 6.0 m** | | Detention time (HRT) | **24 – 48 h** | | MLSS | **3000 – 6000 mg/L** | | F/M ratio | **0.04 – 0.10 kg BOD/kg MLVSS·d** | | SRT (θc) | **20 – 30 days** | | Recirculation ratio | **50 – 100 % of Q** | | Velocity of flow | **0.25 – 0.35 m/s** | | Oxygen required | **1.5 – 2.0 kg O₂/kg BOD removed** | | Sludge yield (Y) | **0.3 – 0.5 kg VSS/kg BOD** | ## 1.4 Historical Evolution (KTU Syllabus Highlight) - **1950s — Dr. A. Pasveer** (Netherlands) developed the **Pasveer Ditch** using brush-type aerators for small villages. - **1960s — D.A. Grieves, Lakeside Ltd** invented the **Rotor Aerator** (cage rotor) for deeper ditches. - **1960s — Eimco** (later Envirex) developed the **Orbal™** — multi-channel ditch with disc aerators. - **1970s — Haworth, Hooper & Kock** of Smit-Durban developed the **Carrousel™** — a deep ditch with vertical-shaft low-speed aerators. > [!NOTE] > The four patented variants — **Pasveer, Carrousel, Orbal, and Owite-Schreiber** — are the only configurations tested for KTU descriptive questions. Memorize the inventor and aerator type for each. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Process Configuration & Components An oxidation ditch consists of **five essential elements**: 1. **Ring/Oval Channel** — Reinforced concrete, earthen embankment, or steel; elliptical layout minimizes dead zones. 2. **Aeration Device** — Either a horizontal-shaft **brush aerator** (submerged to 8–30 cm) or a **cage rotor** (immersed 12–40 cm) that provides both oxygen transfer and propulsive velocity. 3. **Influent Distribution** — Fed at the upstream side of the rotor for maximum mixing time. 4. **Effluent Withdrawal** — Passes into a secondary clarifier (Settling Tank). 5. **Return Activated Sludge (RAS)** — Recirculated at 50–100 % of Q to maintain MLSS concentration. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Slow Circulation The induced velocity of **0.25–0.35 m/s** serves **two simultaneous functions**: - **Aeration:** The rotor/blade tips dip into the water and throw droplets into the air, increasing air–water interfacial area → high **O₂ transfer efficiency (OTE = 1.2 – 2.5 kg O₂/kWh)**. - **Mixing:** The horizontal velocity prevents settling of MLSS within the ditch — **minimum velocity ≥ 0.15 m/s** to keep particles in suspension; **0.30 m/s** is the design standard. ## 2.3 Biochemical Reactions Inside the Ditch Inside the ditch, the following reactions occur sequentially along the loop: 1. **Carbonaceous Oxidation (Aerobic):** $$C_5H_7NO_2 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 5CO_2 + 2H_2O + NH_3 + \text{Energy}$$ 2. **Nitrification (Autotrophic, by *Nitrosomonas* & *Nitrobacter*):** $$NH_4^+ + 2O_2 \rightarrow NO_3^- + 2H^+ + H_2O$$ 3. **Partial Denitrification (Anoxic zones near bottom):** $$NO_3^- + \text{Organic C} \rightarrow N_2 \uparrow + CO_2 + H_2O$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Because of the long **SRT (20–30 days)** and high dissolved oxygen (**DO = 1.5–2.0 mg/L** in the bulk), oxidation ditches are the **preferred design for ammonia-sensitive receiving waters** (e.g., rivers flowing into Vembanad Lake in Kerala). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorize for ESE & University Exam.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V = Q \times HRT$ | Volume of ditch required | m³ | | 2 | $A = V / d$ | Cross-sectional area (V = volume, d = depth) | m² | | 3 | $F/M = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V \times X}$ | Food to Microorganism ratio | d⁻¹ | | 4 | $X = \dfrac{\theta_c \cdot Y(S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$ | MLVSS concentration | mg/L | | 5 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + (Q - Q_w)X_e}$ | Solids Retention Time (SRT) | days | | 6 | $O_2 = \dfrac{Q (S_0 - S) \times 1.42 \times Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c} + 4.57 Q(NH_4)$ | Total Oxygen Demand | kg O₂/d | | 7 | $P = \dfrac{O_2}{\text{SOTR} \times \text{OTE}}$ | Aerator power required | kW | | 8 | $v = \dfrac{Q (1+R)}{A_c}$ | Channel velocity (R = recirculation) | m/s | | 9 | $RAS\% = \dfrac{X}{X_r - X} \times 100$ | Recirculation percent | % | | 10 | $N = \dfrac{P}{\pi D L \eta}$ | Number of rotors (η = efficiency) | integer | **Standard Coefficients (KEEP for KTU problems):** | Coefficient | Value | |---|---| | Yield coefficient, $Y$ | 0.4 – 0.6 kg VSS/kg BOD | | Endogenous decay, $k_d$ | 0.04 – 0.08 d⁻¹ | | Oxygen per unit biomass | **1.42** kg O₂/kg VSS | | Oxygen per unit NH₄⁺ nitrified | **4.57** kg O₂/kg N | | Kinetic constant, $K_s$ | 60 – 100 mg/L BOD | | Maximum specific growth, $\mu_m$ | 3 – 5 d⁻¹ | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Decentralized municipal plants** (Kerala: KWA rural schemes, smart-village projects under AMRUT). - **Industrial effluents** with high ammonia — dairy, slaughterhouse, food processing. - **BOD-sensitive receiving waters** (KTU frequently cites Vembanad backwaters). - **Energy-conscious plants** — Carrousel 2000 with biological phosphorus removal (Bio-Denipho) saves up to **40 % energy** vs. conventional ASP. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Ditch Volume from HRT (Typical 7-Mark Question) **Problem [KTU University Exam, Model]:** A small township generates $Q = 5{,}000$ m³/d of wastewater with $BOD_5 = 250$ mg/L. Design an oxidation ditch with **HRT = 30 hours** and **depth = 1.5 m**. Calculate the volume, cross-sectional area, and channel length (assume width:length = 1:5 elliptical layout). ### Step 1 — Convert HRT to consistent units $$HRT = 30 \text{ h} = \dfrac{30}{24} = 1.25 \text{ d}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Volume of Ditch $$V = Q \times HRT$$ $$V = 5{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \times 1.25 \text{ d}$$ $$V = 6{,}250 \text{ m}^3$$ ### Step 3 — Cross-sectional area $$A_c = \dfrac{V}{d} = \dfrac{6{,}250}{1.5}$$ $$A_c = 4{,}166.67 \text{ m}^2$$ ### Step 4 — Wetted perimeter / channel dimensions For an elliptical layout with aspect ratio $\dfrac{L}{W} = 5$ and $L \times W = A_c$: $$5 W^2 = 4{,}166.67$$ $$W = \sqrt{833.33} = 28.87 \text{ m}$$ $$L = 5 \times 28.87 = 144.34 \text{ m}$$ ### Step 5 — Oval circumference (perimeter of travel) For an ellipse approximation: $$C \approx \pi (L + W) / 2 = \pi(144.34 + 28.87)/2 \approx 271.9 \text{ m}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The velocity check:** $v = Q(1+R)/A_c$. With $R = 1$, $A_c$ must be large enough to keep $v \le 0.35$ m/s — this is the designer's primary constraint. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Aerator Oxygen Demand & Power **Problem:** For the same plant, design the rotor aerator. Given: $S_0 = 250$ mg/L, $S = 10$ mg/L (effluent), $Q = 5{,}000$ m³/d, $Y = 0.5$, $k_d = 0.06$ d⁻¹, $\theta_c = 25$ days. ### Step 1 — Oxygen for carbonaceous BOD $$\text{O}_2^{\text{Carb}} = \dfrac{Q(S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d \theta_c} \times 1.42 - 1.42 \cdot k_d \cdot X \cdot V \cdot \theta_c / \theta_c$$ Simplified KTU formula (Metcalf & Eddy): $$O_2 = \dfrac{Q(S_0 - S) \times 1.42}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$$ $$\Delta S = 250 - 10 = 240 \text{ mg/L} = 0.240 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ $$Q \cdot \Delta S = 5{,}000 \times 0.240 = 1{,}200 \text{ kg/d}$$ $$1 + k_d \theta_c = 1 + 0.06 \times 25 = 2.5$$ $$O_2^{\text{Carb}} = \dfrac{1{,}200 \times 1.42}{2.5} = 681.6 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}$$ ### Step 2 — Add nitrification oxygen (if NH₄⁺ present) Assume $NH_4 = 30$ mg/L, complete nitrification: $$O_2^{\text{Nitr}} = 4.57 \times Q \times NH_4$$ $$O_2^{\text{Nitr}} = 4.57 \times 5{,}000 \times 0.030 = 685.5 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}$$ ### Step 3 — Total Oxygen Required $$O_2^{\text{Total}} = 681.6 + 685.5 = 1{,}367.1 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}$$ ### Step 4 — Power requirement of rotor aerator Standard Oxygen Transfer Efficiency (SOTE) for a cage rotor = **1.8 kg O₂/kWh** (with submergence correction): $$P = \dfrac{O_2^{\text{Total}}}{\text{SOTE} \times 24 \text{ h}}$$ $$P = \dfrac{1{,}367.1}{1.8 \times 24}$$ $$P = 31.65 \text{ kW}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** A 35 kW motor with a single rotor is sufficient. KTU answers accept rotor specs of $D = 1.0$ m, $L = 3.0$ m at 70–80 rpm. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Oxidation Ditch Variants | Feature | Pasveer Ditch | Carrousel | Orbal | Owite-Schreiber | |---|---|---|---|---| | Year | 1954 | 1967 | 1960 | 1957 | | Shape | Oval | Ring with central wall | 3 nested rings | Ring | | Depth (m) | 1.0 – 1.5 | 2.5 – 5.0 | 2.0 – 3.6 | 1.5 – 3.0 | | Aerator | Brush | Vertical-shaft | Disc | Brush + disc | | OTE (kg O₂/kWh) | 1.2 – 1.5 | 1.8 – 2.5 | 1.5 – 2.0 | 1.2 – 1.6 | | HRT (h) | 24 – 36 | 18 – 36 | 8 – 36 | 24 – 36 | | Best for | < 5,000 PE | Large plants | N-rich waste | Small towns | > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **Orbal** (multiple channels, disc aerators) with **Carrousel** (single deep channel, vertical-shaft aerators). Orbal uses **concentric multi-channel** while Carrousel uses a **deep, single-loop** design. ## 3.4 Design Verification — Solids Mass Balance A complete design requires checking the clarifier underflow concentration $X_r$: $$X_r = \dfrac{X(1 + R) - R \cdot X_e}{1}$$ Typical $X_e = 10$ mg/L, $X = 4{,}000$ mg/L, $R = 1$: $$X_r = 4{,}000(1+1) - 1(10) = 7{,}990 \text{ mg/L}$$ This $X_r$ is then used to size the sludge return pump. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Plant-Level Flow Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Influent WW] --> B[Preliminary Treatment
Screening and Grit Removal] B --> C[Primary Clarifier
optional in OD plants] C --> D[Oxidation Ditch
Ring Channel with Rotor] D --> E[Secondary Clarifier
Settling Tank] E --> F[Chlorination or UV] F --> G[Treated Effluent Discharge] E --> H[Return Activated Sludge RAS] H --> D E --> I[Waste Activated Sludge WAS] I --> J[Sludge Thickener and Digester] ``` ## 4.2 Internal Ditch Flow and Aeration Layout ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OD[Oxidation Ditch Ring Channel] direction LR nodeR[Rotor Aerator
Horizontal Shaft
70 to 80 rpm] nodeA[Aerobic Zone
DO 1.5 to 2.0 mg per L] nodeB[Anoxic Zone
DO less than 0.2 mg per L] nodeC[Mixing Zone
Velocity 0.3 m per s] nodeR --> nodeA nodeA --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeR end Inff[Influent Entry] --> nodeB nodeA --> Outff[Effluent to Clarifier] ``` ## 4.3 Rotor Aerator Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB M[Electric Motor 25 to 40 kW] --> GB[Gearbox Reducer 10 to 1] GB --> SH[Horizontal Shaft 70 to 80 rpm] SH --> BB[Bearings and Pillow Blocks] SH --> CR[Cage Rotor with Blades
Submerged 12 to 40 cm] CR --> A1[Air Contact and O2 Transfer] CR --> V1[Propulsive Velocity 0.3 m per s] A1 --> Eff[O2 Dissolved into Mixed Liquor] V1 --> Mix[MLSS Kept in Suspension] ``` ## 4.4 Typical Cross-Section of Channel with Rotor Position ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph XS[Channel Cross Section Side View] WS[Water Surface] --> AS[Air Space 30 cm] AS --> RT[Rotor Diameter 1.0 m] RT --> IMM[Immersion Depth 12 to 40 cm] IMM --> WL[Water Level Line] WL --> CH[Channel Depth 1.5 to 3.0 m] CH --> CB[Channel Base Concrete] end ``` ## 4.5 Complete Treatment Train with Mass Streams ```mermaid flowchart LR Qin[Q 5000 m3 per d] --> Inff1[Influent BOD 250 mg per L] Inff1 --> ODproc[OD Process] ODproc --> S1[Effluent BOD less than 10 mg per L] ODproc --> MLSS[MLSS 4000 mg per L] S1 --> CLAR[Clarifier] CLAR --> Qeff[Q Effluent 5000 m3 per d] CLAR --> QRAS[Q RAS 5000 m3 per d] CLAR --> QWAS[Q WAS 50 m3 per d] QRAS --> ODproc QWAS --> DIG[Sludge Digester] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]** What is an oxidation ditch? List any **two** distinguishing features of an oxidation ditch system. **Model Answer (3 marks):** An oxidation ditch is a **modified activated sludge process** in which wastewater is circulated in an oval/ring-shaped channel at a velocity of **0.25–0.35 m/s** by mechanical aerators (brush/rotor/disc). **Two distinguishing features:** 1. **Long HRT (24–48 h) and high SRT (20–30 d)** — operates as extended aeration; very stable operation. 2. **Single, ring-shaped aeration basin** with combined aeration and mixing in one unit — eliminates need for separate aeration tanks and return sludge pumping in some configurations (no dedicated return sludge in Pasveer variant). --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Compare the **F/M ratio** and **MLSS** of a conventional activated sludge plant with that of an oxidation ditch. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Conventional ASP | Oxidation Ditch | |---|---|---| | F/M (kg BOD/kg MLVSS·d) | 0.2 – 0.5 | 0.04 – 0.10 | | MLSS (mg/L) | 1,500 – 3,000 | 3,000 – 6,000 | | SRT (days) | 5 – 15 | 20 – 30 | The oxidation ditch operates at **lower F/M** (substrate-limited) and **higher MLSS**, favouring nitrification and stable sludge quality. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) > **Q.A [KTU University Exam — Apr 2024]** > (a) **Explain with a neat sketch the working of a Pasveer oxidation ditch.** Discuss the role of the brush aerator. **(7 marks)** > (b) **Design an oxidation ditch** for a community of population 50,000 with per-capita wastewater flow 150 L/d and BOD of 200 mg/L. Assume HRT = 30 h, depth = 1.5 m, and the channel has an elliptical layout with L/W = 5. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** - **(1 mark)** Definition: ring/oval channel, brush-type horizontal aerator at one end, velocity 0.30 m/s induced. - **(2 marks)** Sketch: influent pipe → ditch with brush rotor → effluent weir → secondary clarifier with RAS line. - **(2 marks)** Brush aerator: half-submerged (8–30 cm) with steel bristles/boards; throws water into air in a fountain pattern → high OTE (1.2–1.5 kg O₂/kWh); provides mixing velocity. - **(2 marks)** Operating parameters: HRT 24–36 h, MLSS 4,000–6,000 mg/L, BOD removal > 95 %, ammonia nitrification > 90 %. **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Flow & BOD load** $$Q = 50{,}000 \times 150 = 7.5 \times 10^6 \text{ L/d} = 7{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$ $$\text{BOD load} = 7{,}500 \times 0.200 = 1{,}500 \text{ kg BOD/d}$$ **Step 2 — Ditch volume from HRT** $$V = 7{,}500 \times (30/24) = 9{,}375 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Cross-sectional area** $$A_c = V / d = 9{,}375 / 1.5 = 6{,}250 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4 — Channel dimensions (L/W = 5)** $$5W^2 = 6{,}250 \rightarrow W = 35.36 \text{ m}, \; L = 176.78 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — F/M check** (Assume X = 4,000 mg/L) $$F/M = \dfrac{1{,}500}{9{,}375 \times 4.0} = 0.040 \text{ kg BOD/kg MLVSS·d} \quad \checkmark$$ (Value lies within design range 0.04–0.10) **Step 6 — Rotor specification** $$O_2 = 1.5 \times 1{,}500 = 2{,}250 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}$$ $$P = \dfrac{2{,}250}{1.8 \times 24} = 52.1 \text{ kW} \rightarrow \text{Use 55 kW motor with two rotors of } D = 1.0 \text{ m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Warning:** KTU examiners award **1 mark** for correct unit conversion, **2 marks** for HRT/V calculation, **2 marks** for area/length, and **2 marks** for F/M verification. Skipping F/M verification costs 2 marks. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) > **Q.B [KTU University Exam — Apr 2024]** > (a) **Compare Carrousel and Orbal oxidation ditch systems** under the following heads: (i) Channel geometry, (ii) Aerator type, (iii) OTE, (iv) Typical application. **(7 marks)** > (b) **Estimate the oxygen requirement and power of brush aerators** for a wastewater flow of 3,000 m³/d with BOD reduction from 300 mg/L to 15 mg/L. Assume $Y = 0.5$, $k_d = 0.06$ d⁻¹, $\theta_c = 25$ d, complete nitrification of NH₄⁺ = 25 mg/L. Brush OTE = 1.5 kg O₂/kWh. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** | Head | Carrousel | Orbal | |---|---|---| | (i) Geometry | Single deep ring (2.5–5.0 m) | **3 concentric oval channels** (multi-channel) | | (ii) Aerator | **Vertical-shaft low-speed surface aerator** | **Disc aerators** on horizontal shafts | | (iii) OTE | 1.8 – 2.5 kg O₂/kWh | 1.5 – 2.0 kg O₂/kWh | | (iv) Application | Large plants (> 20,000 m³/d) with Bio-P | N-rich waste; flow 5,000 – 50,000 m³/d | **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Carbonaceous oxygen demand** $$Q(S_0 - S) = 3{,}000 \times (0.300 - 0.015) = 855 \text{ kg/d}$$ $$1 + k_d \theta_c = 1 + 0.06 \times 25 = 2.5$$ $$O_2^{\text{Carb}} = \dfrac{855 \times 1.42}{2.5} = 485.6 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}$$ **Step 2 — Nitrification oxygen demand** $$O_2^{\text{Nitr}} = 4.57 \times 3{,}000 \times 0.025 = 342.75 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}$$ **Step 3 — Total oxygen** $$O_2^{\text{Total}} = 485.6 + 342.75 = 828.4 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}$$ **Step 4 — Power** $$P = \dfrac{828.4}{1.5 \times 24} = 23.0 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5 — Specification** - Use **two brush aerators of 12 kW each** with $L = 3.0$ m, brush diameter 0.5 m, submerged 15 cm, rotating at 60 rpm. - Velocity check: $v = Q(1+1)/A_c$ — confirm $A_c$ gives $v$ in 0.25–0.35 m/s. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Using $O_2 = 1.5 \times \text{BOD}$ (rule of thumb) instead of the rigorous Metcalf & Eddy formula. The rigorous formula with $Y$, $k_d$, $\theta_c$ carries **5 marks**; the rule-of-thumb approach carries only **2 marks**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Oxidation Ditches** - ✅ Oxidation ditch = **extended aeration ASP** in **ring/oval channel** with **combined aeration + mixing**. - ✅ Velocity: **0.25–0.35 m/s** (designed for 0.30); minimum 0.15 m/s to prevent settling. - ✅ HRT = **24–48 h**; SRT = **20–30 d**; F/M = **0.04–0.10**; MLSS = **3,000–6,000 mg/L**. - ✅ **Four main variants** (must memorize): **Pasveer (brush, shallow), Carrousel (vertical-shaft, deep), Orbal (multi-channel, disc), Owite-Schreiber (brush + disc, mid-depth)**. - ✅ OTE hierarchy: **Carrousel > Orbal > Pasveer** (best energy efficiency = Carrousel). - ✅ Volume formula: $V = Q \times HRT$ → cross-section $A_c = V/d$ → elliptical layout $L \cdot W = A_c$. - ✅ Oxygen formula (Metcalf & Eddy): $O_2 = \dfrac{Q(S_0-S) \times 1.42}{1 + k_d \theta_c} + 4.57 \, Q \cdot [NH_4^+]$. - ✅ Power: $P = O_2 / (\text{SOTE} \times 24)$; SOTE in kg O₂/kWh. - ✅ Two biochemical advantages: (1) simultaneous **BOD + nitrification** due to high SRT; (2) **partial denitrification** in anoxic micro-zones near channel bottom. - ✅ Common KTU mistakes to avoid: confusing **Orbal vs Carrousel** channel layout; using **MLSS instead of MLVSS** in F/M; ignoring **F/M verification** step in design. - ✅ Kerala context: **KTU loves KWA-style municipal plants** and cites **Vembanad Lake / Periyar River** as ammonia-sensitive receiving waters where oxidation ditches are preferred.

topics.title: Tertiary Treatment - Ion Exchange
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tertiary Treatment - Ion Exchange

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Ion Exchange

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ion Exchange** is a unit operation in tertiary (or advanced) wastewater treatment involving the **reversible stoichiometric interchange of ions** between a solid phase (the ion exchange resin or zeolite) and a liquid phase (the wastewater), in which the solid phase (resin) retains its physical structure throughout the process. It is governed by the principle of **electroneutrality** and **mass action kinetics**, and is principally employed for the removal of dissolved ionic species such as hardness cations ($Ca^{2+}$, $Mg^{2+}$), heavy metals ($Pb^{2+}$, $Cd^{2+}$, $Hg^{2+}$), nitrate ($NO_3^-$), fluoride ($F^-$), and total dissolved solids (TDS) to meet stringent discharge/reuse standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Ion exchange is classified as a **physico-chemical (advanced) tertiary treatment** process. It is a *separation* process, not a *destruction* process. The contaminant ions are simply transferred from the wastewater onto the resin, and the resin is later regenerated using a concentrated regenerant solution (e.g., NaCl, HCl, NaOH).

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Magnetic Ion Swap"

Imagine a busy marketplace (the wastewater stream) where shoppers (dissolved ions like $Ca^{2+}$, $Mg^{2+}$, heavy metals) are trying to pass through a security checkpoint. The security guards (**ion exchange resin beads**) have a special rule: they will only let shoppers pass if they hand over a "voucher" — a benign ion like $Na^+$ or $H^+$ that the guard already holds.

- A shopper wanting to enter ($Ca^{2+}$) must give the guard **two $Na^+$ vouchers** (since $Ca^{2+}$ is doubly charged and electroneutrality must be preserved).
- The guard happily swaps the vouchers for the shopper's "identity" (the ion gets bound to the resin).
- The shopper ($Ca^{2+}$) walks out of the marketplace, removed from the wastewater.
- After the guards run out of $Na^+$ vouchers (resin exhaustion), they are "recharged" at the central bank (regeneration with concentrated brine), receiving fresh vouchers to resume work.

This continuous, reversible swap is exactly how an **ion exchange cycle** operates in a treatment plant.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box — Important KTU Terminology**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Resin** | An insoluble polymeric matrix (typically polystyrene-DVB) carrying fixed functional groups (e.g., $-SO_3^-$, $-N(CH_3)_3^+$) and mobile counter-ions. |
| **Cation Exchanger** | Resin with **negatively charged** fixed groups; exchanges **cations** ($Na^+$, $Ca^{2+}$). |
| **Anion Exchanger** | Resin with **positively charged** fixed groups; exchanges **anions** ($Cl^-$, $NO_3^-$, $F^-$). |
| **Selectivity** | The preference of a resin for one ion over another, governed by charge, hydrated radius, and polarizability. |
| **Regeneration** | The process of restoring the resin to its original ionic form using a concentrated regenerant solution. |

**Standard Engineering Metrics (bolded for emphasis):**
- **Exchange Capacity:** Typically **0.5 to 2.0 meq/mL** of resin (or **4 to 5 meq/g** dry resin for strong-acid cation resin).
- **Service Flow Rate:** **8 to 40 BV/h** (Bed Volumes per hour).
- **Backwash Rate:** **8 to 15 m/h**.
- **Regenerant Dosage:** **80 to 300 g NaCl/L resin** (cation softening) or **50 to 200 g HCl/L resin** (demineralization).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Ion Exchange Reaction — A Mass-Action Phenomenon

Consider a generic cation exchange between a divalent hardness ion ($Ca^{2+}$) in water and a monovalent counter-ion ($Na^+$) on a strong-acid cation (SAC) resin:

$$2R - Na^+ + Ca^{2+}_{(aq)} \rightleftharpoons R_2 - Ca^{2+} + 2Na^+_{(aq)}$$

Where $R$ represents the insoluble resin matrix with the fixed functional group. The **double-arrow** indicates reversibility — the resin can be regenerated by reversing the equilibrium using a high concentration of $Na^+$ (brine).

**The 'Why' Behind the Process:**
- The functional group (e.g., $-SO_3^-$) is permanently bonded to the resin skeleton and cannot escape.
- The counter-ion ($Na^+$) is mobile and exchangeable.
- When a more "selectable" ion ($Ca^{2+}$, with higher charge) is present, mass action drives the exchange forward.
- **Why electroneutrality is enforced:** If one $Ca^{2+}$ leaves the resin (occupying 2 fixed negative sites), exactly two $Na^+$ must leave to keep the resin charge-neutral.

### 2.2 Classification of Ion Exchange Resins (KTU Board-Favorite Topic)

| Resin Type | Functional Group | Active Form | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strong Acid Cation (SAC)** | Sulfonic acid, $-SO_3^-H^+$ | $H^+$ or $Na^+$ form | Full demineralization, water softening |
| **Weak Acid Cation (WAC)** | Carboxylic, $-COO^-H^+$ | $H^+$ form | Alkalinity reduction, partial softening |
| **Strong Base Anion (SBA) Type I** | Quaternary ammonium, $-N(CH_3)_3^+OH^-$ | $OH^-$ or $Cl^-$ | Silica removal, full demineralization |
| **Strong Base Anion (SBA) Type II** | Dimethylethanol ammonium | $Cl^-$ form | Nitrate removal |
| **Weak Base Anion (WBA)** | Tertiary amine, $-NR_2$ | Free base | Strong acid removal, dealkalization |

### 2.3 Steps in a Complete Ion Exchange Cycle (Service + Regeneration)

A typical fixed-bed ion exchange column undergoes the following sequence (each step is a high-frequency KTU question):

1. **Service (Exhaustion) Run:** Wastewater flows down through the resin bed; contaminant ions are exchanged onto the resin. The bed gradually loses capacity. Terminated at the **breakthrough point**.
2. **Backwash:** Reverse upward flow of water to fluidize the bed, remove suspended solids, and reclassify the resin (fine particles on top, coarse at bottom).
3. **Regenerant Introduction:** Concentrated regenerant (NaCl, HCl, NaOH) is passed through the bed, typically **co-current** (downflow) or **counter-current** (upflow) to the service flow direction.
4. **Slow Rinse (Displacement Rinse):** Displaces the spent regenerant from the resin pores with dilute regenerant.
5. **Fast Rinse (Final Rinse):** Fresh water flushes residual regenerant and prepares the bed for the next service cycle.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Print This for ESE Preparation**

| S.No. | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning & Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{(C_i - C_e) \cdot V}{m}$ | $Q$ = Mass-based exchange capacity (meq/g); $C_i, C_e$ = influent, effluent concentration (meq/L); $V$ = throughput volume (L); $m$ = mass of dry resin (g) |
| 2 | $q_{op} = \dfrac{C_0 \cdot V_{bt}}{V_{resin}}$ | $q_{op}$ = Operating capacity (meq/mL); $C_0$ = inlet concentration (meq/L); $V_{bt}$ = volume treated to breakthrough (L); $V_{resin}$ = bed volume (mL) |
| 3 | $K_{A/B} = \dfrac{[A]_R \cdot [B]_W}{[A]_W \cdot [B]_R}$ | Selectivity coefficient of ion A over ion B (dimensionless); subscript $R$ = resin phase, $W$ = water phase |
| 4 | $BV = \dfrac{V_{treated}}{V_{resin}}$ | Bed Volumes treated (dimensionless throughput metric) |
| 5 | $\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{\text{Capacity obtained}}{\text{Theoretical capacity}} \times 100\%$ | Regeneration efficiency (%) — typically **60 to 90 %** |
| 6 | $R_{regen} = \dfrac{m_{regen, used}}{m_{regen, theoretical}}$ | Regenerant dose ratio (typically **1.2 to 2.0** for SAC softening) |
| 7 | $t_{cycle} = \dfrac{q_{op} \cdot V_{resin}}{Q \cdot C_i}$ | Service cycle time (h); $Q$ = flow rate (L/h) |
| 8 | $\Delta P_{bed} = \dfrac{150 \mu L v}{d_p^2} \cdot \dfrac{(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3}$ | Pressure drop (Kozeny-Carman); $\mu$ = viscosity, $L$ = bed depth, $v$ = velocity, $d_p$ = particle dia, $\varepsilon$ = porosity |

**Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used by plant designers to (a) size the resin volume, (b) estimate the operating cost (regenerant chemical consumption), (c) predict when breakthrough will occur, and (d) schedule regeneration cycles. They form the **backbone of numerical problems** in KTU End Semester Examinations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Operating Exchange Capacity of a Fixed-Bed Column

Consider a cylindrical column of cross-sectional area $A$ (m²) and resin bed depth $L$ (m). Wastewater with inlet concentration $C_0$ (meq/L) and flow rate $Q$ (L/h) enters the top. The breakthrough curve is plotted as effluent concentration $C_e$ vs. time $t$.

**Step 1: Total equivalents of contaminant entering the column up to breakthrough time $t_{bt}$:**

$$E_{in} = C_0 \cdot Q \cdot t_{bt}$$

The units work out as: $\text{(meq/L)} \times \text{(L/h)} \times \text{(h)} = \text{meq}$ (milli-equivalents).

**Step 2: Total equivalents of contaminant exiting the column (un-adsorbed) up to breakthrough:**

$$E_{out} = \int_{0}^{t_{bt}} C_e(t) \cdot Q \cdot dt = Q \cdot \int_{0}^{t_{bt}} C_e(t)\, dt$$

This integral is the **area under the breakthrough curve** from $t = 0$ to $t = t_{bt}$.

**Step 3: Mass balance — equivalents retained on the resin = equivalents in − equivalents out:**

$$E_{ret} = C_0 \cdot Q \cdot t_{bt} - Q \cdot \int_{0}^{t_{bt}} C_e(t)\, dt$$

$$E_{ret} = Q \cdot \int_{0}^{t_{bt}} \left[ C_0 - C_e(t) \right] dt$$

**Step 4: Define the operating (working) exchange capacity as equivalents retained per unit volume of resin:**

$$q_{op} = \dfrac{E_{ret}}{V_{resin}} = \dfrac{Q}{A \cdot L} \cdot \int_{0}^{t_{bt}} \left[ C_0 - C_e(t) \right] dt$$

**Step 5: For a symmetric breakthrough curve (often a valid KTU assumption), the area under the curve $\int_0^{t_{bt}} C_e\,dt$ is approximately $\dfrac{1}{2} C_0 \cdot t_{bt}$. Substituting:**

$$q_{op} \approx \dfrac{Q \cdot C_0 \cdot t_{bt}}{2 \cdot A \cdot L} = \dfrac{C_0 \cdot V_{bt}}{2 \cdot V_{resin}}$$

Where $V_{bt} = Q \cdot t_{bt}$ is the treated volume at breakthrough.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The factor of **½** in the denominator reflects that the resin bed is utilized symmetrically — the bottom half of the bed is fully exhausted, while the top half is still pristine. This 50 % theoretical maximum is why real operating capacities are typically **60–80 %** of the total (stoichiometric) capacity.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Selectivity Coefficient & Its Engineering Meaning

The reaction for $Ca^{2+}$ exchanging with $Na^+$ on a cation resin is:

$$Ca^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2 Na^+_{(R)} \rightleftharpoons Ca^{2+}_{(R)} + 2 Na^+_{(aq)}$$

**Step 1: Write the mass-action (equilibrium) expression for the above reaction:**

$$K_{Ca/Na} = \dfrac{[Ca^{2+}]_R \cdot [Na^+]_W^{\,2}}{[Na^+]_R^{\,2} \cdot [Ca^{2+}]_W}$$

**Step 2: Apply the equivalent fraction convention.** Let $X_{Ca}$ = equivalent fraction of $Ca^{2+}$ on the resin; then $(1 - X_{Ca})$ = equivalent fraction of $Na^+$. Similarly, define $x_{Ca}$ in water. The expression becomes:

$$K_{Ca/Na} = \dfrac{X_{Ca}}{1 - X_{Ca}} \cdot \dfrac{(1 - x_{Ca})^2}{x_{Ca}} \cdot \dfrac{C_T}{Q_{resin}}$$

Where $C_T$ = total cation concentration in water (eq/L) and $Q_{resin}$ = total resin capacity (eq/L).

**Step 3: Solving for $X_{Ca}$ (the loading on the resin):**

$$X_{Ca} = \dfrac{K_{Ca/Na} \cdot x_{Ca}}{(K_{Ca/Na} - 1) x_{Ca} + C_T / Q_{resin}}$$

**Why this matters:** A higher $K_{Ca/Na}$ means the resin **strongly prefers $Ca^{2+}$** over $Na^+$. For SAC resins, $K_{Ca/Na} \approx 1.5$ to 3.0 in dilute solutions (but in concentrated brine during regeneration, the equilibrium reverses, allowing $Na^+$ to displace $Ca^{2+}$ — this is the **thermodynamic foundation of regeneration**).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Regenerant Dose & Operating Cost

**Step 1: Stoichiometric regenerant requirement:**

For a cation resin in the $Na^+$ cycle, the regeneration reaction is:

$$R_2 - Ca^{2+} + 2 NaCl \rightarrow 2 R - Na^+ + CaCl_2$$

The minimum (stoichiometric) moles of NaCl required to regenerate $n_{Ca}$ equivalents of $Ca^{2+}$:

$$n_{NaCl,\,min} = 2 \cdot n_{Ca} \quad \text{(mol)}$$

Or equivalently, **117 g NaCl per equivalent** of $Ca^{2+}$ (molar mass of NaCl = 58.5 g/mol, multiplied by 2).

**Step 2: Practical (excess) regenerant dose — incorporate regeneration ratio $R_{regen}$:**

$$m_{NaCl,\,practical} = R_{regen} \cdot n_{Ca} \cdot 117 \text{ g}$$

For typical softening, $R_{regen} = 1.5$ to 2.0, meaning **175 to 235 g NaCl per equivalent** is actually used.

**Step 3: Salt consumption per cubic meter of treated water:**

$$S_{specific} = \dfrac{m_{NaCl,\,practical}}{V_{treated}} = \dfrac{R_{regen} \cdot C_0 \cdot 117 \text{ g}}{q_{op} \times 1000} \quad \text{(g/L treated)}$$

**Step 4: Numerical example (KTU-style):** A water softener treats water with $C_0 = 5$ meq/L hardness, $q_{op} = 1.2$ eq/L resin, $R_{regen} = 1.6$. Calculate salt per litre:

$$S_{specific} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 117}{1.2} = \dfrac{0.936}{1.2} = 0.78 \text{ g/L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Check:** This is in the expected range (typical domestic softeners use 50–100 g salt per litre of resin per cycle). If a student gets a value 10× higher, the most common error is **forgetting to convert meq → eq** (the $10^{-3}$ factor) or **using the wrong valence** for $Ca^{2+}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Process Flow of an Ion Exchange Softening System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Wastewater Inlet] --> B[Pre-treatment Screen and Sand Filter]
    B --> C[Pressure Reducing and Flow Distribution]
    C --> D[Service Run - Ion Exchange Column Cation Resin Na Form]
    D --> E{Treated Water Meets Target Hardness}
    E -- Yes --> F[Softened Water to Storage or Reuse]
    E -- No --> G[Breakthrough Detected - Initiate Regeneration]
    G --> H[Backwash with Treated Water]
    H --> I[Brine Injection - Concentrated NaCl 10 percent]
    I --> J[Slow Rinse Displacement]
    J --> K[Fast Rinse with Treated Water]
    K --> L[Resin Restored to Na Form]
    L --> D
    I --> M[Waste Brine to Neutralization and Disposal]
    H --> N[Backwash Water to Settler]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Follow the rectangular blocks (processes) and the diamond (decision — has the treated water met the hardness target?). The loop from $D \to L \to D$ shows the **closed service-regeneration cycle**, while the side arrows to $M$ and $N$ show **waste streams** that must be managed.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Two-Stage Demineralization (Full Ion Exchange Train)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1 - Cation Exchange]
        A1[Influent] --> A2[SAC Resin in H Form]
        A2 --> A3[Decationized Water with Acidified pH]
    end
    A3 --> B1[CO2 Degassifier Removes Bicarbonate]
    B1 --> B2[Anion Exchange Column SBA in OH Form]
    B2 --> B3[Demineralized Water near Pure]
    A2 --> C1[Acid Regenerant HCl Dilute 4 to 6 percent]
    C1 --> A2
    B2 --> C2[Caustic Regenerant NaOH Dilute 4 percent]
    C2 --> B2
    A2 --> D1[Waste Acid to Neutralization]
    B2 --> D2[Waste Caustic to Neutralization]
```

**Why two stages?** A single column cannot remove both cations and anions because the resin must carry only one type of counter-ion. Passing water first through a **strong-acid cation (SAC) column in the $H^+$ form** replaces all cations with $H^+$ (producing the corresponding acids, e.g., $HCl$, $H_2SO_4$, $H_2CO_3$). The $H_2CO_3$ is then stripped in a **degassifier**, and the remaining acidified water passes through a **strong-base anion (SBA) column in the $OH^-$ form** that removes all anions, yielding nearly pure water.

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Breakthrough Curve Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start of Service Run t equals 0] --> B[Effluent Ce is approximately 0 meq/L]
    B --> C[Mass Transfer Zone MTZ moves down the bed]
    C --> D[Ce begins to rise - Curve inflection]
    D --> E[Breakpoint Ce equals Cb - typically 0.05 C0]
    E --> F[Column exhausted Ce approaches C0]
    F --> G[Regeneration initiated]
```

**Engineering significance:** The **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)** is the active band of resin where actual exchange is happening. The goal of good design is to keep the MTZ short (typically **0.3 to 1.0 m**) and to regenerate **before** the MTZ reaches the column exit. Plotting $C/C_0$ vs. throughput (BV) produces the classic **S-shaped breakthrough curve** — students must identify (i) the breakthrough concentration $C_b$, (ii) the exhaustion point, and (iii) the area above the curve (which equals the operating capacity).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper, Q1(a)]**

**Q1. (a) Define ion exchange process. List any two advantages of ion exchange over chemical precipitation for heavy metal removal.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. *Definition (2 marks):* Ion exchange is a reversible stoichiometric interchange of ions between an insoluble solid (resin) and a liquid (wastewater), in which the resin retains its physical structure. The reaction: $2R-Na^+ + Ca^{2+} \rightleftharpoons R_2-Ca^{2+} + 2Na^+$.
2. *Advantages over chemical precipitation (1 mark):* (i) Produces reusable water of very high purity (can achieve sub-ppb levels), (ii) does not generate large volumes of toxic sludge (only small volume of regenerant waste), and (iii) resin can be regenerated and reused for thousands of cycles.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper, Q2(b)]**

**Q1. (b) Differentiate between SAC and WAC resins in terms of functional group, operating pH range, and regenerant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | SAC (Strong Acid Cation) | WAC (Weak Acid Cation) |
|---|---|---|
| Functional group | Sulfonic acid ($-SO_3^-H^+$) | Carboxylic ($-COO^-H^+$) |
| Operating pH range | 0 to 14 (full range) | 5 to 14 (above pH 5) |
| Regenerant | HCl or $H_2SO_4$ (typically) | HCl (more efficient), sometimes $CO_2$ |
| Capacity | 1.8 to 2.0 meq/mL | 3.0 to 4.0 meq/mL (higher, but only in alkaline waters) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Choice-Based, Model Question]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q2 (a)** Describe the working principle of an ion exchange column used for water softening. With a neat sketch, explain the steps involved in a complete service-regeneration cycle. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The ion exchange softening process operates on the principle of **reversible stoichiometric exchange of cations** between a strong-acid cation (SAC) resin in the sodium form and the dissolved hardness ions ($Ca^{2+}$, $Mg^{2+}$) in water.

**Working principle (3 marks):**
The resin consists of cross-linked polystyrene beads with fixed $-SO_3^-$ groups carrying mobile $Na^+$ counter-ions. As hard water passes through the bed, the divalent hardness ions preferentially exchange with the monovalent $Na^+$ due to higher charge and selectivity. The reaction is:

$$2R-SO_3^-Na^+ + Ca^{2+} \rightarrow (R-SO_3^-)_2 Ca^{2+} + 2Na^+$$

The water leaving the column is therefore softened (calcium and magnesium replaced by sodium). The reaction continues until the resin is exhausted — at which point the effluent hardness rises sharply (the **breakthrough point**).

**Service-Regeneration cycle (4 marks):**

1. **Service run** — Downflow of hard water; hardness ions exchange with Na+; terminated at breakthrough.
2. **Backwash** — Upflow of water (8–15 m/h) to fluidize the bed, remove turbidity, and re-stratify resin.
3. **Brine (regenerant) introduction** — 8–12 % NaCl solution passed downflow; high $Na^+$ concentration reverses the equilibrium and elutes $Ca^{2+}$, $Mg^{2+}$ from the resin.
4. **Slow rinse** — Displaces spent brine from the bed pores.
5. **Fast rinse** — Final flush with raw water to remove residual salt; bed is ready for the next service run.

The waste brine (containing $CaCl_2$, $MgCl_2$, and excess NaCl) is sent to a **neutralization tank** before disposal.

**Sketch (marks allocated in 7-mark scheme):** Provide the labelled diagram of a typical ion exchange softener with the service and regeneration flow paths (refer to Section 4.1 mermaid diagram).

---

**Q2 (b)** A water softener uses a strong-acid cation resin with total capacity 2.0 eq/L. The bed volume is **500 L**. The feed water has a total hardness of **4.0 meq/L**. The regeneration uses 10 % NaCl at a regeneration ratio of 1.5. Calculate:

(i) The volume of water softened per cycle (to breakthrough, assuming 70 % utilization). (4 marks)
(ii) The mass of NaCl required per regeneration. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given data:**
- Total resin capacity $Q_{total} = 2.0$ eq/L
- Bed volume $V_{resin} = 500$ L
- Feed hardness $C_0 = 4.0$ meq/L $= 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ eq/L
- Regeneration ratio $R_{regen} = 1.5$
- Utilization efficiency $\eta = 70\% = 0.70$

**(i) Volume of water softened per cycle:**

The operating exchange capacity:

$$q_{op} = Q_{total} \times \eta = 2.0 \times 0.70 = 1.4 \text{ eq/L}$$

Total equivalents of hardness removable per cycle:

$$E_{cycle} = q_{op} \times V_{resin} = 1.4 \times 500 = 700 \text{ eq}$$

Volume treated:

$$V_{treated} = \dfrac{E_{cycle}}{C_0} = \dfrac{700}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 175{,}000 \text{ L} = 175 \text{ m}^3$$

**(ii) Mass of NaCl per regeneration:**

Equivalent mass of NaCl = 58.5 g/eq. For a regeneration ratio of 1.5:

$$m_{NaCl} = R_{regen} \times E_{cycle} \times M_{NaCl}$$

$$m_{NaCl} = 1.5 \times 700 \times 58.5 = 61{,}425 \text{ g} \approx 61.4 \text{ kg}$$

**Answers:** (i) $V_{treated} = 175 \text{ m}^3$ per cycle; (ii) $m_{NaCl} \approx 61.4 \text{ kg}$ per regeneration.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q2 (a)** Explain the concept of selectivity in ion exchange. Discuss the selectivity sequence of common cations on a strong-acid cation resin. How does this sequence affect regeneration efficiency? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Selectivity (2 marks):** Selectivity is the preferential uptake of one ion over another by the resin. It is quantified by the **selectivity coefficient** $K_{A/B}$, derived from mass-action equilibrium. A higher $K_{A/B}$ means the resin favours ion A.

**Selectivity Sequence for SAC Resin (3 marks):** For a typical strong-acid cation resin in dilute solutions, the selectivity sequence is:

$$Fe^{3+} > Al^{3+} > Ca^{2+} > Mg^{2+} > K^+ > Na^+ > H^+ > Li^+$$

Key observations:
- **Higher valence** ions are preferred (trivalent > divalent > monovalent).
- For same valence, **smaller hydrated radius** is preferred ($Ca^{2+}$ > $Mg^{2+}$ in many resins because $Mg^{2+}$ has a larger hydration shell).
- $H^+$ has unusually **low** selectivity on SAC (this is why SAC can be regenerated with strong acid — high $[H^+]$ drives the equilibrium to the $H^+$ form).

**Effect on Regeneration (2 marks):**
- During regeneration with NaCl, the **high concentration** of $Na^+$ (much greater than in service) reverses the equilibrium despite $Ca^{2+}$ having higher selectivity.
- However, because $Ca^{2+}$ is held more tightly, a **larger excess of regenerant** is required for higher-selectivity ions. This is why nitrate-selective resins (Type II SBA) need 1.5–2× more regenerant than standard chloride-form resins.
- Counter-current regeneration exploits the selectivity reversal: regenerant meets the most exhausted resin first, achieving **lower leakage** and **lower regenerant consumption** than co-current regeneration.

---

**Q2 (b)** A textile wastewater has $Cu^{2+} = 50$ mg/L. It is to be treated by ion exchange using a chelating resin with operating capacity **0.8 eq/L**. The bed volume is **200 L**. The plant operates **8 hours/day** and the flow rate is **5 m³/h**. Calculate:

(i) The breakthrough time in hours. (3 marks)
(ii) The volume of 5 % HCl regenerant (density 1.02 kg/L) required at 1.4× stoichiometric dose. (4 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given data:**
- $[Cu^{2+}] = 50$ mg/L
- Atomic mass of Cu = 63.5 g/mol; valence = 2; equivalent mass = 31.75 g/eq
- $C_0$ in eq/L: $50 / 31.75 = 1.575 \times 10^{-3}$ eq/L $= 1.575$ meq/L
- $q_{op} = 0.8$ eq/L
- $V_{resin} = 200$ L
- $Q = 5$ m³/h $= 5000$ L/h

**(i) Breakthrough time:**

Total equivalents on resin at breakthrough:

$$E_{bt} = q_{op} \times V_{resin} = 0.8 \times 200 = 160 \text{ eq}$$

Time to breakthrough:

$$t_{bt} = \dfrac{E_{bt}}{Q \times C_0} = \dfrac{160}{5000 \times 1.575 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{160}{7.875} \approx 20.3 \text{ h}$$

**(ii) Regenerant HCl volume:**

Stoichiometric HCl required (1 mol HCl per equivalent of $Cu^{2+}$):

$$m_{HCl, stoi} = E_{bt} \times 36.5 \text{ g} = 160 \times 36.5 = 5840 \text{ g}$$

At 1.4× stoichiometric:

$$m_{HCl, actual} = 1.4 \times 5840 = 8176 \text{ g} = 8.176 \text{ kg}$$

For 5 % HCl solution, mass of solution required:

$$m_{soln} = \dfrac{8.176}{0.05} = 163.5 \text{ kg}$$

Volume:

$$V_{HCl} = \dfrac{163.5}{1.02} \approx 160.3 \text{ L}$$

**Answers:** (i) $t_{bt} \approx 20.3$ h, (ii) $V_{HCl} \approx 160$ L per regeneration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors (loses 1–2 marks):** The most common mistake is mixing **meq/L** with **eq/L**. Always convert to a consistent set before substituting.
> 2. **Valence error in hardness:** When computing equivalent mass of $Ca^{2+}$, students often use 20 g/eq (correct) but then use 40 g/mol in the salt consumption step. For $CaCl_2$ salt, you need **2 moles of NaCl per mole of $Ca^{2+}$** (factor of 2 in the valence).
> 3. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark answers:** KTU 2024 scheme mandates a **neat labelled sketch** for full marks in descriptive questions. Even a hand-drawn-style ASCII flow chart fetches partial credit.
> 4. **Confusing "breakthrough" with "exhaustion":** Breakthrough is when $C_e$ reaches the **permissible limit** (e.g., 5 % of $C_0$); exhaustion is when the resin is **fully spent** ($C_e \approx C_0$). Operating capacity uses breakthrough, not exhaustion.
> 5. **Forgetting the regenerant waste disposal:** KTU expects a one-line note on **brine disposal / neutralization** for a complete answer. Marks are often lost on this environmental angle.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Page Before ESE**

- ✅ Ion exchange is a **physico-chemical tertiary treatment** — a separation, not destruction, process.
- ✅ Two resin types: **Cation exchangers** (fixed $-SO_3^-$, $-COO^-$) and **anion exchangers** (fixed $-N(CH_3)_3^+$).
- ✅ Selectivity hierarchy on SAC: $Fe^{3+} > Al^{3+} > Ca^{2+} > Mg^{2+} > K^+ > Na^+ > H^+ > Li^+$.
- ✅ A complete cycle = **Service → Backwash → Regeneration → Slow rinse → Fast rinse**.
- ✅ Operating capacity is typically **60–80 %** of total capacity (rest is unusable due to incomplete breakthrough).
- ✅ Regeneration uses **excess** of the original counter-ion (NaCl, HCl, NaOH) to drive mass-action equilibrium in reverse.
- ✅ Key operating metric: **Bed Volumes (BV) treated per cycle**; design target typically **200–500 BV** for softening.
- ✅ The breakthrough curve is **S-shaped**; the **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)** is the active exchange band.
- ✅ Two-stage demineralization (SAC-H$^+$ → Degassifier → SBA-OH$^-$) gives near-pure water.
- ✅ **Advantages:** high removal efficiency, regenerable, no sludge; **Disadvantages:** high operating cost (chemicals), brine waste, susceptible to fouling by oil/grease/organics.
- ✅ **Applications:** water softening, demineralization, heavy metal removal, nitrate removal, fluoride removal, ammonia removal (in zeolite columns), and nuclear wastewater treatment.
- ✅ **Common fouling agents:** iron, manganese, oil, humic acid, chlorine — all must be pre-removed to protect the resin.
- ✅ **Environmental note:** The spent regenerant (waste brine) is the **principal environmental liability** of ion exchange plants — it must be neutralized and heavy metals must be precipitated before discharge.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Membrane Separation Techniques: Brief Description of MF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Membrane Separation Techniques: Brief Description of MF ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Microfiltration (MF)** is a **pressure-driven membrane separation process** that uses a porous membrane with nominal pore sizes typically ranging from **0.1 µm to 10 µm** to physically separate suspended solids, colloidal particles, bacteria, and certain large macromolecules from a liquid stream. In the context of biological wastewater treatment, MF is positioned as a **low-pressure physical barrier process** (operating pressures usually between **0.1 bar to 2 bar**, i.e., 10–200 kPa) and is classified as the membrane process with the **largest pore size** in the hierarchy of membrane separations (MF → UF → NF → RO). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** MF occupies the first rung of the membrane separation ladder. It is **not** capable of removing dissolved salts or low-molecular-weight organic contaminants — its function is strictly to intercept **particulate and microbial** matter via a sieve/size-exclusion mechanism. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Strainer" Intuition Imagine pouring a mixture of sand, gravel, and water through a **kitchen colander** (the holes are about 2 mm). The water passes through; the gravel is trapped. Now replace the colander with a much finer mesh (say, 0.2 µm holes) and you have, conceptually, a **microfilter**. The water (permeate) flows through; the suspended particles larger than the pore are rejected (retentate/concentrate). > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy for wastewater:** MF behaves like a **giant, industrial-sized coffee filter** for secondary biological effluent — it lets clarified water through but holds back activated sludge flocs, bacteria, and protozoan cysts. ### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics at a Glance | Parameter | Typical MF Range | |---|---| | **Nominal Pore Size** | **0.1 – 10 µm** (commonly 0.1, 0.2, 0.45 µm) | | **Operating Pressure (TMP)** | **0.1 – 2 bar** (≈ 1 – 30 psi) | | **Membrane Flux (J)** | **500 – 5000 LMH** | | **Target Rejection Size** | Suspended solids, bacteria (≈ 0.5 – 5 µm) | | **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO)** | Not strictly defined (since MF is a sieve, not a solution-diffusion process); analogues reported as **> 500 kDa** | > [!NOTE] > **MWCO** is technically a UF term, but for MF it is sometimes loosely reported as "**> 500,000 Daltons**" to indicate that it retains only very large macromolecules and particles. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and the "How" MF works on the principle of **size exclusion (sieving)**, supplemented to a lesser degree by **adsorptive capture** of particles on the internal pore walls. The driving force is the **transmembrane pressure (TMP)**. - **Why a small TMP is sufficient:** The pores are large (compared to UF/NF/RO), so the hydraulic resistance offered by the membrane is low. Water finds it "easy" to flow. - **How separation occurs:** Particles whose diameter ≥ pore diameter cannot pass through the membrane channels and are physically retained on the feed side. - **How flux is sustained in practice:** A cross-flow (tangential) velocity is applied across the membrane surface to sweep away accumulated solids, mitigating the cake layer. In **dead-end mode**, the membrane is operated in batch/periodic backwash cycles. ### 2.2 The Five-Step Operating Logic of an MF Module 1. **Feed introduction:** Pressurized wastewater (typically secondary clarifier effluent or pre-screened raw sewage) is delivered to the membrane module at a controlled TMP. 2. **Selective permeation:** Water and dissolved species smaller than the membrane's largest pore (and the bulk solvent) pass through as **permeate**. 3. **Particle retention:** Suspended particles, flocs, bacteria, and colloids above the pore size are retained on the **retentate** side. 4. **Concentration polarization & cake formation:** Retained particles accumulate near the membrane, forming a dynamic cake that progressively adds resistance. 5. **Permeate collection & retentate recycle/blowdown:** Cleaned permeate is collected; the concentrated retentate is either recycled to the feed or discharged (in wastewater context, often recycled to the head of the biological stage). ### 2.3 Fouling Mechanisms (Critical for KTU) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fouling is THE most-examined topic on MF in KTU ESE questions.** Memorize the four mechanisms below. - **Pore plugging / pore blocking:** Particles lodge inside the membrane pores — the most permanent form of fouling. - **Cake layer formation:** Particles deposit on the surface forming a porous cake — partially reversible by backwash. - **Adsorption:** Macromolecules and EPS (extracellular polymeric substances from biomass) adsorb onto the membrane polymer — often irreversible. - **Concentration polarization:** A reversible build-up of solutes/particles near the membrane surface, increasing local osmotic/hydraulic resistance. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > In the table below, the absolute-value / magnitude bars are rendered as `\vert` so as not to break markdown table syntax. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Volumetric Flux (J)** | $J = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot t}$ | $L \cdot m^{-2} \cdot h^{-1}$ (LMH) | Volume of permeate per unit membrane area per unit time | | 2 | **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP)** | $\text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{\text{in}} + P_{\text{out}}}{2} - P_{\text{permeate}}$ | bar or kPa | Net driving pressure pushing liquid through the membrane | | 3 | **Darcy's Law (membrane form)** | $J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot R_{m}}$ | LMH | Flux is proportional to TMP and inversely proportional to viscosity × resistance | | 4 | **Membrane Resistance (Rₘ)** | $R_{m} = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot J_{w}}$ | $m^{-1}$ | Intrinsic resistance of the clean membrane (measured with pure water) | | 5 | **Observed Rejection (R_obs)** | $R_{\text{obs}} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{p}}{C_{f}}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of solute/particles rejected; $C_{p}$ = permeate, $C_{f}$ = feed | | 6 | **Mass Balance (Rejection form)** | $C_{p} = C_{f} \left(1 - R_{\text{obs}}\right)$ | mg/L | Permeate concentration in terms of feed and rejection | | 7 | **Volumetric Recovery (Y)** | $Y = \dfrac{V_{p}}{V_{f}}$ × 100 % | % | Volume of permeate recovered as a fraction of feed volume | | 8 | **Cake Resistance Contribution** | $J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \left(R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f}\right)}$ | LMH | Total resistance = membrane + cake + fouling (irreversible) | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In modern municipal and industrial wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs), MF is deployed as: - **Tertiary polishing** of secondary biological effluent (replacing/augmenting sand filters) to produce reuse-quality water. - **Pretreatment** to Nanofiltration (NF) or Reverse Osmosis (RO) in **Water Reuse / Desalination trains** — to protect the more expensive downstream membranes. - **Membrane Bioreactor (MBR)** — MF/UF membranes immersed directly in the aeration tank, replacing the secondary clarifier. - **Sludge thickening** — concentrating waste activated sludge with low energy input. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Membrane Flux from Darcy's Law We begin from the phenomenological **Darcy equation** for flow through a porous medium and adapt it for a membrane. **Step 1 — State Darcy's Law (general form):** $$ \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot R} $$ where $dV/dt$ is the volumetric flow rate, $A$ is the cross-sectional area, $\Delta P$ is the pressure drop, $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity, and $R$ is the hydraulic resistance of the medium. **Step 2 — Convert volumetric flow rate to flux.** By definition, the volumetric flux through the membrane is $J = \dfrac{1}{A}\dfrac{dV}{dt}$. Substituting: $$ J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R} $$ **Step 3 — Specialize to a membrane with $\Delta P$ = TMP and $R$ = $R_{m}$ (clean membrane resistance).** $$ J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot R_{m}} $$ **Step 4 — In the presence of a cake layer and irreversible fouling**, the total resistance becomes the **series sum**: $$ R_{\text{total}} = R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f} $$ **Step 5 — Final governing equation used in MF design and KTU problems:** $$ J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, (R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f})} $$ > **Conversion logic of Step 4:** The cake, fouling layer, and membrane are physically stacked in series; the same volumetric flux passes through each, and pressures add. Therefore resistances add. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Observed Rejection from a Mass Balance **Step 1 — Define rejection.** By definition, observed rejection is the fractional reduction in solute (or particle) concentration across the membrane: $$ R_{\text{obs}} = \dfrac{C_{f} - C_{p}}{C_{f}} $$ **Step 2 — Simplify algebraically.** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{\text{obs}} &= \dfrac{C_{f} - C_{p}}{C_{f}} \\ &= 1 - \dfrac{C_{p}}{C_{f}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for permeate concentration.** $$ C_{p} = C_{f}\left(1 - R_{\text{obs}}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Numerical sanity check (for KTU problem-solving practice):** If $C_{f} = 200 \,\text{mg/L}$ (TSS in secondary effluent) and $R_{\text{obs}} = 0.99$ (typical for a 0.2 µm MF), then: $$ C_{p} = 200 \times (1 - 0.99) = 200 \times 0.01 = 2 \,\text{mg/L} $$ This is consistent with reuse-quality effluent specifications (TSS $<$ 5 mg/L). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transmembrane Pressure from Inlet/Outlet Pressures In a cross-flow module, the feed enters at pressure $P_{\text{in}}$ and exits at $P_{\text{out}}$. The permeate side is at $P_{\text{perm}}$. **Step 1 — Average the feed-side pressure (this is the conventional definition in MF/UF):** $$ P_{\text{feed,avg}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{in}} + P_{\text{out}}}{2} $$ **Step 2 — Subtract permeate pressure to obtain the net driving force:** $$ \text{TMP} = P_{\text{feed,avg}} - P_{\text{perm}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{in}} + P_{\text{out}}}{2} - P_{\text{perm}} $$ **Step 3 — Example (typical MF immersed module in an MBR):** $P_{\text{in}} = 0.3$ bar, $P_{\text{out}} = 0.1$ bar, $P_{\text{perm}} \approx 0$ bar (suction-driven, permeate withdrawn by pump at slight vacuum). $$ \text{TMP} = \dfrac{0.3 + 0.1}{2} - 0 = 0.20 \,\text{bar} $$ > **Conversion logic:** The TMP equation assumes a **linear** pressure drop along the feed channel. For highly viscous flows or long modules, a logarithmic mean pressure correction can be used, but is **not** required at KTU 2024 level. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MF vs. Other Membrane Processes | Feature | **MF** | UF | NF | RO | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Pore size / Å** | 0.1 – 10 µm | 0.01 – 0.1 µm | ≈ 1 – 10 nm | non-porous (≈ 0.1 nm) | | **Driving Pressure (bar)** | 0.1 – 2 | 1 – 5 | 5 – 20 | 10 – 80 | | **Removes** | Bacteria, suspended solids | Macromolecules, viruses | Divalent salts, organics | Monovalent salts | | **Mechanism** | Sieving | Sieving | Sieving + solution-diffusion | Solution-diffusion | | **Typical Flux (LMH)** | 500 – 5000 | 50 – 500 | 10 – 100 | 5 – 50 | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 MF Process Architecture in a Wastewater Treatment Train ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Influent Wastewater] --> B[Preliminary Treatment
Screening and Grit Removal] B --> C[Primary Clarifier] C --> D[Biological Reactor
Activated Sludge] D --> E[MF Membrane Module
Cross flow Hollow Fiber] E --> F[MF Permeate
Reuse Quality Water] E --> G[Retentate Recycle
or Waste Sludge] F --> H[Optional NF or RO
for Higher Quality Reuse] E -.Backwash.-> I[Periodic Backwash
Air and Water Scour] I --> E ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The flow goes from raw influent on the left to reuse-quality water on the right. The **dashed arrow** indicates a periodic backwash (every 15–60 minutes in real plants) that dislodges the cake layer and partially restores flux. --- ### 4.2 MF Module — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FeedSide["FEED CHANNEL"] F1[Pressurized Feed
Secondary Effluent] F2[Cross flow Velocity
0.1 to 1 m per s] F3[Retentate Outlet
Concentrate] end subgraph MembraneCore["MEMBRANE LAYER"] M1[Porous Support
PVDF or PES] M2[Selective Skin
0.1 to 0.45 micron] M3[Rejection Zone
Size Exclusion Sieve] end subgraph PermeateSide["PERMEATE CHANNEL"] P1[Permeate Flow
Driven by TMP] P2[Permeate Collection
Laminar Outlet] end F1 --> M1 M1 --> M2 M2 --> M3 M3 --> P1 P1 --> P2 F2 -. Tangential Sweep .-> M2 F3 -. Recycle .-> F1 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The feed flows tangentially (perpendicular to the membrane surface in the diagram view); permeate is drawn through the membrane under the influence of TMP.
