The retentate exits for recycle or disposal. --- ### 4.3 Fouling Mechanism Map (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[MF Membrane in Operation] --> B{Operating Time Elapses} B --> C[Concentration Polarization
Reversible Build up] B --> D[Cake Layer Formation
Partially Reversible] B --> E[Pore Blocking
Largely Irreversible] B --> F[Adsorptive Fouling
Irreversible] C --> G[Hydraulic Backwash Removes] D --> H[Air Scour plus Backwash Removes] E --> I[Chemical Cleaning Required] F --> I G --> J[Flux Restored] H --> J I --> K[Clean in Place CIP] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** As operating time accumulates, **four fouling pathways** develop in parallel. Reversible forms are addressed by **hydraulic backwash**; irreversible forms require **chemical cleaning (CIP)** with reagents such as NaOCl, citric acid, or NaOH. --- ### 4.4 Operating Modes of MF — Functional Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR A[MF Operating Mode] --> B[Dead End Mode] A --> C[Cross Flow Mode] B --> B1[Feed Perpendicular to Membrane] B --> B2[Periodic Backwash Required] B --> B3[High Instantaneous Flux] C --> C1[Feed Tangential to Membrane] C --> C2[Continuous Scour of Cake] C --> C3[Lower Fouling Rate] C --> C4[Higher Energy Cost] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Pore-size confusion trap:** Students often write "MF removes dissolved salts" — this is **wrong**. Only RO (and partially NF) removes dissolved salts. MF is a **particulate** barrier. > 2. **Pressure trap:** Do not confuse the operating pressure of MF (0.1–2 bar) with that of RO (10–80 bar). Mixing these numbers is an instant **2-mark deduction**. > 3. **Rejection formula trap:** The observed rejection formula $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$ is **dimensionless** and must be reported as a fraction OR percentage. Students often forget to convert and lose a mark. > 4. **Fouling vs. concentration polarization:** These are **not** the same. CP is a reversible build-up of solutes near the surface; fouling is a more permanent (often adsorptive) change to the membrane. > 5. **MWCO trap:** Strictly, MWCO applies to UF/NF/RO. Stating that MF has an "MWCO of 500 Da" is a typical KTU board-exam error. --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** **Q1. Define microfiltration (MF) and list two of its distinguishing features compared to ultrafiltration (UF).** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** Microfiltration (MF) is a pressure-driven membrane separation process in which a porous membrane with a nominal pore size in the range **0.1 to 10 µm** is used to retain suspended solids, colloidal particles, and bacteria from a liquid stream, while allowing the solvent and dissolved species smaller than the pores to pass through. The two distinguishing features compared to UF are: 1. **Larger pore size:** MF (0.1 – 10 µm) has pores one to two orders of magnitude larger than UF (0.01 – 0.1 µm), and therefore cannot retain viruses or macromolecular solutes. 2. **Lower operating pressure:** MF operates at 0.1 – 2 bar, whereas UF typically requires 1 – 5 bar, because the hydraulic resistance of an MF membrane is much smaller. > **Valuation tip:** 1 mark for the correct definition with units; 1 mark each for the two distinguishing features. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Q2. State the Darcy-based membrane flux equation and explain the role of each term.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** The Darcy-based membrane flux equation is: $$ J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, R_{m}} $$ where: - $J$ = volumetric permeate flux (LMH), - TMP = transmembrane pressure, the net driving force pushing liquid through the membrane (bar), - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the permeate (Pa·s), and - $R_{m}$ = intrinsic hydraulic resistance of the clean membrane ($m^{-1}$). > **Valuation tip:** 1 mark for the formula; 1 mark for two correct terms; 1 mark for a brief physical interpretation (flux is proportional to TMP and inversely proportional to viscosity and resistance). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]** --- #### **Question A** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **cross-flow microfiltration (MF) system** used for tertiary treatment of municipal wastewater. Clearly mark the feed, permeate, retentate, and the membrane module in your diagram. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A municipal secondary effluent with an average **total suspended solids (TSS) of 180 mg/L** is treated by a 0.2 µm MF membrane. Laboratory tests report an **observed TSS rejection of 99.2 %**. Determine the **permeate TSS concentration** and comment on whether the permeate meets the typical reuse standard of TSS $<$ 5 mg/L. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution to (a):** **Working of a Cross-Flow MF System for Tertiary Treatment:** 1. **Feed supply:** Secondary clarifier effluent (post-biological treatment) is collected in a feed tank and pumped through a low-pressure feed pump (typically a centrifugal or progressive cavity pump) into the MF module. 2. **Pressurization:** The feed pump provides the inlet pressure $P_{\text{in}}$ (typically 0.2 – 0.5 bar) required to overcome the membrane resistance and the cross-flow friction loss. 3. **Cross-flow across the membrane:** The feed flows **tangentially** along the membrane surface (hollow fiber, tubular, or flat sheet) with a cross-flow velocity of 0.1 – 1 m/s. This tangential flow continuously sweeps deposited particles off the membrane surface, controlling the cake layer. 4. **Permeate withdrawal:** Permeate is drawn through the membrane by a suction pump or by the pressure differential, collected in a permeate tank, and sent for disinfection and reuse. 5. **Retentate handling:** The concentrated retentate exits the module at $P_{\text{out}}$, is split — a fraction is recycled to the feed tank, and a fraction is bled (wasted) to control the solids concentration in the loop. 6. **Backwash and CIP:** Periodically (every 15 – 60 minutes), the feed is stopped and a reverse flow of permeate (often with air scour) is applied from the permeate side to dislodge the cake layer. Chemical cleaning-in-place (CIP) with NaOCl, NaOH, or citric acid is performed when flux declines below a threshold. > **Valuation key (7 marks):** 1 mark each for steps 1–6, with 1 bonus mark for a correctly labelled block diagram. **Block Diagram (textual representation as expected in answer scripts):** ``` [Feed Tank] --> [Feed Pump] --> | Membrane Module | --> [Permeate Tank] | (Hollow fiber / | | Tubular / Plate) | | | [Retentate Recycle] <-- (P_in) ---- + ------ (P_out) ---> [Waste/Bleed] [Air Scour + Permeate Backwash] <-----> | Module | ``` --- **Model Solution to (b):** **Given:** - Feed TSS: $C_{f} = 180 \,\text{mg/L}$ - Observed rejection: $R_{\text{obs}} = 99.2 \% = 0.992$ - Standard: TSS $<$ 5 mg/L **Step 1 — Apply the rejection formula.** $$ C_{p} = C_{f} \left(1 - R_{\text{obs}}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.** $$ C_{p} = 180 \times (1 - 0.992) = 180 \times 0.008 $$ **Step 3 — Compute.** $$ C_{p} = 1.44 \,\text{mg/L} $$ **Step 4 — Compare with the reuse standard.** $$ C_{p} = 1.44 \,\text{mg/L} \;\; < \;\; 5 \,\text{mg/L} \;\;\checkmark $$ **Conclusion:** The permeate **comfortably meets** the reuse standard for TSS. The 0.2 µm MF membrane, with 99.2 % observed rejection, is **suitable** for producing reuse-quality water from this secondary effluent. > **Valuation key (7 marks):** 2 marks for writing the formula, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for the numerical answer (1.44 mg/L), 1 mark for the comparison, 1 mark for the final conclusion. --- #### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Explain the **four major fouling mechanisms** encountered in microfiltration, classifying each as **reversible** or **irreversible**, and stating the typical mitigation method for each. *(7 marks)* **(b)** An immersed hollow-fiber MF module has $P_{\text{in}} = 0.30$ bar, $P_{\text{out}} = 0.10$ bar, and operates with the permeate under slight vacuum at $P_{\text{perm}} = -0.05$ bar (gauge). Calculate the **transmembrane pressure (TMP)** in bar and in kPa. If the pure-water flux measured on the same membrane is $J_{w} = 1200 \,\text{LMH}$ at this TMP, compute the **clean-membrane resistance $R_{m}$** at $20°\text{C}$, where $\mu_{20°C} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution to (a):** | # | Mechanism | Reversible / Irreversible | Mitigation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Pore plugging** (particles lodged inside pores) | Largely **irreversible** | Chemical cleaning (CIP) with surfactant or oxidant; cannot be removed by backwash alone | | 2 | **Cake layer formation** (deposited solids on surface) | **Partially reversible** | Hydraulic backwash + air scour | | 3 | **Adsorptive fouling** (macromolecules/EPS adsorbed on polymer) | **Irreversible** | Chemical cleaning; surface modification of membrane (hydrophilization) | | 4 | **Concentration polarization** (reversible solute build-up) | **Reversible** | Increasing cross-flow velocity; reducing flux; turbulence promoters | > **Valuation key (7 marks):** 1.5 marks per mechanism (0.5 for description, 0.5 for R/IR classification, 0.5 for mitigation). 1 mark for the concluding comment that **backwash handles reversible fouling, CIP handles irreversible fouling**. --- **Model Solution to (b):** **Step 1 — Compute TMP using the cross-flow equation.** $$ \text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{\text{in}} + P_{\text{out}}}{2} - P_{\text{perm}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the gauge-pressure values.** $$ \text{TMP} = \dfrac{0.30 + 0.10}{2} - (-0.05) = \dfrac{0.40}{2} + 0.05 = 0.20 + 0.05 = 0.25 \,\text{bar} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to kPa.** $$ \text{TMP} = 0.25 \,\text{bar} \times 100 \,\text{kPa/bar} = 25 \,\text{kPa} $$ **Step 4 — Compute $R_{m}$ from Darcy's law rearranged.** $$ R_{m} = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot J_{w}} $$ **Step 5 — Convert units carefully.** - TMP = $25 \,\text{kPa} = 25{,}000 \,\text{Pa}$ - $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ - $J_{w} = 1200 \,\text{LMH} = 1200 \times \dfrac{10^{-3} \,\text{m}^3}{1 \,\text{m}^2 \times 3600 \,\text{s}} = \dfrac{1200}{3600} \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m/s} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{m/s}$ **Step 6 — Substitute and compute.** $$ R_{m} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{1.002 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.333 \times 10^{-4}} $$ Denominator: $$ 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.333 \times 10^{-4} = 3.340 \times 10^{-7} \,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s/m} $$ Therefore: $$ R_{m} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{3.340 \times 10^{-7}} = 7.485 \times 10^{10} \,\text{m}^{-1} $$ > **Final Answer:** TMP = **0.25 bar (25 kPa)**; $R_{m} \approx$ **$7.49 \times 10^{10} \, \text{m}^{-1}$**. > **Valuation key (7 marks):** 2 marks for TMP, 2 marks for unit conversion of $J_w$ to m/s, 2 marks for substitution and arithmetic, 1 mark for the final numerical result. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall):** - **Definition:** MF is a **pressure-driven**, **size-exclusion** membrane process with pores of **0.1 – 10 µm** and operating pressure **0.1 – 2 bar**. - **Removes:** Suspended solids, bacteria, large colloids. **Does NOT remove:** Dissolved salts, viruses (fully), or low-MW organics. - **Position in the membrane hierarchy:** MF $\rightarrow$ UF $\rightarrow$ NF $\rightarrow$ RO (pore size decreases, pressure increases). - **Driving force:** TMP (transmembrane pressure) — the average feed-side pressure minus the permeate pressure. - **Three must-know formulas:** 1. $J = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot t}$ (flux) 2. $R = 1 - C_p / C_f$ (rejection) 3. $\text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{\text{in}} + P_{\text{out}}}{2} - P_{\text{perm}}$ (transmembrane pressure) - **Darcy's law for a fouled membrane:** $J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu(R_m + R_c + R_f)}$ — resistances add in series. - **Fouling mechanisms (4):** Pore plugging, cake layer, adsorption, concentration polarization — distinguish reversible vs. irreversible. - **Mitigation:** **Backwash + air scour** for reversible; **CIP (chemical cleaning-in-place)** for irreversible. - **Operating modes:** **Dead-end** (high instantaneous flux, periodic backwash) vs. **Cross-flow** (continuous tangential scour, lower fouling, higher energy). - **Module configurations:** Hollow fiber, tubular, plate-and-frame, spiral wound. - **Common membrane materials:** Polymeric — PVDF, PES, PS, PP, PTFE, CA. Ceramic membranes are used for harsh chemical/thermal duties. - **Volumetric recovery (Y):** $Y = V_p / V_f \times 100$ % — relevant for system sizing. - **MWCO of MF:** Strictly not defined; if asked, say **"not applicable — MF is a sieve, not a solution-diffusion process"**; if a number is needed, **> 500 kDa** is conventionally used. - **MBR context:** Immersed hollow-fiber MF/UF replaces the secondary clarifier in a **Membrane Bioreactor** — high MLSS, small footprint. - **Permeate TSS calculation pattern:** $C_p = C_f(1 - R)$ — almost always appears as a 7-mark problem in KTU ESE. ---

topics.title: NF Membranes. Reverse Osmosis Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** NF Membranes. Reverse Osmosis Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Nanofiltration (NF) Membranes — Formal Definition

**Nanofiltration (NF)** is a **pressure-driven membrane separation process** that operates in the transitional regime between Ultrafiltration (UF) and Reverse Osmosis (RO). NF membranes are characterized by a pore size in the range of **0.5 – 2 nm** and a Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO) of approximately **200 – 1000 Daltons**. The applied transmembrane pressure is typically **5 – 20 bar (0.5 – 2 MPa)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> NF membranes reject multivalent ions (e.g., $\text{Ca}^{2+}$, $\text{Mg}^{2+}$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$) and low-molecular-weight organic compounds while allowing partial passage of monovalent ions (e.g., $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$). This selective rejection is governed by a combination of **size exclusion (sieve effect)** and **Donnan (electrostatic) exclusion**.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Fine Sieve with a Magnetic Bias"

Imagine a kitchen sieve so fine that only flour-sized particles slip through. Now, attach a weak magnetic charge to the mesh so that metallic, double-charged particles (multivalent ions) are repelled, while single-charged salts slip past more easily. **That is exactly how an NF membrane behaves** — it acts like a nano-scale strainer that screens molecules by both size and electrical charge.

---

## 1.2 Reverse Osmosis (RO) — Formal Definition

**Reverse Osmosis (RO)** is a **pressure-driven membrane process** in which a solvent (typically water) is forced through a *dense, effectively non-porous* semipermeable membrane against its osmotic pressure gradient. The pore size is below **0.5 nm**, and the MWCO is less than **200 Daltons**. Operating pressures range from **10 – 100 bar (1 – 10 MPa)**, depending on feed salinity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Examiner Standard)**
> "Reverse Osmosis is the process in which a hydraulic pressure greater than the osmotic pressure of the feed solution is applied across a semipermeable membrane, causing the solvent to flow from a region of higher solute concentration to a region of lower solute concentration — i.e., in the *reverse* direction of natural osmosis."

### Intuitive Analogy — "Squeezing Water Through a Wall"

Picture two compartments separated by a thin plastic sheet that lets water slip through but blocks salt. Naturally, water flows *toward* the salty side (osmosis). Now, if you press the salty water with a pump harder than its osmotic pressure, you reverse the flow — pure water squeezes out the other side, leaving salt behind. **This is reverse osmosis** — used widely in seawater desalination and industrial water reuse.

### Key Physical Constants & Performance Metrics

| Metric | Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Universal gas constant $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard temperature $T$ | **298.15 K (25 °C)** |
| Pure water density $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Permeate flux (RO typical) | **10 – 30 L/(m²·h)** |
| Salt rejection (RO) | **> 99 %** for seawater |
| Salt rejection (NF) | **20 – 80 %** for NaCl; **> 95 %** for divalent ions |

> [!TIP]
> Always quote operating pressure in **bar** and temperature in **°C** in KTU numerical problems unless the question specifies otherwise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of Reverse Osmosis

The driving force for RO is the **net effective pressure** — the difference between applied hydraulic pressure and osmotic pressure opposing the flow.

### Step-by-Step Logic of RO Operation

1. **Feed Introduction** — Saline water enters the membrane module under high pressure.
2. **Pressure Application** — A high-pressure pump generates pressure exceeding the osmotic pressure of the feed.
3. **Selective Transport** — Water molecules preferentially diffuse through the dense membrane (solution-diffusion mechanism); salts are rejected.
4. **Permeate Collection** — Purified water (permeate) is collected at low pressure.
5. **Concentrate Discharge** — The rejected salts leave as brine/concentrate.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why high pressure?** Because the membrane is so dense that natural osmotic flow would draw water *into* the salt solution. We must overcome this osmotic pressure.
- **Why are salts rejected?** Ions are larger and more hydrated than water; they interact with the membrane polymer matrix, drastically lowering their solubility and diffusivity.
- **Why is permeate of high purity?** The dense membrane acts as a *kinetic* barrier where diffusion rates differ by orders of magnitude.

---

## 2.2 Operating Principle of Nanofiltration

NF combines two rejection mechanisms:

- **Steric Hindrance (Sieve Effect):** Particles larger than the membrane pore are physically excluded.
- **Donnan Exclusion:** The NF membrane typically carries a slight negative surface charge, repelling anions and multivalent cations. This gives NF its characteristic **higher rejection of multivalent ions** compared to monovalent ions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference (RO vs NF)**
> - **RO** = Dense, non-porous → Solution-Diffusion dominates → Rejects virtually everything.
> - **NF** = Nano-porous → Sieve + Charge effects → Selectively rejects multivalent species and organics, allows partial monovalent salt passage.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **exam-ready formulas** for this topic. All symbols are consistent with KTU 2024 Scheme notation.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Osmotic Pressure (van't Hoff) | $\pi = iCRT$ | $i$ = van't Hoff factor (—); $C$ = molar concentration (mol/m³); $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $T$ = absolute temperature (K) |
| 2 | Modified Osmotic Pressure | $\pi = \sum c_i RT$ | $c_i$ = molar concentration of species $i$ (mol/m³) |
| 3 | Permeate Flux (Solution-Diffusion) | $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | $A$ = water permeability (m/s·Pa); $\Delta P$ = transmembrane pressure (Pa); $\Delta \pi$ = osmotic pressure difference (Pa) |
| 4 | Salt Flux | $J_s = B(C_m - C_p)$ | $B$ = salt permeability (m/s); $C_m$ = concentration at membrane surface (mol/m³); $C_p$ = permeate concentration (mol/m³) |
| 5 | Observed Salt Rejection | $R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | $C_f$ = feed concentration (mol/m³ or mg/L) |
| 6 | True Salt Rejection | $R_{true} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_m}$ | $C_m$ = wall concentration at membrane surface |
| 7 | Concentration Polarization | $C_m = C_p + (C_f - C_p)\exp(J_w/k)$ | $k$ = mass transfer coefficient (m/s) |
| 8 | Recovery (Yield) | $Y = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f}$ | $Q_p$ = permeate flow (m³/d); $Q_f$ = feed flow (m³/d) |
| 9 | Mass Balance (Volume) | $Q_f = Q_p + Q_r$ | $Q_r$ = reject/concentrate flow (m³/d) |
| 10 | Mass Balance (Solute) | $Q_f C_f = Q_p C_p + Q_r C_r$ | $C_r$ = concentrate concentration (mg/L) |
| 11 | Osmotic Pressure of NaCl Solution | $\pi \approx 0.75 \times C_{TDS}$ (at 25 °C) | $C_{TDS}$ in mg/L → $\pi$ in kPa (approx. conversion) |

### Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Seawater Desalination (RO):** Use the flux equation to size the pump and membrane area.
- **Industrial Water Reuse (NF):** Use Donnan exclusion to remove hardness without complete demineralization.
- **Wastewater Polishing:** RO/NF as a tertiary step achieves reuse-quality effluent.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Osmotic Pressure (van't Hoff Equation)

We start from the thermodynamic equilibrium condition of a semipermeable membrane. At equilibrium, the chemical potential of the solvent on both sides must be equal.

**Step 1: Write the chemical potential equality.**

$$
\mu_w^{(pure)} = \mu_w^{(solution)}
$$

**Step 2: For an ideal solution, expand the chemical potential of the solvent in the solution:**

$$
\mu_w^{(solution)} = \mu_w^{\circ} + RT \ln x_w
$$

where $x_w$ is the mole fraction of the solvent. Substituting:

$$
\mu_w^{\circ} + RT \ln x_w = \mu_w^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3: Simplify and apply the definition of osmotic pressure work.**

The osmotic pressure $\pi$ is defined as the excess pressure required on the solution side to maintain equilibrium:

$$
\pi V_w = -RT \ln x_w
$$

**Step 4: Use the approximation $\ln x_w \approx -(1 - x_w)$ for dilute solutions.**

Since $x_s$ (mole fraction of solute) is small and $1 - x_w = x_s$:

$$
\pi V_w = -RT \ln x_w \approx RT \cdot x_s
$$

**Step 5: Express in terms of molar concentration.**

For dilute aqueous solutions, $n_s / V_w \approx C$ (molar concentration). Thus:

$$
\pi = \dfrac{n_s}{V_w} RT = CRT
$$

**Step 6: Generalize for non-ideal or dissociated solutes.**

For salts that dissociate (e.g., NaCl → $\text{Na}^+ + \text{Cl}^-$, $i = 2$):

$$
\boxed{\pi = iCRT}
$$

This is the **van't Hoff equation** used universally in RO system design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Permeate Flux (Solution-Diffusion Model)

The **Solution-Diffusion model** treats the dense RO membrane as a region where water dissolves and then diffuses through the polymer matrix.

**Step 1: Define the water flux across the membrane.**

The flux of water per unit membrane area:

$$
J_w = -D_w \dfrac{dC_w}{dx}
$$

where $D_w$ is the diffusion coefficient of water in the membrane and $C_w$ is water concentration in the membrane.

**Step 2: Assume Fickian diffusion under a chemical potential gradient.**

For dilute systems, the chemical potential gradient is converted to a pressure gradient. Integrate across membrane thickness $\delta$:

$$
J_w = \dfrac{D_w K_w}{\delta}(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)
$$

**Step 3: Define the water permeability constant $A$.**

Combining $D_w$, $K_w$ (solubility), and $\delta$ into a single membrane parameter:

$$
A = \dfrac{D_w K_w}{\delta}
$$

**Step 4: Write the final permeate flux equation.**

$$
\boxed{J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is the **backbone of all RO design calculations** in KTU problems. When $\Delta P = \Delta \pi$, the flux is zero — this is the **stagnation condition**. When $\Delta P < \Delta \pi$, reverse flow occurs (natural osmosis).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Salt Rejection Equation

**Step 1: Write the salt flux equation.**

Salt transport through the membrane is governed by:

$$
J_s = B(C_m - C_p)
$$

**Step 2: Define the observed rejection as the ratio of salt concentration removed to that in the feed.**

The permeate salt concentration relative to feed gives:

$$
R_{obs} = \dfrac{C_f - C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute and simplify.**

For a well-designed system with low concentration polarization ($C_m \approx C_f$):

$$
\boxed{R = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}}
$$

**Step 4: Relate to flux for design.**

Since $C_p = J_s / J_w$, and using both flux equations:

$$
C_p = \dfrac{B(C_m - C_p)}{A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)}
$$

Solving for $C_p$ and substituting back yields the more complete design equation:

$$
R = \dfrac{A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)}{A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi) + B} \cdot \dfrac{C_m}{C_f}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mass Balance for a Single-Stage RO System

Given feed flow $Q_f$, permeate flow $Q_p$, recovery $Y$, and feed concentration $C_f$, derive concentrate concentration $C_r$ and permeate concentration $C_p$.

**Step 1: Volume balance.**

$$
Q_f = Q_p + Q_r
$$

Dividing throughout by $Q_f$:

$$
1 = Y + \dfrac{Q_r}{Q_f}
$$

**Step 2: Recovery definition.**

$$
Y = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_p = Y Q_f
$$

**Step 3: Solute (mass) balance.**

$$
Q_f C_f = Q_p C_p + Q_r C_r
$$

**Step 4: Substitute $Q_p$ and $Q_r$ in terms of $Q_f$ and $Y$.**

$$
Q_f C_f = Y Q_f C_p + (1 - Y) Q_f C_r
$$

Dividing by $Q_f$:

$$
C_f = Y C_p + (1 - Y) C_r
$$

**Step 5: Solve for $C_r$.**

$$
\boxed{C_r = \dfrac{C_f - Y C_p}{1 - Y}}
$$

This is a standard result used in KTU 2024 numerical problems.

---

## 3.5 Concentration Polarization Model

When solutes accumulate near the membrane surface, $C_m > C_f$. Using a film-theory approach:

$$
\dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_f - C_p} = \exp\left(\dfrac{J_w}{k}\right)
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. Steady-state solute mass balance on the boundary layer gives exponential growth toward the membrane.
2. As $J_w$ increases, more solute is convected toward the membrane, raising $C_m$.
3. The mass transfer coefficient $k$ is determined empirically (often from Sherwood correlations).

> [!WARNING]
> High concentration polarization reduces **observed rejection** and increases **fouling**. Always include a safety factor in KTU design problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RO/NF System Architecture (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Feed Water Reservoir]
    B[Pre Treatment Strainers and Cartridge Filters]
    C[Chemical Dosing Antiscalant and Acid]
    D[High Pressure Multistage Pump]
    E[NF or RO Membrane Module Pressure Vessel]
    F[Permeate Stream Purified Water]
    G[Concentrate or Brine Stream Reject]
    H[Energy Recovery Device Turbine]
    I[Diversion Valve to Drain]
    J[Permeate Storage Tank]
    K[Post Treatment UV and Chlorination]
    L[Distribution Network for Reuse]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    E --> G
    G --> H
    H --> D
    G --> I
    F --> J
    J --> K
    K --> L
```

### Process Description (Block-Level Mapping)

- **A → B:** Raw water enters the pre-treatment skid to remove suspended solids that may foul the membrane.
- **B → C → D:** Antiscalant dosing prevents mineral scaling; high-pressure pump pressurizes the feed to **10–80 bar** for RO or **5–20 bar** for NF.
- **D → E:** Pressurized feed enters the membrane vessel where **selective separation** occurs.
- **E → F:** Permeate (low TDS) is collected.
- **E → G:** Concentrate exits at high pressure, partially recovered via **Energy Recovery Device (ERD)** in large systems.
- **F → J → K → L:** Permeate is disinfected and sent to distribution.

---

## 4.2 Membrane Cross-Section & Transport Mechanism (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Feed Side High Pressure]
    P1[Polyamide Dense Skin Layer 0 to 0.2 micrometer]
    PS1[Polysulfone Support Layer 40 to 50 micrometer]
    NP1[Non woven Fabric Backing 100 to 200 micrometer]
    PR1[Permeate Side Low Pressure]

    F1 --> P1
    P1 --> PS1
    PS1 --> NP1
    NP1 --> PR1
```

### Layer-wise Function

| Layer | Thickness | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Polyamide Dense Skin | **< 0.2 μm** | Selectivity (rejection of ions) |
| Polysulfone Support | **40 – 50 μm** | Mechanical strength |
| Non-woven Backing | **100 – 200 μm** | Structural support for the thin film |

---

## 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — MF / UF / NF / RO

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MF[Memb MF Pore 0.1 to 10 micrometer Pressure 0.1 to 2 bar]
    UF[Memb UF Pore 0.01 to 0.1 micrometer Pressure 1 to 10 bar]
    NF[Memb NF Pore 0.5 to 2 nm Pressure 5 to 20 bar]
    RO[Memb RO Pore less than 0.5 nm Pressure 10 to 100 bar]
    SUB[Particle Size Spectrum Bacteria Colloids Viruses Divalent Ions Monovalent Ions]
    MF --> SUB
    UF --> SUB
    NF --> SUB
    RO --> SUB
```

> [!TIP]
> Notice how membrane "tightness" increases from MF → RO, while operating pressure and energy consumption also increase. NF occupies the sweet spot for **selective hardness removal** with **lower energy cost than RO**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Differentiate between Nanofiltration (NF) and Reverse Osmosis (RO) membranes in terms of pore size, operating pressure, and salt rejection.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Nanofiltration (NF) | Reverse Osmosis (RO) |
|---|---|---|
| Pore size | **0.5 – 2 nm** | **< 0.5 nm** (effectively non-porous) |
| Operating pressure | **5 – 20 bar** | **10 – 100 bar** |
| Rejection mechanism | Sieve + Donnan | Solution-Diffusion |
| Monovalent salt rejection | Low (20 – 80 %) | Very high (> 99 %) |
| Divalent salt rejection | High (> 95 %) | Very high (> 99 %) |
| Typical application | Water softening, color removal | Desalination, reuse |

NF is preferred for **partial desalination** (softening) due to lower energy demand.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**State the principle of reverse osmosis. What is the minimum pressure required to start the process?**

**Model Answer:**

Reverse Osmosis is a membrane separation process in which a hydraulic pressure greater than the **osmotic pressure of the feed solution** is applied across a semipermeable membrane, causing the solvent to flow from the solution of higher concentration to the solution of lower concentration — opposite to the natural direction of osmosis.

The **minimum applied pressure** must equal the osmotic pressure of the feed. For seawater (~35 000 mg/L TDS), this is approximately:

$$
\pi \approx 0.75 \times 35{,}000 \; \text{kPa} \times 10^{-3} \approx 25 \; \text{bar}
$$

Hence RO systems for seawater operate at **55 – 80 bar**, with the excess over $\pi$ providing the net driving force.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the principle and working of reverse osmosis with a neat sketch. Discuss the construction of a typical RO membrane. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A feed solution containing **2 000 mg/L of NaCl** is processed through an RO membrane at **25 °C**. The applied pressure is **15 bar** and the water permeability constant is $A = 4 \times 10^{-12}$ m/s·Pa. Assuming $i = 2$ for NaCl, calculate: **(i)** the osmotic pressure of the feed, **(ii)** the permeate flux. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Principle:** When a semipermeable membrane separates a dilute solution from a concentrated one, water flows naturally from the dilute to the concentrated side (osmosis). If a pressure **greater than the osmotic pressure** is applied on the concentrated side, the flow direction is **reversed** — pure water moves from the concentrated to the dilute side. This is **Reverse Osmosis**.

**Construction of RO Membrane (Thin-Film Composite):**
1. **Top Polyamide Layer (0.1 – 0.2 μm):** Provides selectivity. Rejects ions by size and polarity.
2. **Middle Polysulfone Support (40 – 50 μm):** Porous intermediate; supports the thin film.
3. **Bottom Non-woven Backing (100 – 200 μm):** Polyester fabric providing mechanical strength.

**Working Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the layered structure.)

**Module Configurations:** Spiral-wound is the most common for wastewater reuse. Hollow-fiber and plate-and-frame are used in specialized applications.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $C_f = 2{,}000$ mg/L NaCl
- $T = 25 \;°\text{C} = 298 \; \text{K}$
- $P = 15 \; \text{bar} = 15 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa}$
- $A = 4 \times 10^{-12}$ m/s·Pa
- $i = 2$ for NaCl

**Step 1: Convert concentration to mol/m³.**

Molar mass of NaCl $M = 58.5$ g/mol.

$$
C = \dfrac{2{,}000 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{kg/m}^3}{58.5 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{kg/mol}} = 34.19 \;\text{mol/m}^3
$$

**Step 2: Calculate osmotic pressure (i).**

$$
\pi = iCRT = 2 \times 34.19 \times 8.314 \times 298
$$

$$
\pi = 169{,}374 \;\text{Pa} \approx 1.69 \;\text{bar}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate net driving pressure (NDP).**

$$
\Delta P - \Delta \pi = 15 - 1.69 = 13.31 \;\text{bar} = 13.31 \times 10^{5} \;\text{Pa}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate permeate flux (ii).**

$$
J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi) = 4 \times 10^{-12} \times 13.31 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
J_w = 5.32 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to LMH (L/m²·h) for practical reporting.**

$$
J_w = 5.32 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 \times 3600 = 19.16 \;\text{L/(m}^2 \cdot \text{h)}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Osmotic pressure $\pi \approx 1.69 \;\text{bar}$
- Permeate flux $J_w \approx 19.2 \;\text{LMH}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the nanofiltration process, its mechanism, and applications in wastewater treatment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An RO plant treats **500 m³/day** of brackish water with **3 000 mg/L TDS** and produces permeate with **150 mg/L TDS** at a **recovery of 70 %**. Calculate: **(i)** permeate and concentrate flow rates, **(ii)** concentrate TDS, **(iii)** observed salt rejection. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Nanofiltration (NF):** A pressure-driven membrane process with MWCO 200 – 1000 Da and pore size 0.5 – 2 nm, operating at 5 – 20 bar.

**Mechanisms:**
1. **Steric Hindrance (Sieve Effect):** Physical exclusion based on molecular size.
2. **Donnan (Electrostatic) Exclusion:** Membrane surface charge repels ions, especially multivalent ones.
3. **Solution-Diffusion Contribution:** Partial diffusion-based transport in the densest regions.

**Advantages over RO:**
- Lower operating pressure → **lower energy cost**.
- Selective rejection of multivalent ions → **partial desalination**.
- Higher flux at equivalent pressure.

**Applications in Wastewater Treatment:**
- **Water softening** (removal of $\text{Ca}^{2+}$, $\text{Mg}^{2+}$, hardness).
- **Decolorization** of textile/dye effluents.
- **Removal of pesticides, herbicides**, and disinfection by-product precursors.
- **Pretreatment to RO** to reduce scaling and fouling.
- **Industrial wastewater reuse** in textile, dairy, and pharmaceutical industries.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $Q_f = 500 \;\text{m}^3/\text{day}$
- $C_f = 3{,}000 \;\text{mg/L}$
- $C_p = 150 \;\text{mg/L}$
- Recovery $Y = 70 \;\% = 0.70$

**Step 1: Calculate permeate flow rate (i, part 1).**

$$
Q_p = Y \times Q_f = 0.70 \times 500 = 350 \;\text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate concentrate flow rate (i, part 2).**

$$
Q_r = Q_f - Q_p = 500 - 350 = 150 \;\text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 3: Apply mass balance to find $C_r$ (ii).**

$$
Q_f C_f = Q_p C_p + Q_r C_r
$$

$$
500 \times 3{,}000 = 350 \times 150 + 150 \times C_r
$$

$$
1{,}500{,}000 = 52{,}500 + 150 C_r
$$

$$
C_r = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000 - 52{,}500}{150} = 9{,}650 \;\text{mg/L}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate observed rejection (iii).**

$$
R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \dfrac{150}{3{,}000} = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{obs} = 95 \;\%}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $Q_p = 350 \;\text{m}^3/\text{day}$
- $Q_r = 150 \;\text{m}^3/\text{day}$
- $C_r = 9{,}650 \;\text{mg/L}$
- Salt rejection $= 95 \;\%$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert bar → Pa** in permeate flux problems: 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa. A common mistake is to compute $J_w = A \times \Delta P$ in bar, giving an answer off by **$10^5$**. Always convert.
> 2. **Using the wrong van't Hoff factor $i$**: For NaCl, $i = 2$; for CaCl₂, $i = 3$; for CaSO₄, $i \approx 2$ (since it is sparingly soluble).
> 3. **Neglecting osmotic pressure in dilute solutions**: Even for brackish water, $\pi$ may be 1–3 bar, comparable to NDP. Always include it.
> 4. **Confusing observed and true rejection**: True rejection uses $C_m$ (membrane surface concentration), not $C_f$. In KTU problems with no concentration polarization data, use **observed rejection**.
> 5. **Forgetting mass balance direction**: $Q_f C_f = Q_p C_p + Q_r C_r$ — sign errors here lead to negative $C_r$, a guaranteed 0.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NF** = nano-porous, **5 – 20 bar**, partial desalination; rejects multivalent ions preferentially.
- **RO** = dense, non-porous, **10 – 100 bar**, near-complete desalination.
- **Osmotic pressure** is the **minimum pressure** required to initiate RO: $\pi = iCRT$ (van't Hoff).
- **Permeate flux equation** is the **design cornerstone**: $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$.
- **Net Driving Pressure (NDP)** = $\Delta P - \Delta \pi$. If NDP ≤ 0, no net permeate flow.
- **Salt rejection** = $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$ (observed) — always expressed as a percentage in answers.
- **Recovery** $Y = Q_p / Q_f$ must lie between **0 and 1**; values above 0.85 require special energy recovery.
- **Mass balance** equations $Q_f = Q_p + Q_r$ and $Q_f C_f = Q_p C_p + Q_r C_r$ are required in every RO numerical problem.
- **Concentration polarization** raises $C_m$ above $C_f$, reducing observed rejection — accounted for by the film-theory exponential.
- **RO membrane construction** is a Thin-Film Composite: polyamide skin + polysulfone support + non-woven backing.
- **Spiral-wound modules** dominate the wastewater reuse market due to high area-to-volume ratio.
- Always quote **operating pressure in bar**, **flux in LMH**, and **rejection in %** in KTU answers.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "FANS"**
> - **F**lux = $A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$
> - **A**pplied pressure > **O**smotic pressure for RO
> - **N**F → **N**anopores → partial **Donnan** rejection
> - **S**alt rejection = $1 - C_p / C_f$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Membrane Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Membrane Materials # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Membrane materials** refer to the specific synthetic or natural substances engineered into thin, semi-permeable films that act as selective barriers in membrane-based separation processes. In the context of **biological wastewater treatment**, membrane materials form the functional heart of technologies such as **Membrane Bioreactors (MBR)**, **Membrane Filtration (MF/UF/NF/RO)**, and **Forward Osmosis (FO)** systems. These materials govern the efficiency, longevity, fouling propensity, and chemical compatibility of the entire treatment train. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A membrane material is the base polymer, ceramic, metallic, or composite substance from which a separation membrane is fabricated, characterized by a defined pore-size distribution, surface chemistry (hydrophobic/hydrophilic balance), mechanical strength, and chemical/thermal stability. The two broad classifications mandated by the KTU syllabus are: - **Organic (Polymeric) Membranes** — fabricated from natural or synthetic polymers. - **Inorganic Membranes** — fabricated from ceramics, metals, glass, or carbon. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Coffee Filter vs. the Sieve:** > Imagine two kitchen tools. A **paper coffee filter** (analogous to a **polymeric membrane**) is cheap, disposable, gets soggy when wet, and works fine for everyday coffee. A **fine-mesh stainless steel sieve** (analogous to a **ceramic/metallic membrane**) is rigid, expensive, lasts for years, can withstand boiling water and harsh scrubbing, but is harder to fabricate into ultra-thin sheets. The choice of "material" determines how long the filter lasts, what it lets through, and how easily it clogs. > > In wastewater treatment, the **membrane material** is exactly this trade-off: polymers are cheap and easy to fabricate but sensitive; ceramics are robust and long-lasting but costly. Engineers must select materials based on the **influent wastewater characteristics**, **bioreactor mixed liquor suspended solids (MLSS)** concentration, and **operational pH/chemical cleaning regime**. ## 1.3 Why Membrane Materials Matter in Biological Wastewater Treatment The choice of membrane material directly controls four critical performance parameters: | Parameter | Impact of Material Selection | |---|---| | **Hydrophobicity / Hydrophilicity** | Determines fouling rate by extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) | | **Surface Roughness** | Influences initial biofilm adhesion and cake layer formation | | **Charge (Zeta Potential)** | Affects electrostatic repulsion of colloidal biomass particles | | **Chemical Resistance** | Governs tolerance to NaOCl, citric acid, and other cleaning agents | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric:** Membrane pore size is universally expressed in **micrometers (µm)** or **nanometers (nm)**, while permeability is reported in **Liters per square meter per hour per bar (L·m⁻²·h⁻¹·bar⁻¹)** — abbreviated as **LMH/bar**. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Membrane Materials — Structured Logic ### A. Organic (Polymeric) Membranes Polymeric membranes dominate the global MBR market (>90% of installed units). They are fabricated via **phase inversion (NIPS/TIPS)**, **stretching**, or **track-etching** techniques. **Key Polymer Families:** 1. **Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF)** - Most widely used MBR material globally. - Semi-crystalline, inherently hydrophobic, but easily modified to hydrophilic via grafting or blending. - Excellent chemical resistance (pH 1–11) and high mechanical strength. - Operating flux: **10–25 L·m⁻²·h⁻¹** (typical MBR). 2. **Polyethersulfone (PES)** - Amorphous, glassy polymer with high glass transition temperature (T_g ≈ 230°C). - Excellent thermal and oxidative stability; tolerates high chlorine concentrations. - More hydrophilic than PS but less than regenerated cellulose. - Common in **ultrafiltration (UF)** hollow-fiber modules. 3. **Polysulfone (PS / PSU)** - Similar to PES but with slightly lower thermal stability. - Good chemical resistance to acids, bases, and oxidants. - More hydrophobic → requires hydrophilic surface modification for MBR use. 4. **Polypropylene (PP)** - Hydrophobic, low cost, used in **microfiltration (MF)** as stretched/track-etched membranes. - Poor hydrophilicity → high fouling; rarely used as standalone MBR material. 5. **Polyethylene (PE)** - Similar profile to PP; used in heat-stretched MF membranes. - Low surface energy, hydrophobic, hence modified for hydrophilicity. 6. **Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) — Teflon** - Highly hydrophobic, chemically inert, thermally stable up to **260°C**. - Used in membrane distillation and specialty gas-liquid separations, not conventional MBRs. 7. **Cellulose Acetate (CA) / Regenerated Cellulose (RC)** - Naturally hydrophilic → excellent anti-fouling property. - Biodegradable and low fouling but **poor chemical resistance** (degrades at pH extremes and with chlorine). - Common in laboratory UF applications. 8. **Polyacrylonitrile (PAN)** - Moderately hydrophilic, good for UF. - Tolerates chlorine better than CA; common in flat-sheet MBR modules. 9. **Polyamide (PA)** - Used in **nanofiltration (NF)** and **reverse osmosis (RO)** thin-film composite (TFC) membranes. - Forms the ultra-thin selective layer atop a porous PES/PS support. 10. **Polyimide (PI)** - High thermal stability, used in **solvent-resistant nanofiltration (SRNF)**. - Relevant for industrial wastewater containing organic solvents. ### B. Inorganic Membranes 1. **Ceramic Membranes** — fabricated from metal oxides. - **Alumina (Al₂O₃):** Most common; alpha-alumina supports with gamma-alumina top layers. - **Zirconia (ZrO₂):** Used in UF layer for pH stability. - **Titania (TiO₂):** Photocatalytic self-cleaning capability under UV. - **Silica (SiO₂):** Used in sol-gel derived ultrafiltration layers. 2. **Metallic Membranes** - **Stainless Steel / Sintered Metal:** Used in MF for high-temperature or harsh chemical applications. - Pore sizes typically **0.1–10 µm**. 3. **Glass Membranes** - Made from sintered glass or Vycor-type porous glass. - Used in specialty gas separations and laboratory scale. 4. **Carbon Membranes** (derived from pyrolysis of polymeric precursors) - Used in gas separation, not common in biological wastewater. ### C. Composite / Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMMs) - Combine an organic polymer matrix with inorganic fillers (e.g., **TiO₂ nanoparticles, graphene oxide (GO), zeolites, CNTs**). - Designed to combine processability of polymers with robustness of inorganics. - **Emerging research area** for next-generation MBRs. > [!NOTE] > **Hydrophilicity Rule of Thumb:** Hydrophilic membranes (CA, PAN, modified PVDF) foul less in MBR applications because water preferentially wets the surface, preventing adsorption of hydrophobic EPS and soluble microbial products (SMP). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Equation / Parameter | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Membrane Permeability (P)** | $P = \dfrac{J}{\Delta P}$ | L·m⁻²·h⁻¹·bar⁻¹ | Defines clean-water flux per unit TMP | | **Flux (J)** | $J = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ (LMH) | Volumetric flow per unit area | | **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP)** | $TMP = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{permeate}$ | bar or kPa | Driving force for MF/UF | | **Recovery (R)** | $R = \dfrac{Q_{permeate}}{Q_{feed}} \times 100\%$ | % | Water recovery ratio | | **Rejection (Rᵢ)** | $R_i = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_{p,i}}{C_{f,i}}\right) \times 100\%$ | % | Selectivity for solute *i* | | **Pore Size Rating** | $d_{pore}$ | µm or nm | MF: 0.1–10 µm; UF: 1–100 nm; NF: 0.5–5 nm; RO: <0.5 nm | | **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO)** | MW at 90% rejection | Daltons (Da) | UF: 1k–500k Da | | **Contact Angle (θ)** | Measure of wettability | degrees (°) | θ < 90° = hydrophilic; θ > 90° = hydrophobic | | **Porosity (ε)** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_{voids}}{V_{total}}$ | dimensionless | 0.3–0.8 typical | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Engineering Note:** Always substitute `\vert` for any absolute-value symbols in handwritten solutions; KTU evaluators treat the vertical bar `|` inside markdown as a table delimiter and may deduct marks for "broken equations." ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **MBR Plants (Kerala Context):** Municipal STPs in Thiruvananthapuram, Kochi, and Kozhikode predominantly use **PVDF hollow-fiber UF membranes** due to the tropical climate, high organic loading from domestic sewage, and need for chemical cleaning tolerance during monsoon-driven load surges. - **Industrial Effluents:** Ceramic Al₂O₃/ZrO₂ membranes are used in dairy, pharmaceutical, and textile effluent treatment where high-temperature CIP (clean-in-place) is required. - **Desalination Pretreatment:** Polyamide TFC membranes form the active layer in seawater RO systems. - **Landfill Leachate:** PTFE and PVDF chemically robust membranes handle extreme pH leachate (pH 3–13). --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Membrane Flux from Darcy's Law (Foundational) The volumetric flux **J** through a clean, porous membrane is governed by **Hagen-Poiseuille flow** through cylindrical pores, combined with the **Kozeny-Carman** modification for tortuous real pores. ### Step 1 — Start with Hagen-Poiseuille Equation For a single straight cylindrical pore of radius *r* and length *L*, the volumetric flow rate *q* is: $$ q = \dfrac{\pi \cdot r^{4} \cdot \Delta P}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L} $$ where: - $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across pore (Pa) - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of permeate (Pa·s) - $L$ = pore length (m) - $r$ = pore radius (m) > **Logic:** This is the classical Navier-Stokes solution for laminar flow through a circular tube — fluid shear at the wall produces a parabolic velocity profile. ### Step 2 — Express Flow per Unit Membrane Area Number of pores per unit membrane area = $\dfrac{n_{pores}}{A_{m}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{A_{m} \cdot \pi r^{2}}$ ... but for unit area simplification, define $N$ = number of pores per m². Total flux per unit area: $$ J = N \cdot q = \dfrac{N \cdot \pi \cdot r^{4} \cdot \Delta P}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L} $$ ### Step 3 — Introduce Membrane Porosity and Tortuosity Define: - $\varepsilon$ = surface porosity = $N \cdot \pi r^{2}$ - $\tau$ = tortuosity (path-length correction, $\tau \geq 1$) Substitute $L = \tau \cdot L_{m}$ (effective path length through membrane of thickness $L_m$): $$ J = \dfrac{\varepsilon \cdot r^{2} \cdot \Delta P}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot \tau \cdot L_{m}} $$ ### Step 4 — Introduce Membrane Resistance R_m Rearrange to the canonical **Resistance-in-Series** form: $$ J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{m}} $$ where the intrinsic membrane resistance is: $$ R_{m} = \dfrac{8 \cdot \tau \cdot L_{m}}{\varepsilon \cdot r^{2}} $$ > **Logic:** The intrinsic resistance scales directly with thickness and tortuosity, but inversely with porosity and the square of pore radius. This is why MBR operators favor thin, highly porous, large-pore UF membranes to maximize sustainable flux. ### Step 5 — Generalized Form with Fouling For a fouled membrane, total resistance becomes: $$ R_{total} = R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{p} + R_{cp} $$ where: - $R_{m}$ = intrinsic membrane resistance - $R_{c}$ = cake layer resistance - $R_{p}$ = pore-blocking resistance - $R_{cp}$ = concentration polarization resistance The operating flux equation becomes: $$ J = \dfrac{TMP}{\mu \cdot R_{total}} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem) **Problem:** A flat-sheet PVDF UF membrane in an MBR has the following specifications: - Membrane thickness $L_m = 150\ \mu m = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}\ m$ - Mean pore radius $r = 0.04\ \mu m = 4 \times 10^{-8}\ m$ - Surface porosity $\varepsilon = 0.65$ - Tortuosity $\tau = 1.4$ - Water viscosity at 25°C: $\mu = 8.9 \times 10^{-4}\ Pa \cdot s$ - Operating TMP = 0.4 bar = $4 \times 10^{4}\ Pa$ **Calculate:** (a) Intrinsic membrane resistance $R_m$, (b) Clean-water flux $J$. ### Solution **Part (a):** $$ R_{m} = \dfrac{8 \cdot \tau \cdot L_{m}}{\varepsilon \cdot r^{2}} $$ Substitute values: $$ R_{m} = \dfrac{8 \times 1.4 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-4})}{0.65 \times (4 \times 10^{-8})^{2}} $$ Numerator: $$ 8 \times 1.4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4} = 16.8 \times 10^{-4} = 1.68 \times 10^{-3} $$ Denominator: $$ 0.65 \times 16 \times 10^{-16} = 10.4 \times 10^{-16} = 1.04 \times 10^{-15} $$ Therefore: $$ R_{m} = \dfrac{1.68 \times 10^{-3}}{1.04 \times 10^{-15}} = 1.615 \times 10^{12}\ m^{-1} $$ **Part (b):** $$ J = \dfrac{TMP}{\mu \cdot R_{m}} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{4}}{(8.9 \times 10^{-4}) \times (1.615 \times 10^{12})} $$ Denominator: $$ 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.615 \times 10^{12} = 14.37 \times 10^{8} = 1.437 \times 10^{9} $$ Therefore: $$ J = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{4}}{1.437 \times 10^{9}} = 2.78 \times 10^{-5}\ m/s $$ Convert to LMH (1 m/s = 3.6 × 10⁶ LMH): $$ J = 2.78 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} = 100.1\ LMH $$ > **Result:** Clean-water flux ≈ **100 LMH**, which is consistent with PVDF UF membrane specifications. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Polymer Selection for MBR | Property | PVDF | PES | PS | PAN | CA | PTFE | Ceramic (Al₂O₃) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Hydrophilicity** | Moderate* | Moderate | Low | High | Very High | Very Low | High | | **Chemical Resistance (pH)** | 1–11 | 1–13 | 1–13 | 2–12 | 4–8 | 0–14 | 0–14 | | **Chlorine Tolerance** | High | High | Moderate | Moderate | Low | Very High | Very High | | **Mechanical Strength** | High | High | High | Moderate | Low | High | Very High | | **Cost (Relative)** | Medium | Medium | Low | Low | Low | High | Very High | | **MBR Suitability** | Excellent | Good | Fair | Good | Fair (lab) | Poor | Excellent (industrial) | | **Lifespan (years)** | 5–10 | 5–8 | 5–8 | 3–5 | 2–4 | 8–15 | 15–25 | *PVDF is hydrophobic as base polymer but commonly modified (blended with hydrophilic additives like PVP or surface-grafted) to enhance hydrophilicity for MBR. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Membrane Materials ```mermaid graph TD A["Membrane Materials"] --> B["Organic Polymeric"] A --> C["Inorganic"] A --> D["Composite Mixed Matrix"] B --> B1["PVDF"] B --> B2["PES Polyethersulfone"] B --> B3["PS Polysulfone"] B --> B4["PP Polypropylene"] B --> B5["PE Polyethylene"] B --> B6["PTFE Teflon"] B --> B7["CA Cellulose Acetate"] B --> B8["PAN Polyacrylonitrile"] B --> B9["PA Polyamide"] B --> B10["PI Polyimide"] C --> C1["Ceramic Al2O3"] C --> C2["Ceramic ZrO2"] C --> C3["Ceramic TiO2"] C --> C4["Metallic Stainless Steel"] C --> C5["Glass Vycor"] C --> C6["Carbon Pyrolysed"] D --> D1["Polymer plus TiO2 Nanoparticles"] D --> D2["Polymer plus Graphene Oxide"] D --> D3["Polymer plus Zeolites"] D --> D4["Polymer plus CNTs"] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Material Selection Flow ```mermaid graph TD S1["Step 1: Define Influent Characteristics
MLSS pH Temperature Organics"] --> S2["Step 2: Select Process Type
MF UF NF RO MBR FO"] S2 --> S3["Step 3: Define Pore Size
MF 0.1 to 10 um
UF 1 to 100 nm
NF 0.5 to 5 nm
RO less than 0.5 nm"] S3 --> S4["Step 4: Match Material Hydrophilicity
Hydrophilic for high fouling MBR
Hydrophobic for MD or gas"] S4 --> S5["Step 5: Check Chemical Compatibility
Chlorine pH Solvents CIP"] S5 --> S6["Step 6: Mechanical and Thermal Check
Tensile strength Tg Operating T"] S6 --> S7["Step 7: Cost Benefit and Lifecycle
CAPEX OPEX Membrane lifespan"] S7 --> S8["Final Material Selection
e.g. PVDF hollow fiber UF for municipal MBR"] ``` ## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Asymmetric Membrane Structure ```mermaid graph LR F1["Top Skin Layer
0.1 to 1 um
Selective Barrier"] --> F2["Finger like Support
50 to 200 um
Provides Mechanical Strength"] F2 --> F3["Sponge like Base
50 to 100 um
Backbone Support"] F3 --> F4["Non woven Backing
100 to 300 um
Handle and Reinforcement"] P1["Feed Flow from Top"] --> F1 F4 --> P2["Permeate Exits from Bottom"] ``` > **Interpretation:** The top skin layer performs the actual separation (sieving mechanism); the underlying porous support offers mechanical robustness without adding excessive hydraulic resistance. This **asymmetric** architecture is fabricated via the **Non-Solvent Induced Phase Separation (NIPS)** process for PVDF, PES, and PS. ## 4.4 Membrane Configuration vs. Material Compatibility ```mermaid graph TD A["Membrane Configuration"] --> B1["Hollow Fiber"] A --> B2["Flat Sheet"] A --> B3["Spiral Wound"] A --> B4["Tubular"] B1 --> M1["PVDF PES PP PE PTFE
Outer or inner skin"] B2 --> M2["PVDF PES PAN CA
Common in cassette MBR"] B3 --> M3["PA TFC on PS or PES support
Standard for NF and RO"] B4 --> M4["Ceramic Al2O3 ZrO2
Industrial high strength"] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** Differentiate between **organic** and **inorganic** membrane materials used in biological wastewater treatment. Give two examples of each. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Organic (Polymeric) Membranes | Inorganic Membranes | |---|---|---| | **Composition** | Carbon-based polymer chains (PVDF, PES) | Metal oxides, metals, glass, carbon | | **Cost** | Low to moderate | High | | **Chemical Resistance** | Limited (pH 1–13 typical) | Excellent (full pH range) | | **Thermal Stability** | Limited (< 200°C) | Excellent (> 400°C for ceramics) | | **Mechanical Strength** | Moderate | Very high | | **Fabrication** | Phase inversion, stretching | Sintering, sol-gel, leaching | | **Examples** | PVDF, PES, PS, CA, PAN, PTFE, PP | Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂, Stainless Steel | > **Marking:** Definition (1M) + Table of differences (1.5M) + Examples (0.5M) = **3 Marks** ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** Why is **PVDF** the most widely used membrane material in modern MBR plants? List any four reasons. **Model Answer:** 1. **High chemical resistance** — tolerates pH 1–11 and oxidants like NaOCl, allowing aggressive chemical cleaning. 2. **Excellent mechanical strength** — withstands backflushing pressures and aeration shear in submerged MBRs. 3. **Tunable hydrophilicity** — base hydrophobic polymer can be blended with PVP or grafted to become hydrophilic, reducing fouling. 4. **Long service life** — typically 5–10 years in municipal MBRs, with proven track record in tropical climates (e.g., Kerala STPs). 5. **High flux** — achievable sustainable flux of **15–25 LMH** in submerged hollow-fiber configuration. 6. **Versatile fabrication** — easily cast into hollow fiber, flat sheet, and tubular geometries. > **Marking:** Any four valid reasons × 0.75 = **3 Marks** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Conflating *pore size* (MF/UF/NF/RO) with *MWCO* (UF only). Evaluators specifically check for this distinction. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing PVDF is "hydrophilic" without clarifying that base PVDF is hydrophobic and requires modification. Marks deducted for imprecision. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to state **units** (LMH, bar, Da). KTU 2024 rubric awards 0.5 marks per missing unit. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Not drawing or mentioning the **asymmetric structure** of polymeric membranes. At least one diagram credit is expected. ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** **(a)** Classify membrane materials used in biological wastewater treatment with a neat hierarchical chart. Discuss the characteristics, advantages, and limitations of **polymeric** and **ceramic** membrane materials. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Resistance-in-Series** model for membrane fouling. Derive an expression for the operating flux of a fouled membrane and identify the four components of total resistance. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **(a) Classification of Membrane Materials (7 Marks)** **Hierarchical Classification (2 Marks):** The membrane materials used in biological wastewater treatment are broadly classified into: 1. **Organic (Polymeric) Membranes** 2. **Inorganic Membranes** (Ceramic, Metallic, Glass, Carbon) 3. **Composite (Mixed Matrix) Membranes** **Polymeric Membrane Characteristics (2.5 Marks):** - Fabricated by **phase inversion (NIPS/TIPS)**, stretching, or track-etching. - **Examples:** PVDF, PES, PS, PP, PE, PTFE, CA, PAN, PA, PI. - **Advantages:** Low cost, easy fabrication into hollow fiber/flat sheet/spiral wound, scalable, established supply chain. - **Limitations:** Limited chemical/thermal tolerance, susceptible to compaction, chlorine degradation over time (especially PA and CA), shorter lifespan than ceramics. **Ceramic Membrane Characteristics (2.5 Marks):** - Fabricated by **sintering of metal oxide powders** (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂) or **sol-gel processing** for UF top layers. - **Examples:** α-Al₂O₃ support with γ-Al₂O₃ or ZrO₂ UF top layer; TiO₂ photocatalytic membranes. - **Advantages:** Exceptional chemical resistance (full pH 0–14), high thermal stability (up to 1000°C for some compositions), high mechanical strength, long lifespan (15–25 years), backwashable at high pressure. - **Limitations:** Very high capital cost (5–10× polymeric), brittle, heavy, limited to specific geometries (primarily tubular and multichannel monoliths), complex fabrication. **(b) Resistance-in-Series Model (7 Marks)** **Conceptual Explanation (2 Marks):** The **Resistance-in-Series** model treats the fouled membrane as analogous to an electrical circuit where hydraulic resistances add in series. The total resistance to permeate flow comprises the **intrinsic membrane resistance** plus resistances contributed by **fouling phenomena** (cake, pore blocking, concentration polarization). **Derivation (3 Marks):** Starting from Darcy's Law for clean water flux through a porous medium: $$ J = \dfrac{TMP}{\mu \cdot R_{total}} $$ For a fouled membrane, the total resistance is the sum of four components: $$ R_{total} = R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{p} + R_{cp} $$ Therefore: $$ J = \dfrac{TMP}{\mu \cdot (R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{p} + R_{cp})} $$ Where: - $R_{m}$ = intrinsic membrane resistance = $\dfrac{8 \tau L_{m}}{\varepsilon r^{2}}$ - $R_{c}$ = cake layer resistance (deposited biomass/EPS on surface) - $R_{p}$ = pore-blocking resistance (particles lodged inside pores) - $R_{cp}$ = concentration polarization resistance (retarded back-diffusion layer) **Identification of Four Resistance Components (2 Marks):** 1. $R_{m}$ — Clean membrane; depends on pore geometry, porosity, thickness, tortuosity. 2. $R_{c}$ — Reversible cake; removed by physical cleaning or relaxation. 3. $R_{p}$ — Irreversible pore blocking; requires chemical cleaning (NaOCl, citric acid). 4. $R_{cp}$ — Concentration polarization; elevated solute concentration at membrane surface increases osmotic pressure, effectively reducing net driving force. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the **fabrication techniques** for polymeric membranes. Explain the **Non-Solvent Induced Phase Separation (NIPS)** process and the **asymmetric structure** it produces. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is **membrane fouling**? How does the **hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity** of the membrane material influence fouling in MBR systems? Suggest **two strategies** to mitigate fouling through material modification. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Fabrication of Polymeric Membranes (7 Marks)** **Common Fabrication Techniques (2 Marks):** 1. **Non-Solvent Induced Phase Separation (NIPS)** — most common for PVDF, PES, PS. 2. **Thermally Induced Phase Separation (TIPS)** — used for PP, PE, PVDF. 3. **Stretching** — for crystalline polymers (PP, PE) to create slit-like pores. 4. **Track-Etching** — for PTFE and PC, produces uniform cylindrical pores via ion bombardment and chemical etching. 5. **Electrospinning** — for nanofiber membranes with high surface area. 6. **Dip-coating / Interfacial Polymerization** — for thin-film composite (TFC) PA membranes on porous supports. **NIPS Process (3 Marks):** The NIPS process (also called **immersion precipitation**) involves: 1. **Dope Preparation:** Polymer (e.g., PVDF) is dissolved in a solvent (e.g., DMF, NMP) along with additives (PVP, PEG) to form a viscous homogeneous casting solution. 2. **Casting:** The dope is cast as a thin film (flat sheet) or extruded through a spinneret (hollow fiber) into a controlled atmosphere. 3. **Immersion:** The cast film is immersed in a **non-solvent coagulation bath** (typically water). Rapid solvent/non-solvent exchange triggers **liquid-liquid demixing**, causing the polymer to precipitate as a porous solid. 4. **Phase Separation Dynamics:** The top surface (in contact with non-solvent) demixes fastest → forms a **dense skin layer** (selective barrier). The bulk demixes via **spinodal decomposition** → forms a **finger-like or sponge-like substructure** providing mechanical support. **Asymmetric Structure (2 Marks):** The resulting membrane has an **integrally skinned asymmetric structure**: - **Top Skin Layer** (0.1–1 µm): Dense, governs selectivity and rejection. - **Finger-like Macrovoids** (50–200 µm): Reduce hydraulic resistance. - **Sponge-like Support** (50–100 µm): Provides mechanical integrity. - **Non-woven Backing** (100–300 µm): Ease of handling. This architecture enables high flux without sacrificing selectivity. **(b) Membrane Fouling and Material Hydrophobicity (7 Marks)** **Definition (1.5 Marks):** **Membrane fouling** is the undesired accumulation of materials on the membrane surface or within its pores, leading to a decline in permeate flux and/or an increase in transmembrane pressure (TMP) during constant-flux operation. In MBRs, foulants include suspended biomass, colloidal particles, extracellular polymeric substances (EPS), soluble microbial products (SMP), and inorganic scalants. **Influence of Hydrophobicity/Hydrophilicity (3 Marks):** - **Hydrophobic membranes** (e.g., base PVDF, PP, PTFE, PS) strongly interact with hydrophobic fractions of EPS and SMP (proteins, humic substances) via **van der Waals** and **hydrophobic interactions**. This leads to rapid irreversible fouling and pore blockage. - **Hydrophilic membranes** (e.g., CA, PAN, modified PVDF) preferentially adsorb water molecules, forming a **hydration layer** that repels hydrophobic foulants. They exhibit lower fouling rates and higher sustainable flux. - The **contact angle (θ)** quantifies wettability: θ < 90° → hydrophilic; θ > 90° → hydrophobic. Lower contact angles correlate with better anti-fouling performance. **Strategies to Mitigate Fouling via Material Modification (2.5 Marks):** 1. **Blending with Hydrophilic Polymers/Additives:** Adding **PVP, PEG, or PEG-block copolymers** to the PVDF casting dope creates a permanently hydrophilic surface by migration of the hydrophilic phase to the membrane/water interface during phase inversion. 2. **Surface Grafting:** Initiated chemical vapor deposition (iCVD), plasma treatment (O₂, NH₃ plasma), or UV-induced grafting of **poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG), zwitterionic polymers (e.g., poly(sulfobetaine)), or acrylic acid** creates a brush-like hydrophilic surface layer that resists protein and EPS adsorption via steric repulsion and hydration. 3. **Incorporation of Nanoparticles (Mixed Matrix Membranes):** Adding **TiO₂, GO, or Ag nanoparticles** to the polymer dope imparts photocatalytic self-cleaning, antimicrobial, and improved hydrophilicity. 4. **Surface Coatings:** Dip-coating with **polydopamine** (mussel-inspired) creates a universal hydrophilic coating on virtually any membrane material. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Topic: Membrane Materials** - ☐ **Membrane material** = the base substance (polymer/ceramic/metal/composite) from which the membrane is fabricated. - ☐ **Two main classes:** **Organic (polymeric)** and **Inorganic (ceramic, metallic, glass, carbon)**. - ☐ **Most common MBR polymer:** **PVDF** (tunable hydrophilicity, chemical resistance, mechanical strength). - ☐ **Most common ceramic:** **Al₂O₃** (α-alumina support, γ-alumina or ZrO₂ UF top layer). - ☐ **Hydrophilic = anti-fouling:** CA, PAN, modified PVDF foul less than PP, PS, base PVDF. - ☐ **Pore-size spectrum:** MF (0.1–10 µm) → UF (1–100 nm) → NF (0.5–5 nm) → RO (< 0.5 nm). - ☐ **MWCO** is used for UF (Daltons); pore size for MF/NF/RO. - ☐ **Flux equation:** $J = \dfrac{TMP}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$; units **LMH**. - ☐ **Permeability:** $P = \dfrac{J}{\Delta P}$ in **LMH/bar**. - ☐ **Resistance-in-Series:** $R_{total} = R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{p} + R_{cp}$. - ☐ **Intrinsic membrane resistance:** $R_{m} = \dfrac{8 \tau L_{m}}{\varepsilon r^{2}}$. - ☐ **NIPS process** produces **asymmetric** membranes with dense skin + porous support. - ☐ **Ceramic advantages:** Full pH tolerance, high T, long life (15–25 yrs); **disadvantage:** high cost. - ☐ **MBR plants in Kerala** typically use **PVDF hollow-fiber UF** in submerged configuration. - ☐ **Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMMs):** Polymer + TiO₂/GO/zeolite = next-gen fouling-resistant materials. - ☐ **Always specify units** (LMH, bar, µm, nm, Da) — KTU deducts marks for missing units. - ☐ **Always clarify** "hydrophilic" vs "base hydrophobic" for PVDF — examiners test this distinction. - ☐ **Remember the four fouling resistances** and the corresponding cleaning strategy (physical vs chemical). ---

topics.title: Types of Membranes - Plate & Frame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Membranes - Plate & Frame

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Plate and Frame Membrane Module** (also called a **Flat Sheet Membrane Module** or **Plate Module**) is a membrane separation device in which flat-sheet membranes are mounted onto rectangular support plates, stacked in parallel, and clamped together within a pressure vessel or frame, analogous to a plate-and-frame heat exchanger or filter press. The feed wastewater is introduced into the channel formed between two adjacent membranes, and the **permeate** (treated water) passes through the membrane into a collection manifold, while the **retentate/concentrate** is recirculated or discharged.

In the context of **Biological Wastewater Treatment (BWW)**, plate and frame modules are extensively used in:
- **Membrane Bioreactors (MBR)** — replacing the secondary clarifier
- **Ultrafiltration (UF)** and **Microfiltration (MF)** of secondary effluent
- **Nanofiltration (NF)** for tertiary treatment and reuse

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The plate and frame configuration is classified as a **flat-sheet (non-spiral, non-tubular) geometry** under membrane module design, with the lowest **packing density (m²/m³)** among industrial modules but the highest **cleaning flexibility** and **membrane replaceability**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sandwich Press"

Imagine a household **sandwich toaster** or a **waffle maker**:
- The two heated plates represent the **support plates**.
- The bread slices represent the **flat-sheet membranes**.
- The cheese/butter oozing out the sides represents the **permeate** squeezing through the membrane under pressure.
- The filling (lettuce, tomato) that stays inside represents the **retained solids / concentrate**.

In a real plate and frame module, dozens of such "membrane sandwiches" are stacked side by side inside a single pressure vessel, with the **feed** entering one end and being forced to pass across each membrane surface, depositing clean water (permeate) into a central manifold and pushing the dirtier water (retentate) toward the other end.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Operating Parameters at a Glance:**
> - **Packing Density:** ~100 to 400 m²/m³ (lowest among module types)
> - **Membrane Area per Module:** 0.1 to 5 m²
> - **Operating Pressure:** 0.1 to 10 bar (depending on UF/MF/NF type)
> - **Cross-flow Velocity:** 0.5 to 2.5 m/s
> - **Flux Range (MBR):** 10 to 25 L/m²·h (LMH)

---

### 1.3 NEP 2020 / OBE Alignment

| Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level | How this topic maps |
|---|---|---|
| **CO3** — Apply membrane technology to biological wastewater treatment | Apply / Analyze | Identifying when plate and frame is preferred over hollow fiber / spiral wound |
| **CO4** — Design MBR systems with appropriate module selection | Design | Selecting plate count, area, and frame geometry |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Components of a Plate and Frame Module

The assembly consists of **five repeating sub-units** stacked in series:

1. **Feed Spacer / Channel Plate** — Creates the cross-flow path and maintains membrane spacing (typically 2–5 mm thick).
2. **Flat-Sheet Membrane** — Cast on a non-woven support, the actual selective barrier.
3. **Permeate Carrier / Support Plate** — A rigid plate (often PVDF, polypropylene, or stainless steel) with internal channels or a porous substructure that collects permeate.
4. **End Plates / Pressure Plates** — Hydraulic or bolt-tightened plates that seal the stack against operating pressure.
5. **Permeate Manifold** — Central pipe collecting clean water from all individual plates.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "F-M-P-E-P":** **F**eed spacer → **M**embrane → **P**ermeate carrier → **E**nd plate → **P**ermeate manifold.

### 2.2 Flow Path and Operating Modes

There are **two operational modes**:

- **Dead-End (Static) Mode** — Feed is pushed perpendicularly through the membrane. Used in batch MBRs, low-fouling applications.
- **Cross-Flow (Tangential) Mode** — Feed flows **parallel** to the membrane surface; only a fraction permeates through. The tangential shear ($u$) scours foulants and controls concentration polarization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The plate and frame module almost always operates in **cross-flow** mode in continuous wastewater treatment because the high suspended solids content (200–1000 mg/L MLSS in MBR) would rapidly blind the membrane in dead-end mode.

### 2.3 Mass Transfer and Foulant Control

The convective transport of water through the membrane is governed by **Darcy's Law for porous media**:

The **flux** $J$ is driven by the **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP)** and opposed by the **viscous resistance** of the membrane and the **fouling/cake layer**:

$$J = \frac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, (R_m + R_c + R_f)}$$

Where:
- $J$ = Permeate flux (m³/m²·s or LMH)
- TMP = Transmembrane pressure (Pa or bar)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of permeate (Pa·s)
- $R_m$ = Intrinsic membrane resistance (m⁻¹)
- $R_c$ = Cake layer resistance (m⁻¹)
- $R_f$ = Fouling resistance from pore blocking/adsorption (m⁻¹)

The **cross-flow velocity** $u$ controls the cake resistance $R_c$ via the empirical correlation:

$$R_c = \frac{k_c \, C_b^{\,n}}{u^{m}}$$

Where $C_b$ is the bulk MLSS concentration and $k_c$, $n$, $m$ are empirical constants.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Symbol Description** | **Typical Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Permeate Flux** | $J = \dfrac{Q_p}{A_m}$ | $Q_p$: permeate flow; $A_m$: membrane area | LMH or m/s |
| **Transmembrane Pressure** | $\text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_p$ | $P_{in}, P_{out}$: feed inlet/outlet; $P_p$: permeate | bar or kPa |
| **Darcy's Flux Law** | $J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, R_{tot}}$ | $R_{tot} = R_m + R_c + R_f$ | m/s |
| **Recovery Rate** | $Y = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100$ | $Q_f$: feed flow | % |
| **Packing Density** | $\phi = \dfrac{A_m}{V_{module}}$ | $V_{module}$: module volume | m²/m³ |
| **Hydraulic Retention Time** | $\theta_h = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{Q_f}$ | $V_{tank}$: bioreactor volume | h |
| **Shear at Membrane Wall** | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{6u}{h_{ch}}$ | $u$: cross-flow velocity; $h_{ch}$: channel height | s⁻¹ |
| **Shear-Induced Diffusion Coefficient** | $D_s = 0.003 \, \gamma_w^{1.3}$ | Empirical (Eckstein et al.) | m²/s |
| **Membrane Area Required** | $A_m = \dfrac{Q_p}{J_{design}}$ | $J_{design}$: design flux | m² |
| **Number of Plates** | $N_p = \dfrac{A_m}{A_{plate}}$ | $A_{plate}$: area per single plate | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **K**TU Pitfall: Many students confuse TMP with the inlet pressure $P_{in}$. TMP is the **net driving force**, which subtracts the permeate side pressure from the **average** of the inlet and outlet feed pressures.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in Wastewater Treatment

- **MBR Retrofit:** Plate modules can be retrofitted into existing activated sludge tanks with minimal civil work.
- **Sludge Thickening:** UF plate modules thicken waste activated sludge from ~1% to ~5% solids.
- **Tertiary Polishing:** Removes residual TSS, colloids, and pathogens (log 4–6 virus removal) before reuse.
- **Decentralized Treatment:** Modular design is ideal for small communities, hospitals, and hotels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transmembrane Pressure (TMP) from First Principles

Consider a feed channel of length $L$ with permeate being withdrawn continuously. The local pressure drops linearly along the channel due to friction.

Let the pressure at any point $x$ along the channel be:

$$P(x) = P_{in} - \Delta P_{friction}(x)$$

Assuming linear pressure drop (uniform flow):

$$P(x) = P_{in} - \left( P_{in} - P_{out} \right) \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$$

The **local transmembrane pressure** at position $x$ is:

$$\text{TMP}(x) = P(x) - P_p$$

To obtain the **average TMP** that drives the total permeate, we integrate over the channel length:

$$\overline{\text{TMP}} = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} \left[ P(x) - P_p \right] dx$$

Substituting the linear pressure profile:

$$\overline{\text{TMP}} = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} \left[ P_{in} - \left( P_{in} - P_{out} \right) \dfrac{x}{L} - P_p \right] dx$$

Separating the integral:

$$\overline{\text{TMP}} = \left( P_{in} - P_p \right) - \dfrac{\left( P_{in} - P_{out} \right)}{L^2} \int_{0}^{L} x \, dx$$

Evaluating the integral $\int_0^L x \, dx = \dfrac{L^2}{2}$:

$$\overline{\text{TMP}} = \left( P_{in} - P_p \right) - \dfrac{\left( P_{in} - P_{out} \right)}{2}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\overline{\text{TMP}} = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_p}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The driving force is the difference between the **average feed channel pressure** and the **permeate side pressure**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Membrane Area from Mass Balance and Design Flux

For a wastewater treatment plant serving a population of $P$ persons with a per-capita sewage flow of $q$ L/capita·day, the **average daily flow** is:

$$Q_{avg} = \dfrac{P \cdot q}{1000} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/day)}$$

A **peaking factor** $K_p$ is applied for design (typically 1.5 to 3.0):

$$Q_{design} = K_p \cdot Q_{avg}$$

In a plate and frame MBR, the permeate flow equals the design flow (neglecting sludge wastage losses):

$$Q_p = Q_{design}$$

If the **design flux** is $J_{design}$ (LMH), convert it to m³/m²·day:

$$J_{design,m} = J_{design} \times 24 \div 1000 \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/m}^2\text{·day)}$$

The required membrane area is:

$$A_m = \dfrac{Q_p}{J_{design,m}}$$

**Example Walkthrough:** $P = 50{,}000$ people, $q = 150$ L/c/d, $K_p = 2.0$, $J_{design} = 15$ LMH.

Step 1: $Q_{avg} = \dfrac{50{,}000 \times 150}{1000} = 7{,}500$ m³/day

Step 2: $Q_{design} = 2.0 \times 7{,}500 = 15{,}000$ m³/day

Step 3: $J_{design,m} = 15 \times 24 \div 1000 = 0.36$ m³/m²·day

Step 4: $A_m = \dfrac{15{,}000}{0.36} = 41{,}666.7$ m²

> This huge area justifies why plate and frame modules are used only at small to medium plants (<10,000 m³/day) unless multiple cassettes are arranged in parallel.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Recovery Rate Limitation by Osmotic Pressure (for high-rejection membranes)

For **NF/RO-type** plate and frame systems treating high-strength wastewater, the osmotic pressure of the concentrate $\pi_c$ reduces the effective driving force:

$$\text{TMP}_{eff} = \text{TMP}_{applied} - \Delta \pi = \text{TMP}_{applied} - (\pi_c - \pi_p)$$

Applying van't Hoff approximation for dilute solutions:

$$\pi = i \, C \, R \, T$$

Where $i$ is the van't Hoff factor, $C$ is molar concentration, $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature.

The **maximum recovery** is reached when $\text{TMP}_{eff} \to 0$:

$$Y_{max} = 1 - \dfrac{\pi_p}{\pi_c} \cdot \dfrac{C_f}{C_f - C_p \cdot Y_{max}}$$

For dilute UF/MF systems used in MBRs, $\pi$ is negligible and $Y$ can approach 95–99%.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Plate and Frame vs. Other Module Types
