| **Feature** | **Plate & Frame** | **Spiral Wound** | **Hollow Fiber** | **Tubular** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Packing Density (m²/m³)** | 100 – 400 | 600 – 1200 | 5000 – 15000 | 50 – 200 |
| **Membrane Cost (₹/m²)** | High (400–800) | Low (80–200) | Very Low (50–150) | Very High (600–1500) |
| **Fouling Resistance** | Moderate | High | High | Very High |
| **Cleaning Ease** | Excellent (plate-by-plate) | Poor | Moderate | Excellent (mechanical sponge ball) |
| **Replacement of Damaged Membrane** | **Easy (single plate)** | Difficult (replace whole element) | Difficult (replace whole bundle) | Easy |
| **Operating Pressure** | Low – High (versatile) | Medium – High | Low – Medium | Low – Medium |
| **Footprint** | Large | Compact | Most compact | Largest |
| **Typical Application** | MBR, small UF/NF, R&D | RO desalination, large WTP | MBR (submerged), UF | High-solids, food, pharma |
| **Capital Cost per m³ treated** | High | Low | Lowest | Highest |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cross-Flow Path Inside a Plate and Frame Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FEED INLET\nRaw wastewater\nQ_f, P_in"] --> B["FEED DISTRIBUTOR\nTop header channel"]
    B --> C["CHANNEL BETWEEN\nMEMBRANE 1 AND 2\nCross-flow direction"]
    C --> D["CHANNEL BETWEEN\nMEMBRANE 3 AND 4\nCross-flow direction"]
    D --> E["CHANNEL BETWEEN\nMEMBRANE N-1 AND N\nCross-flow direction"]
    E --> F["CONCENTRATE OUTLET\nRetentate to drain\nor recycle, P_out"]
    C --> G["PERMEATE\nCARRIER 1\nPermeate collection"]
    D --> H["PERMEATE\nCARRIER 2\nPermeate collection"]
    E --> I["PERMEATE\nCARRIER N\nPermeate collection"]
    G --> J["CENTRAL PERMEATE\nMANIFOLD\nClean water outlet"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

### 4.2 Exploded View of a Single Plate Sub-Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PLATE1["FRAME PLATE A\nRigid PP or SS plate\nOuter sealing gasket"]
    MEM1["FLAT SHEET\nMEMBRANE A\nUF or MF polymer"]
    SPACER["FEED SPACER\nThick diamond mesh\n2-5 mm channel height"]
    MEM2["FLAT SHEET\nMEMBRANE B\nUF or MF polymer"]
    CARRIER["PERMEATE CARRIER PLATE\nPorous PP backing\nWith permeate grooves"]
    PLATE2["FRAME PLATE B\nRigid PP or SS plate\nOuter sealing gasket"]
    PLATE1 --- MEM1
    MEM1 --- SPACER
    SPACER --- MEM2
    MEM2 --- CARRIER
    CARRIER --- PLATE2
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Mass and Energy Flow in MBR Plate Module

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BIOREACTOR["ACTIVATED SLUDGE TANK / BIOLOGICAL REACTOR"]
        B1["Mixed Liquor\nMLSS 8000-12000 mg/L"]
        B2["Aerobic Anoxic Zones\nBOD Nitrification Denitrification"]
        B1 <--> B2
    end
    subgraph MEMSTACK["PLATE AND FRAME MEMBRANE CASSETTE"]
        M1["Feed Distribution\nto Each Plate"]
        M2["Tangential Cross-Flow\nAlong Membrane Surface"]
        M3["Suction by Permeate Pump\nTMP 0.1-0.5 bar"]
        M4["Air Scour Below Plates\nBubble-induced shear"]
        M1 --> M2 --> M3
        M2 --> M4
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS["TREATED WATER AND RESIDUE STREAMS"]
        O1["PERMEATE\nTSS less than 1 mg/L\nBOD less than 5 mg/L\nTo disinfection or reuse"]
        O2["REJECTED SLUDGE\nWasted to sludge handling\nThickened 1 percent to 3 percent TS"]
        O3["RECYCLE CONCENTRATE\nReturned to bioreactor\nor bleed"]
        M3 --> O1
        M2 --> O2
        M2 --> O3
    end
    BIOREACTOR --> MEMSTACK
    B2 --> M1
    O3 --> BIOREACTOR
```

### 4.4 Cleaning-in-Place (CIP) Sequence Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1\nDrain Permeate\nand Backwash"] --> S2["STEP 2\nCEB Chemically\nEnhanced Backwash\nNaOCl 500 mg/L\nor Citric Acid 2 percent"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3\nSoak Period\n30 to 120 minutes"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4\nRinse with\nClean Water"]
    S4 --> S5{"FLUX\nRECOVERED?"}
    S5 -- YES --> S6["RESUME\nNORMAL OPERATION"]
    S5 -- NO --> S7["STEP 6\nFULL CIP\nAcid then Alkali\n45 to 55 degrees C"]
    S7 --> S4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three advantages and two limitations of the **Plate and Frame membrane module** configuration compared to hollow fiber modules in MBR systems.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **Easy maintenance and replacement:** Individual flat-sheet membranes can be removed, cleaned, or replaced without discarding the entire module — saves cost in long-term operation. (1 mark)
2. **High tolerance to suspended solids and fouling:** Operates effectively at MLSS up to 15,000–20,000 mg/L due to strong mechanical rigidity and air-scour compatibility. (1 mark)
3. **Simple construction and scalability:** Modular stacking of plates allows flexible capacity expansion by adding more plates. (1 mark)

**Limitations:**
1. **Low packing density (100–400 m²/m³)** leads to large footprint and high civil cost. *(any 1 of 2 for 0.5 marks × 2)*
2. **High labour for cleaning** due to manual plate handling and the large number of sealing surfaces prone to leakage.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP)** for a cross-flow plate and frame module. Why is the feed inlet pressure not equal to TMP?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
TMP is the net hydraulic pressure difference that drives permeate through the membrane, defined as the **average pressure on the feed side** of the channel minus the **permeate side pressure**:

$$\text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_p$$

**Why $P_{in} \neq$ TMP (1.5 Marks):**
Along the length of the feed channel, the local pressure continuously drops from $P_{in}$ to $P_{out}$ due to **frictional losses** in the spacer-filled channel. Because permeate flux is proportional to local TMP, the flux varies along the channel. The inlet pressure $P_{in}$ is therefore only the *maximum* local pressure, not the average driving force. Using $P_{in}$ would over-predict flux and under-design the system.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled sketch, describe the **construction and working principle of a Plate and Frame membrane module** used in a Membrane Bioreactor (MBR). Explain **cross-flow** versus **dead-end** operation with reference to this module. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A small township generates **4,500 m³/day** of domestic sewage. It is proposed to treat the sewage using a **submerged MBR** with plate and frame UF membranes. Design the system and calculate: **(i)** the **number of plates** required, **(ii) TMP** at the design operating point, and **(iii) the **packing density** of the selected module.

Given:
- Per capita sewage flow = 150 L/c/d
- Population = 30,000
- Design flux $J_{design}$ = 18 LMH
- Feed inlet pressure = 0.45 bar
- Feed outlet pressure = 0.30 bar
- Permeate pressure = 0.05 bar
- Effective area per plate = 0.9 m²
- Module volume = 0.4 m³ (per 50 plates)

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

*Construction (3.5 Marks):*

A plate and frame MBR module consists of the following stacked components:

- **Frame plates (top and bottom):** Rigid plates of polypropylene or stainless steel that clamp the assembly and withstand hydraulic pressure. They contain the inlet/outlet headers and the permeate collection port.
- **Flat-sheet UF/MF membranes:** Cast on a non-woven polyester support, typically 0.2–0.45 µm pore size (MF) or 10–100 kDa MWCO (UF). Material is usually PVDF, PES, or PVDF-blend for chemical resistance.
- **Feed spacer:** A coarse diamond or honeycomb mesh placed between two membrane sheets, defining the **feed channel height (2–5 mm)** and providing the path for cross-flow.
- **Permeate carrier plate:** A rigid, porous plate with internal permeate collection grooves that support the membrane and convey clean water to the central manifold.
- **End plates and tie rods:** Provide compressive sealing via O-rings or flat gaskets.
- **Aeration pipe (submerged configuration):** Located beneath the cassette, it produces coarse bubbles that scour the membrane surface and enhance cross-flow shear.

*Working Principle (2 Marks):*
Mixed liquor from the bioreactor is drawn into the cassette by gravity or a feed pump. It flows tangentially across the membrane surface in a **cross-flow** pattern. A **suction pump** on the permeate side applies a slight negative pressure, drawing clean water through the membrane into the permeate carrier. Suspended solids, biomass, and macromolecules are retained and exit as concentrate that is returned to the bioreactor or wasted.

*Cross-flow vs. Dead-end (1.5 Marks):*
- **Cross-flow (Tangential) Mode:** Feed flows *parallel* to the membrane; permeate is withdrawn *perpendicular*. The continuous tangential velocity creates wall shear that limits cake formation. Used in continuous MBRs.
- **Dead-end (Normal-flow) Mode:** All feed is forced *perpendicular* through the membrane; no tangential flow. Cake builds up rapidly. Used only in batch or low-solids applications.

**(b) Design Calculations (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Design Flow (1 Mark)**

$$Q_f = 30{,}000 \times 150 \div 1000 = 4{,}500 \text{ m}^3\text{/day} \;(\text{given})$$

**Step 2: Convert design flux (1 Mark)**

$$J_{design,m} = 18 \text{ LMH} = 18 \times 24 \div 1000 = 0.432 \text{ m}^3\text{/m}^2\text{·day}$$

**Step 3: Required Membrane Area (1 Mark)**

$$A_m = \dfrac{Q_f}{J_{design,m}} = \dfrac{4{,}500}{0.432} = 10{,}416.67 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Number of Plates (1 Mark)**

$$N_p = \dfrac{A_m}{A_{plate}} = \dfrac{10{,}416.67}{0.9} = 11{,}574 \text{ plates}$$

**Step 5: TMP at design (1 Mark)**

$$\text{TMP} = \dfrac{0.45 + 0.30}{2} - 0.05 = 0.375 - 0.05 = 0.325 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 6: Number of Cassettes and Packing Density (2 Marks)**

Plates per cassette = 50. Total cassettes = $\dfrac{11{,}574}{50} = 231.5 \approx 232$ cassettes.

$$A_{per\,cassette} = 50 \times 0.9 = 45 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{total} = 232 \times 45 = 10{,}440 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\phi = \dfrac{A_{total}}{V_{module,\,total}} = \dfrac{10{,}440}{232 \times 0.4} = \dfrac{10{,}440}{92.8} = 112.5 \text{ m}^2\text{/m}^3$$

> **Note:** A typical submerged plate module has packing density of 100–150 m²/m³ — our result (112.5) is in the realistic range. (0.5 mark sanity check)

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **mechanisms of membrane fouling** in plate and frame MBR modules. Describe **three physical** and **two chemical** methods to control fouling in these modules. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A plate and frame UF module treats industrial effluent with the following data. Compute (i) the **membrane resistance** $R_m$, (ii) the **cake resistance** $R_c$, and (iii) the **cleaning frequency** if the design flux must remain above 30 LMH and TMP is allowed to rise to 0.6 bar before CIP.

Given:
- Pure water flux (clean membrane) at TMP = 0.3 bar is **120 LMH**; water viscosity $\mu_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- Operating TMP = 0.3 bar gives operating flux **45 LMH** with mixed liquor of 10,000 mg/L
- Permeate viscosity $\mu_p = 1.05 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- TMP rise rate: linear at **0.02 bar/day** under constant flux operation
- Threshold TMP for CIP = **0.6 bar**

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Fouling Mechanisms & Control (7 Marks):**

**Fouling Mechanisms (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Pore blocking:** Small colloids, soluble microbial products (SMP), and extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) lodge inside membrane pores, permanently reducing permeability.
2. **Cake layer formation:** Biomass (MLSS) deposits on the membrane surface as a compressible gel-like layer; this is the dominant fouling in MBRs.
3. **Concentration polarization:** Solutes accumulate at the membrane-liquid interface, increasing local viscosity and osmotic pressure at the wall.
4. **Biofilm formation:** Long-term bacterial colonization on the membrane surface, especially at high TMP.
5. **Scaling and precipitation:** CaCO₃, struvite, or silica precipitation when influent hardness is high.

**Physical Control Methods (2 Marks, any three of the following, 0.7 each):**
- **Air scour / bubble-induced shear** beneath submerged cassettes
- **Backwashing** with permeate at periodic intervals
- **Relaxation cycles** (stopping suction for 1–2 min)
- **Cross-flow velocity optimization** (1–2.5 m/s)
- **Mechanical sponge ball cleaning** (for tubular variants)

**Chemical Control Methods (1.5 Marks, any two, 0.75 each):**
- **Maintenance cleaning** with NaOCl (300–500 mg/L) to oxidize organic foulants
- **Recovery CIP** with citric acid (1–2%) to dissolve scaling
- **Enzyme-based cleaners** for protein/polysaccharide foulants

**(b) Resistance & Cleaning Frequency (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Intrinsic Membrane Resistance $R_m$ (2 Marks)**

From Darcy's law with clean water:

$$J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, R_m} \Rightarrow R_m = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu_w \, J_{clean}}$$

Convert units: $J_{clean} = 120 \text{ LMH} = \dfrac{120}{1000 \times 3600} = 3.33 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/m²·s
$\text{TMP} = 0.3 \text{ bar} = 30{,}000$ Pa

$$R_m = \dfrac{30{,}000}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.33 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Cake Resistance $R_c$ (3 Marks)**

Operating flux $J_{op} = 45 \text{ LMH} = 1.25 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/m²·s

$$J_{op} = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu_p \, (R_m + R_c)}$$

$$R_m + R_c = \dfrac{30{,}000}{1.05 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{1.3125 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.286 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$R_c = 2.286 \times 10^{12} - 9.0 \times 10^{11} = 1.386 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Cleaning Frequency (2 Marks)**

Initial TMP = 0.3 bar; threshold TMP = 0.6 bar.

$$\Delta \text{TMP} = 0.6 - 0.3 = 0.3 \text{ bar}$$

TMP rise rate = 0.02 bar/day.

$$\text{Time between CIP} = \dfrac{0.3}{0.02} = 15 \text{ days}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Cleaning frequency} = \text{one CIP every 15 days}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **TMP formula error:** Writing TMP as $P_{in} - P_p$ instead of the average — this single error costs **2 marks** in any flux/TMP question.
> 2. **Unit conversion blunder:** Mixing LMH and m³/m²·day in the same line of calculation. KTU strictly expects unit consistency.
> 3. **Neglecting the peaking factor $K_p$** in design flow — most students use $Q_{avg}$ directly, costing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing "membrane area" with "module volume":** Packing density requires both, but the cassette arrangement must be clearly stated.
> 5. **Not mentioning air scour** in the MBR description — KTU values process-specific engineering detail.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Plate and Frame = flat-sheet membranes stacked between rigid plates** in a sandwich-like configuration.
- 🔹 **Lowest packing density (100–400 m²/m³)** among module types → large footprint.
- 🔹 **Highest maintainability** — membranes can be replaced individually.
- 🔹 **Best suited for MBRs, small-to-medium WWTPs, R&D, and high-fouling feeds.**
- 🔹 **TMP formula:** $\text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_p$ — *use the average, not the inlet!*
- 🔹 **Flux equation:** $J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, R_{tot}}$ where $R_{tot} = R_m + R_c + R_f$.
- 🔹 **Cross-flow velocity** is the primary control for cake resistance in plate and frame modules.
- 🔹 **Typical design flux for MBR UF:** 15–25 LMH; operating TMP: 0.1–0.5 bar.
- 🔹 **Cleaning hierarchy:** Backwash → CEB → Relaxation → CIP (acid + alkaline).
- 🔹 **Design flow** must use **peaking factor** $K_p$, not average flow, for MBR sizing.
- 🔹 **Plate count** = $\dfrac{A_m}{A_{plate}}$; **Packing density** = $\dfrac{A_m}{V_{module}}$.
- 🔹 **Comparison mantra:** "Plate for service, Spiral for cost, Hollow fiber for space, Tubular for filth."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tubular
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tubular

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Tubular Reactor** (also known as a **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** or **Longitudinal Flow Reactor**) in biological wastewater treatment is a continuous-flow reactor configuration in which the wastewater and the biological mass (sludge/biomass) move through a long, narrow, tube-like vessel in a manner that approximates **ideal plug flow**. The fluid elements pass through the reactor in a prescribed sequence without any longitudinal mixing (in the ideal case) and without any element of fluid overtaking or mixing with another.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Module 3 – Biological Reactors & Process Configurations), the tubular reactor is studied as a fundamental flow regime model used to analyze the kinetics of biological reactions such as **BOD removal, nitrification, denitrification, and anaerobic digestion** in long-channel configurations like oxidation ditches, long aeration tanks, and anaerobic tubular digesters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A tubular reactor is mathematically modeled on the principle that **every fluid particle entering the reactor spends an identical hydraulic retention time (HRT) equal to the theoretical value V/Q**, making it the standard for evaluating treatment efficiency when longitudinal back-mixing is negligible.

The governing assumption is the **axial dispersion model (ADM)**, but in its idealized form, the **dispersion coefficient (D) = 0**, giving:

$$D_{ax} = 0 \implies \text{Ideal Plug Flow}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **long, narrow, straight train corridor** (the tubular reactor) with hundreds of passengers (the wastewater + biomass) walking in from one end. Every person who enters the corridor at time $t=0$ will exit at exactly the same time $t = \tau$ (where $\tau = V/Q$). Nobody overtakes anybody else, nobody lags behind, and nobody turns back.

Compare this to a **village square** (a Completely Mixed Flow Reactor, CSTR), where people walk in and instantly get scrambled — some leave immediately, some stay forever. The village square is well-mixed; the corridor is *tubular / plug flow*.

**Key physical insight:**
- The reactor is **long in the flow direction** ($L$) and **narrow in the cross-section** ($A$).
- The **length-to-diameter ratio (L/D)** is very high (typically > 20 for plug flow approximation).
- The **concentration of substrate decreases progressively** from inlet to outlet along the length of the tube.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples in KTU context:** Long rectangular aeration tanks in conventional activated sludge plants, oxidation ditches, plug-flow anaerobic digesters, and biotowers with structured packing all behave as tubular reactors in the longitudinal direction.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Typical L/D ratio for plug flow assumption:** $> 20$ (dimensionless)
- **Typical axial Peclet number (Pe):** $Pe = \frac{uL}{D_{ax}} \to \infty$ for ideal PFR
- **Standard HRT for tubular aeration tanks:** **4 – 8 hours**
- **Standard MLSS concentration:** **2000 – 4000 mg/L**
- **Standard organic loading rate (OLR):** **0.2 – 0.5 kg BOD/m³·day**
- **Reynolds number range in tubular reactors:** $Re > 10^4$ (turbulent) to ensure dispersion-free flow approximation
- **Fluid velocity (u):** typically **0.1 – 0.3 m/s** in biological tubular reactors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** When asked to differentiate between reactor types in Part A (3-mark questions), always state that the **distinguishing feature of a tubular reactor is zero longitudinal mixing with a non-uniform substrate concentration profile along its length**, contrasted with a CSTR which has complete mixing and uniform concentration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A tubular reactor operates under the following structured principles:

1. **Inflow at Inlet (x = 0):** Raw wastewater with substrate concentration $S_0$ enters the tube at flow rate $Q$.
2. **Steady State Assumption:** The flow rate, substrate concentration at inlet, and biomass concentration remain constant with time.
3. **Advection-Dominated Transport:** The substrate moves primarily by **bulk fluid motion (advection)** rather than diffusion or dispersion. Each fluid element retains its identity.
4. **Reaction Along the Length:** As the fluid element travels a differential length $dx$, the substrate concentration drops by $dS$ due to biological reaction (typically modeled by Monod kinetics).
5. **No Radial Gradients:** The cross-section is assumed to have uniform velocity, concentration, and biomass distribution.
6. **Outflow at Exit (x = L):** The treated effluent leaves with substrate concentration $S_e$, satisfying $S_e < S_0$.
7. **Mass Balance on Differential Element:** A mass balance on a thin slice of the reactor gives the **design equation of a PFR**.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why no longitudinal mixing?** Because $D_{ax} = 0$ in the ideal PFR model — this is the defining assumption.
- **Why does concentration vary along length?** Because the reaction is occurring continuously as the fluid traverses the reactor, removing substrate as it goes.
- **Why use a differential mass balance?** Because the rate of change $dS/dx$ varies along the length, requiring integration to find the outlet concentration.

## 2.2 The Core Design Equation of a Tubular Reactor

For a tubular reactor with first-order kinetics ($r = -kS$), the mass balance on a differential element of volume $dV = A\,dx$ yields:

$$\frac{dS}{dx} = -\frac{kS}{u}$$

where:
- $S$ = substrate concentration at position $x$ (mg/L)
- $k$ = first-order reaction rate constant (time⁻¹)
- $u$ = fluid velocity = $Q/A$ (m/s)
- $x$ = axial distance from inlet (m)

Integrating from $x = 0$ (where $S = S_0$) to $x = L$ (where $S = S_e$):

$$S_e = S_0 \cdot e^{-k\tau}$$

where $\tau = V/Q$ is the hydraulic retention time.

For **Monod kinetics** ($r = -\frac{kXS}{K_s + S}$), the design equation becomes:

$$\tau = \frac{1}{kX}\left[(S_0 - S_e) + K_s \ln\left(\frac{S_0}{S_e}\right)\right]$$

This is the **governing design equation for a tubular reactor with Monod kinetics** in KTU examinations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables & Units** | **Boundary Conditions** |
|-------------|----------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| $S_e = S_0 \, e^{-k\tau}$ | Outlet substrate conc. (1st order kinetics) | $S_e, S_0$: mg/L; $k$: hr⁻¹; $\tau$: hr | Valid when $D_{ax} = 0$, first-order reaction |
| $V = Q\tau$ | Reactor volume | $Q$: m³/hr; $\tau$: hr; $V$: m³ | Steady-state flow |
| $\tau = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{L}{u}$ | Hydraulic retention time | $L$: m; $u$: m/hr | Ideal plug flow |
| $\tau = \frac{1}{kX}\left[(S_0 - S_e) + K_s \ln\frac{S_0}{S_e}\right]$ | HRT for Monod kinetics in PFR | $X$: mg/L biomass; $K_s$: mg/L | Applicable for $S > K_s$ and $S < K_s$ |
| $u = \frac{Q}{A}$ | Fluid velocity | $Q$: m³/s; $A$: m² | Continuity equation |
| $Pe = \frac{uL}{D_{ax}}$ | Axial Peclet number | dimensionless | $Pe \to \infty$: ideal PFR; $Pe \to 0$: CSTR |
| $\eta_{PFR} = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ | Treatment efficiency (1st order) | dimensionless, fraction | $0 \leq \eta \leq 1$ |
| $L = \frac{u}{k}\ln\frac{S_0}{S_e}$ | Required reactor length | $L$: m | First-order kinetics |
| $F/M = \frac{QS_0}{VX}$ | Food to Microorganism ratio | day⁻¹ | $0.2 - 0.5$ day⁻¹ for conventional |
| $\theta_c = \frac{VX}{Q X_e}$ | Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT) | days | $\theta_c > \theta_c^{min}$ for washout prevention |
| $r = -\frac{kXS}{K_s + S}$ | Monod reaction rate | mg/L·hr | Single limiting substrate |
| $Y = \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | Biomass yield coefficient | dimensionless | $0.3 - 0.6$ typical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute value bars in equations are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table syntax conflicts. For example, $\vert S_0 - S_e \vert$ is valid in LaTeX but unsafe in markdown tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tubular reactors form the **backbone of municipal and industrial wastewater treatment** for several reasons:

- **High Treatment Efficiency:** Because every fluid element has the same retention time, the average substrate concentration is much lower than in a CSTR of the same volume, leading to better BOD/COD removal.
- **Predictable Performance:** The deterministic nature of plug flow (no residence time distribution spread) makes the design straightforward using the design equation.
- **Energy Efficiency for High-Strength Wastewater:** The high substrate concentration near the inlet drives a rapid reaction rate, naturally matching biological kinetics.
- **Common Applications:**
  * Conventional activated sludge (long rectangular tanks)
  * Oxidation ditches
  * Trickling filters with plug-flow behavior
  * Anaerobic plug-flow digesters
  * Stabilization ponds (when deep and narrow)
  * Tubular photobioreactors for algal wastewater treatment

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Tubular = Think of a long straw.* Substrate is high at the mouth (inlet), low at the tip (outlet), and the longer the straw, the cleaner the drink (effluent).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Mass Balance on a Tubular Reactor

### Step 1: Define the Control Volume

Consider a tubular reactor of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$, with fluid flowing in the $+x$ direction at velocity $u$. Take a differential element of thickness $dx$ at distance $x$ from the inlet.

$$dV = A \, dx$$

### Step 2: Apply the General Mass Balance

The general steady-state mass balance on a control volume is:

$$\text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}$$

At steady state, accumulation = 0, so:

$$\text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} = 0$$

### Step 3: Express Each Term

- **Input (substrate entering the slice):** $Q \cdot S$
- **Output (substrate leaving the slice):** $Q \cdot (S + dS)$
- **Generation (consumption by reaction):** $r \cdot dV$ where $r$ is negative (substrate is consumed)

Therefore:

$$Q \cdot S - Q \cdot (S + dS) + r \cdot A \, dx = 0$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Expression

Expanding the terms:

$$Q \cdot S - Q \cdot S - Q \, dS + r \cdot A \, dx = 0$$

Cancel $Q \cdot S$:

$$-Q \, dS + r \cdot A \, dx = 0$$

Rearrange:

$$Q \, dS = r \cdot A \, dx$$

### Step 5: Substitute $Q = uA$

Since $Q = uA$ from the continuity equation:

$$uA \, dS = r \cdot A \, dx$$

Cancel $A$ from both sides:

$$u \, dS = r \, dx$$

Or equivalently:

$$\frac{dS}{dx} = \frac{r}{u}$$

### Step 6: Apply First-Order Kinetics

For first-order kinetics, $r = -kS$ (negative sign indicates consumption):

$$u \, dS = -kS \, dx$$

$$\frac{dS}{S} = -\frac{k}{u} \, dx$$

### Step 7: Integrate from Inlet to Outlet

Integrate from $x = 0$ (where $S = S_0$) to $x = L$ (where $S = S_e$):

$$\int_{S_0}^{S_e} \frac{dS}{S} = -\frac{k}{u} \int_{0}^{L} dx$$

$$\left[\ln S\right]_{S_0}^{S_e} = -\frac{kL}{u}$$

$$\ln S_e - \ln S_0 = -\frac{kL}{u}$$

$$\ln \frac{S_e}{S_0} = -\frac{kL}{u}$$

### Step 8: Final Form

Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{S_e}{S_0} = e^{-kL/u}$$

Recognizing that $\tau = L/u = V/Q$:

$$\boxed{S_e = S_0 \cdot e^{-k\tau}}$$

This is the **fundamental design equation for a tubular (plug flow) reactor with first-order kinetics**.

### Step 9: Efficiency Form

The fractional substrate removal efficiency is:

$$\eta = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with CSTR

| **Parameter** | **Tubular Reactor (PFR)** | **Completely Mixed (CSTR)** |
|---------------|---------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Mixing | None (longitudinal) | Complete |
| Substrate profile | Varies along length | Uniform |
| Outlet conc. (1st order) | $S_e = S_0 e^{-k\tau}$ | $S_e = \frac{S_0}{1 + k\tau}$ |
| Mean residence time | All = $\tau$ | Distributed |
| For same $S_0, S_e$: required $\tau$ | Smaller (more efficient) | Larger (less efficient) |
| Volume for same removal | Smaller | Larger |
| Application | High-strength waste, narrow land | Shock-load handling, equalization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison:** For a given $\tau$ and first-order $k$, the PFR always gives a **lower** $S_e$ (better treatment) than a CSTR. Conversely, to achieve the same $S_e$, a PFR needs a **smaller** reactor volume. This is a frequently tested 7-mark comparison question.

---

## 3.3 Sample Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A tubular reactor treats a wastewater with $S_0 = 500$ mg/L BOD at a flow rate $Q = 1000$ m³/day. The first-order rate constant is $k = 0.4$ hr⁻¹. If the desired effluent BOD is $S_e = 20$ mg/L, calculate:
1. The required HRT.
2. The reactor volume.
3. The required length if the cross-sectional area is $A = 25$ m².

**Solution:**

### Part (a): Required HRT

Starting from the design equation:

$$S_e = S_0 \cdot e^{-k\tau}$$

Substitute known values:

$$20 = 500 \cdot e^{-0.4 \tau}$$

Divide both sides by 500:

$$\frac{20}{500} = e^{-0.4 \tau}$$

$$0.04 = e^{-0.4 \tau}$$

Take natural logarithm on both sides:

$$\ln(0.04) = -0.4 \tau$$

$$\tau = -\frac{\ln(0.04)}{0.4} = -\frac{(-3.2189)}{0.4} = 8.05 \text{ hours}$$

### Part (b): Reactor Volume

$$V = Q \cdot \tau$$

Convert $Q$ to m³/hr:

$$Q = \frac{1000}{24} = 41.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

$$V = 41.67 \times 8.05 = 335.4 \text{ m}^3$$

### Part (c): Reactor Length

$$L = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{335.4}{25} = 13.42 \text{ m}$$

Check the L/D ratio (assume square section, $D = \sqrt{25/1} = 5$ m, but for plug flow, length is much greater than the hydraulic diameter; this is acceptable for a long rectangular tank with $L = 13.4$ m and width $B = 5$ m).

> [!TIP]
> **Answer Summary:** $\tau = 8.05$ hr, $V = 335.4$ m³, $L = 13.42$ m.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Tubular Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Influent Tank] -->|Q, S0| B[Inlet Distribution Zone]
    B --> C[Reactor Inlet Section x equals 0]
    C --> D[Reaction Zone 1 x equals 0 to L by 4]
    D --> E[Reaction Zone 2 x equals L by 4 to L by 2]
    E --> F[Reaction Zone 3 x equals L by 2 to 3L by 4]
    F --> G[Reaction Zone 4 x equals 3L by 4 to L]
    G --> H[Outlet Collection Zone]
    H --> I[Effluent Discharge Q, Se]
    J[Biomass Sampling Points] -.-> D
    J -.-> E
    J -.-> F
    J -.-> G
    K[Sludge Recycle Line] -.-> C

    style B fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The tubular reactor is segmented into four reaction zones along its length. The **red zones (inlet side)** have high substrate concentration and rapid kinetics, while the **green zones (outlet side)** have low substrate concentration and slower kinetics. This non-uniform profile is the **hallmark of plug flow** and a key distinction from the CSTR.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a tubular reactor. What are the key assumptions in the ideal plug flow model?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **tubular reactor** is a continuous-flow biological treatment unit in which the wastewater flows through a long, narrow vessel with negligible longitudinal mixing, such that each fluid element has the same residence time $\tau = V/Q$.

**Key Assumptions (2 marks for this part):**
1. **Steady-state flow:** $Q$, $S_0$, and $X$ are constant with time.
2. **No axial dispersion:** $D_{ax} = 0$ (ideal plug flow).
3. **Uniform radial mixing:** Velocity and concentration are uniform across the cross-section.
4. **No longitudinal mixing:** No element of fluid overtakes another.
5. **Constant density and temperature.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **ideal PFR assumption of zero longitudinal mixing** with **no mixing at all**. The correct statement is **zero longitudinal (axial) mixing, but perfect radial mixing is assumed** in the standard PFR model.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State the design equation of a tubular reactor with first-order kinetics and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$S_e = S_0 \cdot e^{-k\tau}$$

**Significance of each term (2 marks for this part):**
- $S_0$: Influent substrate concentration (mg/L) — dictates the inlet load.
- $S_e$: Effluent substrate concentration (mg/L) — the treatment target.
- $k$: First-order reaction rate constant (hr⁻¹) — indicates the biological activity.
- $\tau$: Hydraulic retention time (hr) — controlled by $V/Q$.

The equation shows that **the outlet concentration decays exponentially** with both $k$ (biological) and $\tau$ (physical). For the same $\tau$, a higher $k$ (e.g., from higher temperature or biomass) gives a lower $S_e$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option A

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) Derive the design equation of a tubular (plug flow) reactor for first-order substrate removal kinetics. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)**

**(b) A municipal wastewater treatment plant uses a tubular aeration tank of length 30 m, width 6 m, and depth 3 m. The influent flow is 5000 m³/day with BOD of 350 mg/L. The first-order rate constant is 0.25 hr⁻¹. Determine: (i) the effluent BOD, (ii) the treatment efficiency, and (iii) the F/M ratio if MLVSS = 2500 mg/L. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Steady-state, isothermal operation.
2. No axial dispersion ($D_{ax} = 0$).
3. Uniform velocity across the cross-section.
4. Single limiting substrate, first-order kinetics: $r = -kS$.
5. No biomass variation along the length (or X is constant).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Apply steady-state mass balance on a differential element of volume $dV = A\,dx$:

$$Q \cdot S - Q \cdot (S + dS) + r \cdot A \, dx = 0$$

Substituting $r = -kS$ and $Q = uA$:

$$uA \, dS = -kS \cdot A \, dx$$

Cancel $A$:

$$\frac{dS}{S} = -\frac{k}{u} \, dx$$

Integrate from $x = 0, S = S_0$ to $x = L, S = S_e$:

$$\int_{S_0}^{S_e} \frac{dS}{S} = -\frac{k}{u} \int_0^L dx$$

$$\ln \frac{S_e}{S_0} = -\frac{kL}{u} = -k\tau$$

$$\boxed{S_e = S_0 \cdot e^{-k\tau}}$$

---

### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $L = 30$ m, $W = 6$ m, $D = 3$ m
- $Q = 5000$ m³/day = $\frac{5000}{24} = 208.33$ m³/hr
- $S_0 = 350$ mg/L
- $k = 0.25$ hr⁻¹
- $X = 2500$ mg/L

**Reactor volume:**

$$V = L \times W \times D = 30 \times 6 \times 3 = 540 \text{ m}^3$$

**HRT:**

$$\tau = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{540}{208.33} = 2.59 \text{ hr}$$

**(i) Effluent BOD:**

$$S_e = S_0 \cdot e^{-k\tau} = 350 \cdot e^{-0.25 \times 2.59}$$

$$k\tau = 0.25 \times 2.59 = 0.648$$

$$e^{-0.648} = 0.523$$

$$S_e = 350 \times 0.523 = 183.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

**(ii) Treatment Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100 = \frac{350 - 183}{350} \times 100 = 47.7\%$$

**(iii) F/M Ratio:**

Convert $Q$ to m³/day for F/M:

$$Q = 5000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

$$F/M = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} = \frac{5000 \times 350}{540 \times 2500} = \frac{1{,}750{,}000}{1{,}350{,}000} = 1.30 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to **convert units consistently** between $Q$ (m³/day) and $V$ (m³) versus $k$ (hr⁻¹) and $\tau$ (hr). Always convert $Q$ to m³/hr when using $k$ in hr⁻¹. Also, watch for the **mass unit** in F/M — use the same mg/L basis for both $S_0$ and $X$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option B (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Alternative]**

**(a) Compare a tubular reactor with a completely mixed flow reactor (CSTR) for biological wastewater treatment. Use a first-order kinetics model to derive the outlet concentration for both and show which is more efficient for the same HRT. (7 marks)**

**(b) An industrial wastewater has an influent BOD of 800 mg/L and must be reduced to 50 mg/L using a tubular reactor with $k = 0.5$ hr⁻¹}$. The flow rate is 2000 m³/day. Calculate: (i) the required HRT, (ii) the required reactor volume, and (iii) compare the required volume with a CSTR achieving the same treatment. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution Outline (for self-study):**

**Part (a):**
- Derive PFR: $S_e^{PFR} = S_0 e^{-k\tau}$ (already done above).
- Derive CSTR: Mass balance gives $S_e^{CSTR} = \frac{S_0}{1 + k\tau}$.
- Show that for the same $\tau$, $e^{-k\tau} < \frac{1}{1+k\tau}$ for $k\tau > 0$, hence **PFR is more efficient**.
- Conclude with a comparison table (see Section 3.2).

**Part (b):**
- $\tau = -\frac{1}{k}\ln\frac{S_e}{S_0} = -\frac{1}{0.5}\ln\frac{50}{800} = -2 \times (-2.77) = 5.55$ hr.
- $V = Q\tau = \frac{2000}{24} \times 5.55 = 462.5$ m³.
- For CSTR: $\tau_{CSTR} = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{k \cdot S_e} = \frac{750}{0.5 \times 50} = 30$ hr.
- $V_{CSTR} = \frac{2000}{24} \times 30 = 2500$ m³.
- **PFR is 5.4× smaller in volume** — a critical KTU insight.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Mistake:** In Part (a), students often state the comparison qualitatively but **fail to derive both design equations**, leading to a loss of 3-4 marks. In Part (b), unit conversions (m³/day → m³/hr) are the most frequent error source.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tubular Reactors in Biological Wastewater Treatment**

- [x] **Tubular Reactor = Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** — long, narrow, no longitudinal mixing.
- [x] **Defining assumption:** $D_{ax} = 0$, ideal plug flow, $Pe \to \infty$.
- [x] **First-order design equation:** $S_e = S_0 \cdot e^{-k\tau}$.
- [x] **Monod design equation:** $\tau = \frac{1}{kX}\left[(S_0 - S_e) + K_s \ln\frac{S_0}{S_e}\right]$.
- [x] **HRT:** $\tau = V/Q = L/u$.
- [x] **Efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ (PFR) vs. $\eta = \frac{k\tau}{1+k\tau}$ (CSTR).
- [x] **PFR is always more efficient than CSTR** for first-order reactions at equal $\tau$.
- [x] **Substrate concentration varies along length** (high at inlet, low at outlet).
- [x] **Real-world examples:** Long rectangular aeration tanks, oxidation ditches, plug-flow anaerobic digesters, biotowers.
- [x] **SRT ≠ HRT in activated sludge** — recycle loop gives $\theta_c \gg \tau$.
- [x] **Washout condition:** For Monod kinetics, PFR is more resistant to washout than CSTR (lower $\tau_{min}$).
- [x] **High-yield comparison:** For same $S_0, S_e$, PFR volume is **smaller** than CSTR — this is the most tested 7-mark concept.
- [x] **Units discipline:** Always keep $Q$ and $\tau$ in **consistent time units** when using $k$.
- [x] **L/D ratio:** Practical PFR needs $L/D > 20$ for plug flow approximation.
- [x] **Boundary conditions for integration:** $S = S_0$ at $x = 0$; $S = S_e$ at $x = L$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hollow Fibre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hollow Fibre # HOLLOW FIBRE MEMBRANES IN BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Hollow Fibre Membrane (HFM)** is a capillary-type polymeric membrane configuration characterized by an outer diameter typically ranging from **0.5 mm to 2.0 mm**, possessing a self-supporting tubular geometry with an inner lumen (bore) through which fluid is conveyed, and a microporous or dense wall that performs the selective separation function. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBBT526)**, hollow fibre membranes constitute the most widely deployed membrane geometry in **Membrane Bioreactors (MBR)** and advanced tertiary wastewater treatment systems, owing to their exceptionally high **packing density (surface area to volume ratio)** reaching values of **15,000 – 30,000 m²/m³**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Hollow fibre membranes are studied under the broader theme of *Membrane-Based Biological Wastewater Treatment Systems*, specifically as the filtration element within Submerged and Side-stream Membrane Bioreactors (SMBR / SSMBR). ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Straw Bundle" Analogy Imagine a **bundle of drinking straws packed tightly together inside a sealed container**, where each straw is extremely thin (almost hair-like). Now imagine: - The **inside of each straw** is the **lumen** — this is where clean water (permeate) flows out. - The **wall of each straw** acts as an **ultra-fine sieve** — it has microscopic pores that let water and tiny dissolved molecules through, but blocks bacteria, suspended solids, and large molecules. - The **dirty wastewater** is either pushed through the straw walls from outside-in or sucked through the wall from inside-out. - The space **between the straws** holds the activated sludge or feed water. This entire "straw bundle" is the **Hollow Fibre Membrane Module**. Just as a bundle of straws can filter a huge volume of water in a small space, hollow fibres provide enormous filtration area in a compact footprint — perfect for modern wastewater treatment plants where space is at a premium. ## 1.3 Classification of Hollow Fibre Configurations | Mode | Flow Direction | Driving Force | Application | |------|----------------|---------------|-------------| | **Inside-Out (In→Out)** | Feed inside lumen, permeate collected outside shell | TMP applied on lumen side | Side-stream MBR, UF drinking water | | **Outside-In (Out→In)** | Feed outside, permeate drawn through wall into lumen | Suction (vacuum) on lumen | Submerged MBR (most common) | > [!NOTE] > **Why Outside-In Dominates in MBR:** The feed in a Membrane Bioreactor is **mixed liquor suspended solids (MLSS)** at concentrations of **8,000 – 12,000 mg/L**. Pushing this thick sludge through narrow lumens would cause **instant fouling and clogging**. Operating outside-in lets the fibre wall act as a barrier while air-scouring bubbles continuously clean the outer surface. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Key Parameters (KTU Examination Frequently Tested) The following numerical benchmarks are **must-know values** for KTU 2024 ESE: - **Fibre outer diameter (OD):** 0.5 – 2.0 mm - **Fibre inner diameter (ID):** 0.3 – 1.0 mm - **Pore size (MF range):** 0.1 – 0.45 µm - **Pore size (UF range):** 1 – 100 kDa (MWCO) - **Packing density:** 15,000 – 30,000 m²/m³ - **Operating TMP:** 0.1 – 0.5 bar (submerged) | 1 – 3 bar (side-stream) - **Design Flux:** 15 – 25 LMH (submerged MBR) - **MLSS Tolerance:** up to **15,000 – 20,000 mg/L** - **Membrane materials:** PVDF (most common), Polyethersulfone (PES), Polypropylene (PP), Polysulfone (PS) # DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic Decomposition ### Step 1: Feedwater Contact with Membrane Surface The mixed liquor (biologically treated wastewater containing biomass) approaches the outer surface of the hollow fibres (in outside-in mode). The membrane is submerged directly in the aeration tank of the bioreactor. ### Step 2: Transmembrane Pressure (TMP) Application A **suction pump** applies negative pressure (typically **−0.1 to −0.5 bar**) on the lumen side. This pressure differential is the **driving force** for permeation. ### Step 3: Selective Permeation Through the Pore Structure Water and dissolved species smaller than the pore size pass through the membrane wall into the lumen. Suspended solids, bacteria (e.g., *E. coli* ~ 1 µm), and macromolecules are rejected and retained in the tank. ### Step 4: Collection of Permeate Clean permeate flows through the lumen and is collected at the module outlet for disinfection or reuse. ### Step 5: Continuous Membrane Cleaning (Anti-Fouling Strategy) **Aeration (air-scouring)** is supplied below the fibre bundle. Rising bubbles create: - **Cross-flow shear** along the fibre surface - **Fibre vibration** (lateral movement) - **Slug-flow regimes** that dislodge the cake layer > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** Membrane filtration is fundamentally a **mass transfer limited by fouling**. Without continuous hydraulic/physical cleaning, the cake layer (deposited biomass) would rapidly reduce flux to zero. The 2-second backwash cycle and continuous aeration are engineered to operate the system at **sustainable flux** (below the critical flux threshold). ## 2.2 Critical Flux Concept — The Heart of Membrane Operation The **Critical Flux (Jcrit)** is defined as the flux below which no fouling occurs, and above which particles begin to deposit irreversibly on the membrane surface. $$ J_{crit} = \frac{TMP}{\mu \cdot R_m} $$ Where: - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of permeate (Pa·s) - $R_m$ = intrinsic membrane resistance (m⁻¹) - $TMP$ = transmembrane pressure (Pa) ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | $A = \pi \cdot D_o \cdot L \cdot N$ | Total membrane area of N fibres of length L and outer diameter $D_o$ | m² | | 2 | $J = \dfrac{Q_p}{A}$ | Membrane flux (permeate flow per unit area) | m³/m²·s or LMH | | 3 | $TMP = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{permeate}$ | Transmembrane pressure (for inside-out) | Pa or bar | | 4 | $R_t = R_m + R_c + R_p$ | Total resistance = membrane + cake + pore blocking | m⁻¹ | | 5 | $J = \dfrac{TMP}{\mu \cdot R_t}$ | Resistance-in-series model (Darcy's law) | m/s | | 6 | $R_m = \dfrac{TMP_0}{\mu \cdot J_0}$ | Clean water membrane resistance (from baseline test) | m⁻¹ | | 7 | $\text{Rejection} \ (\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100$ | Solute rejection percentage | % | | 8 | $\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f}$ | Volumetric recovery ratio | dimensionless | | 9 | $P_d = \dfrac{A_{membrane}}{V_{module}}$ | Packing density | m²/m³ | | 10 | $\beta = \dfrac{V_{permeate}}{A \cdot t}$ | Specific flux (permeate volume per area per time) | LMH | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule (Per Engine Protocol):** In markdown tables, never use the vertical bar `\|` for absolute value. The above table uses \vert only where mathematically required; all other table separators are the structural pipe character used solely by markdown. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Hollow fibre MBR systems are deployed globally in: - **Municipal sewage treatment** (e.g., Singapore's NEWater, used for water reuse) - **Industrial effluent polishing** (pharma, textile, food processing) - **Decentralized / package plants** for residential complexes - **Hospital wastewater** (high pathogen removal) - **Landfill leachate treatment** The compact footprint (a 50,000 m³/day MBR plant occupies ~40% less land than conventional CAS) is the primary economic driver. # STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Membrane Area for a Hollow Fibre Module **Problem Statement:** A submerged MBR uses hollow fibre membranes with outer diameter $D_o = 1.5 \ mm$. Each fibre is $L = 2.0 \ m$ long. The module contains $N = 12,000$ fibres. Calculate the total membrane area. **Step 1 — Identify the Geometry:** Each fibre is a cylinder. The lateral (curved) surface area of one fibre exposed to filtration is: $$ A_{single} = \pi \cdot D_o \cdot L $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the Values:** $$ A_{single} = \pi \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3} \ m) \times (2.0 \ m) $$ **Step 3 — Compute:** $$ A_{single} = \pi \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \ m^2 = 9.4248 \times 10^{-3} \ m^2 $$ **Step 4 — Multiply by Number of Fibres:** $$ A_{total} = N \cdot A_{single} = 12{,}000 \times 9.4248 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ A_{total} = 113.10 \ m^2 $$ **Final Answer:** The total membrane area is approximately **113.1 m²**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sustainable Flux Determination (Resistance-in-Series Model) **Problem Statement:** A hollow fibre MBR operates with $TMP = 0.3 \ bar$. Clean water test gives $R_m = 1.2 \times 10^{12} \ m^{-1}$. During operation, the cake layer resistance is $R_c = 4.5 \times 10^{12} \ m^{-1}$ and pore blocking resistance is $R_p = 0.8 \times 10^{12} \ m^{-1}$. Permeate viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \ Pa \cdot s$. Calculate operating flux and identify the dominant resistance. **Step 1 — State the Darcy Resistance Model:** $$ J = \frac{TMP}{\mu \cdot R_t} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate Total Resistance:** $$ R_t = R_m + R_c + R_p $$ $$ R_t = (1.2 + 4.5 + 0.8) \times 10^{12} = 6.5 \times 10^{12} \ m^{-1} $$ **Step 3 — Convert TMP to Pascals:** $$ TMP = 0.3 \times 10^5 \ Pa = 3.0 \times 10^4 \ Pa $$ **Step 4 — Substitute into Flux Equation:** $$ J = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{4}}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (6.5 \times 10^{12})} $$ **Step 5 — Compute Numerator and Denominator Separately:** $$ \text{Numerator} = 3.0 \times 10^{4} \ Pa $$ $$ \text{Denominator} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.5 \times 10^{12} = 6.5 \times 10^{9} \ Pa \cdot s \cdot m^{-1} $$ **Step 6 — Final Division:** $$ J = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{4}}{6.5 \times 10^{9}} = 4.615 \times 10^{-6} \ m/s $$ **Step 7 — Convert to LMH (Liters per m² per hour):** $$ J_{LMH} = 4.615 \times 10^{-6} \ \frac{m}{s} \times \frac{1000 \ L}{1 \ m^3} \times \frac{3600 \ s}{1 \ h} $$ $$ J_{LMH} = 4.615 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} = 16.62 \ LMH $$ **Step 8 — Identify Dominant Resistance:** $$ \%R_m = \frac{1.2}{6.5} \times 100 = 18.46\% $$ $$ \%R_c = \frac{4.5}{6.5} \times 100 = 69.23\% $$ $$ \%R_p = \frac{0.8}{6.5} \times 100 = 12.31\% $$ **Conclusion:** The **cake layer (Rc) contributes ~69%** of total resistance, confirming that the *operational* (not membrane-intrinsic) fouling dominates. This justifies the need for **air-scouring and relaxation cycles** in hollow fibre MBR design. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Packing Density of a Hollow Fibre Module **Problem Statement:** A module has internal volume $V_{mod} = 0.05 \ m^3$. The total membrane area is $A = 113.1 \ m^2$ (from Derivation 1). Calculate the packing density. **Step 1 — Apply the Definition:** $$ P_d = \frac{A}{V_{mod}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute:** $$ P_d = \frac{113.1}{0.05} = 2262 \ m^2/m^3 $$ **Step 3 — Compare to Standard Range:** Standard hollow fibre packing density is **15,000 – 30,000 m²/m³**. Our calculated value (2,262 m²/m³) is low because we used only 12,000 fibres in a relatively large module. For high-density MBR cassettes, fibre count typically exceeds **100,000 fibres per m² of cassette footprint**. > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Note for KTU Students:** Always convert LMH ↔ m/s using the factor $1 \ LMH = 2.778 \times 10^{-7} \ m/s$, or $1 \ m/s = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \ LMH$. This conversion is a frequent source of numerical errors in ESE answers. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Fouling Mechanisms in Hollow Fibre Membranes | Fouling Type | Cause | Location | Mitigation Strategy | |--------------|-------|----------|---------------------| | **Cake layer formation** | Biomass deposition on surface | External wall | Air-scouring, relaxation | | **Pore blocking** | Solute particles enter pores | Within wall | Backwash, chemical cleaning | | **Pore narrowing** | Adsorption of organics (EPS/SMP) | Internal pore | Coagulation pretreatment | | **Biofouling** | Bacterial colonization | Surface + pores | Chlorination, anti-microbial coating | | **Scaling** | Precipitation of CaCO₃, silica | Surface | pH control, antiscalants | | **Wetting (for hydrophobic)** | Surfactants reduce surface tension | All surfaces | Hydrophilic modification (PVDF) | # STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hollow Fibre MBR Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Raw Influent Wastewater] --> nodeB[Preliminary Treatment
Screening and Grit Removal] nodeB --> nodeC[Equalization Tank
Flow and Load Buffering] nodeC --> nodeD[Anoxic Zone
Denitrification Reactor] nodeD --> nodeE[Aeration Tank
Activated Sludge Bioreactor] nodeE --> nodeF[Hollow Fibre Membrane Cassette
Submerged UF Module] nodeF --> nodeG[Permeate Suction Pump
Vacuum Driven] nodeG --> nodeH[Disinfection Unit
UV or Chlorination] nodeH --> nodeI[Treated Effluent
Reuse or Discharge] nodeE --> nodeJ[Return Activated Sludge
RAS Recycle Loop] nodeJ --> nodeD nodeF --> nodeK[Waste Activated Sludge
WAS Discharge] nodeL[Blower System
Air Scour for Fibres] --> nodeF nodeM[Backwash Tank
Periodic Reverse Flush] --> nodeF nodeN[Chemical Cleaning In Place
CIP NaOCl and Citric Acid] --> nodeF ``` ## 4.2 Hollow Fibre Filtration — Inside-Out vs Outside-In Configuration ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IA[Inside-Out Mode] nodeA1[Feed in Lumen] --> nodeA2[Membrane Wall Pore] nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Permeate collected outside] end subgraph OB[Outside-In Mode - MBR Dominant] nodeB1[Feed outside fibres
MLSS Suspension] --> nodeB2[Suction applied at lumen] nodeB2 --> nodeB3[Permeate flows inside lumen] end ``` ## 4.3 Fouling Build-up and Cleaning Cycle Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeS1[Start Filtration Cycle] --> nodeS2[Apply TMP Suction] nodeS2 --> nodeS3{Cake Layer Detected
TMP Rising above Threshold} nodeS3 -->|No| nodeS2 nodeS3 -->|Yes| nodeS4[Stop Permeation
Relaxation Phase 1 to 2 min] nodeS4 --> nodeS5[Air Scour Burst
High Intensity Bubbles] nodeS5 --> nodeS6[Backwash Reverse Flush
Permeate pushed outward] nodeS6 --> nodeS7{Permeability Recovered} nodeS7 -->|Yes| nodeS2 nodeS7 -->|No| nodeS8[Chemical CIP
NaOCl 500 ppm plus Citric Acid 2 percent] nodeS8 --> nodeS2 ``` ## 4.4 Component Cross-Section (Conceptual Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OUTHF[Single Hollow Fibre Wall Architecture] nodeZ1[Outer Skin Layer
Selective Barrier 0.1 to 0.45 micron] --> nodeZ2[Support Layer
Finger Like Macrovoids] nodeZ2 --> nodeZ3[Inner Skin Layer
Optional UF Skin] nodeZ3 --> nodeZ4[Central Lumen
Permeate Collection Channel] end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric (nodeA, nodeB1, etc.). No reserved keywords used as standalone node names. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without embedded formatting. # KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** **Define hollow fibre membrane. List any two advantages of hollow fibre configuration over flat sheet membranes in MBR applications.** **Model Answer:** A hollow fibre membrane is a self-supporting capillary polymeric membrane with an inner lumen through which permeate is withdrawn, and a porous wall that performs the separation. Typical outer diameter is 0.5 – 2.0 mm. **Two Advantages:** 1. **Very high packing density** (15,000 – 30,000 m²/m³) compared to flat sheet (500 – 1,000 m²/m³), enabling compact plant footprint. 2. **Self-supporting structure** eliminates the need for a backing support, allowing backwash operation without mechanical deformation. 3. *(Optional third)* Higher tolerance to suspended solids due to outside-in flow configuration. **[Marks Distribution: Definition 1 + Advantage 1 + Advantage 1 = 3 marks]** --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** **What is critical flux? Why is it important in the design of hollow fibre MBR systems?** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Critical flux is the maximum sustainable flux below which no irreversible fouling occurs on the membrane surface. Above this threshold, particles deposit irreversibly, causing rapid trans-membrane pressure (TMP) rise. **Importance in Design:** - The design flux for a hollow fibre MBR is always selected at **70 – 80% of critical flux** to provide a safety margin. - Operating above critical flux causes rapid, irreversible cake layer formation, demanding frequent chemical cleaning and shortening membrane life. - The air-scouring rate, relaxation frequency, and backwash intensity are all designed to keep the operating flux below Jcrit. **[Marks Distribution: Definition 1.5 + Importance 1.5 = 3 marks]** --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a **submerged hollow fibre Membrane Bioreactor (MBR)**. Discuss the role of aeration in controlling membrane fouling. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Working Principle:** In a submerged hollow fibre MBR, the membrane cassette is directly immersed in the aeration tank of an activated sludge system. Mixed liquor suspended solids (MLSS) at 8,000 – 12,000 mg/L surrounds the fibres. A permeate suction pump applies negative pressure (TMP = 0.1 – 0.5 bar) on the lumen side, drawing clean water through the membrane wall in an **outside-in** flow pattern. The permeate is collected through a permeate manifold and sent for disinfection. The biomass remains in the tank, achieving **complete solids-liquid separation** without a secondary clarifier. **Role of Aeration:** 1. **Biological oxygen supply** to maintain microbial activity (BOD removal, nitrification). 2. **Cross-flow shear** along fibre surface to scour away deposited cake. 3. **Fibre vibration and movement** induced by rising bubbles prevents cake consolidation. 4. **Slug-flow regime** creates localized high-velocity zones for in-situ cleaning. 5. **Oxygen gradient control** allowing simultaneous nitrification-denitrification in some configurations. **[Sketch: See SECTION 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — adapt to manual drawing in exam]** **Suggested Sketch Components (Draw in Answer Sheet):** - Rectangular aeration tank - Hollow fibre cassette submerged inside - Air diffuser at bottom - Permeate suction line - Sludge recycle line --- **(b)** A hollow fibre MBR is to treat 500 m³/day of domestic wastewater. Design flux is 18 LMH. Each fibre has an outer diameter of 1.4 mm and active length of 1.8 m. Determine: (i) total membrane area required, (ii) total number of fibres needed, and (iii) packing density if the module volume is 0.08 m³. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Total Membrane Area:** Convert flux to m³/m²·s: $$ 18 \ LMH = 18 \times \frac{1}{1000} \times \frac{1}{3600} \ m^3/m^2 \cdot s = 5.0 \times 10^{-6} \ m/s $$ Daily permeate volume = 500 m³/day = 500/24 = 20.833 m³/h = 5.787 × 10⁻³ m³/s $$ A = \frac{Q_p}{J} = \frac{5.787 \times 10^{-3}}{5.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1157.4 \ m^2 $$ **(ii) Number of Fibres:** Area of one fibre: $$ A_{single} = \pi \times D_o \times L = \pi \times 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.8 = 7.916 \times 10^{-3} \ m^2 $$ $$ N = \frac{A_{total}}{A_{single}} = \frac{1157.4}{7.916 \times 10^{-3}} = 146{,}209 \ fibres $$ **(iii) Packing Density:** $$ P_d = \frac{1157.4}{0.08} = 14{,}467.5 \ m^2/m^3 $$ This value falls within the typical hollow fibre range (15,000 – 30,000 m²/m³) — design is acceptable. **[Marks: Part (i) 2 + Part (ii) 3 + Part (iii) 2 = 7 marks]** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Set]** **(a)** Compare **inside-out** and **outside-in** flow configurations in hollow fibre membranes. Under what conditions is each preferred? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | Parameter | Inside-Out | Outside-In | |-----------|-----------|-----------| | Feed location | Inside lumen | Outside shell side | | Permeate collection | Outside shell | Inside lumen | | Pumping | Pressure on lumen | Vacuum on lumen / suction | | Suitable feed | Low turbidity water, side-stream MBR | High MLSS submerged MBR | | Fouling tendency | High (lumen blockage) | Low (cross-flow possible) | | Backwash | Easy (reverse flush) | Easy (reverse flush) | | Energy demand | Higher (positive pressure) | Lower (vacuum-driven) | **Inside-Out is preferred when:** - Feed water has low suspended solids (< 500 mg/L) - Side-stream MBR configuration is used - Higher operating pressures (1 – 3 bar) are acceptable - The lumen is less prone to breakage under internal pressure **Outside-In is preferred when:** - Feed is **mixed liquor** (MLSS = 8,000 – 12,000 mg/L) in submerged MBR - Operating TMP is low (0.1 – 0.5 bar) - Continuous air-scouring can be applied on the outside - Energy efficiency is a priority (vacuum < pressurization) **[Marks: 3 comparison table + 2 inside-out + 2 outside-in = 7 marks]** --- **(b)** Explain the **resistance-in-series model** for membrane fouling. A hollow fibre UF membrane has the following data: clean water flux = 250 LMH at TMP = 0.5 bar. After operating with mixed liquor, flux dropped to 30 LMH at the same TMP. If membrane resistance $R_m$ remains unchanged, calculate cake layer resistance $R_c$. Viscosity of permeate = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Conceptual Explanation:** The resistance-in-series model treats membrane fouling as additive hydraulic resistances in series, following Darcy's law: $$ J = \frac{TMP}{\mu(R_m + R_c + R_p)} $$ - $R_m$ = intrinsic membrane resistance - $R_c$ = cake layer resistance (reversible, removable by air-scour) - $R_p$ = pore blocking resistance (semi-reversible, removed by chemical CIP) **Numerical Solution:** Convert fluxes: $$ J_0 = 250 \ LMH = 6.944 \times 10^{-5} \ m/s $$ $$ J_{op} = 30 \ LMH = 8.333 \times 10^{-6} \ m/s $$ Convert TMP: $$ TMP = 0.5 \ bar = 5.0 \times 10^4 \ Pa $$ **Step 1 — Calculate Clean Membrane Resistance Rm:** $$ R_m = \frac{TMP}{\mu \cdot J_0} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^4}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.944 \times 10^{-5}} $$ $$ R_m = \frac{5.0 \times 10^4}{6.944 \times 10^{-8}} = 7.20 \times 10^{11} \ m^{-1} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate Total Resistance at Operating Flux:** $$ R_t = \frac{TMP}{\mu \cdot J_{op}} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^4}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.333 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ R_t = \frac{5.0 \times 10^4}{8.333 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.0 \times 10^{12} \ m^{-1} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for Cake Resistance (assuming $R_p \approx 0$):** $$ R_c = R_t - R_m = 6.0 \times 10^{12} - 0.72 \times 10^{12} = 5.28 \times 10^{12} \ m^{-1} $$ **Interpretation:** The cake layer contributes **~88% of total fouling resistance**, confirming that **physical cleaning (air-scouring, backwash)** is the most effective control strategy in this scenario. **[Marks: Conceptual 2 + Step 1: 2 + Step 2: 1.5 + Step 3: 1.5 = 7 marks]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors (LMH ↔ m/s):** Forgetting to convert between LMH and m³/m²·s is the **#1 cause of numerical answer errors** in KTU 2024 valuation. Always show the conversion step explicitly. > 2. **Confusing OD vs ID:** Membrane area calculations must use the **outer diameter** for outside-in mode and **inner diameter** for inside-out mode. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks. > 3. **Skipping Darcy's law derivation:** Examiners award 1.5 marks for explicitly stating the formula before substitution. A "bare substitution" without the formula statement loses 1 mark. > 4. **Forgetting to mention the role of aeration:** In any MBR question, failure to discuss air-scouring/anti-fouling mechanisms costs 2 marks. > 5. **Not identifying dominant resistance:** When resistance values are given, the examiner expects a quantitative comparison (% contribution) — a qualitative statement alone is insufficient. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Hollow Fibre Membranes — Module 3)** - **Definition:** Self-supporting capillary membrane (OD 0.5 – 2.0 mm) with lumen for permeate withdrawal. - **Two Flow Modes:** Inside-Out (side-stream MBR) and Outside-In (submerged MBR, dominant in modern plants). - **Packing Density:** 15,000 – 30,000 m²/m³ — significantly higher than flat sheet or tubular configurations. - **Common Materials:** PVDF (most preferred for chemical resistance and mechanical strength), PES, PS, PP. - **Operating TMP:** 0.1 – 0.5 bar (submerged) and 1 – 3 bar (side-stream). - **Design Flux (MBR):** 15 – 25 LMH — must always be **below critical flux** for sustainable operation. - **MLSS Tolerance:** up to 15,000 – 20,000 mg/L (vs. 3,000 – 5,000 mg/L in CAS). - **Anti-Fouling Strategies:** Air-scouring (continuous), relaxation (1 – 2 min every 8 – 15 min), backwash (periodic reverse flush), CIP with NaOCl + citric acid. - **Key Resistance Equation:** $R_t = R_m + R_c + R_p$ — cake layer typically dominates in MBR. - **Critical Flux Equation:** $J_{crit} = TMP / (\mu \cdot R_m)$ — operating flux kept at 70 – 80% of $J_{crit}$. - **Conversion Factor:** 1 LMH = 2.778 × 10⁻⁷ m/s (memorize this). - **Membrane Area Formula:** $A = \pi \cdot D_o \cdot L \cdot N$ (cylindrical surface). - **Packing Density:** $P_d = A_{membrane} / V_{module}$ — direct indicator of compactness. - **Submerged vs Side-Stream:** Submerged (vacuum, low energy) is preferred for municipal MBR; Side-stream (pressurized, high energy) for industrial applications. - **Rejection Efficiency:** Hollow fibre UF achieves > 99.9% removal of suspended solids, bacteria, and turbidity. - **Engineering Advantage:** Eliminates secondary clarifier and tertiary sand filter — 40 – 50% smaller footprint vs. CAS.

topics.title: Spiral Wound Membranes. Adsorption: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Spiral Wound Membranes. Adsorption: Introduction # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Spiral Wound Membranes (SWM) ### Formal Academic Definition A **Spiral Wound Membrane (SWM)** is a compact, high-surface-area membrane module configuration widely employed in advanced wastewater treatment and desalination. It is fabricated by enclosing flat-sheet semipermeable membranes around a perforated central permeate collection tube, with **feed spacers** and **permeate carriers** sandwiched between membrane leaves, then spirally winding the entire envelope around the central tube. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Spiral wound modules are the **industry workhorse** for Reverse Osmosis (RO) and Nanofiltration (NF) plants worldwide, valued for their high **packing density (800–1000 m²/m³)** and cost-effectiveness in tertiary wastewater polishing and water reuse applications. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **Swiss roll cake** being prepared for filtration: - The cake sponge (membrane) is layered with cream (feed spacer) and jam (permeate carrier). - The roll is then sliced — the **permeate (clean water)** travels spirally inward toward a hollow center straw (the perforated tube), while the **feed (dirty water)** flows lengthwise along the outside. - Contaminants too large to pass through the membrane **sponge** are left behind in the **feed stream** and exit as **concentrate**. This clever geometry packs enormous filtration area into a small cylindrical cartridge — exactly the engineering trick that makes modern RO plants economically viable. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Geometric Constants & Standard Metrics:** > - **Standard Element Diameter:** 2.5 inch, 4 inch, 8 inch (≈ 200 mm commercial standard) > - **Standard Element Length:** 40 inches (≈ 1016 mm) > - **Packing Density:** 800 – 1000 m²/m³ > - **Operating Pressure:** 10 – 80 bar (depending on RO/NF/UF application) > - **Feed Channel Spacer Thickness:** 0.7 – 1.1 mm (28 – 45 mil) > - **Cross-flow Velocity:** 0.1 – 0.3 m/s --- ## 1.2 Adsorption – Introduction ### Formal Academic Definition **Adsorption** is a **surface-based mass-transfer separation process** in which solute molecules (the **adsorbate**) present in a fluid phase become concentrated and held onto the surface of a solid (the **adsorbent**) through physical or chemical forces. It is fundamentally a **surface phenomenon**, distinct from absorption where molecules penetrate into the bulk of a liquid or solid. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **magnet attracting iron filings**: - The **magnet** = the adsorbent (e.g., activated carbon, with billions of microscopic pores) - The **iron filings** = the adsorbate (e.g., dissolved organic pollutants, color bodies, odorous compounds) - When wastewater passes through a bed of activated carbon, pollutant molecules "stick" to the vast internal surface — leaving cleaner water behind. - It's like a **molecular sponge** that traps contaminants on its inner walls rather than absorbing them into its body. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Context:** Adsorption is treated as a **physicochemical tertiary polishing step** following biological treatment. It removes **refractory organics, micropollutants, colour, odour, and trace toxins** that are non-biodegradable — bridging the gap between conventional activated sludge and advanced membrane separation. ### Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Adsorbent** | The solid that provides the surface | Activated carbon, silica gel, zeolite, activated alumina | | **Adsorbate** | The substance being adsorbed | Phenol, dyes, humic substances, pesticides | | **Sorbent** | Generic term covering both phases | Used in industrial literature | | **Sorption** | Umbrella term for adsorption + absorption | Often used when mechanism is unclear | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Henry's Law Constant (a foundational metric):** The linear partitioning coefficient $K_d$ characterizes the affinity of an adsorbate toward an adsorbent. In wastewater engineering, a high $K_d$ (L/g) implies strong pollutant removal potential and is a key design input for GAC contactors. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Spiral Wound Membrane — Structural & Operational Theory ### 2.1.1 Component Architecture A single SWM **element** (or "leaf") is built as follows: 1. **Two Membrane Sheets:** A flat rectangular sheet of semipermeable membrane (e.g., polyamide thin-film composite for RO) is folded in half lengthwise. 2. **Permeate Carrier:** A porous tricot fabric or non-woven sheet is placed between the two membrane layers, with three edges sealed — the open fourth edge is glued to the central perforated tube. 3. **Feed Spacer:** A diamond or ladder-shaped net (typically 28–45 mil thick) is placed on the outside of the membrane envelope to maintain an open feed channel, promote turbulence, and support the membrane against pressure-induced deformation. 4. **Winding:** The complete envelope is spirally wound around the **perforated central tube (ATD — Anti-Telescoping Device)** which collects the permeate. 5. **Outer Wrap:** A fiberglass or polypropylene outer shell prevents telescoping and protects the element. 6. **Feed/Brine Seals:** Rubber brine seals on the outer surface direct feed flow and prevent bypass. ### 2.1.2 Flow Path Logic — "How it Works" - **Feed flow** enters one end of the pressure vessel (which houses 1–6 elements in series) and travels **axially** along the feed channel. - Under the **transmembrane pressure (TMP)**, water and small solutes permeate through the membrane. - **Permeate** flows **spirally** through the permeate carrier toward the central tube, where it is collected and exits the vessel. - **Concentrate (retentate)** continues axially and exits the other end of the vessel as brine. ### 2.1.3 Engineering 'Why' Behind the Design | Design Feature | Engineering Justification | |---|---| | Spiral geometry | Maximizes membrane area per vessel volume (high packing density) | | Feed spacer | Promotes turbulence → reduces concentration polarization & fouling | | Cross-flow operation | Continuously sweeps contaminants off membrane surface | | Central permeate tube | Provides low-resistance collection path for clean water | | Anti-telescoping device | Prevents membrane shift under high differential pressure | ### 2.1.4 Advantages & Limitations > [!IMPORTANT] > **Advantages:** > - Highest **commercial packing density** among all module types > - Low capital cost per m² of membrane area > - Scalable via parallel pressure vessels > - Standardized inter-element O-rings allow easy field replacement > - Compatible with RO, NF, UF, and even some MF duties > [!WARNING] > **Limitations:** > - **Susceptible to fouling and particulate plugging** (feed must be pre-treated; SDI < 5 typically required) > - **Difficult to clean mechanically** (no backflush capability in standard RO configuration) > - **High feed-side pressure drop** in long vessels > - **Concentration polarization** still degrades performance > - Telescoping failure if brine seals wear --- ## 2.2 Adsorption — Theoretical Framework ### 2.2.1 Mechanism of Adsorption The migration of an adsorbate molecule from bulk solution to the adsorbent surface proceeds through three sequential stages: 1. **External (Film) Diffusion:** Molecule migrates through the hydrodynamic boundary layer surrounding the adsorbent particle. 2. **Internal (Intra-particle) Diffusion:** Molecule diffuses into the porous structure (pore diffusion) or along pore walls (surface diffusion). 3. **Surface Reaction / Attachment:** Final attachment via Van der Waals forces (physisorption) or covalent/ionic bond (chemisorption). > [!NOTE] > **Slowest step controls the rate.** For **Granular Activated Carbon (GAC)** in wastewater, intra-particle diffusion is often the rate-limiting step, justifying the use of the **Weber-Morris intraparticle diffusion model** in KTU derivations. ### 2.2.2 Classification of Adsorption | Type | Driving Force | Energy | Reversibility | Activation Energy | Example | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Physisorption (Physical)** | Van der Waals forces | Low (5–40 kJ/mol) | Reversible | None (instantaneous) | Dye on activated carbon | | **Chemisorption (Chemical)** | Covalent/ionic bonds | High (40–400 kJ/mol) | Often irreversible | Significant | Oxygen on metal catalyst | ### 2.2.3 Factors Influencing Adsorption Capacity | Factor | Effect | |---|---| | **Surface area of adsorbent** | ↑ Area → ↑ Capacity (Activated carbon: 500–1500 m²/g) | | **Pore size distribution** | Micropores (<2 nm) trap small molecules; macropores aid transport | | **Temperature** | Physisorption: exothermic → capacity ↓ with T↑; Chemisorption: may increase | | **pH** | Affects adsorbate ionization (e.g., phenol adsorption optimal at low pH) | | **Initial concentration** | Higher C₀ → higher loading until saturation | | **Contact time** | Required to reach equilibrium (typically 2–6 hours in batch) | | **Competing species** | Multiple solutes compete for the same active sites | | **Agitation** | Enhances film diffusion → faster kinetics | ### 2.2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Adsorption & Membrane Performance | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) \, V}{m}$ | Adsorption capacity at equilibrium | mg/g | | 2 | $R(\%) = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e)}{C_0} \times 100$ | Percentage removal | dimensionless | | 3 | $C_t = C_0 \, e^{-k_1 t}$ | First-order kinetic model | $k_1$ in 1/min | | 4 | $\ln(q_e - q_t) = \ln q_e - k_1 t$ | Pseudo-first-order linear form | Lagergren equation | | 5 | $\dfrac{t}{q_t} = \dfrac{1}{k_2 q_e^2} + \dfrac{t}{q_e}$ | Pseudo-second-order linear form | Ho & McKay model | | 6 | $q_t = k_i \, t^{0.5} + C$ | Weber-Morris intra-particle diffusion | $k_i$ in mg/(g·min⁰·⁵) | | 7 | $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m b} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$ | **Langmuir isotherm** (linear form) | $q_m$ = max capacity (mg/g), $b$ = Langmuir constant (L/mg) | | 8 | $R_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + b C_0}$ | Langmuir separation factor (favorability) | $0 < R_L < 1$ → favorable | | 9 | $\log q_e = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \log C_e$ | **Freundlich isotherm** (linear form) | $K_F$ (mg/g)(L/mg)¹/ⁿ; $n$ = intensity | | 10 | $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m c} + \dfrac{(c-1)}{V_m c} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | **BET isotherm** (linear form) | For multilayer gas adsorption | | 11 | $J = L_P (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | **Solution-Diffusion** model (RO flux) | $L_P$ = permeability, $\Delta\pi$ = osmotic pressure | | 12 | $A_m = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) \cdot N$ | Membrane area per element (approx) | $D$ = element dia, $d$ = tube dia, $N$ = turns | | 13 | $TMP = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{permeate}$ | Transmembrane pressure | bar or kPa | | 14 | $Re_{spacer} = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, d_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number in feed spacer | $d_h$ = hydraulic diameter | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** > - **Langmuir** is used to predict **monolayer saturation capacity** $q_m$ — vital for sizing GAC contactors in **Municipal Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)** targeting COD/colour removal. > - **Freundlich** captures **heterogeneous surface** behaviour typical of industrial effluents (e.g., textile dye bath wastewater). > - **BET** is employed to **characterize the adsorbent** itself (standard surface area measurement per ISO 9277). > - **Solution-Diffusion model** is the foundation of RO system design — used by software like **ROSA (Dupont)** and **WAVE (Hydranautics/Nitto)**. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm ### Assumptions 1. The adsorbent surface has a **finite number of identical active sites**. 2. Each site can hold **only one adsorbate molecule** (monolayer coverage). 3. No lateral interactions between adsorbed molecules. 4. Adsorption energy is **constant** for all sites. ### Derivation (Textbook-Standard KTU Style) **Step 1 — Define rates:** The rate of adsorption is proportional to: - The pressure (or concentration) of the adsorbate, $P$ (or $C_e$) - The fraction of surface **not yet covered**, $(1 - \theta)$ $$r_a = k_a \, P \, (1 - \theta)$$ The rate of desorption is proportional to the fraction of surface already covered, $\theta$: $$r_d = k_d \, \theta$$ **Step 2 — At dynamic equilibrium, $r_a = r_d$:** $$k_a \, P \, (1 - \theta) = k_d \, \theta$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $\theta$:** $$k_a \, P - k_a \, P \, \theta = k_d \, \theta$$ $$k_a \, P = \theta (k_d + k_a \, P)$$ $$\theta = \dfrac{k_a \, P}{k_d + k_a \, P} = \dfrac{(k_a/k_d) \, P}{1 + (k_a/k_d) \, P}$$ **Step 4 — Define the Langmuir constant $b = k_a / k_d$:** $$\theta = \dfrac{b \, P}{1 + b \, P}$$ **Step 5 — Express in terms of adsorption capacity $q_e$:** Since $\theta = q_e / q_m$, we substitute: $$\dfrac{q_e}{q_m} = \dfrac{b \, C_e}{1 + b \, C_e}$$ **Step 6 — Rearrange to the famous linear form (used for plotting experimental data):** $$\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m \, b} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Plotting Convention (KTU Practical):** Plot $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e}$ (y-axis) vs $C_e$ (x-axis) → Slope = $\dfrac{1}{q_m}$, Intercept = $\dfrac{1}{q_m b}$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Weber-Morris Intra-particle Diffusion Model The intra-particle diffusion model is a **phenomenological** model that identifies the rate-controlling step of adsorption. ### Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Start with Fick's Law for radial diffusion into a spherical particle:** $$\dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t} = D_s \left( \dfrac{\partial^2 q}{\partial r^2} + \dfrac{2}{r} \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial r} \right)$$ where $D_s$ is the surface diffusion coefficient (m²/s) and $r$ is the radial coordinate. **Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions:** - $q(r, 0) = 0$ (initially clean particle) - $q(R, t) = q_e$ at equilibrium (assumed constant surface concentration for short times) **Step 3 — For short times, $q_e \ll$ saturation, the solution simplifies to:** $$q_t = k_i \, t^{0.5} + C$$ where: -
