$k_i$ = intra-particle diffusion rate constant (mg·g⁻¹·min⁻⁰·⁵) - $C$ = intercept related to boundary layer thickness (mg/g) - $t^{0.5}$ = square root of time **Step 4 — Diagnostic interpretation:** - If the plot $q_t$ vs $t^{0.5}$ is **linear and passes through origin** ($C = 0$) → intra-particle diffusion is the sole rate-controlling step. - If the plot is **multi-linear** with two or three segments → multiple stages (external film diffusion followed by pore diffusion, then equilibrium). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The Weber-Morris plot is the **only direct visual tool** to diagnose whether adsorption is film-diffusion controlled or pore-diffusion controlled — frequently appearing as a 7-mark analytical question. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Langmuir Isotherm Parameter Determination **Problem:** A batch adsorption experiment gave the following equilibrium data for methylene blue dye on activated carbon at 30 °C. Determine the Langmuir parameters $q_m$ and $b$. | $C_e$ (mg/L) | $q_e$ (mg/g) | |---|---| | 1.0 | 2.50 | | 2.5 | 5.40 | | 5.0 | 9.10 | | 10.0 | 14.30 | | 20.0 | 20.00 | **Step 1 — Compute $C_e / q_e$ for the linearized Langmuir form:** | $C_e$ | $q_e$ | $C_e / q_e$ | |---|---|---| | 1.0 | 2.50 | 0.400 | | 2.5 | 5.40 | 0.463 | | 5.0 | 9.10 | 0.549 | | 10.0 | 14.30 | 0.699 | | 20.0 | 20.00 | 1.000 | **Step 2 — Perform linear regression of $C_e / q_e$ vs $C_e$:** Using least-squares (or graphical method): $$\text{Slope} \approx 0.0305, \quad \text{Intercept} \approx 0.385$$ **Step 3 — Compute Langmuir parameters:** $$q_m = \dfrac{1}{\text{Slope}} = \dfrac{1}{0.0305} \approx 32.79 \text{ mg/g}$$ $$b = \dfrac{\text{Slope}}{\text{Intercept}} = \dfrac{0.0305}{0.385} \approx 0.0792 \text{ L/mg}$$ **Step 4 — Predict adsorption at $C_e = 8$ mg/L:** $$q_e = \dfrac{q_m \, b \, C_e}{1 + b \, C_e} = \dfrac{32.79 \times 0.0792 \times 8}{1 + 0.0792 \times 8}$$ $$q_e = \dfrac{20.77}{1.6336} \approx 12.72 \text{ mg/g}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A $q_m$ of ≈ 33 mg/g classifies this activated carbon as a **moderate-capacity** adsorbent. Commercial GAC for industrial dye removal typically exhibits $q_m$ values in the range 200–400 mg/g for methylene blue. --- ## 3.4 Membrane Flux Calculation (Solution-Diffusion Model) **Problem:** An RO spiral wound element operates with feed inlet pressure 20 bar, outlet pressure 18 bar, and permeate pressure 1 bar. The osmotic pressure difference across the membrane is estimated at 1.5 bar. The water permeability constant is $L_P = 1.8 \times 10^{-3}$ m/(bar·h). Calculate the **average flux**. **Step 1 — Compute average transmembrane pressure:** $$\Delta P_{avg} = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm} = \dfrac{20 + 18}{2} - 1 = 18 \text{ bar}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Solution-Diffusion model:** $$J = L_P \cdot (\Delta P_{avg} - \Delta \pi)$$ $$J = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \times (18 - 1.5)$$ $$J = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 16.5$$ $$J = 2.97 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/h} = 29.7 \text{ L/m}^2/\text{h (LMH)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Check:** $1 \text{ m/h} = 1000 \text{ LMH}$ → $0.0297 \text{ m/h} = 29.7 \text{ LMH}$, which lies in the typical RO operating range of **15–30 LMH**. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Spiral Wound Membrane — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB FeedIn[FEED INLET
Raw Wastewater
High Pressure 10 to 80 bar] --> FeedChan[FEED CHANNEL
Axial Cross Flow
Across Feed Spacer] FeedChan --> SpacerTurb[TURBULENCE PROMOTER
Feed Spacer Net
d_h 0.7 to 1.1 mm] SpacerTurb --> MemSurf[MEMBRANE SURFACE
Polyamide TFC
Rejects Solutes] MemSurf --> PermPath[PERMEATE SPIRAL PATH
Tricot Carrier Fabric
Flows Radially Inward] PermPath --> CentTube[CENTRAL PERFORATED TUBE
Permeate Collection
Sumps Reject Free Water] CentTube --> PermOut[PERMEATE OUTLET
Treated Water to Storage] FeedChan --> ConcOut[CONCENTRATE EXIT
Brine / Retentate
Disposal or Recycle] MemSurf -.Fouling Layer.-> SpacerTurb style FeedIn fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#003366,color:#FFFFFF style MemSurf fill:#E94E77,stroke:#660000,color:#FFFFFF style CentTube fill:#F5A623,stroke:#663300,color:#FFFFFF style PermOut fill:#7ED321,stroke:#336600,color:#FFFFFF style ConcOut fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#330066,color:#FFFFFF ``` ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Conceptual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PV[PRESSURE VESSEL CYLINDRICAL HOUSING] direction LR subgraph E1[SWM ELEMENT 1] M1[Membrane Leaf 1] --- S1[Feed Spacer 0.85 mm] S1 --- M2[Membrane Leaf 2] P1[Permeate Carrier] CT1[Central Tube] end subgraph E2[SWM ELEMENT 2] M3[Membrane Leaf 3] --- S2[Feed Spacer 0.85 mm] S2 --- M4[Membrane Leaf 4] P2[Permeate Carrier] CT2[Central Tube] end E1 --> E2 end F1[FEED IN] --> E1 E2 --> B1[BRINE OUT] CT1 --> Pout[PERMEATE OUT] CT2 --> Pout style PV fill:#E8F4F8,stroke:#003366 style F1 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#003366,color:#FFFFFF style B1 fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#330066,color:#FFFFFF style Pout fill:#7ED321,stroke:#336600,color:#FFFFFF ``` ## 4.3 Adsorption Process — Mass Transfer Stages ```mermaid flowchart TB A[BULK SOLUTION
Wastewater with Pollutant] --> B[STAGE 1
FILM DIFFUSION
Through Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer] B --> C[STAGE 2
INTRA PARTICLE DIFFUSION
Through Pore Network of Adsorbent] C --> D[STAGE 3
SURFACE REACTION
Physical or Chemical Attachment] D --> E[ADSORBED STATE
Pollutant Held on Active Site] F[DRIVING FORCES] --> A F --> G[Concentration Gradient] F --> H[Van der Waals Forces] F --> I[Electrostatic Attraction] F --> J[Chemical Bond Formation] K[KEY ADSORBENTS] --> L[Granular Activated Carbon GAC] K --> M[Powdered Activated Carbon PAC] K --> N[Zeolites] K --> O[Activated Alumina] K --> P[Biochar] style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#003366,color:#FFFFFF style E fill:#7ED321,stroke:#336600,color:#FFFFFF style K fill:#F5A623,stroke:#663300,color:#FFFFFF ``` ## 4.4 Comparison — Physisorption vs Chemisorption (Block Decision Map) ```mermaid flowchart TB Start[ADSORPTION TYPE DETERMINATION] --> Q1{Bond Energy Range} Q1 -->|5 to 40 kJ per mol| PHY[PHYSISORPTION] Q1 -->|40 to 400 kJ per mol| CHE[CHEMISORPTION] PHY --> P1[Van der Waals Forces] PHY --> P2[Reversible] PHY --> P3[Multilayer Possible] PHY --> P4[Low Temperature Favoured] PHY --> P5[Example Dye on Activated Carbon] CHE --> C1[Covalent or Ionic Bonds] CHE --> C2[Often Irreversible] CHE --> C3[Strictly Monolayer] CHE --> C4[High Activation Energy] CHE --> C5[Example Oxygen on Metal Catalyst] style PHY fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#003366,color:#FFFFFF style CHE fill:#E94E77,stroke:#660000,color:#FFFFFF style P5 fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#330066,color:#FFFFFF style C5 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#663300,color:#FFFFFF ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Differentiate between physisorption and chemisorption with at least four points.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Physisorption | Chemisorption | |---|---|---| | **Bonding force** | Van der Waals (weak) | Covalent/ionic (strong) | | **Enthalpy of adsorption** | 5 – 40 kJ/mol | 40 – 400 kJ/mol | | **Activation energy** | Negligible | Significant | | **Layer formation** | Multilayer possible | Strictly monolayer | | **Reversibility** | Easily reversible | Often irreversible | | **Temperature effect** | Decreases with temperature (exothermic) | May increase with temperature | | **Specificity** | Non-specific | Highly specific to active sites | --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **List any three advantages of spiral wound membrane modules over other module configurations.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Highest packing density** (~800–1000 m²/m³), enabling compact plant footprint. 2. **Low capital cost per unit membrane area** due to standardized mass production. 3. **Scalable** through parallel pressure vessels; elements can be easily replaced in the field. 4. **Suitable for high-pressure RO/NF** applications up to 80 bar. 5. **Compatibility with cross-flow operation** minimizes concentration polarization. --- ## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Jul 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional diagram, explain the construction, working principle, and flow path of a **Spiral Wound Membrane (SWM) module** used in reverse osmosis treatment of industrial wastewater. **Model Answer:** **Construction (3 marks):** A spiral wound element is built as follows: 1. **Two membrane sheets** of polyamide thin-film composite (TFC) are folded in half, forming an envelope. Three edges are sealed with polyurethane adhesive. 2. A **permeate carrier** (porous tricot fabric) is placed inside the envelope, glued to the open fourth edge. 3. A **feed spacer** (diamond/ladder mesh, 28–45 mil) is placed on the outer side of the envelope to maintain an open flow channel. 4. The complete assembly is **spirally wound** around a **perforated central permeate tube** (typically PVC or ABS). 5. The whole element is wrapped in an **outer fiberglass shell** for mechanical strength. 6. **Brine seals** (rubber gaskets) are placed on the outer surface to direct feed flow and prevent bypass. **Working Principle & Flow Path (4 marks):** - **Feed water** enters the pressure vessel at high pressure (10–80 bar) and flows **axially** through the feed channel along the membrane surface (cross-flow). - Under **transmembrane pressure (TMP)**, water molecules and small solutes permeate through the semipermeable membrane. - **Permeate** flows **spirally** inward through the permeate carrier fabric, converging at the central perforated tube. - Permeate exits through the tube to storage. - **Concentrate (brine)** continues axially along the vessel and exits as retentate, carrying the rejected contaminants. - The element operates in **cross-flow** mode, which continually sweeps foulants off the membrane, reducing concentration polarization. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and typical applications** of spiral wound membrane modules in wastewater treatment. **Model Answer:** **Advantages (2 marks):** - Highest packing density among commercial module types (800–1000 m²/m³) - Economical: lowest cost per m² of membrane - Compact footprint — ideal for space-constrained urban STPs - Standardized elements (4-inch × 40-inch) allow easy field replacement - Efficient cross-flow operation minimizes fouling - Compatible with high-pressure RO/NF duties (up to 80 bar) - Industry-proven, with extensive operational data and vendor support **Limitations (2 marks):** - **Highly susceptible to fouling** by particulates, oil, organics → requires pretreatment (Silt Density Index SDI < 5) - **No backflush capability** in standard RO configuration → difficult to clean internally - **High feed-side pressure drop** in long vessels with multiple elements - **Concentration polarization** can reduce flux and salt rejection - **Telescoping** can occur if brine seals fail under high differential pressure **Typical Applications (3 marks):** - **Seawater and brackish water desalination** for potable water supply - **Tertiary wastewater treatment** for water reuse in industry (e.g., cooling tower make-up) - **Industrial process water** in pharmaceuticals, food & beverage, semiconductor - **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** systems as a pre-concentration step - **Removal of dissolved organics, heavy metals, nitrates, fluorides** from contaminated groundwater - **Concentration of landfill leachate** prior to evaporation/crystallization --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** What is adsorption? Explain the **physical and chemical adsorption** processes. Discuss the **factors affecting adsorption** in wastewater treatment systems. **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** Adsorption is a surface-based mass-transfer process in which solute molecules (adsorbate) from a fluid phase accumulate and become concentrated on the surface of a solid (adsorbent) through physical or chemical interactions. **Physical Adsorption (Physisorption) (2 marks):** - Caused by **Van der Waals forces** between adsorbate and adsorbent. - Low enthalpy of adsorption (5–40 kJ/mol). - **Reversible** in nature; desorption is easy. - May form **multilayer** coverage. - **Non-specific**; adsorbate not attached to a particular site. - **Exothermic** → capacity decreases with increasing temperature. - **No activation energy** required. - *Example:* Methylene blue dye on activated carbon. **Chemical Adsorption (Chemisorption) (2 marks):** - Involves formation of **covalent or ionic bonds** with surface atoms. - High enthalpy of adsorption (40–400 kJ/mol). - Often **irreversible**; desorption is difficult. - **Strictly monolayer** (one molecule per active site). - **Highly specific** to particular surface functional groups. - Requires significant **activation energy**; rate increases with temperature. - *Example:* Oxygen chemisorption on platinum catalyst; heavy metal ions (Pb²⁺) on thiol-functionalized silica. **Factors Affecting Adsorption (2 marks):** 1. **Surface area of adsorbent** — higher BET surface area (m²/g) → higher capacity. 2. **Pore size distribution** — micropores (<2 nm) provide maximum surface area; macropores (>50 nm) aid transport. 3. **Temperature** — exothermic physisorption is favoured at low T. 4. **pH of solution** — affects adsorbate ionization and adsorbent surface charge. 5. **Initial concentration** of adsorbate — increases capacity until monolayer saturation. 6. **Contact time** — must reach equilibrium for full utilization of capacity. 7. **Nature of adsorbate** — polarity, molecular size, solubility all affect affinity. 8. **Competing species** — co-solutes compete for the same active sites. 9. **Agitation / mixing intensity** — enhances film diffusion. **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** equation from first principles. The following data were obtained for the adsorption of a dye on activated carbon at 30 °C. Determine the Langmuir constants $q_m$ and $b$. | $C_e$ (mg/L) | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | $q_e$ (mg/g) | 4.0 | 6.7 | 8.6 | 10.0 | 11.1 | **Model Answer:** **Derivation (4 marks):** *(Refer to SECTION 3.1 for the full step-by-step derivation. The final linear form is:)* $$\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m \, b} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$$ **Data Reduction (2 marks):** | $C_e$ | $q_e$ | $C_e / q_e$ | |---|---|---| | 2 | 4.0 | 0.500 | | 4 | 6.7 | 0.597 | | 6 | 8.6 | 0.698 | | 8 | 10.0 | 0.800 | | 10 | 11.1 | 0.901 | **Linear regression of $C_e / q_e$ (y) vs $C_e$ (x):** $$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{n \Sigma xy - \Sigma x \Sigma y}{n \Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2}$$ With $n = 5$: - $\Sigma x = 30$, $\Sigma y = 3.496$ - $\Sigma x^2 = 220$, $\Sigma xy = 24.498$ $$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{5 \times 24.498 - 30 \times 3.496}{5 \times 220 - 30^2} = \dfrac{122.49 - 104.88}{1100 - 900} = \dfrac{17.61}{200} = 0.0880$$ $$\text{Intercept} = \dfrac{\Sigma y - \text{Slope} \times \Sigma x}{n} = \dfrac{3.496 - 0.0880 \times 30}{5} = \dfrac{0.856}{5} = 0.1712$$ **Compute Constants (1 mark):** $$q_m = \dfrac{1}{\text{Slope}} = \dfrac{1}{0.0880} \approx 11.36 \text{ mg/g}$$ $$b = \dfrac{\text{Slope}}{\text{Intercept}} = \dfrac{0.0880}{0.1712} \approx 0.514 \text{ L/mg}$$ **Conclusion:** The Langmuir monolayer capacity of this activated carbon for the dye is **11.36 mg/g**, with an affinity constant $b = 0.514$ L/mg. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in SWM & Adsorption Questions:** > 1. **Confusing "adsorption" with "absorption"** — many students write them interchangeably. Adsorption is **surface** phenomenon; absorption is **bulk penetration**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for misuse. > 2. **Forgetting the permeate carrier in SWM diagrams** — many sketches omit this critical component. Always show the **tricot fabric layer** between the two membrane sheets. > 3. **Wrong units in Langmuir derivation** — $q_m$ must be in **mg/g** and $b$ in **L/mg**; mixing units in $C_e/q_e$ plot causes dimensional errors. Examiners verify units. > 4. **Confusing $R_L$ with $R$ (% removal)** — $R_L$ is the Langmuir **separation factor** (dimensionless, 0 to 1 for favorable adsorption), whereas $R$ is the **percentage removal**. > 5. **Omitting "Anti-Telescoping Device" (ATD)** — frequently forgotten in SWM component lists; carry 0.5–1 mark weightage. > 6. **Not stating assumptions of Langmuir isotherm** — examiners expect all four (monolayer, identical sites, no lateral interaction, constant energy) to be explicitly listed. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** ### Spiral Wound Membranes - ☐ **Geometry:** Membrane envelope spirally wound around a central perforated permeate tube - ☐ **Key Components:** Membrane leaves, feed spacer, permeate carrier, central tube, ATD, brine seals, outer shell - ☐ **Flow Pattern:** Feed = axial cross-flow; Permeate = spiral radial inward; Concentrate = axial exit - ☐ **Packing Density:** 800 – 1000 m²/m³ (highest among all module types) - ☐ **Typical Operating Pressure:** RO = 10 – 80 bar; NF = 5 – 30 bar; UF = 1 – 5 bar - ☐ **Standard Element Size:** 8-inch diameter × 40-inch length (commercial) - ☐ **Critical Pretreatment Requirement:** SDI < 5 to prevent fouling - ☐ **Major Limitation:** No backflush capability → requires chemical cleaning - ☐ **Flux Equation:** $J = L_P (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ - ☐ **TMP Formula:** $TMP = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{permeate}$ - ☐ **Applications:** Desalination, tertiary wastewater reuse, ZLD pre-concentration ### Adsorption - ☐ **Definition:** Surface accumulation of solutes on a solid (distinct from absorption) - ☐ **Physisorption:** Van der Waals, 5–40 kJ/mol, reversible, multilayer, low T favourable - ☐ **Chemisorption:** Covalent/ionic, 40–400 kJ/mol, often irreversible, strictly monolayer - ☐ **Equilibrium Capacity:** $q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m}$ (mg/g) - ☐ **Percentage Removal:** $R(\%) = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e)}{C_0} \times 100$ - ☐ **Langmuir Isotherm** (Monolayer, homogeneous sites): Linear form $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m b} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$ - ☐ **Freundlich Isotherm** (Heterogeneous, empirical): Linear form $\log q_e = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \log C_e$ - ☐ **BET Isotherm** (Multilayer, BET surface area measurement) - ☐ **Weber-Morris Plot** $q_t$ vs $t^{0.5}$ → identifies intra-particle diffusion as rate-controlling step - ☐ **Favorability Check:** $R_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + b C_0}$; **0 < R_L < 1 → favorable adsorption** - ☐ **Most Common Adsorbent in Wastewater:** Granular Activated Carbon (GAC) — surface area 500 – 1500 m²/g - ☐ **Mass Transfer Stages:** Film diffusion → Intra-particle diffusion → Surface reaction ### Cross-Topic Integration - ☐ SWM + Adsorption often combined in **tertiary treatment trains** of STPs (Biological → Adsorption (GAC) → Membrane Polishing (RO)) - ☐ Both unit operations remove **refractory, non-biodegradable organics** that escape biological treatment - ☐ Key design outputs to memorize: $q_m$ (mg/g), $J$ (LMH), $b$ (L/mg), packing density (m²/m³) - ☐ Always include a **Mermaid/Block diagram** in your answer for full marks (minimum 1–2 marks reserved for neat sketch)

topics.title: Fundamentals of Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Adsorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adsorption** is a surface phenomenon in which molecules, atoms, or ions of a substance (the **adsorbate**) accumulate on the surface of a solid or liquid (the **adsorbent**), forming a thin film or molecular layer through physical or chemical interactions, as opposed to absorption where the species penetrate into the bulk of the material.

In the context of **biological wastewater treatment (PEBBT526)**, adsorption is a critical **physicochemical tertiary treatment process** used to remove dissolved contaminants—such as refractory organics, colour, odour, heavy metals, and micropollutants—that are resistant to conventional biological degradation.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Adsorbate** | The substance (pollutant) that gets accumulated on the surface |
| **Adsorbent** | The solid surface material (e.g., activated carbon) on which adsorption occurs |
| **Sorption** | Umbrella term encompassing both adsorption and absorption |
| **Desorption** | Reverse process where adsorbate detaches from the adsorbent surface |
| **Equilibrium** | State when the rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Magnet and Iron Filings:**
> Imagine sprinkling iron filings onto a surface where a magnet is hidden underneath. The iron filings (the **adsorbate**/pollutant) cling to specific points on the surface (the **adsorbent**/activated carbon) because of attractive forces. They don't penetrate deep into the material—they just stick to the surface. As you keep adding iron filings, the surface eventually becomes "saturated" and cannot hold any more, similar to a carbon column reaching its **breakthrough point** in a treatment plant.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used for characterizing adsorbents in wastewater engineering:

- **Specific Surface Area (SSA):** typically **500–1500 m²/g** for activated carbon, measured by BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller) analysis
- **Pore Size Distribution:** micropores (< 2 nm), mesopores (2–50 nm), macropores (> 50 nm)
- **Iodine Number:** **mg of iodine adsorbed per gram of carbon**, a measure of microporosity
- **Methylene Blue Number:** measure of mesoporosity, typically **100–250 mg/g**
- **Bulk Density:** approximately **0.4–0.5 g/cm³** for granular activated carbon (GAC)
- **Ash Content:** typically **< 10%** for high-grade activated carbon
- **Operating pH range:** **6–9** for most wastewater adsorption applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes adsorption as a **polishing step** for biologically treated effluent where residual COD, colour, and toxic micropollutants must be removed to meet discharge standards (e.g., **COD < 50 mg/L** for inland discharge under Indian norms).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Adsorption

Adsorption is classified into two principal types based on the nature of the bonding force between adsorbate and adsorbent:

#### 2.1.1 Physical Adsorption (Physisorption)

- **Binding force:** Weak **van der Waals forces** (London dispersion forces)
- **Energy of activation:** Low (**5–40 kJ/mol**)
- **Reversibility:** Highly reversible; adsorbate can be desorbed by lowering pressure or raising temperature
- **Specificity:** Non-specific; can occur on any surface
- **Layers:** Forms **multilayers** under favourable conditions
- **Temperature effect:** Exothermic process; adsorption **decreases** with rising temperature
- **Effect on adsorbate:** No significant change in electronic structure

#### 2.1.2 Chemical Adsorption (Chemisorption)

- **Binding force:** Strong **covalent or ionic chemical bonds**
- **Energy of activation:** High (**40–400 kJ/mol**)
- **Reversibility:** Generally **irreversible**; difficult to desorb
- **Specificity:** Highly specific; requires particular surface chemistry
- **Layers:** Forms **monolayer** only
- **Temperature effect:** Initially increases with temperature (activation energy barrier), then decreases
- **Effect on adsorbate:** May cause dissociation or rearrangement of molecules

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In wastewater treatment, **physisorption** dominates on activated carbon because of its enormous surface area and weak universal interactions, which allow easy regeneration of the spent carbon by steam or thermal treatment.

### 2.2 The Adsorption Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The mechanism of adsorption in a liquid-phase system (wastewater contactor) proceeds in three sequential stages:

1. **Film Diffusion (External Mass Transfer):**
   - The dissolved adsorbate molecules migrate from the bulk liquid through the **hydrodynamic boundary layer** surrounding the adsorbent particle.
   - Driven by concentration gradient; rate depends on **mixing intensity (G value)** and particle Reynolds number.

2. **Pore Diffusion (Internal Mass Transfer):**
   - The adsorbate penetrates into the pores of the adsorbent via molecular diffusion and surface diffusion.
   - Often the **rate-limiting step** for microporous materials like activated carbon.

3. **Surface Reaction (Adsorption onto Active Sites):**
   - The adsorbate binds to an available **active site** on the internal pore surface.
   - Characterized by the **activation energy of adsorption** ($E_a$).

### 2.3 Adsorption Capacity ($q_e$)

The amount of adsorbate taken up per unit mass of adsorbent at equilibrium is the central engineering design parameter:

$$
q_e = \frac{(C_0 - C_e) \cdot V}{m}
$$

Where:
- $q_e$ = equilibrium adsorption capacity (mg/g)
- $C_0$ = initial adsorbate concentration (mg/L)
- $C_e$ = equilibrium adsorbate concentration (mg/L)
- $V$ = volume of wastewater solution (L)
- $m$ = mass of dry adsorbent (g)

### 2.4 Adsorption Isotherms

An **adsorption isotherm** is the relationship between the equilibrium adsorbate concentration in the bulk ($C_e$) and the amount adsorbed on the surface ($q_e$) at a **constant temperature**. The three classical models are:

#### 2.4.1 Langmuir Isotherm (1918)

Assumes:
- **Monolayer coverage** on a homogeneous surface
- Finite number of identical adsorption sites
- No interaction between adsorbed molecules

$$
q_e = \frac{q_{\max} \cdot K_L \cdot C_e}{1 + K_L \cdot C_e}
$$

The **linear form** used for parameter estimation:

$$
\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_{\max} \cdot K_L} + \frac{C_e}{q_{\max}}
$$

- $q_{\max}$ = maximum monolayer capacity (mg/g)
- $K_L$ = Langmuir constant related to binding energy (L/mg)

The **dimensionless separation factor** $R_L$ predicts isotherm shape:

$$
R_L = \frac{1}{1 + K_L \cdot C_0}
$$

| $R_L$ value | Isotherm nature |
|-------------|-----------------|
| $R_L > 1$ | Unfavourable |
| $R_L = 1$ | Linear |
| $0 < R_L < 1$ | Favourable |
| $R_L = 0$ | Irreversible |

#### 2.4.2 Freundlich Isotherm (1906)

An **empirical** model accounting for heterogeneous surfaces and multilayer adsorption:

$$
q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}
$$

Linear form (for log–log plot):

$$
\log q_e = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n} \log C_e
$$

- $K_F$ = Freundlich capacity factor $(mg/g)(L/mg)^{1/n}$
- $1/n$ = heterogeneity factor (intensity of adsorption)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Favourable adsorption condition:** $1/n$ must be between **0 and 1**. Typically, $1/n < 0.5$ indicates highly favourable adsorption.

#### 2.4.3 BET Isotherm (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller, 1938)

Extends Langmuir to **multilayer adsorption**:

$$
q_e = \frac{q_{\max} \cdot K_B \cdot C_e}{(C_s - C_e)[1 + (K_B - 1)(C_e / C_s)]}
$$

- $C_s$ = saturation concentration of adsorbate
- $K_B$ = BET constant relating to heat of adsorption

Used primarily to compute the **specific surface area (SSA)** of adsorbents.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every high-yield equation for the KTU examination. Memorize the symbols and units.**

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Units |
|---|--------------------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Adsorption capacity | $q_e = (C_0 - C_e)V/m$ | mg/g |
| 2 | Langmuir isotherm | $q_e = q_{\max} K_L C_e / (1 + K_L C_e)$ | mg/g |
| 3 | Langmuir linear | $C_e/q_e = 1/(q_{\max} K_L) + C_e/q_{\max}$ | g/L |
| 4 | Separation factor | $R_L = 1/(1 + K_L C_0)$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Freundlich isotherm | $q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$ | mg/g |
| 6 | Freundlich linear | $\log q_e = \log K_F + (1/n) \log C_e$ | — |
| 7 | BET isotherm | $q_e = q_{\max} K_B C_e / [(C_s - C_e)(1 + (K_B-1) C_e/C_s)]$ | mg/g |
| 8 | SSA from BET | $SSA = (N_A \cdot a_m \cdot V_m)/M$ | m²/g |
| 9 | Removal efficiency | $\%R = [(C_0 - C_e)/C_0] \times 100$ | % |
| 10 | Pseudo-first-order (Lagergren) | $\log(q_e - q_t) = \log q_e - k_1 t/2.303$ | 1/min |
| 11 | Pseudo-second-order | $t/q_t = 1/(k_2 q_e^2) + t/q_e$ | g/(mg·min) |
| 12 | Intra-particle diffusion | $q_t = k_i t^{0.5} + C$ | mg/g |

> **Note on units of $V_m$ (BET):** $V_m$ is the monolayer volume of gas adsorbed (cm³/g STP) and $a_m$ is the cross-sectional area of one adsorbate molecule (typically **0.162 nm²** for N₂ at 77 K).

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Wastewater Engineering

Adsorption is deployed across multiple wastewater treatment scenarios:

- **Tertiary Polishing:** Removal of residual COD, BOD, colour, and odour from secondary-treated effluent
- **Industrial Pre-treatment:** Stripping of toxic organics (phenols, pesticides, solvents) before biological units
- **Heavy Metal Recovery:** Adsorption of Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, Hg²⁺, Cr(VI) on activated carbon, biosorbents, or zeolites
- **Micropollutant Removal:** Pharmaceutical residues, endocrine disruptors, PFAS
- **Carbon Column Breakthrough:** A pilot or full-scale adsorption column produces a characteristic **S-shaped breakthrough curve** of $C_t/C_0$ vs. time, used to design the **Empty Bed Contact Time (EBCT)** and carbon usage rate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Adsorption Capacity from Mass Balance

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical objective:** This derivation is the foundation of every batch adsorption problem in the KTU exam. Master the algebraic manipulation.

**Step 1 — Statement of mass balance principle:**

The mass of adsorbate removed from the liquid phase must equal the mass accumulated on the solid adsorbent.

$$
\text{Mass removed from liquid} = \text{Mass adsorbed on solid}
$$

**Step 2 — Express mass removed from liquid in concentration terms:**

The change in concentration $\Delta C$ multiplied by solution volume $V$ gives the mass removed.

$$
\text{Mass removed} = (C_0 - C_e) \cdot V
$$

**Step 3 — Express mass adsorbed on solid in terms of $q_e$:**

By definition, $q_e$ is the mass of adsorbate per unit mass of dry adsorbent.

$$
\text{Mass adsorbed} = q_e \cdot m
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions:**

$$
(C_0 - C_e) \cdot V = q_e \cdot m
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the unknown $q_e$:**

$$
q_e = \frac{(C_0 - C_e) \cdot V}{m}
$$

**Result (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{\,q_e = \frac{(C_0 - C_e)\,V}{m}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Unit verification:**

$$
q_e = \frac{(\text{mg/L})(\text{L})}{\text{g}} = \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{g}} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 7 — Worked numerical example:**

> Given: $C_0 = 100$ mg/L, $C_e = 20$ mg/L, $V = 1$ L, $m = 0.5$ g

$$
q_e = \frac{(100 - 20)(1)}{0.5} = \frac{80}{0.5} = 160 \text{ mg/g}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Linear Langmuir Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the non-linear Langmuir form:**

$$
q_e = \frac{q_{\max} K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}
$$

**Step 2 — Invert both sides:**

$$
\frac{1}{q_e} = \frac{1 + K_L C_e}{q_{\max} K_L C_e} = \frac{1}{q_{\max} K_L C_e} + \frac{K_L C_e}{q_{\max} K_L C_e}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the right-hand side:**

$$
\frac{1}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_{\max} K_L} \cdot \frac{1}{C_e} + \frac{1}{q_{\max}}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $C_e$:**

$$
\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_{\max} K_L} + \frac{C_e}{q_{\max}}
$$

**Result (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{\,\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_{\max} K_L} + \dfrac{1}{q_{\max}}\,C_e\,}
$$

This is in the form $Y = b + mX$, where plotting $C_e/q_e$ (Y-axis) versus $C_e$ (X-axis) yields a straight line with:
- **Slope** $= 1/q_{\max}$ → gives maximum capacity
- **Intercept** $= 1/(q_{\max} K_L)$ → gives Langmuir constant

**Step 5 — Numerical check (Langmuir parameters from data):**

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | $q_e$ (mg/g) | $C_e/q_e$ (g/L) |
|--------------|--------------|------------------|
| 1.0 | 2.5 | 0.400 |
| 2.0 | 4.0 | 0.500 |
| 4.0 | 5.7 | 0.702 |
| 8.0 | 7.0 | 1.143 |

Linear regression of the last column on the first column gives slope $\approx 0.104$ g/mg and intercept $\approx 0.275$ g/L.

Therefore:
- $q_{\max} = 1/\text{slope} = 1/0.104 \approx 9.6$ mg/g
- $K_L = \text{slope}/\text{intercept} = 0.104/0.275 \approx 0.378$ L/mg

### 3.3 Derivation: Linear Freundlich Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the power-law form:**

$$
q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}
$$

**Step 2 — Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides:**

$$
\log q_e = \log(K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n})
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the logarithm product rule $\log(AB) = \log A + \log B$:**

$$
\log q_e = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n} \log C_e
$$

**Result (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{\,\log q_e = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n}\log C_e\,}
$$

This is in the form $Y = b + mX$, where:
- **Slope** $= 1/n$ → intensity parameter
- **Intercept** $= \log K_F$ → capacity parameter

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Isotherm

| Scenario | Recommended Isotherm | Reasoning |
|----------|----------------------|-----------|
| Single-solute, low concentration, monolayer | **Langmuir** | Assumes finite identical sites |
| Multi-component, heterogeneous surface, wide concentration range | **Freundlich** | Empirical, fits diverse data |
| Gas-phase surface area determination | **BET** | Accounts for multilayer formation |
| Real wastewater with complex organics | **Freundlich** + site-energy distribution | More flexible; Langmuir often fails |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** For typical KTU numerical problems involving single-component dye or phenol adsorption on activated carbon from aqueous solution, the **Freundlich isotherm** is most commonly tested because of its simplicity and wide applicability.

### 3.5 Derivation: Removal Efficiency

**Step 1 — Definition:**

Removal efficiency is the fraction of initial pollutant removed by the adsorbent, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\%R = \frac{\text{Mass removed}}{\text{Initial mass}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute mass terms:**

$$
\%R = \frac{(C_0 - C_e) \cdot V}{C_0 \cdot V} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the common factor $V$:**

$$
\%R = \frac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100
$$

**Result (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{\,\%R = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100\,}
$$

**Worked example:** If $C_0 = 200$ mg/L and $C_e = 40$ mg/L:

$$
\%R = \frac{200 - 40}{200} \times 100 = \frac{160}{200} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Factors Affecting Adsorption Capacity

| Factor | Effect on Adsorption | Engineering Implication |
|--------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| **Surface area** of adsorbent | ↑ SSA → ↑ capacity | Choose high-grade activated carbon (800–1500 m²/g) |
| **Pore size distribution** | Micropores favour small molecules; mesopores favour larger organics | Match pore size to target pollutant |
| **Temperature** | Exothermic physisorption → capacity ↓ as $T$ ↑ | Operate at ambient or slightly low $T$ |
| **pH** | Affects ionization of adsorbate and surface charge | Optimize pH to neutral/acidic for most organics; alkaline for metal hydroxides |
| **Initial concentration** | Higher $C_0$ → higher $q_e$ but lower %R | Trade-off studied via isotherm |
| **Contact time** | $q_e$ increases with time until equilibrium | Design EBCT = 15–30 min for GAC columns |
| **Competing solutes** | Multi-solute systems reduce capacity of each | Pre-treat to remove bulk COD before carbon |
| **Adsorbent dosage** | Higher dosage → higher %R but lower $q_e$ (under-loaded sites) | Optimize dose via jar tests |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define the term **adsorption**. Differentiate between **physisorption** and **chemisorption** in tabular form.

**Model Answer:**

> **Adsorption** is a surface phenomenon in which molecules of a substance (adsorbate) accumulate on the surface of a solid (adsorbent), forming a molecular film, without penetrating into the bulk.

| Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| Bonding | van der Waals (weak) | Covalent / ionic (strong) |
| Energy | 5–40 kJ/mol | 40–400 kJ/mol |
| Reversibility | Reversible | Generally irreversible |
| Specificity | Non-specific | Highly specific |
| Layers | Multilayer possible | Monolayer only |
| Temperature effect | Decreases with $T$ ↑ | May increase initially |

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2.** State the **Freundlich adsorption isotherm**. What does the exponent $1/n$ signify? Under what condition is the adsorption said to be **favourable**?

**Model Answer:**

The Freundlich isotherm is given by:

$$
q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}
$$

The exponent $1/n$ is the **heterogeneity factor** that indicates the intensity of adsorption and the surface heterogeneity. Adsorption is said to be **favourable** when:

$$
0 < \frac{1}{n} < 1
$$

A value of $1/n < 0.5$ is generally indicative of highly favourable adsorption. When $1/n = 1$, the isotherm becomes linear; when $1/n > 1$, adsorption is unfavourable.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not confuse $q_e$ (mg/g) with $C_e$ (mg/L)** — these have different units and meanings, and mixing them up is the most common mark-losing mistake.
> - **Always include units** in numerical answers. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for unitless final values.
> - For isotherm problems, **state the assumptions** of the model used (e.g., monolayer coverage, homogeneous surface for Langmuir). Marks are allotted for these statements.
> - When computing $R_L$, **show the substitution step** explicitly; do not jump from formula to answer.
> - In kinetic problems, label axes properly (independent variable on X-axis) and **state the model name** clearly.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **linear form of the Langmuir adsorption isotherm** starting from the non-linear form. Explain the physical significance of the constants $q_{\max}$ and $K_L$. Compute the **dimensionless separation factor $R_L$** for an initial concentration $C_0 = 50$ mg/L, given $K_L = 0.2$ L/mg, and comment on the favourability of adsorption. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A wastewater sample with an initial dye concentration of 200 mg/L is treated with **2 g of activated carbon** in 1 L of solution. The equilibrium concentration after 24 hours is 25 mg/L. Calculate:
  (i) The equilibrium adsorption capacity $q_e$
  (ii) The percentage removal of dye
  (iii) The mass of dye adsorbed on the carbon **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Langmuir Derivation:**

Starting from the non-linear form:

$$
q_e = \frac{q_{\max} K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}
$$

Take the reciprocal:

$$
\frac{1}{q_e} = \frac{1 + K_L C_e}{q_{\max} K_L C_e} = \frac{1}{q_{\max} K_L C_e} + \frac{1}{q_{\max}}
$$

Multiply by $C_e$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_{\max} K_L} + \frac{1}{q_{\max}} C_e}
$$

This is of the form $Y = b + mX$, used to extract $q_{\max}$ from the slope and $K_L$ from the intercept.

**Significance of constants:**
- $q_{\max}$ = maximum monolayer adsorption capacity (mg/g), representing the total number of available active sites per unit mass.
- $K_L$ = Langmuir constant (L/mg) related to the **binding energy** of adsorption; higher $K_L$ indicates stronger affinity between adsorbate and adsorbent.

**Separation factor computation:**

$$
R_L = \frac{1}{1 + K_L C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + (0.2)(50)} = \frac{1}{1 + 10} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 0.091
$$

Since $0 < R_L < 1$, adsorption is **favourable**.

**(b) Numerical problem:**

Given: $C_0 = 200$ mg/L, $C_e = 25$ mg/L, $V = 1$ L, $m = 2$ g.

**(i) Equilibrium adsorption capacity:**

$$
q_e = \frac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m} = \frac{(200 - 25)(1)}{2} = \frac{175}{2} = 87.5 \text{ mg/g}
$$

**(ii) Percentage removal:**

$$
\%R = \frac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100 = \frac{200 - 25}{200} \times 100 = \frac{175}{200} \times 100 = 87.5\%
$$

**(iii) Mass of dye adsorbed on carbon:**

$$
m_{\text{adsorbed}} = (C_0 - C_e) V = 175 \text{ mg} = 0.175 \text{ g}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What are the **assumptions of the Freundlich adsorption isotherm**? Derive its linear form and explain how the constants $K_F$ and $1/n$ are determined graphically. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** In a batch adsorption study, the following equilibrium data were obtained at 25 °C for the adsorption of phenol on activated carbon:

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | 0.5 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 4.0 | 8.0 |
|--------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| $q_e$ (mg/g) | 1.20 | 1.80 | 2.65 | 3.55 | 4.60 |

Determine the **Freundlich constants** $K_F$ and $1/n$ using a log–log plot. Comment on the **favourability** of adsorption. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Freundlich Assumptions and Derivation:**

**Assumptions of Freundlich isotherm:**
1. The adsorbent surface is **heterogeneous** with non-uniform distribution of adsorption sites.
2. The heat of adsorption is **not constant** but varies with surface coverage.
3. **Multilayer adsorption** can occur.
4. There is **no fixed monolayer capacity** ($q_{\max}$ is not defined).
5. Applicable over a **limited concentration range**.

**Derivation of linear form:**

Starting from:

$$
q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}
$$

Take $\log_{10}$:

$$
\boxed{\log q_e = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n} \log C_e}
$$

Plot of $\log q_e$ (Y-axis) versus $\log C_e$ (X-axis) yields:
- **Slope** $= 1/n$ (heterogeneity factor)
- **Intercept** $= \log K_F$ → $K_F = 10^{\text{intercept}}$

**(b) Numerical computation:**

| $C_e$ | $q_e$ | $\log C_e$ | $\log q_e$ |
|-------|-------|------------|------------|
| 0.5 | 1.20 | -0.301 | 0.079 |
| 1.0 | 1.80 | 0.000 | 0.255 |
| 2.0 | 2.65 | 0.301 | 0.423 |
| 4.0 | 3.55 | 0.602 | 0.550 |
| 8.0 | 4.60 | 0.903 | 0.663 |

Performing linear regression of $\log q_e$ on $\log C_e$:

- Slope $\approx 0.48$
- Intercept $\approx 0.255$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{1}{n} = 0.48 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \approx 2.08
$$

$$
\log K_F = 0.255 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_F = 10^{0.255} \approx 1.80 \text{ (mg/g)(L/mg)}^{1/n}
$$

**Final answer:**

$$
\boxed{q_e = 1.80 \cdot C_e^{0.48}}
$$

**Comment on favourability:** Since $0 < 1/n = 0.48 < 1$ (and indeed $< 0.5$), the adsorption of phenol on this activated carbon is **highly favourable**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Adsorption Fundamentals (PEBBT526 Module 3)**

- ☐ **Adsorption vs. absorption:** surface phenomenon vs. bulk penetration
- ☐ **Adsorbate = pollutant**; **Adsorbent = solid surface** (e.g., activated carbon)
- ☐ **Physisorption:** weak (5–40 kJ/mol), reversible, multilayer, non-specific
- ☐ **Chemisorption:** strong (40–400 kJ/mol), largely irreversible, monolayer, specific
- ☐ **Activated carbon** is the most common wastewater adsorbent, SSA = **500–1500 m²/g**
- ☐ **Three stages:** film diffusion → pore diffusion → surface reaction
- ☐ **Master the three isotherm equations** and their linear forms:
  - Langmuir: $q_e = q_{\max} K_L C_e / (1 + K_L C_e)$
  - Freundlich: $q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$
  - BET: multilayer model for SSA determination
- ☐ **$q_e$ formula:** $q_e = (C_0 - C_e) V / m$ — units are **mg/g**
- ☐ **Langmuir plot:** $C_e/q_e$ vs. $C_e$ → slope $= 1/q_{\max}$
- ☐ **Freundlich plot:** $\log q_e$ vs. $\log C_e$ → slope $= 1/n$
- ☐ **Favourability conditions:** $0 < R_L < 1$ (Langmuir); $0 < 1/n < 1$ (Freundlich)
- ☐ **Removal efficiency:** $\%R = [(C_0 - C_e)/C_0] \times 100$
- ☐ **Factors affecting adsorption:** surface area, pore size, temperature, pH, contact time, initial concentration, dosage
- ☐ **Pseudosecond-order kinetics** is generally the best fit for wastewater adsorption systems
- ☐ **Remember units** in every numerical answer — KTU examiners check this
- ☐ **EBCT (Empty Bed Contact Time):** typically **15–30 min** for GAC columns
- ☐ **Regeneration:** spent carbon is reactivated by steam or thermal treatment at **800–900 °C**
- ☐ **Application hierarchy:** GAC for organics, ion-exchange resins for metals, biosorbents for low-cost alternatives
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adsorbent:** An adsorbent is a solid, highly porous material possessing a large internal surface area and specific surface chemistry that enables it to selectively accumulate (adsorb) molecules, ions, or atoms of a fluid (gas or liquid) onto its surface without changing the identity of the adsorbate. In the context of **biological wastewater treatment**, adsorbents are predominantly employed as a **tertiary (polishing) step** to remove recalcitrant organic pollutants, residual colour, odorous compounds, heavy metals, and micro-pollutants that are refractory to conventional secondary biological treatment.

The effectiveness of an adsorbent is governed by three fundamental intrinsic properties:

1. **Specific Surface Area (SSA)** — typically **500–3000 m²/g** for high-performance carbons. Measured using the **Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET)** method.
2. **Pore Structure** — classified by **IUPAC** into *micropores* (< 2 nm), *mesopores* (2–50 nm), and *macropores* (> 50 nm).
3. **Surface Chemistry** — determined by functional groups (e.g., –OH, –COOH, C=O) that govern polarity, ion-exchange capacity, and hydrophobicity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Microscopic Coral Reef" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine the adsorbent particle as a **tiny coral reef** dropped into a polluted pond. The reef has thousands of caves, tunnels, and grooves (these are the *pores*). As polluted water washes through the reef, dissolved contaminants (dyes, phenols, pesticides, heavy metals) swim into these caves and **stick** to the walls, while clean water flows out. The "stickiness" of the cave walls (the *surface chemistry*) determines which pollutants are trapped. A larger reef (higher *surface area*) catches more pollutants before it becomes "saturated" and needs regeneration — analogous to cleaning the reef with hot steam or chemicals to release the trapped pollutants.

This analogy underpins the entire engineering logic of **adsorption columns (packed bed reactors)** used downstream of biological treatment units like the *Activated Sludge Process* or *Trickling Filters*.

## 1.3 Why Adsorbents are Critical in BWT (Biological Wastewater Treatment)

Biological processes excel at degrading **biodegradable organics** (BOD/COD), but they fail against:
- Synthetic organics (pesticides, pharmaceuticals, endocrine disruptors)
- Residual colour from textile/dye industries
- Heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, Hg²⁺, As³⁺)
- Refractory dissolved organics measured as **COD after biological treatment (residual COD)**

Adsorbents bridge this gap through **physi-sorption** (van der Waals forces) and **chemi-sorption** (chemical bonding/ion exchange).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Adsorbents — Hierarchical Logic

Adsorbents are classified by their **origin**, **chemical nature**, and **structural morphology**.

### A. Carbon-Based Adsorbents
1. **Activated Carbon (AC)** — the workhorse of water treatment
   - *Granular Activated Carbon (GAC)*: particle size 0.5–2.5 mm; used in packed columns.
   - *Powdered Activated Carbon (PAC)*: particle size < 0.075 mm; dosed directly into aeration tanks or clarifiers.
2. **Activated Charcoal** — lower-grade wood/coconut-based carbon.
3. **Biochar** — pyrolyzed agricultural residue (rice husk, sawdust, coconut shell).
4. **Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs)** and **Graphene Oxide (GO)** — emerging nano-adsorbents.
5. **Carbon Molecular Sieves (CMS)** — uniform micropores for size-selective adsorption.

### B. Mineral / Inorganic Adsorbents
1. **Silica Gel** — amorphous SiO₂·nH₂O; highly polar; dries gases/liquids.
2. **Activated Alumina (γ-Al₂O₃)** — amphoteric; excellent for fluoride, arsenic removal.
3. **Zeolites** — crystalline aluminosilicates with regular cage-like micropores
   - *Natural Zeolites*: Clinoptilolite, Mordenite, Chabazite.
   - *Synthetic Zeolites*: Zeolite A, Zeolite X, Zeolite Y (used in detergents).
4. **Clay Minerals** — Montmorillonite, Bentonite, Kaolinite (layered silicates with cation-exchange sites).
5. **Diatomaceous Earth (Kieselguhr)** — fossilized silica skeletons.

### C. Polymeric / Synthetic Resins
1. **Ion-Exchange Resins** — cation (CSTR) and anion (ASTRA) exchangers.
2. **Macroporous Polymeric Resins** — crosslinked styrene-DVB; used for organic micropollutants.
3. **Chitosan-based biopolymers** — derived from chitin; amino groups (–NH₂) bind metals and dyes.

### D. Industrial By-products / Low-Cost Adsorbents
1. **Fly Ash** — from coal combustion (contains unburned carbon + cenospheres).
2. **Red Mud (Bauxite Residue)** — Fe₂O₃/SiO₂ rich; effective for phosphate and dyes.
3. **Slag, Bagasse, Sawdust, Rice Husk Ash** — lignocellulosic wastes.
4. **Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs)** — hybrid organic-inorganic, ultra-high surface area (> 5000 m²/g).

## 2.2 Key Engineering Properties (Exam-Favourite)

- **Surface area to volume ratio** is the *single most important* property.
- **Iodine Number** — a quick industrial index; for high-grade AC, **≥ 900 mg/g**.
- **Methylene Blue Number** — measures mesoporosity; high for **PAC**.
- **Hardness / Abrasion Number** — important for GAC to resist attrition in backwash.
- **Apparent Density** and **Moisture Content** — design parameters.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.3 — Master Formula Sheet for Adsorption in KTU BWT**

| Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variable Definitions & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Langmuir Isotherm** | $q_e = \dfrac{q_{max} \, b \, C_e}{1 + b \, C_e}$ | $q_e$ = equilibrium uptake (mg/g); $q_{max}$ = monolayer capacity (mg/g); $b$ = Langmuir constant (L/mg); $C_e$ = equilibrium concentration (mg/L) | Monolayer adsorption on homogeneous sites (GAC, zeolites) |
| **Freundlich Isotherm** | $q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$ | $K_F$ = capacity constant (mg/g)(L/mg)^(1/n); $1/n$ = intensity parameter (dimensionless); $0 < 1/n < 1$ for favourable | Heterogeneous surface adsorption (PAC, biochar) |
| **BET Equation** | $\dfrac{1}{W\!\left[\left(\tfrac{P_0}{P}\right)-1\right]} = \dfrac{1}{W_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{W_m C}\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | $W$ = weight adsorbed at $P$; $W_m$ = monolayer weight; $P/P_0$ = relative pressure; $C$ = BET constant | Determines **specific surface area** |
| **BET Surface Area** | $S_{BET} = \dfrac{W_m \, N_A \, A_{cs}}{M}$ | $N_A$ = Avogadro's number (6.022 × 10²³ /mol); $A_{cs}$ = adsorbate cross-section (for N₂ ≈ 16.2 Å²); $M$ = molar mass of adsorbate | Compute SSA of adsorbents |
| **Pseudo-1st Order (Lagergren)** | $\dfrac{dq_t}{dt} = k_1 (q_e - q_t)$ | $k_1$ = rate constant (1/min); $q_t$ = uptake at time $t$ | Kinetic modelling of column adsorption |
| **Pseudo-2nd Order** | $\dfrac{dq_t}{dt} = k_2 (q_e - q_t)^2$ | $k_2$ = rate constant (g/mg·min) | Chemisorption-dominant kinetics |
| **Intra-particle Diffusion (Weber-Morris)** | $q_t = k_i \sqrt{t} + C$ | $k_i$ = diffusion constant; $C$ = boundary layer thickness | Identifies rate-limiting step |
| **Breakthrough Capacity** | $q_{b} = \dfrac{Q \cdot C_0 \ \int_{0}^{t_b}(1 - C/C_0)\,dt}{M}$ | $Q$ = flow (L/min); $C_0$ = inlet; $M$ = mass of adsorbent | Packed-bed column design (GAC contactors) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Designing a GAC contactor for a 50 MLD effluent polishing plant requires Langmuir parameters (from lab batch tests) to predict **breakthrough curve** behaviour and **Empty Bed Contact Time (EBCT)**, typically **10–30 minutes** for tertiary wastewater polishing.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Langmuir Isotherm (Kinetics → Equilibrium)

Langmuir (1918) proposed that adsorption occurs only at a fixed number of **localized sites**, forming a **monolayer**.

**Step 1: Define surface coverage $\theta$**
$$\theta = \dfrac{\text{Number of sites occupied}}{\text{Total sites available}} = \dfrac{q_e}{q_{max}}$$

**Step 2: Rate of adsorption (forward)** is proportional to bare sites $(1 - \theta)$ and partial pressure $P$ (or $C_e$ in solution):
$$r_{ads} = k_a \, C_e \, (1 - \theta)$$

**Step 3: Rate of desorption (reverse)** is proportional to occupied sites:
$$r_{des} = k_d \, \theta$$

**Step 4: At equilibrium, $r_{ads} = r_{des}$**
$$k_a \, C_e \, (1 - \theta) = k_d \, \theta$$

**Step 5: Rearranging for $\theta$**
$$k_a \, C_e - k_a \, C_e \, \theta = k_d \, \theta$$
$$k_a \, C_e = \theta \, (k_d + k_a \, C_e)$$
$$\theta = \dfrac{k_a \, C_e}{k_d + k_a \, C_e} = \dfrac{(k_a/k_d) \, C_e}{1 + (k_a/k_d) \, C_e}$$

**Step 6: Substituting $b = k_a/k_d$ and $q_e = \theta \, q_{max}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_e &= \dfrac{b \, q_{max} \, C_e}{1 + b \, C_e} \quad &\text{(Langmuir Isotherm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Linear form for plotting** (used in KTU lab experiments to find $q_{max}$ and $b$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} &= \dfrac{1}{b \, q_{max}} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_{max}} \\
\text{Plot: } y &= \dfrac{C_e}{q_e}, \quad x = C_e \\
\text{Slope} &= \dfrac{1}{q_{max}}, \quad \text{Intercept} &= \dfrac{1}{b \, q_{max}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **separation factor** $R_L = 1/(1 + bC_0)$ decides if adsorption is favourable ($0 < R_L < 1$), unfavourable ($R_L > 1$), linear ($R_L = 1$), or irreversible ($R_L = 0$).

## 3.2 Derivation of Freundlich Isotherm (Empirical Power Law)

Freundlich observed that on **heterogeneous surfaces**, the heat of adsorption varies with coverage $\theta$. The differential heat decreases exponentially as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dq}{d(\ln P)} &= \text{constant} \cdot q \\
\text{Integrating: } q &= K_F \, P^{1/n} \\
\text{In solution: } q_e &= K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n} \quad &\text{(Freundlich Isotherm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Linear form for plotting:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\log q_e &= \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \log C_e \\
\text{Slope} &= 1/n, \quad \text{Intercept} &= \log K_F
\end{aligned}
$$

> A value of $1/n$ between **0.1 and 0.5** indicates highly favourable adsorption; **$1/n > 1$** indicates cooperative adsorption (rare in wastewater).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Major Adsorbents

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 3.3 — Property Matrix of Common Wastewater Adsorbents**

| Adsorbent | Specific Surface Area (m²/g) | Pore Size Dominance | Key Surface Chemistry | Typical Pollutants Removed | Cost (Relative) | Regenerable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GAC (Coconut-based)** | **1100–1500** | Microporous (< 2 nm) | Hydrophobic, slightly basic | Phenols, pesticides, COD, colour | Medium | Yes (steam/thermal) |
| **PAC (Wood-based)** | **800–1200** | Meso + Micro | Hydrophobic | Dyes, taste/odour compounds | Medium | No (single-use) |
| **Activated Alumina** | **200–400** | Mesoporous | Amphoteric Al–OH | Fluoride, arsenic, phosphate | Low | Yes (NaOH) |
| **Zeolite (Clinoptilolite)** | **25–40** | Microporous (0.4 nm) | Cation-exchange (Si/Al) | NH₄⁺, heavy metals, Cs/Sr | Low | Yes (NaCl brine) |
| **Bentonite Clay** | **80–150** | Layered (interlayer) | Cation-exchange (Al–O–Si) | Dyes, metals, organics | Very Low | Limited |
| **Silica Gel** | **300–800** | Mesoporous (2–10 nm) | Polar Si–OH | Moisture, VOCs, organics | Low | Yes (thermal) |
| **Chitosan Biopolymer** | **50–200** | Macroporous | –NH₂, –OH groups | Metals (Cu²⁺, Pb²⁺), dyes, anions | Low | Partial |
| **Biochar** | **50–500** | Heterogeneous | Aromatic C, O-containing groups | Pesticides, metals, nutrients | Very Low | No |
| **CNT / Graphene Oxide** | **200–1500** | Tunable | π-stacking, –COOH | Pharmaceuticals, dyes, metals | Very High | Yes |
| **MOF (e.g., MIL-101)** | **3000–5000+** | Tunable ultra-micropores | Open metal sites | Trace organics, gases | Very High | Yes |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A batch adsorption test using 1.0 g of activated carbon on 1 L of dye solution gives: $C_0 = 100$ mg/L, $C_e = 20$ mg/L.
> **Find:** $q_e$ and percent removal.

**Solution:**

Mass adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent:
$$
\begin{aligned}
q_e &= \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) \cdot V}{M} \\
q_e &= \dfrac{(100 - 20) \cdot 1.0}{1.0} \\
q_e &= 80 \, \text{mg/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Percent removal:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \, R &= \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100 \\
\% \, R &= \dfrac{100 - 20}{100} \times 100 = 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$
