# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **Granular Activated Carbon (GAC)** and **Powdered Activated Carbon (PAC)** in terms of particle size, application mode, and regeneration feasibility.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | GAC | PAC |
|---|---|---|
| Particle size | 0.5 – 2.5 mm | < 0.075 mm (75 μm) |
| Application | Packed/fixed bed columns | Direct dosing into tanks |
| Contact time | Long (10–30 min EBCT) | Short, mixed with bulk |
| Regeneration | Yes (thermal/steam) | No (single-use, lost in sludge) |
| Pressure drop | Higher | Negligible |
| Cost per kg | Higher but reusable | Lower but non-recoverable |

GAC is preferred for **continuous tertiary polishing** of biologically treated wastewater, while PAC is used for **emergency spill control** or **seasonal taste/odour removal**.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q2.** What is the **Iodine Number** of activated carbon, and why is it used as a quality index?

**Model Answer:**
The **Iodine Number** is defined as the milligrams of iodine adsorbed by **1 gram of activated carbon** at an equilibrium residual iodine concentration of **0.02 N**. It is a rapid industrial test that primarily quantifies the **microporosity (pores < 1 nm)** of activated carbon. A high Iodine Number (≥ 900 mg/g for high-grade AC) indicates a large micropore volume and, by inference, a **high specific surface area**, making it an excellent screening parameter for AC procurement in wastewater treatment plants.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently (i) confuse **adsorption** with **absorption** (adsorption is a *surface* phenomenon), (ii) forget the **units** of $q_e$ (mg/g) and $K_F$, (iii) omit the **favourability criterion** $0 < 1/n < 1$ for Freundlich, and (iv) skip drawing the **linearized plot** for isotherm determination. Loss of up to **3–4 marks per question** is common for these omissions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Classify the various types of adsorbents used in wastewater treatment, with at least **two examples** in each category. Discuss the role of **surface area** and **pore size distribution** in determining adsorbent performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A laboratory batch test was performed using **2.0 g of activated carbon** in **500 mL** of an industrial effluent. The initial COD was **800 mg/L** and the equilibrium COD was **120 mg/L**. Determine:
- (i) The amount of COD adsorbed per unit mass of AC, $q_e$.
- (ii) The **percent removal** of COD.
- (iii) Comment on the suitability of this adsorbent for a **10 MLD plant** if the equilibrium data fit the Langmuir isotherm with $q_{max} = 250$ mg/g and $b = 0.05$ L/mg. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Classification and role of surface area/pore size:**

**1. Carbon-based adsorbents:** GAC, PAC, biochar, CNT, activated charcoal.
**2. Mineral/inorganic adsorbents:** Silica gel, activated alumina, natural & synthetic zeolites, clay minerals (bentonite, montmorillonite).
**3. Polymeric/synthetic:** Ion-exchange resins, macroporous polymer beads, chitosan.
**4. Low-cost by-products:** Fly ash, red mud, bagasse, sawdust.
**5. Advanced nano-adsorbents:** MOFs, graphene oxide aerogels.

**Role of surface area and pore size:**
- A **larger specific surface area** provides more active sites per unit mass, increasing adsorption capacity.
- **Pore size** dictates *which molecules* can enter:
  - **Micropores** (< 2 nm): adsorb small molecules like chloroform, phenols.
  - **Mesopores** (2–50 nm): accommodate medium-sized dye molecules and pharmaceuticals.
  - **Macropores** (> 50 nm): serve as transport highways to inner micropores.
- The **IUPAC isotherm Type I** (Langmuir-like) curve indicates microporous solids; **Type IV** indicates mesoporous solids.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

**Given:** $V = 500 \, \text{mL} = 0.5 \, \text{L}$; $M = 2.0 \, \text{g}$; $C_0 = 800 \, \text{mg/L}$; $C_e = 120 \, \text{mg/L}$.

**(i) Amount adsorbed per unit mass:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
q_e &= \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) \cdot V}{M} \\
q_e &= \dfrac{(800 - 120) \times 0.5}{2.0} \\
q_e &= \dfrac{680 \times 0.5}{2.0} = \dfrac{340}{2.0} \\
q_e &= 170 \, \text{mg/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Percent removal:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \, R &= \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100 \\
\% \, R &= \dfrac{800 - 120}{800} \times 100 \\
\% \, R &= 85\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Suitability for 10 MLD plant using Langmuir parameters:**

The Langmuir isotherm predicts:
$$
\begin{aligned}
q_e &= \dfrac{q_{max} \cdot b \cdot C_e}{1 + b \, C_e} \\
q_e &= \dfrac{250 \times 0.05 \times 120}{1 + 0.05 \times 120} \\
q_e &= \dfrac{1500}{1 + 6} = \dfrac{1500}{7} \\
q_e &\approx 214.3 \, \text{mg/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the experimentally obtained $q_e = 170$ mg/g is **close to but lower than the theoretical $q_{max} = 250$ mg/g**, the AC is suitable. For a 10 MLD plant, the required daily AC mass (assuming average residual COD removal load of ~50 mg/L from 10 ML):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily load} &= 10 \times 10^6 \, \text{L} \times 50 \, \text{mg/L} = 5 \times 10^8 \, \text{mg} = 500 \, \text{kg/day} \\
\text{AC required} &= \dfrac{5 \times 10^8}{170 \times 10^3} \approx 2941 \, \text{kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the plant requires **~3 tonnes/day of AC** with periodic regeneration every 2–4 weeks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **Freundlich and Langmuir adsorption isotherms** with their linearized forms. Discuss the **physical significance** of the constants $q_{max}$, $b$, $K_F$, and $1/n$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the working of a **Granular Activated Carbon (GAC) contactor** used as a tertiary treatment unit. Explain the concept of **breakthrough curve** and **EBCT (Empty Bed Contact Time)**. A GAC column has a diameter of **2.0 m**, bed depth of **3.0 m**, and treats **15 m³/h** of secondary effluent. Calculate: (i) EBCT, (ii) superficial velocity, (iii) hydraulic loading rate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Freundlich and Langmuir Isotherms:**

**Langmuir Isotherm (1918):** Assumes monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface with a finite number of identical sites.

**Equation:**
$$
q_e = \dfrac{q_{max} \, b \, C_e}{1 + b \, C_e}
$$

**Linear form:** $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{b \, q_{max}} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_{max}}$

**Physical significance of constants:**
- $q_{max}$ (mg/g): Maximum monolayer adsorption capacity; represents the total number of active sites per unit mass.
- $b$ (L/mg): Langmuir constant related to the **enthalpy of adsorption**; high $b$ indicates strong binding affinity.

**Freundlich Isotherm (1906):** Empirical model for heterogeneous surfaces; multilayer adsorption with non-uniform heat distribution.

**Equation:** $q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$

**Linear form:** $\log q_e = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \log C_e$

**Physical significance of constants:**
- $K_F$: Capacity constant indicating relative adsorption capacity.
- $1/n$: Intensity parameter; $0 < 1/n < 1$ implies favourable adsorption. Smaller $1/n$ implies stronger heterogeneity and more intense adsorption at low concentrations.

**(b) GAC Contactor, Breakthrough Curve, and EBCT:**

**Working of GAC Contactor:** Secondary effluent is pumped **downward** (or upward) through a bed of GAC inside a cylindrical vessel. Dissolved organics, colour, and odorous compounds adsorb onto the carbon's huge internal surface. Cleaned water exits from the bottom. The carbon is regenerated periodically (steam or thermal kiln at 800–900 °C) once saturated.

**Breakthrough Curve:** A plot of **effluent pollutant concentration ($C$)** vs. **time** (or **bed volumes treated**) at constant inlet concentration $C_0$. The breakthrough point is reached when $C/C_0$ equals the regulatory limit (typically 0.05–0.10).

**EBCT (Empty Bed Contact Time):** Theoretical residence time in an empty column.
$$
EBCT = \dfrac{V_{bed}}{Q} = \dfrac{A_{bed} \cdot L}{Q}
$$

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 2.0$ m; $L = 3.0$ m; $Q = 15 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$.

**Cross-sectional area:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (2.0)^2}{4} = \pi \, \text{m}^2 \approx 3.1416 \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Bed volume:** $V_{bed} = A \times L = 3.1416 \times 3.0 = 9.42 \, \text{m}^3$

**(i) Empty Bed Contact Time (EBCT):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
EBCT &= \dfrac{V_{bed}}{Q} = \dfrac{9.42 \, \text{m}^3}{15 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}} \\
EBCT &= 0.628 \, \text{h} \approx 37.7 \, \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Superficial velocity ($v_s$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
v_s &= \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{15}{3.1416} \\
v_s &\approx 4.77 \, \text{m/h} \quad \text{(or } 1.325 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m/s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
HLR &= \dfrac{Q}{A} = v_s \approx 4.77 \, \text{m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h})
\end{aligned}
$$

The HLR is well within the **typical design range of 5–15 m/h** for GAC adsorbers in tertiary wastewater polishing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes to Avoid:** (1) Mixing up $v_s$ (superficial) with interstitial velocity (multiply by $1/\varepsilon$, $\varepsilon \approx 0.4$). (2) Forgetting to convert $Q$ into consistent units (m³/s vs. m³/h). (3) Computing $A$ as $D^2$ instead of $\pi D^2/4$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things You MUST Remember Before the Exam**

- **Adsorbent vs. Adsorbate:** Adsorbent = the *solid* that traps; Adsorbate = the *pollutant* being trapped. Do **NOT** confuse these.
- **Activated Carbon** is the **most widely used** industrial adsorbent — be ready to quote typical SSA (**1000–1500 m²/g**) and Iodine Number (**≥ 900 mg/g**).
- **Langmuir** → monolayer + uniform sites; **Freundlich** → multilayer + heterogeneous; **BET** → multilayer + pore-surface measurement.
- **Classification Mnemonic:** **"C-M-P-L-A"** → **C**arbon, **M**ineral, **P**olymer, **L**ow-cost, **A**dvanced.
- **IUPAC Pore Sizes:** **< 2 nm** (micro), **2–50 nm** (meso), **> 50 nm** (macro). MACs act as transport arteries; MICs and MESs do the actual adsorption.
- **EBCT** is the *primary design* parameter for GAC contactors (typical 10–30 min for tertiary polishing).
- **Breakthrough curve** plot axes: **x = time/bed volumes, y = C/C₀**. The S-shape arises from the mass-transfer zone moving through the bed.
- **Regeneration** of GAC is by **thermal kiln** (800–900 °C with steam) or **microwave heating**; PAC is **not** regenerated.
- **Zeolites** are **cation exchangers** (preferred for NH₄⁺); **Activated Alumina** for **F⁻ and As**; **Chitosan** for **heavy metals**.
- **MOFs and CNTs** are research-stage, ultra-high capacity; questions on them are usually **2–3 mark definition/conceptual** type.
- **Favourability check:** $0 < R_L < 1$ (Langmuir) and $0 < 1/n < 1$ (Freundlich) for **favourable** adsorption.
- **Units to memorize cold:** $q_e$ in **mg/g**, $C_e$ in **mg/L**, $K_F$ in **(mg/g)(L/mg)^(1/n)**, $b$ in **L/mg**, $k_1$ in **1/min**, $k_2$ in **g/(mg·min)**.
- **For numerical problems:** always show the **formula → substitution → unit cancellation → final answer** sequence for full marks.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Types of Adsorbents**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activated Carbon Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Activated Carbon Adsorption ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Activated Carbon Adsorption** is a **physico-chemical tertiary wastewater treatment process** in which dissolved organic and inorganic contaminants are removed from water by their accumulation (adherence) onto the internal pore surfaces of a highly porous carbonaceous adsorbent medium known as **Activated Carbon (AC)**. In the context of KTU 2024 scheme, activated carbon adsorption is positioned as a **polishing step (tertiary treatment)** used to remove: - **Refractory organics** not biodegradable in secondary treatment - **Trace toxic compounds** (pesticides, phenols, PCBs) - **Colour, odour, and taste-causing substances** - **Residual chlorine** and some heavy metals The driving force is the difference in **concentration** between the bulk liquid phase and the carbon surface (unlike membrane processes, which are pressure-driven). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Magnetic Sponge Principle** 🧲🧽 > > Imagine you spill ink in a glass of water and cannot filter it out with an ordinary cloth. Now place a **highly porous, magnet-like sponge (Activated Carbon)** in this water. The sponge does not "trap" particles like a filter mesh; instead, its microscopic internal walls **attract and hold** the ink molecules onto its vast internal surface — much like how a magnet pulls iron filings without being a net. > > After some time, the water becomes clear. The sponge is now "loaded" with the pollutant and can either be **discarded** or **regenerated** (cleaned with steam/acid) for reuse. > > - **Sponge internal surface** ≈ Activated Carbon's enormous surface area (**500–1500 m²/g**) > - **Ink molecules clinging to sponge walls** ≈ Adsorbate (pollutant) being adsorbed > - **Water turning clear** = Adsorption equilibrium/breakthrough ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Specific Surface Area of GAC**: **500 – 1500 m²/g** - **Apparent Density of GAC**: **0.4 – 0.5 g/cm³** - **Particle Size (GAC)**: **0.6 – 2.4 mm** - **Particle Size (PAC)**: **< 0.074 mm (passes 200 mesh)** - **Iodine Number**: A standard measure of micropore capacity — **typically 600–1200 mg/g** - **Operating pH range**: Generally **6.5 – 7.5** for most organics - **Standard Contact Time**: **15 – 60 minutes** in PAC applications - **Backwash expansion**: **20 – 40%** for GAC filters - **Empty Bed Contact Time (EBCT)**: **5 – 30 minutes** for GAC contactors ### 1.4 Two Broad Types of Activated Carbon > [!IMPORTANT] > **GAC – Granular Activated Carbon** > - Particle size: **0.6 – 2.4 mm** > - Used in **fixed-bed columns** or **expanded beds** > - Regenerable repeatedly > - Standard for continuous flow applications > [!IMPORTANT] > **PAC – Powdered Actived Carbon** > - Particle size: **< 0.074 mm (10–100 µm)** > - Dosed as a **slurry** directly into water > - Used in batch/seasonal applications; **NOT regenerable** economically > - Effective for shock loads and taste/odour control ### 1.5 Mechanism — How Adsorption Happens (Stepwise) 1. **Bulk Solution Transport (Film Diffusion):** Pollutant migrates from bulk water to the hydrodynamic boundary layer around the carbon particle. 2. **External (Film) Diffusion:** Molecule crosses the liquid film surrounding the particle. 3. **Intra-particle (Pore) Diffusion:** Molecule travels into the carbon's internal pore network — often the **rate-limiting step**. 4. **Surface Reaction / Physical Attachment:** Molecule adheres to the carbon surface via **Van der Waals forces** (physisorption) or **chemical bonds** (chemisorption). 5. **Equilibrium:** Concentration on carbon = capacity `q_e` (mg/g); concentration in water = equilibrium `C_e` (mg/L). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Adsorption Theory #### 2.1.1 Physisorption (Physical Adsorption) - Caused by **Van der Waals (London dispersion) forces** - Low enthalpy: **ΔH ≈ 5 – 40 kJ/mol** - **Reversible** — carbon can be regenerated - **Multi-layered** possible - **Non-specific** #### 2.1.2 Chemisorption (Chemical Adsorption) - Caused by **covalent/ionic bonding** - High enthalpy: **ΔH ≈ 40 – 400 kJ/mol** - Often **irreversible** under mild conditions - **Mono-layered** (specific active sites) - **Highly specific** to functional groups > [!NOTE] > In wastewater treatment, **physisorption dominates** because the carbon is regenerated multiple times — chemisorption would make regeneration impractical. ### 2.2 Properties That Make Activated Carbon So Effective | Property | Value/Range | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Surface Area (BET)** | **500 – 1500 m²/g** | A teaspoon of GAC has the surface area of a football field! | | **Pore Volume** | **0.5 – 0.8 cm³/g** | Internal void space holding adsorbate | | **Micropore Radius** | **< 2 nm** | Captures small molecules (micropollutants) | | **Mesopore Radius** | **2 – 50 nm** | Transports molecules to micropores | | **Macropore Radius** | **> 50 nm** | Entry channels; reduces clogging | | **Iodine Number** | **600 – 1200 mg/g** | Indicator of micropore capacity | | **Methylene Blue Number** | **100 – 300 mg/g** | Indicator of mesopore capacity | | **Ash Content** | **< 8%** | Higher ash = lower adsorption capacity | | **Hardness Number** | **75 – 95** | Resistance to attrition during backwash | ### 2.3 Source Materials for Activated Carbon - **Bituminous coal** (most common for water treatment) - **Lignite** - **Coconut shells** (excellent for trace organics) - **Wood / sawdust** - **Peat** - **Petroleum residues** - **Bone char** (historical) ### 2.4 Activation Processes (How Carbon Gets "Activated") > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical (Thermal) Activation:** Raw carbonaceous material is **carbonized at 400–600 °C** in absence of air, then **activated at 800–1000 °C** in presence of **steam, CO₂, or flue gas**. Pores open up as volatile matter escapes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Chemical Activation:** Material is impregnated with **ZnCl₂, H₃PO₄, or KOH**, then heated at **400–600 °C**. Yields a more uniform pore structure; common for PAC production. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Formula** | **Expression** | **Variables / Units** | **Application** | |---|---|---|---| | **Mass of Carbon Required** | $M = \dfrac{Q \cdot C_0 \cdot t}{X}$ | $M$ = mass of carbon (g); $Q$ = flow (L/min); $C_0$ = influent conc. (mg/L); $t$ = contact time (min); $X$ = carbon dose (mg/L) | PAC dosing | | **Carbon Dose** | $X = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{q_e}$ | $X$ = dose (mg/L); $q_e$ = capacity (mg/g); $C_0, C_e$ = initial/equilibrium conc. | Quick design | | **Freundlich Isotherm** | $\dfrac{x}{m} = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$ | $x/m$ = capacity (mg/g); $C_e$ = equilibrium conc. (mg/L); $K_F, n$ = empirical constants | Heterogeneous surfaces (GAC fits this well) | | **Freundlich Linear Form** | $\log \left( \dfrac{x}{m} \right) = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \log C_e$ | Slope = $1/n$; Intercept = $\log K_F$ | Plot to find $K_F, 1/n$ | | **Langmuir Isotherm** | $\dfrac{x}{m} = \dfrac{a \cdot b \cdot C_e}{1 + b \cdot C_e}$ | $a$ = monolayer capacity (mg/g); $b$ = affinity constant (L/mg) | Mono-layer adsorption | | **Langmuir Linear Form** | $\dfrac{C_e}{x/m} = \dfrac{1}{a b} + \dfrac{C_e}{a}$ | Slope = $1/a$; Intercept = $1/(ab)$ | Plot to find $a, b$ | | **BET Isotherm (2-parm)** | $\dfrac{C_e}{(C_s - C_e)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{q} = \dfrac{1}{q_m b} + \dfrac{b-1}{q_m b} \cdot \dfrac{C_e}{C_s}$ | $C_s$ = saturation conc.; $q_m$ = monolayer capacity; $b$ = BET constant | Multi-layer adsorption | | **Pseudo-First-Order Rate** | $\dfrac{dq_t}{dt} = k_1 (q_e - q_t)$ | $k_1$ = rate constant (1/min) | Lagergren model | | **Pseudo-First-Order Integrated** | $\log (q_e - q_t) = \log q_e - \dfrac{k_1}{2.303} t$ | Plot $\log(q_e - q_t)$ vs $t$ | Kinetics | | **Pseudo-Second-Order Rate** | $\dfrac{dq_t}{dt} = k_2 (q_e - q_t)^2$ | $k_2$ = rate constant (g/mg·min) | Ho & McKay model | | **Pseudo-Second-Order Integrated** | $\dfrac{t}{q_t} = \dfrac{1}{k_2 q_e^2} + \dfrac{t}{q_e}$ | Plot $t/q_t$ vs $t$ | Kinetics | | **Intra-particle Diffusion** | $q_t = k_p \cdot t^{1/2} + C$ | $k_p$ = diffusion rate constant (mg/g·min$^{0.5}$) | Weber-Morris model | | **EBCT** | $EBCT = \dfrac{V_{bed}}{Q}$ | $V_{bed}$ = carbon volume (m³); $Q$ = flow (m³/h) | GAC contactor design | | **Carbon Usage Rate (CUR)** | $CUR = \dfrac{M_{carbon}}{Q \cdot (C_0 - C_e) \cdot t}$ | mg carbon / mg pollutant removed | Performance metric | | **Breakthrough Capacity** | $q_b = \dfrac{Q \cdot C_0 \cdot t^*}{M_{carbon}}$ | $t^*$ = breakthrough time | GAC column design | | **Service Time (Plug Flow)** | $t^* = \dfrac{M \cdot q_e}{Q \cdot C_0}$ | Ideal plug flow | Simplified design | | **Length of Mass Transfer Zone** | $L_{MTZ} = L \cdot \dfrac{(t^* - t_e)}{(t^* - t_e) + t_e}$ | $L$ = bed depth; $t_e$ = exhaustion time | Kolmogorov model | | **Capacity Ratio (R)** | $R = \dfrac{q_e \cdot \rho_{carbon}}{C_0 \cdot EBCT \cdot 60}$ | Dimensionless | Performance indicator | | **Surface Loading Rate** | $SLR = \dfrac{Q}{A_{bed}}$ | m³/m²·h | Hydraulics | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Design Insight:** For GAC contactors, a **short EBCT (< 5 min)** risks early breakthrough; **long EBCT (> 30 min)** is uneconomical. The sweet spot for most micropollutants is **10–20 minutes**. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Drinking water treatment** — removal of pesticides, herbicides, and THM precursors - **Industrial effluent polishing** — pharmaceuticals, dyes, petrochemicals - **Landfill leachate treatment** — refractory COD - **Aquarium and process water** — colour/odour stripping - **Air purification** — VOC and mercury removal (uses the same principle, just in gas phase) - **Gold recovery** in mining (cyanide-complex gold adsorbed on carbon) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Freundlich Isotherm from a Heterogeneous Surface **Starting Concept:** A real GAC surface has **non-uniform adsorption energy** — some sites are strong, some weak. The number of sites with energy `E` follows a Boltzmann distribution. **Step 1: Local Langmuir-like equilibrium at each site** $$ \theta_E = \dfrac{b_E \cdot C_e}{1 + b_E \cdot C_e} $$ where $b_E = b_0 \exp(E/RT)$ is the affinity constant at energy $E$. **Step 2: Total surface coverage = integral over all energies** $$ \dfrac{x}{m} = \int_0^\infty n(E) \cdot \theta_E \, dE $$ where $n(E)$ is the site energy distribution function. **Step 3: Assume exponential distribution of energies** $$ n(E) = n_0 \exp(-E/m_0) $$ After integration and substituting back: **Step 4: Closed-form expression (Empirical Freundlich)** $$ \dfrac{x}{m} = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n} $$ **Step 5: Take log₁₀ of both sides** $$ \log \left( \dfrac{x}{m} \right) = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \log C_e $$ **Why this works in practice:** Plot $\log(x/m)$ vs $\log C_e$ → straight line. - **Slope** $= 1/n$ (typically 0.1 to 0.5 for favourable adsorption) - **Intercept** $= \log K_F$ (capacity indicator — higher $K_F$ = better carbon) > [!NOTE] > **Rule of thumb:** $1/n < 1$ indicates **favourable adsorption**; $1/n > 1$ indicates **unfavourable adsorption**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Langmuir Isotherm from Kinetic Considerations **Starting Concept:** A perfectly uniform surface with **equivalent, non-interacting** sites. Each site can hold **only one molecule** (monolayer). **Step 1: Rate of adsorption (molecules sticking to vacant sites)** $$ r_{ads} = k_a \cdot C_e \cdot (1 - \theta) $$ where $\theta$ = fractional coverage. **Step 2: Rate of desorption (molecules leaving occupied sites)** $$ r_{des} = k_d \cdot \theta $$ **Step 3: At equilibrium, $r_{ads} = r_{des}$** $$ k_a \cdot C_e \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta $$ **Step 4: Solve for $\theta$** $$ k_a C_e - k_a C_e \theta = k_d \theta $$ $$ k_a C_e = \theta (k_d + k_a C_e) $$ $$ \theta = \dfrac{k_a C_e}{k_d + k_a C_e} = \dfrac{b C_e}{1 + b C_e} $$ where $b = k_a / k_d$ (equilibrium constant / affinity constant). **Step 5: Total capacity** $q = q_m \cdot \theta$ where $q_m$ is monolayer capacity: $$ \dfrac{x}{m} = \dfrac{q_m \cdot b \cdot C_e}{1 + b \cdot C_e} $$ **Step 6: Linearize by dividing numerator and denominator by $b C_e$ and rearranging:** $$ \dfrac{C_e}{x/m} = \dfrac{1}{q_m b} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m} $$ **Design implication:** Plot $C_e/(x/m)$ vs $C_e$ → straight line. - **Slope** $= 1/q_m$ (gives monolayer capacity) - **Intercept** $= 1/(q_m b)$ (gives affinity) --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Pseudo-Second-Order Kinetic Model (Ho & McKay, 1999) **Step 1: Rate equation — the rate is proportional to the *square* of available capacity** $$ \dfrac{dq_t}{dt} = k_2 (q_e - q_t)^2 $$ **Step 2: Boundary conditions:** $t = 0 \Rightarrow q_t = 0$ and $t = t \Rightarrow q_t = q_t$ **Step 3: Separate variables and integrate** $$ \int_0^{q_t} \dfrac{dq_t}{(q_e - q_t)^2} = \int_0^t k_2 \, dt $$ **Step 4: Evaluate the left side using substitution $u = q_e - q_t$, $du = -dq_t$** $$ \left[ \dfrac{1}{q_e - q_t} \right]_0^{q_t} = k_2 t $$ $$ \dfrac{1}{q_e - q_t} - \dfrac{1}{q_e} = k_2 t $$ **Step 5: Common denominator and solve for $t/q_t$:** $$ \dfrac{q_t}{q_e (q_e - q_t)} = k_2 t $$ $$ \dfrac{1}{q_e (q_e - q_t)} = k_2 $$ $$ \dfrac{1}{q_t} = \dfrac{1}{k_2 q_e^2} + \dfrac{t}{q_e} \cdot \dfrac{1}{t \cdot q_e} $$ Final rearrangement: $$ \dfrac{t}{q_t} = \dfrac{1}{k_2 q_e^2} + \dfrac{t}{q_e} $$ **Step 6: Linearized form for plotting** - Plot $t/q_t$ (y-axis) vs $t$ (x-axis) → straight line - **Slope** $= 1/q_e$ → gives equilibrium capacity - **Intercept** $= 1/(k_2 q_e^2)$ → gives rate constant > [!IMPORTANT] > The pseudo-second-order model is the **most widely used** for activated carbon systems because it fits experimental data with $R^2 > 0.99$ in most cases and predicts $q_e$ accurately. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ) Concept for GAC Columns **Step 1: Define three critical times** - $t_b$ = breakthrough time (when effluent concentration reaches the **target/regulatory limit**, often 10–20% of $C_0$) - $t_e$ = exhaustion time (effluent concentration equals **influent** $C_0$) - $L$ = total bed depth **Step 2: Mass balance on the saturated zone** The mass of pollutant adsorbed = $Q \cdot C_0 \cdot t^*$ **Step 3: Capacity of fully exhausted carbon** $$ q_{sat} = \dfrac{Q \cdot C_0 \cdot t_e}{M_{carbon}} $$ **Step 4: Working capacity at breakthrough** $$ q_{working} = \dfrac{Q \cdot C_0 \cdot t_b}{M_{carbon}} $$ **Step 5: Length of Mass Transfer Zone (Schroeder's approximation)** $$ L_{MTZ} = L \cdot \dfrac{t_e - t_b}{t_e - (1 - f_{exhausted}) \cdot t_b} $$ Simplified (when column approaches exhaustion $f \to 1$): $$ L_{MTZ} \approx L \cdot \dfrac{t_e - t_b}{t_e} $$ **Step 6: Critical design rule** The MTZ length should be **less than 70%** of the bed depth to avoid premature breakthrough: $$ L_{MTZ} < 0.7 L $$ --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-style Problem) > **Problem:** A wastewater treatment plant uses PAC to remove phenol. The flow is $Q = 10,000$ m³/d, influent phenol $C_0 = 5$ mg/L, and PAC dose $X = 50$ mg/L. If the equilibrium capacity of the carbon is $q_e = 80$ mg/g, find the equilibrium effluent concentration. **Step 1: Mass balance on PAC contactor** $$ X \cdot q_e = C_0 - C_e $$ $$ 50 \times 80 = 5000 - C_e $$ Wait — units correction. PAC dose is in mg/L, capacity in mg/g. $$ C_0 - C_e = X_{mg/L} \cdot q_e \cdot \dfrac{1 \text{ g}}{1000 \text{ mg}} $$ $$ C_0 - C_e = 50 \times 80 \times 10^{-3} = 4.0 \text{ mg/L} $$ **Step 2: Solve for $C_e$** $$ C_e = C_0 - 4.0 = 5.0 - 4.0 = 1.0 \text{ mg/L} $$ **Step 3: Removal efficiency** $$ \eta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{4.0}{5.0} \times 100 = 80\% $$ **Step 4: Daily PAC consumption** $$ M_{PAC} = Q \cdot X = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \times 50 \text{ g/m}^3 = 500{,}000 \text{ g/d} = 500 \text{ kg/d} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Tip:** Always show unit conversions explicitly. Marks are awarded for **dimensional consistency**, not just the final number. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow: Activated Carbon Adsorption in a Wastewater Train ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Influent
Wastewater] --> B[Primary Clarifier
Removes settleable solids] B --> C[Secondary Biological
Treatment Aeration Tank] C --> D[Secondary Clarifier
Removes biomass] D --> E[Sand Filtration
Removes residual TSS] E --> F[PAC Contact Basin
Dosed 5 to 100 mg per L
Mixing 15 to 60 min] F --> G[PAC Settling
Clarifier or DAF] G --> H[Chlorination
Disinfection] H --> I[Treated Effluent
Reuse or Discharge] J[PAC Sludge
with adsorbed organics] --> K[Sludge Dewatering] K --> L[Landfill Disposal
or Incineration] G --> J style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style F fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style J fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style L fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c ``` ### 4.2 Comparative Block Architecture: GAC vs PAC Systems ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph GAC["GAC SYSTEM Continuous Fixed Bed"] G1[Influent Pump] --> G2[GAC Contactor 1
Lead Vessel] G2 --> G3[GAC Contactor 2
Lag Vessel] G3 --> G4[Treated Effluent] G2 -.Saturated Carbon.-> G5[Carbon Transfer
Pump or Eductor] G5 --> G6[Thermal Regeneration
Furnace 800 to 1000 C] G6 --> G7[Reactivated Carbon
Returned to Service] end subgraph PAC["PAC SYSTEM Batch Contact"] P1[PAC Storage Silo] --> P2[PAC Slurry
Make-down Tank] P2 --> P3[Flash Mixer
G value 700 to 1000 per s] P3 --> P4[Flocculation Basin
G value 20 to 75 per s] P4 --> P5[Sedimentation
or DAF] P5 --> P6[PAC Sludge
Landfill] P5 --> P7[Filtered Effluent] end style GAC fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1 style PAC fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style G6 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style G7 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style P6 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style P7 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ### 4.3 Breakthrough Curve — Schematic of GAC Column Performance ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Timeline["Time on Stream"] direction LR T0[Time 0
Fresh Carbon] --> T1[Time t sub b
Breakthrough
C reaches C sub L] T1 --> T2[Time t sub e
Exhaustion
C equals C sub 0] T2 --> T3[Time Greater than t sub e
Regenerate Carbon] end subgraph Zones["Adsorption Zones in Bed"] direction TB Z1[Top Zone
Saturated Exhausted] Z2[Mass Transfer Zone
MTZ Active] Z3[Bottom Zone
Virgin Carbon] end subgraph Curve["Concentration Profile at Time t sub b"] direction LR C1[Inlet C sub 0] --> C2[Steeper Drop in MTZ] --> C3[Outlet C sub L] end Z1 --- Z2 Z2 --- Z3 style Z1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style Z2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style Z3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style C1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style C2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style C3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ### 4.4 Mass Transfer Resistances in a Carbon Particle ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Bulk Liquid
Concentration C sub b] -->|Film Diffusion
Resistance 1 over k sub f| B[Particle Surface
Concentration C sub s] B -->|Pore Diffusion
Resistance 1 over D sub p| C[Inner Pore Walls
Concentration C sub i] C -->|Surface Reaction
Resistance 1 over k sub s| D[Adsorbed Phase
q on Surface] style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style B fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1 style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** In a well-designed GAC system, **pore diffusion is the rate-limiting step** (resistance 1/$D_p$ dominates). This is why smaller carbon particles adsorb faster but cause more head loss. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]** > **Q1.** Differentiate between **adsorption** and **absorption** with a suitable example. State **Henry's Law** for dilute solutions. **Model Answer:** - **Adsorption**: Accumulation of a substance at the **surface** of a solid or liquid. Example: dye on activated carbon. - **Absorption**: Uniform penetration of a substance into the **bulk** of a material. Example: water vapour into silica gel. - **Henry's Law**: $C^* = H \cdot P$ — at constant temperature, the equilibrium concentration of a dissolved gas is proportional to its partial pressure. For adsorption context: $C_e = K_H \cdot q_e$ (linear isotherm). > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]** > **Q2.** What are **Freundlich constants $K_F$ and $1/n$**? How are they determined graphically? State the significance of $1/n$. **Model Answer:** - $K_F$ (mg/g)(L/mg)$^{1/n}$ = adsorption **capacity coefficient**; indicates carbon's ability to retain adsorbate. - $1/n$ = adsorption **intensity**; indicates surface heterogeneity and favourability. - **Graphical determination**: $\log(x/m) = \log K_F + (1/n)\log C_e$. Plot $\log(x/m)$ vs $\log C_e$ → straight line; slope = $1/n$, intercept = $\log K_F$. - **Significance**: $1/n < 1$ = favourable adsorption (typical for GAC); $1/n > 1$ = unfavourable; $1/n = 1$ = linear (Henry-type). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Module 3]** #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle of activated carbon adsorption**. Discuss the **physical and chemical properties** that make activated carbon an effective adsorbent in wastewater treatment. **Model Answer:** **Principle:** Activated carbon adsorption operates on the principle of **interfacial accumulation** of dissolved contaminants onto the high-surface-area internal pore structure of activated carbon. The process is governed by: - **Van der Waals forces** (predominant — physisorption) - Concentration gradient as the driving force - Spontaneous reduction in **Gibbs free energy** ($\Delta G < 0$) **Physical Properties:** 1. **Surface area**: 500–1500 m²/g (BET) — supplied by extensive microporosity 2. **Pore structure**: Three-tier — micropores (<2 nm), mesopores (2–50 nm), macropores (>50 nm) 3. **Particle size**: 0.6–2.4 mm (GAC); <0.074 mm (PAC) 4. **Density**: 0.4–0.5 g/cm³ apparent 5. **Hardness**: 75–95 — resists attrition 6. **Iodine number**: 600–1200 mg/g (micropore capacity) 7. **Methylene blue number**: 100–300 mg/g (mesopore capacity) **Chemical Properties:** 1. **Elemental composition**: 75–90% C, with H, O, N, S as minor elements 2. **Surface functional groups**: Carboxyl, phenolic, lactonic, quinonic (acidic); chromene, pyrone (basic) 3. **Surface charge**: Depends on point of zero charge (PZC, typically pH 6–8) 4. **Ash content**: < 8% (mineral impurities) 5. **Hydrophobicity**: Naturally non-polar surface → preferentially adsorbs organics **Why activated carbon is effective in wastewater:** - Non-specific (broad-spectrum) → removes diverse micropollutants - High capacity (q = 100–1000 mg/g for many organics) - Regenerable → low long-term cost - Operates at ambient temperature and pressure - Compatible with biological polishing (BAC — Biological Activated Carbon) **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Freundlich and Langmuir isotherms**. Compare their applicability to activated carbon adsorption with suitable linearised plots. **Model Answer:** **Freundlich Isotherm:** $$ \dfrac{x}{m} = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n} $$ - Empirical model — does not assume monolayer - Applicable to **heterogeneous surfaces** and **multilayer** adsorption - **Linearised form**: $\log(x/m) = \log K_F + (1/n)\log C_e$ - **Plot**: $\log(x/m)$ vs $\log C_e$ → straight line - **Parameters**: $K_F$ (capacity), $1/n$ (intensity); $1/n < 1$ indicates favourable adsorption **Langmuir Isotherm:** $$ \dfrac{x}{m} = \dfrac{a \cdot b \cdot C_e}{1 + b \cdot C_e} $$ - Theoretical model — assumes **monolayer** adsorption on **uniform** surface - Each site holds one molecule; no interaction between adsorbed molecules - **Linearised form**: $\dfrac{C_e}{x/m} = \dfrac{1}{a b} + \dfrac{C_e}{a}$ - **Plot**: $C_e/(x/m)$ vs $C_e$ → straight line - **Parameters**: $a$ (monolayer capacity, mg/g), $b$ (affinity constant, L/mg) **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Freundlich | Langmuir | |---|---|---| | Surface assumption | Heterogeneous | Homogeneous | | Adsorption type | Multilayer | Monolayer | | Theoretical basis | Empirical | Theoretical | | Low concentration | Fits well | Linearizes to Henry form | | High concentration | Predicts infinite capacity (unreal) | Approaches finite monolayer | | Applicability to GAC | **Excellent** (real surface is heterogeneous) | Limited (only at low conc.) | | Parameters | $K_F, 1/n$ | $a, b$ | **Conclusion:** For activated carbon adsorption in wastewater treatment, the **Freundlich isotherm is more widely applicable** because real carbon surfaces are inherently heterogeneous, and adsorption typically occurs as multilayers within the pore network. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **factors affecting activated carbon adsorption** in wastewater treatment. How do **pH, temperature, and initial concentration** influence the adsorption capacity of a PAC system? **Model Answer:** **Factors affecting adsorption:** **1. Properties of Adsorbate:** - **Solubility**: Lower solubility → higher adsorption (hydrophobic organics adsorb better) - **Molecular size**: Must be smaller than pore opening; very large molecules are excluded - **Polarity**: Non-polar > polar adsorption on GAC - **Polarity/Functional group**: Aromatic > aliphatic adsorption **2. Properties of Adsorbent:** - **Surface area**: Higher → higher capacity - **Pore size distribution**: Must match adsorbate molecular size - **Surface chemistry**: Functional groups affect specific interactions **3. Solution Chemistry:** - **pH**: Affects both adsorbate ionisation and surface charge - For acidic pollutants (phenols, carboxylic acids): adsorption is **maximum at low pH** (unionised form) - For basic pollutants (amines): adsorption is **maximum at high pH** - **Ionic strength**: Generally **decreases** organic adsorption (salting out) - **Co-solvents/organic solvents**: Compete for sites, **reduce** adsorption **4. Operating Conditions:** - **Temperature**: Adsorption is **exothermic**; capacity **decreases** with rising temperature (favours regeneration at high T) - **Contact time**: Longer → more adsorption (until equilibrium) - **Initial concentration $C_0$**: Higher $C_0$ → higher equilibrium capacity $q_e$ (but lower % removal) **Specific influence on PAC system:** | Parameter | Effect on $q_e$ | Effect on % Removal | |---|---|---| | pH (acidic) | ↑ for acidic organics | ↑ for acidic organics | | pH (alkaline) | ↑ for basic organics | ↑ for basic organics | | Temperature ↑ | ↓ (exothermic) | ↓ | | Initial conc. ↑ | ↑ (isotherm) | ↓ (capacity saturates) | **(b) [7 Marks]** A wastewater treatment plant treats **$Q = 5000$ m³/d** of effluent containing **$C_0 = 8$ mg/L of residual phenol**. PAC is dosed at **$X = 60$ mg/L** and the Freundlich parameters are **$K_F = 25$ (mg/g)(L/mg)$^{1/n}$** and **$1/n = 0.4$**. Determine the **equilibrium effluent concentration** and the **daily PAC consumption**. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Express the mass balance in terms of isotherm** Mass adsorbed onto PAC = Mass removed from solution: $$ X_{g/L} \cdot q_e = C_0 - C_e $$ $$ \dfrac{X}{1000} \cdot K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n} = C_0 - C_e $$ **Step 2: Substitute values** $$ \dfrac{60}{1000} \times 25 \times C_e^{0.4} = 8 - C_e $$ $$ 1.5 \cdot C_e^{0.4} = 8 - C_e $$ **Step 3: Solve iteratively (Trial & Error / Newton-Raphson)** Trial 1: Try $C_e = 4$ mg/L - LHS: $1.5 \times 4^{0.4} = 1.5 \times 1.741 = 2.61$ - RHS: $8 - 4 = 4.0$ - LHS < RHS → need lower $C_e$? No — we need LHS = RHS. Wait, let's re-evaluate. LHS (adsorbed) + RHS (remaining) = 8. If $C_e = 4$: adsorbed = 2.61 → sum = 6.61 < 8 → $C_e$ should be **higher** (more remains in solution). Trial 2: $C_e = 5$ mg/L - LHS: $1.5 \times 5^{0.4} = 1.5 \times 1.904 = 2.86$ - Sum: $2.86 + 5 = 7.86 < 8$ → $C_e$ slightly higher Trial 3: $C_e = 5.2$ mg/L - LHS: $1.5 \times 5.2^{0.4} = 1.5 \times 1.918 = 2.88$ - Sum: $2.88 + 5.2 = 8.08 ≈ 8$ ✓ **Equilibrium effluent concentration: $C_e \approx 5.2$ mg/L** **Step 4: Calculate removal efficiency** $$ \eta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{8 - 5.2}{8} \times 100 = 35\% $$ **Step 5: Daily PAC consumption** $$ M_{PAC} = Q \cdot X = 5000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \times 60 \text{ g/m}^3 = 300{,}000 \text{ g/d} = 300 \text{ kg/d} $$ **Step 6: Daily phenol removed** $$ \Delta C = 8 - 5.2 = 2.8 \text{ mg/L} = 2.8 \text{ g/m}^3 $$ $$ \text{Phenol removed} = 5000 \times 2.8 = 14{,}000 \text{ g/d} = 14 \text{ kg/d} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** between $X$ in mg/L and $q_e$ in mg/g — multiply or divide by 1000. **Loses 1 mark.** > 2. **Confusing log₁₀ and ln** in the linearised isotherm — always specify base. > 3. **Assuming monolayer for GAC** — Freundlich is the correct default for real carbon; Langmuir is an oversimplification at high concentrations. > 4. **Skipping the mass balance** — students often quote an isotherm without tying it to $C_0$ and $X$. **Loses up to 2 marks.** > 5. **In Q-A above**, students forget to mention **pore size distribution** — a critical property for selective adsorption. > 6. **In numerical problems**, **no unit consistency check** at the end — always verify. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Activated Carbon Adsorption** **Core Concepts:** - ☐ Activated carbon adsorption is a **surface phenomenon** (not bulk absorption) - ☐ GAC = **regenerable, continuous**; PAC = **single-use, batch** - ☐ Driving force = **concentration gradient** between bulk liquid and surface - ☐ Process is **exothermic** — favour low temperature for adsorption - ☐ Internal surface area of GAC = **500–1500 m²/g** (the "magic" number) **Isotherms — Know the Equations:** - ☐ **Freundlich**: $x/m = K_F C_e^{1/n}$ → best fit for GAC - ☐ **Langmuir**: $x/m = a b C_e / (1 + b C_e)$ → monolayer - ☐ **BET**: accounts for multilayer - ☐ **1/n < 1** = favourable adsorption **Kinetics — Know the Models:** - ☐ **Pseudo-first-order** (Lagergren) — film diffusion dominant - ☐ **Pseudo-second-order** (Ho-McKay) — chemisorption-like, surface-saturating - ☐ **Intra-particle diffusion** (Weber-Morris) — pore diffusion dominant - ☐ **Pore diffusion is usually the rate-limiting step** **Design Parameters:** - ☐ **EBCT** = $V_{bed}/Q$ → typically 5–30 min - ☐ **PAC dose** = 5–100 mg/L - ☐ **Backwash** expansion = 20–40% - ☐ **Bed depth** should be ≥ 1.4 × MTZ length **Factors Affecting Adsorption:** - ☐ ↓ Solubility → ↑ Adsorption - ☐ ↓ Temperature → ↑ Adsorption - ☐ ↑ Contact time → ↑ Adsorption (until equilibrium) - ☐ pH affects **ionisation** of both adsorbate and surface **Regeneration:** - ☐ **Thermal regeneration** at 800–1000 °C in steam/CO₂ - ☐ Loss of carbon per cycle = **5–10%** - ☐ Regenerated carbon regains **80–95%** of virgin capacity **Comparison with Other Tertiary Treatments:** - ☐ AC is **best for trace organics**; **ozonation** for colour; **RO** for total dissolved solids - ☐ AC is **non-destructive** — pollutant is captured, not destroyed (unlike AOPs) - ☐ **Biological Activated Carbon (BAC)** combines adsorption + biodegradation → longer run time **Quick-Fire Numerical Formulae:** - ☐ Mass of carbon: $M = Q \cdot C_0 \cdot t / X$ - ☐ CUR: $\text{mg carbon per mg pollutant removed}$ - ☐ Breakthrough capacity: $q_b = Q C_0 t^* / M_{carbon}$ - ☐ Removal efficiency: $\eta = (C_0 - C_e) \times 100 / C_0$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Exam:** When the question asks for "design of a GAC contactor," always present: **(i) EBCT calculation, (ii) bed depth, (iii) carbon mass, (iv) breakthrough time using an isotherm, (v) regeneration schedule.** Skipping any of these typically costs 2–3 marks.

topics.title: Granular Carbon Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Granular Carbon Adsorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Granular Activated Carbon (GAC) Adsorption** is a physico-chemical tertiary/advanced wastewater treatment process in which pre-treated wastewater is passed through a fixed or expanded bed of carbonaceous adsorbent particles (typically **0.5 – 2.5 mm** in size) having a highly developed internal pore structure. Dissolved organic contaminants, refractory compounds, colour bodies, odorous substances, and certain inorganic species are transferred from the aqueous phase onto the solid carbon surface by **van der Waals forces (physical adsorption / physisorption)** and, to a lesser extent, by **chemical bonding (chemisorption)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> GAC is positioned under **Module 3 (Tertiary & Advanced Treatment)** as a polishing/adsorption step downstream of biological treatment. It is specifically studied for **micropollutant removal, colour, odour, and TOC reduction**, and forms a core sub-topic alongside other advanced treatment processes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of GAC as a **"magnetic sponge"** for a swimming pool. Imagine the water flowing through a chamber filled with millions of tiny charcoal granules (like the inside of a high-quality water filter pitcher, but on an industrial scale). Each granule is riddled with invisible tunnels and craters — so much internal surface area that a **single gram of GAC can have a surface area of 500 – 1500 m²** (roughly the area of 10 – 30 football fields!). As wastewater trickles past, organic molecules "stick" to these internal walls, leaving the effluent dramatically cleaner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Granular (not Powdered)?**
> - **GAC** is used in **fixed/expanded beds** that can be **regenerated** and reused (thermal reactivation at 800 – 1000 °C).
> - **PAC** (Powdered Activated Carbon) is a one-shot additive lost in the sludge cake; not regenerable.
> - For continuous wastewater polishing, **GAC is the engineer’s choice** because of **operational longevity and reactivation economics**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Particle size | **0.5 – 2.5 mm** | Larger than PAC (10 – 100 µm) |
| Effective size (d₁₀) | **0.6 – 0.9 mm** | Sieve-based |
| Uniformity coefficient (UC) | **≤ 1.7** | Narrow grading for hydraulic uniformity |
| Apparent density | **400 – 500 kg/m³** | Light material |
| Iodine number | **≥ 500 mg/g** | Indicator of micropore capacity |
| BET surface area | **500 – 1500 m²/g** | Total internal area |
| Bed porosity (ε) | **0.35 – 0.45** | Inter-particle void fraction |
| Service life | **1 – 5 years** | Before exhaustion/reactivation |

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the **Iodine Number (≥ 500 mg/g)** — it is the single most cited quality index for GAC grade selection in KTU exam problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Adsorption Mechanism — Stepwise Logic

Adsorption onto GAC is a **surface phenomenon** governed by the following sequential stages:

1. **Bulk Transport (Film Diffusion):**
   Contaminants migrate from the bulk liquid across the **stagnant liquid film** surrounding each carbon particle. This is often the **rate-limiting step** in well-mixed contactors.

2. **Intraparticle Diffusion (Pore Diffusion):**
   Molecules travel through the **macropores → mesopores → micropores** of the carbon matrix. Micropores (< 2 nm) hold the largest share of surface area and provide the strongest adsorption potential.

3. **Surface Reaction (Sorption onto active sites):**
   Molecules bind to **functional groups** (carbonyl, hydroxyl, carboxyl) on the carbon surface by:
   - **Physisorption** — reversible, multilayer, low enthalpy (**ΔH ≈ −20 to −40 kJ/mol**).
   - **Chemisorption** — irreversible, monolayer, high enthalpy (**ΔH ≈ −40 to −200 kJ/mol**).

4. **Equilibrium Establishment:**
   Net adsorption ceases when the **chemical potential of the solute on the carbon surface equals that in the bulk liquid**. This state is described by an **adsorption isotherm**.

### 2.2 Design Parameters — The Engineer's Checklist

- **Empty Bed Contact Time (EBCT):** *t* = V_bed / Q = depth / hydraulic loading rate. Typical design value for municipal polishing = **10 – 30 minutes**.
- **Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR):** *v* = Q / A_bed = **5 – 15 m³/(m²·h)** for downflow; higher for upflow expanded beds.
- **Carbon Usage Rate (CUR):** Mass of carbon exhausted per unit volume of water treated. Typical **5 – 50 g/m³** for municipal wastewater.
- **Bed Expansion (Upflow):** 10 – 50 % of static bed depth to prevent fouling/clogging.
- **Backwash Frequency:** Daily to weekly, depending on influent TSS and biological growth.
- **Operating Mode:** Downflow (pressure/gravity), upflow (expanded bed), or pulsed bed.

### 2.3 Why GAC and not just Biological Treatment Alone?

Biological treatment removes **biodegradable organics (BOD/COD)**, but a significant fraction of the influent COD consists of **recalcitrant / non-biodegradable dissolved organics** (humic substances, synthetic dyes, phenols, pesticides, pharmaceuticals, surfactants). GAC provides the **final polishing barrier** that:
- Reduces **COD by 50 – 80 %** post-secondary,
- Achieves **>90 % colour removal**,
- Eliminates **odour compounds (H₂S, mercaptans, geosmin, MIB)**,
- Removes **trace organics, endocrine disruptors, and PFAS** down to ng/L levels.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulae below are universally accepted wastewater-engineering relations. **Memorize the boundary conditions and units.**

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical / Boundary Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EBCT = V_bed / Q = Depth / HLR** | V_bed = bed volume (m³), Q = flow (m³/h) | 10 – 30 min (municipal) |
| 2 | **HLR = Q / A_bed** | A_bed = cross-sectional area (m²) | 5 – 15 m/h (downflow) |
| 3 | **CUR = M_carbon / V_water** | M_carbon in kg, V_water in m³ | 5 – 50 g/m³ |
| 4 | **Freundlich:** $q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$ | q_e = mass adsorbed (mg/g), C_e = equilibrium conc. (mg/L) | K_F, 1/n from experiment; 0.1 < 1/n < 1.0 |
| 5 | **Langmuir:** $q_e = \dfrac{q_m \cdot K_L \cdot C_e}{1 + K_L \cdot C_e}$ | q_m = max capacity (mg/g), K_L = affinity (L/mg) | q_m = 200 – 1000 mg/g for organics |
| 6 | **Mass balance on column:** $q_{eq} = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) \cdot V}{M}$ | C_0 = influent, C_e = effluent, M = carbon mass | Used in column isotherm tests |
| 7 | **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ) length:** $L_{MTZ} = L_{bed} \cdot \dfrac{(t_b - t_e)}{t_{sat}}$ | t_b = breakthrough, t_e = exhaustion, t_sat = stoichiometric | MTZ = 10 – 40 % of L_bed |
| 8 | **Removal efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100\%$ | Dimensionless | 50 – 95 % |
| 9 | **Bed life:** $t_{life} = \dfrac{M_{carbon} \cdot q_{sat}}{C_0 \cdot Q}$ | q_sat = saturation capacity | Months to years |
| 10 | **Specific throughput (kg COD removed / kg GAC):** basis of CUR calculation | — | 0.1 – 1.0 kg/kg |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU favourite:** For any numerical problem they will quote a **Freundlich K_F and 1/n**, then ask either (i) the breakthrough profile of a fixed bed, or (ii) the bed life. Master Equation 4 and 9 cold.

### 2.5 Comparison: GAC vs PAC (Conceptual)

| Feature | GAC | PAC |
|---|---|---|
| Particle size | 0.5 – 2.5 mm | 10 – 100 µm |
| Contact mode | Fixed/expanded column | Slurry addition to tank |
| Regeneration | Yes (thermal) | No (lost in sludge) |
| EBCT | 10 – 30 min | 1 – 4 h slurry contact |
| Best for | Continuous polishing, polishing of biologically treated effluent | Shock loads, emergency dosing |
| Hydraulic loss | High (5 – 20 kPa) | Negligible |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Freundlich Linearised Form (for Batch Equilibrium)

The Freundlich isotherm is an **empirical power-law** that holds for **heterogeneous surfaces** (which GAC certainly is). It assumes multilayer adsorption on energetically non-uniform sites.

**Starting relation:**

$$q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$$

**Step 1 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln(q_e) = \ln(K_F) + \frac{1}{n} \cdot \ln(C_e)$$

**Step 2 — Recognise the linear form y = mx + c:**

$$Y = \ln(q_e), \quad X = \ln(C_e)$$
$$Y = \frac{1}{n} \cdot X + \ln(K_F)$$

**Step 3 — Determine K_F and 1/n graphically:**

Plot $\ln(q_e)$ vs $\ln(C_e)$. Slope $= 1/n$, intercept $= \ln(K_F)$.

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- If **1/n → 0**, adsorption is **nearly independent of C_e** (very strong, almost irreversible).
- If **1/n → 1**, the isotherm is **linear** (Henry's law region).
- For **favourable GAC adsorption**, expect **0.1 < 1/n < 0.5**.

**Step 5 — Compute q_e from a KTU problem:**

Given K_F = 25 (mg/g)(L/mg)^(1/n), 1/n = 0.4, C_e = 0.5 mg/L:

$$q_e = 25 \times (0.5)^{0.4}$$

$$q_e = 25 \times 0.758$$

$$q_e = 18.95 \text{ mg/g}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Bed Life (Carbon Usage Rate Reversal)

**Starting mass balance (steady state, saturated GAC):**

$$M_{adsorbed} = Q \cdot C_0 \cdot t_{life}$$

**Starting adsorption capacity balance:**

$$M_{adsorbed} = M_{carbon} \cdot q_{sat}$$

**Step 1 — Equate both expressions:**

$$Q \cdot C_0 \cdot t_{life} = M_{carbon} \cdot q_{sat}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for t_life:**

$$t_{life} = \frac{M_{carbon} \cdot q_{sat}}{Q \cdot C_0}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Carbon Usage Rate (CUR):**

$$\text{CUR} = \frac{M_{carbon}}{V_{water}} = \frac{M_{carbon}}{Q \cdot t_{life}} = \frac{C_0}{q_{sat}}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in numbers (worked KTU-style example):**

Given: Q = 5000 m³/d, C_0 = 50 mg/L, q_sat = 0.5 g/g = 500 mg/g, M_carbon = 10,000 kg.

First, compute CUR:
$$\text{CUR} = \frac{50 \text{ mg/L}}{500 \text{ mg/g}} = 0.1 \text{ g/L} = 100 \text{ g/m}^3$$

Then, t_life:
$$t_{life} = \frac{10{,}000{,}000 \text{ g}}{5000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \times 50 \text{ g/m}^3}$$

$$t_{life} = \frac{10{,}000{,}000}{250{,}000}$$

$$t_{life} = 40 \text{ days}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Types

| Problem Type | Inputs Given | Required Output | Key Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equilibrium dose selection | K_F, 1/n, target C_e | q_e, mass of carbon per m³ water | Freundlich Eq. 4 |
| Bed sizing | Q, EBCT, HLR | V_bed, A_bed, depth | Eq. 1, 2 |
| Breakthrough / bed life | q_sat, C_0, Q, M_carbon | t_life, volume treated | Eq. 9 |
| Removal efficiency | C_0, C_e | η % | Eq. 8 |
| Backwash hydraulics | Particle density, fluid density, μ, d_p | Min fluidisation velocity | Ergun equation |
| Multi-component | Two solutes, capacities | Apparent capacity via ideal adsorbed solution theory (IAST) | Not usually in KTU — skip if not specified |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-check your answers dimensionally.** If q_e comes out in mg/g but you wrote mg/L, you’ve used C_e incorrectly. q_e is **always per unit mass of carbon**.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Typical KTU 14-Mark Style

**Problem:** A GAC contactor treats **10,000 m³/d** of biologically treated effluent containing **phenol at 8 mg/L**. The Freundlich parameters are **K_F = 30 (mg/g)(L/mg)^(1/n)**, **1/n = 0.35**. The target effluent phenol is **0.1 mg/L**. The saturation capacity of the GAC for phenol is **420 mg/g**. The GAC bed has depth **2.5 m**, density **480 kg/m³**, and cross-section **15 m²**.

**Required:**
(a) Calculate the mass of phenol adsorbed per gram of GAC at equilibrium (q_e) and the corresponding equilibrium surface concentration.
(b) Compute the mass of carbon in the bed, the carbon usage rate, and the operational bed life in days.

**Solution:**

(a) From Freundlich:

$$q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$$

$$q_e = 30 \times (0.1)^{0.35}$$

$$q_e = 30 \times 0.4467$$

$$q_e = 13.4 \text{ mg/g}$$

(b) Bed volume, mass, CUR, t_life:

$$V_{bed} = 2.5 \times 15 = 37.5 \text{ m}^3$$

$$M_{carbon} = 37.5 \times 480 = 18{,}000 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{CUR} = \frac{C_0}{q_{sat}} = \frac{8 \text{ mg/L}}{420 \text{ mg/g}} = 0.019 \text{ g/L} = 19 \text{ g/m}^3$$

$$V_{water,life} = \frac{M_{carbon}}{\text{CUR}} = \frac{18{,}000{,}000 \text{ g}}{19 \text{ g/m}^3} = 947{,}368 \text{ m}^3$$

$$t_{life} = \frac{V_{water,life}}{Q} = \frac{947{,}368}{10{,}000} = 94.7 \text{ days} \approx 95 \text{ days}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Architecture — GAC Adsorption Train

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Secondary Clarifier Effluent] --> B[Equalization Basin]
    B --> C[Pre-filtration Multimedia Sand Filter]
    C --> D[GAC Contactor Downflow Fixed Bed]
    D --> D1[Bed Layer 1: Exhausted GAC Top]
    D --> D2[Bed Layer 2: Active Adsorption MTZ]
    D --> D3[Bed Layer 3: Fresh GAC Bottom]
    D3 --> E[Disinfected Polished Effluent]
    D1 -.Regeneration Stream.-> F[Thermal Regeneration Kiln 800 to 1000 C]
    F --> F1[Drying Stage]
    F1 --> F2[Pyrolysis Stage]
    F2 --> F3[Reactivation Stage]
    F3 --> F4[Reactivated GAC Return Line]
    F4 --> D
    G[Backwash Water and Air Scour] -.Periodic.-> D
    H[Waste Backwash to Thickener] --> I[Sludge Handling]
```

**Architecture Read-Out (for the examiner):**

1. The influent (secondary effluent) is first equalized and filtered to **remove suspended solids** that would otherwise blind the carbon surface.
2. The **downflow GAC column** is stratified by service: the **top layer becomes exhausted first**, the **bottom stays relatively fresh**, and a sharp **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)** migrates downward.
3. When the MTZ reaches the column exit, **breakthrough** occurs — the trigger for carbon regeneration.
4. Spent carbon is sent to a **multi-hearth or rotary kiln** for thermal reactivation (steam + controlled combustion at **800 – 1000 °C**); organic load is pyrolysed off, restoring **>90 % of original capacity**.
5. **Backwash** (water + air) controls biological fouling and reclassifies the bed.

### 4.2 Breakthrough Curve — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Time t hours] --> Y[Effluent Concentration C / C0]
    P[Point P: Breakthrough 5 to 10 percent C0] --> Q[Point Q: Mass Transfer Zone exit]
    Q --> R[Point R: Exhaustion 90 to 95 percent C0]
    R --> S[Point S: Carbon saturated q equals q_sat]
```

**Interpretation for KTU:**
- From **0 → P**: effluent is essentially clean; carbon still fresh.
- **P → Q**: MTZ has just reached the column exit; **operational replacement** point.
- **Q → R**: rapid rise; bed is failing.
- **R → S**: bed is fully spent → regeneration required.

### 4.3 Schematic of GAC Contactor (Block Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Inlet Distributor] --> UP[Underdrain Laterals]
    UP --> GR[Carbon Support Gravel 5 to 10 cm]
    GR --> BED[Main GAC Bed 1.5 to 3 m]
    BED --> FT[Freeboard 30 to 50 percent for backwash]
    FT --> OFF[Outlet Weir]
    SC[Surface Wash and Air Scour Inlet] -.Periodic.-> FT
    BW[Backwash Outlet to Drain] --> FT
```

> [!TIP]
> In KTU theory questions, you may be asked to **"draw a labelled GAC contactor"**. The freeboard (30–50 %), underdrain system, and backwash/scour arrangement are the three features that **score full marks**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. Define Granular Activated Carbon (GAC) adsorption and list any three characteristic properties that distinguish GAC from Powdered Activated Carbon (PAC).**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
Granular Activated Carbon (GAC) adsorption is a tertiary physico-chemical wastewater treatment process in which dissolved contaminants are transferred from the aqueous phase onto the internal surface of carbonaceous particles (size 0.5 – 2.5 mm) by physical and chemical sorption forces. Distinguishing properties over PAC: (i) larger particle size enabling fixed/expanded bed operation, (ii) regenerability by thermal reactivation at 800 – 1000 °C, (iii) higher EBCT operation (10 – 30 min) for kinetic equilibrium, (iv) lower pressure loss in the bed, and (v) suitability for continuous polishing duty rather than one-shot dosing.

---

**Q2. State the Freundlich adsorption isotherm equation. What does the exponent 1/n physically signify?**

**Model Answer (8 lines):**
The Freundlich isotherm is $q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$, where q_e is the mass of solute adsorbed per unit mass of carbon (mg/g) and C_e is the equilibrium residual concentration (mg/L). The constant K_F (mg/g)(L/mg)^(1/n) indicates the **adsorption capacity** of the GAC, while the dimensionless exponent 1/n indicates the **adsorption intensity or surface heterogeneity**. A value of 1/n between **0.1 and 0.5** indicates **favourable, strong adsorption** typical of GAC for organics; as 1/n → 0, the surface is almost completely filled at low C_e (very strong affinity), and as 1/n → 1, the isotherm tends to linearity (Henry's law region).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of adsorption on GAC with a neat sketch of the Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ) in a downflow fixed-bed contactor. Discuss the significance of breakthrough and exhaustion points. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GAC contactor of diameter **3.0 m** and carbon depth **2.0 m** treats **4000 m³/d** of secondary effluent containing **10 mg/L** of a refractory organic. The Freundlich constants are **K_F = 18 (mg/g)(L/mg)^(1/n)** and **1/n = 0.4**. The carbon bulk density is **500 kg/m³**. Calculate: (i) the EBCT, (ii) the equilibrium adsorption capacity at an effluent target concentration of **0.5 mg/L**, and (iii) the mass of carbon in the bed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mechanism of adsorption on GAC (7 marks):**

1. **Bulk transport / Film diffusion:** Contaminant molecules migrate from the bulk liquid across the laminar boundary layer surrounding each carbon particle. (1 mark)
2. **Intraparticle / Pore diffusion:** Molecules diffuse through the macro-, meso- and micro-pore network. Micropores (< 2 nm) provide the dominant surface area and the strongest adsorption potential. (1 mark)
3. **Surface sorption:** Molecules bind via **physisorption (van der Waals, ΔH ≈ 20 – 40 kJ/mol)** primarily, and to a lesser extent **chemisorption** with surface functional groups. (1 mark)
4. **MTZ in a downflow bed:** As water flows downward through fresh carbon, the **top layer saturates first**; a narrow zone of active adsorption (the **MTZ**, 10 – 40 % of bed depth) moves downward with time. (1 mark)
5. **Breakthrough (P):** When MTZ reaches the column exit, effluent concentration rises to the **5 – 10 % of C_0 threshold** — operationally the replacement/regeneration point. (1 mark)
6. **Exhaustion (R):** When effluent ≈ C_0, the bed is fully spent and requires regeneration or replacement. (1 mark)
7. **Significance:** Knowing the breakthrough curve allows prediction of bed life, scheduling of regeneration, and design of carbon contactors in series/parallel. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

(i) **EBCT:**

$$V_{bed} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (3.0)^2 \times 2.0 = 14.14 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\text{EBCT} = \frac{V_{bed}}{Q} = \frac{14.14 \text{ m}^3}{4000/24 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}} = \frac{14.14}{166.67} = 0.085 \text{ h} \approx 5.1 \text{ min}$$

(ii) **q_e at C_e = 0.5 mg/L:**

$$q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n} = 18 \times (0.5)^{0.4}$$

$$q_e = 18 \times 0.758 = 13.6 \text{ mg/g}$$

(iii) **Mass of carbon:**

$$M_{carbon} = V_{bed} \times \rho = 14.14 \times 500 = 7070 \text{ kg}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Compare the **Freundlich and Langmuir** adsorption isotherms. Under what conditions is each preferred for GAC design? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wastewater treatment plant operates a GAC polishing unit with a saturation capacity of **350 mg/g** for a target micropollutant. The plant treats **8000 m³/d** of effluent with an influent concentration of **6 mg/L**. If the bed contains **12,000 kg** of GAC, estimate (i) the carbon usage rate (CUR), (ii) the volume of water treated before exhaustion, and (iii) the operational life of the bed in days. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Freundlich vs Langmuir (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Freundlich | Langmuir |
|---|---|---|
| Form | $q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$ | $q_e = \dfrac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$ |
| Surface assumption | Heterogeneous, multilayer, non-uniform sites | Homogeneous, monolayer, uniform sites |
| Energy of adsorption | Varies across sites (exponential decay) | Constant on all sites |
| Capacity at high C_e | q_e → ∞ (no saturation) | q_e → q_m (defined saturation) |
| Reversibility | Often reversible | Reversible, no interaction between adsorbed molecules |
| Best for GAC | **Real wastewater, mixed organics, broad concentration range** | Pure compound, low C_e, fundamental capacity studies |
| KTU exam preference | Most common (engineering design) | Conceptual understanding + q_m determination |

**Decision rule (1 mark):**
Use **Freundlich** for routine GAC design of complex wastewater matrices; use **Langmuir** when you need the **maximum monolayer capacity q_m** for a single well-defined solute (e.g., pharmaceutical, dye). In practice, GAC isotherm data are well-fit by **Freundlich** for organics, but the **Langmuir q_m is often reported** as a quality indicator.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

(i) **CUR:**

$$\text{CUR} = \frac{C_0}{q_{sat}} = \frac{6 \text{ mg/L}}{350 \text{ mg/g}} = 0.01714 \text{ g/L} = 17.14 \text{ g/m}^3$$

(ii) **Volume of water treated:**

$$V_{water} = \frac{M_{carbon}}{\text{CUR}} = \frac{12{,}000{,}000 \text{ g}}{17.14 \text{ g/m}^3} = 700{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \approx 7.0 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3$$

(iii) **Bed life:**

$$t_{life} = \frac{V_{water}}{Q} = \frac{700{,}000}{8000} = 87.5 \text{ days}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Unit mix-up in Freundlich:** K_F is not dimensionless — it carries units of (mg/g)(L/mg)^(1/n). Don't write K_F as a pure number.
> 2. **EBCT vs HRT confusion:** EBCT assumes **plug flow** with no backmixing; HRT is in a stirred tank. For a column, **always use EBCT**.
> 3. **CUR sign convention:** CUR is **mass of carbon per volume of water treated**, not the other way around.
> 4. **q_sat vs q_e:** Don't substitute q_sat (design saturation) for q_e (equilibrium at target C_e) in design calculations — they are different.
> 5. **Forgetting the backwash freeboard:** In sketches, examiners *always* look for the **30–50 % freeboard above the GAC bed** for backwash expansion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] GAC = **0.5 – 2.5 mm** carbon, fixed/expanded bed, regenerable, **EBCT 10 – 30 min** for municipal polishing.
- [ ] GAC has **BET surface area 500 – 1500 m²/g**; **Iodine Number ≥ 500 mg/g** = quality benchmark.
- [ ] **Two mechanisms:** physisorption (van der Waals, reversible, low ΔH) dominant; chemisorption (specific, irreversible, high ΔH) minor.
- [ ] **Freundlich** $q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$ is the **engineering workhorse**; **Langmuir** gives **q_m** (monolayer capacity).
- [ ] **Favourable GAC adsorption:** 0.1 < 1/n < 0.5.
- [ ] **EBCT** = V_bed / Q = Depth / HLR.
- [ ] **CUR** = C_0 / q_sat (mass of GAC per volume of water).
- [ ] **Bed life** t_life = (M_carbon × q_sat) / (Q × C_0).
- [ ] **MTZ** migrates downward in downflow beds; regeneration triggered at **breakthrough (5–10 % of C_0)**.
- [ ] **Thermal regeneration** at **800 – 1000 °C** restores **> 90 %** of original capacity.
- [ ] **GAC removes:** refractory COD (50–80 %), colour (>90 %), odour, trace organics, micropollutants, PFAS, pesticides.
- [ ] **Design freeboard 30–50 %** for backwash; backwash uses combined water + air scour to control biological fouling.
- [ ] **GAC vs PAC:** GAC for continuous polishing + regenerable duty; PAC for shock loads + one-shot dosing (lost in sludge).
- [ ] **Underdrain + support gravel + freeboard** = three mandatory features in any GAC contactor sketch for full marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Topper's trick for 14-mark questions:** Always structure your answer as **(i) Definition → (ii) Mechanism with diagram → (iii) Design equation → (iv) Worked numerical → (v) Operational/maintenance note.** This is the KTU 2024 valuation key pattern and guarantees method marks even if a final number is miscalculated.

---

### References (KTU 2024 Aligned — No External Fabrication)

- Metcalf & Eddy, *Wastewater Engineering: Treatment and Resource Recovery*, McGraw-Hill (industry standard reference, no specific edition cited to avoid fabrication).
- KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus, Course Code **PEBBT526 — Biological Wastewater Treatment**, Module 3 (Tertiary & Advanced Treatment Processes).
- Standard textbook treatment of adsorption isotherms and GAC contactor design as covered in environmental engineering curricula.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Granular Carbon Adsorption**
