topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reactor Configurations. Advanced Oxidation Processes: Theory of Advanced Oxidation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Reactor Configurations in Biological Wastewater Treatment

A **Reactor Configuration** refers to the engineered geometric and hydraulic arrangement of the vessel in which biological, chemical, or physico-chemical reactions occur during wastewater treatment. The configuration determines the **flow regime (mixing pattern)**, **substrate utilization kinetics**, **biomass retention strategy**, and ultimately the **effluent quality**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A reactor configuration in wastewater engineering is a physical system (tank, basin, or contactor) characterized by its **hydraulic flow pattern** (continuous vs. batch, mixed vs. plug-flow) and **biomass retention mode** (suspended growth vs. attached growth) within which biochemical oxidation of organic matter is carried out.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Reactors

**A. Based on Hydraulic Flow Pattern**
1. **Batch Reactor** — Feed filled, treated, and discharged in discrete cycles.
2. **Continuous-Flow Reactors:**
   - **Completely Mixed / Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** — Perfect back-mixing; uniform composition throughout.
   - **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** — No back-mixing; fluid elements move as discrete "plugs."
   - **Dispersed Plug Flow Reactor** — Real-world intermediate between PFR and CSTR.

**B. Based on Biomass Growth Mode**
1. **Suspended Growth** — Biomass freely dispersed in mixed liquor (e.g., Activated Sludge, SBR).
2. **Attached Growth** — Biomass fixed on a medium (e.g., Trickling Filter, RBC, MBBR).
3. **Hybrid / Membrane-Coupled** — Combination (e.g., MBR, IFAS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Reactor as a Cooking Pot:**
> Think of a kitchen pot cooking a curry.
> - A **CSTR** is like a continuously stirred large pot: ingredients are added and mixed instantly — every drop has the same taste at any instant.
> - A **PFR** is like a long conveyor passing through an oven: each "slice" of food experiences different conditions along the line.
> - A **Batch Reactor** is like preparing a single dish: fill, cook, serve, repeat.
> Choosing the right "pot" is what makes a treatment plant efficient or inefficient.

---

## 1.2 Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) — Theory

### 1.2.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** **Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)** are defined as a set of chemical treatment procedures designed to remove organic (and in some cases inorganic) pollutants in water and wastewater by oxidation through reactions with **highly reactive hydroxyl radicals (•OH)** — typically generated *in situ* by combining oxidants (O₃, H₂O₂), catalysts (Fe²⁺, TiO₂), and/or energy sources (UV, ultrasound, electron beam).

AOPs are characterized by:
- Generation of the **hydroxyl radical (•OH)** as the primary oxidant.
- Oxidation potential of **E° = +2.80 V (vs. SHE)** — second only to fluorine.
- **Non-selective, near-diffusion-controlled** reaction rates ($k \approx 10^{8} \text{ to } 10^{10} \; M^{-1}s^{-1}$).
- Capability to **fully mineralize** recalcitrant compounds to CO₂, H₂O, and inorganic salts.

### 1.2.2 Why AOPs? — The Need

Conventional biological treatment fails when wastewater contains:
- **Biorefractory organics** (pesticides, pharmaceuticals, dyes, phenols, surfactants).
- **Priority Pollutants** (USEPA list).
- **Endocrine Disrupting Compounds (EDCs)**.
- **Compounds toxic to microbes** (cyanide, high-COD industrial effluents).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — AOPs as the "Special Forces":**
> Imagine your regular activated-sludge army (microbes) refuses to attack certain "guerrilla" pollutants because they are too tough or poisonous. AOPs are like calling in **Special Forces (•OH radicals)** that have the highest destructive power, attack indiscriminately, and leave no trace (CO₂ + H₂O). They are expensive, so you use them only when the army fails.

### 1.2.3 Classification of AOPs (Theory-Based)

| Family | Energy/Agent Source | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Ozone-based** | Chemical | O₃, O₃/H₂O₂, O₃/UV, O₃/catalyst |
| **Hydrogen Peroxide-based** | Chemical | H₂O₂/Fe²⁺ (Fenton), H₂O₂/UV, H₂O₂/Fe²⁺/UV (Photo-Fenton) |
| **Photocatalytic** | UV + Semiconductor | TiO₂/UV, ZnO/UV |
| **Electrochemical** | Electrical | Anodic oxidation, BDD electrodes, Electro-Fenton |
| **Wet Air Oxidation** | High T & P | WAO, CWAO (catalytic) |
| **Sonochemical** | Ultrasound (US) | US/Fe²⁺, US/O₃, US/H₂O₂ |
| **Hybrid / Coupled** | Multiple | UV/O₃/H₂O₂; H₂O₂/Fe³⁺/US |

---

## 1.3 Physical & Operational Metrics (Standard Constants)

- **Standard reduction potential of •OH:** **E° = +2.80 V (SHE)** (second highest after F₂ at +2.87 V).
- **Half-life of •OH in water:** **~ 10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁶ seconds** (extremely short).
- **Diffusion-controlled rate constant with organic substrates:** **k ≈ 10⁸ – 10¹⁰ M⁻¹ s⁻¹**.
- **Steady-state [•OH] in AOP reactors:** typically **10⁻¹² – 10⁻¹⁵ M**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The extremely short lifetime and low steady-state concentration make direct measurement of •OH impractical. AOP performance is therefore **inferred** from target pollutant disappearance or from the use of probe compounds (e.g., pCBA — *para*-chlorobenzoic acid).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reactor Configurations — Theory of Operation

### 2.1.1 Batch Reactor

- **Operation:** Fill → React → Settle → Draw → Idle (repeated).
- **Mass balance (time-dependent):** $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -r$
- **Substrate removal follows:** $C = C_0 \, e^{-kt}$ (first-order).
- **No inflow/outflow during reaction** → simple, flexible, no short-circuiting.
- **Examples:** SBR (Sequencing Batch Reactor), equalization basins used for batch studies.

> [!TIP]
> SBR is the most common "batch" application in municipal wastewater treatment. A typical 4-hour cycle: Fill (1 h) → React (2 h) → Settle (0.5 h) → Draw (0.5 h).

### 2.1.2 Completely Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)

- **Assumption:** Influent disperses **instantaneously** → concentration everywhere = effluent concentration.
- **Steady-state mass balance:** $0 = Q(C_0 - C) - Vr$
- **For first-order kinetics:** $C = \dfrac{C_0}{1 + k\theta}$, where $\theta = V/Q$ (HRT).
- **Advantages:** Robust to shock loads, simple design.
- **Disadvantage:** Effluent substrate concentration always equals in-reactor concentration → higher residual.

### 2.1.3 Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)

- **Assumption:** No axial mixing; each "plug" has a fixed residence time $\theta$.
- **Steady-state differential balance:** $Q \, \dfrac{dC}{dx} = -r$
- **For first-order kinetics:** $\dfrac{C}{C_0} = e^{-k\theta}$
- **For Monod kinetics (numerical integration required).**
- **Examples:** Long aeration tanks, plug-flow aerated lagoons, primary clarifier weirs modeled as PFR segments.

### 2.1.4 Comparison: PFR vs CSTR (First-Order Substrate Removal)

For the **same** volume, flow, and influent concentration:
- **PFR effluent:** $C_{PFR} = C_0 \, e^{-k\theta}$ → lower residual → more efficient.
- **CSTR effluent:** $C_{CSTR} = \dfrac{C_0}{1 + k\theta}$ → higher residual → less efficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Insight:** The PFR is mathematically always more efficient than a single CSTR for a first-order or any monotonic decreasing kinetic expression. Multiple CSTRs in series approach PFR behavior.

### 2.1.5 Attached-Growth Configurations

- **Trickling Filter** — Wastewater sprayed over rock/plastic media; biofilm forms; air flows naturally or forced.
- **Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)** — Rotating discs partly submerged; biofilm alternates air/water exposure.
- **Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor (MBBR)** — Free-floating plastic carriers in aerated tank.
- **Fluidized Bed Bioreactor (FBBR)** — Sand/biomass particles fluidized by upward flow.

### 2.1.6 Membrane Bioreactor (MBR)

- Combines activated sludge with **ultrafiltration/microfiltration membranes**.
- **SRT and HRT decoupled** → very high MLSS (8,000–12,000 mg/L), small footprint, excellent effluent.
- Disadvantage: membrane fouling, energy cost.

---

## 2.2 Advanced Oxidation Processes — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 The Hydroxyl Radical (•OH) — Nature

- **Electronic structure:** One unpaired electron; highly electrophilic.
- **Reaction modes:** Hydrogen abstraction, electrophilic addition, electron transfer.
- **End-products of complete oxidation:** $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, mineral acids, mineral salts.

### 2.2.2 General AOP Rate Law

For a target pollutant P with hydroxyl radical:

$$r_P = -\frac{d[P]}{dt} = k_{\bullet OH,\,P} \, [\bullet OH] \, [P]$$

Because [•OH] is constant at pseudo-steady state:

$$r_P = k_{obs} \, [P] \quad \text{(pseudo-first order)}$$

Integrating:

$$[P]_t = [P]_0 \, e^{-k_{obs} t}$$

where $k_{obs} = k_{\bullet OH,\,P} \, [\bullet OH]_{ss}$ is the **observed pseudo-first-order rate constant**.

### 2.2.3 Critical Theory of •OH Generation Pathways

**(a) Fenton Reaction (1894, H.J.H. Fenton)**

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{Fe}^{2+} \rightarrow \bullet \text{OH} + \text{OH}^- + \text{Fe}^{3+}$$

The reduced iron is regenerated:
$$\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + \bullet \text{OOH} + \text{H}^+$$
(slower — the rate-limiting step, $k \approx 0.001 \text{ to } 0.01 \; M^{-1}s^{-1}$)

**(b) Ozone / Hydrogen Peroxide (Peroxone)**

$$\text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \bullet \text{OH} + \text{O}_2 + \bullet \text{OOH}$$
$$\text{2O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{3O}_2 + \text{2}\bullet \text{OH}$$

**(c) Ozone + UV (Photo-ozonation)**

$$\text{O}_3 + h\nu \, (\lambda < 300 \, \text{nm}) \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + \text{O}(^1D)$$
$$\text{O}(^1D) + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{2}\bullet \text{OH}$$

**(d) H₂O₂ + UV (UV/H₂O₂)**

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + h\nu \, (\lambda \approx 254 \, \text{nm}) \rightarrow \text{2}\bullet \text{OH}$$

**(e) Heterogeneous Photocatalysis (TiO₂/UV)**

$$\text{TiO}_2 + h\nu \, (\lambda < 385 \, \text{nm}) \rightarrow e^-_{cb} + h^+_{vb}$$
$$h^+_{vb} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \bullet \text{OH} + \text{H}^+$$
$$e^-_{cb} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{O}_2^{\bullet -}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Definition | Expression | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) | $\theta = V/Q$ | s, h, or d | All reactor designs |
| 2 | Solids Retention Time (SRT / $\theta_c$) | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}$ | d | Activated sludge, MBR |
| 3 | Food-to-Microorganism ratio | $F/M = \dfrac{Q S_0}{V X} = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta X}$ | d⁻¹ | Aeration tank operation |
| 4 | Mean Cell Residence Time | Same as SRT | d | Activated sludge design |
| 5 | CSTR — first-order effluent | $C = \dfrac{C_0}{1 + k\theta}$ | mg/L | Single CSTR |
| 6 | PFR — first-order effluent | $C = C_0 e^{-k\theta}$ | mg/L | Plug flow systems |
| 7 | n-CSTRs in series (1st order) | $C = \dfrac{C_0}{(1 + k\theta/n)^n}$ | mg/L | Multiple CSTRs |
| 8 | Monod specific growth | $\mu = \dfrac{\mu_{max} S}{K_s + S}$ | d⁻¹ | Bio-kinetics |
| 9 | Substrate utilization (Pirt) | $r_{su} = -\dfrac{\mu_{max} S X}{Y(S+K_s)}$ | mg/L·d | Activated sludge |
| 10 | Pseudo-1st-order AOP decay | $C = C_0 e^{-k_{obs} t}$ | mg/L | Batch AOP kinetics |
| 11 | Second-order •OH rate | $r = k_{\bullet OH,P} [\bullet OH] [P]$ | M/s | Fundamental AOP |
| 12 | Fenton stoichiometry | $\dfrac{[\text{H}_2\text{O}_2]}{[\text{Fe}^{2+}]} \approx 5 \text{ to } 25$ | — | Optimum ratio |
| 13 | Ozone dose requirement | $D_{O_3} = \dfrac{Q \, (C_0 - C)}{Y_{O_3}}$ | g/h | Ozonation design |
| 14 | Photo-Fenton quantum yield | $\phi_{\bullet OH} \approx 0.6$ (at 254 nm) | mol/Einstein | UV-AOP design |
| 15 | Reynolds number (pipe reactor) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | — | Flow regime check |
| 16 | Dispersion number | $D/uL$ | — | PFR deviation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid the pitfall:** In the table above, every "**/**" replaces what would otherwise be a vertical pipe in a markdown row. Do **NOT** confuse reactor symbols. The formulas for CSTR and PFR look superficially similar — make sure to apply the right one for the right configuration.

### 2.3.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal STP design:** PFR vs CSTR choice affects energy consumption and footprint.
- **Industrial ETP:** SBR/MBR preferred for variable influent; Fenton used as **pre-treatment** before biological step.
- **Pharma/textile effluent:** AOPs are the **only** economically viable route to destroy AOX (adsorbable organic halides) and non-biodegradable color.
- **Drinking water:** AOPs as polishing step to remove taste/odor compounds (geosmin, MIB), pesticides (atrazine).

---

## 2.4 Reactor Selection Logic — Engineering Heuristic

| Influent Characteristic | Recommended Reactor |
|---|---|
| Constant flow, high strength | CSTR (activated sludge) |
| Variable / shock load | SBR or multiple CSTR in series |
| Recalcitrant / toxic | Fenton + Bio (SBR/CSTR) |
| Color / trace organics | PFR + Ozone / AOP |
| Space-constrained | MBR |
| Low-strength, rural | Trickling filter / RBC |
| Ammonia-rich, low C/N | MBBR / IFAS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight (KTU Board Value Point):** In real plants, **reactor choice is driven by kinetics + hydraulics + land availability + cost**, not by theoretical efficiency alone. A PFR gives best first-order removal, but if the substrate follows Monod kinetics, the difference between PFR and CSTR shrinks at high $S/K_s$ ratio.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)

The HRT is the **average time a fluid element spends inside the reactor** under steady state.

**Step 1 — Definition of volumetric flow rate and reactor volume.**
- Reactor volume: $V$ (m³)
- Volumetric flow rate: $Q$ (m³/h)

**Step 2 — Consider a tracer pulse of mass $M$ entering the reactor.**
The mass $M$ must leave with the exit stream. If the average residence time is $\theta$, then the rate of mass leaving is approximately $M/\theta$.

**Step 3 — Express the rate of mass leaving in terms of flow and concentration.**
$$M = C_{out} \cdot V$$
$$\text{Rate of mass leaving} = Q \cdot C_{out} = \dfrac{M}{\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\theta$.**
$$\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$$

> This confirms the **fundamental HRT identity.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CSTR Steady-State Mass Balance with First-Order Kinetics

**Step 1 — General steady-state mass balance on substrate $S$:**
$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} - \text{Removed}$$
$$0 = Q S_0 - Q S - r V$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the first-order removal rate:** $r = kS$
$$0 = Q S_0 - Q S - k S V$$

**Step 3 — Group terms containing $S$:**
$$0 = Q S_0 - S(Q + kV)$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $Q$:**
$$0 = S_0 - S\left(1 + \dfrac{kV}{Q}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\theta = V/Q$:**
$$0 = S_0 - S(1 + k\theta)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $S$:**
$$S = \dfrac{S_0}{1 + k\theta}$$

> This is the **canonical CSTR first-order effluent equation.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PFR Steady-State Mass Balance with First-Order Kinetics

**Step 1 — Take a control volume of thickness $dx$ at distance $x$ from inlet.**
**Step 2 — Apply steady-state mass balance:**
$$0 = Q S \big|_{x} - Q S \big|_{x+dx} - k S \, A \, dx$$
where $A$ is the cross-sectional area and $A \, dx$ is the differential volume $dV$.

**Step 3 — Expand and divide by $dx$:**
$$0 = -Q \dfrac{dS}{dx} - k S A$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and use $V = A \, x$ (with $A = V/L$ for constant cross-section):**
$$Q \, \dfrac{dS}{dx} = -k S A$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $x = 0$ ($S = S_0$) to $x = L$ ($S = S$):**
$$\int_{S_0}^{S} \dfrac{dS}{S} = -\dfrac{kA}{Q} \int_{0}^{L} dx$$

**Step 6 — Apply the integral $\int dS/S = \ln S$:**
$$\ln \dfrac{S}{S_0} = -\dfrac{kA L}{Q}$$

**Step 7 — Recognize $V = AL$ and $\theta = V/Q$:**
$$\ln \dfrac{S}{S_0} = -k \theta$$

**Step 8 — Exponentiate to obtain final form:**
$$\dfrac{S}{S_0} = e^{-k\theta} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; S = S_0 \, e^{-k\theta}$$

> This is the **canonical PFR first-order effluent equation.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Comparison: PFR vs CSTR Efficiency Ratio

**Step 1 — Form the ratio $\eta$ of PFR/CSTR effluent at the same $\theta$ and $k$:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{S_{CSTR}}{S_{PFR}} = \dfrac{S_0/(1+k\theta)}{S_0 e^{-k\theta}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{e^{k\theta}}{1 + k\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Use Taylor expansion for $e^{k\theta}$ near $k\theta = 0$:**
$$e^{k\theta} \approx 1 + k\theta + \dfrac{(k\theta)^2}{2} + \dfrac{(k\theta)^3}{6} + \cdots$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into $\eta$:**
$$\eta \approx \dfrac{1 + k\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}(k\theta)^2 + \cdots}{1 + k\theta} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{2}(k\theta)^2 + \cdots$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** for any $k\theta > 0$, we have $\eta > 1$, meaning the PFR effluent is **always lower** than the CSTR effluent. The advantage grows quadratically with $k\theta$.

> This is the **rigorous proof** that the PFR is more efficient than a single CSTR for first-order reactions.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — SRT Relationship to Wastewater Loading

**Step 1 — Define biomass balance on the aeration tank:**
$$V \dfrac{dX}{dt} = Y Q S_0 - Q_w X_w - Q_e X_e - k_d V X$$

**Step 2 — Steady state ($dX/dt = 0$):**
$$0 = Y Q S_0 - (Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e) - k_d V X$$

**Step 3 — Define SRT:** $\theta_c = \dfrac{V X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to express $1/\theta_c$ in terms of net biomass yield:**
$$\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \dfrac{Q S_0}{V X} - k_d = Y \cdot (F/M) - k_d$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to solve for F/M (design equation):**
$$\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{1}{Y}\left(\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d\right)$$

> This is the **fundamental activated-sludge design equation** linking F/M, SRT, yield, and decay coefficient.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — AOP Pseudo-First-Order Kinetics

**Step 1 — Fundamental rate law:**
$$r = -\dfrac{d[P]}{dt} = k_{\bullet OH} [\bullet OH] [P]$$

**Step 2 — Apply pseudo-steady state for •OH (formation rate = consumption rate):**
$$\dfrac{d[\bullet OH]}{dt} = R_{form} - k_{\bullet OH} [\bullet OH] [P] - k_s [\bullet OH] [S] = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for steady-state [•OH]:**
$$[\bullet OH]_{ss} = \dfrac{R_{form}}{k_{\bullet OH} [P] + k_s [S]}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the rate law. Assuming [S] (scavenger) is in excess, define $k_{obs}$:**
$$k_{obs} = k_{\bullet OH} [\bullet OH]_{ss} \approx \text{constant}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate first-order equation:**
$$-\dfrac{d[P]}{[P]} = k_{obs} \, dt \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ln \dfrac{[P]_0}{[P]} = k_{obs} t$$

**Step 6 — Final solution:**
$$[P](t) = [P]_0 \, e^{-k_{obs} t}$$

> This is the **universal "apparent first-order" equation** used to fit all AOP experimental data.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Fenton Stoichiometry & Optimum pH

**Step 1 — Master Fenton reaction:**
$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \bullet \text{OH} + \text{OH}^-$$
with $k \approx 63 \text{ to } 76 \; M^{-1} s^{-1}$.

**Step 2 — Catalyst regeneration (Haber-Weiss):**
$$\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + \bullet \text{OOH} + \text{H}^+$$
with $k \approx 0.001 \text{ to } 0.01 \; M^{-1} s^{-1}$ (very slow).

**Step 3 — Iron speciation as a function of pH:**
- At pH < 3, $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ exists predominantly as free ion — reactive.
- At pH 4 – 9, iron precipitates as $\text{Fe(OH)}_3$ — catalyst lost.
- **Optimum pH ≈ 2.8 – 3.0** for Fenton process.

**Step 4 — Optimum $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 / \text{Fe}^{2+}$ molar ratio:**
Empirically observed at $\approx 5$ – $25$ (typically $\sim 10$). Below this, radical scavenging by $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ dominates; above, $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ itself scavenges •OH:
$$\bullet \text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \bullet \text{OOH}$$

> This is the **theoretical reason** Fenton works only in a narrow pH window.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Reactor Suitability for AOPs

| Reactor | Suitable AOP | Why | Key Design Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| CSTR (stirred tank) | Fenton, Electro-Fenton | Requires mixing; homogeneous | HRT, $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ dose, pH |
| PFR (tubular) | O₃, UV/H₂O₂, TiO₂/UV | Plug flow maximizes $\Delta$•OH exposure | Contact time, UV dose |
| Bubble column | Ozonation, $\text{O}_3/\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ | Gas-liquid contact critical | $k_L a$, gas holdup |
| Thin-film / falling film | O₃, UV | High gas-transfer | $k_L a$, Reynolds number |
| Batch (lab scale) | All AOPs | Kinetic studies | $k_{obs}$, $t_{1/2}$ |
| Photocatalytic slurry | TiO₂/UV | Suspension needs mixing & light | Lamp intensity, [TiO₂] |
| Fixed-bed photocatalytic | TiO₂-coated | No separation needed | Surface area, $Re$ |

---

## 3.9 Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1 — CSTR vs PFR
A wastewater with $S_0 = 500$ mg/L BOD enters a reactor of volume $V = 1000$ m³ at $Q = 250$ m³/h. The first-order rate constant is $k = 0.4$ h⁻¹. Compare effluent BOD from a CSTR and a PFR.

**Step 1 — Compute HRT:**
$$\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{1000}{250} = 4 \, \text{h}$$

**Step 2 — CSTR effluent:**
$$S_{CSTR} = \dfrac{500}{1 + (0.4)(4)} = \dfrac{500}{1 + 1.6} = \dfrac{500}{2.6} \approx 192.3 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — PFR effluent:**
$$S_{PFR} = 500 \cdot e^{-(0.4)(4)} = 500 \cdot e^{-1.6} = 500 \cdot 0.2019 \approx 100.95 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — PFR is more efficient:** $\approx 91$ mg/L lower BOD → ~47% lower residual.

### Example 2 — AOP Kinetic Constant
A pollutant is removed in an AOP reactor following $C_t = C_0 \, e^{-k_{obs} t}$. If $C_0 = 100$ mg/L and $C_t = 12$ mg/L after 60 minutes, find $k_{obs}$ and the half-life.

**Step 1 — Solve for $k_{obs}$:**
$$k_{obs} = -\dfrac{1}{t} \ln \dfrac{C_t}{C_0} = -\dfrac{1}{60} \ln \dfrac{12}{100} = -\dfrac{1}{60} \cdot (-2.1203) \approx 0.0353 \, \text{min}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Half-life:**
$$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{obs}} = \dfrac{0.693}{0.0353} \approx 19.6 \, \text{min}$$

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between CSTR and PFR with respect to hydraulic regime and substrate removal efficiency for a first-order reaction.**

**Model Answer:**

- **CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor):** Assumes complete back-mixing; the concentration is uniform throughout the reactor and equal to the effluent concentration. The steady-state effluent substrate concentration for a first-order reaction is:
$$S_{CSTR} = \dfrac{S_0}{1 + k\theta}$$
- **PFR (Plug Flow Reactor):** Assumes no axial mixing; fluid moves as discrete plugs. Effluent substrate is:
$$S_{PFR} = S_0 e^{-k\theta}$$
- For the **same** HRT $\theta$ and rate constant $k$, the PFR always gives a lower effluent concentration. The efficiency ratio is $\dfrac{S_{CSTR}}{S_{PFR}} = \dfrac{e^{k\theta}}{1+k\theta} > 1$ for $k\theta > 0$.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What are Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)? List any four AOPs with their key feature.**

**Model Answer:**

AOPs are a set of treatment methods that **generate hydroxyl radicals (•OH)** *in situ* to oxidize recalcitrant organics into CO₂ and H₂O. The hydroxyl radical has an oxidation potential of **E° = +2.80 V**.

Four AOPs:
1. **Fenton Process** — $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2/\text{Fe}^{2+}$; works best at pH 2.8 – 3.0.
2. **Ozonation** — $\text{O}_3$ alone or combined with $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$/UV.
3. **UV/H₂O₂** — photolysis of $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ at 254 nm to yield •OH.
4. **TiO₂/UV Photocatalysis** — heterogeneous; UV excites TiO₂ to form e⁻/h⁺ pairs producing •OH.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU ESE Pattern):** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and is typically divided into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student must answer one full question from each module-internal choice pair.

---

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches and mathematical expressions, describe the **steady-state operation of a CSTR and a PFR** for first-order substrate removal. Show that for the same HRT, the PFR is always more efficient.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An activated-sludge aeration tank has $V = 1500$ m³, $Q = 500$ m³/d, $S_0 = 400$ mg/L BOD, $X = 2500$ mg/L VSS, $Y = 0.6$ mg VSS/mg BOD, and $k_d = 0.05$ d⁻¹. Calculate:
  (i) HRT
  (ii) F/M ratio
  (iii) SRT
  (iv) Effluent soluble BOD assuming $K = 0.05$ d⁻¹ first-order.

**Model Solution to (a):**

**CSTR Mass Balance:**
$$0 = Q S_0 - Q S - k S V$$
$$\Rightarrow S_{CSTR} = \dfrac{S_0}{1 + k\theta}$$
where $\theta = V/Q$.

**PFR Mass Balance (per unit length):**
$$Q \dfrac{dS}{dx} = -k S A$$
Integrating:
$$S_{PFR} = S_0 e^{-k\theta}$$

**Efficiency Comparison:** Forming the ratio
$$\dfrac{S_{CSTR}}{S_{PFR}} = \dfrac{e^{k\theta}}{1+k\theta}$$
Taylor expansion: $e^{k\theta} = 1 + k\theta + \dfrac{(k\theta)^2}{2} + \cdots$, so the ratio simplifies to:
$$\dfrac{S_{CSTR}}{S_{PFR}} \approx 1 + \dfrac{(k\theta)^2}{2} > 1$$
Hence PFR effluent is always lower. The PFR is preferred for first-order removal.

**Sketches:** (concentration profile across reactor length: PFR — exponential decay; CSTR — sudden drop to constant).

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) HRT:**
$$\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{1500}{500} = 3 \, \text{d}$$

**(ii) F/M ratio:**
$$\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta X} = \dfrac{400}{3 \times 2500} = 0.0533 \, \text{d}^{-1}$$

**(iii) SRT (from design equation):**
$$\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot (F/M) - k_d = 0.6 \times 0.0533 - 0.05 = -0.0180 \, \text{d}^{-1}$$
This gives a **negative** SRT → indicates the chosen $\theta$ is too short to maintain biomass; the aeration tank is overloaded. Either increase $\theta$ or decrease $S_0$ / increase $X$ / use higher-yield sludge.

*Revised* — if $S_0$ were $200$ mg/L, $F/M = 0.0267$ d⁻¹, then $1/\theta_c = 0.6(0.0267) - 0.05 = -0.034$, still negative. If $S_0 = 100$ mg/L, $F/M = 0.0133$ d⁻¹, $1/\theta_c = 0.6(0.0133) - 0.05 = -0.042$, still negative. The original problem has no steady state; this is a common KTU "trick" question.

**Worked numerical output (assuming direct question):** Use the design equation for SRT if $F/M$ is given (alternative method): if $F/M = 0.2$ d⁻¹ were specified, $\theta_c = 1/(0.6 \times 0.2 - 0.05) = 1/(0.07) = 14.3$ d.

**(iv) Effluent BOD (first-order, CSTR):**
$$S_e = \dfrac{S_0}{1 + K \theta} = \dfrac{400}{1 + 0.05 \times 3} = \dfrac{400}{1.15} = 347.8 \, \text{mg/L}$$
Removal efficiency = $\dfrac{400 - 347.8}{400} \times 100 \approx 13\%$. Indicates very low efficiency — consistent with overloaded reactor.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **theory of Advanced Oxidation Processes** with reference to the hydroxyl radical. Discuss the **Fenton, UV/H₂O₂, and O₃/H₂O₂** systems with the relevant reactions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a batch AOP study, a pollutant at 50 mg/L decayed to 5 mg/L in 90 minutes following first-order kinetics. Determine:
  (i) The pseudo-first-order rate constant $k_{obs}$.
  (ii) The half-life.
  (iii) The time required to achieve 99% removal.

**Model Solution to (a):**

- AOPs rely on the **hydroxyl radical (•OH)** as the chief oxidant. Its standard reduction potential is **+2.80 V**, second only to fluorine. It reacts non-selectively with most organics at near-diffusion-controlled rates ($10^8 - 10^{10} \; M^{-1} s^{-1}$).

- **Fenton Process:** The reaction $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{Fe}^{2+} \rightarrow \bullet \text{OH} + \text{OH}^- + \text{Fe}^{3+}$ generates •OH. Optimum pH is 2.8 – 3.0 because Fe²⁺ precipitates as $\text{Fe(OH)}_3$ at higher pH. The molar ratio $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 / \text{Fe}^{2+} \approx 10$ gives maximum •OH yield.

- **UV/H₂O₂:** At 254 nm (low-pressure Hg lamp), $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ homolytically cleaves: $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow 2\bullet \text{OH}$. Quantum yield $\phi \approx 0.5$. A free-radical chain follows, leading to non-selective oxidation.

- **O₃/H₂O₂ (Peroxone):** Hydroperoxide ion formed by ozone-hydroxide reaction accelerates •OH generation:
$$\text{O}_3 + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{HO}_2^- + \text{O}_2$$
$$\text{O}_3 + \text{HO}_2^- \rightarrow \bullet \text{OH} + \text{O}_2^{\bullet -} + \text{O}_2$$
•OH yield is high; used for trace organics, color, and micropollutants in drinking water.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) $k_{obs}$:**
$$k_{obs} = -\dfrac{1}{t} \ln \dfrac{C_t}{C_0} = -\dfrac{1}{90} \ln \dfrac{5}{50} = -\dfrac{1}{90} \cdot (-2.3026) = 0.0256 \, \text{min}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Half-life:**
$$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{obs}} = \dfrac{0.693}{0.0256} = 27.1 \, \text{min}$$

**(iii) 99% removal time:**
$$t_{99\%} = -\dfrac{1}{k_{obs}} \ln \dfrac{0.01 C_0}{C_0} = -\dfrac{1}{0.0256} \ln(0.01) = \dfrac{4.605}{0.0256} \approx 180 \, \text{min}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — examiner's red flags:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing CSTR and PFR formulas.** A CSTR gives $S = S_0 / (1 + k\theta)$, a PFR gives $S = S_0 e^{-k\theta}$. Mixing these up loses 4–7 marks instantly.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the exponential identity in efficiency comparison.** You must show $\dfrac{e^{k\theta}}{1+k\theta} > 1$ explicitly to claim "PFR is better."
> 
> 3. **AOP-related traps:**
>    - Fenton optimum pH is **2.8–3.0** (not 7). Many students write 7.
>    - Hydroxyl radical potential is **+2.80 V**, not 1.23 V (that is O₂).
>    - H₂O₂ photolysis uses **254 nm**, not 365 nm (TiO₂ uses 365 nm).
> 
> 4. **Sign convention on F/M and SRT:** A negative SRT indicates the system cannot sustain itself. Examiners *expect* you to identify and comment on this.
> 
> 5. **AOP kinetics units:** $k_{obs}$ should always carry the unit **time⁻¹** (min⁻¹ or h⁻¹) because it is pseudo-first-order.
> 
> 6. **Drawing PFR/CSTR diagrams with no labels on axes** (e.g., "Distance" or "Time") — lose 1–2 marks in design-type questions.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Reactor Configurations in Wastewater Engineering:**
  - CSTR: completely mixed; effluent = in-tank; formula $S = S_0/(1 + k\theta)$.
  - PFR: plug flow; no back-mixing; formula $S = S_0 e^{-k\theta}$.
  - SBR: batch, time-cyclic; one tank performs fill-react-settle-draw.
  - Attached growth: Trickling Filter, RBC, MBBR, FBBR.
  - Membrane: MBR (UF/MF coupling with activated sludge).

- **Design Equations (must memorize):**
  - HRT: $\theta = V/Q$.
  - SRT: $\theta_c = VX/(Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e)$.
  - F/M: $S_0/(\theta X)$.
  - Design link: $1/\theta_c = Y(F/M) - k_d$.

- **PFR > CSTR for first-order reactions** (proved via Taylor expansion of the efficiency ratio).

- **Advanced Oxidation Processes:**
  - All AOPs share the common goal of producing **•OH (E° = +2.80 V)**.
  - •OH reacts at near-diffusion rates ($10^8 - 10^{10} \; M^{-1} s^{-1}$) and is **non-selective**.
  - Pseudo-first-order: $C = C_0 e^{-k_{obs} t}$; $k_{obs} = k_{\bullet OH} [\bullet OH]_{ss}$.

- **Key AOP Reactions:**
  - Fenton: $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{Fe}^{2+} \rightarrow \bullet \text{OH} + \text{OH}^- + \text{Fe}^{3+}$.
  - Photo-Fenton: Fenton + UV → accelerated Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ regeneration.
  - UV/H₂O₂: $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow 2\bullet \text{OH}$ (254 nm).
  - O₃/H₂O₂: Peroxone — high •OH yield.
  - TiO₂/UV: $h^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \bullet \text{OH} + \text{H}^+$.

- **Fenton Optimum Conditions:**
  - pH **2.8 – 3.0** (precipitation of Fe(OH)₃ above pH 4).
  - $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 / \text{Fe}^{2+}$ molar ratio **~5–25** (typically 10).

- **Selection Heuristic:** Biological reactors for biodegradable streams; AOPs (especially Fenton) as **pre- or post-treatment** for biorefractory streams; PFR for trace organics; CSTR for shock loads.

- **Common S.I. units in KTU numericals:**
  - $V$ in m³, $Q$ in m³/d, $\theta$ in d, $k$ in d⁻¹, $S$ in mg/L, $X$ in mg/L, $F/M$ in d⁻¹.

- **Avoid pipe character in markdown tables** (use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value when writing inline math) — this is a **typesetting rule** that examiners sometimes mark for.

> *End of Module 4 Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme — PEBBT526 Biological Wastewater Treatment*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Oxidizing Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Oxidizing Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **oxidizing agent (oxidant)** is a chemical species that accepts electrons from another substance (the reductant) during a redox reaction, thereby causing the **oxidation** of the target compound. In the context of **Biological Wastewater Treatment (Module 4 – Tertiary/Advanced Treatment)**, oxidizing agents are used for **disinfection**, **pathogen inactivation**, **removal of refractory organics**, **micropollutant degradation**, **colour and odour removal**, and **in-situ chemical oxidation (ISCO)** of recalcitrant compounds.

The standard redox potential ($E^\circ$, measured in **Volts, V**) determines the relative oxidizing power of an agent. A higher positive $E^\circ$ value indicates a stronger oxidizing tendency.

$$ \text{Oxidizing Agent} + n\,e^- \longrightarrow \text{Reduced Form} \quad (E^\circ,\; \text{V vs. SHE}) $$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy: "The Pac-Man of Molecules"**
> Think of an oxidizing agent as a hungry **"electron Pac-Man."** Whenever it meets a pollutant molecule, it "eats" electrons from that molecule, ripping its structure apart. The hungrier the Pac-Man (higher the redox potential), the more aggressive the destruction. For example, **Ozone ($O_3$)** is a very hungry Pac-Man ($E^\circ = +2.07$ V), while dissolved **Oxygen ($O_2$)** is a moderate one ($E^\circ = +1.23$ V). In wastewater plants, we choose the "hunger level" of the oxidant based on how tough the pollutant is to destroy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEBBT526 – Module 4):**
> Module 4 of the syllabus covers **disinfection and advanced chemical oxidation**. Students are expected to classify the major oxidizing agents, recall their **redox potentials**, dose ranges (**mg/L**), contact times, and applications in municipal/industrial effluent treatment.

## 1.3 Classification Overview

Oxidizing agents used in wastewater treatment can be broadly classified as:

1. **Halogen-based oxidants** – Chlorine, Chlorine dioxide, Bromine, Iodine
2. **Oxygen-based oxidants** – Oxygen, Ozone, Hydrogen peroxide, Peracetic acid, Peroxone
3. **Metal-based oxidants** – Potassium permanganate, Ferrate(VI)
4. **Combined/Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)** – Fenton, Photo-Fenton, UV/H₂O₂, O₃/UV, O₃/H₂O₂

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Theory of Each Oxidizing Agent

### 2.1.1 Chlorine (Cl₂) and Hypochlorite (OCl⁻)

- Most widely used **disinfectant** in the world.
- In water, Cl₂ forms **hypochlorous acid (HOCl)**, which dissociates to **hypochlorite ion (OCl⁻)** depending on pH.

$$ \text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HOCl} + \text{H}^+ + \text{Cl}^- $$

$$ \text{HOCl} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{OCl}^- \quad (pK_a \approx 7.5) $$

- HOCl is **80–100 times more effective** than OCl⁻ as a disinfectant.
- Forms **disinfection by-products (DBPs)** like trihalomethanes (THMs) and haloacetic acids (HAAs).
- **Dose:** 5–20 mg/L for municipal wastewater; **Contact time:** 30 min minimum (CT value).

### 2.1.2 Chlorine Dioxide (ClO₂)

- A **selective oxidant**; does **not** form THMs.
- Effective over a wide pH range (**4–10**).
- Used for taste, odour, and colour control.

$$ \text{ClO}_2 + 5\,e^- + 4\,\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{Cl}^- + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (E^\circ = +0.95 \text{ V}) $$

### 2.1.3 Ozone (O₃)

- One of the **strongest commercial oxidants** ($E^\circ = +2.07$ V).
- Generated **on-site** by corona discharge (electrical) or UV radiation.
- Decomposes spontaneously, producing **hydroxyl radicals (·OH)** — the actual oxidizing species in water.

$$ \text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{O}_2 $$

$$ \text{O}_3 + \text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{HO}_2^- + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{O}_2^{-\cdot} + \text{H}^+ $$

- Disadvantages: **High capital cost**, no residual, energy-intensive.

### 2.1.4 Hydrogen Peroxide (H₂O₂)

- A "green" oxidant; byproduct is only water and oxygen.
- Weak on its own; powerful when combined with **UV**, **O₃**, or **Fe²⁺** (Fenton).
- Stabilized forms (35%, 50%) used industrially.

$$ \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + 2\,\text{H}^+ + 2\,e^- \longrightarrow 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (E^\circ = +1.78 \text{ V}) $$

### 2.1.5 Potassium Permanganate (KMnO₄)

- A solid, easy-to-handle, deep purple oxidant.
- Effective for **iron/manganese removal**, odour control, and **micropollutant oxidation**.
- Reduces to insoluble **MnO₂**, which aids in coagulation.

$$ \text{MnO}_4^- + 8\,\text{H}^+ + 5\,e^- \longrightarrow \text{Mn}^{2+} + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (E^\circ = +1.51 \text{ V}) $$

### 2.1.6 Peracetic Acid (PAA / CH₃COOOH)

- Increasingly popular **eco-friendly** disinfectant.
- Strong bactericidal and virucidal action.
- Decomposes to **acetic acid, water, and oxygen**.

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{COOOH} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 $$

### 2.1.7 Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)

AOPs generate **hydroxyl radicals (·OH)**, which have an extremely high redox potential ($E^\circ \approx +2.80$ V) and attack organics non-selectively.

| AOP System | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| Fenton (Fe²⁺/H₂O₂) | Fe²⁺ catalyzes H₂O₂ → ·OH |
| Photo-Fenton (UV/Fe²⁺/H₂O₂) | UV accelerates Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ cycling |
| O₃/H₂O₂ (Peroxone) | H₂O₂ accelerates O₃ decomposition → ·OH |
| O₃/UV | UV photolyses O₃ → ·OH |
| UV/H₂O₂ | UV photolyses H₂O₂ → 2·OH |
| TiO₂/UV (Photocatalysis) | Semiconductor generates e⁻/h⁺ pairs → ·OH |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all key KTU-relevant parameters. Vertical bars have been replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Oxidizing Agent | Chemical Formula | Redox Potential $E^\circ$ (V) | Typical Dose (mg/L) | Best Operating pH | By-products / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fluorine | $F_2$ | $+2.87$ | Not used (toxic) | — | Reference standard only |
| Hydroxyl radical | $\cdot OH$ | $+2.80$ | Generated in situ | 2–11 (AOP-dependent) | Non-selective, very short life ($\sim \mu s$) |
| Ozone | $O_3$ | $+2.07$ | 1–15 | 6–9 | No residual, forms bromate |
| Hydrogen Peroxide | $H_2O_2$ | $+1.78$ | 10–500 | 6–8 | Safe, water as byproduct |
| Permanganate | $MnO_4^-$ | $+1.51$ | 1–5 | 6.5–8.5 | Forms $MnO_2$ sludge |
| Chlorine Dioxide | $ClO_2$ | $+0.95$ | 0.5–5 | 4–10 | No THMs |
| Hypochlorous Acid | $HOCl$ | $+1.49$ | 5–20 | 6.5–7.5 | Forms THMs/HAAs |
| Hypochlorite | $OCl^-$ | $+0.94$ | 5–20 | $\geq 7.5$ | Forms THMs/HAAs |
| Dissolved Oxygen | $O_2$ | $+1.23$ | Saturation | Aerobic | Slow kinetics |
| Peracetic Acid | $CH_3COOOH$ | $+1.81$ | 1–15 | 5–8 | Acetic acid residue |
| Bromine | $Br_2$ | $+1.07$ | 1–5 | 6–8 | Forms brominated DBPs |
| Ferrate(VI) | $FeO_4^{2-}$ | $+2.20$ | 1–10 | 7–9 | Forms $Fe(OH)_3$ coagulant |

## 2.3 Key Engineering Equations

**Residual Disinfectant Concentration Model (Chick–Watson Law):**

$$ \ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k\,C^n\,t $$

Where:
- $N$ = surviving microorganisms (CFU/100 mL)
- $N_0$ = initial count
- $k$ = disinfection rate constant
- $C$ = residual disinfectant concentration (mg/L)
- $n$ = dilution coefficient (≈1 for chlorine)
- $t$ = contact time (min)

**Ozone Demand Equation:**

$$ [O_3]_{dose} = [O_3]_{demand} + [O_3]_{residual} $$

**Fenton Reaction Stoichiometry:**

$$ \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \cdot\text{OH} + \text{OH}^- $$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: pH-Dependent Speciation of Free Chlorine

This is a **classic KTU question**. We need to show that as pH rises above 7.5, the dominant species shifts from HOCl to OCl⁻, reducing disinfection efficiency.

### Step 1 – Write the Equilibrium

$$ \text{HOCl} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{OCl}^- \quad ; \quad K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]} $$

### Step 2 – Convert $K_a$ to $pK_a$

$$ pK_a = -\log_{10}(K_a) = 7.5 \;\text{(at 25°C)} $$

### Step 3 – Express Ratio of OCl⁻ to HOCl

$$ \frac{[\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]} = \frac{K_a}{[\text{H}^+]} $$

### Step 4 – Substitute $[\text{H}^+] = 10^{-pH}$

$$ \frac{[\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]} = 10^{(pH - pK_a)} = 10^{(pH - 7.5)} $$

### Step 5 – Calculate Fractions

Define the fraction of HOCl as $\alpha_{HOCl}$:

$$ \alpha_{HOCl} = \frac{[\text{HOCl}]}{[\text{HOCl}] + [\text{OCl}^-]} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(pH - 7.5)}} $$

Similarly:

$$ \alpha_{OCl^-} = \frac{10^{(pH - 7.5)}}{1 + 10^{(pH - 7.5)}} $$

### Step 6 – Numerical Evaluation at pH 6.0, 7.5, and 9.0

- **At pH 6.0:**

$$ \alpha_{HOCl} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{6.0 - 7.5}} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{-1.5}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0316} = 0.9694 \;\;(\approx 96.9\%) $$

- **At pH 7.5:**

$$ \alpha_{HOCl} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{0}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \;\;(50\%) $$

- **At pH 9.0:**

$$ \alpha_{HOCl} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{1.5}} = \frac{1}{1 + 31.62} = 0.0307 \;\;(\approx 3.1\%) $$

### Step 7 – Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At pH ≥ 7.5, more than half the chlorine exists as the much weaker OCl⁻. Therefore, **chlorination plants aim to operate at pH < 7.5** to maintain high HOCl fraction and ensure effective disinfection. This is why **chlorine dose is increased in alkaline wastewater**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Chlorine Dose Requirement

Given: Flow $Q = 10,\!000$ m³/d, Required chlorine dose $D = 8$ mg/L, Chlorine contact time $t = 30$ min.

### Step 1 – Convert Dose to g/m³

$$ D = 8 \text{ mg/L} = 8 \text{ g/m}^3 $$

### Step 2 – Total Chlorine Required per Day

$$ M_{Cl_2} = Q \times D = 10{,}000 \;\text{m}^3/\text{d} \times 8 \;\text{g/m}^3 = 80{,}000 \;\text{g/d} = 80 \;\text{kg/d} $$

### Step 3 – For Commercial Liquid Chlorine (100% Cl₂)

If chlorine is supplied in **50-kg cylinders**:

$$ \text{Number of cylinders/day} = \frac{80}{50} = 1.6 \;\text{cylinders} $$

### Step 4 – For Sodium Hypochlorite (NaOCl, 10% available chlorine)

Available Cl₂ per litre:

$$ C_{avail} = 0.10 \times 1000 \;\text{g/L} \times \frac{71}{74.5} = 95.3 \;\text{g available Cl}_2/\text{L} $$

Volume of NaOCl per day:

$$ V_{NaOCl} = \frac{80{,}000 \;\text{g/d}}{95.3 \;\text{g/L}} = 839.5 \;\text{L/d} \approx 0.84 \;\text{m}^3/\text{d} $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: CT Value Calculation (KTU Frequently Asked)

The **CT concept** is the product of residual disinfectant concentration ($C$, mg/L) and contact time ($t$, minutes), used to ensure regulatory disinfection compliance.

**Given:** $C = 2.0$ mg/L residual chlorine, $t = 30$ min.

$$ CT = C \times t = 2.0 \;\text{mg/L} \times 30 \;\text{min} = 60 \;\text{mg·min/L} $$

For a target pathogen, **CT$_{99.9}$** (3-log inactivation) requirement must be met. Typical values:

| Pathogen | CT$_{99.9}$ (mg·min/L) at pH 7 |
|---|---|
| E. coli | 0.25–0.5 |
| Rotavirus | 0.05–0.1 |
| Giardia | 30–70 |
| Cryptosporidium | ~5,400 (chlorine-resistant) |

> [!NOTE]
> For **Cryptosporidium**, chlorine is practically ineffective. **UV, ozone, or ClO₂** are preferred alternatives — this is a KTU favourite conceptual point.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Selection of Oxidizing Agent

| Criterion | Cl₂ | O₃ | ClO₂ | KMnO₄ | H₂O₂ | UV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Oxidizing Power ($E^\circ$) | Medium | Very High | Medium | Medium | High | N/A (photons) |
| Cost | Low | High | Medium | Medium | Medium | High |
| Residual | Yes | No | Slight | No | No | No |
| THM Formation | Yes | No | No | No | No | No |
| pH Sensitivity | High | Moderate | Low | Low | Low | Low |
| Cryptosporidium Control | Poor | Good | Good | Poor | Poor | Excellent |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Oxidizing Agents

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oxidizing Agents in Wastewater Treatment]:::root --> B[Halogen-Based]:::branch
    A --> C[Oxygen-Based]:::branch
    A --> D[Metal-Based]:::branch
    A --> E[Advanced Oxidation AOPs]:::branch

    B --> B1[Chlorine Cl2]
    B --> B2[Sodium Hypochlorite NaOCl]
    B --> B3[Chlorine Dioxide ClO2]
    B --> B4[Bromine Br2]
    B --> B5[Iodine I2]

    C --> C1[Dissolved Oxygen O2]
    C --> C2[Ozone O3]
    C --> C3[Hydrogen Peroxide H2O2]
    C --> C4[Peracetic Acid PAA]

    D --> D1[Potassium Permanganate KMnO4]
    D --> D2[Ferrate VI FeO4 2-]

    E --> E1[Fenton Fe2+ H2O2]
    E --> E2[Photo-Fenton UV Fe2+ H2O2]
    E --> E3[O3 H2O2 Peroxone]
    E --> E4[O3 UV]
    E --> E5[UV H2O2]
    E --> E6[TiO2 Photocatalysis]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef branch fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

    TANK --> RAD[Generation of Hydroxyl Radical OH E0 +2.80 V]:::rad
    RAD --> OX[Non-selective Oxidation of Organics]:::rxn
    OX --> OUT[CO2 + H2O + Ferric Sludge FeOH3]:::out

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between HOCl and OCl⁻ as disinfectants. Which is more effective and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | HOCl (Hypochlorous Acid) | OCl⁻ (Hypochlorite Ion) |
|---|---|---|
| Dissociation State | Undissociated | Dissociated |
| Charge | Neutral | Negative |
| Disinfection Efficiency | **80–100× more effective** | Weaker |
| Dominant pH Range | pH < 7.5 | pH > 7.5 |
| Cell Penetration | Easy (neutral molecule) | Difficult (charged) |

**HOCl is more effective** because being a neutral molecule, it diffuses easily through the negatively charged bacterial cell wall, whereas OCl⁻ is repelled. Hence, chlorination plants prefer to operate at **pH < 7.5** to maximize HOCl fraction.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List four advantages and two disadvantages of Ozone as a wastewater disinfectant.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Strongest commercial oxidant** ($E^\circ = +2.07$ V) — kills bacteria, viruses, and protozoa effectively.
2. Generates **hydroxyl radicals (·OH)** for advanced oxidation.
3. **No THM formation** — no chlorinated DBPs.
4. **Improves colour, taste, and odour** of effluent simultaneously.
5. Decomposes to O₂ — leaves no harmful residue.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **High capital and operating cost** (corona discharge generators are energy-intensive).
2. **No residual disinfectant** — recontamination possible in the distribution system.
3. May form **bromate** (a suspected carcinogen) if bromide is present.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Classify the major **oxidizing agents** used in biological wastewater treatment with at least **two examples** in each category. Discuss their **relative oxidation strengths** using standard redox potentials. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat labelled sketch, describe the **Fenton process** for advanced oxidation. Derive the key reaction, state the optimum pH, and list the engineering advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) – Classification of Oxidizing Agents:**

Oxidizing agents used in wastewater treatment are classified as:

| Category | Examples | Approx. $E^\circ$ (V) |
|---|---|---|
| Halogen-based | $Cl_2$, $ClO_2$, $HOCl$, $NaOCl$, $Br_2$ | +0.95 to +1.49 |
| Oxygen-based | $O_3$, $H_2O_2$, $PAA$, $O_2$ | +1.23 to +2.07 |
| Metal-based | $KMnO_4$, $K_2FeO_4$ (Ferrate) | +1.51 to +2.20 |
| Advanced Oxidation (AOPs) | Fenton, UV/H₂O₂, O₃/UV, TiO₂/UV | $E^\circ(\cdot OH) \approx +2.80$ |

**Relative Strength Order (descending):**

$$ \cdot OH \;>\; F_2 \;>\; \text{Ferrate(VI)} \;>\; O_3 \;>\; H_2O_2 \;>\; KMnO_4 \;>\; Cl_2 \;>\; O_2 $$

A higher $E^\circ$ indicates a stronger thermodynamic tendency to accept electrons (i.e., stronger oxidizing power). Therefore, $\cdot OH$ generated in AOPs is the strongest, capable of mineralizing recalcitrant compounds to $CO_2$ and $H_2O$.

**Part (b) – Fenton Process:**

**Reaction:**

$$ \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \cdot\text{OH} + \text{OH}^- $$

The generated $\cdot OH$ radical is non-selective and attacks dissolved organics (RH):

$$ \cdot\text{OH} + \text{RH} \longrightarrow \text{R}^{\cdot} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \text{ (mineralization)} $$

The Fe³⁺ produced precipitates as **ferric hydroxide sludge**:

$$ \text{Fe}^{3+} + 3\,\text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_3 \downarrow $$

**Optimum Conditions:**
- **pH = 3.0 – 4.0** (best: 2.8–3.5). At higher pH, iron precipitates as Fe(OH)₃; at lower pH, ·OH formation is suppressed.
- **Fe²⁺ : H₂O₂ molar ratio** ≈ 1 : 5 to 1 : 25.
- Typical doses: Fe²⁺ = 5–50 mg/L; H₂O₂ = 50–500 mg/L.

**Schematic (Block Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WW[Wastewater] --> FM[Mixing Tank pH 2.8-3.5]
    FE[FeSO4 Solution] --> FM
    HP[30% H2O2] --> FM
    FM --> RX[Fenton Reactor HRT 30-60 min]
    RX --> NEUT[Neutralization Tank pH 7-8]
    NEUT --> SED[Settler]
    SED --> EFF[Treated Effluent]
    SED --> SL[Ferric Sludge FeOH3]
```

**Advantages:**
- Simple, inexpensive reagents.
- High COD removal (60–90%) for refractory wastewater.
- Ferric sludge acts as a coagulant for further TSS removal.

**Limitations:**
- Narrow pH window (2.8–3.5).
- Generates large volumes of iron sludge.
- H₂O₂ handling risks (oxidizer, exothermic decomposition).
- Cannot be used when effluent must remain colourless.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the **pH-dependent speciation of chlorine** in water. Derive the Henderson–Hasselbalch-type expression and determine the **HOCl and OCl⁻ fractions at pH 6.0, 7.5, and 9.0**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **mechanism, advantages, and limitations of Ozone disinfection**. With suitable equations, show how ozone decomposes to form hydroxyl radicals. Why is ozone preferred over chlorine for wastewater containing **bromide ions**? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) – pH-Dependent Speciation:**

The equilibrium is:

$$ \text{HOCl} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{OCl}^- \quad ; \quad pK_a = 7.5 \text{ at 25°C} $$

Using the Henderson–Hasselbalch form:

$$ pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\frac{[\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]} $$

Rearranging:

$$ \frac{[\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]} = 10^{(pH - 7.5)} $$

Fractions:

$$ \alpha_{HOCl} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(pH - 7.5)}}, \quad \alpha_{OCl^-} = \frac{10^{(pH - 7.5)}}{1 + 10^{(pH - 7.5)}} $$

**Calculations:**

- **pH 6.0:** $\alpha_{HOCl} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 10^{-1.5}} = \dfrac{1}{1.0316} = 0.969 \approx 96.9\%$
- **pH 7.5:** $\alpha_{HOCl} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 10^{0}} = 0.500 = 50.0\%$
- **pH 9.0:** $\alpha_{HOCl} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 10^{1.5}} = \dfrac{1}{32.62} = 0.0307 \approx 3.1\%$

**Inference:** At pH ≥ 7.5, the dominant species is the much weaker OCl⁻. Therefore, **chlorine disinfection is most effective below pH 7.5**.

**Part (b) – Ozone Disinfection:**

**Mechanism:**
1. **Direct molecular oxidation** by $O_3$ (selective, attacks specific functional groups).
2. **Indirect radical oxidation** by $\cdot OH$ generated from ozone decomposition (non-selective, very fast).

**Key Equations:**

$$ \text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{O}_2 $$

$$ \text{O}_3 + \text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{HO}_2^{-} + \text{O}_2 $$

$$ \text{HO}_2^{-} + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \cdot\text{OH} + \text{O}_2^{-} + \text{O}_2 $$

The $\cdot OH$ radical ($E^\circ = +2.80$ V) attacks and mineralizes organics:

$$ \cdot\text{OH} + \text{Organics} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Advantages:**
- **Highest oxidation potential** among commercial oxidants.
- Effective against **chlorine-resistant pathogens** like *Cryptosporidium* and *Giardia*.
- No THM/HAAs formation.
- Simultaneous **disinfection + decolourization + deodorization**.

**Limitations:**
- High capital & energy cost.
- No residual; risk of regrowth.
- Forms **bromate (BrO₃⁻)** in bromide-containing water — a probable human carcinogen.
- Toxic gas; requires careful handling.

**Why ozone over chlorine for bromide waters?**
When chlorine is used in bromide-containing wastewater, it forms **brominated DBPs (bromoform, bromoacetic acids)** which are more carcinogenic than their chlorinated analogues. Ozone, although it can oxidize Br⁻ to BrO₃⁻, can be controlled using **ammonia or H₂O₂ addition** and at appropriate doses produces fewer total DBPs. **Chlorine dioxide** is, however, the most preferred alternative since it does not react with Br⁻ at all.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in 'Oxidizing Agents' Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state redox potential values** — always quote $E^\circ$ in **Volts vs. SHE** for each agent. Omitting units costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing HOCl and OCl⁻ effectiveness** — remember HOCl is **~80× stronger**, not 2×. Numerical comparison must be correct.
> 3. **Missing DBPs in chlorine answers** — examiners expect explicit mention of **THMs (CHCl₃)** and **HAAs**.
> 4. **Not specifying optimum pH for Fenton** — writing just "low pH" loses marks. Always quote **pH 2.8–3.5**.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation step** in pH-speciation — KTU wants the **Henderson–Hasselbalch derivation** explicitly, not just the final numbers.
> 6. **Forgetting by-products** of Fenton (Fe(OH)₃ sludge) and Ozone (BrO₃⁻ in bromide water).
> 7. **Units mismatch** — always write dose in **mg/L**, redox potential in **V (Volts)**, CT value in **mg·min/L**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** An oxidizing agent accepts electrons; its strength is given by the **standard redox potential $E^\circ$ (V)**.
- **Strongest commercial oxidant:** **Ozone ($O_3$)**, $E^\circ = +2.07$ V. Strongest of all (theoretically) is the **hydroxyl radical ($\cdot OH$)**, $E^\circ \approx +2.80$ V, generated in AOPs.
- **Chlorine speciation** is governed by $pK_a = 7.5$ at 25°C; HOCl dominates below this pH.
- **HOCl is ~80–100× more effective** than OCl⁻ because it is a neutral molecule.
- **DBP formation:** Chlorine → THMs/HAAs; Ozone → Bromate (in Br⁻ water); ClO₂ → No DBPs.
- **Fenton process** uses Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ at **pH 2.8–3.5** to generate $\cdot OH$.
- **Peracetic acid (PAA)** is an emerging eco-friendly disinfectant decomposing to **acetic acid + O₂**.
- **Chick–Watson Law:** $\ln(N/N_0) = -k\,C^n\,t$ governs disinfection kinetics.
- **CT value** = $C \times t$ is the regulatory metric for disinfection performance.
- **Cryptosporidium** is highly resistant to chlorine — use **UV, Ozone, or ClO₂**.
- **Permanganate ($KMnO_4$)** is favoured for **iron/manganese removal** and odour control, and produces $MnO_2$ as a useful byproduct.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** Use the oxidant whose $E^\circ$ comfortably exceeds the target pollutant's oxidation potential, while balancing **cost, residual, and DBP formation**.
- **For KTU exams:** Always quote the $E^\circ$ value, the dose range (mg/L), optimum pH, and at least one by-product for **every** oxidant you mention.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT526 - BIOLOGICAL WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ozone Based and Non-Ozone Based Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)

**Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)** are a set of chemical treatment procedures designed to remove organic and inorganic materials from wastewater by oxidation through reactions with **hydroxyl radicals (•OH)**. These radicals are extremely powerful, non-selective oxidants capable of mineralizing a wide range of recalcitrant pollutants that are resistant to conventional biological treatment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** AOPs are characterized by *in-situ* generation of highly reactive **hydroxyl radicals (•OH)** with an oxidation potential of **2.80 V**, second only to fluorine (2.87 V).

## 1.2 Classification of AOPs

AOPs are broadly classified into two categories based on the primary oxidant/reagent used:

### A. Ozone-Based Processes
1. **Ozonation (O₃)** — Standalone ozone treatment
2. **O₃/H₂O₂ (Peroxone Process)**
3. **O₃/UV (Ozone + Ultraviolet radiation)**
4. **O₃/Catalyst (Heterogeneous catalytic ozonation)**

### B. Non-Ozone Based Processes
1. **Fenton Process (Fe²⁺/H₂O₂)**
2. **Photo-Fenton (Fe²⁺/H₂O₂/UV)**
3. **UV/H₂O₂**
4. **UV/TiO₂ (Heterogeneous Photocatalysis)**
5. **Wet Air Oxidation (WAO)**
6. **Supercritical Water Oxidation (SCWO)**
7. **Electrochemical Oxidation**

## 1.3 The Hydroxyl Radical: The Heart of AOPs

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Symbol | **•OH** |
| Oxidation Potential | **2.80 V (vs. NHE)** |
| Lifetime | **~10⁻⁹ seconds** in water |
| Selectivity | Non-selective (attacks most organics) |
| Reaction Rate Constant | **10⁸ – 10¹⁰ M⁻¹s⁻¹** |

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Pac-Man of Molecules":**
> Imagine the hydroxyl radical (•OH) as an extremely hungry "Pac-Man" that devours almost any organic pollutant molecule it encounters in wastewater. Unlike a picky eater (like chlorine which targets specific compounds), •OH is non-selective — it attacks virtually everything in its path, breaking complex toxic molecules (pesticides, dyes, pharmaceuticals) into smaller, harmless fragments (CO₂, H₂O, and mineral acids). The AOP systems discussed in this module are simply different "kitchens" designed to produce this Pac-Man (•OH) most efficiently.

## 1.5 Comparative Oxidation Potential Table

| Oxidant | Oxidation Potential (V) | Relative Strength |
|---|---|---|
| Fluorine (F₂) | 2.87 | Reference |
| **Hydroxyl Radical (•OH)** | **2.80** | 2nd strongest |
| Ozone (O₃) | 2.07 | 3rd |
| Hydrogen Peroxide (H₂O₂) | 1.77 | Moderate |
| Permanganate (MnO₄⁻) | 1.67 | Moderate |
| Chlorine (Cl₂) | 1.36 | Weak |
| Oxygen (O₂) | 1.23 | Weakest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The hydroxyl radical's **2.80 V** oxidation potential makes it the most practical, universally applicable oxidant in water treatment — surpassing even ozone (2.07 V) in raw oxidizing power.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 OZONE-BASED PROCESSES

### 2.1.1 Ozonation (O₃) — Standalone Process

**Ozone (O₃)** is an allotrope of oxygen produced industrially by **corona discharge** method, where dry air or pure oxygen is subjected to a high-voltage electric field (typically **6–20 kV**).

#### Reactions in Aqueous Phase:

**Step 1:** Ozone dissolves in water (Henry's Law applies):
$$O_{3(g)} \rightleftharpoons O_{3(aq)}$$

**Step 2:** Direct molecular ozone reaction (slow, selective):
$$O_3 + \text{Organics} \rightarrow \text{Products (aldehydes, carboxylic acids)}$$

**Step 3:** Indirect radical pathway (fast, non-selective) — initiated by hydroxide ion:
$$O_3 + OH^- \rightarrow HO_2^- + O_2$$

$$HO_2^- + O_3 \rightarrow HO_2^{\bullet} + O_3^{-\bullet}$$

$$HO_2^{\bullet} \rightleftharpoons O_2^{-\bullet} + H^+$$

$$O_2^{-\bullet} + O_3 \rightarrow O_3^{-\bullet} + O_2$$

$$O_3^{-\bullet} + H^+ \rightarrow HO_3^{\bullet}$$

$$HO_3^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{\textbullet}OH + O_2$$

#### Why and How:
- The **direct pathway** dominates at **low pH (< 4)**, with selective attack on specific functional groups (C=C, amines, etc.).
- The **indirect radical pathway** dominates at **high pH (> 9)**, producing the highly reactive •OH radical.
- **Optimum pH** for ozonation in most applications lies in the range **6.5 – 7.5** to balance both pathways.

#### Key Engineering Parameters:
- **Ozone dose:** typically **1–20 mg/L** for disinfection; **5–50 mg/L** for oxidation
- **Contact time:** **5–30 minutes**
- **CT value (disinfection):** **0.02–0.2 mg·min/L** for viruses
- **Ozone transfer efficiency:** **80–95%** in modern fine-bubble diffusers
- **Henry's Constant for O₃:** **H = 100 atm·mol/mol** (low solubility compared to O₂)

---

### 2.1.2 O₃/H₂O₂ Process (Peroxone Process)

Hydrogen peroxide accelerates the decomposition of ozone into hydroxyl radicals.

#### Core Reaction Chain:
$$H_2O_2 \rightleftharpoons HO_2^- + H^+$$

$$HO_2^- + O_3 \rightarrow HO_2^{\bullet} + O_3^{-\bullet}$$

$$HO_2^{\bullet} \rightleftharpoons H^+ + O_2^{-\bullet}$$

$$O_2^{-\bullet} + O_3 \rightarrow O_3^{-\bullet} + O_2$$

$$O_3^{-\bullet} + H^+ \rightarrow HO_3^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{\textbullet}OH + O_2$$

#### Why and How:
- H₂O₂ acts as an **initiator** for ozone decomposition at a much faster rate than OH⁻ alone.
- The **peroxone process** is more effective at neutral pH than O₃ alone.
- Optimum H₂O₂:O₃ molar ratio = **0.5 – 1.0**

#### Applications:
- Removal of **MTBE, atrazine, trichloroethylene (TCE)**
- Pulp and paper mill effluent
- Pharmaceutical wastewater

---

### 2.1.3 O₃/UV Process

UV photolysis of ozone produces hydrogen peroxide, which in turn generates hydroxyl radicals.

#### Reaction Sequence:
$$O_3 + hv(\lambda < 310\,nm) \rightarrow O_2 + O(^1D)$$

$$O(^1D) + H_2O \rightarrow H_2O_2$$

$$H_2O_2 + hv \rightarrow 2\,\text{\textbullet}OH$$

#### Why and How:
- UV light **energizes** ozone molecules, leading to their photolysis and secondary •OH formation.
- Most effective for **chlorinated VOCs** and **recalcitrant organics**.
- LPHO (Low Pressure High Output) UV lamps with **254 nm** are commonly used.

---

### 2.1.4 O₃/Catalytic Ozonation (Heterogeneous)

Catalysts used: **TiO₂, MnO₂, Fe₂O₃, CuO, activated carbon, zeolites**

The catalyst surface initiates radical chain reactions and also provides adsorption sites for pollutants.

---

## 2.2 NON-OZONE BASED PROCESSES

### 2.2.1 Fenton Process (Fe²⁺/H₂O₂)

Discovered by **H.J.H. Fenton in 1894**, this is one of the most widely used industrial AOPs.

#### Core Mechanism (Haber-Weiss Cycle):

**Step 1 — Initiation (Catalyst regeneration, rate-determining):**
$$Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + \text{\textbullet}OH + OH^- \qquad (k_1 = 63 - 76\,M^{-1}s^{-1})$$

**Step 2 — Propagation:**
$$Fe^{3+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + HO_2^{\bullet} + H^+ \qquad (k_2 = 0.001 - 0.01\,M^{-1}s^{-1})$$

**Step 3 — Termination:**
$$Fe^{2+} + \text{\textbullet}OH \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + OH^-$$

$$\text{\textbullet}OH + \text{\textbullet}OH \rightarrow H_2O_2$$

#### Why and How:
- Fenton process works best in **acidic conditions (pH 2.5 – 4.0)** because:
  - At higher pH, Fe³⁺ precipitates as **Fe(OH)₃** sludge.
  - Below pH 2.5, H₂O₂ becomes stable as an oxonium ion (H₃O₂⁺).
- **Optimum Fe²⁺:H₂O₂ molar ratio** ≈ **1:5 to 1:10**
- **Optimum H₂O₂ dose:** depends on COD, typically **0.3–3.0 × COD**
- **Temperature:** ambient to **40 °C** (decomposition of H₂O₂ accelerates above 50 °C)

#### Limitations:
- Production of **iron sludge** (Fe(OH)₃)
- Narrow pH window
- Slow regeneration of Fe²⁺ (rate-limiting step)

---

### 2.2.2 Photo-Fenton (Fe²⁺/H₂O₂/UV)

UV/visible light accelerates Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ regeneration, expanding the useful pH range to **4 – 6**.

#### Key Reaction:
$$Fe(OH)^{2+} + hv \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + \text{\textbullet}OH$$

This step bypasses the slow thermal regeneration of Fe²⁺.

#### Advantages:
- Higher •OH yield
- Reduced iron sludge
- Works at higher pH

---

### 2.2.3 UV/H₂O₂ Process

A clean, sludge-free AOP where UV photolysis of H₂O₂ directly yields •OH radicals.

#### Core Reaction:
$$H_2O_2 + hv(\lambda = 254\,nm) \rightarrow 2\,\text{\textbullet}OH \qquad (\phi = 0.5\,mol/Einstein)$$

#### Key Parameters:
- **Lamp type:** Low-pressure mercury vapor (LP) at **254 nm**
- **H₂O₂ dose:** **5–100 mg/L**
- **UV dose:** **20–50 mJ/cm²**
- **pH:** 6.5 – 7.5 (optimum)
- **Quantum yield (φ):** 0.5 mol •OH per mole of H₂O₂ photolyzed

#### Advantages:
- No sludge formation
- No chemicals added except H₂O₂
- Simple operation

#### Limitations:
- **High alkalinity and turbidity** reduce UV transmission
- Carbonate/bicarbonate scavenges •OH (use as •OH scavengers):
  $$\text{\textbullet}OH + HCO_3^- \rightarrow CO_3^{-\bullet} + H_2O$$

---

### 2.2.4 UV/TiO₂ Photocatalysis (Heterogeneous)

**TiO₂ (anatase form)** is the most widely used photocatalyst due to its chemical stability, low cost, and non-toxicity.

#### Mechanism:
**Step 1 — Photoexcitation** (bandgap energy **E_bg = 3.2 eV**, λ ≤ 387 nm):
$$TiO_2 + hv \rightarrow e^-_{(CB)} + h^+_{(VB)}$$

**Step 2 — Charge carrier separation and trapping:**
$$h^+_{(VB)} + H_2O \rightarrow \text{\textbullet}OH + H^+$$

**h⁺ + OH⁻ → •OH** (surface hydroxyl oxidation)

**Step 3 — Reduction pathway:**
$$e^-_{(CB)} + O_2 \rightarrow O_2^{-\bullet}$$

**Step 4 — Mineralization:**
$$\text{Organics} + \text{\textbullet}OH / O_2^{-\bullet} \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + \text{mineral acids}$$

#### Why TiO₂?
- **Bandgap:** 3.2 eV (UV activation)
- **Quantum efficiency:** 1–10%
- Forms used: **Anatase, Rutile, Brookite** (anatase is most photocatalytically active)

---

### 2.2.5 Wet Air Oxidation (WAO)

**Wet Air Oxidation** involves the oxidation of soluble or suspended organic compounds in aqueous solution using **molecular oxygen (air)** as the oxidant at elevated temperatures and pressures.

#### Operating Conditions:
- **Temperature:** **125–320 °C**
- **Pressure:** **0.5–20 MPa** (must keep water in liquid phase)
- **Residence time:** **15–120 minutes**
- **Oxidant:** Air or pure O₂

#### General Reaction:
$$\text{Organic matter} + O_2 \xrightarrow{T, P} CO_2 + H_2O + \text{intermediates}$$

#### Why and How:
- At high T and P, O₂ dissolves better and reacts non-selectively.
- Suitable for effluents too toxic for biological treatment.
- Typically achieves **75–90% COD reduction** alone.

---

### 2.2.6 Supercritical Water Oxidation (SCWO)

Water above its **critical point (T_c = 374 °C, P_c = 22.1 MPa)** behaves as a non-polar solvent, fully miscible with organics and oxygen.

#### Reaction:
$$\text{Organics} + O_2 \xrightarrow{T > 374°C, P > 22.1\,MPa} CO_2 + H_2O + N_2 + \text{halides}$$

#### Advantages:
- **>99.99% destruction** in <1 minute
- No off-gas emissions
- Complete mineralization

#### Challenges:
- Corrosion of reactors (Ni-based alloys used)
- Salt precipitation plugging
- High capital cost

---

### 2.2.7 Electrochemical Oxidation

Direct or indirect oxidation of pollutants at anodes (BDD, PbO₂, Ti/SnO₂, etc.) producing •OH at the anode surface:

$$M + H_2O \rightarrow M(\text{\textbullet}OH) + H^+ + e^-$$

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Ozone Decomposition Kinetics in Water

### Problem Setup:
The decomposition of dissolved ozone in pure water follows pseudo-first-order kinetics. Derive the integrated form and the half-life expression.

**Starting differential equation:**
$$-\frac{d[O_3]}{dt} = k_d \cdot [O_3]$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange the differential equation** by dividing both sides by [O₃]:
$$-\frac{1}{[O_3]} \frac{d[O_3]}{dt} = k_d$$

**Step 2 — Integrate** from initial concentration [O₃]₀ at t = 0 to [O₃] at time t:
$$\int_{[O_3]_0}^{[O_3]} -\frac{d[O_3]}{[O_3]} = \int_0^t k_d \cdot dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals:**
$$-\ln[O_3] \Big\vert_{[O_3]_0}^{[O_3]} = k_d \cdot t$$

$$\ln[O_3]_0 - \ln[O_3] = k_d \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange using logarithm properties:**
$$\ln\left(\frac{[O_3]_0}{[O_3]}\right) = k_d \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Final integrated rate law:**
$$[O_3] = [O_3]_0 \cdot e^{-k_d t}$$

**Step 6 — Half-life derivation.** Set [O₃] = [O₃]₀/2:
$$\ln\left(\frac{[O_3]_0}{[O_3]_0/2}\right) = \ln(2) = k_d \cdot t_{1/2}$$

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_d} = \frac{0.693}{k_d}$$

### Typical Values at 20 °C, pH 7:
- k_d ≈ **0.01 min⁻¹** → t₁/₂ ≈ **69 minutes**
- k_d ≈ **1.5 min⁻¹** at pH 10 → t₁/₂ ≈ **0.46 minutes**

This shows the **strong pH dependence** of ozone decomposition — basis for •OH generation at high pH.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Fenton Process Optimum H₂O₂ Dose from Stoichiometry

### Problem:
Calculate the stoichiometric H₂O₂ required to completely mineralize **100 mg/L of phenol (C₆H₅OH)** using the Fenton process.

**Step 1 — Write the mineralization equation:**

Phenol mineralizes to CO₂ and H₂O:
$$C_6H_5OH + 14\,H_2O_2 \rightarrow 6\,CO_2 + 17\,H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Molecular weights:**
- C₆H₅OH = 6(12) + 6(1) + 16 = **94 g/mol**
- H₂O₂ = 2(1) + 2(16) = **34 g/mol**

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric H₂O₂ mass required per gram of phenol:**

From the balanced equation: 1 mol C₆H₅OH consumes 14 mol H₂O₂
$$\frac{14 \times 34\,g\,H_2O_2}{94\,g\,phenol} = \frac{476}{94} = 5.06\,g\,H_2O_2/g\,phenol$$

**Step 4 — Apply to 100 mg/L phenol:**
$$H_2O_2 = 100 \times 5.06 = 506\,mg/L\,H_2O_2$$

**Step 5 — Add empirical safety factor.** Practical Fenton application uses **1.0–1.5× stoichiometric dose** to overcome scavenging by Fe²⁺ and side reactions:
$$H_2O_2^{practical} = 1.2 \times 506 = 607\,mg/L$$

**Step 6 — Required Fe²⁺ dose** at Fe²⁺:H₂O₂ molar ratio of 1:10:
$$n_{H_2O_2} = \frac{607}{34} = 17.85\,mmol/L$$

$$n_{Fe^{2+}} = \frac{17.85}{10} = 1.785\,mmol/L$$

$$[Fe^{2+}] = 1.785 \times 55.85 = 99.7\,mg/L$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Insight:** Industrial Fenton systems often use **Fe²⁺ dose of 50–200 mg/L** when treating 100–1000 mg/L COD wastewater, confirming our derived value is realistic.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Quantum Yield Calculation for UV/H₂O₂

### Problem:
A UV reactor delivers **2.0×10¹⁸ photons/s** at **254 nm** to water containing **10 mg/L H₂O₂**. Calculate the rate of •OH generation.

**Step 1 — Convert photon flux to Einstein/s** (1 Einstein = 1 mol of photons):
$$\Phi_E = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{18}\,photons/s}{6.022 \times 10^{23}\,photons/mol} = 3.32 \times 10^{-6}\,Einstein/s$$

**Step 2 — Apply quantum yield** (φ = 0.5 mol •OH per Einstein at 254 nm):
$$r_{\text{\textbullet}OH} = \phi \times \Phi_E = 0.5 \times 3.32 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$r_{\text{\textbullet}OH} = 1.66 \times 10^{-6}\,mol/s = 1.66\,\mu mol/s$$

**Step 3 — Convert to per liter basis** (assuming 1 L reactor):
$$r_{\text{\textbullet}OH} = 1.66 \times 10^{-6}\,M/s = 1.66\,\mu M/s$$

**Step 4 — Compare to radical demand.** A typical micropollutant at 1 µg/L (≈ 1 nM) is consumed by •OH at k ≈ 10⁹ M⁻¹s⁻¹:
$$r_{consumption} = k[\text{\textbullet}OH][Pollutant]$$

This demonstrates that even low •OH fluxes efficiently destroy trace organics.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Ozone Mass Transfer Rate

The rate of ozone transfer from gas to liquid phase in a bubble reactor is given by:

$$\frac{dC_L}{dt} = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$

**Where:**
- $C_L$ = dissolved ozone concentration (mg/L)
- $C^*$ = saturation concentration (Henry's Law)
- $k_L$ = liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (m/s)
- $a$ = specific interfacial area (m²/m³)
- $k_L a$ = volumetric mass transfer coefficient (s⁻¹)

**Saturation concentration from Henry's Law:**
$$C^* = \frac{P_{O_3}}{H \cdot R \cdot T}$$

**Where:**
- $P_{O_3}$ = partial pressure of O₃ in gas (atm)
- H = Henry's constant (atm·m³/mol)
- R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- T = temperature (K)

**Steady-state balance (when dC_L/dt = 0):**
$$C_L^{ss} = C^* = \frac{P_{O_3}}{H \cdot R \cdot T}$$

This shows why **high O₃ partial pressure** and **low temperature** improve O₃ dissolution.
